Here, vegetable tempura and vegetable-filled hand-rolls (temaki) could work as a starting point, but the soba tsuyu and most sauces at Gonpachi locations are traditionally dashi-based (bonito/katsuobushi), so a fully vegan meal is not guaranteed without asking staff directly and being prepared for limited options.
places.hours_raw: Daily, including weekends and holidays: 11:30-23:00 (food last order 22:00, drinks last order 22:30)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Azumabashi, Taito-ku (riverside, at the foot of Az
places.cuisine: Izakaya / Yakitori / hand-made soba with Sum
places.notes: Gonpachi Asakusa Azumabashi opened in January 2017 as one of the newer branches of the long-running Gonpachi izakaya group. It sits in a quiet spot on the bank of the Sumida River at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, about a 30-second walk from Exit 4 of Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Gonpachi Asakusa Azumabashi opened in January 2017 as one of the newer branches of the long-running Gonpachi izakaya group. It sits in a quiet spot on the bank of the Sumida River at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, about a 30-second walk from Exit 4 of Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) and roughly a minute from the Toei Asakusa Line exit.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made soba — buckwheat that is stone-ground fresh each morning and hand-cut in house — alongside binchotan-charcoal-grilled yakitori skewers and hand-rolled temaki sushi made with premium nori.

anecdotes.text: The first floor is built around a communal counter cut from a single piece of kusu (camphor) wood from Kyushu, said to be around 200 years old, while the second floor has a U-shaped ash-wood counter; large windows on both floors look straight onto the Sumida River, giving a completely different mood by day and by night.

cultural_comfort.note: It's part of a well-known Japanese restaurant group in the heart of tourist-heavy Asakusa, so some visitor-friendly accommodation is likely, but English menus/staff for this specific branch were not directly confirmed in available sources.

places.name: 丹想庵 健次郎 (Tansouan Kenjiro)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen hand-makes two soba styles fresh daily — a nihachi noodle (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour) and an inaka (country-style) noodle — both of which contain wheat flour by recipe. No dedicated 100%-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option was found in any source, and both noodle types are almost certainly boiled in shared water. This is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-sake restaurant offering tempura and other seasonal side dishes alongside the noodles. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or bonito-free broth option was found in any source, and traditional soba kaeshi/dashi in shops of this style is typically bonito-based. Not confirmed safe or accommodating for vegan diners without direct inquiry.

places.notes: Suzuki apprenticed in soba-making at a temple in Tendo, Yamagata, before opening his own shop behind Sensoji's Kannon Hall in Asakusa's quiet Kannon-ura backstreets. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Trained at a soba-focused Buddhist temple in Tendo City, Yamagata Prefecture — one of northeastern Japan's most celebrated buckwheat-growing regions.
chef_bio.background: Suzuki apprenticed in soba-making at a temple in Tendo, Yamagata, before opening his own shop behind Sensoji's Kannon Hall in Asakusa's quiet Kannon-ura backstreets.
chef_bio.philosophy: Suzuki insists on sourcing the season's best domestic raw buckwheat groats and stone-grinding them by hand through the entire process, believing that freshly milled flour is what gives the noodles their aroma and character.
chef_bio.specialty: Two hand-made soba served fresh daily: a delicate nihachi noodle (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour) prized for its faint aroma and smooth finish, and a heartier inaka (country-style) noodle milled for a deeper buckwheat fragrance and firmer bite.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, Tansouan (丹想庵), was given to Suzuki by the head priest of the soba temple in Tendo, Yamagata where he trained; '丹想' points to sensing one's feelings from the body's core.
The kitchen grinds top-grade domestic buckwheat with a stone mill and hand-cuts two distinct soba styles fresh every day, pairing them with a range of sake and seasonal accompaniments.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Two soba noodle types are hand-cut fresh each day — a nihachi (80% buckwheat/20% wheat) and an inaka (country-style) noodle — both containing wheat by recipe; no gluten-free or 100%-buckwheat noodle was found offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, Japanese-oriented soba-and-sake restaurant in a quiet Asakusa backstreet; no English-language service was confirmed, so a translation app and some patience with a Japanese-only menu are recommended, though phone/Tabelog reservations are available.

places.name: 浅草鳩家 ヴィーガンフルーツサンド (Asakusa Hatoya Vegan Fruits Sand)
places.gf_detail: The bread is a standard soft white bread (soy milk is used to replace dairy/egg, not to make it gluten-free) and no source reviewed confirmed a gluten-free bread option or any allergen/celiac protocol. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free needs without calling ahead to confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Dedicated all-vegan fruit-sandwich specialty stall: no egg or dairy anywhere on the menu, with both the bread and the whipped cream made from soy milk. No dashi, katsuo/bonito, or other hidden animal ingredients are mentioned in any source (the menu is fruit sandwiches only, not a savory/dashi-based menu).
places.neighborhood: 浅草 伝法院通り
places.cuisine: ヴィーガンフルーツサンド・サンドイッチ
places.notes: A small specialty takeout counter on Denpo-in-dori, the lantern-lined shopping street that leads to the gates of Sensoji Temple in Asakusa, dedicated entirely to fruit sandwiches (furutsu sando) made without any egg or dairy. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small specialty takeout counter on Denpo-in-dori, the lantern-lined shopping street that leads to the gates of Sensoji Temple in Asakusa, dedicated entirely to fruit sandwiches (furutsu sando) made without any egg or dairy.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's premise is that a fruit sandwich does not need dairy cream or egg to taste rich: both the bread and the whipped cream are made from soy milk instead, so vegans and people with egg or milk allergies can eat the same treat as everyone else strolling past the temple.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit sandwiches piled generously with seasonal fresh fruit (strawberry, kiwi, mandarin orange, and other seasonal fruit) in soy-milk whipped cream between soft white bread, priced from about 600 yen.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the sandwiches as packed full rather than skimpy — whole large strawberries and kiwi tucked inside as well as arranged on top — calling it 'nothing like the ordinary fruit sandwiches that go easy on the filling.'
The stall sits immediately beside the entrance to Sensoji Temple on Denpo-in-dori (on the left just after passing through Kaminarimon), making it a popular walk-and-eat stop for temple visitors.
The shop has run a plant-based-egg collaboration menu with another maker, described in local food press as a well-known Asakusa vegan fruit-sandwich specialist.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source reviewed confirms a gluten-free bread option; the standard bread appears to be conventional wheat bread with only the dairy/egg removed, not the gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop's stated selling point is that it uses no egg or dairy anywhere in its bread or cream, since it is a dedicated all-vegan fruit-sandwich specialty stall (not a mixed menu alongside animal-based items).

positives.text: Both the bread and the whipped cream are made from soy milk instead of dairy and egg, so the shop is positioned as safe to eat for vegans and for people with egg or milk allergies.
Compared with a standard dairy-cream fruit sandwich, the soy-cream version is described as roughly a third of the calories and near-zero cholesterol.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple walk-up takeout counter directly on Asakusa's most tourist-heavy street (Denpo-in-dori, steps from Sensoji), with a visual fruit-sandwich menu and no reservation or Japanese conversation needed to order.

places.name: しらいわ 浅草店 (Shiraiwa Asakusa-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard Kansai-style okonomiyaki/monjayaki restaurant: the batter is wheat-flour based, and typical seasonings (soy sauce, Worcestershire-style okonomiyaki sauce, dashi) contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found (official brand page, Tabelog, Gnavi, Hot Pepper, review sites). Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on egg-and-flour okonomiyaki/monjayaki batter typically containing bonito-flake (katsuo) dashi, along with pork, seafood, and cheese toppings standard to Kansai-style okonomiyaki. No vegan or vegetarian options, hidden-dashi disclosure, or plant-based menu items were found in any source. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct kitchen confirmation.

chef_bio.specialty: Kansai-style okonomiyaki and monjayaki cooked tableside on the teppan, plus teppanyaki stir-fried dishes.

anecdotes.text: A diner review of the restaurant is titled 'Carbonara monja!', pointing to less-traditional fusion items (e.g. a carbonara-style monjayaki) alongside the standard menu.
A visitor report notes the shop opened in the Asakusa Shin-Nakamise shopping street on April 24, 2023.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 天藤 (Tentō)
places.gf_detail: All tempura is fried in a standard wheat-flour batter and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (traditional Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free batter, tamari substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe option was mentioned in any source — not appropriate for a gluten-free or celiac diner.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on shrimp, kisu (whiting), and other seafood tempura served with a dashi-based tentsuyu (traditionally bonito-based). No vegetarian or vegan tempura set, vegetable-only course, or dashi substitute was identified in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-17:00 (some days may close earlier if sold out); closed Mondays, following day if Monday is a holiday
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1902 (Meiji 35), 天藤 (Tentō) is one of Asakusa's older tendon (tempura rice bowl) specialists, sitting at the corner entrance to Hoppy-dori a few minutes from Asakusa Station. It is now run by the second generation of the founding family. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1902 (Meiji 35), 天藤 (Tentō) is one of Asakusa's older tendon (tempura rice bowl) specialists, sitting at the corner entrance to Hoppy-dori a few minutes from Asakusa Station. It is now run by the second generation of the founding family.
chef_bio.specialty: Edo-style tendon — a bowl topped with shrimp, kisu (Japanese whiting), eggplant, and a scallop-and-squid kakiage, all fried to a distinctively dark color and finished with a rich, assertively savory tentsuyu sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been singled out by actor Shirō Sano as serving one of his favorite ("best") tendon bowls in Tokyo, and has drawn long lines of customers after appearing on television and social media.

cultural_comfort.note: Cash-only, reservation-free counter shop popular with both locals and tourists; expect a wait, Japanese-only menu norms, and no dedicated English support confirmed.

places.name: まるごとVeganダイニング浅草 (Marugoto Vegan Dining Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant states that every dish on the menu is gluten-free (wheat-free) by house policy — this is a whole-kitchen commitment, not a special subset of the menu. The kitchen also excludes the "five pungent" vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, Chinese chives, rakkyo/shallot), alcohol, added sugar, and food additives, and the site says many items are free of Japan's 28 designated priority allergens. No source found described a dedicated fryer, a formal cross-contamination protocol, or whether soy sauce/other condiments used are specifically wheat-free (vs. tamari) — those specifics should be confirmed with staff for celiac-level safety rather than assumed from the general gluten-free branding.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is 100% plant-based: no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, honey, or animal-derived stock/dashi. The owner and all staff are vegan themselves. No dish-level ingredient breakdown (e.g., confirming absence of bonito/katsuo dashi in specific items) was found beyond the blanket "all plant-based, no animal extract broths" claim, but that claim is explicit and restaurant-wide rather than menu-section-specific.
places.hours_raw: Lunch Tue-Sun 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:30); Dinner Wed-Sun 18:00-20:30 (LO 20:00, drink LO 20:30); closed Mondays; Tuesday dinner service closed. Exact days/hours are republished monthly on the official site (calendar pages titled "〇年〇月営業日のご案内"), so the schedule can shift month to month.
places.neighborhood: 花川戸 (Hanakawado), Asakusa, Taitō-ku
places.notes: Koyama Konomi became vegetarian in spring/summer 2017 and vegan later that same summer. The turning point traces back to 2016, when a scene in Osamu Tezuka's manga "Buddha" — in which a rabbit sacrifices its own life for a human — planted the first seed of doubt that eventually l · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Koyama Konomi became vegetarian in spring/summer 2017 and vegan later that same summer. The turning point traces back to 2016, when a scene in Osamu Tezuka's manga "Buddha" — in which a rabbit sacrifices its own life for a human — planted the first seed of doubt that eventually led her toward vegetarianism. Her shift progressed in stages: first for health reasons, then out of concern for animals, and finally for the environment.
chef_bio.philosophy: Koyama believes that how veganism is communicated matters most — rather than lecturing, she wants guests to first have a delicious, enjoyable meal, and let that experience become a gentle entry point for thinking about animals and the planet. Because she and her entire staff are vegan themselves, she says the restaurant can attentively serve both committed vegans and the vegan-curious.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% plant-based, gluten-free lunch plates, salad lunches, curry rice, and gluten-free sweets, all made without the "five pungent" vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, Chinese chives, rakkyo), alcohol, added sugar, or additives.

anecdotes.text: In 2016, seeing a scene in Osamu Tezuka's manga Buddha in which a rabbit gives up its own life for a human planted the seed that eventually led Koyama to vegetarianism, then veganism, the following year.

Koyama's path to veganism moved through stages — first motivated by health, then by concern for animals, and finally by concern for the environment — before she and her staff opened a restaurant where everyone on staff is vegan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: When customers said they're allergic to gluten, staff told them that all ingredients are gluten free, but they can't ensure contamination of seasonings during their production.
Staff made sure customers understood that everything was gluten free, with the exception of the beer listed on the menu.
They serve an array of beverages including coffee, tea, and beer. ... All ingredients are gluten free but they do have a disclaimer about cross contamination.
The entire menu is GF and vegan, and they do provide a disclaimer about cross contamination from the facility, but all food in the cafe is GF.

positives.text: Every dish on the menu is 100% plant-based, gluten-free (wheat-free), free of the five pungent vegetables, alcohol-free, and made without additives or added sugar, using organic or pesticide/chemical-fertilizer-free vegetables; the site says many dishes are also free of Japan's 28 designated allergens.
The owner and the entire staff are vegan themselves, which the restaurant highlights as letting them serve vegan and vegan-curious guests attentively.

cultural_comfort.note: The official site has dedicated English pages (About, Access) covering the concept and how to find the restaurant, but day-to-day details like the menu and monthly hours announcements appear to be Japanese-only.

places.name: 蕎亭 大黒屋 (Kyotei Daikokuya)
places.gf_detail: The noodles themselves are a genuine plus for celiac-aware diners: they are juwari (十割) soba, meaning 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat blended in, milled fresh in-house. However, no source found confirms a wheat-free (tamari) dipping sauce, a dedicated allergen protocol, or a dedicated fryer — and the menu includes tempura, which is normally a wheat-flour batter and a common cross-contact risk in traditional soba kitchens. Because this is a small reservation-only shop, the most reliable approach is to explain the allergy by phone when booking (03-3874-2986) rather than assume safety on arrival.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegetable-based dashi alternative, or vegan-specific accommodation was found in any source reviewed. Traditional Edo-style soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is conventionally made with a katsuobushi (bonito) dashi base, and nothing in the available sources indicates this shop offers an exception, so vegan diners should not assume a plant-based option is available without asking directly.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree: some list Thursday-Saturday 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30), reservation only; others list Tuesday-Saturday 18:00-22:00, or a 12:00-14:00/17:00-22:00 split. Phone: 03-3874-2986, fully reservation-based (完全予約制).

Kanno came to soba relatively late, at 28, first working at a neighborhood soba shop in Ichi · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Kyotei Daikokuya was opened roughly 45 years ago by Shigeo Kanno (菅野成雄, b. 1943 Tokyo) and his wife Masae (雅江) in a quiet backstreet of "oku-Asakusa," behind the main temple-front bustle. Kanno came to soba relatively late, at 28, first working at a neighborhood soba shop in Ichigaya before being drawn to the noodles of Issa-an (一茶庵) in Nishi-Kanda. He trained there for four years and later studied directly under the celebrated soba master Yasuo Katakura (片倉康雄), founder of the influential Issa-an lineage that shaped much of modern hand-made soba in Japan. At 34 he set up his own shop along Asakusa's Yanagi-dori, which grew into today's Kyotei Daikokuya.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kanno mills his own buckwheat fresh in-house using a bank of traditional stone mills (reported as 11 to 14 depending on the source) and hand-kneads 100%-buckwheat (juwari, 十割) noodles with no wheat blended in, chasing what he calls the "ideal soba" through consistency achieved only by repetition and daily self-milling.
chef_bio.specialty: Juwari soba (十割そば) made from heirloom buckwheat varieties sourced directly from growers in Niigata and Nagano, stone-ground fresh each day; also known for its sobagaki (そばがき, warm buckwheat dumplings) eaten with fresh-grated wasabi and soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: Journalist Toshihiko Ueno wrote an entire biography about the shop and the Kanno couple's life devoted to soba, published as 'そば打ち一代 浅草・蕎亭大黒屋見聞録' ("A Life of Soba-Making: Chronicles of Asakusa's Kyotei Daikokuya").
Soba researcher Toranosuke Katayama, founder of the Japan Soba Preservation Society, has singled out Kyotei Daikokuya's soba as among the best in Tokyo.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are 十割 (juwari) — 100% stone-ground buckwheat with no wheat flour mixed in — but no gluten-free protocol, allergen statement, or dedicated fryer was found in any source; tempura is on the menu, which typically involves a wheat-flour batter and shared fryer oil in traditional soba kitchens.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No source found describes the tsuyu (dipping sauce) recipe or confirms a bonito-free dashi option; traditional Edo-style soba tsuyu is generally dashi-based.

positives.text: Buckwheat is sourced directly from named growers in Niigata and Nagano and stone-milled fresh in-house daily using multiple traditional stone mills, rather than using pre-milled commercial flour.

cultural_comfort.note: A fully reservation-only, cash-preferred, Japanese-language soba specialist tucked into a residential backstreet — wonderful for visitors comfortable arranging a phone reservation (or booking through a hotel concierge) and dining without English menus, but not a walk-in-friendly spot.

places.name: うなぎ 色川 (Unagi Irokawa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, substitutions, or dedicated preparation are mentioned anywhere. The signature tare (dipping/basting sauce) is a soy-sauce-based sauce continuously replenished since 1861, which by nature contains wheat; unadon rice bowls, kabayaki, and skewers are all served with this same tare. As a tiny, no-reservation, high-volume lunch counter with a single fixed menu, it is not set up to accommodate special dietary requests.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around eel (unadon/unajū, kabayaki, shirayaki, grilled liver skewers) with no plant-based main dishes. Rice and side items are likely prepared with traditional dashi/tare that can include fish-derived stock. There is no indication of any vegan or vegetarian option.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 Monday–Saturday, closing earlier if the eel sells out (no lunch reservations taken — first-come, first-served with a queue). Closed Sundays, national holidays, and additional irregular days. Older sources mention an evening service from 17:00 until sold out, but this could not be confirmed as currently offered.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Kaminarimon (near Sensō-ji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Irokawa has run as a small, family-operated unagi (freshwater eel) specialty shop near Kaminarimon and Sensō-ji since 1861, making it one of the neighborhood's oldest surviving eel restaurants. It appears on Taito City's official roster of businesses founded during the Edo period · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — founded in 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the year before the Meiji era began
chef_bio.background: Irokawa has run as a small, family-operated unagi (freshwater eel) specialty shop near Kaminarimon and Sensō-ji since 1861, making it one of the neighborhood's oldest surviving eel restaurants. It appears on Taito City's official roster of businesses founded during the Edo period.
chef_bio.philosophy: The eel is grilled to order over charcoal and hand-fanned with a uchiwa while being basted in a tare sauce that has been continuously topped up and passed down since the shop's founding, a technique handed from one generation of cooks to the next rather than a recipe remade from scratch.
chef_bio.specialty: Unadon/unajū (grilled eel over rice), kabayaki and shirayaki (sauced and unseasoned grilled eel), and grilled eel liver (kimo) skewers

anecdotes.text: The shop is listed on Taito City's official registry of 'Edo Sōgyō' (businesses founded in the Edo period) as 'Unagi Irokawa, established 1861 (Bunkyū 1).'
Because the shop takes no lunch reservations and often sells out by early-to-mid afternoon, a queue forms on the street before opening — a long-running fixture of the Asakusa eel scene.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tare (grilling sauce) is a soy-sauce-based sauce that has been continuously topped up since the restaurant opened in 1861, giving the eel its dark, slightly sweet glaze — this traditional style of tare is soy-sauce-based and therefore contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an old-school, no-reservation, cash-and-queue kind of shop with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so first-time foreign visitors should expect a traditional, Japanese-only experience and be ready to wait in line.

places.name: エヴ？（et vous?） (Et vous?)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or celiac protocol is documented anywhere for this restaurant. As a French bistro, sauces (roux-based), bread service, and desserts routinely contain wheat, and nothing found suggests otherwise. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm specifics dish-by-dish; this is not a place with confirmed gluten-free infrastructure.
places.vegan_detail: The house concept emphasizes seasonal vegetables, but this is an omnivorous French bistro (meat and fish feature on the menu), not a vegetarian or vegan kitchen, and no dedicated vegan course or dish list is documented. Classic French technique typically relies on butter, cream, and meat/fish stock, which would need to be avoided or substituted case-by-case. Vegan diners should call ahead and discuss substitutions directly with the chef rather than assuming a ready-made vegan option.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & public holidays: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:00), Dinner 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Mondays and the 1st/3rd Tuesday of each month (following Tuesday if Monday is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: Hanakawado, Asakusa (Taito-ku)
places.notes: et vous? is a small, chef-owned French bistro tucked in a quiet residential backstreet of Hanakawado, just north of Sensō-ji, about a five-minute walk from Asakusa Station. Owner-chef Okabe runs the restaurant around a self-styled concept the shop calls "Life Food" — cooking that · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: et vous? is a small, chef-owned French bistro tucked in a quiet residential backstreet of Hanakawado, just north of Sensō-ji, about a five-minute walk from Asakusa Station. Owner-chef Okabe runs the restaurant around a self-styled concept the shop calls "Life Food" — cooking that is meant to let people across generations sit down and eat the same table of food safely and without worry. To do that, the kitchen leans hard on seasonal vegetables sourced through relationships built directly with trusted producers, rather than through a general wholesale market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Okabe's cooking is described as not bound strictly by classical French technique — flavors, cooking methods and ingredient pairings are combined more freely, guided by the chef's own sensibility, with the aim of food that is gentle on the body ("eat and become beautiful/healthy") rather than rich and heavy in the classic bistro style.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal-vegetable-forward French course cuisine — the restaurant's own "Life Food" courses built around a rotating lineup of vegetables at their peak, plus an original "Edo cuisine" line that folds Edo-period Tokyo culinary ideas into a French frame.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's own description frames it as a hidden, adult-feeling retreat in a residential pocket beyond Sensō-ji — reached down a quiet backstreet rather than a main road.
The "Life Food" concept is explained as researching and designing food so that three generations of one family could safely share the same table.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is a French bistro built around meat- and fish-based courses with a vegetable-forward philosophy layered on top, not a vegetarian or vegan kitchen — classic French technique (butter, cream, meat/fish stock) should be assumed in dishes not specifically flagged as plant-based.

positives.text: The kitchen's stated concept centers on sourcing fresh, seasonal vegetables directly through relationships with trusted producers, and building courses around them.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations run through phone or Japanese booking platforms (Ikyu, Gnavi, OZmall) with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so a translation app or Japanese-speaking companion will help.

places.name: 穴子料理と地酒 浅草 川井 (Anago Ryōri to Jizake Asakusa Kawai (Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional anago (conger eel) specialty restaurant, not a gluten-free establishment. Its menu explicitly includes tempura and fried preparations of anago (wheat-flour batter), and conventional anago kabayaki/glazing sauces in Japanese cuisine are soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat) — no dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or celiac protocol was found in any source consulted. Even the signature sashimi may be served with soy-based condiments. Diners with celiac disease should not treat this restaurant as safe without directly confirming ingredients with staff in Japanese, and should expect wheat exposure to be likely across most of the menu.
places.vegan_detail: This is a specialty seafood restaurant centered entirely on conger eel, and it also serves Bungo beef and Bungo chicken. Every dish revolves around fish or meat; there is no vegan or vegetarian menu, no plant-based option, and no indication that any sauces, dashi, or glazes are prepared without fish or animal stock. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Lunch (Wed–Sun & holidays): 12:00–14:30 (L.O. 14:00). Dinner (Tue–Sun & holidays): 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Mondays.

Its proprietor had spent years working as a sushi chef in Ōita, Kyūshū, before becoming captivated by anago (conger eel) and building a restaurant devoted to it. The shop later changed its name  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Ōita, Kyūshū (proprietor's background)
chef_bio.background: Opened in 1989 as あな太朗 (Anatarou) on a quiet side street off Kokusai-dori in Asakusa. Its proprietor had spent years working as a sushi chef in Ōita, Kyūshū, before becoming captivated by anago (conger eel) and building a restaurant devoted to it. The shop later changed its name to 穴子料理と地酒 浅草 川井 (Asakusa Kawai), continuing under the same conger-eel-and-local-sake concept.
chef_bio.philosophy: Source fresh conger eel daily — including wild anago from the Bungo Strait between Kyūshū and Shikoku — and show it off every way possible: raw as sashimi, slow-glazed kabayaki-style in a proprietary sauce simmered about 45 minutes, plus Kyūshū specialties like Bungo beef and Bungo chicken from the owner's home region grilled on hot stones.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago sashimi (conger eel sashimi) — prized for its crunchy texture and the eel's own natural sweetness

anecdotes.text: The restaurant traces back to 1989, when it opened as あな太朗 (Anatarou); its proprietor had spent years as a sushi chef in Ōita, Kyūshū, before conger eel captivated him enough to build a restaurant around it.
A Nikkei feature on the shop highlighted three decades of devotion to conger eel, singling out the sashimi — made with wild anago from the Bungo Strait — as its proudest dish.

Beyond sashimi, the kitchen simmers fresh conger eel daily in a house sauce for about 45 minutes for a fragrant, glazed kabayaki-style preparation, and also grills Bungo beef and Bungo chicken from Ōita on hot stones.
The shop later changed its name from あな太朗 (Anatarou) to 穴子料理と地酒 浅草 川井, keeping the same anago-and-local-sake concept.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu explicitly includes tempura and fried preparations of anago alongside sashimi and grilled styles, indicating wheat-flour batter is used in the same kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Japanese anago-and-sake restaurant with no evidence of an English menu, English-speaking staff, or English-language web presence; reservations and inquiries appear to be handled by phone in Japanese.

places.name: 甘味処 彩夏 (Kanmidokoro Saika)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergen statement, or cross-contamination protocol was found. That said, the shop's core repertoire is built on naturally wheat-free ingredients — kanten (agar) for anmitsu/mitsumame, azuki bean paste, matcha, and warabimochi (made from bracken/potato starch, not wheat flour) — so several base items are plausibly gluten-free by composition. However, toppings (cookies, wafers, sauces) and shared prep surfaces are unconfirmed, and no soy-sauce or dashi use is documented (it's a sweets shop, not a savory kitchen). Celiac diners should ask staff directly about specific ingredients and any shared equipment before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or confirmation of dashi/bonito-free prep was found (not typically relevant for a sweets shop). Kanten-based anmitsu/mitsumame and kuromitsu (brown-sugar syrup) are traditionally plant-based, and plain shaved ice with fruit syrup can likely be made vegan, but many parfait and sundae items include dairy ice cream, soft serve, or condensed milk, and there's no confirmed vegan-labeled option. Vegan diners should ask staff to point out or adapt dairy-free items.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–19:00 daily, no fixed closed day (some sources note possible irregular closures)
places.neighborhood: 浅草 仲見世通り (Asakusa, just off Nakamise-dori, near Sensō-ji)
places.cuisine: 和菓子・甘味処・かき氷
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kanmidokoro Saika is an independent traditional Japanese sweets (kanmi) shop near Sensō-ji and Nakamise-dori, serving classic kanmi alongside its well-known shaved ice. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened July 4, 2012, tucked in a small alley just off Nakamise-dori, about a two-minute walk from Tokyo Metro Ginza Line Asakusa Station Exit 6.
chef_bio.background: Kanmidokoro Saika is an independent traditional Japanese sweets (kanmi) shop near Sensō-ji and Nakamise-dori, serving classic kanmi alongside its well-known shaved ice.
chef_bio.philosophy: Close attention to ice quality and shaving technique, paired with housemade matcha jelly and syrups made from carefully sourced Japanese ingredients, across a menu of 80+ sweets.
chef_bio.specialty: Fluffy, 'powder-snow' style shaved ice (kakigori) — the shop shaves unusually hard ice blocks with a very sharp blade to get an ultra-fine, light texture said to feel barely cold on the first bite. Also known for anmitsu, mitsumame, oshiruko, parfaits and warabimochi made with Hokkaido azuki, Uji matcha and Okinawan brown sugar.

anecdotes.text: A shop-feature interview highlights Saika's colorful 'choco milk' shaved ice, decorated pop-style with sprinkles, as a signature seasonal offering that photographs well and delights first-time visitors.

positives.text: The core menu leans on agar-based anmitsu/mitsumame, azuki bean paste, matcha and warabimochi (bracken-starch, not wheat) — ingredients that are naturally wheat-free and, in some preparations, dairy-free — giving gluten-free and vegan-leaning diners a few plausible base choices to build from, even though no formal allergy protocol is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits in one of Tokyo's most tourist-trafficked pockets (just off Nakamise-dori near Sensō-ji), so basic English or pointing at photos will likely get by, but no English menu or English-speaking staff is confirmed from available sources.

places.name: 悦納 (injoy) (Etsunō (Injoy))
places.gf_detail: No source found describes a dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu labeling. The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by default. Soy sauce/tamari wheat content and cross-contamination handling were not documented in any source reviewed — celiac or strict-GF diners should call ahead and confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Consistently described across multiple independent sources as a fully vegan shōjin-ryori kitchen: no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs, and no "five pungent" alliums (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion) per Buddhist dietary tradition. Broths appear to be vegetable-based rather than fish/bonito-based, per reviewer commentary on the noodle dishes. No katsuo/dashi exceptions were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-20:30 (L.O. 19:30), Monday and Wednesday-Sunday; closed Tuesdays. Lunch is walk-in; dinner is reservation-only, requested by 3:00pm the previous day.
places.neighborhood: Taito-ku, Asakusa (Hanakawado), directly by Asakusa Station
places.notes: No source found describes a dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu labeling. The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by defaul

chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames shōjin ryori as drawing out the umami, nutritional value and beauty already present in each plant-based ingredient, with a stated ethos that nothing should be wasted — down to a single grain of rice or drop of juice.
chef_bio.specialty: Oriental vegan shōjin ryori — recurring signature dishes mentioned across reviews include dandan (tantan) noodles, a vegetable-broth noodle soup, ramen, bibimbap, and a lion's mane mushroom dish

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has hosted seasonal, hands-on events such as an Oriental-vegan chimaki (rice dumpling) making experience, and prepared a fully vegan osechi (Japanese New Year) box for 2023.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is described as excluding all animal-derived ingredients as well as the Buddhist "five pungent roots" (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion), consistent with strict shōjin-style plant-based cooking.

positives.text: Menu and site descriptions consistently state the food uses no animal products at all (fully vegan shōjin ryori), not just a vegetarian subset of dishes.
A reviewer noted the noodle-dish broth was vegetable-only yet tasted deeply umami, suggesting deliberate care in building flavor without fish-based dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations (required for dinner) are made by phone, and no confirmed English menu or English-speaking-staff claim was found, though the restaurant is covered favorably by several English-language vegan/travel sites.

places.name: 食事処 酒肴 浅草 水口 (Shokujidokoro Sakana Asakusa Mizuguchi (Mizu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. The menu is built around breaded/fried items (mixed fry, korokke), sauced pork and hamburg-steak dishes, and standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, so wheat exposure should be assumed throughout the kitchen. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific dishes, marking, or accommodations were found in any source. The menu is heavily meat- and fish-based (pork specialties, hamburg steak, fried items, seafood), and traditional Japanese dashi (typically katsuobushi/bonito-based) should be assumed in soups and simmered dishes unless the restaurant confirms otherwise. Not recommended as a vegan option based on available information.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草駅周辺）
places.cuisine: 定食・いり豚・昭和食堂
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mizuguchi began as a plain teishoku-ya serving the workers and performers of Asakusa Rokku in the postwar years. It has grown into a sprawling two-floor shokudō-and-izakaya known for its huge, old-fashioned menu spanning Japanese, Western and Chinese-style home cooking — everythi · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: family-run Showa-era teishoku-ya (set-meal diner) and neighborhood eatery/izakaya
chef_bio.origin: Opened in 1950 (Shōwa 25) in Asakusa Rokku, Asakusa's old entertainment district, and has stayed in the same neighborhood ever since — marking roughly 75 years in business as of the mid-2020s.
chef_bio.background: Mizuguchi began as a plain teishoku-ya serving the workers and performers of Asakusa Rokku in the postwar years. It has grown into a sprawling two-floor shokudō-and-izakaya known for its huge, old-fashioned menu spanning Japanese, Western and Chinese-style home cooking — everything from fried horse mackerel and pork sauté to omurice, ginger pork, mapo tofu and hand-mixed hamburg steak — served in a boisterous, nostalgic Shōwa-retro dining room.
chef_bio.philosophy: A no-frills, high-volume local shokudō where regulars — from neighborhood workers to solo diners stopping in for a drink — are as much a part of the atmosphere as the food.
chef_bio.specialty: Iri-buta — pork and onion stir-fried in Mizuguchi's own curry-tinged, sweet-savory sauce, reportedly created together with one of the shop's cooks around the time of the Tokyo air raids of 1945 and still the table everyone orders.

anecdotes.text: Iri-buta is said to have existed in some form before the war and to have been worked out with one of the shop's cooks around the time of the 1945 Tokyo air raids.
The comedy duo Naitsu (ナイツ), who often riff on Asakusa in their act, are described as regulars who frequent the shop.
Local write-ups describe the restaurant as a 100-plus-item menu institution beloved by Asakusa locals, with the Shōwa-retro dining room drawing return visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: A very local, Japanese-language diner with a huge handwritten-style menu; no English menu or allergy accommodations were found in any source, so visitors comfortable navigating in Japanese (or with a translation app) will have the easiest time.

places.name: 天麩羅 中清 (Tempura Nakasei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, batter substitution, or dedicated frying process was found in any source. Traditional Edo-mae tempura batter is wheat-flour based, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce that accompanies it is standard dashi-and-soy-sauce (both typically containing wheat: soy sauce as an ingredient, bonito/kelp dashi as a shared base). No information confirms a dedicated gluten-free fryer or ingredient list, so this should be assumed unsafe for celiac diners unless confirmed directly with the restaurant by phone.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetable-only or vegan course was found in any source. The restaurant's identity and signature dish (Raijin-age) are built around shrimp and shellfish, and the standard tentsuyu dipping sauce relies on bonito (katsuo) dashi, which is not vegan. No plant-based menu or hidden-dashi accommodation is documented, so this should be treated as unsuitable for vegan diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon/Thu/Fri): Lunch 11:30–14:30, Dinner 17:00–21:00. Weekends & public holidays: 11:30–20:00. Closed every Tuesday and Wednesday, plus December 31–January 1.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 — 5-minute walk from Sensō-ji (Asakusa)
places.notes: An 1870-founded Edo-mae tempura house near Sensō-ji, run by descendants of its samurai-turned-restaurateur founder, known for its quiet courtyard-pond setting, sukiya-style private tatami rooms, and a kakiage recipe kept within the family for generations. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Family-run since the Meiji era, now several generations on
chef_bio.origin: Founded in the last years of the Edo period as a tempura food stall on Hirokoji street by Nakagawa Tetsuzō, a former samurai from Suruga who gave up his rank to start a business in Edo. In 1870 (Meiji 3) the family opened a permanent shop in front of the old Asakusa Public Hall, on the same ground it occupies today. The restaurant's name combines "中" from the Nakagawa family name with "清" from Seigorō, the second-generation owner.
chef_bio.background: An 1870-founded Edo-mae tempura house near Sensō-ji, run by descendants of its samurai-turned-restaurateur founder, known for its quiet courtyard-pond setting, sukiya-style private tatami rooms, and a kakiage recipe kept within the family for generations.
chef_bio.philosophy: A multi-generation commitment to Edo-mae tempura as it was cooked in old Tokyo — thick-battered, sesame-oil-fried, whole ingredients rather than fillets — with the signature kakiage recipe treated as a piece of family craft passed one generation to the next.
chef_bio.specialty: Raijin-age (雷神揚げ) — an oversized kakiage of shiba shrimp and akoyagi (pen shell) adductor muscle, fried crisp in sesame oil using a technique handed down within the family since the first generation. The name was coined by a regular customer, French-literature scholar Yutaka Tatsuno, who thought the finished kakiage resembled the thunder drums carried by the Raijin (Thunder God) statue at Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate.

anecdotes.text: The shop appears repeatedly in the writings of novelist Nagai Kafū, and novelist Ikenami Shōtarō was also a regular visitor.
Writer Kubo Mantarō was a childhood friend of the third-generation owner, Tetsutarō, and used to play with him in the restaurant's courtyard.
The restaurant was used as a filming location for the TV drama 'Kurosagi'.

The 'Raijin-age' kakiage — shiba shrimp and akoyagi adductor fried in sesame oil — is described as a technique 'passed down to a single successor per generation' (一子相伝), originally devised by a former-samurai founder.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains an English-language section of its official site and is bookable through English-facing reservation platforms (e.g. Autoreserve, Last Minute Japan), but seating is private-room, course-only and reservation is effectively required, so a visitor with zero Japanese should book ahead through one of those English channels rather than walk in.

places.name: 浅草うどん (Asakusa Udon)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-flour udon shop — the noodles are made from wheat, so there is no confirmed gluten-free noodle option. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen-free process has been documented; tempura toppings appear to be fried to order in a shared fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, on-site confirmation, and even then the base noodle itself remains a wheat product.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, broth, or preparation has been documented. Traditional Japanese udon tsuyu is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and no source confirms an exception at this shop, so vegan diners should ask staff directly about the broth base before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-21:00 (reported at opening, October 2025)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chiba began an apprenticeship as an itamae (traditional Japanese chef) at age 18 and trained for five years. At 24 he struck out on his own, opening a dining bar, and went on to run other food-and-drink businesses. In August of a recent year he founded a company called Asakusa-ch · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Grew up locally in Asakusa
chef_bio.background: Chiba began an apprenticeship as an itamae (traditional Japanese chef) at age 18 and trained for five years. At 24 he struck out on his own, opening a dining bar, and went on to run other food-and-drink businesses. In August of a recent year he founded a company called Asakusa-cho ("Asakusa Town"), deliberately registering it on the 8th of the month for its association with sue-hirogari, a fan-shaped character read as a symbol of ever-widening prosperity. Asakusa Udon, opened near the Hanayashiki amusement park in October 2025, is the company's flagship shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: "The town raises the people, and the people raise the town" (街が人を育み、人が街を育む) — the stated motto behind Chiba's company and this shop.
chef_bio.specialty: Kake udon and bukkake udon (served hot, either in broth or dipping-style), to which diners add freshly fried tempura (such as kara-age chicken or shrimp) and toppings like a seasoned egg yolk or beef and shiitake mushroom.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a 43-square-meter space near Asakusa Hanayashiki, with 10 standing counter spots on the ground floor and 28 table seats upstairs; it opens at 7am to serve breakfast to guests of nearby hotels and stays open into the evening for casual after-work drinking.
Chiba named his holding company Asakusa-cho and incorporated it on August 8th specifically for the auspicious, ever-widening-fortune connotation of the number 8 (sue-hirogari) in Japanese culture.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits in the heavily tourist-trafficked strip near Sensoji and Hanayashiki and markets breakfast service to nearby hotel guests, but no English menu or multilingual staff have been confirmed in available sources.

places.name: 蕎上人（駒形蕎上人） (Sobashonin (Komagata Sobashonin))
places.gf_detail: Sources confirm the buckwheat itself is sourced 100% domestically, from high-elevation Aizu (Fukushima) fields, and is stone-milled in-house, but none specify the buckwheat-to-wheat ratio of the actual noodles (i.e., whether they are juwari/100%-buckwheat or a standard nihachi blend that uses wheat as a binder). The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is the traditional kaeshi-and-bonito-dashi type; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or dedicated fryer was mentioned in any source. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the flour blend and sauce ingredients before ordering — this shop does not market itself as gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The broths are built on traditional katsuobushi (bonito) dashi and kaeshi, and signature dishes include duck (鴨南そば). No vegetarian or vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or meat-free tsuyu option was mentioned in any available source — standard dishes here are not vegan-safe.
places.hours_raw: Lunch approx. 11:30-14:00/14:30, Dinner approx. 17:00-20:00/20:30 (exact close times vary slightly between sources); closed Mondays and the 2nd & 4th Sunday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Komagata, near Komagata Bridge along the Sumida Ri
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1989 as "Asakusa Issa-an," the shop was renamed "Komagata Sobashonin" in October 1997. The owner, Hiranuma Takayuki, trained under Katakura Yasuo, a noted postwar soba craftsman credited with establishing hand-cut, thin-sliced specialty soba as a standard of the trade.  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: owner/soba craftsman: Hiranuma Takayuki (name spelling per secondary sources; not independently confirmed via primary site)
chef_bio.background: Opened in 1989 as "Asakusa Issa-an," the shop was renamed "Komagata Sobashonin" in October 1997. The owner, Hiranuma Takayuki, trained under Katakura Yasuo, a noted postwar soba craftsman credited with establishing hand-cut, thin-sliced specialty soba as a standard of the trade. The shop also runs its own soba- and udon-making school, whose 800-plus graduates have gone on to open their own shops.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows three principles for good soba: freshly milled flour, freshly made noodles, freshly boiled water. Buckwheat is sourced from high-elevation (700m+) fields in the Aizu region of Fukushima Prefecture and stone-milled in-house on five large mills, ground slowly to preserve aroma and flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Five-color soba (五色そば) — a tasting set of five hand-cut noodle varieties (plain/seiro, rustic/inaka, poppy-seed/keshi-kiri, lemon/lemon-kiri, and tea/cha-kiri) — and duck nanban soba (鴨南そば) served in a yuzu-scented duck broth.

anecdotes.text: About ten rotating varieties of hand-cut soba are made daily, including seiro, inaka (rustic), lemon-kiri, keshi-kiri (poppy seed), shiso-kiri, and goma-kiri (sesame).
The duck broth in the kamo-nanban soba carries a yuzu fragrance, balancing the sweetness of long green onion and duck against a carefully judged kaeshi-and-dashi blend.
Buckwheat flour comes from Aizu, Fukushima, from fields above 700m elevation, and is stone-ground in-house.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba is served with a traditional kaeshi (soy-sauce-based tare) and bonito dashi; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative was mentioned in any available source.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood soba craftsman's shop; no English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found in any source, so a few basic Japanese food words will help when ordering.

places.name: ぱいち (Paichi)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-heavy traditional yōshoku menu: the signature beef stew is built on a roux-thickened demi-glace sauce simmered for days, and other specialties (menchi-katsu, tonkatsu-style cutlets, ebi-fry, katsu sando) are all breaded in wheat panko and deep-fried. No source mentions a gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce option. Should be treated as unsafe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every dish identified across sources is meat- or dairy-centered — beef stew, tongue stew, menchi-katsu, tonkatsu, gratin — and no plant-based dish, dashi substitution, or vegetarian option was mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00 (L.O.), 17:00–20:00 (L.O.); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ぱいち has been run by the same family for three generations since 1936, tucked in a backstreet corner one block north of Kaminarimon-dōri. It began life as a small bar rather than a restaurant. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in Shōwa 11 (1936) as a drinking establishment in Asakusa's entertainment district; later became a family-run yōshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant, now in its third generation.
chef_bio.background: ぱいち has been run by the same family for three generations since 1936, tucked in a backstreet corner one block north of Kaminarimon-dōri. It began life as a small bar rather than a restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: Even as the storefront signage and neighborhood around it have changed over the decades, the family has kept the flavor and spirit of the original shop largely unchanged, serving affordable, nostalgic Shōwa-era Western food to neighborhood regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef stew (ビーフシチュー) served bubbling in an iron pot: the demi-glace sauce is specially researched and simmered for about 4 days, and the beef itself is slow-simmered for 7-8 hours for a rich but light, soup-like sauce. Also known for tongue stew (タンシチュー), menchi-katsu, and katsu sando.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name is said to come from 一杯 (ippai, "one drink") — a nod to its origins as a bar — with the word playfully reversed to create ぱいち (Paichi).
The sign advertising the restaurant's "famous beef stew" was hand-written by the current (third-generation) owner's uncle.
The restaurant has long been popular with entertainment-industry regulars connected to Asakusa's theater and performance scene, drawn in by the beef stew.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school neighborhood eatery with an all-Japanese menu and no evidence of English signage, English menu, or foreign-language support — comfortable for Japanese speakers but not set up for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.

places.name: 高級伊奈利寿司専門店 福寿家 (Fukujuya (Kokyu Inari-zushi Senmon-ten Fukuj)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented anywhere for this shop. The aburaage tofu pouches are simmered in a housemade broth, and traditional inari sushi of this kind is typically made with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce; this has not been confirmed one way or the other for Fukujuya specifically, so it should not be assumed gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The core ingredients — aburaage tofu pouches, vinegared rice, and vegetable fillings such as sweet potato, lotus root, and pickled ginger — are plant-based, and no meat or seafood toppings appear in any menu description found. However, the housemade broth used to simmer the tofu pouches is not documented as dashi-free; if it uses bonito (katsuo) dashi, as is traditional for many inari sushi shops, it would not be strictly vegan. This has not been confirmed by the shop itself, so vegan diners should ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00, or until sold out; closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Hanakawado)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in Taishō 11 (1922) in Asakusa by founder Mine Yasu, who coined the shop's own term "伊奈利寿司" (Inari-zushi, written with a distinctive kanji) for its inside-out style of inari sushi. The shop continued through a 2nd and 3rd generation after the war, but closed in 2009 when  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founded by Mine Yasu (峰ヤス), 1st generation, 1922
Currently run by the 4th-generation owner (name not publicly documented)
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa (Hanakawado), Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in Taishō 11 (1922) in Asakusa by founder Mine Yasu, who coined the shop's own term "伊奈利寿司" (Inari-zushi, written with a distinctive kanji) for its inside-out style of inari sushi. The shop continued through a 2nd and 3rd generation after the war, but closed in 2009 when the aging owners could no longer continue. After about a decade dormant, the 4th generation revived it in 2020 through a crowdfunding campaign aimed at keeping the 100-year-old business alive, reopening in Hanakawado, Asakusa with a refreshed look while keeping the original recipe and technique.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder's technique of turning the fried-tofu pouch inside-out before wrapping it around the rice is meant to evoke "turning things around" (裏を返す), a play on words expressing the wish that customers will turn around and come back again.
chef_bio.specialty: Inari sushi ("伊奈利寿司") made by turning the aburaage tofu pouch inside-out to wrap vinegared rice, plus original varieties such as 塩伊奈利 (salt-seasoned inari, using a secret salt seasoning instead of the traditional simmered broth) and sweet-potato, lotus-root, and pickled-ginger inari.

anecdotes.text: The shop closed for roughly a decade starting in 2009 because the aging 2nd/3rd-generation owners could no longer continue it, and was brought back in 2020 through a crowdfunding project by the 4th generation determined to preserve the shop's 100-year history.

A visiting food blogger who expected a plain, old-fashioned inari sushi was surprised to find lotus-root and pickled-ginger varieties unlike anything they had tried, calling it proof of "the strength of a specialty shop."
The aburaage is sourced directly from specialty tofu producers in Niigata and Saitama, and the rice from Miyagi, then simmered in a housemade broth to give the pouches a juicy, umami-rich flavor.

positives.text: Every publicly described variety of the shop's inari sushi (classic broth-simmered, salt-seasoned, sweet potato, lotus root, pickled ginger) is built from tofu, rice, and vegetables, with no meat or seafood toppings mentioned in any source reviewed.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering, phone reservations, and available signage/menu information found online are all in Japanese; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found in any source.

places.name: かき氷処 浅草氷室堂 浅草本店 (Asakusa Himurodo (Kakigori Shop) – Asakusa M)
places.gf_detail: No source discusses gluten-free preparation directly. The base shaved ice and homemade fruit syrups are plausibly wheat-free, but no source confirms whether any syrup or topping contains wheat-derived ingredients, and toppings like the mitarashi-dango version use a soy-sauce-based glaze — soy sauce in Japan is very often wheat-based unless specified otherwise, and this was not confirmed either way here. No information on shared equipment or cross-contamination practices was found. Ask staff directly about specific menu items before ordering if celiac.
places.vegan_detail: No source specifically addresses vegan suitability. Fruit-based shaved ice syrups are often plant-based, but no source confirms the absence of dairy (e.g., condensed milk) in the base syrups or toppings, and menu items such as the mitarashi-dango kakigori use a glaze whose exact ingredients (potential mirin/dashi) are unconfirmed. Ask staff about specific items.

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Asakusa Himurodo's Asakusa main branch opened in May 2023 in the Crest Asakusa building, a short walk from Asakusa Station. It is a dedicated kakigori (shaved ice) shop built around ice quality: the shop uses carefully selected, high-purity ice that is prized for melting almost i · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Asakusa Himurodo's Asakusa main branch opened in May 2023 in the Crest Asakusa building, a short walk from Asakusa Station. It is a dedicated kakigori (shaved ice) shop built around ice quality: the shop uses carefully selected, high-purity ice that is prized for melting almost instantly, giving the dessert what fans describe as a roughly "three-minute" window of peak texture before it's meant to be eaten.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on the purity of the ice itself and on natural, additive-free syrups made in-house, with menus that shift with the seasons to showcase fruit at its best.
chef_bio.specialty: Shaved ice topped with handmade syrups made without artificial colors, flavors, or sweeteners, built around seasonal fruit. Standouts include the fresh-strawberry kakigori and an Asakusa-location-exclusive ripe sweet potato (mitsuimo) kakigori, plus a version topped with real mitarashi dango.

anecdotes.text: The shop's ice is so fine and high-purity that it is said to have only about a three-minute window before it loses its ideal texture, a point of pride highlighted in visitor write-ups.
The Asakusa main store has a location-exclusive menu item, a fully ripened sweet potato (mitsuimo) shaved ice, alongside standards like fresh strawberry and a mitarashi-dango-topped version.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking staff was confirmed in any source, though the shop sits in heavily-touristed Asakusa where some picture-menu/gesture communication is typical.

places.name: 浅草 江戸前 三松寿司 (Asakusa Edomae Mimatsu Sushi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, ingredient substitutions, or allergy protocol were mentioned in any source found. As a traditional Edomae sushi counter, nigiri here would be finished with standard shoyu (which contains wheat) and the anago is glazed with a soy-based nitsume sauce (also typically wheat-containing); there is no indication of a gluten-free soy sauce alternative or a dedicated allergy-safe process. This is not a restaurant that can be recommended as safe for celiac diners without calling ahead and confirming directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu items were found in any source. The menu is built entirely around raw and simmered fish/shellfish (kohada, saba, anago, and other hikarimono); traditional Edomae shari (vinegared sushi rice) and sauces of this style typically involve dashi that often contains bonito (katsuobushi). Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-22:00 weekdays (Mon, Tue, Fri); Sat/Sun/Holidays 12:00-15:00 and 17:00-21:00; last order roughly 30 minutes before close. Closed Wednesday and Thursday per Tabelog, with additional irregular closures noted — some other listings show only Wednesday as the regular closed day.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa)
places.cuisine: 江戸前寿司・穴子・光りもの
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Edomae sushi counter near Sensōji that has operated continuously since 1958 (Shōwa 33). Multiple independent write-ups describe it today as carried on by a mother-and-daughter team, with a female sushi chef at the counter — unusual in a trade historically dominated by men · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small Edomae sushi counter near Sensōji that has operated continuously since 1958 (Shōwa 33). Multiple independent write-ups describe it today as carried on by a mother-and-daughter team, with a female sushi chef at the counter — unusual in a trade historically dominated by men — which several visitors note makes the small room feel easy for solo women diners.
chef_bio.specialty: Hikarimono (silver-skinned fish such as kohada and saba) and anago (simmered conger eel), the shop's two signature items

anecdotes.text: Founded in Shōwa 33 (1958), the shop is described as an established name now kept going by a mother-and-daughter team.
Its two 'flagship' items are hikarimono and anago, praised for soft, melt-in-the-mouth texture.
A blog post highlights the restaurant specifically for having a female sushi chef making the nigiri.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional 16-seat neighborhood sushi counter with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, though several reviewers note the atmosphere is welcoming to solo diners, including women.

places.name: ドーター ブティック トーキョー (Daughter Boutique Tokyo)
places.gf_detail: The shop describes itself as a dedicated 100% plant-based, gluten-free workshop: no wheat flour, dairy, or egg is used in any product (rice flour and other gluten-free flours are the base), and the company states there is no cross-contamination risk because only gluten-free, plant-based ingredients enter the kitchen. No specific information was found on soy sauce or dashi in the savory cookie varieties (e.g., aonori/vegan-cheese cookies), so those with additional allergies should ask staff about individual items.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan bakery: no dairy, eggs, honey, or other animal products in any product; sweetened with beet sugar and maple syrup rather than refined white sugar. As an all-plant kitchen there is no meat/fish/dashi cross-contact concern, though specific ingredient lists for hidden dashi (relevant mainly to savory dishes, not applicable here) were not separately confirmed.
places.neighborhood: Matsugaya, Taito-ku (Kappabashi/Iriya area, just south of As
places.notes: DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE was founded in 2014 as a vegan sweets brand, initially building a following through pop-up events, department-store fairs, and farmers markets before opening its first standalone shop, DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE TOKYO, in Matsugaya, Taito-ku in August 2018, where café-st · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: representative director, Daughter Planning Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: Started by Horikawa after she developed her own skin sensitivity linked to certain foods and began baking gentler sweets for herself before turning it into a business.
chef_bio.background: DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE was founded in 2014 as a vegan sweets brand, initially building a following through pop-up events, department-store fairs, and farmers markets before opening its first standalone shop, DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE TOKYO, in Matsugaya, Taito-ku in August 2018, where café-style items were added alongside its baked-goods lineup.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sweets that are kind to the heart and body, but still 'delicious and fun' — made without wheat, dairy, or egg, using rice flour and other gluten-free flours, sweetened with beet sugar and maple syrup rather than refined white sugar, and without chemical synthetic additives.
chef_bio.specialty: An extensive lineup (roughly 60 varieties) of vegan, gluten-free cookies — including savory versions with vegan cheese or aonori — plus muffins, tarts, brownies, pound cakes, and gift cookie tins such as a yakuzen (herbal) cookie tin and a vegan butter cookie tin.

anecdotes.text: Founder Kumiko Horikawa developed her recipes after struggling with skin sensitivity linked to certain foods, turning her personal search for gentler sweets into the DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE brand, founded in 2014.

After years of selling through pop-up events, department-store fairs, and farmers markets, DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE TOKYO opened its first standalone shop in Matsugaya, Taito-ku in August 2018, adding café-style items to its baked-goods lineup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Operates as a dedicated plant-based, gluten-free workshop — only gluten-free and plant-based ingredients are used in production, so the company states there is no cross-contamination concern.

The FindMeGlutenFree celiac review that surfaced under Daughter Boutique searches actually describes a different, explicitly non-dedicated restaurant: 'even though they are not dedicated for Gluten Free they have many GF option menu and as the owners daughter is gluten free also.' It is a mismatch and provides NO celiac-grade corroboration for Daughter Boutique.

Japanese consumer-affairs guidance warns that rice-flour (米粉) products may contain wheat-derived ingredients and that allergy accidents have occurred; combined with no mandatory Japanese cross-contamination labeling, an all-GF bakery's 'no cross-contamination' claim is not self-verifying without third-party non-gluten (<20ppm) testing.

Independent write-ups indicate the brand currently operates largely as an online shop / OEM / wholesale workshop with no vetted dine-in retail presence, limiting independent celiac on-site verification.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch summary of jewelfruits.com / prtimes.jp opening announcements

vegan_cross_contact.text: As a fully plant-based bakery, no animal-derived ingredients (dairy, egg, honey) are used anywhere in the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: For in-person pickup, customers are advised to reserve items by phone or email at least a few days in advance rather than simply walking in.

positives.text: Every item is made without wheat, dairy, or egg, using natural sweeteners like beet sugar and maple syrup instead of refined white sugar or artificial sweeteners, and without chemical synthetic additives.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering (phone or email) and packaging are Japanese-first with no confirmed English menu or staff, though the shop is referenced in several English-language gluten-free Tokyo travel guides.

places.name: 駒形どぜう 本店 (Komagata Dozeu (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found in any available source. The signature dojō-nabe and related dishes are cooked in a shōyu-based broth; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed from wheat, so the broth and most seasoned dishes should be assumed to contain gluten. No online allergy menu exists (Japanese-language only, walk-in service), so anything beyond plain grilled items would need in-person confirmation with staff, which is not guaranteed to be in English. Not recommended for celiac diners without that direct, cautious confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: This is a single-protein specialty restaurant built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and related fish dishes cooked in fish/animal-based broth; no vegetarian or vegan dish was found in any menu source. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Komagata, Taitō-ku (Asakusa)
places.notes: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisi

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Edo/Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisine ever since and still operates from a building in the Edo-period 'dashigeta-zukuri' merchant-house style.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving Edo-period dining customs and atmosphere alongside the food itself — low tatami seating, unchanged recipes, and a building whose upper floor famously has no street-facing windows.
chef_bio.specialty: Dojō-nabe — whole pond loach simmered tableside in a shallow iron pot in a shōyu-based broth, the dish the restaurant is named for.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's distinctive four-character spelling 'どぜう' (instead of the standard five-character 'どぢやう') dates to a 1806 fire that destroyed the original shop. Considered the five-character spelling to be bad luck, the founder had a well-known signboard calligrapher write it as four characters instead; the shop's renewed popularity led other loach restaurants across Edo to copy the spelling.

During the Edo period the shogunate discouraged eating four-legged animals, so loach became an important source of animal protein for ordinary townspeople — a role the restaurant is remembered for supporting.

The main hall keeps its Edo-era merchant-house construction, and the street-facing second floor has no windows — traditionally explained as a way to avoid appearing to look down on a passing daimyo's procession.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dojō-nabe broth is a shōyu-based warishita typical of this dish style; conventional Japanese soy sauce is wheat-brewed, so the broth is not inherently gluten-free. This is general knowledge about the dish type, not a restaurant-specific confirmed statement.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and other fish-based items prepared in a shared kitchen; no vegetarian or vegan dish was identified in any menu listing found online.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 喜美松（㐂美松） (Kimimatsu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, substitutions, or dedicated preparation were found in any available source. This is a traditional motsuyaki/yakitori izakaya whose grilling tare sauce and simmered motsu-nikomi stew would, per standard practice for this cuisine, typically rely on soy sauce (wheat); no menu or staff statement addressing this was found. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu items were found in any source. The entire menu centers on pork and beef offal, yakitori-style skewers, and meat sashimi/tataki — there is no indication of any plant-based dish, substitution, or accommodation.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 17:00〜22:30／土・日・祝 休み (reported with minor variation across listings as 17:00-22:30, 17:30-22:00, or 17:30-23:00; 17:00-22:30 weekdays was the most consistently repeated figure)
