places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 喜美松 began life decades ago as a fugu (pufferfish) restaurant before transforming into a yakitori/motsuyaki izakaya. It has now been serving the Asakusa Kannon-ura neighborhood — the quiet backstreet area just north of Sensoji Temple — for more than 47-50 years, becoming one of th · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (shitamachi/old-downtown neighborhood behind Sensoji Temple)
chef_bio.background: 喜美松 began life decades ago as a fugu (pufferfish) restaurant before transforming into a yakitori/motsuyaki izakaya. It has now been serving the Asakusa Kannon-ura neighborhood — the quiet backstreet area just north of Sensoji Temple — for more than 47-50 years, becoming one of the shitamachi (old downtown) district's well-known offal-grilling institutions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Freshness of offal is treated as central to the food, with pork sourced directly from a pig farm in Saitama rather than through a general wholesaler.
chef_bio.specialty: Pork motsu (offal) specialties: motsuyaki skewers of parts such as kashira (head), shiro (intestine) and kobukuro, alongside a long-simmered motsu-nikomi (offal stew). The shop was historically also known for boiled pork motsu-sashi (raw offal sashimi), though at least one 2024 local report noted this raw item had been discontinued from the menu.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sources its pork offal fresh, directly from a pig farm in Saitama, and is known for popular skewers of kashira, shiro and kobukuro as well as boiled motsu-sashi.

The shop's entrance is marked by a distinctive large wooden tub (taru) at the door and a red paper lantern, a recognizable local landmark reached by heading north from the Asakusa 3-chome intersection.
In a well-known ranking of Tokyo motsuyaki shops by motsuyaki writer Hamada Nobuo, the restaurant was placed as the number-two shop on the east side ("east's number two").

A 2024 local Asakusa news piece was devoted to the day the shop's long-running motsu-sashi (raw offal sashimi) item disappeared from the menu, reflecting how closely regulars track the shop's offerings.

cultural_comfort.note: A deep-local, Japanese-language shitamachi izakaya with no confirmed English menu, English signage, or official website — visitors without at least basic Japanese or a translation app should expect a fairly unpolished, locals-first experience.

places.name: オマージュ (HOMMAGE) (Hommage)
places.gf_detail: The tasting menus season French dishes with Japanese soy-sauce moromi and miso, which typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce substitution is documented anywhere found. What is documented is that, because the course is fully omakase, the kitchen asks guests in advance about allergies and disliked ingredients and adapts the menu accordingly — so gluten-free requests can likely be raised and discussed, but there is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free track or celiac-level cross-contamination controls, so this should be treated as ask-and-verify, not a confirmed-safe kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The omakase-only tasting menus are built around seasonal French technique with fish and meat as the central protein, and no vegan menu, plant-based tasting course, or dashi/katsuo disclosure was found in any source. The kitchen does ask about dietary dislikes before service, so a vegan guest could raise the request, but there is no evidence the restaurant regularly accommodates a full vegan course — this should be treated as ask-and-hope rather than a confirmed option.
places.hours_raw: ランチ 11:30〜15:00 (L.O.12:30) ／ ディナー 18:00〜22:00 (L.O.19:30)。定休日：月曜日・火曜日（月曜日が祝日の場合は火曜日が休業日となる場合あり）。コースはおまかせのみ。
places.neighborhood: 浅草（つくばエクスプレス浅草駅近く）
places.notes: After training in Japan and then at Michelin-starred restaurants in France — including under chef Régis Marcon in Paris and at a one-star house in southern France — Arai returned to Japan and spent a year working as a fish broker at the Tsukiji market to save the capital he neede · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Born and raised in Asakusa, Tokyo (b. 1974)
chef_bio.background: After training in Japan and then at Michelin-starred restaurants in France — including under chef Régis Marcon in Paris and at a one-star house in southern France — Arai returned to Japan and spent a year working as a fish broker at the Tsukiji market to save the capital he needed. In 2000, at age 26, he opened HOMMAGE in his home neighborhood of Asakusa; the restaurant was promoted to two Michelin stars in the 2018 Tokyo guide and has held that rating since.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name, French for "tribute" or "respect," reflects Arai's guiding idea: gratitude toward nature's ingredients, the producers who grow them, his mentors, his staff, his family, and the guests who return to the restaurant. He emphasizes personal relationships with producers over simply sourcing by region or prestige.
chef_bio.specialty: An omakase-only tasting menu that fuses classical French technique with Japanese ingredients and umami-building elements — Arai is known for working fermented soy products such as soy-sauce moromi and miso into French-style dishes rather than a single fixed signature plate.

anecdotes.text: After his apprenticeship in France under chef Régis Marcon, Arai came home and worked a year as a Tsukiji fish broker purely to fund opening his own restaurant in the Asakusa neighborhood where he grew up.

Arai seasons French dishes with Japanese soy-sauce moromi and kuromame (black bean) miso from Marushin Honke, saying the fermented depth changes the whole complexity of a plate and lets him speak to both Japanese and international palates.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chef Arai works Japanese fermented soy products — soy-sauce moromi and kuromame (black bean) miso — into his French dishes as a seasoning base, which typically means wheat-containing soy products are present in multiple courses of the tasting menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Because the dinner course is entirely omakase, the restaurant asks guests in advance about allergies and disliked ingredients so the multi-course menu can be tailored before each visit.

positives.text: HOMMAGE holds two stars in the Michelin Guide, recognized for delicate French cooking that folds in Japanese ingredients and umami depth rooted in the chef's Asakusa upbringing.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations and the pre-visit allergy/dislike questionnaire for the omakase course are handled by phone or Japanese booking platforms; no confirmed English-language menu was found, so guests who are not comfortable communicating dietary needs in Japanese may want to book through a concierge or bilingual reservation service.

places.name: お好み焼 鉄板焼 つる次郎（浅草 つる次郎 本店） (Asakusa Tsurujiro (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Ts)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's signature okonomiyaki/monjayaki batter is explicitly built on domestic wheat flour ("国産の香り高い小麦粉") as one of its four core showcased ingredients, and no gluten-free batter alternative, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe protocol was mentioned in any source found. Wheat is a structural ingredient of the dish itself, not just an optional add-on, so this is not a safe venue for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented: the signature batter itself is built on a three-day whole-chicken-and-vegetable dashi (per the restaurant's own description of its core ingredients), so even a vegetable-topping order would still contain chicken-derived broth. No vegan menu, substitution, or dashi alternative was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sun/Holidays 11:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00). No fixed closed day, but irregular closures possible.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (浅草), roughly 3 minutes on foot from Kaminarimon Gat
places.cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き・鉄板焼き
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An independently run okonomiyaki, monjayaki and teppanyaki restaurant in the heart of Asakusa, about three minutes on foot from Kaminarimon Gate. The main store has an open teppan counter where guests watch the cook griddle the batter directly in front of them, plus table and sem · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — a short walk from Kaminarimon Gate/Sensoji
chef_bio.background: An independently run okonomiyaki, monjayaki and teppanyaki restaurant in the heart of Asakusa, about three minutes on foot from Kaminarimon Gate. The main store has an open teppan counter where guests watch the cook griddle the batter directly in front of them, plus table and semi-private seating on the first floor and a tatami sunken-kotatsu banquet room upstairs. The restaurant was featured on the TV program 'Arita Tetsuhei to Kosurarenai Machi. '
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around a few carefully sourced ingredients: a whole-chicken-and-vegetable broth extracted over three days, eggs from Aichi Prefecture, fragrant domestic wheat flour, and cabbage that is hand-cut fresh daily with the cut style changed by season.
chef_bio.specialty: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki made with a chicken-and-vegetable dashi simmered for three days, used as the base of the signature batter

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's three-day chicken-and-vegetable dashi, Aichi eggs, domestic wheat flour and daily hand-cut, seasonally-adjusted cabbage are cited as the core of its okonomiyaki and monjayaki.
The shop was covered on the TV program 'Arita Tetsuhei to Kosurarenai Machi' as an Asakusa okonomiyaki spot.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant's showcased dashi is made from whole chicken simmered with vegetables over three days, and this broth underlies the okonomiyaki/monjayaki batter — meaning the core dish is not vegan by default and cannot simply be made vegan by omitting toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains its own English-language official website with English blog posts, reflecting the heavy foreign-tourist traffic around Kaminarimon/Sensoji, though on-site staff English fluency was not independently confirmed.

places.name: ドチャク（Dochaku） (Dochaku)
places.gf_detail: Core menu leans on wheat: pasta and pizza are explicitly part of the lineup alongside the organic-vegetable dishes. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or celiac-safe handling was mentioned in any source checked. Assume standard wheat exposure throughout (pasta, pizza dough, likely flour-based sauces) and do not rely on this spot for celiac-safe dining without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on seasonal organic vegetables (raw, steamed, or roasted preparations) but the kitchen also serves chicken liver pâté, gizzard confit, lamb skewers, and seafood sourced from Ofunato, so most dishes are not vegan and use of dashi/animal stock in sauces or pasta is unconfirmed. There may be vegetable-only small plates to combine into a meal, but nothing marketed as a vegan menu or vegan course was found — ask staff and expect to negotiate a plate rather than order confidently off-menu.
places.neighborhood: 西浅草（Nishi-Asakusa）
places.cuisine: フレンチ・イタリアン・ワインバー
places.notes: Dochaku is a three-story renovated old townhouse turned natural-wine bistro: a standing bar on the ground floor, counter and table seating on the second floor, and a private terrace room on the third floor above a small attached wine shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: owner/operating group also runs several other concept restaurants in Asakusa
chef_bio.origin: Opened September 5, 2022 in Nishi-Asakusa; run by a small Asakusa-based restaurant group that operates several concept restaurants in the neighborhood out of a renovated, more than 50-year-old private house.
chef_bio.background: Dochaku is a three-story renovated old townhouse turned natural-wine bistro: a standing bar on the ground floor, counter and table seating on the second floor, and a private terrace room on the third floor above a small attached wine shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "Dochaku" (土着) is an architectural term meaning something rooted to its particular place. The owners' interest in organic vegetables led them to natural wine as what they felt was the truest pairing for vegetable-centered cooking, and the restaurant is framed as a project meant to grow slowly with 'this place, this time, these people. ' The parent group describes its broader approach as 'standing close to people and the town,' using its handful of Asakusa restaurants to build community as part of neighborhood-making.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward Italian-French small plates built around seasonal organic produce — dishes cited by local guides include chicken liver pâté, duck/chicken gizzard confit, a chunky organic vegetable gratin, steamed organic vegetables, and lamb skewers, alongside pasta and pizza, paired with a rotating list of over 100 natural wines kept in a third-floor cellar.

anecdotes.text: Vegetables and meat are sourced from local producers and seafood comes from the fishing port in Ofunato, with an emphasis on small-scale wine and food producers.
The restaurant's name comes from an architectural term meaning 'rooted in the land,' reflecting a concept built around organic vegetables paired with natural wine.
A blog piece frames the restaurant as a hideaway where diners feel the connection between land, season, and people ('土地と季節と人のつながり').

cultural_comfort.note: Booking and menu are primarily Japanese-language (reservations run through Hot Pepper, Ikyu, and R-gnavi), though the Tabelog listing has an English page for browsing before you go.

places.name: Lethe（レーテ） (Lethe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free process is documented anywhere. As a classical Southern French bistro, dishes routinely involve wheat (bread service, roux-thickened sauces, house-made sausage and cassoulet). Diners with celiac disease or wheat allergy should call ahead and discuss ingredients directly, since no verified gluten-free accommodation exists.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on Southern French meat classics — duck confit, house-made pork sausage, cassoulet, and bouillabaisse — with no vegan dishes, plant-based substitutions, or dashi/bonito disclosures mentioned in any available source. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.

chef_bio.philosophy: The name Lethe comes from the river of forgetfulness in Greek mythology — the owners chose it because they want guests to forget their daily worries and linger unhurried over a slow meal and good wine.
chef_bio.specialty: Duck confit cured and cooked over four days, and a hearty cassoulet built on house-made sausage, alongside a bouillabaisse.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name is drawn from Lethe, the river of oblivion in Greek myth, reflecting a wish for diners to set aside daily stress and relax over dinner.
A blogger visiting for a birthday dinner singled out the cassoulet and bouillabaisse as the house specialties.
The bistro opened near Kaminarimon in spring 2022 as a hideaway serving Southern French regional dishes and wine.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, Japanese-run neighborhood bistro with no evidence of an English menu or multilingual staff online, so reservations and ordering are likely smoothest in Japanese.

No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific accommodation was mentioned in any source found.

No vegan dishes, plant-based dashi, or vegan menu options were found in any source.

chef_bio.background: The restaurant is run by a female owner who came to soba from an unrelated career in the beauty industry. She took up soba-making almost by chance, and her natural aptitude was striking from the start — accounts describe her impressing trained soba craftsmen within just a couple of days of lessons, and completing her training in about 20 days before deciding to open her own shop in July 2015.
chef_bio.philosophy: Despite her unconventional path into the trade, she is described as studious and uncompromising about her craft — closely attentive to the quality of her buckwheat flour and continually refining her noodle-making technique.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made 'nihachi' (two parts wheat to eight parts buckwheat) soba, served alongside a seasonal 'soba-mae' selection of sake-pairing dishes made from ingredients sourced fresh each day, such as sweetfish, conger eel, and breaded pork cutlet.

anecdotes.text: After only about two days of soba lessons her noodles were already drawing praise, and she went on to impress professional soba craftsmen with difficult techniques before completing her training in roughly 20 days and opening the restaurant in July 2015.

The restaurant was featured on TV Tokyo's food-and-drink drama 'Wakako Zake,' which highlighted the proprietress hand-rolling the soba and her lively banter with the restaurant's master during service.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, locally-loved counter soba shop with a Japanese-language-first experience and reservations customary for the evening course, though it can be booked through English-capable platforms.

places.name: おばんざい味ゆぅ (Obanzai Miyuu (Aji-Yuu))

The kitchen uses homemade miso and traditional seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi (which can contain wheat and bonito) are likely used unless the vegetable-forward dishes happen to avoid them. Because the menu changes daily and nothing about wheat-free preparation is documented, celiac diners should call ahead (050-5869-7791) and confirm which of the day's dishes are safe.
places.vegan_detail: No confirmed all-vegan menu. The obanzai are vegetable-heavy and change daily, but obanzai izakaya menus typically also include fish- or meat-based items and traditional dashi (often bonito-based), and no source confirms a katsuo/dashi-free or fully plant-based option. Vegan diners should ask staff which of the day's dishes are free of fish stock, bonito flakes, and animal products.

anecdotes.text: Guests pick their dishes from large plates lined up on the counter — a self-selection obanzai format — with the 5-8 items on offer changing daily depending on what's in season.
Miyuu curates her sake list around breweries in Fukushima and Hiroshima that she came across through personal connections, pairing them with her home-style cooking.

positives.text: The daily menu emphasizes seasonal vegetables and homemade miso, giving it a naturally plant-leaning lean-up, though this has not been documented as a formal vegan or allergy-safe program.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter-only izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and phone-only reservations — most comfortable with a Japanese-speaking companion or guide rather than walking in solo without any Japanese.

chef_bio.chef_name: 椿豪（つばき たけし）・椿澄子（つばき すみこ）夫妻
chef_bio.background: Couple-run bistro opened in 2016 in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreets. Before opening, the chef worked at a restaurant in Kagurazaka, and the couple say they were inspired toward an à la carte, drop-in dining style after visiting a restaurant in Ebisu around a decade before opening their own place.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name しみいる (from 沁み入る, "to seep in / soak in") reflects the aim of serving food and wine that quietly seep into the heart, in a calm, unhurried hideaway atmosphere down a narrow alley behind Sensō-ji.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal-vegetable-driven French cuisine paired with natural wine, using produce and meat from small-scale producers and seafood landed at Ofunato port.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is counted among what food writers call the "Kannon-ura French Four Heavenly Kings" (観音裏フレンチ四天王), a small cluster of well-regarded French bistros in this quiet Asakusa backstreet.

Featured by Asakusa High School teacher Kanbayashi-sensei in dancyu's "Asakusa Lunch Guide" series as an example of authentic French cooking in the Kannon-ura area, part of a project in which he ate lunch at 708 Asakusa restaurants.
Profile piece describing the couple's cooking as food that "seeps into every organ" (五臓六腑にしみいる), built on a deliberately à la carte menu to build repeat regulars.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, Japanese-language hideaway bistro down a narrow alley — English-language reservations are possible through platforms like TableCheck, but don't expect an English menu or much spoken English on site.

places.vegan_detail: The broth is pork bone and chicken based, blended with miso, and standard toppings include pork chashu, pork back fat, and a soy-marinated egg. No plant-based broth or vegan/vegetarian menu option was found in any source covering the shop.

chef_bio.background: The owner trained for roughly 17 years within the Keisuke (けいすけ) ramen group, one of Tokyo's well-known ramen brand families, before striking out on his own to open this small counter shop in Asakusa in April 2023.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name, 美々 (Bibi), is described by food writers as expressing the owner's core wish for the food to be 'beautifully presented and deliciously eaten. '
chef_bio.specialty: Jukusei Miso Ramen (熟成味噌らーめん) — a soup built from four types of aged Niigata miso and around 15 ingredients, served with medium-thick noodles and topped with chashu pork, a marinated soft-boiled egg, stir-fried vegetables, menma, back fat, ginger, and yuzu.

anecdotes.text: Owner Noriaki Takeishi spent about 17 years training within the Keisuke ramen group before opening this independent shop in Asakusa in April 2023.

Diners are served a separate koji-miso paste made with monkfish (anglerfish) liver alongside the bowl, meant to be stirred in partway through the meal for an 'aji-hen' (taste-change) experience that shifts the soup's flavor as you eat.
The restaurant's name, 美々 (Bibi), is said to express the owner's wish for food that is 'beautifully presented and deliciously eaten.'

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature soup is a pork bone and chicken based broth blended with aged miso, and standard toppings include pork chashu and pork back fat plus a soy-marinated egg — no plant-based broth, protein, or vegetarian/vegan option is mentioned in any coverage of the shop.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering appears to go through a Japanese-language ticket machine with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff in any source reviewed; it's a short walk from Asakusa Station and draws some tourist foot traffic, but non-Japanese speakers should come with the dish name written out or a translation app.

places.name: 江戸もんじゃひょうたん (Edo Monja Hyotan)

No rice-flour or gluten-free batter substitute, dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated cooking surface, or allergen protocol was found for this shop in any source, so it should not be considered safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan-specific accommodation was found for this shop. A diner could in principle ask for a vegetable-only monjayaki (cabbage, corn, mochi, cheese) omitting seafood/meat toppings, but standard monjayaki broth and the Worcestershire-style sauce used at Tokyo monjayaki shops are typically bonito/fish-based, and this has not been confirmed either way here — vegans should ask staff directly about the dashi and sauce before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00–5:00 the following morning (L.O. 4:00); Saturdays, Sundays and holidays 10:00–5:00 the following morning (L.O. 4:00). One aggregator listing instead shows 11:00–21:00, so hours should be reconfirmed before a visit.
places.notes: Edo Monja Hyotan has run for roughly two and a half decades on a backstreet just off Nakamise-dori, a very short walk from Sensoji. The shop is styled to feel like an old Edo-period shophouse: guests slide open the front door and pass a nostalgic dagashi (penny-candy) corner befo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Edo Monja Hyotan has run for roughly two and a half decades on a backstreet just off Nakamise-dori, a very short walk from Sensoji. The shop is styled to feel like an old Edo-period shophouse: guests slide open the front door and pass a nostalgic dagashi (penny-candy) corner before reaching the dining room and a tatami seating area upstairs.
chef_bio.specialty: Mentai-mochi-cheese monjayaki (spicy cod roe, rice cake and cheese folded into the classic Tokyo-style griddled batter dish) is the shop's best-known order, alongside its "tokusen" (deluxe) monjayaki piled with mentaiko, shrimp, squid and mochi.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has appeared repeatedly on the TV program Docchi no Ryori Show and draws a steady stream of celebrity visitors.
Opening the sliding front door, visitors are greeted by an old-fashioned dagashi (penny-candy) counter before the dining area, giving the shop a nostalgic, time-slip feel.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the shop sits right in tourist Asakusa near Sensoji, but expect a lively, mostly Japanese-speaking room typical of a local monjayaki parlor rather than an English-fluent staff.

places.name: ちんや (Chinya)

There is no evidence of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol. This should not be treated as celiac-safe without direct, advance confirmation by phone (03-3841-0010), and even then cross-contact risk in a shared kitchen built around soy-sauce-based cooking is likely.
places.vegan_detail: Chinya is a wagyu beef sukiyaki/shabu-shabu specialty restaurant; the signature preparation involves meat simmered in a soy-based warishita and dipped in raw egg. No vegan menu, plant-based sukiyaki option, or vegetarian course is advertised on the official site or major reservation platforms.

roles: Historic sukiyaki (wagyu beef hot pot) restaurant founded in 1880
Reopened in 2022 under WDI Group with the former proprietor and longtime staff involved
chef_bio.background: Chinya traces its roots to the Edo period, when the shop's predecessor was known locally as 'Chin-ya' because it kept and supplied prized 'chin' (Japanese Chin) lapdogs to daimyo lords and wealthy merchants, and also served as their veterinarian — the unusual name stuck even after the family opened it as a restaurant in 1880 (Meiji 13). It became a dedicated sukiyaki specialty shop in 1903 and grew into one of Asakusa's oldest and most storied sukiyaki houses. The aging Meiji-era building, worn down further by pandemic-era business struggles, closed in August 2021. WDI Group stepped in to preserve the Chinya name and traditions, reopening the restaurant in spring 2022 at a new address in Hanakawado overlooking the Sumida River, with former proprietor Fumihiko Sumiyoshi and former staff members continuing to be involved in day-to-day operations.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carrying more than 140 years of sukiyaki tradition, craftsmanship and flavor forward for new generations, as expressed on the restaurant's own site: preserving 'its tradition, its dedication and its taste for the future and for the world. '
chef_bio.specialty: Sukiyaki made with 'tekisashi-niku' (適サシ肉) — female Japanese Black Wagyu beef raised for around 30 months from carcasses under 480kg, chosen for a balance of marbling and lean meat, then aged to concentrate flavor. It is cooked tableside in a copper pot with a traditional sweetened soy-sauce warishita and dipped in raw egg.

anecdotes.text: Before it was a restaurant, the shop was known in the Edo period as 'Chin-ya' because it kept and supplied treasured 'chin' lapdogs to daimyo lords and wealthy merchants, and also acted as their veterinarian — the name carried over when the family became restaurateurs in 1880.

When the original Meiji-era building could no longer be maintained and the pandemic hit business hard, Chinya closed in August 2021; WDI Group then preserved the brand and reopened it in March 2022 on the banks of the Sumida River in Hanakawado, with the former proprietor and former staff members returning to work there.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sukiyaki at Chinya is cooked in a traditional warishita (sweetened soy-sauce sauce); standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, and no wheat-free or tamari-based alternative is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core dish is built around wagyu beef cooked in a shared pot with a soy-based sauce and finished with a raw-egg dip, so there is no realistic way to keep a vegan order separate from meat-based cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: Chinya maintains a full English website with an English-language history/story section and menu PDF, plus English-friendly online reservation platforms (TableCheck, Tabelog), making it comfortable to book and dine at without Japanese ability.

No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated GF preparation, or wheat-free broth/seasoning option is documented anywhere in available sources. Standard ramen shoyu tare and broths in this style typically contain wheat-derived soy sauce. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house specialty is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth combined with seafood (魚介) stock; no vegetarian or vegan menu items, plant-based broth, or dashi-free option is documented in any available source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

chef_bio.background: Fuji Ramen was opened by a chef who previously worked at Yoroiya, a well-known long-established ramen shop in Asakusa, before starting his own counter-only shop nearby on Kokusai-dori.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is built around self-made noodles, made with close attention to the wheat used, paired with a rich, slow-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Tonkotsu ramen topped with cabbage and mayu (burnt garlic oil) — a distinctive combination for a pork-bone broth shop; the shop also offers tsukemen (dipping noodles).

小麦からこだわった自家製麺のらーめんのお店で、豚骨系濃厚スープが売り (A shop built on self-made noodles that are particular about the wheat used, with a rich tonkotsu-style broth as its selling point).

cultural_comfort.note: Order at a ticket vending machine (食券機) typical of small Tokyo ramen counters; the shop is popular with foreign tourists given its location right by Asakusa Station and Kokusai-dori, but no English menu was confirmed, so basic Japanese or pointing helps.

Not recommended for celiac diners without direct advance confirmation by phone (03-3841-3200), and even then a safe outcome is unlikely given the shared-oil, wheat-batter format.

No vegan menu, vegetable-only tempura set, or dashi substitution is documented on the official site or in press coverage, so vegan and vegetarian diners would need to ask staff directly about ingredient substitutions and shared-fryer cross-contact.

The business later moved to its present spot immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate in Asakusa and, founded in 1837 (Tenpō 8), is · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founded by Sadakichi (定吉), a native of Mikawa Province (present-day Aichi Prefecture) who came to Edo and first worked in the egg-distribution trade before turning to tempura
chef_bio.origin: Mikawa Province (present-day Aichi Prefecture) via Nihonbashi, Edo
chef_bio.background: Sansada traces its origin to around 1831, when Sadakichi began selling Edo-mae fish and shellfish tempura from a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō. The business later moved to its present spot immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate in Asakusa and, founded in 1837 (Tenpō 8), is considered the oldest continuously operating tempura restaurant in Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: A single-storefront philosophy — no branches, ever — built on nearly two centuries of frying Edo-mae tempura in sesame oil at the same spot beside Kaminarimon Gate.
chef_bio.specialty: Kakiage (mixed tempura of small Edo-mae fish and shellfish, offered in large/medium/small sizes) and the tendon rice bowl, both fried in aromatic sesame oil in the traditional Edo style.

anecdotes.text: The shop is immortalized in the old Asakusa saying "first Asakusa, second Kannon, third Sansada's tempura" (一に浅草、二に観音、三に三定の天ぷら), placing it alongside Sensō-ji itself as a must-see of the neighborhood.

Founder Sadakichi originally battered small fish and shellfish caught in the waters near Edo and deep-fried them in sesame oil at a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō district — the beginning of what became Sansada; the shop's name is a contraction of "Mikawaya Sadakichi" (三河屋定吉).

The restaurant has never opened branch locations, said to be rooted in the philosophy that the owner should never leave the shop; today it occupies only its original storefront on Kaminarimon-dōri plus an adjoining building.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits directly beside Kaminarimon Gate and serves huge numbers of tourists, so staff are used to non-Japanese-speaking guests and a laminated English menu is available on request, though the main menu and ordering are still primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 梅むら (Umemura)
places.gf_detail: The signature mame-kanten is built from red peas, kanten (agar) jelly, and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup) — none of these are wheat-derived ingredients, so the core dish appears naturally free of wheat by composition. However, no dedicated gluten-free protocol, ingredient labeling, or staff allergy-handling statement from the shop was found, and other menu items (dressings, tokoroten with tare sauce, etc.) were not individually verified for hidden soy-sauce or wheat-based seasonings. Celiac diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The signature mame-kanten (red peas, kanten agar, kuromitsu black-sugar syrup) appears plant-based by ingredient composition, with no dairy, egg, or dashi evident in that specific dish. However, several other listed menu items — cream anmitsu (クリームあんみつ) and cream mitsumame (クリームみつ豆) — explicitly contain dairy cream, so the menu overall is mixed rather than vegan-friendly across the board. No staff confirmation of hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., gelatin, bonito-based flavoring) was found, so diners should ask before ordering.

The house version starts with  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — founded in 1968 (Showa 43)
chef_bio.background: Umemura opened in Asakusa's 'ura-Asakusa' backstreets (just behind Sensō-ji) in 1968 and is widely credited by Tokyo food writers as the birthplace of mame-kan / mame-kanten, the bean-and-agar sweet that later spread to sweets shops across the city. The house version starts with Hokkaido red peas (aka-endou) simmered for around five hours until tender, served over firm-textured kanten agar with a pour of kuromitsu syrup. The shop is now run by the second generation of the founding family, who has kept the original recipe and method largely unchanged.
chef_bio.philosophy: Continuity of the original 1968 recipe and technique, passed down within the family rather than modernized or expanded into a chain.
chef_bio.specialty: 豆かんてん (mame-kanten) — a simple dessert of slow-simmered red peas, kanten agar jelly, and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup)

anecdotes.text: The shop and its mame-kanten have been featured in the gourmet manga/TV drama Kodoku no Gurume (Solitary Gourmet), which helped cement its reputation as the 'birthplace' of mame-kan.
Described as the 'originator' (発祥) of mame-kan in Asakusa, with beans cooked roughly five hours before serving.

positives.text: The shop's signature mame-kanten is made from a short, identifiable ingredient list — Hokkaido red peas simmered about five hours, kanten agar, and kuromitsu syrup — with no meat, fish, or dashi in the base recipe.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, Japanese-only old-town sweets shop with no apparent English menu or website, but it is a well-known, frequently visited stop for foreign tourists exploring backstreet Asakusa, so basic pointing-and-ordering from the short, photo-friendly menu should be manageable.

places.name: 浅草名代らーめん 与ろゐ屋 (Asakusa Nadai Ramen Yoroiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle or soy-sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere, including on the vegan ramen. The noodles are standard wheat chuka-soba noodles (the vegan version is described only as "egg-free," not wheat-free), the tare is a wafu shoyu (soy sauce) base which in Japanese ramen shops is almost always wheat-containing, and the gyoza wrapper is also wheat-based. There is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free preparation area. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should treat this as not a safe option.
places.vegan_detail: There is a dedicated vegan ramen (about ¥1,250) whose broth is made only from vegetables and kombu (Chinese cabbage, onion, dried shiitake mushroom, kombu, carrot) — no katsuobushi, niboshi, or meat stock — finished wafu-shoyu style with sansho pepper leaf and yuzu aroma, and made with egg-free noodles. However, this is one item on an otherwise omnivore, fish/meat-dashi-heavy menu (the standard ramen and most other dishes use pork bone, chicken, niboshi and bonito), so strict vegans should specifically order the vegan ramen and confirm current preparation with staff rather than assuming other menu items are safe.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), on Denboin-dori, about a 1-minute walk from Se
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The founder previously ran several ramen shops in Chiyoda and elsewhere before deciding to recreate, in Asakusa, the taste of a bowl of "chuka soba" he remembered from a soba shop near his childhood home — one whose broth carried a subtle aroma of dried sardines and bonito rather · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The founder previously ran several ramen shops in Chiyoda and elsewhere before deciding to recreate, in Asakusa, the taste of a bowl of "chuka soba" he remembered from a soba shop near his childhood home — one whose broth carried a subtle aroma of dried sardines and bonito rather than the heavier style typical of Chinese-style ramen restaurants. He opened Yoroiya on Denboin-dori in July 1992. The shop's name traces back to his father's menswear shop, called "Kairoiya. "
chef_bio.philosophy: Recreate the gentle, dashi-forward chuka soba flavor of old Asakusa/Tokyo soba-ya cooking rather than the richer tonkotsu or heavily seasoned styles that became common elsewhere, and keep that flavor consistent for the neighborhood's regulars as well as visitors.
chef_bio.specialty: Wafu (Japanese-style) shoyu ramen: a broth built from pork bone and chicken carcass, finished with carefully selected niboshi (dried sardines), katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and kombu, with a light touch of yuzu aroma. The shop also serves a morning ramen (asa-ra) service from 8:30am, and gyoza made with garlic-free Oyama chicken thigh and harusame glass noodles instead of pork.

anecdotes.text: The owner opened his first ramen shop in Chiyoda Ward and ran several others before returning to Asakusa in 1992 to recreate the ramen he remembered from a soba shop near his childhood home.
The shop has been loved by local residents for roughly three decades and has been inducted into the 'Hall of Fame' of Ishigami-bon, the annual ramen guidebook published since 1998.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free noodle, soy-sauce, or preparation option is advertised anywhere in the menu information found, including for the vegan ramen; standard wheat noodles and wafu shoyu tare appear to be used throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan ramen is a distinct menu item made in a shop that otherwise cooks meat- and fish-based broths (pork bone, chicken, niboshi, katsuobushi) on the same line; no statement about separate vegan-only equipment or pots was found.

positives.text: The vegan ramen's broth is described as made entirely from vegetables and kombu (Chinese cabbage, onion, dried shiitake, kombu, carrot) with no animal-derived dashi, and the noodles used for it are egg-free.
The gyoza is made without pork or garlic, using Oyama chicken thigh and harusame (glass noodles), which may suit some dietary preferences though the wrapper is wheat-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A one-minute walk from Sensoji and hugely popular with foreign visitors (long lunchtime queues of overseas tourists are reported); the shop keeps English and simplified-Chinese menus for non-Japanese-speaking guests.

places.name: 初小川 (Hatsuogawa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The eel is grilled rather than fried, but the kabayaki tare is a traditional soy-sauce-based sauce continuously replenished since 1907; that style of shoyu is typically wheat-based, and no source confirms a wheat-free/tamari tare, dedicated GF menu, or celiac protocol. Treat as not celiac-safe absent direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-appropriate: the entire menu is built around grilled eel (unaju/unadon and related eel dishes), an animal product, and no vegetarian or vegan dishes, plant-based tare, or dashi substitutions are documented anywhere in available sources.
places.hours_raw: 12:00–13:30 and 17:00–19:30 daily (lunch and dinner service); 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly day off)
places.neighborhood: 浅草・雷門 (Asakusa, near Kaminarimon gate — Asakusa Station area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hatsuogawa has operated as a dedicated eel restaurant in Asakusa for over a century, through the Meiji, Taisho, Showa and Heisei eras, in a modest shopfront near Kaminarimon. It is one of the district's recognized old-line eel houses and was named to the 2022 'Unagi Hyakumeiten'  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in Meiji 40 (1907) in Asakusa, Taito-ku — one of the neighborhood's old-line (shinise) unagi specialists, still on its original side street a few minutes from Kaminarimon gate.
chef_bio.background: Hatsuogawa has operated as a dedicated eel restaurant in Asakusa for over a century, through the Meiji, Taisho, Showa and Heisei eras, in a modest shopfront near Kaminarimon. It is one of the district's recognized old-line eel houses and was named to the 2022 'Unagi Hyakumeiten' (100 Notable Eel Restaurants) list and carries a Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition in the Tokyo guide.
chef_bio.philosophy: A made-to-order approach (no pre-grilled eel sitting in a case) paired with a single tare sauce that has been kept going and topped up continuously since the shop opened in 1907, rather than remade from scratch.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style unaju/unadon — eel is not held ready-made; each order is butterflied, steamed, then finished over charcoal (shichirin) after an order comes in, so the fat renders out and the flesh stays soft and light rather than heavy.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name is said to combine 'Hatsu' from the founder's given name Hatsutaro and 'Ogawa,' associated with the eel trade, forming Hatsuogawa (初小川).
The dry-leaning (karakuchi) tare in use today is the same base that has been continuously replenished since the restaurant's 1907 founding.

positives.text: Eel is prepared to order (butterflied, steamed, then charcoal-grilled after the order is placed) rather than held pre-cooked, and the cooking method is grilling rather than deep-frying, so there is no shared tempura fryer in the process.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only, old-school Asakusa eel shop with no official English-language site found — expect a Japanese-language menu and limited English service, so some Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: おにぎり浅草宿六 (Onigiri Asakusa Yadoroku)
places.gf_detail: Plain rice and nori are naturally gluten-free, but several popular fillings (notably the simmered shrimp "ami") and any seasoning sauces used in the kitchen are likely soy-sauce based, and Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. No source found confirms a wheat-free soy sauce, a dedicated gluten-free prep area, or a formal allergy protocol here — this is a small, decades-old kitchen making everything by hand in one space, so ask staff about the specific filling and any seasoning before ordering if you need to avoid gluten strictly.
places.vegan_detail: Most of the shop's roughly 20 fillings are fish- or seafood-based (salted salmon, tarako/cod roe, dried baby shrimp, salted squid, salmon roe), so the only clearly plant-based option identified is the umeboshi (pickled plum) filling, or plain salted rice with nori. No source confirms whether the plain rice itself ever touches dashi/bonito stock in preparation, and the same small kitchen handles all the fish fillings, so strict vegans should ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–17:00, Monday/Tuesday/Wednesday/Thursday/Friday/Saturday. Dinner from around 17:00–17:30, Monday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday only (no dinner Tuesday or Wednesday). Both lunch and dinner end whenever the day's rice sells out, so actual closing times vary. Closed all day Sunday.
