cultural_comfort.note: A traditional shitamachi monjayaki shop with no confirmed English menu, though it sits on Hisago-dori, a tourist-frequented shopping street near Sensoji, so simple ordering with pointing/photos is manageable even without Japanese.

places.name: 田中そば店 浅草店 (Tanaka Sobaten Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard chūka-soba/ramen shop: the noodles are ordinary wheat-based chūka-men, not gluten-free, and the broth is niboshi (dried sardine) and shoyu based — soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat. No source mentions a gluten-free noodle substitution, a dedicated gluten-free prep area, or any allergen accommodation. Celiac and gluten-avoidant diners should treat this shop as unsuitable.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) dashi with shoyu, and pork chāshū is a core topping across the menu. No source mentions any vegetarian or vegan broth/noodle option — fish- and meat-derived stock is central to essentially every dish, so vegan and vegetarian diners should treat this shop as unsuitable.
places.hours_raw: Most sources (Navitime, Yahoo Map, Gnavi-derived listings) state 11:00-21:00 every day, no closed days. One aggregator reported a different, possibly older schedule: weekdays 11:00-15:45 and 17:00-21:00 (split), weekends/holidays 11:00-21:00 straight through. Confirm current hours before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（台東区）、浅草駅6番出口すぐ
places.notes: The Asakusa branch opened on August 16, 2018, under the sign "中華そば専門 田中そば店" (Chūka-soba Specialist Tanaka Sobaten). It took over the storefront that had previously housed an Aomori-style niboshi ramen shop called "つし馬" (Tsushima). The parent company, Tanaka Shoten, opened its fir · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: ramen/soba shop, part of the small Tanaka-Shoten ramen group
chef_bio.origin: Adachi-ku, Tokyo (parent shop opened Dec 2000)
chef_bio.background: The Asakusa branch opened on August 16, 2018, under the sign "中華そば専門 田中そば店" (Chūka-soba Specialist Tanaka Sobaten). It took over the storefront that had previously housed an Aomori-style niboshi ramen shop called "つし馬" (Tsushima). The parent company, Tanaka Shoten, opened its first shop in Adachi-ku in December 2000 and gradually expanded across Tokyo and beyond (Asakusa, Sendai, Akihabara, Misato — a 15th location opened in February 2021), always chasing the same simple goal: 'oishii' (delicious).
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's guiding idea is 気軽に食べる田舎そば — 'unpretentious, countryside-style soba you can eat without fuss' — aiming for a nostalgic, almost salt-ramen-like flavor built on a niboshi-and-shoyu broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Chūka soba (a niboshi/dried-sardine and shoyu based broth reminiscent of Kitakata-style ramen) and the Asakusa-exclusive Tsugaru Niboshi Ramen and Bari Niboshi Soba, which revive dishes from the shop's Tsushima-era predecessor.

anecdotes.text: The Asakusa shop occupies the former location of the Aomori niboshi ramen shop Tsushima, and keeps two of that shop's dishes alive as an Asakusa-only menu: Tsugaru Niboshi Ramen and Bari Niboshi Soba.
Tanaka Shoten's company history traces from its December 2000 opening in Adachi-ku through expansion into Asakusa, Sendai, Akihabara and Misato, guided by a consistent focus on 'oishii'.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering goes through a ticket vending machine (券売機) at the entrance with no table service to guide you, so being able to point at pictures/buttons and pay cash is enough — a little comfort with a Japanese-only setup helps, but no conversation is required.

places.name: まるごと北海道 浅草店 (Marugoto Hokkaido Asakusa Branch)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The all-you-can-eat courses include tempura-battered crab and other fried items, crab hot pot with shared broth, and likely soy-sauce-based seasonings — a high-risk setting for a strict gluten-free or celiac diet, with no evidence the kitchen accommodates it.
places.vegan_detail: This is a crab and Hokkaido seafood specialty restaurant centered on all-you-can-eat crab courses, crab hot pot, and kani-zōsui; no plant-based, vegetarian, or vegan menu items were found in any source, and the food concept (crab sashimi, hot pot, tempura, congee) is seafood-based throughout with no indication of dashi alternatives.
places.hours_raw: 土・日・祝、ランチ：11:00～15:00（L.O.14:30、ドリンクL.O.14:45）／月～土、ディナー：17:00～23:00（L.O.22:30、ドリンクL.O.22:45）／日・祝日、ディナー：17:00～22:30（L.O.22:00、ドリンクL.O.22:15）。定休日：無。（2026年2月28日にビル老朽化に伴い一時休業、隣接ビルへ移転し6月1日に再開）
places.neighborhood: 浅草（つくばエクスプレス浅草駅／浅草国際通り）
places.notes: Marugoto Hokkaido ("All of Hokkaido") started as an Asakusa antenna shop bringing Hokkaido specialty foods to Tokyo, and this Asakusa branch built an izakaya around that sourcing model: king crab (tarabagani), snow crab (zuwaigani) and hair crab (kegani) shipped in directly from  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Hokkaido specialty crab & seafood izakaya, operated by Marugoto Hokkaido Co., Ltd. (est. 2004), an Asakusa company running a cluster of Hokkaido-themed shops and restaurants near Kaminarimon
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — sister restaurant to the company's Jingisukan tavern and Hokkaido product shop nearby
chef_bio.background: Marugoto Hokkaido ("All of Hokkaido") started as an Asakusa antenna shop bringing Hokkaido specialty foods to Tokyo, and this Asakusa branch built an izakaya around that sourcing model: king crab (tarabagani), snow crab (zuwaigani) and hair crab (kegani) shipped in directly from Hokkaido and served in all-you-can-eat courses just off Kokusai-dori, a one-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: A Hokkaido antenna-shop concept — deliver Hokkaido's crab and seafood directly to Asakusa at approachable prices rather than treating crab as a rare luxury.
chef_bio.specialty: Three-crab all-you-can-eat course (king crab, snow crab, hair crab) served as sashimi, crab hot pot, grilled and fried crab, and finished with kani-zōsui (crab rice porridge); the restaurant recommends its house-made crab vinegar (自家製カニ酢) as a condiment for the crab.

anecdotes.text: A local Taito-ward feature piece calls crab "the king of winter flavors" and highlights being able to eat one's fill of it casually at this Asakusa shop.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found, so expect a Japanese-language, locals-and-tour-group izakaya experience despite the Asakusa tourist-district location.

places.name: 銀花堂 (Ginkado)
places.gf_detail: Daifuku mochi and red bean (anko) filling are naturally wheat-free by nature, but the shop has not published anything about the dusting starch used (potato starch vs. wheat flour), shared prep surfaces, or cross-contact with the custard/cream-based daifuku flavors. No dedicated gluten-free process has been confirmed — ask staff directly before eating if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: Two of the five daifuku flavors (shio-koshi red bean and matcha) appear to be plant-based, since they're built on standard mochi and anko without cream; the other three (white peach whipped cream, custard, strawberry whipped cream) contain dairy and/or egg. The strawberry skewers (ichigo-kushi) are fresh fruit, typically with a sugar glaze. None of this has been explicitly confirmed as vegan by the shop itself, so ask staff before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (weekdays), 10:00-19:00 (Sat/Sun/holidays); irregular closures (不定休)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Nakamise-dori
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Originally a kanzashi (hair ornament) and Japanese craft-goods shop founded roughly 280 years ago near Sensoji Temple on Nakamise-dori — the shop name "Ginka" (銀花, "silver flower") is said to derive from the ornamental kanzashi it sold to geisha. After foot traffic collapsed duri · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Originally a kanzashi (hair ornament) and Japanese craft-goods shop founded roughly 280 years ago near Sensoji Temple on Nakamise-dori — the shop name "Ginka" (銀花, "silver flower") is said to derive from the ornamental kanzashi it sold to geisha. After foot traffic collapsed during the COVID-19 pandemic, the shop was renewed in 2022 from a traditional crafts store into a fruit-and-wagashi sweets stall.
chef_bio.specialty: Ichigo daifuku (strawberry mochi with red bean paste) and ichigo-kushi (fresh strawberry skewers)

anecdotes.text: The shop began as a kanzashi outfitter near Sensoji roughly 280 years ago; its name references the ornamental "silver flowers" (kanzashi) it originally sold.

The old crafts shop was reborn in 2022 as a fruit and wagashi sweets stand after pandemic-era foot traffic on Nakamise-dori dried up; it now sells strawberry skewers, cut melon, and strawberry daifuku.
A visiting blogger described the strawberry daifuku as filled with a plump, juicy strawberry paired with gently sweetened red bean paste and soft mochi.

cultural_comfort.note: A walk-up sweets stand on Tokyo's most tourist-trodden street — you can order by pointing at the photo menu, but there's no confirmed English signage or English-speaking staff, so a little Japanese or gestures help, especially for allergy questions.

places.name: 鈴芳 (Suzuyoshi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is documented anywhere. The menu is built around grilled skewers with tare glaze, miso-based motsu-ni, and a soy-based Korean-style beef tendon stew — tare and miso in Japanese izakaya cooking are almost always wheat-containing (soy sauce), and there is no mention of a dedicated fryer or wheat-free soy sauce option. Treat as unsafe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is meat/offal/seafood-centered (yakitori, motsu-ni, beef tendon stew, whelk, sashimi, grilled fish); no plant-based or vegetarian dishes were identified in any source, and dashi/stock use is unconfirmed but presumed to be conventional (likely katsuo/bonito-based). Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（ホッピー通り）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Suzuyoshi has stood on Asakusa's Hoppy-dori since 1974 and is regularly cited as one of the street's most historic surviving taverns — a holdout of the postwar Showa-era drinking-street atmosphere as many neighboring bars have closed or been rebuilt amid the area's redevelopment  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa (opened 1974)
chef_bio.background: Suzuyoshi has stood on Asakusa's Hoppy-dori since 1974 and is regularly cited as one of the street's most historic surviving taverns — a holdout of the postwar Showa-era drinking-street atmosphere as many neighboring bars have closed or been rebuilt amid the area's redevelopment around the Tokyo Skytree and the Olympics. A visiting food writer who first came roughly twenty years ago recalled the building still had an external staircase up to a residential unit above the bar, a detail that has since disappeared as the block modernized.
chef_bio.specialty: Draft (nama/taru-nama) Hoppy — Suzuyoshi is widely cited as the only shop left on Hoppy-dori that still pours genuine draft Hoppy rather than the bottled version, served white, black, or half-and-half. It's paired with a spicy, Korean-influenced beef tendon stew (piri-kara gyusuji-ni) and a miso-based motsu-ni (offal stew), alongside grilled skewers, whelk simmered in soy, sashimi, and grilled fish.

anecdotes.text: 浅草ホッピー通りでも屈指の歴史を誇る『鈴芳』は、このお店でしか飲めないという樽生ホッピーやピリ辛テイストの韓国風牛すじ煮が人気です。

The author recalls a visit roughly 20 years earlier when the building still had an outdoor staircase leading to an upstairs residence — a vestige of Showa-era Hoppy-dori that has largely vanished as the surrounding blocks were rebuilt.

cultural_comfort.note: Cash-only, walk-in-only Shitamachi tavern with no confirmed English menu; one visitor account describes being brushed off when asking for an English menu, so English-speaking first-timers will likely be more comfortable with a translation app or local companion.

places.name: 二天門やぶ (Nitenmon Yabu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free preparation were mentioned in any available source. As a conventional soba shop, the noodles are very likely a standard wheat-blended (tsunagi) soba rather than 100% buckwheat (jūwari), and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) and other dishes would be expected to use ordinary wheat-based soy sauce and bonito dashi — but this restaurant-specific detail was not directly confirmed in sourced material. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not consider this a safe option without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or accommodations were mentioned in any available source. The shop's signature dish is a pork cutlet rice bowl (katsudon), and as a conventional soba/udon shop its broths and tsuyu almost certainly rely on standard bonito (katsuo) dashi throughout the menu. No confirmation of a kombu-only or vegan dashi option was found.
places.hours_raw: Most listings show 11:00–19:30 daily; at least one source lists 11:00–17:00. Closed Thursdays and New Year's Day per most sources; one source instead lists irregular (不定休) closures. Phone: 03-3841-8419.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (in front of Sensōji's Nitenmon Gate)
places.notes: Nitenmon Yabu is a long-established (老舗) soba shop that has stood at the Nitenmon Gate approach to Sensōji since 1954. The shop underwent a renovation (reported around 2017) that gave it a bright, modern concrete-and-glass exterior, though the interior is described as retaining t · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — founded 1954, directly in front of Sensōji's Nitenmon (Two Deva Kings) Gate on the temple's east side
chef_bio.background: Nitenmon Yabu is a long-established (老舗) soba shop that has stood at the Nitenmon Gate approach to Sensōji since 1954. The shop underwent a renovation (reported around 2017) that gave it a bright, modern concrete-and-glass exterior, though the interior is described as retaining the calm, unpretentious feel typical of an old Shitamachi (downtown) noodle shop.
chef_bio.specialty: Katsudon (pork cutlet over rice) — among Asakusa's soba-shop-style rice bowls, local food writers repeat the saying that for tempura rice bowl (tendon) you go to Owariya, and for katsudon you go to Nitenmon Yabu. The menu otherwise covers a standard range of soba, udon, donburi and drinking snacks.

anecdotes.text: Among Asakusa's soba-shop rice bowls, the local saying goes: for tendon (tempura rice bowl) it's Owariya, for katsudon it's Nitenmon Yabu — a nod to how well-regarded this shop's pork cutlet bowl is among old-school Asakusa soba restaurants.

The shop sits just east of Sensōji, right in front of the Nitenmon Gate, and was founded in 1954; despite a modern renovation with bare concrete walls, stepping inside reveals the calm atmosphere typical of a downtown (shitamachi) soba shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, traditional neighborhood soba shop with no confirmed English menu; although it sits right at a major tourist landmark (Sensōji's Nitenmon Gate), ordering is likely easiest with a translation app or by pointing at photos.

places.name: 豆徳 浅草店 (Mametoku Asakusa-ten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer, or celiac-specific information was found in any source. As a traditional mamegashi (bean/nut confectionery) shop, some products are likely flavored or coated with soy sauce (shōyu, which in Japan typically contains wheat) or other wheat-based seasonings, and shared equipment across the 80+ item lineup is likely. Plain roasted beans may be lower-risk, but nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without asking staff and checking individual package ingredient labels — not recommended for celiac-level strictness without on-site verification.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan certification or labeling was found. Many plain roasted bean and nut items are inherently plant-based, but the broader 80+ item lineup (including sweetened, chocolate-coated, or novelty items like the MAMERON BAR) may contain honey, dairy (milk chocolate), or other animal-derived ingredients, and traditional Japanese confections can hide bonito/katsuo-derived seasonings. Vegans should check individual ingredient labels or ask staff per item rather than assume the whole product line is vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–18:00, no fixed closed days (年中無休)
places.neighborhood: 浅草（東武・都営・メトロ）／浅草新仲見世商店街, near Sensō-ji Kaminarimon
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mametoku traces back to 1869 (Meiji 2), when the Tokunaga family began in Fukuyama, Hiroshima as a grain and dried-goods merchant. The business shifted early on to focus on roasted bean snacks, and the shop took the name "Mametoku" (豆徳), combining mame (beans) with the Tokunaga f · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: confectionery maker (mamegashi / roasted bean & nut snacks)
chef_bio.origin: Fukuyama, Hiroshima Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Mametoku traces back to 1869 (Meiji 2), when the Tokunaga family began in Fukuyama, Hiroshima as a grain and dried-goods merchant. The business shifted early on to focus on roasted bean snacks, and the shop took the name "Mametoku" (豆徳), combining mame (beans) with the Tokunaga family name of its founder, Tokunaga Shintarō. The Asakusa branch is one of several Mametoku locations run by the parent company, Tokunaga Seika Co. , Ltd. , and opened on December 21, 2020, in the Asakusa Shin-Nakamise shopping arcade, about a 3-minute walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon (Thunder Gate).
chef_bio.philosophy: The company describes finishing its bean and nut confections by hand rather than relying solely on automated production, aiming to offer simple, everyday bean-based treats as part of a healthy diet, while continually developing new flavors alongside its traditional recipes.
chef_bio.specialty: Takezumi Mame (bamboo-charcoal-coated beans), developed in 2002, is the brand's longtime signature item. The Asakusa store additionally sells an exclusive item called "MAMERON BAR," a stick-shaped wafer topped with colorful roasted nuts.

anecdotes.text: 豆徳浅草店opened December 21, 2020, roughly a 3-minute walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon (Raijin) Gate, inside the Asakusa Shin-Nakamise arcade, and is known locally for an Asakusa-exclusive item, the colorful nut-topped "MAMERON BAR."

The Mametoku name and business history date to 1869 in Fukuyama, Hiroshima, where the company (Tokunaga Seika Co., Ltd.) began as a grain merchant before specializing in bean confections; the shop name derives from founder Tokunaga Shintarō's surname combined with "mame" (beans).

cultural_comfort.note: It's a simple walk-up snack counter in a tourist-heavy shopping arcade — pointing at packaged bean and nut snacks and paying works fine even without Japanese, though staff may not speak much English.

places.name: 紅鶴 (Benizuru)
places.gf_detail: The signature pancakes are made with rice flour (米粉) and kibi sugar instead of wheat flour and granulated sugar — this is the shop's core differentiator and is confirmed across many independent sources. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, equipment, or wheat-free certification was found in any source; the menu also includes items such as waffles whose flour was not confirmed wheat-free, and sauces/toppings (hollandaise, marmalade) have unconfirmed ingredients. Treat this as "rice-flour-forward" rather than allergy-certified, and confirm cross-contact directly with staff if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly: the signature dishes are built around bacon, fried eggs, hollandaise sauce (butter and egg yolk based), and honey as the standard sweetener across the pancake menu. No vegan alternative, plant-based substitution, or vegan-labeled item was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: Reported hours vary by source: some list 9:30-17:00, others 10:00-17:00, with same-day numbered queue tickets (整理券) distributed as early as 7:00-8:30am for the roughly 10am opening. The kitchen commonly sells out and closes before the nominal end time. Closed days are described as irregular (不定休) by most sources, though one blog specifies Wednesdays — unconfirmed.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa, 2 min walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Stati
places.notes: The signature pancakes are made with rice flour (米粉) and kibi sugar instead of wheat flour and granulated sugar — this is the shop's core differentiator and is confirmed across many independent sources. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, equipment, or wheat-free certifica · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Before it was 紅鶴, this counter was a kissaten (old-style coffee shop) called "フラミンゴ" (Flamingo), run by the current owner's family in Asakusa for more than 50 years. In 2017 the owner, Makita-san, renewed the shop and renamed it 紅鶴 — which is simply the Japanese kanji word for "flamingo" — carrying the old shop's identity into its new pancake-focused form.
chef_bio.philosophy: Makita-san builds the pancakes around rice flour and kibi (raw cane) sugar instead of wheat flour and granulated sugar, and tops them with honey rather than maple syrup, aiming for a lighter batter that doesn't sit heavy in the stomach — his stated goal is a pancake that men, older customers and children can all enjoy comfortably, not just a dessert-cafe crowd.
chef_bio.specialty: Bacon and Fried Egg Pancake (ベーコンと目玉焼きのパンケーキ) — a three-layer soufflé-style rice-flour pancake stacked with honey, marmalade and bacon, topped with a fried egg and a generous pour of hollandaise sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name and premise trace directly back to the owner's parents' decades-old kissaten "Flamingo," which he reopened as 紅鶴 (its kanji equivalent) in 2017 when he pivoted the menu to rice-flour pancakes.

The cafe drew a wave of new attention after being featured on the TV program 沸騰ワード10 in September 2025, with reports (and rumor) that Justin Bieber had eaten there alongside a long list of visiting Japanese celebrities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: FindMeGlutenFree listing: Benitsuru is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease; contact the establishment directly to inquire about safety precautions. No celiac-grade positive reviews present.
Waffles (ワッフル) are offered as a separate walk-in item; sources describe them without confirming rice flour, leaving wheat waffles as a plausible cross-contact source in the same small kitchen.

Allergen guidance: rice-flour products in Japan frequently contain blended wheat, Japan has no cross-contamination labeling obligation, and such products are often processed in rooms that also handle wheat — so 'made with rice flour' does not equal celiac-safe.

Signature dishes are built on bacon, fried egg, hollandaise sauce, marmalade and honey (e.g. 'Bacon and Fried Egg' ¥2,200 with honey, marmalade, bacon and hollandaise), with no vegan alternative — confirming vegan = no.

positives.text: The pancake batter itself is made with rice flour and kibi (raw cane) sugar rather than wheat flour and granulated sugar — described by the owner as a deliberate choice to make a lighter pancake that's easier to digest.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu was confirmed and there is no phone/online booking — you must queue in person for a same-day numbered ticket, a distinctly Japanese-style system that is nonetheless well documented in English-language travel blogs and manageable if you arrive early and follow the queue etiquette.

places.name: しゃぶしゃぶ温野菜 浅草店 (Shabu-shabu On'yasai Asakusa-ten (Shabu Shab)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or accommodation was found. The house dashi broths are the medium everything is cooked in, and the signature "Sandaime Kiwami Dashi" is explicitly built with soy sauce (a wheat-based white soy sauce), so wheat is present in the shared cooking liquid itself, not just in a separate dipping sauce — meaning even plain meat/vegetables cooked in it pick up gluten. A chain-wide allergen reference sheet is reportedly available on request, but shared pots and cooking equipment mean cross-contact is essentially unavoidable in the all-you-can-eat format. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, specific confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The core menu is all-you-can-eat meat and vegetable shabu-shabu; the named default broths are fish-stock based (sardine, soda-bonito, mackerel), and even the soy-milk broth is finished with bonito and kombu dashi. A plain kombu (kelp-only) broth reportedly exists among the broth choices, which could in principle avoid animal-derived dashi, but no confirmed fully plant-based tare/dipping sauce or vegan protocol was found — a vegan diner would need to ask in detail and likely couldn't rely on any of the signature broths.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草駅周辺）
places.cuisine: 野菜しゃぶしゃぶ・野菜鍋
places.notes: Onyasai built its identity around vegetable-forward shabu-shabu: an all-you-can-eat format pairing thinly sliced meats with a large rotating selection of domestic vegetables, centered on a lineup of proprietary house-made dashi broths rather than a single generic soup base. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: National restaurant chain operated by Reins International (part of the Colowide group) — this is one branch (Asakusa) of the nationwide "Onyasai" vegetable shabu-shabu chain, not an independent or chef-owned establishment.
chef_bio.background: Onyasai built its identity around vegetable-forward shabu-shabu: an all-you-can-eat format pairing thinly sliced meats with a large rotating selection of domestic vegetables, centered on a lineup of proprietary house-made dashi broths rather than a single generic soup base.
chef_bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat vegetable and meat shabu-shabu with a choice of signature broths, most notably the "Sandaime Kiwami Dashi" (三代目極みだし), a seafood-based broth blending sardine, soda-bonito and mackerel with a white soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: For its 25th anniversary, the chain added two new proprietary dashi options to its grand menu — "Umi no Megumi Dashi" (a citrus-and-seafood broth) and "Awa-iro Ushio Dashi" (a sea bream and sea-salt broth) — continuing its broth-first approach to vegetable shabu-shabu.
The chain's signature broth, Sandaime Kiwami Dashi, is built from a fish-stock base of iwashi (sardine), soda-katsuo and saba (mackerel) finished with a premium white soy sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The all-you-can-eat shabu-shabu format uses shared pots and cooking/washing equipment across meat and vegetable items, creating cross-contact risk for allergen-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chain's signature "Sandaime Kiwami Dashi" broth is built around a white soy sauce blended with sardine, soda-bonito and mackerel stock — soy sauce (and thus wheat) is in the base cooking liquid itself, not only in a side dipping sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The default/signature broths are fish-stock based (sardine, soda-katsuo, mackerel), and even the soy-milk broth option is cut with bonito and kombu dashi, so no broth on the standard menu is confirmed vegan; the restaurant's core product is all-you-can-eat meat shabu-shabu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff can reportedly provide a printed 7-major-allergen reference sheet on request, but the chain notes shared cooking/washing equipment means cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.

positives.text: The chain is built around a large, frequently-rotating selection of domestic (国産) vegetables served alongside meat in an all-you-can-eat format, with several distinct proprietary broths to choose from.

cultural_comfort.note: A mainstream Japanese chain restaurant with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its central Asakusa location (2 minutes from Asakusa Station) and an English Tabelog listing suggest some familiarity with tourists.

places.name: 黒毛和牛 壱乃屋 浅草 (Kuroge Wagyu Ichinoya Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: The dish's signature "warishita" sauce is homemade from soy sauce (shoyu) and mirin — both typically contain wheat — and no gluten-free sauce alternative, dedicated fryer, or allergen-free protocol is mentioned anywhere. Because the menu is essentially one composed dish built on this sauce, there is no visible way to avoid wheat exposure. Treat as not safe for celiac/gluten-free diners without a direct, in-person conversation with staff (and even then, cross-contact risk in a tiny single-dish kitchen is likely high).
places.vegan_detail: This is a single-specialty wagyu beef restaurant built entirely around a rare-steak rice bowl finished with an egg yolk and a beef-based dashi broth (hitsumabushi-style pour). No vegetarian or vegan preparation, substitute, or plant-based version of the signature dish was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (near Kaminarimon Gate, 浅草駅徒歩1〜3分)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop's owner trained for eight years at a restaurant with roughly 260 years of history in Nihonbashi Ningyōchō before striking out to open their own place in Asakusa, wanting to share Japanese food culture and tradition through a single dish. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — opened December 2022
chef_bio.background: The shop's owner trained for eight years at a restaurant with roughly 260 years of history in Nihonbashi Ningyōchō before striking out to open their own place in Asakusa, wanting to share Japanese food culture and tradition through a single dish.
chef_bio.philosophy: Positioned as an evolution of Edo-period cuisine reimagined as a modern rice-bowl format, aiming to carry Japanese culinary tradition from Asakusa out into the world.
chef_bio.specialty: 黒毛和牛レアステーキ重 (Premium Rare Wagyu Steak Rice Bowl) — thin-sliced rare wagyu over rice, described as 'three flavors in one meal'.

anecdotes.text: Diners are guided through a three-stage way of eating the bowl: first plain to taste the meat and sauce, then mixed with a rich egg yolk, and finally finished hitsumabushi-style by pouring a beef dashi broth with condiments over the rice.
The shop has drawn continuous daily lines since its December 2022 opening and has been featured on Japanese gourmet TV (e.g. Hirunandesu!, Oct 2024).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house 'warishita' sauce is homemade from soy sauce and mirin — no gluten-free or wheat-free version is mentioned in any source found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu centers on wagyu beef, egg yolk, and a beef-based dashi broth; no vegan, vegetarian, or plant-based option was found in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu and service are Japanese-first with a queue-based, single-dish walk-in format typical of a small Asakusa specialty shop; expect to point/order simply rather than have detailed English allergy conversations.

places.name: ジムジュム（JIMJUM／JIM JUM） (Jimjum (Jim Jum))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free process, allergy menu, or dedicated fryer was found in any source. Thai cooking here likely relies on soy sauce and other seasonings that can contain wheat, and dishes are cooked in a small home-style kitchen with shared equipment. Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering — nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. The restaurant's signature hot pot and many other dishes contain chicken and seafood, and Thai cooking commonly uses fish sauce and oyster sauce. Vegans/vegetarians should ask the staff directly about vegetable-only options and hidden animal-derived seasonings (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste) before ordering — nothing here is confirmed vegan-safe.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa) — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] JIMJUM is run single-handedly by a Thai proprietress (referred to by regulars as "mama"), who cooks each dish herself in a small, home-style setting on the second floor of a building near Asakusa Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Thailand
chef_bio.background: JIMJUM is run single-handedly by a Thai proprietress (referred to by regulars as "mama"), who cooks each dish herself in a small, home-style setting on the second floor of a building near Asakusa Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious pursuit of authentic, home-style Thai cooking — welcoming guests the way you'd welcome a friend into your home, and trying to keep prices reasonable for regulars even as food costs rise.
chef_bio.specialty: JIMJUM nabe — a Thai-style shabu-shabu hot pot built on a tom yum goong broth, packed with herbs, chicken, seafood and vegetables, marketed as a healthy, detoxifying dish.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has been ranked No.1 in the Thai Curry category of Japan's Curry Database for three consecutive years.
It's run by a single Thai proprietress working alone, described by visitors as a cozy, home-style Thai spot where "everything you eat is delicious" despite the minimal, small setting.
Even amid rising food prices, the restaurant has been noted for holding the line on its budget-friendly lunch pricing (lunches under 1,000 yen).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, one-woman-run neighborhood Thai spot in the heart of Asakusa — simple, friendly interactions are easy, but don't expect an English menu or extensive English-speaking staff.

places.name: わらび餅もとこ 浅草店 (Warabimochi Motoko - Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: Traditional warabi mochi (bracken-starch jelly coated in kinako soy flour, served with kuromitsu syrup) is typically wheat-free by nature, but no source found states this shop's specific recipe, whether any wheat-containing thickeners/starches are blended in, or gives any allergen/cross-contamination information. No mention of a dedicated fryer (this is not a fried item) or of soy-sauce use. Confirm directly with staff before relying on this for celiac-level safety.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific statement from the shop was found. Kinako (soy flour) and kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) used on plain warabi mochi are typically plant-based, but the shop's signature milk tea items are dairy-based (matcha/hōjicha/black tea milk teas), so those are not vegan as described unless a plant-milk substitute is confirmed in person. No information found on dashi/bonito use (unlikely relevant to a sweets shop, but unconfirmed).
places.hours_raw: 平日10:00-17:00、土日祝10:00-18:00（定休日：月曜）※現在リニューアル工事のため近隣の仮店舗（浅草2-7-22）で営業中
places.neighborhood: Asakusa Nishi-Sandō, 1 min walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Branch of a long-running Sugamo warabi-mochi specialty shop; opened its Asakusa location in December 2021 on the Nishi-Sandō street leading toward Sensō-ji. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Warabi mochi specialty shop (branch of the Sugamo original)
chef_bio.origin: Sugamo, Tokyo (original shop, 15+ years)
chef_bio.background: Branch of a long-running Sugamo warabi-mochi specialty shop; opened its Asakusa location in December 2021 on the Nishi-Sandō street leading toward Sensō-ji.
chef_bio.specialty: Warabi mochi milk tea (their original creation) and fresh boat-shaped warabi mochi for eating while walking

anecdotes.text: The original わらび餅もとこ shop has been a beloved fixture in Sugamo, Tokyo for over 15 years; the Asakusa branch opened in December 2021 on the Nishi-Sandō approach street just west of Sensō-ji, and quickly became known for lines of customers.

The shop is credited with originating the 'warabi mochi milk tea' drink — chewy warabi-mochi pearls served in a milk tea (matcha, hōjicha, or black tea base, plus seasonal fruit punch versions) — alongside boat-shaped (fune-gata) fresh warabi mochi sold for eating while strolling Nishi-Sandō.
As of mid-2026 the Asakusa shop is operating from a temporary spot about one minute's walk from its usual address while its original location undergoes renovation.

cultural_comfort.note: It's a simple walk-up takeout/eat-in sweets counter near Sensō-ji with a picture-friendly menu of a handful of items, so pointing and ordering works fine even without Japanese, though menus and any staff conversation are likely Japanese-only.

places.name: 居酒屋 轟 浅草駅前店 (Izakaya Todoroki Asakusa Ekimae-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free option, or allergen labeling was found on the official site or any listing/review site searched (official site, Tabelog, Hot Pepper Gourmet, Jalan, Hitosara). As a standard izakaya it very likely uses wheat-containing soy sauce and tare marinades, and offers fried/tempura-style items; no information on dedicated fryers or cross-contamination practices was found. Celiac-safe dining here cannot be confirmed and should not be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian/vegan-friendly restaurant. Its "vegetable" skewers (野菜巻き串 — lettuce, Hakata scallion, lotus root, asparagus, tomato) are all wrapped in thin pork belly, not plant-only. The menu otherwise centers on yakitori, horse sashimi, and motsu nabe (animal offal hot pot). No plant-based dashi/broth or dedicated vegan dishes were found in any source searched.
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草駅前）
places.notes: This Asakusa branch is one of several shops using the 轟 (Todoroki) name operated by a small regional izakaya company (株式会社絆/Kizuna Co.), which runs sister Hakata- and grilled-food izakaya in other Japanese cities. The Asakusa location sits essentially at the foot of Tokyo Metro A · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Hakata-style izakaya, part of a small regional restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Hakata (Fukuoka) cuisine transplanted to Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: This Asakusa branch is one of several shops using the 轟 (Todoroki) name operated by a small regional izakaya company (株式会社絆/Kizuna Co. ), which runs sister Hakata- and grilled-food izakaya in other Japanese cities. The Asakusa location sits essentially at the foot of Tokyo Metro Asakusa Station's Ginza Line exit and focuses on Hakata home-style dishes: yakitori, horse sashimi, and its signature motsu nabe.
chef_bio.specialty: Hakata-style motsu nabe (beef/pork offal hot pot) with a collagen-rich broth

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's vegetable-wrapped skewers (a Hakata specialty in which thin pork is wrapped around a vegetable) use only domestically grown Japanese vegetables, hand-wrapped in the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-language neighborhood izakaya (net-reservation via Hot Pepper Gourmet) with no confirmed English menu or allergy-specific staff training, so non-Japanese-speaking diners with dietary restrictions should confirm ingredients in Japanese or bring a translation aid before ordering.

places.name: 点心爛漫 (Tenshin Ranman)
places.gf_detail: Every dish described in available coverage — pan-fried gyoza, shumai, xiaolongbao, steamed buns — uses standard wheat-flour wrappers, and no gluten-free substitution, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Treat this as not viable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners; there is no indication staff are set up to prevent cross-contact even if you tried to order around the wrappers.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers entirely on pork, shrimp and crab dim sum and gyoza; no vegetarian or plant-based dish is mentioned in any review, blog, or listing found. Not a realistic option for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict. Some directory listings (Ekiten, an Asakusa tourism guide) give regular daytime-into-evening hours around 10:30/11:30–21:30 with either Wednesday or Monday as a weekly closed day. But independent narrative sources — dancyu's Asakusa lunch column, a Gurunavi feature, and a blogger's December 2024 visit report — consistently describe it as a weekend/holiday-focused operation that also opens irregularly on some weekday evenings (Tuesdays/Thursdays reported) when a second cook comes in. No single authoritative current hours could be confirmed.
places.neighborhood: 西浅草 (Nishi-Asakusa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A closet-sized dim sum counter that opened in 2010 in a Nishi-Asakusa backstreet near the Kappabashi kitchenware district, a few minutes' walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station. It's run out of a single storefront window with steam baskets fogging the glass, mostly by one owne · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: dim sum cook (originally brought from China)
occasional second cook, a former washoku chef, on some weekday evenings
chef_bio.background: A closet-sized dim sum counter that opened in 2010 in a Nishi-Asakusa backstreet near the Kappabashi kitchenware district, a few minutes' walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station. It's run out of a single storefront window with steam baskets fogging the glass, mostly by one owner at a five-stool counter, with a dim sum cook originally brought over from China making the dumplings.
chef_bio.philosophy: There's no fixed script here — write-ups describe an 'omakase' course that changes visit to visit depending on who's cooking and what's fresh, rather than a set menu recited the same way every time.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-skinned pan-fried gyoza packed with cabbage, chives and pork, alongside shrimp and crab shumai, xiaolongbao and other bamboo-steamer dim sum, most of it priced around a flat 500 yen a plate.

anecdotes.text: A December 2024 blog visit found the tiny five-seat counter tucked in a backstreet near Kappabashi, steam rising from the shopfront window, serving thick-skinned pan-fried gyoza, shrimp-and-crab shumai and soup dumplings.

A Gurunavi feature on the shop's roughly 2,000-yen omakase course described a running joke among regulars: the more times you visit, the fewer dim sum dishes show up, because a second cook — a former Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef who comes in some weekday evenings — keeps sneaking in seasonal, non-dim-sum dishes like simmered daikon with yellowtail and curry pork buns.

dancyu's 'Asakusa Lunch Guide' column ranked it 99th on a 100-spot list of the writer's favorite Asakusa lunches, describing a cramped five-stool room where dim sum is made by a chef the owner brought over from China, the shop having opened in 2010.

A gyoza-focused blog highlighted the lunch set — four pan-fried gyoza plus two shumai with rice and soup — priced at a flat 500 yen, calling out the unusually large amount of filling packed into each dumpling.

cultural_comfort.note: This reads as an old-school, Japanese-only backstreet counter with no evidence of English menus, picture menus, or foreign-visitor signage in any of the coverage found — come with at least basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: 浅草 生田庵（いくたあん） (Asakusa Ikutaan)
places.gf_detail: This shop is built entirely around housemade wheat noodles in a soy-sauce-based broth. No gluten-free noodle substitute, allergy menu, or any gluten-free accommodation was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a rich animal-based stock (pork/chicken) topped with a layer of rendered lard, and the signature dishes are served with pork chashu. No vegetarian or vegan menu item was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (Mon–Fri) and 10:00–21:00 (Sat, Sun, holidays) per one aggregated listing; other directories instead list a flat 11:00–23:00 and/or note irregular (不定休) closures. Sources disagree, so treat exact hours as approximate and confirm by phone if possible.
places.neighborhood: 花川戸 (Hanakawado), Asakusa, Taito-ku — adjacent to Tobu Asaku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 生田庵 opened in February 2021 on Umamichi-dori near its intersection with Denpo-in-dori, a very short walk from Tobu Asakusa Station and the Kaminarimon/Sensoji area. It is a small, independently run counter-only shop rather than a chain. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 生田庵 opened in February 2021 on Umamichi-dori near its intersection with Denpo-in-dori, a very short walk from Tobu Asakusa Station and the Kaminarimon/Sensoji area. It is a small, independently run counter-only shop rather than a chain.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner has spoken of wanting to make it 'the best ramen shop in Asakusa' — the stated approach is to avoid gimmicks or showiness and instead stick to basic technique and orthodox, straightforward ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: 中華そば (chuka-soba) — a soy-sauce-based ramen topped with a cap of rendered lard that keeps the broth hot throughout the meal, hand-sliced tender chashu pork, and housemade noodles; the shop also offers a spicy version and a cold dipping-noodle (tsukemen/mori-chuka) style.

anecdotes.text: The soup carries a layer of lard on its surface that keeps it hot from the first sip to the last, with each mouthful delivering salt, broth depth, and richness of fat together.
The owner has described the goal of becoming 'the best ramen shop in Asakusa,' keeping the shop unpretentious and built on simple, orthodox ingredients rather than novelty.

cultural_comfort.note: Order via a Japanese-only ticket vending machine at the entrance, typical of a small neighborhood ramen counter — no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff was found, so basic Japanese or pointing at the machine's photos/labels will help.

places.name: 海鮮丼乙ASAKUSA (Kaisendon Otsu ASAKUSA)
places.gf_detail: Bowls are rice topped with raw seafood (tuna, salmon, scallop, sea urchin), which is naturally free of gluten as a base, but tuna is commonly served "zuke" (soy-marinated) in this style of shop and soy sauce is typically provided alongside. No gluten-free soy sauce, allergy menu, or other GF accommodation was found in any available source, so celiac diners should ask staff directly about the marinade and condiments before ordering; no tempura or fried items appear on the menu, so there is no fryer cross-contact concern identified.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-based donburi shop built entirely around raw fish and shellfish (tuna, scallop, sea urchin, etc.); no vegetable-only, plant-based, or vegan menu items were found in any source, and the format of the restaurant makes a vegan option unlikely.
places.hours_raw: 水〜日・祝日 11:00〜19:30（売り切れ次第閉店）、月・火定休
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Tsukuba Express Station)
places.notes: Bowls are rice topped with raw seafood (tuna, salmon, scallop, sea urchin), which is naturally free of gluten as a base, but tuna is commonly served "zuke" (soy-marinated) in this style of shop and soy sauce is typically provided alongside. No gluten-free soy sauce, allergy menu, · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.specialty: Build-your-own kaisendon (seafood rice bowl) served in three set tiers — Ume, Take, and Matsu — topped with scallop, tuna (including toro), and sea urchin.

cultural_comfort.note: No English signage, menu, or official English website was found; ordering is likely Japanese-only, though the shop is listed on Uber Eats/Demae-can delivery apps which can help non-Japanese-speaking visitors.

places.name: 浅草むぎとろ 本店 (Asakusa Mugitoro (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was found in any available source. This is a traditional Japanese restaurant relying on conventional soy sauce and dashi seasonings and offering tempura-style dishes on some menus, so wheat exposure and cross-contamination should be assumed likely. Diners with celiac disease or severe wheat allergy should not treat this as a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant's signature seasoning is a secret dashi (broth) based on bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and kelp, used since the 1929 founding, so the core mugitoro dish and much of the kaiseki menu are not vegan. No vegan-specific menu, substitutions, or plant-based dashi option were found in available sources.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon, by the Sumida River, near Sensō-ji)
places.notes: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he i

chef_bio.background: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he is said to have traveled the country searching for the best mountain yam before settling on a strain and opening a shop built entirely around tororo (grated yam). The restaurant has been run by the same family and stayed on essentially the same stretch of riverbank for nearly a century, expanding over the decades into a multi-floor kaiseki restaurant while keeping the original tororo recipe at its core.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's founding wish was simply 'we want everyone to be able to eat one bowl of mugitoro' — a focus on making a single humble, health-associated dish available to ordinary diners, which still shapes the menu today.
chef_bio.specialty: Mugitoro (麦とろ) — barley-mixed rice topped with tororo (grated Japanese mountain yam, made from a strong, low-astringency yamato-imo), seasoned with a secret bonito-and-kelp dashi recipe passed down since the 1929 founding. The restaurant also serves broader tororo-kaiseki courses and a 'guro-don' (fatty tuna) rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Founder Tazō Nakajima worked at a produce company and, wanting to go into a food business of his own, is said to have traveled the country before finding what he considered the finest mountain yam — the discovery that became the basis for the restaurant.
The shop hung its noren for the first time on June 10, 1929, at the foot of the Sumida River, with the founding wish that 'everyone could eat a single bowl of mugitoro.'

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, high-traffic tourist destination steps from Sensō-ji with an English-language site/menu and English-language press coverage, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate even without Japanese.

places.name: 悦納 (injoy) (Etsunō (Injoy))
places.gf_detail: No source found describes a dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu labeling. The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by default. Soy sauce/tamari wheat content and cross-contamination handling were not documented in any source reviewed — celiac or strict-GF diners should call ahead and confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: Consistently described across multiple independent sources as a fully vegan shōjin-ryori kitchen: no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs, and no "five pungent" alliums (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion) per Buddhist dietary tradition. Broths appear to be vegetable-based rather than fish/bonito-based, per reviewer commentary on the noodle dishes. No katsuo/dashi exceptions were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-20:30 (L.O. 19:30), Monday and Wednesday-Sunday; closed Tuesdays. Lunch is walk-in; dinner is reservation-only, requested by 3:00pm the previous day.
places.neighborhood: Taito-ku, Asakusa (Hanakawado), directly by Asakusa Station
places.notes: No source found describes a dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu labeling. The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by defaul

chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames shōjin ryori as drawing out the umami, nutritional value and beauty already present in each plant-based ingredient, with a stated ethos that nothing should be wasted — down to a single grain of rice or drop of juice.
chef_bio.specialty: Oriental vegan shōjin ryori — recurring signature dishes mentioned across reviews include dandan (tantan) noodles, a vegetable-broth noodle soup, ramen, bibimbap, and a lion's mane mushroom dish

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has hosted seasonal, hands-on events such as an Oriental-vegan chimaki (rice dumpling) making experience, and prepared a fully vegan osechi (Japanese New Year) box for 2023.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is described as excluding all animal-derived ingredients as well as the Buddhist "five pungent roots" (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion), consistent with strict shōjin-style plant-based cooking.

positives.text: Menu and site descriptions consistently state the food uses no animal products at all (fully vegan shōjin ryori), not just a vegetarian subset of dishes.
A reviewer noted the noodle-dish broth was vegetable-only yet tasted deeply umami, suggesting deliberate care in building flavor without fish-based dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations (required for dinner) are made by phone, and no confirmed English menu or English-speaking-staff claim was found, though the restaurant is covered favorably by several English-language vegan/travel sites.

places.name: 蕎上人（駒形蕎上人） (Sobashonin (Komagata Sobashonin))
places.gf_detail: Sources confirm the buckwheat itself is sourced 100% domestically, from high-elevation Aizu (Fukushima) fields, and is stone-milled in-house, but none specify the buckwheat-to-wheat ratio of the actual noodles (i.e., whether they are juwari/100%-buckwheat or a standard nihachi blend that uses wheat as a binder). The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is the traditional kaeshi-and-bonito-dashi type; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or dedicated fryer was mentioned in any source. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the flour blend and sauce ingredients before ordering — this shop does not market itself as gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The broths are built on traditional katsuobushi (bonito) dashi and kaeshi, and signature dishes include duck (鴨南そば). No vegetarian or vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or meat-free tsuyu option was mentioned in any available source — standard dishes here are not vegan-safe.
places.hours_raw: Lunch approx. 11:30-14:00/14:30, Dinner approx. 17:00-20:00/20:30 (exact close times vary slightly between sources); closed Mondays and the 2nd & 4th Sunday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Komagata, near Komagata Bridge along the Sumida Ri
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1989 as "Asakusa Issa-an," the shop was renamed "Komagata Sobashonin" in October 1997. The owner, Hiranuma Takayuki, trained under Katakura Yasuo, a noted postwar soba craftsman credited with establishing hand-cut, thin-sliced specialty soba as a standard of the trade.  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: owner/soba craftsman: Hiranuma Takayuki (name spelling per secondary sources; not independently confirmed via primary site)
chef_bio.background: Opened in 1989 as "Asakusa Issa-an," the shop was renamed "Komagata Sobashonin" in October 1997. The owner, Hiranuma Takayuki, trained under Katakura Yasuo, a noted postwar soba craftsman credited with establishing hand-cut, thin-sliced specialty soba as a standard of the trade. The shop also runs its own soba- and udon-making school, whose 800-plus graduates have gone on to open their own shops.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows three principles for good soba: freshly milled flour, freshly made noodles, freshly boiled water. Buckwheat is sourced from high-elevation (700m+) fields in the Aizu region of Fukushima Prefecture and stone-milled in-house on five large mills, ground slowly to preserve aroma and flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Five-color soba (五色そば) — a tasting set of five hand-cut noodle varieties (plain/seiro, rustic/inaka, poppy-seed/keshi-kiri, lemon/lemon-kiri, and tea/cha-kiri) — and duck nanban soba (鴨南そば) served in a yuzu-scented duck broth.

anecdotes.text: About ten rotating varieties of hand-cut soba are made daily, including seiro, inaka (rustic), lemon-kiri, keshi-kiri (poppy seed), shiso-kiri, and goma-kiri (sesame).
The duck broth in the kamo-nanban soba carries a yuzu fragrance, balancing the sweetness of long green onion and duck against a carefully judged kaeshi-and-dashi blend.
Buckwheat flour comes from Aizu, Fukushima, from fields above 700m elevation, and is stone-ground in-house.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba is served with a traditional kaeshi (soy-sauce-based tare) and bonito dashi; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative was mentioned in any available source.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood soba craftsman's shop; no English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found in any source, so a few basic Japanese food words will help when ordering.

places.name: かき氷処 浅草氷室堂 浅草本店 (Asakusa Himurodo (Kakigori Shop) – Asakusa M)
places.gf_detail: No source discusses gluten-free preparation directly. The base shaved ice and homemade fruit syrups are plausibly wheat-free, but no source confirms whether any syrup or topping contains wheat-derived ingredients, and toppings like the mitarashi-dango version use a soy-sauce-based glaze — soy sauce in Japan is very often wheat-based unless specified otherwise, and this was not confirmed either way here. No information on shared equipment or cross-contamination practices was found. Ask staff directly about specific menu items before ordering if celiac.
places.vegan_detail: No source specifically addresses vegan suitability. Fruit-based shaved ice syrups are often plant-based, but no source confirms the absence of dairy (e.g., condensed milk) in the base syrups or toppings, and menu items such as the mitarashi-dango kakigori use a glaze whose exact ingredients (potential mirin/dashi) are unconfirmed. Ask staff about specific items.
places.neighborhood: 浅草三丁目
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Asakusa Himurodo's Asakusa main branch opened in May 2023 in the Crest Asakusa building, a short walk from Asakusa Station. It is a dedicated kakigori (shaved ice) shop built around ice quality: the shop uses carefully selected, high-purity ice that is prized for melting almost i · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Asakusa Himurodo's Asakusa main branch opened in May 2023 in the Crest Asakusa building, a short walk from Asakusa Station. It is a dedicated kakigori (shaved ice) shop built around ice quality: the shop uses carefully selected, high-purity ice that is prized for melting almost instantly, giving the dessert what fans describe as a roughly "three-minute" window of peak texture before it's meant to be eaten.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on the purity of the ice itself and on natural, additive-free syrups made in-house, with menus that shift with the seasons to showcase fruit at its best.
chef_bio.specialty: Shaved ice topped with handmade syrups made without artificial colors, flavors, or sweeteners, built around seasonal fruit. Standouts include the fresh-strawberry kakigori and an Asakusa-location-exclusive ripe sweet potato (mitsuimo) kakigori, plus a version topped with real mitarashi dango.

anecdotes.text: The shop's ice is so fine and high-purity that it is said to have only about a three-minute window before it loses its ideal texture, a point of pride highlighted in visitor write-ups.
The Asakusa main store has a location-exclusive menu item, a fully ripened sweet potato (mitsuimo) shaved ice, alongside standards like fresh strawberry and a mitarashi-dango-topped version.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking staff was confirmed in any source, though the shop sits in heavily-touristed Asakusa where some picture-menu/gesture communication is typical.

places.name: 駒形どぜう 本店 (Komagata Dozeu (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found in any available source. The signature dojō-nabe and related dishes are cooked in a shōyu-based broth; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed from wheat, so the broth and most seasoned dishes should be assumed to contain gluten. No online allergy menu exists (Japanese-language only, walk-in service), so anything beyond plain grilled items would need in-person confirmation with staff, which is not guaranteed to be in English. Not recommended for celiac diners without that direct, cautious confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: This is a single-protein specialty restaurant built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and related fish dishes cooked in fish/animal-based broth; no vegetarian or vegan dish was found in any menu source. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Komagata, Taitō-ku (Asakusa)
places.notes: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisi

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Edo/Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisine ever since and still operates from a building in the Edo-period 'dashigeta-zukuri' merchant-house style.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving Edo-period dining customs and atmosphere alongside the food itself — low tatami seating, unchanged recipes, and a building whose upper floor famously has no street-facing windows.
chef_bio.specialty: Dojō-nabe — whole pond loach simmered tableside in a shallow iron pot in a shōyu-based broth, the dish the restaurant is named for.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's distinctive four-character spelling 'どぜう' (instead of the standard five-character 'どぢやう') dates to a 1806 fire that destroyed the original shop. Considered the five-character spelling to be bad luck, the founder had a well-known signboard calligrapher write it as four characters instead; the shop's renewed popularity led other loach restaurants across Edo to copy the spelling.

During the Edo period the shogunate discouraged eating four-legged animals, so loach became an important source of animal protein for ordinary townspeople — a role the restaurant is remembered for supporting.

The main hall keeps its Edo-era merchant-house construction, and the street-facing second floor has no windows — traditionally explained as a way to avoid appearing to look down on a passing daimyo's procession.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dojō-nabe broth is a shōyu-based warishita typical of this dish style; conventional Japanese soy sauce is wheat-brewed, so the broth is not inherently gluten-free. This is general knowledge about the dish type, not a restaurant-specific confirmed statement.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and other fish-based items prepared in a shared kitchen; no vegetarian or vegan dish was identified in any menu listing found online.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: おばんざい味ゆぅ (Obanzai Miyuu (Aji-Yuu))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The kitchen uses homemade miso and traditional seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi (which can contain wheat and bonito) are likely used unless the vegetable-forward dishes happen to avoid them. Because the menu changes daily and nothing about wheat-free preparation is documented, celiac diners should call ahead (050-5869-7791) and confirm which of the day's dishes are safe.
places.vegan_detail: No confirmed all-vegan menu. The obanzai are vegetable-heavy and change daily, but obanzai izakaya menus typically also include fish- or meat-based items and traditional dashi (often bonito-based), and no source confirms a katsuo/dashi-free or fully plant-based option. Vegan diners should ask staff which of the day's dishes are free of fish stock, bonito flakes, and animal products.
places.hours_raw: 16:00–23:00, closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, near Sensō-ji / Kaminarimon, along Kōen-dōri (Tsuku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Miyuu, the proprietress, opened this counter-only obanzai spot on Asakusa's Kōen-dōri in May 2026. She hand-cooks a daily-changing lineup of 5-8 obanzai (home-style Japanese side dishes), leaning on seasonal vegetables, homemade miso, and ingredients sourced from her home prefect

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Miyuu, the proprietress, opened this counter-only obanzai spot on Asakusa's Kōen-dōri in May 2026. She hand-cooks a daily-changing lineup of 5-8 obanzai (home-style Japanese side dishes), leaning on seasonal vegetables, homemade miso, and ingredients sourced from her home prefecture of Hiroshima.
chef_bio.specialty: A rotating selection of 5-8 handmade obanzai dishes, chosen by the guest from large plates set along the counter, capped off with the chef's-recommendation 'Aji-Yuu Mankitsu' course (obanzai, a signature dish, rice, dessert, and a drink).

anecdotes.text: Guests pick their dishes from large plates lined up on the counter — a self-selection obanzai format — with the 5-8 items on offer changing daily depending on what's in season.
Miyuu curates her sake list around breweries in Fukushima and Hiroshima that she came across through personal connections, pairing them with her home-style cooking.

positives.text: The daily menu emphasizes seasonal vegetables and homemade miso, giving it a naturally plant-leaning lean-up, though this has not been documented as a formal vegan or allergy-safe program.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter-only izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and phone-only reservations — most comfortable with a Japanese-speaking companion or guide rather than walking in solo without any Japanese.

places.name: しみいる（Simi:l） (Shimiiru (Simi:l))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or dedicated gluten-free preparation was found in any source. As a classic French bistro, sauces, roux, and bread service commonly involve wheat flour, and no information on cross-contamination handling or a dedicated fryer was available. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm case-by-case; do not assume any dish is safe without asking.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant emphasizes seasonal vegetables from small-scale producers, but it is a classic French bistro built around meat and Ofunato-sourced seafood, with butter- and cream-based techniques typical of the cuisine. No vegan menu or plant-based tasting course was found in any source, so vegan diners should contact the restaurant in advance to ask what can be adapted; do not assume dishes are free of animal-based stock, butter, or dairy.
places.neighborhood: 浅草・観音裏（Tsukuba Express 浅草駅エリア）
places.cuisine: フレンチ・ナチュラルワイン
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Couple-run bistro opened in 2016 in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreets. Before opening, the chef worked at a restaurant in Kagurazaka, and the couple say they were inspired toward an à la carte, drop-in dining style after visiting a restaurant in Ebisu around a decade before open · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 椿豪（つばき たけし）・椿澄子（つばき すみこ）夫妻
chef_bio.background: Couple-run bistro opened in 2016 in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreets. Before opening, the chef worked at a restaurant in Kagurazaka, and the couple say they were inspired toward an à la carte, drop-in dining style after visiting a restaurant in Ebisu around a decade before opening their own place.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name しみいる (from 沁み入る, "to seep in / soak in") reflects the aim of serving food and wine that quietly seep into the heart, in a calm, unhurried hideaway atmosphere down a narrow alley behind Sensō-ji.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal-vegetable-driven French cuisine paired with natural wine, using produce and meat from small-scale producers and seafood landed at Ofunato port.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is counted among what food writers call the "Kannon-ura French Four Heavenly Kings" (観音裏フレンチ四天王), a small cluster of well-regarded French bistros in this quiet Asakusa backstreet.

Featured by Asakusa High School teacher Kanbayashi-sensei in dancyu's "Asakusa Lunch Guide" series as an example of authentic French cooking in the Kannon-ura area, part of a project in which he ate lunch at 708 Asakusa restaurants.
Profile piece describing the couple's cooking as food that "seeps into every organ" (五臓六腑にしみいる), built on a deliberately à la carte menu to build repeat regulars.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, Japanese-language hideaway bistro down a narrow alley — English-language reservations are possible through platforms like TableCheck, but don't expect an English menu or much spoken English on site.

places.name: 雷門 三定（さんさだ） (Kaminarimon Sansada (Sansada))
places.gf_detail: Tempura batter follows the traditional Edo-style wheat-flour recipe, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat) — no gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific menu is documented on the official site or in press/tourism coverage. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct advance confirmation by phone (03-3841-3200), and even then a safe outcome is unlikely given the shared-oil, wheat-batter format.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on Edo-mae seafood tempura (kakiage, shrimp, fish) fried in shared sesame oil, and the traditional tentsuyu dipping sauce is typically dashi-based (often including bonito/katsuo). No vegan menu, vegetable-only tempura set, or dashi substitution is documented on the official site or in press coverage, so vegan and vegetarian diners would need to ask staff directly about ingredient substitutions and shared-fryer cross-contact.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, directly beside Kaminarimon Gate
places.notes: Sansada traces its origin to around 1831, when Sadakichi began selling Edo-mae fish and shellfish tempura from a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō. The business later moved to its present spot immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate in Asakusa and, founded in 1837 (Tenpō 8), is · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founded by Sadakichi (定吉), a native of Mikawa Province (present-day Aichi Prefecture) who came to Edo and first worked in the egg-distribution trade before turning to tempura
chef_bio.origin: Mikawa Province (present-day Aichi Prefecture) via Nihonbashi, Edo
chef_bio.background: Sansada traces its origin to around 1831, when Sadakichi began selling Edo-mae fish and shellfish tempura from a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō. The business later moved to its present spot immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate in Asakusa and, founded in 1837 (Tenpō 8), is considered the oldest continuously operating tempura restaurant in Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: A single-storefront philosophy — no branches, ever — built on nearly two centuries of frying Edo-mae tempura in sesame oil at the same spot beside Kaminarimon Gate.
chef_bio.specialty: Kakiage (mixed tempura of small Edo-mae fish and shellfish, offered in large/medium/small sizes) and the tendon rice bowl, both fried in aromatic sesame oil in the traditional Edo style.

anecdotes.text: The shop is immortalized in the old Asakusa saying "first Asakusa, second Kannon, third Sansada's tempura" (一に浅草、二に観音、三に三定の天ぷら), placing it alongside Sensō-ji itself as a must-see of the neighborhood.

Founder Sadakichi originally battered small fish and shellfish caught in the waters near Edo and deep-fried them in sesame oil at a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō district — the beginning of what became Sansada; the shop's name is a contraction of "Mikawaya Sadakichi" (三河屋定吉).

The restaurant has never opened branch locations, said to be rooted in the philosophy that the owner should never leave the shop; today it occupies only its original storefront on Kaminarimon-dōri plus an adjoining building.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits directly beside Kaminarimon Gate and serves huge numbers of tourists, so staff are used to non-Japanese-speaking guests and a laminated English menu is available on request, though the main menu and ordering are still primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 十和田 メトロ通り店（手打ちそば十和田） (Teuchi Soba Towada (Metro-dori Branch))
places.gf_detail: No source found mentions gluten-free options, a dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodations. This is a traditional handmade-soba and tempura shop; the flour blend ratio (100% buckwheat vs. wheat-blended) for the noodles isn't stated in any source found, tempura batter is wheat-based, and the tsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (soy sauce traditionally contains wheat) per the shop's own description of its bonito-based, MSG-free tsuyu. Not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or substitutions are mentioned in any source found. The shop's signature tsuyu (dipping sauce) is explicitly made with real bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, not a plant-based stock, so the standard soba here is not vegan or vegetarian as served.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: Towada is part of an old Asakusa soba lineage associated with the Tominaga family. A frequently cited figure, okami (proprietress) Tomoe Tominaga, is remembered locally for helping lead Asakusa's revival after visitor traffic declined following the 1964 Tokyo Olympics — founding  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The Towada name traces back to roots as far as Meiji 17 (1884) on Asakusa's Nakamise-dori; accounts differ on the exact modern founding (one source ties it to a Taisho-era (1924) confectionery, Kikusuido, that later became a soba shop; another gives Showa 44/1969). What's consistent across sources is that it is a long-running, family-rooted Asakusa soba house with more than one branch, including this Metro-dori location.
chef_bio.background: Towada is part of an old Asakusa soba lineage associated with the Tominaga family. A frequently cited figure, okami (proprietress) Tomoe Tominaga, is remembered locally for helping lead Asakusa's revival after visitor traffic declined following the 1964 Tokyo Olympics — founding the Asakusa Okamisan-kai (proprietresses' association) and helping launch the Asakusa Samba Carnival. The shop's soba is handmade by craftsman Kawamura Takashi, who has spent 28 years training and working as a soba artisan and is now described as 'the face of Towada. '
chef_bio.philosophy: Towada sources its buckwheat (Nanbu soba flour) from contract farms in the mountains around Lake Towada, straddling Aomori/Akita/Iwate, with flour delivered fresh to Tokyo daily. The tsuyu dipping sauce is made in-house using only bonito (katsuobushi) flakes, with no MSG or chemical additives, and the shop uses spring water drawn from the Ryusendo limestone cave in Iwate's Iwaizumi. Other ingredients are sourced directly from Tohoku producers — Tono-area vegetables, Aomori duck, Akita hinai-jidori chicken.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuretsubo soba (暮坪そば) — soba noodles served with grated kuretsubo kabu, a rare, sharply pungent mountain turnip from the Kuretsubo district of Tono, Iwate, used in place of wasabi as a condiment. It's cited repeatedly as a dish essentially unique to this shop in Tokyo.

anecdotes.text: Chef Kawamura Takashi has been a soba craftsman, including his apprenticeship, for 28 years and is now the public face of the Towada shop in Asakusa.

Okami Tomoe Tominaga is remembered as a 'legendary proprietress' who helped revive Asakusa after the 1964 Olympics diverted tourism away from the neighborhood, founding the Asakusa Okamisan-kai and helping start the Asakusa Samba Carnival.
The shop's roots are described as tracing back to a Meiji 17 (1884) establishment on Nakamise-dori.
Kuretsubo soba, topped with a rare mountain turnip sourced directly from Tono, Iwate, is called a dish you can only get at this shop in Tokyo.

positives.text: The tsuyu (dipping sauce) is made fresh in-house daily using only real bonito flakes, with no chemical seasonings or additives — a point of pride for flavor quality, though it is fish-based, not vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known, long-established soba shop two minutes from Asakusa Station that regularly serves tourists, but no source confirms an English or picture menu, so basic Japanese or pointing may be needed to ask about ingredients.

places.name: 粥や佐藤 (Kayuya Sato (Kayu-ya Sato))
places.gf_detail: The congee itself is rice-based, but the standard sets are served with steamed dumplings/dim sum (点心) made with wheat wrappers, and no source mentions a wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or any formal gluten-free protocol. This is a small 8-seat open counter kitchen where wheat-containing items are prepared alongside everything else, so cross-contact should be assumed. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6700-9888) to ask specifically about the dashi ingredients and dumpling handling rather than assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dashi that forms the base of every bowl of congee is made from niboshi (dried sardines), chicken, katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and dried shrimp — all animal-derived — and no source mentions a plant-based broth alternative. Steamed side dishes (dumplings/dim sum) also appear to use conventional, non-vegan fillings. There is no indication this restaurant can accommodate a vegan diner.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30), midday break 15:30-16:30; closed Sunday, Monday, Tuesday
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kannonura, behind Sensoji), Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner, Mr. Sato, tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) while in Taipei and was so struck by its flavor that he spent years testing his own recipe at home before opening a dedicated porridge shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened in Musashi-Kosugi (Kawasaki) in December 2018; relocated to Asakusa's 'Kannonura' backstreet (behind Sensoji) in March 2024 after five years at the original location.
chef_bio.background: The owner, Mr. Sato, tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) while in Taipei and was so struck by its flavor that he spent years testing his own recipe at home before opening a dedicated porridge shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: A belief that a MSG-free, painstakingly made natural dashi is the soul of a good bowl of congee; the menu centers on two evolving porridge sets rather than an extensive menu.
