chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese-style rice porridge (congee) simmered in a self-made 'natural dashi' built from kombu, dried shiitake mushroom, niboshi (dried sardines), chicken, katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and dried shrimp, served as set menus with small steamed side dishes.

anecdotes.text: Owner Sato first tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) in Taipei and, unable to forget the flavor, repeatedly tested his own recipe before opening a porridge specialty shop.
The shop opened in Musashi-Kosugi in December 2018 and relocated to Asakusa's Kannonura backstreet in March 2024 after five years at the original site.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny 8-seat counter shop on a quiet backstreet behind Sensoji with no confirmed English menu or signage, so visitors should expect a Japanese-only ordering experience, cash-only payment, and a possible wait at busy times.

places.name: 光家 (Kushiage Mitsuya (光家))
places.gf_detail: Every skewer is coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) and deep-fried, and the shared dipping sauce is an Osaka-style Worcestershire-type sauce, which typically contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation, separate fryer, or wheat-free menu is mentioned anywhere. This is a high-risk venue for celiac disease or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The confirmed menu is centered on meat, fish, and cheese skewers (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake, shiso pork belly). No vegetable-only or vegan skewers, and no vegan accommodation, were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 平日16:00〜22:00、土日祝13:00〜20:00（水曜定休）／ Weekdays 16:00–22:00, weekends & holidays 13:00–20:00, closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 花川戸 (Hanakawado), Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The proprietress, a former geisha born and raised in Asakusa, opened this standing kushiage counter in December 2001 a short walk from Asakusa Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 光也 (Mitsuya) — known to regulars as "光家姐さん" (the Mitsuya-neesan)
chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (born and raised locally)
chef_bio.background: The proprietress, a former geisha born and raised in Asakusa, opened this standing kushiage counter in December 2001 a short walk from Asakusa Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: She set out to build a casual counter where a woman could feel comfortable walking in and drinking alone — and kept the same simple panko and oil for years so the light, crisp coating never changes.
chef_bio.specialty: Panko-breaded, deep-fried kushiage skewers (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake) served Osaka-style with a shared Worcestershire-type dipping sauce and free cabbage.

anecdotes.text: Regulars are said to range in age from 4 to 94, with an unusually high share of women dining solo.
Like most kushiage counters, the house rule is no double-dipping in the communal sauce; a blogger who visited for skewers and lemon sours affectionately calls her "光家姐さん."

gf_cross_contamination.text: Skewers are coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) before deep-frying.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping is done in a shared Osaka-style Worcestershire-type sauce, with a house 'no double-dipping' rule since the sauce pot is communal.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The confirmed menu is fish-, meat-, and dairy-based (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake, shiso-wrapped pork belly); no vegetable-only or plant-based skewers were found in any source reviewed.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic, no-frills standing kushiage counter with local etiquette (no double-dipping the communal sauce); no English menu was found, so some Japanese phrases or pointing at the counter items will help.

places.name: うなぎの蒲の穂焼 牛タン 焼鳥 馬刺し いづも 浅草横町 (Izumo (Asakusa Yokocho))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-ingredient disclosure, or dedicated-fryer information was found. As a traditional unagi/izakaya menu, the eel glaze (tare) and dipping sauces are almost certainly soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). Treat as unsafe for celiac diners absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No plant-based or vegan menu items were found. The menu centers on eel, beef tongue, chicken yakitori, and horse sashimi, and dashi/tare bases are unlikely to be free of fish/bonito. This does not appear to be a viable option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree: Asakusa Yokocho's own shop page lists 11:00–22:00 daily; one aggregator lists weekdays 12:00–22:00 / weekends & holidays 12:00–23:00; another lists 12:00–23:00 daily, matching the overall Asakusa Yokocho facility hours (12:00–23:00, last order 22:00).
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草横町）
places.notes: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the To

roles: Eel (unagi) specialty izakaya, one of several branches of the いづも eel-skewer concept
chef_bio.origin: Osaka (Fukushima)
chef_bio.background: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the Tokyo Rakutenchi Asakusa Building.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated aim is to make traditionally pricey, formal unagi more affordable and approachable by serving it izakaya-style in a lively, casual setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Unagi tamadon and unagi gyudon (eel rice bowls with egg or beef), unagi kamahoyaki (semi-grilled eel fillet), and eel kushiyaki skewers, served alongside grilled beef tongue, yakitori, and horse sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka honten relocated and relaunched with new menu items in 2024, described as an effort to carry Japanese eel food culture forward while keeping it reasonably priced ('大阪一リーズナブルな鰻屋').
The Asakusa branch sits inside 'Asakusa Yokocho,' a food-and-festival themed dining hall built to let visitors experience Japanese festival culture alongside food from multiple genres.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits inside a tourist-oriented "food and festival" dining hall a minute from Asakusa Station with an English Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: ジムジュム（JIMJUM／JIM JUM） (Jimjum (Jim Jum))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free process, allergy menu, or dedicated fryer was found in any source. Thai cooking here likely relies on soy sauce and other seasonings that can contain wheat, and dishes are cooked in a small home-style kitchen with shared equipment. Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering — nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. The restaurant's signature hot pot and many other dishes contain chicken and seafood, and Thai cooking commonly uses fish sauce and oyster sauce. Vegans/vegetarians should ask the staff directly about vegetable-only options and hidden animal-derived seasonings (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste) before ordering — nothing here is confirmed vegan-safe.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa) — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] JIMJUM is run single-handedly by a Thai proprietress (referred to by regulars as "mama"), who cooks each dish herself in a small, home-style setting on the second floor of a building near Asakusa Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Thailand
chef_bio.background: JIMJUM is run single-handedly by a Thai proprietress (referred to by regulars as "mama"), who cooks each dish herself in a small, home-style setting on the second floor of a building near Asakusa Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious pursuit of authentic, home-style Thai cooking — welcoming guests the way you'd welcome a friend into your home, and trying to keep prices reasonable for regulars even as food costs rise.
chef_bio.specialty: JIMJUM nabe — a Thai-style shabu-shabu hot pot built on a tom yum goong broth, packed with herbs, chicken, seafood and vegetables, marketed as a healthy, detoxifying dish.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has been ranked No.1 in the Thai Curry category of Japan's Curry Database for three consecutive years.
It's run by a single Thai proprietress working alone, described by visitors as a cozy, home-style Thai spot where "everything you eat is delicious" despite the minimal, small setting.
Even amid rising food prices, the restaurant has been noted for holding the line on its budget-friendly lunch pricing (lunches under 1,000 yen).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, one-woman-run neighborhood Thai spot in the heart of Asakusa — simple, friendly interactions are easy, but don't expect an English menu or extensive English-speaking staff.

places.name: 甲州屋 (Koshuya)
places.gf_detail: No source indicates 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodles or any gluten-free accommodation. As a standard soba shop, the noodles are almost certainly a wheat-blended buckwheat noodle, and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce based, which in Japan is typically wheat-containing. No mention of a dedicated fryer or allergy protocol. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. The shop's known dishes (duck seiro, oyster curry soba) are meat/seafood-based, and standard soba tsuyu is typically bonito-dashi based. Not identified as vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:45, closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Hisago-dori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An established (老舖, "old, trusted shop") soba restaurant on Hisago-dori, the covered shopping arcade in Asakusa's historic Rokku entertainment district, close to Sendōji. The shopfront was renovated to a sleek, modern look, standing out among the traditional storefronts that line · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: soba shop on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping arcade
chef_bio.background: An established (老舖, "old, trusted shop") soba restaurant on Hisago-dori, the covered shopping arcade in Asakusa's historic Rokku entertainment district, close to Sendōji. The shopfront was renovated to a sleek, modern look, standing out among the traditional storefronts that line the rest of the arcade.
chef_bio.specialty: Kamo seiro (duck seiro soba) — cold soba noodles for dipping into a hot broth loaded with duck meat, served in noticeably generous, greenish-tinted noodle portions (reviewers note roughly 1. 5x the usual amount).

anecdotes.text: The shop is also known for a talked-about oyster curry soba (牌蛠カレーそば), made with fresh Hiroshima oysters in a rich curry-flavored broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood soba shop with a Japanese-language menu and no advertised English support beyond its Tabelog listing, so ordering may require pointing at photos or basic Japanese.

places.name: 小さな韓国 あぷろ 東京渋谷店 (Chiisana Kankoku Apuro Tokyo Shibuya-ten (Li)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found. As a Korean BBQ/street-food restaurant, core items likely involve wheat: pajeon (pancakes, wheat flour), tteokbokki and other dishes typically finished with gochujang or soy-sauce-based sauces (both commonly contain wheat), and fried items (e.g., cheese balls) probably share fryers with breaded/battered foods. Grilled meats (e.g., plain samgyeopsal) may be lower-risk if ordered without sauces, but this has not been confirmed with the restaurant, and cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Celiac diners should not rely on this restaurant without calling ahead and confirming ingredients directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-labeled items were found. This is a meat-centric Korean BBQ/street-food restaurant (signature dish is pork belly samgyeopsal); vegetable-forward items like pajeon (pancakes) typically contain egg, and broths/sauces commonly used in Korean cooking (doenjang, gochujang, anchovy/fish-based stock) often include animal-derived or fish-derived ingredients (e.g., dashi/bonito-style flavoring is common in Japanese-Korean fusion contexts). Vegan diners should ask staff directly about ingredients in any vegetable dish before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 12:00–23:30 (food L.O. 22:30, drink L.O. 23:00); Sat–Sun 11:00–24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30). Sources disagree slightly on exact times (one listing gives 11:00–24:00 for all days), so treat as approximate.
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (near Shinsen Station)
places.notes: "Chiisana Kankoku Apuro" (小さな韓国あぷろ, literally "Little Korea Apuro") is a Korean-cuisine restaurant chain founded in Fukuoka in 2010, during the second wave of the Korean cultural boom in Japan (the era of K-pop acts like KARA and BIGBANG breaking into the Japanese market). Run by · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Korean BBQ / Korean street-food restaurant, part of a Fukuoka-based chain
chef_bio.origin: Fukuoka, Japan
chef_bio.background: "Chiisana Kankoku Apuro" (小さな韓国あぷろ, literally "Little Korea Apuro") is a Korean-cuisine restaurant chain founded in Fukuoka in 2010, during the second wave of the Korean cultural boom in Japan (the era of K-pop acts like KARA and BIGBANG breaking into the Japanese market). Run by the company (有)あぷろ under representative 水本尚宏, the chain has grown to roughly 20 locations, mostly around Fukuoka but extending into the Kansai and Kanto regions, including this Shibuya branch on Dogenzaka near Shinsen Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's stated approach is to bring authentic Korean culture, not just food, to Japan: ingredients are sourced domestically or from Korea, and seasonings and kimchi are made in-house. The Shibuya branch leans into the K-pop/Korean-wave aesthetic, pairing an open-kitchen street-food-stall style ground floor with a second-floor space built around large screens and K-pop music videos for a more immersive, party-oriented atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut samgyeopsal (grilled pork belly) using pork from the Kirishima foothills, grilled tableside by staff, alongside apuro's signature pajeon (Korean savory pancakes).

anecdotes.text: The chain opened in 2010 during the second Korean Wave (Hallyu) boom in Japan and has since expanded to about 20 locations centered on Fukuoka, reaching into the Kanto and Kansai regions.

The Shibuya store's 1st floor is built around an open kitchen and vivid drum-can (street-stall style) tables, while the 2nd floor is a K-pop-themed space with a large screen and jukebox for a livelier, party atmosphere.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japan-based Korean-food chain in a busy Shibuya nightlife street; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so expect a standard Japanese-language dining experience with some English via ordering apps/QR menus common at similar izakaya-style chains.

places.name: トロワバグヴェール (Trois Bagues Vertes)
places.gf_detail: The signature dishes are crepes made from a wheat-based batter (with some whole-wheat flour blended in for texture, not gluten-free), and the gratin crepe uses a wheat-flour béchamel sauce. No gluten-free crepe batter, substitutions, or dedicated GF menu items were found in any source reviewed.
places.vegan_detail: The crepe batter, gratin béchamel sauce, and puddings all rely on dairy and eggs; no vegan-labeled dishes, substitutions, or plant-based options were found in any menu description reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri 12:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); Sat/Sun/Holidays 12:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: Kanda Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidobashi Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Trois Bagues Vertes is the second location of Café Trois Bagues, a Jimbocho kissaten (Japanese-style coffee shop) that has been running since 1976. The name plays on the founding family's surname, Miwa (三輪): in French, trois means 'three' and bague means 'ring,' echoing the kanji · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: sister café to Café Trois Bagues (est. 1976)
chef_bio.origin: Second location of the 1976-founded Café Trois Bagues in Jimbocho, opened June 2024 in Kanda Sarugakucho
chef_bio.background: Trois Bagues Vertes is the second location of Café Trois Bagues, a Jimbocho kissaten (Japanese-style coffee shop) that has been running since 1976. The name plays on the founding family's surname, Miwa (三輪): in French, trois means 'three' and bague means 'ring,' echoing the kanji 輪 ('ring') in Miwa. The original café is now run by second-generation owner Tokuko Miwa. In June 2024 the family opened this second branch a short walk away in Kanda Sarugakucho, near Suidobashi, decorated with a pale-green ('vertes') ceiling, antique furniture, and plants.
chef_bio.specialty: Gratin crepe and Crepe Pudding à la Mode, paired with nel-drip (flannel filter) coffee

anecdotes.text: Staff member Midorikawa-san explained that crepes were chosen for the new shop simply because there was no dedicated crepe shop in the Jimbocho area at the time.
The signature gratin crepe wraps the same béchamel-based gratin used in the original café's famous gratin toast, topped with tomato sauce and cheese and baked.
The shop name Trois Bagues is a play on the founding family's surname Miwa (三輪): trois (three) + bague (ring), echoing the kanji 輪 ('ring').

cultural_comfort.note: A retro, Japanese-style kissaten café with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service found in any source, so plan for ordering in Japanese.

places.name: おかゆと麺のお店 粥餐庁 京王モール店（粥餐庁 新宿京王モール店） (Kayu Sanchin (Okayu to Men Kayu Sanchin) - S)
places.gf_detail: Plain rice congee is naturally gluten-free by grain, but no dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was found. The restaurant also serves wheat noodle dishes (tantan noodles) in the same kitchen, and Japanese/Chinese-style seasoning here likely relies on standard soy sauce (contains wheat) and possibly wheat-thickened sauces for some toppings. No cross-contamination protocol or celiac-specific information was found in available sources. Ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering if you need strict gluten-free food.
places.vegan_detail: Menu includes a multi-vegetable congee item, which is a helpful vegetarian-leaning option, but this is not a dedicated vegan restaurant. Broth bases at congee/Chinese-style shops like this commonly use chicken stock even in "vegetable" dishes, and this has not been confirmed one way or the other for this location. Dashi/bonito-based seasonings are also common in Japanese-influenced Chinese cooking. Vegans (and anyone avoiding hidden animal stock or fish-derived seasoning) should ask staff specifically what stock/broth is used before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 新宿駅西口・京王モール (Shinjuku Station West Exit, Keio Mall undergrou
places.notes: Kayu Sanchin (粥餐庁) is a Chinese/Taiwanese-style congee (okayu) and noodle specialty chain. The brand's operations were transferred to Green House Foods Co., Ltd. (a major Tokyo-based food-service company headquartered in Nishi-Shinjuku) in 2008, and it now runs multiple branches, · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Chain restaurant operated by Green House Foods Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: Chinese/Taiwanese congee tradition, now run as a Japanese chain restaurant
chef_bio.background: Kayu Sanchin (粥餐庁) is a Chinese/Taiwanese-style congee (okayu) and noodle specialty chain. The brand's operations were transferred to Green House Foods Co. , Ltd. (a major Tokyo-based food-service company headquartered in Nishi-Shinjuku) in 2008, and it now runs multiple branches, including this one tucked into the underground Keio Mall passage directly connected to Shinjuku Station's south exit / west exit area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Positioned as a healthy, comforting meal for busy commuters — porridge made with a white-rice and brown-rice blend, served from early morning (7am on weekdays) through late evening, with abundant vegetable and protein toppings to choose from.
chef_bio.specialty: Congee (rice porridge) made from a white-and-brown rice blend, with topping options such as Taiwanese-style braised pork (lu rou), plum, steamed chicken, shrimp with cheese and tomato, and a multi-vegetable congee (lotus root, daikon, sweet potato, broccoli, cherry tomato, etc. ); the shop's special tantan noodles are also a recommended item.

anecdotes.text: A long-running, popular spot inside Keio Mall where lines form at lunchtime for its rice-blend congee.
Open as early as 7:00am on weekdays, making it one of the few sit-down 'proper meal' options near Shinjuku Station before the morning commute rush.

positives.text: Menu includes a dedicated multi-vegetable congee option with toppings such as lotus root, daikon, sweet potato, broccoli, and cherry tomato, giving vegetarian-leaning diners a clear plant-forward choice.

cultural_comfort.note: The shop's listing indicates menu/signage support for English, Chinese, and Korean in addition to Japanese, and it's a well-trafficked spot inside a major station mall, so ordering should be manageable for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.

places.name: 丸はし (Maruhashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free preparation, 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodle, or dedicated fryer is advertised anywhere in the sources found. As a standard machi-soba shop, the soba is very likely a wheat-blended (nihachi-style) noodle, and the tsuyu dipping sauce/broth almost certainly relies on wheat-based shoyu; the tendon is deep-fried tempura, with no mention of a separate gluten-free fryer. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or option is advertised. As a traditional soba shop, the dashi/broth used for the tsuyu and soups is very likely katsuobushi (bonito)-based, and the tendon batter/frying is standard tempura preparation — not suitable for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation (in Japanese) from staff.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict: one listing gives Mon-Sat 11:00-16:00 (closed Sun/holidays); a more detailed listing gives Mon-Fri 11:30-15:00 & 17:00-19:00, Sat 11:30-15:00 (closed Sun/holidays). The record below uses the more detailed split-hours version but this should be confirmed by phone (03-3407-1212).
places.neighborhood: 渋谷区東（渋谷駅南東側）／ Shibuya, Higashi (south/east side of Shibuya S
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This Shibuya branch sits down a back alley off the main road near Shibuya's south/east side, marked by a soba sign and blue noren, and has long been a lunchtime fixture for nearby office workers rather than a destination restaurant. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The Maruhashi soba lineage traces to a shop founded in Ueno Hirokoji in Taisho 7 (1918); a second-generation owner opened what became the main store in Gakugeidaigaku in 1956, from which roughly a dozen branches (including this Higashi/Shibuya shop, near Namiki-bashi) spread across Tokyo and Kanagawa over the following decades.
chef_bio.background: This Shibuya branch sits down a back alley off the main road near Shibuya's south/east side, marked by a soba sign and blue noren, and has long been a lunchtime fixture for nearby office workers rather than a destination restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: A no-frills neighborhood soba shop (machi-soba) valued for consistency, affordability, and the quiet pride of an old-school local counter rather than any modern reinvention.
chef_bio.specialty: Tendon (tempura rice bowl), about ¥900, served with miso soup and pickles; also mori/kake/tempura soba from about ¥450 and udon.

anecdotes.text: A local food blog titled its writeup 'A shop with the pride of a neighborhood soba restaurant,' describing hearty, reasonably priced soba and tendon served in a modest, workaday setting near Shibuya station.

An article on the wider Maruhashi lineage recounts the shop's roots to a 1918 Ueno founding and traces how the family's soba shops, including this Shibuya branch, spread through the city over roughly a century.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, cash-only neighborhood soba counter with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff — expect to order in Japanese from a printed menu or wall signage.

places.name: 菩提樹 (Bodaiju)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitutions, or accommodations were found in any source. The signature tonkatsu is coated in fresh wheat panko breadcrumbs, and the hamburg steak is served in a demi-glace sauce (roux-based sauces in this style of cooking are typically wheat-thickened, though the recipe itself was not confirmed). Fried items share cooking oil across the menu with no dedicated allergen-free fryer identified. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu items, substitutions, or accommodations were found. The menu is centered on wagyu beef hamburg steak and pork tonkatsu, both meat-based dishes; sauces (demi-glace) are typically meat-stock based in this style of cooking, though not explicitly confirmed. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.neighborhood: 本郷／水道橋 (Hongo, Bunkyō-ku, near Suidōbashi Station)
places.notes: Bodaiju has been serving hearty Western-style (yōshoku) comfort food near Suidōbashi Station for more than four decades. It operates under the same restaurant group as Katsuyoshi (かつ吉), a historic tonkatsu specialist founded in 1962, and shares that group's focus on hand-made fri · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Yōshoku restaurant within the Katsuyoshi restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Suidōbashi, Bunkyō-ku, Tokyo — operating from the same basement location for over 40 years
chef_bio.background: Bodaiju has been serving hearty Western-style (yōshoku) comfort food near Suidōbashi Station for more than four decades. It operates under the same restaurant group as Katsuyoshi (かつ吉), a historic tonkatsu specialist founded in 1962, and shares that group's focus on hand-made fried and grilled classics.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen states a commitment to hand-making everything from fresh ingredients and avoiding additives and preservatives.
chef_bio.specialty: A5 wagyu hamburg steak (A5和牛ハンバーグ) — fresh A5-grade Japanese wagyu ground in-house, formed into hamburg patties, and slow-simmered in a house-made demi-glace sauce; also known for traditional tonkatsu made with daily-sourced branded domestic pork wrapped in fresh panko and slow-fried in a corn-and-sesame oil blend.

anecdotes.text: The A5 wagyu hamburger lunch set is limited to roughly 20 servings a day and has built a devoted following among lunchtime regulars.
Beyond the hamburg and tonkatsu, regulars are said to come specifically for the shiso gohan (perilla-leaf rice), a labor-intensive side dish that has become something of a hidden signature.
The dining room is described as wood-toned with brick walls and jazz playing throughout, giving the space an unhurried, old-school yōshoku atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature tonkatsu is coated in fresh wheat panko and fried in a shared corn-salad-oil and sesame-oil blend used across the fried menu; no gluten-free fryer or breading alternative was identified in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: All sources found are Japanese-language (menu, reservation site, blogs); no English menu or English-speaking service was confirmed, so bring a translation tool for any allergy conversation.

places.name: 鶏そば みた葉 (Tori Soba Mitaba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option or allergen protocol was found in any source. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the tare/seasoning is not confirmed wheat-free. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian broth option was found. The signature and apparently only broth style offered is a rich chicken paitan (鶏白湯) built from chicken bones/collagen, which is inherently non-vegan; dashi/bonito use in any secondary items is not documented either way.
places.neighborhood: Kabukicho, Shinjuku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is run by a female owner-chef who trained under the well-known ramen brand Menya Buichi (麺屋武一) before striking out on her own; the counter is staffed by a mostly female crew, which multiple diner write-ups call out as unusual for a Kabukicho late-night ramen shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Sano Mai (referred to online as "Mitaba's president," 佐野 / さのまい)
chef_bio.origin: Opened May 22, 2018 in Kabukicho, near Shinjuku Ward Office
chef_bio.background: The shop is run by a female owner-chef who trained under the well-known ramen brand Menya Buichi (麺屋武一) before striking out on her own; the counter is staffed by a mostly female crew, which multiple diner write-ups call out as unusual for a Kabukicho late-night ramen shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Positioned as an easy, unintimidating late-night stop in Kabukicho — open from late morning straight through to 6am — welcoming solo diners (including women) into a nightlife district where that isn't always the norm.
chef_bio.specialty: 特製濃厚鶏白湯 (Tokusei Nokou Tori-Paitan) — a rich, collagen-heavy chicken paitan broth served with thin noodles; described by reviewers as creamy and full-bodied but not heavy.

anecdotes.text: A blog reviewer described the shop as run by a female owner making 'a bowl that isn't scary,' piled high with vegetables, standing out from Kabukicho's typical late-night ramen scene.
A ramen-focused blog notes the shop is known for chicken-centered bowls spanning rich tori-paitan, lighter clear broths, and Jiro-inspired styles.
Reviewers on Retty specifically highlight that the owner and staff are women running a chicken-paitan specialist in the heart of Kabukicho.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, all-Japanese neighborhood ramen counter in Kabukicho with no evidence of an English menu, so a little Japanese (or pointing at photos/the Tabelog menu) goes a long way.

places.name: 浅草にし座わ（浅草馳走にし座わ） (Asakusa Chisou Nishizawa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, statement, or allergy policy was found on any source. As a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant, the omakase course very likely relies on soy sauce and dashi (both commonly containing wheat/gluten and bonito-derived ingredients), and may include tempura or breaded items. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or wheat-free soy sauce. Celiac diners should call ahead (050-8881-7620) to discuss the course in detail before booking.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation was found. The omakase is built around sashimi, charcoal-grilled fish/meat, and clay-pot rice made with seasonal fish, and likely uses bonito (katsuo) dashi throughout — there is no indication the kitchen offers a plant-based course or can rework the set menu to remove fish/dashi.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito City — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Stat
places.notes: Asakusa Chisou Nishizawa (浅草馳走にし座わ) opened on July 17, 2024 in Asakusa. According to information associated with the restaurant's Instagram account, the chef previously worked at establishments in both Asakusa and Ginza before opening this restaurant. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Asakusa Chisou Nishizawa (浅草馳走にし座わ) opened on July 17, 2024 in Asakusa. According to information associated with the restaurant's Instagram account, the chef previously worked at establishments in both Asakusa and Ginza before opening this restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: A seasonal, chef's-choice (omakase) course built around whatever ingredients are best at the time of the visit, served in a calm, refined room alongside a curated selection of Shizuoka-prefecture sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Clay-pot rice (kamameshi-style donabe gohan) made with seasonal fish or sea bream, served as part of a multi-course omakase that also includes sashimi and charcoal-grilled dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, reservation-only Japanese omakase restaurant booked mainly through Japanese platforms (Hot Pepper, Autoreserve, Tabelog Japanese site); no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found, so some Japanese ability or a hotel/concierge booking will help.

places.name: 麺屋 鶏路地 (Menya Tori Roji)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen shop using conventional wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) broth base; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free preparation, or allergen accommodation was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: All broths (shoyu, shio, tantan) are built on tori paitan — a slow-simmered all-chicken stock — so there is no vegetable or plant-based broth option. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 (Mon-Thu, Sun); 11:00-翌7:00 / 11:00-7:00 next day (Fri-Sat, late night); 無休 (no closed days)
places.neighborhood: Shibuya Center-gai
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Tori Roji is a small, quietly-run ramen counter tucked in the basement of a building on Shibuya's Center-gai, just a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Menya Tori Roji is a small, quietly-run ramen counter tucked in the basement of a building on Shibuya's Center-gai, just a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name, Tori Roji (鶏路地), is a pun on the Japanese pronunciation of 'trilogy' (torirojii) — a nod to its concept of three chicken-broth ramen styles built from one base stock.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori paitan (creamy, slow-simmered all-chicken broth) ramen, offered in three versions: shoyu, shio, and tantan.

anecdotes.text: The counter is run by a single, quiet ('taciturn') male operator, giving the shop the low-key, no-frills character typical of Tokyo's small hole-in-the-wall ramen counters.

cultural_comfort.note: A local walk-in ramen counter run by one operator with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff on site; ordering will likely require some Japanese or menu-pointing, though Tabelog offers an English-language listing for reference.

places.name: 豆香房 水道橋店 (Mamekobo Suidobashi (Self-Roasted Coffee))
places.gf_detail: Straight black/drip coffee is inherently gluten-free, but no gluten-free labeling, dedicated equipment, or specific allergen information was found for the food items sold here (e.g., handmade sandwiches, baked goods). Ask staff about ingredients before ordering any food item.
places.vegan_detail: Plain black/drip coffee is vegan. No confirmation was found of plant-based milk alternatives for lattes/cappuccinos, or of the ingredients (egg, mayonnaise, meat, dashi, etc.) in the handmade sandwiches sold here. Ask staff about milk alternatives and sandwich fillings before ordering.
places.neighborhood: Suidobashi (水道橋), Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mamekobo (豆香房) is a self-roasting specialty coffee company with a small group of shops in the Kanda/Suidobashi area of Chiyoda-ku. The Suidobashi branch is a small, traditional coffee-shop-style café a short walk from the station, built around beans the company roasts itself. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Mamekobo (豆香房) is a self-roasting specialty coffee company with a small group of shops in the Kanda/Suidobashi area of Chiyoda-ku. The Suidobashi branch is a small, traditional coffee-shop-style café a short walk from the station, built around beans the company roasts itself.
chef_bio.philosophy: The roastery uses small-scale roasting machines rather than large industrial ones so each batch can be controlled delicately to bring out the individual character of the beans, and beans are sold/served only within about five days of roasting. The shop is a member of the Japan Specialty Coffee Association.
chef_bio.specialty: In-house roasted specialty coffee — the drip coffee on offer rotates through different single-origin and micro-lot beans up to three times a day (morning, midday, afternoon).

anecdotes.text: The company roasts specialty micro-lot and rare coffee lots in-house on small-batch roasters for delicate flavor control, and is a member of the Japan Specialty Coffee Association.
The coffee selection changes up to three times a day (morning, midday, and afternoon), and all beans sold are roasted within the previous five days.

cultural_comfort.note: Small, locals-oriented specialty coffee shop near Suidobashi Station with no confirmed English menu or signage; basic Japanese or a translation app will help when ordering.

places.name: 麺屋海神 新宿店 (Menya Kaijin Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations found. The custom noodles are made from a blend of two wheat flours (小麦粉/domestic wheat flour, starch, salt, egg white/kansui per ingredient listings), and toppings such as shrimp tsumire and chicken tsukune also contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was found; celiac diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature and essentially only broth is made from grilled fish bones/heads (about seven kinds of fresh fish, varying daily), and standard toppings include shrimp tsumire (fish/shrimp meatballs) and chicken tsukune. No plant-based broth or vegan menu option was found.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku Station Southeast Exit (新宿駅東南口)
places.notes: A long-running specialty salt-ramen shop just steps from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, currently run by owner Endo Etsuko. When it opened, there were no other ramen shops in the immediate area, which at the time was known more as an adult nightlife district. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded 2007 (originally opened 2004 in Ikebukuro under the name どう楽, produced by ramen critic Hideyuki Ishigami before rebranding and relocating to the current Shinjuku spot on October 25, 2007)
chef_bio.background: A long-running specialty salt-ramen shop just steps from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, currently run by owner Endo Etsuko. When it opened, there were no other ramen shops in the immediate area, which at the time was known more as an adult nightlife district.
chef_bio.philosophy: Because the fish delivered each morning changes, the soup's exact flavor shifts day to day — guests are invited to enjoy those daily variations rather than a fixed recipe. The noodles are a custom blend of two wheat flours, thin but firm enough to stand up to the broth.
chef_bio.specialty: あら炊き塩らぁめん (grilled fish-bone salt ramen) — broth made by first flame-searing the bones and heads of about seven kinds of fresh fish (varies daily, e. g. sea bream, yellowtail) and then simmering them slowly, carefully skimming impurities for a clean, concentrated seafood flavor without any fishy edge.

anecdotes.text: 当時、近辺には他にラーメン店は無く、歓楽街も近い『大人の街』だった (At the time it opened, there were no other ramen shops nearby, and the area was known as an 'adult district' close to the nightlife strip).
入荷する魚は毎日異なるので、日ごとに変わるスープの味を楽しめる (Because the fish delivered varies daily, guests can enjoy how the soup's flavor changes from day to day).

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free menu or process was found; noodles and multiple toppings contain wheat, so cross-contamination is essentially unavoidable in this kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-bone based (grilled fish scraps) and toppings include shrimp and chicken, so there is no vegan-safe base at this shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A local specialty ramen shop with Japanese-only signage and menu conventions typical of Tokyo counter ramen shops; no English menu was confirmed.

places.name: アーンドラ・ダイニング 渋谷 (Andhra Dining Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: The menu includes naturally wheat-free rice dishes (biryani, dosa, idli) alongside wheat-based breads (naan, chapati, poori) made in the same kitchen. No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocol was confirmed in available sources, so diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about wheat cross-contact before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetarian version of the Andhra Meals thali is available alongside vegetable curries, dosa, and rice dishes. However, South Indian vegetarian cooking commonly uses ghee and dairy, and no confirmation was found that vegan (fully dairy-free) preparation is offered by default, so strict vegans should ask staff which dishes can be made without ghee/dairy.
places.neighborhood: Udagawa-cho, Shibuya (Dogenzaka/Udagawa-cho area, ~3-4 min w
places.notes: Chef Yamara Ramanaiah trained in five-star hotel kitchens in Hyderabad before moving to Japan, where he opened Andhra Kitchen in Okachimachi and is widely credited as a driving force behind Japan's South Indian food boom. Andhra Dining Shibuya is a sister restaurant to Andhra Kit · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Yamara Ramanaiah (ヤマラ・ラーマナイア)
roles: founding/head chef of the Andhra restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Andhra Pradesh, India
chef_bio.background: Chef Yamara Ramanaiah trained in five-star hotel kitchens in Hyderabad before moving to Japan, where he opened Andhra Kitchen in Okachimachi and is widely credited as a driving force behind Japan's South Indian food boom. Andhra Dining Shibuya is a sister restaurant to Andhra Kitchen and Andhra Dining Ginza, part of a small, family-run group that later added Andhra Dhaba, run by relatives.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the home-style and restaurant flavors of Andhra Pradesh — built on dozens of hand-blended spices — to Japan without watering them down for local palates.
chef_bio.specialty: Hyderabad Dum Biryani — a slow-cooked, spice-layered rice dish with meat or vegetables, served on select days

anecdotes.text: Chef Ramanaiah trained in five-star hotel kitchens in Hyderabad before coming to Japan and is credited as a key figure in popularizing South Indian cuisine there.
The Shibuya location sits on the 4th floor, set back from the street-level bustle, giving diners a calmer space to enjoy South Indian cooking just a few minutes from Shibuya Station.
The restaurant's Hyderabad Dum Biryani, rice layered with slow-cooked spices and meat or vegetables, is offered on select days and is one of its most talked-about dishes.

positives.text: A vegetarian version of the Andhra Meals thali is offered as a standard menu option alongside the non-vegetarian version, so vegetarians aren't limited to side dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known, foreigner-frequented Shibuya spot with an English Tabelog listing and English-language press coverage, so basic English support is likely, though not confirmed as fully bilingual service.

places.name: サームロット (Saamrot)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy-safe protocol was found in any source. Thai/Vietnamese dishes here (pad thai, stir-fries, tom yum, gapao) commonly rely on soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce, which frequently contain wheat, and fried items are likely cooked in shared equipment. Diners with celiac disease should ask the kitchen directly about ingredients for each dish before ordering; nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: The menu has vegetable-based dishes (mixed vegetable stir-fry, kang kong/water spinach stir-fry, spring rolls) that lean vegetarian, but no dedicated vegan menu or vegan certification was found. Thai and Vietnamese kitchens routinely season vegetable dishes and broths with fish sauce, oyster sauce, or shrimp paste, and soups may use non-vegan stock, so strict vegans should ask specifically for dishes made without fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste rather than assuming any vegetable dish is automatically vegan.
places.neighborhood: Kabukicho, Shinjuku
places.notes: Saamrot has served Thai and Vietnamese food in Kabukicho for more than two decades, tucked in the alleys between the Golden Gai nightlife strip and Hanazono Shrine. It has been recognized by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce / Thai Select as a restaurant serving authentic Thai cuis · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Saamrot has served Thai and Vietnamese food in Kabukicho for more than two decades, tucked in the alleys between the Golden Gai nightlife strip and Hanazono Shrine. It has been recognized by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce / Thai Select as a restaurant serving authentic Thai cuisine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Saamrot means "three tastes" in Thai, referring to the sweet, spicy, and sour balance that Thai cooking is built around.
chef_bio.specialty: Gapao (Thai basil stir-fry) rice served in several versions — beef, pork, seafood, black tiger prawn — plus a tom yum kung the restaurant is known for locally.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's tom yum kung is described as having earned top marks from all five judges on a Thai-food television competition.
The name Saamrot ("three tastes") refers to the sweet-spicy-sour balance that anchors the kitchen's approach to Thai cooking.
The restaurant sits on the second floor of a mixed-use building in an alley between Kabukicho's Golden Gai and Hanazono Shrine, with a compact, low-lit dining room of around 30 table seats.

positives.text: The menu includes vegetable-forward dishes such as stir-fried mixed vegetables and morning glory (kang kong / water spinach) alongside the standard Thai and Vietnamese offerings.

cultural_comfort.note: Tabelog maintains an English-language listing and the restaurant is used to Kabukicho's mixed foreign/local nightlife crowd, but no English menu or English-speaking staff was confirmed, so a few basic Japanese phrases will help.

places.name: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan 渋谷本店 (GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan Shibuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: The shop states it uses no wheat flour anywhere on the menu, substituting rice flour (Yamagata Shonai "Haenuki" rice) even in savory items like fried chicken and taco rice, which is why it markets itself as a gluten-free specialty shop. However, this is described in sources as a "no wheat flour" commitment rather than a certified strict celiac protocol: at least one English-language celiac reviewer reported becoming ill after the taco rice and cautioned that hidden gluten sources (e.g., wheat in soy sauce/seasonings used in savory dishes) may not be fully screened, and that the shop's definition of "gluten-free" may lean toward "wheat-free." Sweets/churros are more consistently reported as safe than the savory menu. Celiac travelers should ask specifically about soy sauce, dashi, and seasoning ingredients before ordering savory items.
places.vegan_detail: The core menu (rice-flour churros, fried chicken, taco rice) is not vegan by default, as it includes meat and likely dairy/egg in some sweets. However, sources note the shop offers seasonal vegan-and-gluten-free-compatible desserts, such as a shaved ice made with homemade almond cream and soy milk cream instead of condensed milk, indicating some deliberate vegan-friendly options exist alongside the main gluten-free-focused menu. Vegan diners should ask about egg/dairy in specific baked goods and about hidden animal-derived seasonings (e.g., dashi, bonito) in savory dishes, which were not specifically documented in available sources.
places.hours_raw: 月・水・木・金・土・日 11:00-21:00（ラストオーダー 20:30）、火曜定休。一部情報源では第2・第3木曜日も休みとの記載あり（要確認）。
places.neighborhood: 神泉 (Shinsen), Shibuya-ku
places.cuisine: グルテンフリーカフェ
places.notes: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avanは、小麦粉を一切使わず、山形県庄内産の米（はえぬき）を丁寧に製粉した米粉だけで作るグルテンフリー専門店として神泉駅近くにオープン。渋谷本店はブランドの中心となる店舗で、後に上野エキュートなど他店舗も展開している。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: グルテンフリー専門カフェ／米粉スイーツ専門店
chef_bio.origin: 渋谷区神泉（Shinsen, Shibuya）
chef_bio.background: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avanは、小麦粉を一切使わず、山形県庄内産の米（はえぬき）を丁寧に製粉した米粉だけで作るグルテンフリー専門店として神泉駅近くにオープン。 渋谷本店はブランドの中心となる店舗で、後に上野エキュートなど他店舗も展開している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「小麦を使わない」ことを店全体で徹底し、スイーツだけでなく総菜メニュー（唐揚げ、タコライスなど）まで含めて小麦粉不使用にこだわることで、グルテンに配慮したい人が安心して選べる場を目指している。
chef_bio.specialty: 米粉チュロス（Rice flour churros）— 山形県庄内産の極細挽き米粉と米油を使い、外はサクサク、中はもちもちの食感に仕上げた看板メニュー。 フレーバー展開も豊富。

anecdotes.text: 「チュロスがやみつきになるグルテンフリーカフェ、神泉『avan 渋谷本店』」として紹介され、米粉チュロスの食感と味が高く評価されている。
米粉タイムズ等のグルメメディアでも、山形県庄内産のブランド米「はえぬき」を極細挽きした米粉を使用したチュロスとして紹介されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: One English-language celiac reviewer reported becoming ill after eating the taco rice, cautioning that the shop's gluten-free claim may only mean wheat-flour-free rather than fully celiac-safe, and recommending caution for celiacs especially with savory menu items.
gf_cross_contamination.source: web search summary of findmeglutenfree.com / celiac travel review aggregation

gf_cross_contamination.text: Another reviewer, noting it functions as a dedicated gluten-free facility, said staff seemed confident about what they could serve even with a language barrier, rating the experience positively (5 stars).
gf_cross_contamination.source: web search summary of findmeglutenfree.com review aggregation

gf_cross_contamination.text: A reviewer reported becoming ill after having the taco rice and noted that, based on the shop's website, they seem to think 'gluten-free' only equals 'wheat free,' cautioning those with Celiac to be careful.
Tabelog listing title is now prefixed 【移転】/[Relocated] — 'GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan 渋谷本店' has relocated from the original Shinsen location.

Avan moved to JR Ueno station (Ecute Ueno) and now only serves churros (100% rice flour, takeout only); the menu changed significantly from when the negative taco rice review was posted, so the full savory menu (taco rice, fried chicken) described in the label no longer operates at this shop.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described across reviews as friendly and accommodating, going out of their way to meet customer requests, with both English and Japanese menus available.
staff_allergy_handling.source: web search summary of celiac/GF review aggregation (findmeglutenfree.com)

positives.text: Entire menu (not just desserts) is made without wheat flour, using rice flour milled from Yamagata Shonai-grown rice, which is the basis for its reputation as a dedicated gluten-free specialty shop.

cultural_comfort.note: English menu is available and staff are described as friendly and welcoming, though full English fluency is not guaranteed, so simple Japanese phrases or a translation app help for detailed allergy questions.

places.name: ANIKU produced by 新井屋（エーニク プロデュースド バイ アライヤ） (ANIKU produced by Araiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, statement, or dedicated preparation is documented anywhere. Grilled meat cuts themselves are typically naturally gluten-free, but yakiniku tare (dipping/marinade sauce) commonly contains soy sauce made with wheat, and any breaded or sauced side dishes would not be GF. Celiac diners should ask staff directly (in Japanese) about tare ingredients and cross-contact on the shared grill before ordering plain-salt (塩) cuts.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric yakiniku restaurant (premium wagyu and fresh horumon/offal) with no vegan menu, vegan options, or plant-based statements found in any source. Broth/tare bases are not documented but yakiniku sauces commonly include dashi/bonito-derived or animal-based ingredients. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町（水道橋）/ Kanda-Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidōbashi Sta
places.notes: 新井英樹氏は調理師専門学校を卒業後、ホテルの洋食シェフや病院の調理職を経て、焼肉の道を志し「叙々苑」で修業。その後、三河島「山田屋」で5年間、牛・豚問わず正肉からホルモンまでの焼肉全般の技術を習得した。2013年に独立し「焼肉ホルモン 新井屋」を渋谷にオープン。当初は豚専門店だったが客足が伸びず、1年ほどで牛タン・牛カルビ・牛ロースを追加して人気店に成長。2018年に阿佐ヶ谷店、2019年に渋谷本店拡大、2021年に「はなれ」、2022年に「にかい」を開業し、ANIKU produced by 新井屋（水道橋）は新井氏が初めて手がけた「プロデュース」業態 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: produce chef / owner of parent restaurant 新井屋
chef_bio.background: 新井英樹氏は調理師専門学校を卒業後、ホテルの洋食シェフや病院の調理職を経て、焼肉の道を志し「叙々苑」で修業。 その後、三河島「山田屋」で5年間、牛・豚問わず正肉からホルモンまでの焼肉全般の技術を習得した。 2013年に独立し「焼肉ホルモン 新井屋」を渋谷にオープン。 当初は豚専門店だったが客足が伸びず、1年ほどで牛タン・牛カルビ・牛ロースを追加して人気店に成長。 2018年に阿佐ヶ谷店、2019年に渋谷本店拡大、2021年に「はなれ」、2022年に「にかい」を開業し、ANIKU produced by 新井屋（水道橋）は新井氏が初めて手がけた「プロデュース」業態の一号店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 本店「新井屋」と同じ仕入れルートで新鮮なホルモンと上質な和牛を調達し、「肉のプロの目利き」による選定を看板とする。 古民家を改装した落ち着いた空間で、見た目のインパクトと肉そのものの品質を両立させる料理を提供する。
chef_bio.specialty: タワーカルビ（積み重ねた和牛カルビをわさびで食べる、ジェンガのような見た目の名物メニュー）

anecdotes.text: タワーカルビは積まれたお肉がジェンガのような見た目でインスタ映えする一品で、わさびをつけてアクセントに食べるのが特徴。和牛ならではの上品な脂の旨みを堪能できる。
ANIKU1本ステーキはドラゴンのように長い迫力のある逸品で、ハサミを使わずに箸で切れるほど柔らかい。ホルモンは新鮮で丁寧に下処理されており、どの部位も臭みは一切ない。
新井英樹さんは2013年に独立し「焼肉ホルモン新井屋」をオープン。最初は豚専門の焼肉店として立ち上げたが客足が伸びず、1年ほど経ってから牛タン・牛カルビ・牛ロースを加えた。2018年阿佐ヶ谷店、2019年渋谷店、2021年「はなれ」、2022年「にかい」と展開。

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-style yakiniku restaurant in a renovated old house with no English menu or dietary accommodations documented; expect to navigate in Japanese or with translation help.

places.name: 食堂 長野屋 (Shokudo Naganoya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodations found in any source. The menu centers on katsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet — wheat flour breading) and katsu curry (curry roux is almost always wheat-thickened in Japanese shokudo), plus dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce. No mention of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free substitutions. Not recommended for celiac diners without confirming directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options or menu identified in any source. The specialty dish (nikudofu) contains beef and is simmered in a soy-based broth; the accompanying miso soup contains tofu/seaweed and is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi per standard Japanese practice. The rest of the menu (katsudon, grilled mackerel, simmered fish dishes, katsu curry) is meat- and fish-centric. Not suitable for vegan diners without extensive modification, and no evidence staff offer such modification.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-21:00 or 12:00-22:00 (sources differ); closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 新宿 東南口 (Shinjuku Southeast Exit)
places.cuisine: 定食・ポークカツカレー・家庭料理
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in Taisho 4 (1915), in the middle of World War I, near what would become Shinjuku Station's southeast exit. The building has been rebuilt twice since — it burned down during World War II and was rebuilt, and the current building dates to the postwar high-growth era when N · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in Taisho 4 (1915), in the middle of World War I, near what would become Shinjuku Station's southeast exit. The building has been rebuilt twice since — it burned down during World War II and was rebuilt, and the current building dates to the postwar high-growth era when Naganoya came to own the property outright — making it a rare shokudo with over a century of continuous history steps from one of the world's busiest train stations.
chef_bio.philosophy: A working-class daily-use shokudo (cafeteria) rather than a destination restaurant — cheap, filling teishoku served fast to office workers, regulars, and the occasional daytime drinker, preserving a Showa-era dining atmosphere just steps from the ultramodern station.
chef_bio.specialty: 肉どうふ (nikudofu) — beef and tofu simmered until dark brown in a sweet-savory soy broth, served with rice and miso soup; also known for katsudon and katsu curry

anecdotes.text: Naganoya was founded in Taisho 4 (1915), amid World War I, and has weathered Shinjuku's constant redevelopment around it ever since; the current building is the third to stand on the site, rebuilt after wartime fire damage and again during the postwar economic boom.

The signature nikudofu (meat and tofu) is simmered until dark brown, the tofu soaking up the soy-based broth for a balance of saltiness and sweetness that pairs well with a drink; it comes with a miso soup containing tofu and seaweed.
Walking in feels like stepping into the Showa era — a woman greets guests at the counter while regulars sit quietly reading the newspaper, just one minute from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit.

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old neighborhood shokudo with handwritten menus and a regulars-only feel — no English menu was found in any source, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board will help.

places.name: オーガニックごはん かえもん 浅草本店 (Organic Gohan Kaemon Asakusa Honten)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen is fully plant-based and miso soup/broths reportedly use a vegetable-scrap broth rather than bonito dashi, which removes some common hidden-animal risks. However, wheat is present in at least one standard menu item (a soba-flour and organic *wheat*-flour pancake is a signature dish), and no source confirms a dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated cookware/fryer, or wheat-free (tamari-style) soy sauce. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should call ahead and confirm which specific buffet/set dishes are wheat-free that day.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based kitchen: no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish, and soy-based meat substitutes are used in some items. Miso soup/broths reportedly use a vegetable-scrap broth rather than bonito dashi. The kitchen also avoids the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, scallion, Chinese chive, shallot), which additionally suits Buddhist-style (shojin) vegetarian diners. Format is largely an organic-vegetable buffet plus set courses.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun: 11:00–15:30 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:00). Closed Tuesdays (reserved for private events).
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Hanakawado), roughly 1-2 minutes' walk from Asakusa
places.notes: Kaemon opened as an organic restaurant near Asakusa Station built around a self-described "food barrier-free" (食のバリアフリー) concept: a menu meant to be safely enjoyed by guests of any nationality, religion, or dietary restriction. It is run by owner-chef Uehara Kazunori, who has des · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner / head chef: Uehara Kazunori (上原一徳)
Hall manager: Imai Masafumi (今井雅文)
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (chef born 1964, per his own public profile)
chef_bio.background: Kaemon opened as an organic restaurant near Asakusa Station built around a self-described "food barrier-free" (食のバリアフリー) concept: a menu meant to be safely enjoyed by guests of any nationality, religion, or dietary restriction. It is run by owner-chef Uehara Kazunori, who has described publicly moving toward extreme minimal eating ("breatharian"-style, at most one meal a day, sometimes weeks without food) since around 2014 while continuing to develop the restaurant's menu. The current flagship (本店) location is on the 3rd floor of a building called AUSPICE in Hanakawado, a couple of minutes from Asakusa Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: "Food barrier-free" dining: no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy, plus deliberate avoidance of the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, scallion, Chinese chive, shallot) used in Buddhist shojin cooking, so the restaurant calls its cuisine "Oriental Vegetarian. " The kitchen sources seasonal organic produce and reworks the menu around whatever arrives.
chef_bio.specialty: Soba-flour and organic wheat pancake (そば粉と有機小麦のパンケーキ), a long-running signature item; also a rotating organic-vegetable buffet and set courses ("かえもんディナーセット", "旬の厳選野菜コース")

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's stated aim is to be a "food barrier-free" restaurant so that people can eat without worry regardless of nationality or religion, avoiding onion, garlic, and leek in addition to meat, fish, egg, and dairy.

Produce is sourced from an organic farm in Chiba ("Hyakushou Farm"), and the kitchen aims to develop around 300 new menu items a year, testing new dishes daily to match whatever seasonal vegetables arrive.

Hall manager Imai Masafumi, interviewed by Meat Free Monday All Japan, described Kaemon's cuisine as built on shojin (Buddhist temple cooking) principles with the five-allium restriction, and said the restaurant also runs a volunteer "Fairy System" where helpers are compensated in an internal currency called "MON" rather than as conventional employees.

Owner Uehara Kazunori has publicly described becoming increasingly able to go without food after time spent traveling in mountainous regions, adopting a very-low-food ("breatharian") lifestyle from around 2014 while continuing to run the restaurant's kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen does not prepare meat, fish, egg, or dairy dishes at all — it is a fully plant-based operation, and miso soup/broths reportedly use a vegetable-scrap broth ("vege broth") instead of bonito-based dashi.

positives.text: Restaurant explicitly markets itself as "food barrier-free," avoiding meat, fish, egg, dairy, and the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, scallion, Chinese chive, shallot) so that diners with a range of dietary and religious restrictions can eat there.
Listed on Vegewel's curated directory of vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free-friendly restaurants near Asakusa Station.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant appears on English-language Tabelog and halal/vegan tourist directories aimed at foreign visitors, suggesting some accommodation for non-Japanese speakers, but the on-site level of spoken English was not confirmed from available sources.

places.name: ひない小町 渋谷店 (Hinai Komachi Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergy protocol was found. Yakitori here is typically offered either tare-glazed (a soy-based sauce that contains wheat) or shio (salt-grilled, no soy sauce) — shio-style skewers would be the safer ask, but cross-contact on a shared charcoal grill and in shared sauces/marinades is unconfirmed either way. The signature kiritanpo nabe hot pot uses a chicken-based dashi broth whose full seasoning (soy sauce, etc.) is not documented. Celiac diners should not assume safety without asking staff directly and should not rely on this listing alone.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken (Hinai-jidori) specialty izakaya; there is no vegan or vegetarian menu. Vegetable yakitori skewers (野菜串) exist as individual items, but they are grilled on the same charcoal grill as meat skewers and the restaurant's signature broths (e.g., kiritanpo nabe) are chicken-stock based, so hidden animal-derived dashi/stock is likely throughout the menu. Not a suitable choice for strict vegans; only worth considering for flexible vegetarians willing to ask detailed questions about individual vegetable items.
places.neighborhood: 神泉（渋谷区道玄坂、神泉駅・渋谷駅から徒歩圏内）
places.cuisine: 秋田郷土料理・比内地鶏焼き鳥
places.notes: 比内地鶏は名古屋コーチン・薩摩地鶏と並ぶ日本三大地鶏の一つで、秋田県の特産品。当店は毎朝秋田から直送される比内地鶏を備長炭でじっくり焼き上げる串焼き専門店として営業しており、2025年3月に全面改装してリニューアルオープンした。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 秋田県（比内地鶏の産地）
chef_bio.background: 比内地鶏は名古屋コーチン・薩摩地鶏と並ぶ日本三大地鶏の一つで、秋田県の特産品。 当店は毎朝秋田から直送される比内地鶏を備長炭でじっくり焼き上げる串焼き専門店として営業しており、2025年3月に全面改装してリニューアルオープンした。
chef_bio.philosophy: 秋田直送の比内地鶏と備長炭焼きへのこだわりを軸に、秋田郷土料理を提供する
chef_bio.specialty: 比内地鶏の備長炭焼き鳥（白レバー串が特に人気）ときりたんぽ鍋

anecdotes.text: 看板メニューのきりたんぽ鍋は、比内地鶏から取った出汁に野菜・まいたけ・しらたきともちもちの「きりたんぽ」を合わせた一品で、農林水産省の「郷土料理百選」に選ばれた料理。
比内地鶏の串焼きは360円から提供され、白レバー串が最も人気のメニューとされる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard yakitori tare glaze is soy-sauce based (contains wheat); no confirmation found of a wheat-free tare or dedicated GF sauce.

positives.text: Shio (salt-grilled, no soy-based tare) yakitori is a standard menu option alongside tare-glazed skewers, which may reduce wheat exposure if confirmed with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: A local Japanese izakaya experience with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help when asking about ingredients.

places.name: カオ（KHAO） (KHAO)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer confirmed; omakase format limits substitution and the restaurant may decline guests with extensive allergies.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or dedicated vegan track has been confirmed. Thai curries and nam prik gaeng at KHAO commonly rely on fish sauce, shrimp paste, and other animal-derived seasonings as part of traditional preparation, and the fixed omakase format limits substitution; the restaurant has indicated it may decline guests with extensive dietary restrictions. Vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly in advance to confirm whether any accommodation is possible.
places.hours_raw: 月・火・木・金・土・祝日 18:00～21:30（定休日：水・日）／完全予約制、コース開始時間同時スタート
places.neighborhood: 神保町 (Jimbocho), 千代田区
places.notes: Ishiyama Koichi (b. 1977) has roughly 30 years of cooking experience; he first visited Thailand at age 35, became captivated by Thai cuisine, and trained at Bangkok's one-Michelin-star restaurant Nahm. Hozumi Eri (b. 1986) moved to Thailand at age 24 to apprentice under a Thai ch · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Ishiyama Koichi and Hozumi Eri (石山公一・穂積依里)
chef_bio.origin: The husband-and-wife chef duo previously ran the Thai restaurant "Pissanulok" in Nagoya (opened 2016), which was listed in the Gault&Millau guide three years running. In June 2024 they relocated the restaurant to Tokyo's Jimbocho neighborhood and renamed it KHAO.
chef_bio.background: Ishiyama Koichi (b. 1977) has roughly 30 years of cooking experience; he first visited Thailand at age 35, became captivated by Thai cuisine, and trained at Bangkok's one-Michelin-star restaurant Nahm. Hozumi Eri (b. 1986) moved to Thailand at age 24 to apprentice under a Thai chef and spent about two years studying regional nam prik gaeng (curry pastes) across the country before returning to Japan; the two met in Bangkok.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking that honors Thai culinary tradition while drawing on Japanese ingredients and technique, served as an omakase counter experience with all guests starting together.
chef_bio.specialty: Thai curry (gaeng) made with coconut milk the chefs press fresh in-house, paired with hand-made nam prik gaeng (curry paste) developed from Hozumi's research trips around Thailand.

anecdotes.text: The name KHAO means "white" in Thai.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (aggregated from restaurant coverage, see sources)

anecdotes.text: Within about a year of reopening in Tokyo, KHAO earned one Michelin star in the MICHELIN Guide Tokyo 2026 — reported as Japan's first Michelin star awarded to a Thai restaurant.
The restaurant seats only 8 at a counter where diners watch the cooking process up close.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant's own listing notes that guests with extensive allergies or strong food dislikes may have their reservation declined, since the menu is a single fixed omakase course.

positives.text: The chefs make their coconut milk and curry pastes (nam prik gaeng) from scratch in-house rather than using pre-made bases, giving them more direct control over ingredients than many Thai kitchens.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, fully reservation-based Japanese fine-dining counter; booking is typically done in Japanese (phone or Japanese reservation platforms) and the restaurant has noted it may decline guests with extensive allergies, so travelers should communicate dietary needs clearly in advance.

places.name: タカマル鮮魚店 本館 (Takamaru Sengyoten Honkan)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. Plain sashimi/sushi cuts of fish are naturally gluten-free, but the menu also includes fried items (tempura/karaage-style dishes) and shoyu-based preparations; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no confirmation of a gluten-free soy sauce alternative, a dedicated fryer, or shared-fryer avoidance. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm ingredients dish-by-dish.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-specialty izakaya built around raw and cooked fish; no vegan menu, plant-based substitutions, or dashi/bonito-free options were found in any source. Broth and many prepared dishes should be assumed to use fish-based dashi/bonito unless confirmed otherwise by staff. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without direct confirmation.
