He moved to Tokyo at age 22 originally aiming to become an actor, but ended up joining a major seafood company, where he quickly rose to store manager and buyer. He later worked as a "fish concierge," supply · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Amagasaki, Hyogo Prefecture (founder's hometown)
chef_bio.background: Founder Masataka Aihara was born in 1972 in Amagasaki, Hyogo Prefecture. He moved to Tokyo at age 22 originally aiming to become an actor, but ended up joining a major seafood company, where he quickly rose to store manager and buyer. He later worked as a "fish concierge," supplying seafood to major supermarket chains and running tuna-breakdown demonstration events to boost sales. In 2004 he founded Takamaru Co. , Ltd. and obtained a Tsukiji market trading license, running wholesale and retail-training contracts. Unsatisfied with fixed annual contracts that were not tied to results, he founded Takamaru Sengyoten in 2009 to test his own sales ability directly through retail and restaurant operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Steady, word-of-mouth growth rather than flashy advertising: the first shop opened in 2009 in a converted 18-tsubo garage; as regulars increased and space ran short, the company opened additional branches within walking distance rather than expanding aggressively.
chef_bio.specialty: Sashimi and sushi made from high-grade fish sourced directly from Tsukiji (now Toyosu) each morning and sold at near-wholesale prices, with the seafood itself as the centerpiece rather than elaborate cooking.

anecdotes.text: 2009年に18坪のガレージを改装して1号店を創業し、2011年に徒歩1〜2分の場所に2号店をオープンし、翌年12月に青梅街道沿いに3号店を開店した。各店とも派手な広告宣伝は一切しておらず、開業当初は苦労したが、徐々に常連客が増えて手狭になると、徒歩圏内に新店を出店するという、堅実な展開を進めてきた。
築地まぐろの解体師が運営する魚やで、毎朝とれたて新鮮な高級築地鮮魚を中卸価格で調理・販売する。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) typically contains wheat; no confirmation was found that Takamaru Sengyoten offers a wheat-free soy sauce alternative for sashimi/sushi service.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: General knowledge of standard shoyu composition; no restaurant-specific source confirms or denies an alternative.

positives.text: The restaurant's core dishes are simply prepared sashimi and sushi cuts of whole fish purchased directly from Tsukiji, which as raw, unseasoned cuts are inherently free of wheat/gluten before any sauce is added.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling local seafood izakaya with an open-terrace feel; no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff, so expect a Japanese-only experience aside from third-party reservation sites (Tabelog, HotPepper) that offer some English/romanized listings.

places.name: 十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷 渋谷東急本店前店 (Juwari Soba Sagatani – Shibuya Tokyu Honten-)
places.gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are 十割 (100% buckwheat, no wheat flour or yamaimo binder), which is a genuinely wheat-free noodle base. However, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a traditional kaeshi made from soy sauce and mirin over bonito dashi, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — no wheat-free tsuyu has been confirmed for this chain. Tempura and kakiage (fried in wheat-flour batter) are also on the menu, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate cooking water for the soba has been confirmed. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about the tsuyu recipe and frying setup before ordering, and treat this as a moderate-risk rather than a safe choice.
places.vegan_detail: The soba tsuyu (dipping/broth sauce) is built on a traditional bonito-based dashi with soy sauce and mirin — not vegan. No kombu-only or otherwise plant-based dashi option was found in available menus or reviews, and there is no evidence of a vegetarian-adapted broth at this location despite the dry buckwheat noodles themselves containing no egg or animal product.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 7:00–22:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 10:00–21:00; closed Jan 1–3. (Note: some older listings report different hours, including 24-hour operation, so current hours should be reconfirmed.)
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂
places.notes: The soba noodles themselves are 十割 (100% buckwheat, no wheat flour or yamaimo binder), which is a genuinely wheat-free noodle base. However, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a traditional kaeshi made from soy sauce and mirin over bonito dashi, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: soba chain restaurant (no individual chef publicly named)
chef_bio.origin: Part of the Sagatani (嵯峨谷) chain, which opened its first shop in 2011; the Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is now run by For You Inc. (株式会社フォーユー).
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the idea of "五たて" (five freshnesses): freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried, and served fresh — aiming to prove that a fast, affordable stand-and-eat-style soba shop doesn't have to cut corners on using 100% buckwheat.
chef_bio.specialty: 十割そば (juwari soba) — noodles made from 100% buckwheat with no wheat-flour binder, milled fresh in-house

anecdotes.text: Sagatani installed automated stone mills at its shop fronts so whole buckwheat groats are ground into flour on-site and used the same day, rather than buying pre-milled soba flour.

The chain opened its first branch in 2011 around the idea of selling genuine 100%-buckwheat soba for as little as roughly 350 yen, challenging the convention that cheap stand-and-eat soba shops use flour blended with wheat.
The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is noted among fans for its kamo seiro (duck dipping soba) as a heartier option alongside the classic mori/kake soba.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and kakiage (fried in wheat-flour batter) are regular menu items alongside the soba, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer has been confirmed for this location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu is a soy-sauce-and-mirin kaeshi over a concentrated bonito dashi ('だしよく、甘め濃いめのきりっとした...カエシ'); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tsuyu has been confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The soba tsuyu is dashi-based with bonito rather than a kombu-only broth, so the standard bowl is not vegan; no plant-based dashi alternative was found in available menu information.

positives.text: The soba itself is 十割 (100% buckwheat) with no wheat flour or mountain-yam binder added — a genuinely wheat-free noodle, which is unusual for such an affordable chain.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a self-service Japanese-language food-ticket vending machine (pick a ticket, sit, wait for your number) with no confirmed English menu, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, though the process is simple to follow visually.

places.name: 80円焼鳥 信長 水道橋店 (80-Yen Yakitori Nobunaga, Suidobashi Branch)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free options or accommodations were found. As a yakitori-and-izakaya menu, dishes are very likely to use soy-sauce-based tare glaze (contains wheat) on the skewers, and fried items such as chicken karaage and chicken katsu are breaded/battered with wheat flour. No information was found on a dedicated fryer, allergy menu, or staff training for celiac-safe handling, so this is not a safe choice for strict gluten-free/celiac diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu items, marking, or accommodations were found. The restaurant's entire concept is centered on chicken yakitori (80-yen chicken skewers) and meat-based izakaya sides (chicken katsu, etc.), making it fundamentally unsuited to a vegan diet; side dishes such as salads or vegetable stir-fries likely still involve shared cookware/dashi that could include bonito (katsuo) stock, though this specific detail was not directly confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 11:00-14:00, 17:00-24:00 (lunch + dinner); Sat-Sun: 17:00-24:00 (dinner only). One source separately describes hours as daily 17:00-last order 23:00 (close 24:00) with no lunch service; treat lunch-hour availability as unconfirmed/possibly seasonal.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区 水道橋 (Suidobashi, Chiyoda-ku)
places.notes: This branch is part of a small Tokyo yakitori chain (multiple branches under the Nobunaga/Hideyoshi names, e.g. Suidobashi, Jimbocho, Kiba) built around a Sengoku (Warring States)-warlord theme, matching its name (Nobunaga = the 16th-century daimyo Oda Nobunaga). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Tokyo chain izakaya specializing in 80-yen charcoal-grilled yakitori
chef_bio.background: This branch is part of a small Tokyo yakitori chain (multiple branches under the Nobunaga/Hideyoshi names, e. g. Suidobashi, Jimbocho, Kiba) built around a Sengoku (Warring States)-warlord theme, matching its name (Nobunaga = the 16th-century daimyo Oda Nobunaga).
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated corporate philosophy is "まじめにふざけろ" ("be seriously playful"), paired with a promise of "fast, cheap, delicious" (早い・安い・旨い) food and a customer-first attitude.
chef_bio.specialty: 80-yen charcoal (binchotan)-grilled chicken yakitori skewers, made with domestic chicken including limited rare cuts; also known for its jumbo-portion 'Baka-mori' side dishes (chicken katsu, fries, salad, bean-sprout stir-fry).

anecdotes.text: Hanging lanterns give the shop a lively, festival-like feel, and the interior is decorated with katana (sword) objects and folding fans evoking the Sengoku warlord era referenced in the restaurant's name.
The signature 'Baka-mori' (jumbo-portion) side-dish series was created in response to customers asking for bigger servings, reflecting the chain's playful house philosophy.
Course menus are named after Sengoku figures, e.g. the 9-dish 'Sengoku Busho (Warlord) Course' with house-made offal stew, and the 11-dish 'Nobunaga Great Daimyo Course.'

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare (basting glaze) at Japanese yakitori restaurants is standardly soy-sauce based and therefore typically contains wheat; no gluten-free/tamari alternative was mentioned for this restaurant in any source found.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: General yakitori-preparation inference, not restaurant-specific confirmation

cultural_comfort.note: A mainstream Japanese chain izakaya near Suidobashi Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its cheap-skewer format and picture-friendly course menus are easy to navigate with pointing and basic Japanese.

places.name: 天ぷら 天秀 (Tempura Tenhide)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Edo-mae tempura batter is wheat-flour based, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce/dashi typically relies on soy sauce (usually wheat-containing) — neither is confirmed to have a gluten-free alternative here. Search-sourced restaurant information states that all tempura items are fried in the same oil, making full allergen removal difficult even when a dietary request is made in advance. No dedicated gluten-free menu, rice-flour batter, or dedicated fryer was found in available sources, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, detailed confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on seafood tempura (ebi, anago/conger eel, kisu, seasonal fish) and tendon rice bowls, plus seasonal vegetable tempura; the tentsuyu dipping sauce and any accompanying soups are almost certainly dashi-based (typically bonito/katsuobushi) per standard practice at traditional Edo-mae tempura restaurants of this type. No vegetarian or vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan course was identified in available sources.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–13:30 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat & public holidays 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00) only, no lunch service; closed Sundays; also typically closed around New Year (approx. Dec 30–Jan 5)
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku, Shinjuku West Exit area)
places.notes: Tenhide (天秀) was later established independently by a member of that same family lineage, continuing the technique and spirit of Tenkane. Sources describe the restaurant as carrying forward four generations of tempura craft rooted in the 1903 origin. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: proprietor family descends from the Tenkane (1903) tempura lineage
chef_bio.origin: Rooted in a tempura lineage dating to 1903 (Meiji 36), when founder Kanekichi Ishinabe began a tempura yatai (street stall) in Yotsuya, Tokyo; the stall was later formalized into the restaurant Tenkane (天兼) near Shinjuku Mitsukoshi during the Taisho era.
chef_bio.background: Tenhide (天秀) was later established independently by a member of that same family lineage, continuing the technique and spirit of Tenkane. Sources describe the restaurant as carrying forward four generations of tempura craft rooted in the 1903 origin.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasis on premium, seasonally sourced ingredients and high-quality frying oil, applying traditional Edo-mae tempura technique passed down across generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Edo-mae style tempura courses, tempura rice bowls (tendon), and anago (conger eel) tempura.

anecdotes.text: A dedicated restaurant profile describes Tenhide as carrying forward "four generations of skill" rooted in the 1903 Tenkane lineage, with the current shop founded independently by a later-generation family member.

A feature on the Tenkane lineage traces its start to a 1903 tempura yatai stall in Yotsuya Igamachi founded by Kanekichi Ishinabe, later becoming a storefront near Shinjuku Mitsukoshi in the Taisho era.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Restaurant information indicates all tempura items are fried in the same shared oil, so full removal of allergens (including gluten) cannot be guaranteed even with advance notice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As with most traditional Edo-mae tempura restaurants, the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce and dashi based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat unless a specific wheat-free (tamari) alternative is used. No wheat-free soy sauce option was confirmed in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Because all tempura (seafood, meat, and vegetable) is fried in the same oil, vegetable tempura would not be separated from seafood items in the fryer.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Restaurant information suggests staff will try to accommodate dietary requests when informed at the time of reservation or on arrival, but note that complete allergen exclusion is difficult given the shared frying oil.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, formal old-lineage tempura restaurant near Shinjuku Station; expect primarily Japanese-language menus and service, with counter and semi-private tatami seating typical of established Edo-mae tempura shops.

places.name: セキネ (Sekine)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option is mentioned anywhere. The nikuman wrapper is a steamed wheat-flour bun and the shumai wrapper is also wheat-based, so both signature items contain wheat. No source discusses a dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or celiac-safe handling — treat as unsuitable for a gluten-free/celiac diet.
places.vegan_detail: All signature items are meat-based: the nikuman filling is pork mince with onion, and the shumai is also pork. No vegetarian or vegan version is mentioned in any source found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00, no closed days (年中無休)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa 1-chome, near Shin-Nakamise / Fureai-dori, close to
places.cuisine: Dim sum / yum cha takeout counter
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A century-old family-run takeout counter on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise/Fureai-dori corner, known for keeping its recipe unchanged since before the war. It became a favorite of local Asakusa entertainers and comedians over the decades. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1921 (Taisho 10) as a bakery in Iriya, Taito-ku; opened its Asakusa storefront in 1935 (Showa 10) as an eatery, selling steamed pork buns and shumai from a stall beside it.
chef_bio.background: A century-old family-run takeout counter on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise/Fureai-dori corner, known for keeping its recipe unchanged since before the war. It became a favorite of local Asakusa entertainers and comedians over the decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: A small, old-fashioned shop that prides itself on an unchanged, simple recipe — just pork, onion, and technique — rather than trends.
chef_bio.specialty: Nikuman (steamed pork bun) with a juicy, broth-like filling of minced pork and onion, and handmade oversized shumai.

anecdotes.text: Comedian and filmmaker Beat Takeshi reportedly declared this shop's meat bun 'the most delicious in Japan.'
Entertainer couple Hayashiya Pe & Paako are said to always give this shop's meat buns as souvenirs.
The bun's filling releases a broth-like juice because the pork mince is mixed with finely chopped onion, which gives off moisture as it cooks.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple point-and-order takeout window on a busy Asakusa tourist street with a short, priced menu (buns, shumai) — easy to order without Japanese, though no English menu is confirmed.

places.name: 松栄 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店 (Matsue Shibuya Scramble Square)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen protocol was found in any source. Nigiri sushi rice and raw fish are typically gluten-free on their own, but the traditional dipping shoyu almost certainly contains wheat, and some tsumami/appetizer items in the omakase course may include fried or breaded preparations. Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly about wheat-free soy sauce and cross-contact before booking.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-centric Edomae sushi restaurant whose omakase courses (from roughly 13,200 yen, including assorted appetizers, 10 pieces of nigiri, and soup) are built entirely around raw seafood. No vegan menu, vegetable-based sushi, or plant-based course was found in any source, and traditional Edomae tsumami and soup courses commonly rely on bonito-based dashi. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:20, drink L.O. 14:30), 17:00-23:00 (food L.O. 21:30, drink L.O. 22:30), daily; closed irregularly following the Shibuya Scramble Square building's schedule

places.notes: Matsue (松栄) began as a neighborhood sushi shop in Ebisu in 1966 and has since been recognized in Tabelog's Hyakumeiten (Top 100) sushi rankings. The company later expanded to a handful of related locations in Ebisu, and on November 1, 2019 opened this branch on the 12th floor of  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: sushi restaurant (multi-location group, Shibuya Scramble Square branch)
chef_bio.origin: Ebisu, Tokyo (original honten founded 1966)
chef_bio.background: The company later expanded to a handful of related locations in Ebisu, and on November 1, 2019 opened this branch on the 12th floor of the newly built Shibuya Scramble Square tower, directly above Shibuya Station at the famous crossing.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes careful attention to the temperature and serving order of each piece of nigiri, and to sourcing seasonal, market-fresh seafood rather than a fixed, unchanging menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi omakase courses, built around seasonal fish selected personally by the restaurant's buyers ('meikiki', an eye for quality) from Toyosu Market and regional markets in Hokkaido and Kyushu.

anecdotes.text: The Ebisu honten was founded in 1966 as an ordinary neighborhood sushi shop with wax food-sample displays out front; in 1992 it repositioned itself, removing its old signage and printed menu list to focus on an omakase, chef's-choice format.
The Shibuya Scramble Square branch, 'Sushi Matsue,' opened on November 1, 2019, as part of the new commercial tower directly connected to Shibuya Station.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in the very tourist-friendly Shibuya Scramble Square complex with an English Tabelog listing and English-language online reservation page, but day-to-day menu and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 浦島たろう (Urashima Tarou)
places.gf_detail: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter is inherently wheat-flour based, and the restaurant's stated signature is a katsuobushi (bonito) dashi used throughout the menu. No gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free option is mentioned anywhere in available listings, and standard Japanese seasonings used in this cuisine (shoyu, Worcestershire-style okonomiyaki sauce) typically contain wheat. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen's stated specialty dashi is drawn from katsuobushi (bonito flakes), and typical okonomiyaki/monjayaki preparations here include egg, mayonnaise, pork, or seafood toppings (e.g., beef tendon negiyaki). No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegetarian-adapted dish was found in any listing.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidobashi), Chiyoda City — near Suidobashi Station wes
places.cuisine: お好み焼き・もんじゃ
places.notes: A tatami-and-horigotatsu (sunken kotatsu) style okonomiyaki and monjayaki izakaya about a one-minute walk from the west exit of Suidobashi Station, geared toward group parties, birthday celebrations, and women's get-togethers with private-room bookings for 36-48 guests. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A tatami-and-horigotatsu (sunken kotatsu) style okonomiyaki and monjayaki izakaya about a one-minute walk from the west exit of Suidobashi Station, geared toward group parties, birthday celebrations, and women's get-togethers with private-room bookings for 36-48 guests.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter dashi from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) sourced from Toyosu Market, treating that stock as the foundation of its cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki topped with yakisoba noodles, and Osaka-style beef tendon and scallion (gyusuji negiyaki) okonomiyaki, alongside an Italian-style cheese-cream monjayaki.

anecdotes.text: For birthday guests, the restaurant presents a heart-shaped okonomiyaki as a surprise gift.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house dashi used in the okonomiyaki/monjayaki batter is made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes), an animal product, and is described as a specialty of the kitchen, with no plant-based dashi alternative found.

cultural_comfort.note: All discoverable listings, menus, and reservation channels are in Japanese with no sign of an English menu or English-speaking service, so plan to order by pointing at photos or with a translation app.

places.name: eat more SOUP&BREAD（イートモア スープアンドブレッド） (Eat More Soup & Bread)
places.gf_detail: This is a bakery-café built around freshly baked bread — wheat is central to nearly every dish on the menu. No gluten-free bread, buns, or dedicated fryer/prep area were found in any source, and soups/stews are prepared in the same kitchen as the bread. Not recommended for celiac diners; no evidence of any gluten-free accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat- and dairy-based dishes (beef stew, hamburger steak, lobster bisque, cheese-and-onion gratin soup) and standard wheat bread; no vegan-labeled items, plant-based bread, or dairy-free markers were found in any source. With roughly 20 rotating soups, some vegetable-forward options may exist, but this could not be confirmed, and hidden dairy/cream or animal-based stock is likely in most soups. Vegans would need to ask staff about specific ingredients for any given soup.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 daily (lunch 11:00-15:00, tea 15:00-17:00, dinner from 17:00, per menu segmentation)
places.neighborhood: 新宿 (Shinjuku) — Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly connected to S
places.cuisine: パン・スープ・カフェ
places.notes: eat more SOUP&BREAD is a bakery-café concept operated by Big Eats Co., Ltd. (株式会社ビッグイーツ), which also runs the plant-forward "eat more greens" café in Azabu-Juban. This branch occupies the 7th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly connected to Shinjuku Station's east side. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: eat more SOUP&BREAD is a bakery-café concept operated by Big Eats Co. , Ltd. (株式会社ビッグイーツ), which also runs the plant-forward "eat more greens" café in Azabu-Juban. This branch occupies the 7th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly connected to Shinjuku Station's east side.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself as a warm, "cute" bakery café meant to be comforting for both body and mind, built around the simple idea of baking bread fresh in-house every day and pairing it with hearty, seasonal soup.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked bread paired with slow-simmered soups and stews — roughly 20 rotating varieties made from seasonal ingredients, including beef stew, pumpkin potage, lobster bisque, and a cheese-and-onion gratin soup.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 和牛らーめん 極 (Wagyu Ramen Goku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option is mentioned anywhere. This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen/tsukemen shop; the broth is a wagyu bone paitan with a shio (salt) tare, but noodles are ordinary wheat noodles and no gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated prep process was found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option is mentioned anywhere; the entire menu is built around a wagyu (beef) bone broth and wagyu beef chashu (e.g. 和牛らーめん, 和牛まぜそば, 和牛飯), so there is no plant-based path through this menu.

He then spent about two years developing his own wagyu-paitan (beef-bone white broth) recipe,  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 店主(オーナー・ラーメン職人) Ishijima Tetsuya（石島鉄也）
chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Ishijima Tetsuya worked as a bartender from his teens into his early twenties before moving into the ramen world, where he trained for roughly a decade at shops around Tokyo. He then spent about two years developing his own wagyu-paitan (beef-bone white broth) recipe, testing it in borrowed kitchens before opening this shop on Miyamasuzaka on April 24, 2025. The space itself was converted from a former izakaya into a compact, roughly 12-tsubo counter shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ishijima set out to build a ramen shop that doesn't look or feel like a typical ramen-ya — a bright, cafe-like, wood-toned room meant to be comfortable for solo women and families alike — while keeping an eye toward eventually introducing the shop's rich wagyu broth to diners from overseas.
chef_bio.specialty: 極上和牛らーめん (Gokujō Wagyu Ramen) — a rich wagyu bone paitan-shio broth topped with wagyu beef chashu cut from three different parts of the cow.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened April 24, 2025 on Miyamasuzaka, a street the neighborhood is positioning as a developing 'ramen street'; the roughly 12-tsubo space was converted from a former izakaya into a 16-seat counter shop with a street-facing window counter.
Ishijima worked as a bartender in his teens and early twenties, then spent about ten years training at ramen shops in Tokyo before spending two years developing his own wagyu white-broth recipe.

cultural_comfort.note: A newly opened (2025), design-conscious shop whose owner has explicitly said he built the concept with an eye toward eventually welcoming overseas diners, and it has an English-language Tabelog listing, but there is no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so come prepared to point at the menu or use a translation app.

places.name: まぐろとシャリ (Maguro to Shari)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi information is documented anywhere in the available sources. The core dish (tuna over vinegared rice) is likely soy-sauce marinated (shoyu typically contains wheat) and no allergy-specific menu or staff protocol was found. Celiac diners should not assume safety here without directly confirming with staff in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/katsuo-avoidance information is documented. The restaurant's entire concept is centered on raw/marinated tuna over vinegared rice, so it is not vegan-compatible as currently described, and no vegetarian substitution was mentioned in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30), no listed closed days
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya, near Shibuya Station south exit)
places.notes: Maguro to Shari is a tuna-bowl specialty shop that opened in Shibuya on October 26, 2021, supervised by the acclaimed Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku. It sources natural wild bluefin tuna through a professional network working with the Sumiyoshi Fisheries Group, choosing fish rais · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: supervised by Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku
chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (opened 2021, supervised by Ginza's Hakkoku sushi restaurant)
chef_bio.background: Maguro to Shari is a tuna-bowl specialty shop that opened in Shibuya on October 26, 2021, supervised by the acclaimed Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku. It sources natural wild bluefin tuna through a professional network working with the Sumiyoshi Fisheries Group, choosing fish raised in cold waters for high fat content and rich flavor.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around two core elements only — natural tuna and red-vinegar shari rice — letting the quality of the fish and the acidity of the rice carry the dish rather than heavy sauces or many toppings.
chef_bio.specialty: Maguro-Shari-Don — a customizable tuna and red-vinegar-rice bowl where diners choose the tuna and rice portions in gram increments, from a standard size up to an extra-large 'Kiwami' portion, with toppings such as sea urchin and salmon roe available.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant grand-opened in Shibuya on October 26, 2021 as a tuna-bowl specialty shop supervised by the Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku, using only natural wild tuna raised in cold ocean waters for higher fat content and umami.
Diners can build their own bowl, choosing tuna and rice quantities in 40g and 50g increments respectively, from a standard bowl (130g tuna, 250g rice) up to a 'Kiwami' extreme-size portion.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, English-menu-friendly bowl counter aimed at casual solo diners and tourists near Shibuya Station, but with no specific gluten-free or vegan accommodations documented, so allergy communication would need to happen in Japanese or with translation help.

places.name: なぜ蕎麦にラー油を入れるのか。渋谷店 (Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka. Shibuya Te)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, 十割 (100% buckwheat) option, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source. The noodles are described as thick, chewy, house-made soba but not confirmed as 100% buckwheat, and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a traditional soy-sauce-based broth, which in Japan almost always contains wheat. There is no mention of allergy protocols or a separate gluten-free process, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, dashi substitution, or plant-based option was found. The chain's signature and most-promoted dishes are meat-based (肉そば/meat soba, chicken soba), and the tsuyu dipping sauce is a traditional soy-based broth that in Japan is typically made with bonito (katsuo) dashi. Treat as not vegan-friendly absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: 昼の部 11:00〜16:00、夜の部 17:30〜21:30（日曜日は21:00まで、第1・第3日曜日は設備点検のため20:00閉店との情報あり）

("Why Do You Put Chili Oil in Soba?") chain, now with roughly 15 locations around Tokyo (including Shinjuku, Akihabara,  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founder, Ikebukuro Mibu / Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka. chain
chef_bio.origin: Brand founded in Ikebukuro, Tokyo in 2010; this location is the Shibuya branch
chef_bio.background: The brand began as a catchphrase for founder Mibu Hirofumi's first shop, "Ikebukuro Mibu," opened in 2010. It grew into the "Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka. " ("Why Do You Put Chili Oil in Soba? ") chain, now with roughly 15 locations around Tokyo (including Shinjuku, Akihabara, and Shinbashi) under the company Nomimono Co. , Ltd. The concept was conceived as a reaction to the decline of classic soba culture, aiming to reclaim the mid-price dining niche (roughly 500-1000 yen) that ramen had taken over, using a hearty, "junk-food" style meat soba.
chef_bio.philosophy: "蕎麦をもっと自由に楽しく" ("Make soba more free and fun") — breaking from traditional soba conventions to build a bold, modern "soba culture. "
chef_bio.specialty: 肉そば (meat tsukesoba / dipping soba) — thick, chewy house-made noodles served with a slightly sweet-savory dipping broth spiked with the shop's own chili oil (rayu)

anecdotes.text: The provocative shop name itself doubles as an advertisement — its odd phrasing ("Why do you put chili oil in soba?") sparks curiosity and has become a talking point that drives customers to try it.

The Shibuya branch sits along the main street beside Shibuya Stream, about a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station's C2 exit, and is known for very generous noodle portions aimed at office workers looking for a filling, fast meal.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local chain soba shop near Shibuya Station; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu/photos will help when ordering.

places.name: ピッツェリア カポリ (Pizzeria Capoli)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional AVPN-certified Neapolitan pizzeria using imported Italian Caputo wheat flour for its dough; no gluten-free crust, gluten-free menu, or celiac-specific handling was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The classic Marinara pizza (tomato sauce, garlic, oregano, olive oil — no cheese) is vegan by standard Neapolitan recipe, and it is on the menu here alongside the Margherita. Beyond that, no dedicated vegan menu, dairy-free cheese substitute, or vegan labeling was found; the kitchen and wood oven are shared with meat and dairy-topped pizzas (e.g., pepperoni, mozzarella), so cross-contact is possible and unconfirmed. Best to double-check ingredients with staff in person.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri (and days before public holidays): 11:00-15:00, 17:00-22:30 | Sat: 11:00-15:30, 16:00-22:30 | Sun & public holidays: 11:00-15:30, 16:00-22:00. A 330 yen (tax incl.) dinner table charge applies with a 90-minute seating limit.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku (south exit, Shinjuku Station)
places.notes: PIZZERIA CAPOLI opened in March 2011 in the basement of the Shinjuku Youth Building (PAX), a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's south exit. It is a certified member of the Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN, the "True Neapolitan Pizza Association"), the Naples-based  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Operated by Total Well Co., Ltd., a Shinjuku-based restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Naples-style Neapolitan pizza tradition, adapted in Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: PIZZERIA CAPOLI opened in March 2011 in the basement of the Shinjuku Youth Building (PAX), a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's south exit. It is a certified member of the Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN, the "True Neapolitan Pizza Association"), the Naples-based body that authenticates pizzerias following strict Neapolitan pizza-making standards.
chef_bio.philosophy: Follows AVPN's traditional Neapolitan pizza discipline: the wood-fired oven was hand-built by Stefano Ferrara, a master oven craftsman from Naples, using stone and sand shipped in from Naples itself. The kitchen uses imported Italian Caputo flour and adjusts dough hydration, water temperature and fermentation time daily to match Tokyo's changing temperature and humidity.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza — the classic Margherita (tomato, mozzarella, basil) and the cheese-free Marinara (tomato, garlic, oregano, olive oil), plus a house Americana topped with capers, corn, pepperoni, paprika, red onion and cherry tomatoes. Most pizzas can be ordered half-and-half so diners can try two varieties at once.

anecdotes.text: The pizza oven was personally built by Stefano Ferrara, known as a master craftsman among Naples oven-makers, using materials specially transported from Naples.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (synthesized web search results referencing multiple Japanese food-blog listings for PIZZERIA CAPOLI)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant recently switched to Caputo flour from Italy, which it credits with improving both the flavor and texture of its dough, and adjusts ingredients daily based on temperature and humidity.
Listed as an AVPN Japan (真のナポリピッツァ協会) certified pizzeria, with contact capoli@totalwell.co.jp.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu and ordering (via tablet) are primarily Japanese-oriented with an English Tabelog listing for reservations, but no evidence of an English-speaking staff or English allergy menu, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help when asking about ingredients.

places.name: 肉野菜炒め ベジ郎 渋谷総本店 (Vejiro Shibuya Sohonten (Niku-Yasai Itame Ve)
places.gf_detail: No source confirms gluten-free practices. The flavor choices include soy sauce (醤油) and miso based sauces, which typically contain wheat in Japan; fried chicken (唐揚げ) is used as a meat option and its coating ingredients were not confirmed. No information found on a dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce (tamari), or cross-contact precautions. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about the shio-tonkotsu (salt) option and sauce ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A "vegetables only" (野菜だけ) order without meat is available, but this is a stir-fry restaurant built around pork/chicken and adjustable pork back fat (背脂); no source confirms the vegetable-only dish is cooked without animal fat, or that the shoyu/miso/ponzu sauces are free of bonito (katsuo) dashi. Treat as a possible option to request and confirm with staff, not a guaranteed vegan dish.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:10 (L.O. 21:40), daily, with irregular (不定休) closures

That effort became the foundation for Vejiro, a  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Restaurant brand of Food Supply Co., a Tokyo-based vegetable wholesaler
chef_bio.origin: Founded by Food Supply Co., a produce (vegetable) wholesaler run by president Atsushi Takekawa (born 1979, Tottori), who founded the company in 2009.
chef_bio.background: During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, Takekawa launched a contact-free 'drive-through vegetable stand' to sell produce directly to consumers after many of the company's restaurant clients closed and growers lost their usual buyers. That effort became the foundation for Vejiro, a meat-and-vegetable stir-fry restaurant designed to move large volumes of fresh vegetables and support the producers who had been left with unsold crops. The Shibuya main store (総本店) opened near Bunkamura in Dogenzaka in December 2021 and quickly became one of Shibuya's most talked-about lunch/dinner spots, known for long lines.
chef_bio.philosophy: Turn a produce wholesaler's surplus-vegetable problem into a restaurant concept — build a dish that lets one diner eat a very large quantity of vegetables in a single sitting, giving growers a reliable outlet for their harvest.
chef_bio.specialty: 肉野菜炒め定食 (meat & vegetable stir-fry set meal), Jiro-ramen-inspired: a mountain of stir-fried vegetables (customers can order it 'mashi-mashi', with reports of roughly 400g of vegetables per plate) tossed with sliced pork or fried chicken, seasoned with a choice of soy sauce, miso, ponzu, or salt-tonkotsu, and topped with an adjustable amount of pork back fat.

anecdotes.text: A single plate is built around roughly 400g of stir-fried vegetables — described as close to a full day's recommended vegetable intake in one meal.
The restaurant grew directly out of Takekawa's pandemic-era 'drive-through vegetable shop,' created after Food Supply's restaurant clients shut down and growers lost their delivery destinations.

positives.text: The menu includes a vegetables-only order (no meat), for diners who want a plant-forward version of the stir-fry.
The restaurant was created specifically to serve unusually large portions of fresh vegetables per plate, sourced through the operator's own produce-wholesale business.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering happens via a Japanese-language customization system (rice, sauce, vegetable/fat quantity) at a small, often-crowded counter, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board helps.

places.name: goo ITALIANO 渋谷本店 (goo ITALIANO Shibuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The signature pastas are explicitly traditional wheat pasta imported from Gragnano, Italy, so celiac diners should not assume any gluten-free accommodation and should call ahead to confirm before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegetarian or vegan menu was confirmed in any sourced listing. The restaurant serves vegetable-forward Italian dishes (salads, vegetable antipasti) as part of its regional menu, but sauces and dishes may contain meat, seafood (e.g., anchovy), or dairy typical of Italian cooking, and no plant-based substitution policy or hidden-ingredient disclosure was found. Vegan diners should confirm specific dishes with staff before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays and weekends alike: Lunch 11:30-15:00, Dinner 17:00-23:00 (last order 22:00)
places.neighborhood: 宮益坂, 渋谷
places.notes: goo ITALIANO is an Italian restaurant group run by Tokyo hospitality company Take Five (株式会社テイクファイブ), with this Shibuya location as its original/flagship (本店) branch, near Miyamasuzaka a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's east exit. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Italian regional-cuisine restaurant, part of a small multi-location group (also operates a Yoyogi-Uehara branch)
chef_bio.background: goo ITALIANO is an Italian restaurant group run by Tokyo hospitality company Take Five (株式会社テイクファイブ), with this Shibuya location as its original/flagship (本店) branch, near Miyamasuzaka a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's east exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around a "local deliciousness from Italy's 20 regions" concept: the menu is shaped by dishes and ingredients the chef encountered while traveling around Italy, and it is updated whenever something new and impressive is found, with the goal of keeping regional Italian cooking casual, approachable and affordable enough for everyday visits.
chef_bio.specialty: Spaghettoni al limone (lemon cream spaghettoni made with Setouchi lemons, a play on a Sicilian classic) and pasta made from Gragnano-imported durum wheat, slow-dried at low temperature for a chewy texture and wheat aroma.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's food philosophy is to serve only what genuinely surprised or moved the team during trips through Italy, refreshing the menu as new discoveries are made.
The name "goo ITALIANO" is a coined term blending "GOOD Italian" with the growl ("goo") of a stomach craving delicious food.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a Japanese-run neighborhood Italian restaurant with an English Tabelog listing and online table booking, but expect Japanese-language menus and staff — basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, especially for allergy questions.

places.name: 肉ビストロ灯 (Niku Bistro Akari)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol is advertised anywhere. The menu is meat-forward (charcoal-grilled block meat can be naturally simple) but also includes clearly wheat-containing items such as pasta ("シェフのパスタ"), pâté de campagne, and aligot, and courses are often served as fixed sets, so gluten exposure is plausible unless specifically arranged. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead to confirm which grilled-meat items and sauces are wheat-free.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused charcoal-grill bistro with no vegan menu, plant-based mains, or vegan accommodations advertised in any source found. Sides such as green salad or French fries may be vegetable-based but are not confirmed dairy/animal-broth-free, and the concept centers entirely on block meat and wine.

places.notes: Niku Bistro Akari opened in the backstreets of Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome as a new meat-and-wine concept from Zekkocho (株式会社絶好調), the Shinjuku izakaya group led by Masaki Yoshida (of Zekkocho Teppen), built around pairing whole block meat seared over charcoal with a locked, lantern-l · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Niku Bistro Akari opened in the backstreets of Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome as a new meat-and-wine concept from Zekkocho (株式会社絶好調), the Shinjuku izakaya group led by Masaki Yoshida (of Zekkocho Teppen), built around pairing whole block meat seared over charcoal with a locked, lantern-lit wine cellar guests browse themselves.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing famous meat brands, the kitchen selects cuts by season and quality, rotating 6-8 different kinds of block meat daily and searing them hard over binchotan charcoal so the centers stay rare and juicy.
chef_bio.specialty: Block meat -- beef, pork, lamb, and game such as venison and wild boar -- grilled whole over binchotan charcoal and served rare and juicy, alongside a hand-picked cellar of about 150 wines.

anecdotes.text: Diners choose their own bottle by stepping into a key-locked, lantern-lit wine cellar stocked with roughly 150 red, white, and sparkling wines.

The kitchen rotates 6-8 kinds of block meat a day -- from standard beef, pork, and lamb to game like venison and wild boar -- chosen for seasonality and quality rather than brand name, then seared hard over binchotan charcoal.
Meats such as Kumamoto 'aka-gyu' beef and Iwate pork are grilled over roaring binchotan charcoal and brought to the table as showstopping platters.
The restaurant opened on July 7 in Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome as a new bone-in-meat-and-wine concept from Zekkocho Teppen founder Masaki Yoshida.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant is listed on Tabelog's English site and on English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck), but no dedicated English menu or English-speaking-staff guarantee was confirmed, so basic Japanese or gesture-based ordering should be expected.

places.name: 鳥祐（【旧店名】鳥鈴） (Toriyu (formerly Torisuzu))
places.gf_detail: Skewers are grilled to order over charcoal; ordering shio (salt) seasoning avoids the soy-sauce-based tare, which typically contains wheat. However, no dedicated gluten-free menu, prep area, or fryer has been identified, and shio and tare skewers appear to be cooked on the same shared grill, creating a likely cross-contact risk. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on chicken yakitori (liver, gizzard, seseri, asparagus wrapped in pork, etc.) with no vegan menu or dedicated plant-based dishes identified. Typical izakaya side dishes may use bonito/katsuo-based dashi. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00; Sat, national holidays & the day before a holiday 17:00-23:40; closed Sundays

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner has been devoted to yakitori craft for 39 years, with this shop operating at its Shibuya, Meiji-dori-area location for 20 years. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The owner has been devoted to yakitori craft for 39 years, with this shop operating at its Shibuya, Meiji-dori-area location for 20 years.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner personally grills every skewer over charcoal each day, favoring careful, attentive preparation over volume.
chef_bio.specialty: Shio (salt-seasoned) charcoal-grilled yakitori, made with brand chicken sourced from Tottori (Takumi no Daiyama Chicken and Bisakura Chicken)

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the shio (salt) seasoning is especially well executed, with several preferring it to the tare (sauce) skewers, and praise the tenderness of items like liver, seseri (neck meat), and gizzard.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local yakitori-ya with no confirmed English menu, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) helps when ordering skewers and specifying shio (salt) vs. tare (sauce).

No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated preparation area, or allergy protocol was found in any source, so this is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broths are built on pork bone and seafood (niboshi/katsuo dried-sardine and bonito) dashi — not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based menu items or dashi alternative were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:00 (some listings report closing as late as 22:00/23:00, likely reflecting menu-price-era changes), Closed Sunday

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chuka Soba Sakurazaka opened on September 18, 2004, on Sakura-zaka, a small cherry-tree-lined slope in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka-cho. The shop takes its name from that street. It has kept its 14-seat counter running for over two decades in a corner of Shibuya where restaurants typica · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Chuka Soba Sakurazaka opened on September 18, 2004, on Sakura-zaka, a small cherry-tree-lined slope in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka-cho. The shop takes its name from that street. It has kept its 14-seat counter running for over two decades in a corner of Shibuya where restaurants typically turn over quickly.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba and tsukemen (dipping noodles) built on a rich pork-bone and seafood broth (gyokai-tonkotsu) with a creamy, milky body and a pronounced bonito (katsuo) aroma; a shio (salt) soba is also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: Opened in 2004 and still operating from the same small counter on Sakura-zaka in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka-cho, an area known for how quickly local businesses come and go.

A 2025 visit review describes the shop's weathered wooden facade and a thick, well-worn natural-wood counter inside, and notes the tsukemen broth's sardine (niboshi) and seafood notes over springy medium-thick noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: Old-school 14-seat counter ramen shop with a ticket vending machine and no reservations or evident English menu, so a little Japan-dining familiarity helps.

places.name: 三代目 天ぷらすずき (Sandaime Tempura Suzuki (3rd Generation Temp)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free batter, or dedicated fryer is mentioned in any source found. Tempura here is standard wheat-flour batter, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is a dashi-and-soy-sauce base, which conventionally contains wheat. No accommodation for celiac or gluten-intolerant diners is documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or substitutions are documented. The menu is centered on seafood tempura (shrimp, oysters, assorted seafood) with some vegetable tempura pieces, but these are fried in shared oil with seafood, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce uses bonito dashi. Not suitable for strict vegans without prior arrangement, which has no evidence of being offered.

Sun/holidays: 11:30-21:30 (L.O.21:00). Note: hours may shift around Tokyo Dome events per one listing; a separate listing gives Sat/Sun as 16:00-23:00, so treat evening start times as approximate.

roles: Owner/tempura chef — family business now in its third generation (the name "三代目" means "3rd generation")
chef_bio.specialty: À la carte tempura fried to order — shrimp, oysters, and assorted seafood and vegetables

anecdotes.text: The shop sits under the elevated JR tracks by Suidobashi Station's East Exit and is known for frying each piece of tempura fresh to order rather than pre-frying in batches.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a Japanese-menu neighborhood tempura counter with no confirmed English signage or menu; reservations are made through Japanese platforms (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, AutoReserve).

places.name: ヴィーガンカフェ モンキーマジック (Vegan Cafe Monkey Magic (Veganic Monkey Magi)
places.gf_detail: This is a fully vegan kitchen but not a certified or dedicated gluten-free kitchen. Reviewers note that some dishes may use regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce or other wheat-based ingredients unless flagged in advance; the chef will tailor the tasting menu around allergies if told when you book (via Instagram/LINE), but no dedicated GF fryer, GF soy sauce, or formal cross-contamination controls have been confirmed. Celiac diners should communicate the severity of their needs clearly before booking and treat any assurance as best-effort rather than certified.
places.vegan_detail: Every dish on the menu is vegan — the cafe is built entirely on 100% naturally cultivated (pesticide- and fertilizer-free) vegetables and house-made natural fermentation seasonings, with no meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or food additives. As a dedicated vegan kitchen it should not use animal-derived dashi/bonito by default, but confirm any specific ingredient concerns (e.g. use of alcohol/mirin, sourced condiments) when reserving.
places.hours_raw: Reservation-only; open Thursday–Saturday. Thursday dinner 19:00-22:00; Friday & Saturday lunch 12:00-14:30 and dinner 19:00-22:00, served as fixed course menus (e.g. a ¥4,000 Friday lunch course and a ¥6,000 Saturday course). Historical listings show different day/hour patterns, so confirm the current schedule via Instagram (@veganic_monkey_magic) or LINE before booking.

places.notes: Saeki graduated from Dokkyo University's Faculty of Foreign Languages (English Department) and worked in administrative roles before turning toward food around 2005. In 2012 she co-opened a shizen-saibai (naturally-cultivated, pesticide- and fertilizer-free) vegetable restaurant, · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Saeki graduated from Dokkyo University's Faculty of Foreign Languages (English Department) and worked in administrative roles before turning toward food around 2005. In 2012 she co-opened a shizen-saibai (naturally-cultivated, pesticide- and fertilizer-free) vegetable restaurant, and in January 2020 she opened Vegan Cafe Monkey Magic in a quiet alley behind Hanayashiki in Asakusa, on the ground floor of a small inn called Oyado Matsumura.
chef_bio.philosophy: Saeki was vegan herself for several years but stepped back from strict veganism for health reasons; she says she now channels that firsthand experience into cooking she describes as 'vegan food that nourishes the heart,' built entirely on naturally cultivated vegetables and natural fermentation seasonings with no additives.
chef_bio.specialty: A monthly-changing, multi-course tasting menu (including a seasonal vegan take on sushi) built around 100% naturally cultivated vegetables and house-made fermented seasonings.

anecdotes.text: Once vegan herself, Saeki quit strict veganism for health reasons and has used that perspective to create what she calls 'vegan food that nourishes the heart' rather than just the body.
The chef performs in costume as the restaurant's monkey mascot, 'Sarutchaman,' greeting and entertaining guests table-side as part of the dining experience.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a vegan kitchen rather than a certified gluten-free one; no dedicated GF fryer or formal cross-contamination controls were confirmed, so celiac diners should discuss the severity of their needs when booking.

The meat-less nikujaga dish includes kuruma fu (wheat gluten rings), which would not be suitable for those with celiac disease; VegeMap advises choosing a menu only after telling staff about allergies.
Menu features a wheat-gluten rice bowl (車麩丼) and whole-grain wheat gluten (全粒粉車麩); seasonings are naturally-fermented (traditional wheat-containing shoyu).
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch aggregate of tabelog.com, tambo3.net, vegeness.com, vegelab.net

gf_cross_contamination.text: It is a one-woman operation — Chef Monkey-san does all food preparation and cooking herself — with no mention of gluten-free protocols or cross-contamination controls.
Owner Atsuko Saeki (佐伯敦子) has left veganism personally (脱ヴィーガン) but keeps the menu fully plant-based as a business/food-safety decision, explicitly declining to add fish or meat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Some dishes may use regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce or other wheat-based ingredients unless you flag this when booking — ask for a wheat-free adjustment in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant is reservation-only, and the chef designs the tasting course after you tell her about any vegan/vegetarian needs or allergies at the time of booking.

positives.text: The kitchen is fully vegan — no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs anywhere on the menu — built on naturally cultivated (pesticide- and fertilizer-free) vegetables and additive-free seasonings.

cultural_comfort.note: This reservation-only spot is typically booked in Japanese via Instagram DM or LINE, and the chef's tableside "Sarutchaman" performance is in Japanese, so a translation app or local help makes booking and dining noticeably smoother, even though several English-language travel guides have featured the restaurant.

places.name: フルール ド サラザン (Fleur de Sarrasin)
places.gf_detail: The signature galette batter is 100% stone-milled Japanese buckwheat with no wheat flour, milled fresh in-house each morning — a naturally gluten-free base. However, reviewers confirm this is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility: some fillings and the traditional dipping sauce can involve soy sauce or other ingredients containing wheat. Staff (the French-trained owner in particular) are described as knowledgeable and willing to identify safe dishes once a gluten allergy or celiac disease is disclosed, but diners must ask rather than assume every item is safe.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was confirmed. Traditional galette dipping sauce and some fillings are likely to include Japanese dashi (often bonito/katsuo-based) or other animal ingredients typical of the shop's Edo-style presentation. Vegan diners should ask staff to confirm ingredients dish-by-dish rather than assume any item is vegan by default.
places.hours_raw: 11:45-15:00 (food L.O. 14:00) and 17:45-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00), daily including weekends/holidays; irregular closures announced via Instagram/Facebook; on Sunday/holiday evenings the shop may close early once the day's buckwheat dough is sold out.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa, near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station / Tawara
places.notes: A husband-and-wife team who spent years living and working in France, absorbing French galette-making technique and Japanese-craft-cider culture. The owner additionally trained at a soba restaurant in Kanda to deepen his grounding in Japanese buckwheat craft before opening the sh · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Owner (referred to as Mr. Tamakoshi in press coverage) with his wife
chef_bio.origin: Japan (owners spent several years living and working in France before returning)
chef_bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who spent years living and working in France, absorbing French galette-making technique and Japanese-craft-cider culture. The owner additionally trained at a soba restaurant in Kanda to deepen his grounding in Japanese buckwheat craft before opening the shop in Nishi-Asakusa on April 10, 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: To bridge French galette culture and Japanese soba culture by sourcing buckwheat grain directly from domestic producers and milling it on-site each morning, so guests taste the grain's full aroma — served in a distinctly Japanese, chopstick-friendly format alongside a curated list of 30-40+ Japanese domestic craft ciders.
chef_bio.specialty: Galette made from 100% domestically grown buckwheat, hand-selected and stone-milled fresh in-house every morning; the finished galette is pre-rolled and cut into bite-sized pieces so it can be eaten smartly with chopsticks, Edo-style, and is served with a thickened soba dipping sauce instead of the usual knife-and-fork Breton presentation.

anecdotes.text: Unlike a typical galetterie where the crepe is eaten with a knife and fork, here it arrives pre-rolled and sliced into bite-sized pieces so it can be picked up with chopsticks, paired with a thickened soba dipping sauce rather than a simple cheese-and-ham filling.

A traveler with celiac disease recalled the owner bringing out raw buckwheat grain and demonstrating the in-house stone mill, then walking her through exactly which galettes and sauces were safe to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is not a dedicated gluten-free facility — some galette fillings and sauces contain gluten, so diners with celiac disease are advised to tell staff in advance and confirm each dish before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: When a customer disclosed having celiac disease, the chef went through the menu and identified which galettes use a soy sauce containing wheat so those could be avoided.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The French-trained owner is described as very knowledgeable about gluten allergies, showing guests the raw buckwheat grain and the in-house stone mill and explaining which items are safe once notified of an allergy.

positives.text: The galette batter is 100% buckwheat flour, milled fresh in-house every morning, giving the core dish a naturally gluten-free base before any fillings or sauces are added.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, Japanese-run galette shop with a Japanese-only official site, but it appears on multiple English-language gluten-free travel guides and reviewers with celiac disease report staff patiently walking them through safe menu choices.

places.name: 香川一福ART (Kagawa Ippuku ART)

No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi option was mentioned in the official press release, official site, or any food-media coverage reviewed. Tempura is fried in the same shared fryer as other items with no indication of separation. Not suitable for a gluten-free or celiac diet.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat-based creative udon (pork kimchi chili-oil udon, beef udon, chicken tempura over rice) and follows Kagawa Ippuku's traditional Sanuki-style dashi preparation, which conventionally includes bonito (katsuobushi). No vegan-labeled dish, vegetable-only dashi, or plant-based menu option was mentioned in any source reviewed.

ART is positioned as a new 'brand' from the same company, built around the idea of reconstructing tradit · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Udon specialty restaurant, new creative-concept branch of Kagawa Ippuku
chef_bio.origin: Kagawa Prefecture (Sanuki udon heritage)
chef_bio.background: Kagawa Ippuku ART is a creative-udon concept shop opened May 9, 2025, by Kagawa Ippuku, a Sanuki udon specialist that has earned the Michelin Bib Gourmand three years running. ART is positioned as a new 'brand' from the same company, built around the idea of reconstructing traditional Japanese udon through modern technique and outside culinary collaborations, in a retro interior mixing wood, tile, and blue-painted panels with 12 counter seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames 'ART' as a double meaning (French and English) for udon's capacity to take on many different expressions — reworking a traditional Japanese noodle through modern, cross-cultural technique rather than treating Sanuki udon as fixed or purely traditional.
chef_bio.specialty: Rotating creative udon collaborations — e. g. a beef/meat udon supervised by the Ginza restaurant Shinohara, and a French-curry udon using curry from the Michelin-starred Kitajima-tei — alongside standards like green curry udon and pork-kimchi chili-oil udon. The kitchen alternates noodle thickness by calendar day (thick noodles on even-numbered days, thin noodles on odd-numbered days).

anecdotes.text: Menu items are developed with outside supervising chefs, including a beef udon overseen by Ginza's Shinohara and a French curry udon using curry from the Michelin-starred Kitajima-tei.
Noodle thickness changes with the calendar: thick noodles are served on even-numbered days and thin noodles on odd-numbered days.

cultural_comfort.note: Official information is Japanese-only and the shop is a small 12-seat counter concept store; an English-language Tabelog listing exists, but no English menu or English-speaking-staff mention was found.

places.name: らーめん ぽっぽっ屋 水道橋店 (Popposhi-ya Suidobashi-ten (Ramen Popposhiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options found. The noodles are custom extra-thick wheat noodles (100% "オーション" brand wheat) made specially by Asakusa Kaikarō, with no gluten-free noodle alternative mentioned anywhere. The tare is a standard soy-sauce (shoyu) based sauce, which typically contains wheat. No dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol was mentioned in any source.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broth is an animal-based stock (pork leg bones and chicken simmered with vegetables), and no plant-based broth, noodle, or topping option was found in any source. No dashi/katsuobushi specifics were found, but the tonkotsu/chicken base alone rules out vegan suitability.

places.notes: A small, Jiro-inspired (二郎系) ramen counter two minutes from Suidōbashi Station's east exit, part of a tiny local group that also runs a Nihonbashi honten and a Harumi Triton branch. The shop is known in the neighborhood's crowded ramen scene for oversized portions and a less inti · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo (Kanda-Misaki-chō)
chef_bio.background: A small, Jiro-inspired (二郎系) ramen counter two minutes from Suidōbashi Station's east exit, part of a tiny local group that also runs a Nihonbashi honten and a Harumi Triton branch. The shop is known in the neighborhood's crowded ramen scene for oversized portions and a less intimidating ordering style than classic Jiro shops.
chef_bio.philosophy: Comfortable, approachable take on Jiro-style ramen — big, filling bowls without the gruff counter-culture reputation of the originals.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic ³700 ramen (tonkotsu/genkotsu-and-chicken broth, sweet shoyu tare, extra-thick custom noodles), also available as tsukemen or aburasoba, with build-your-own toppings and volume upgrades.

anecdotes.text: A blogger documented ordering the shop's oversized "large-plus-plus" ramen, noting the finished bowl weighed roughly 2kg once loaded with extra noodles and toppings.
A visitor blog described trying the shop's tsukemen (dipping noodles) alongside the standard ramen.
A ramen guide notes the owner lacks the intimidating manner often associated with Jiro-style shops, making it easier for first-time customers to order comfortably.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are custom wheat noodles (100% "Ocean" brand wheat) made by Asakusa Kaikarō; no gluten-free noodle substitute is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare (seasoning base) is described as a sweet soy-sauce (shoyu) based sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made by long-simmering pork leg bones (genkotsu) together with chicken (momiji) and vegetables — an animal-based stock with no vegetable-only broth option mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only Jiro-style ramen shop with Japanese ordering customs (specify noodle amount and topping "mashi" adjustments at order time); no English menu or English-language accommodation was found in any source.

places.name: バンタイ (タイ国料理 バンタイ) (Ban-Thai)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was confirmed in any source I could access. As a traditional Thai kitchen with a 100+ item menu, dishes very likely use standard soy sauce and oyster sauce (both typically contain wheat) as base seasonings; Pad Thai uses rice noodles but its sauce composition is unconfirmed. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering — no online source addresses this.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu is advertised by the restaurant or confirmed by any source I could access (this contrasts with the brief's cuisine tag). The menu does include vegetable and tofu-based dishes (e.g., stir-fried Thai vegetables in oyster sauce, tofu-and-minced-meat soup), but traditional Thai seasoning relies heavily on fish sauce (nam pla), which is very likely present in most dishes by default, including many that look plant-based, unless the kitchen is specifically asked to omit it. Vegan diners should ask staff before ordering rather than assume any dish is animal-product-free.
