places.notes: Opened in Kabukicho, Shinjuku in the late 1980s (one source cites 1985, another 1987) by an owner who fell in love with the taste of Thai food while traveling in Thailand, at a time when Tokyo had only two or three dedicated Thai restaurants. Nearly 40 years later it remains a we · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in Kabukicho, Shinjuku in the late 1980s (one source cites 1985, another 1987) by an owner who fell in love with the taste of Thai food while traveling in Thailand, at a time when Tokyo had only two or three dedicated Thai restaurants.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids premade curry/seasoning pastes, mixing spices fresh for each dish, aiming for a clear, assertive sweet-spicy-sour "Bangkok flavor. " The owner still visits regularly to taste-check dishes so the flavor stays consistent.
chef_bio.specialty: Tom Kha Gai (coconut-chicken soup) and Larb Gai (spicy minced chicken salad) are frequently singled out by reviewers; Pad Thai is also a long-standing menu staple.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was founded after the owner fell in love with Thai food while traveling in Thailand and wanted to share that experience, opening at a time when there were only two or three specialist Thai restaurants in all of Tokyo.
The owner still comes by regularly to check the taste, a routine credited with keeping the flavor unchanged for close to 40 years.
The interior is furnished with Thai-made furniture and tableware so diners feel transported to Thailand while still in Japan.

cultural_comfort.note: The menu is reported to be available in English and several other languages (Korean, Chinese, Thai, French, Italian, Spanish, German), and the restaurant has hundreds of reviews from international visitors on TripAdvisor and Tabelog, making it an easy stop for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 横浜家系らーめん侍 渋谷本店 (Yokohama Iekei Ramen Samurai Shibuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: Only standard thick, wavy wheat noodles (from Sakai Seimen) are offered — no gluten-free noodle substitute is mentioned anywhere. The tonkotsu-shoyu tare is soy-sauce based and virtually certain to contain wheat. No source mentions a dedicated fryer, celiac-safe handling, or any gluten-free accommodation; this is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop and should be treated as unsafe for celiac/gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a tonkotsu (pork bone) broth blended with chicken oil in the shop's signature 'gattun' style and finished with pork chashu; no plant-based broth, protein substitute, or vegetarian/vegan option is offered or mentioned in any review or listing.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂／渋谷駅圏内
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained at Yokohama Ramen Machida-ya (町田家) in Machida, a disciple shop of the historic green-signboard Iekei ramen lineage founded by Takasago-ya (たかさご家) — a branch of the Iekei family tree separate from the main Yoshimura-ya (吉村家) line. The lineage runs Takasago-ya → M · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Yokohama Iekei ramen, Takasago-ya green-signboard lineage via Machida-ya
chef_bio.background: The owner trained at Yokohama Ramen Machida-ya (町田家) in Machida, a disciple shop of the historic green-signboard Iekei ramen lineage founded by Takasago-ya (たかさご家) — a branch of the Iekei family tree separate from the main Yoshimura-ya (吉村家) line. The lineage runs Takasago-ya → Machida-ya (Machida honten) → Samurai (Shibuya). The owner has roughly 20 years of experience in Iekei ramen, including his apprenticeship years.
chef_bio.philosophy: Continuing the distinctive green-signboard Takasago-ya style inherited through Machida-ya, distinct from the mainline Yoshimura-ya branch of Iekei ramen
chef_bio.specialty: 'Gattun'-style concentrated tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: thick, wavy noodles from Sakai Seimen in a rich pork-bone broth finished with chicken oil, topped with large-cut pork chashu, spinach and nori

anecdotes.text: The shop relocated in August 2021 to its current spot on Dogenzaka near Shibuya Station, continuing to fly the green signboard associated with the Takasago-ya lineage of Iekei ramen.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Iekei ramen counter where customers are typically asked (in Japanese) for their preferred noodle firmness, broth richness and oil amount; no English menu was confirmed in any source.

places.name: スタンドヒーロー (Stand Hero)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodations are mentioned in any source. Signature items are wheat-battered/fried (kushikatsu, the famous thick ham cutlet) and sashimi is typically served with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff, and no evidence such confirmation is offered.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is heavily fish- and meat-based (sashimi, fried pork/ham cutlets, kushikatsu) with vegetables described as only a small fraction of the offering; no vegan options, plant-based substitutions, or hidden-dashi/bonito disclosures were found in any source. Not recommended for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30); Sat 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); closed Sundays and public holidays. (One secondary source lists Mon-Sat as a uniform 17:00-23:00 — treat closing time as approximate and confirm by phone if precision matters.)
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidōbashi Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Stand Hero opened in October 2006, run by owner Katsuji Inoue. Inoue trained for roughly a decade in traditional Japanese kappo cuisine before working at the izakaya group Shofudo (a seafood wholesaler that also ran several izakaya), where he helped launch multiple stores, and th · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Stand Hero opened in October 2006, run by owner Katsuji Inoue. Inoue trained for roughly a decade in traditional Japanese kappo cuisine before working at the izakaya group Shofudo (a seafood wholesaler that also ran several izakaya), where he helped launch multiple stores, and then went independent to open his own standing bar near Suidobashi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Each day Inoue visits seven or eight fish wholesalers himself and buys whatever looks best that morning, then chalks roughly 40 sashimi items on the blackboard at that day's market price (ranging from about 50 to over 600 yen a piece), so the menu and prices genuinely change day to day.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut ham cutlet (atsugiri hamu-katsu, sliced 2-3cm thick) and a daily-changing sashimi selection sourced fresh each morning.

anecdotes.text: The thick-cut ham cutlet became well known enough to be featured on the TV program Tamori Club.
Squeezed into just 9.5 tsubo (about 31 square meters), the standing bar has been running for more than seventeen years and sees over 80 customers a day.

The owner tours 7-8 seafood suppliers every morning and buys what's freshest, resulting in around 40 sashimi items priced from roughly 50 to 600-plus yen written on a blackboard menu that changes daily; a 3-piece sashimi platter is offered Saturdays only.

cultural_comfort.note: A locals' standing bar with a handwritten Japanese-only blackboard menu, cash-only payment, and no English signage found, so some Japanese (or a translation app) will help.

places.name: コンテナ（CONTAINER Cafe&Bar） (CONTAINER (Container Cafe & Bar))
places.gf_detail: Multiple independent sources consistently report that every cake on the menu is made gluten-free, sweetened with mineral-rich kibi (unrefined cane) sugar rather than refined white sugar. However, no source confirms whether the savory/meal menu, bar items, or specific sauces are gluten-free, and there is no mention of a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, dedicated fryer, or formal cross-contamination protocol — call ahead to confirm for celiac-level needs.
places.vegan_detail: No source found makes an explicit vegan claim for this cafe — descriptions focus specifically on gluten-free cakes and organic/low-caffeine drinks, with no confirmation of egg-free or dairy-free preparation. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about individual cake ingredients (eggs, butter, dairy) before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict: one consistent pairing (repeated across two independent search summaries, likely both citing the same original listing) gives Tue/Wed/Fri/Sat 13:00–18:00, closed Sun/Mon/Thu plus irregular holidays. Another source gives cafe 13:00–16:30 + dinner 18:00–21:30 (Sun 13:00–17:30), closed only Monday. A third gives Sun 13:00–18:00, Mon closed, Tue–Sat 13:00–17:00 and 18:00–22:00. Hours should be confirmed via the official Instagram/SNS or by phone (03-6457-7526) before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目
places.cuisine: グルテンフリーカフェ・体に優しいケーキ
places.notes: CONTAINER was opened in 2009 by owner Masahiko Onoe, who left his hometown of Fukuoka for Tokyo to pursue a dream of running his own café, gaining experience at a restaurant-operations company before striking out on his own. He still runs the cafe himself and bakes all the cakes. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Fukuoka, Japan (owner's hometown)
chef_bio.background: CONTAINER was opened in 2009 by owner Masahiko Onoe, who left his hometown of Fukuoka for Tokyo to pursue a dream of running his own café, gaining experience at a restaurant-operations company before striking out on his own. He still runs the cafe himself and bakes all the cakes.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is "kindness to heart and body" (心と体にやさしさを): every cake on the menu is made gluten-free and sweetened only lightly with mineral-rich, unrefined kibi (cane) sugar instead of white sugar.
chef_bio.specialty: Brazilian-style pudim (purin) pudding — the cafe's best-known dessert — alongside a classic gateau chocolat and a Basque cheesecake, with rotating seasonal cakes.

anecdotes.text: Tucked into a retro building that also houses the Suehirotei rakugo theater and furnished with antiques and curated music, it's described in local press as "a hideaway café you don't want to tell anyone about," known for unwinding over body-friendly cake and coffee.
A 2023 feature highlighted the Brazilian pudim as the cafe's famous signature item and covered four new autumn dessert releases.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The lunch menu includes lighter items like risotto, croque monsieur, and salads, while dinner features high-protein, low-sugar dishes — croque monsieur is a wheat-bread sandwich, confirming active wheat/flour handling in the same kitchen that makes the GF cakes.
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://san-tatsu.jp/articles/183522/ (via search summary of the article's menu description)

gf_cross_contamination.text: GF claims across all sources are scoped specifically to cakes ('All cakes are gluten-free, made with mineral-rich brown sugar'); no source states the savory food, bar items, or sauces are gluten-free, and none mentions any cross-contamination control or dedicated kitchen.

CONTAINER Cafe&Bar does not appear in FindMeGlutenFree's Shinjuku City or Shinjuku 'Most Celiac Friendly' listings — no celiac/coeliac diner reviews exist to corroborate real cross-contamination safety.

staff_allergy_handling.text: All cakes on the menu are made gluten-free using kibi (unrefined cane) sugar rather than white sugar; for specific allergy needs beyond gluten, callers are directed to confirm ingredients by phone.

positives.text: Every cake on the menu is gluten-free, lightly sweetened with mineral-rich kibi sugar.
Organic coffee and tea are used, including a 97%-decaffeinated coffee option and 100% fruit juices/organic cordial sodas for those avoiding caffeine or seeking lighter drink options.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, independently run neighborhood cafe with no confirmed English menu or signage — basic Japanese or a translation app will help when ordering or asking about ingredients.

places.name: イェネガ (yinega (Yinega))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or celiac protocol was found. The restaurant's own signature dish, couscous served with mutton and root vegetables in tomato sauce, is wheat-based and contains gluten, so it must be avoided by celiac diners. Other dishes on the multinational menu (Kenyan curry, Thai curry, coco rice, pho, and rotating West African stews like yassa/mafé/moambé) may be rice- or stew-based and could be safer, but wheat-containing soy sauce, shared fryers, and cross-contamination have not been confirmed one way or the other — ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated vegetarian course (ベジタリアンコース, listed at 4,400 yen tax included) is offered alongside the regular meat/seafood-forward multinational menu. Because the cuisine is African/international rather than Japanese, hidden katsuo/bonito dashi is less of a risk than at a typical Japanese restaurant, but the vegetarian course has not been confirmed to be strictly vegan (free of dairy, egg, or honey) — ask staff to confirm for a fully vegan meal.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-16:00, 18:00-24:30 daily (some listings show slightly different closing times, e.g. 18:00-00:30) — irregular closures, best to call ahead
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya, near the top of Miyamasu-zaka, ~4 min walk from
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yinega has operated since around 2013 in a quiet spot near the top of Miyamasu-zaka, just a few minutes' walk from the crowds of Shibuya Station. It's run as a personal, well-traveled kitchen rather than a themed chain outlet — reviewers describe it as a cozy hideaway with world/ · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Chef originally from Burkina Faso; menu draws on Africa, the Middle East, South Asia, and South America.
chef_bio.background: Yinega has operated since around 2013 in a quiet spot near the top of Miyamasu-zaka, just a few minutes' walk from the crowds of Shibuya Station. It's run as a personal, well-traveled kitchen rather than a themed chain outlet — reviewers describe it as a cozy hideaway with world/ethnic music (and a permanent DJ booth) that feels far removed from central Shibuya despite the short walk.
chef_bio.philosophy: A retreat-like, music-filled space serving dishes the owner/chef has encountered while cooking across different cuisines, rather than a single-country tasting menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Couscous served with mutton and root vegetables in a tomato sauce — described by one diner as 'the world's smallest pasta,' piled generously with vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The chef previously worked as a cook for an embassy kitchen before opening this multinational restaurant in Shibuya.
The signature couscous, served with mutton and root vegetables in tomato sauce, is called 'the world's smallest pasta' and comes loaded with vegetables — reviewers call it quite good.
The dining room has a permanent DJ booth and plays ethnic/world music regularly, reinforcing the restaurant's identity as a laid-back escape from the Shibuya crowds.

positives.text: The restaurant lists a specific, priced vegetarian course (4,400 yen) as a standing menu option rather than an off-menu special-request-only accommodation.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains an English-language version of its own website, but no source confirms English-speaking staff or an English menu on-site, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) is the safer assumption.

places.name: ヴェジェトゥス プラス（vegetus+） (Vegetus Plus)
places.gf_detail: Multiple independent listings describe the shop as offering "vegan and gluten-free menu options," built around organic/low-pesticide vegetables, brown rice, and homemade dressings/sauces made without preservatives or chemical seasoning. However, no source specifies whether soy sauce used is wheat-free, whether a dedicated fryer exists, or what cross-contamination precautions are in place in what is a small, all-in-one kitchen — celiac-level safety should not be assumed without asking staff directly.
places.vegan_detail: The daily-changing deli lineup includes vegan-compatible dishes alongside non-vegan ones (the shop's own philosophy is "vegetables plus" animal protein/fat, not exclusively plant-based), so not everything on offer is vegan. Staff can identify which of the day's rotating dishes are vegan. No confirmation was found on whether hidden dashi/bonito is used in the non-vegan items or avoided in the vegan ones — worth double-checking on-site.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金・祝前日 11:00〜15:00（売り切れ次第終了）／定休日：土曜・日曜・祝日
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町（水道橋駅徒歩約6分）
places.cuisine: 有機野菜・ベジタリアン・グルテンフリー対応
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop was opened out of a wish to help people eat healthy meals as part of daily life. Before opening, the owner, Hasegawa, read through dozens of books and magazines researching what 'healthy' actually means, having found the word used everywhere but rarely defined with any s · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The shop was opened out of a wish to help people eat healthy meals as part of daily life. Before opening, the owner, Hasegawa, read through dozens of books and magazines researching what 'healthy' actually means, having found the word used everywhere but rarely defined with any specificity.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around organic and low-pesticide vegetables grown without chemical fertilizers, cold-pressed rapeseed and extra-virgin olive oil, natural Italian sea salt, and unrefined sugar — with all dressings and sauces made in-house, free of chemical seasonings and preservatives.
chef_bio.specialty: One-plate lunch set of three rotating deli dishes with green salad and brown rice (around 1,050 yen); the deli menu changes daily with one new item introduced weekly.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from 'vegetus,' the Latin root behind the word 'vegetarian,' meaning 'full of vitality.' The owner added 'plus' because the intent isn't a vegetables-only diet — it's wanting guests to also get energy from animal-based protein and fat for an active life.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Search results across HotPepper, san-tatsu, and generalist directories describe the shop only as offering 'vegan, gluten-free, and takeout' as tag-style options with no detail; explicit statement that results 'do not contain specific information about Celiac disease accommodations, detailed gluten-free menu items, or wheat allergy warnings' and to 'contact the restaurant directly.'

Shop philosophy and preparation detail describe cold-pressed oils, sea salt, unrefined cane sugar, 'no chemical seasonings or preservatives,' and 'all dressings and sauces are homemade' — a natural-food posture with no mention of wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated fryer, or allergen separation.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch snippet summarizing san-tatsu.jp/articles/312127 and shop description

gf_cross_contamination.text: HappyCow / FindMeGlutenFree celiac-review search for 'Vegetus Plus Suidobashi gluten free celiac' returned no listing or celiac review for this specific shop — only unrelated dedicated-GF venues (Gluten Free T's Kitchen, Gluten Free 61).
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch: happycow.net / findmeglutenfree.com results (no match for Vegetus+)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shop's self-described concept is 'vegetables plus' animal protein/fat with a daily-changing deli of ~10 items mixing vegan and non-vegan dishes; no confirmation that vegan items avoid dashi/bonito or that non-vegan dashi is kept separate.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch snippet summarizing vegetusplus.com concept and san-tatsu.jp/spots/312567

positives.text: Meals are made with organic and low-pesticide vegetables, brown rice, and homemade dressings/sauces with no preservatives or chemical seasoning, and the shop explicitly lists vegan and gluten-free options among its rotating deli dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood lunch deli with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app or simple Japanese is helpful when asking about specific ingredients.

places.name: 個室居酒屋 番屋 西新宿住友ビル店 (Koshitsu Izakaya Banya Nishi-Shinjuku Sumito)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce is documented anywhere for this restaurant. Even the dedicated vegetarian tasting course contains wheat: it includes 梅おろしうどん (ume-oroshi udon, wheat noodles) and tempura (wheat-flour batter), and the kitchen's general soy sauce is standard (wheat-containing). There is no indication of gluten-free accommodation beyond a general willingness to discuss allergies with staff, which the restaurant itself does not guarantee to be fully allergen-free.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated vegetarian tasting course (8 dishes — mizuna simmered greens, avocado/yuba sashimi, steamed vegetable platter, mixed-bean salad, asparagus/shiitake/shishito tempura, tofu-and-eggplant in thick sauce, ume-oroshi udon, and soy-milk ice cream — plus 2 hours all-you-can-drink, roughly ¥5,500-6,500) is explicitly described by the restaurant as containing no meat, fish, or dairy. It must be arranged in advance and is offered to accompany a vegetarian guest within a group otherwise ordering the standard menu, rather than being a standalone always-available vegan menu. Hidden dashi/katsuobushi in the rest of the (non-vegetarian-course) menu is not addressed by any source found, so diners should assume standard Japanese kitchen practices (bonito-based dashi) apply outside the designated course.
places.neighborhood: 西新宿・都庁前
places.notes: Part of the 番屋 (Banya) group of private-room izakaya, this Nishi-Shinjuku branch occupies the 49th floor of the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building. Its dining rooms — sunken-kotatsu seating, tatami rooms, and sofa seating — are arranged around floor-to-ceiling windows looking out over th · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Part of the 番屋 (Banya) group of private-room izakaya, this Nishi-Shinjuku branch occupies the 49th floor of the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building. Its dining rooms — sunken-kotatsu seating, tatami rooms, and sofa seating — are arranged around floor-to-ceiling windows looking out over the Shinjuku skyline, and it is used heavily for business dinners and private parties.
chef_bio.specialty: Sashimi platters sourced from Tsukiji, and yakitori/tsukune skewers made from selected domestic chicken, with the meal traditionally finished with the restaurant's signature white curry udon.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant markets its private dining rooms, roughly 200 meters above the street, primarily around their night view of Shinjuku — a signature blog post walks through which rooms have the best skyline view for a dinner date or client entertaining.

positives.text: Rather than leaving vegetarian diners to piece together a meal from a meat- and fish-centric menu, the restaurant has a named, pre-set vegetarian course (8 dishes) explicitly described as free of meat, fish, and dairy, bookable when a vegetarian guest is part of a group.

cultural_comfort.note: English-language booking is available through TableCheck and other reservation platforms, but on-site menus and service are primarily Japanese, so bring a translated allergy card or key phrases when discussing dietary restrictions.

places.name: テンキ (Tenki)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen's core tempura batter (used across most menu items, including the signature shrimp dish) is made with all-purpose wheat flour, cornstarch, rice flour, dry yeast and beer — gluten is built into the standard preparation. No gluten-free batter, substitution, or dedicated fryer has been reported by any source.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood tempura with fish-based mousses and sauces — e.g., the signature shrimp dish incorporates fish sauce and a shrimp-shell reduction — and no vegetable-only tasting course or vegan menu has been documented in any source, so it is not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–24:00, irregular days off (不定休); reported closures vary slightly by listing
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (Sakuragaoka-cho), near Shinsen/Shibuya Station
places.notes: The kitchen's core tempura batter (used across most menu items, including the signature shrimp dish) is made with all-purpose wheat flour, cornstarch, rice flour, dry yeast and beer — gluten is built into the standard preparation. No gluten-free batter, substitution, or dedicated · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Co-owner: Keita Mera (目良慶太, IT-industry background)
chef_bio.origin: French-trained chef who worked in Tokyo French kitchens (including Akebonobashi's Sucre Salé) and in Paris before becoming owner-chef of Bistro Rigole in Sangenjaya, where he earned the Michelin Bib Gourmand in 2018 and 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kameyatani breaks tempura down into five components — ingredient, batter, frying oil, seasoning and presentation — and reworks each one dish-by-dish, applying French technique (mousselines, reduction sauces, fermented batters) to the traditional tempura format, paired with a roughly 300-bottle list of mostly white and orange natural wines.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature shrimp tempura: a round parcel of shrimp wrapped in a shrimp mousseline (blended with coconut oil, sesame paste and fish sauce), coated in a tomato-tinted, beer-leavened batter, fried in sesame and grapeseed oil, and served over a concentrated Américaine sauce made from the shrimp heads and shells.

anecdotes.text: The signature shrimp dish was conceived before the restaurant even opened, inspired by Southeast Asian shrimp toast; the head-and-shell Américaine sauce is built to ooze out when the piece is bitten into.

Tenki opened in March 2023 as the second restaurant from the team behind KAMERA (a shumai and oolong-highball standing bar in Shibuya's Hyakkendan), and grew into one of the neighborhood's most in-demand counters within its first year.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core tempura batter across the menu is a wheat-flour, cornstarch, rice-flour and beer mixture leavened with dry yeast and tinted with tomato powder — there is no separate gluten-free batter or fryer described.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature and most-discussed dishes are seafood-based, using shrimp mousseline blended with Vietnamese fish sauce (nyokumamu) and finished with an Américaine sauce made from shrimp heads and shells — animal products run through the core preparation, not just as a garnish.

cultural_comfort.note: A trendy, reservation-driven counter bar with no confirmed English menu or multilingual staff; expect a Japanese-speaking experience, best booked via a Japanese-reading concierge or platform.

places.name: 肥後一文字や (Higo Ichimonjiya)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard tachigui-soba shop with no gluten-free accommodations found or claimed anywhere. Soba here is very likely a wheat-buckwheat blend typical of budget standing-soba counters, the tsuyu dipping/broth is soy-sauce (wheat) based, and the signature dish is wheat-battered deep-fried tempura (pork tempura) cooked in what is presumably a shared fryer. No dedicated gluten-free fryer, noodle, or sauce option was documented. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodations were found. The shop's dashi/broth is presumed bonito(katsuo)-based like most standard soba-ya, and its signature dish is pork tempura soba, making it a meat-forward, non-vegan-friendly establishment with no documented plant-based options.
places.neighborhood: 神保町・水道橋（神田猿楽町）

The shop leans into its owner's personal interests — Kumamoto ("Higo") references in the name and decor, plus a visible fondness for the Hanshin Tigers base · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Named for "Higo," the historical name for the Kumamoto region, reflecting the owner's Kumamoto ties
chef_bio.background: A small standing-soba counter tucked on a backstreet between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, run day-to-day by its owner. The shop leans into its owner's personal interests — Kumamoto ("Higo") references in the name and decor, plus a visible fondness for the Hanshin Tigers baseball team — giving an otherwise utilitarian tachigui-soba stand a distinct, homey character.
chef_bio.specialty: 豚天そば (pork tempura soba) — a bowl topped with an oversized slab of deep-fried pork

anecdotes.text: The shop's best-known bowl is topped with a single, oversized piece of pork tempura draped over the noodles, priced in the 360–400 yen range — a favorite of nearby office and university workers looking for a fast, filling meal.
A reviewer noted the shop is run by an owner who is an enthusiastic Hanshin Tigers fan, with the counter and walls decorated accordingly.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic tachigui (standing) soba counter where you order from a Japanese-only ticket vending machine and hand the stub to the owner — no English menu was found, so some Japanese comfort helps.

places.name: 新宿ワイン酒場 (Shinjuku Wine Sakaba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any source found. The menu includes wheat-containing items typical of an Italian/Spanish-style bar (e.g., gyoza dumplings, likely bread/pasta-style small plates), and it operates as a general shared kitchen. Celiac diners should not assume safety here — ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No source confirmed a vegan menu or vegan labeling. The menu includes a vegetable appetizer platter (¥910) alongside oysters, shellfish, and Iberico pork gyoza, and Japanese-Italian fusion dishes here commonly rely on bonito/katsuo-based dashi and animal-based stocks that are not disclosed. A vegetable side dish could likely be assembled, but there is no confirmed dedicated vegan option — vegans should ask staff about hidden dashi/animal stock before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 17:00–01:00 (approx., next-day close); Sat–Sun: 15:00–01:00 (approx., next-day close). Sourced from aggregated listing data; exact holiday hours unconfirmed.

It is an independently run wine bar (not part of a chain), seating roughly 20 across counter and table seats. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner personally curates the natural-wine list
chef_bio.background: A small, narrow second-floor bar tucked up a stairway near Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, in the Shinjuku Sanchome dining area. It is an independently run wine bar (not part of a chain), seating roughly 20 across counter and table seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around natural wine paired with seasonal, Italian/Spanish-leaning small plates — the idea being that the food and the glass-poured natural wines are chosen to enhance each other.
chef_bio.specialty: Small-plate tapas-style dishes such as "Ebi no Taiyo" (shrimp), Iberico pork gyoza, oysters, and a vegetable appetizer platter, alongside 10+ natural wines available by the glass (many under ¥500/glass).

anecdotes.text: The owner carefully selects natural wines said to be gaining attention for their health-conscious appeal, keeping 10+ types available by the glass at all times.
Reviewers describe the space as compact and cozy, centered on a counter, about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking-staff signals were found in available sources; this reads as a locals'-style neighborhood wine bar best suited to visitors comfortable ordering in Japanese or by pointing at photos.

places.name: 京都宇治 藤井茗縁 (Kyoto Uji Fujii Meien)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer/prep-area statement was found in any source reviewed. The menu includes 宇治抹茶そば (Uji-matcha soba) — most Japanese soba (not 十割/100%-buckwheat) contains wheat flour as a binder — and Kyoto-style obanzai dishes, which typically use shoyu (soy sauce, usually wheat-containing) and dashi. Treat as a higher-risk option for celiac diners and ask staff directly about wheat in the soba, soy sauce, and dashi before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan menu. The offering centers on Kyoto-style obanzai kaiseki sets, kamameshi rice dishes, matcha soba, and matcha/hojicha parfaits and other tea sweets. Traditional obanzai and dashi-based dishes in this style of Japanese cooking commonly use bonito (katsuobushi) stock, and the parfait/sweets menu likely contains dairy and egg. No statement found about plant-based dashi or dairy-free desserts — ask staff about the dashi base and dessert ingredients before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00), daily; hours follow the Tokyu Plaza Shibuya / Shibuya Fukuras building's operating calendar. Weekend/holiday seating may run in four time slots (11:00–13:00 / 13:00–15:00 / 15:00–17:00 / after 17:00) with a 90-minute seating limit per a Tabelog reservation-conditions snippet.

places.notes: The shop is run by Fujii Chaen, a tea-growing family business in Uji, Kyoto that has cultivated tea on its own roughly 1.2-hectare plantation since the Tenbun era of the Muromachi period (mid-1500s) — about 480 years of continuous tea cultivation. This Shibuya location, opened De · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Operated as a branded tea salon of Fujii Chaen, a family-run Uji tea garden (est. Muromachi era)
chef_bio.origin: Uji, Kyoto
chef_bio.background: The shop is run by Fujii Chaen, a tea-growing family business in Uji, Kyoto that has cultivated tea on its own roughly 1. 2-hectare plantation since the Tenbun era of the Muromachi period (mid-1500s) — about 480 years of continuous tea cultivation. This Shibuya location, opened December 5, 2019 on the 6th floor of Tokyu Plaza Shibuya (Shibuya Fukuras), was one of the garden's first branded retail/dining outposts outside Uji itself.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the family tea garden's own Uji leaves — grown and processed under centuries-old practice — directly to Tokyo diners, using the tea itself (sencha, matcha, hojicha) as the centerpiece of both savory dishes and desserts rather than as a mere flavoring.
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha and hojicha brûlée-style parfaits, kamameshi (iron-pot) rice dishes, an obanzai-style kaiseki tea-meal set, and Uji-matcha soba, all built around the garden's own tea leaves

anecdotes.text: 京都宇治 藤井茗縁 opened December 5, 2019 inside Tokyu Plaza Shibuya, described as a tea salon where guests can enjoy food and sweets made with quality leaves nurtured by the 480-year-old Uji Fujii Chaen tea garden.
Fujii Chaen has continuously grown tea on its own plantation in Uji since the Tenbun era of the Muromachi period, and the Shibuya shop lets guests taste dishes and desserts made from those leaves.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a major Shibuya Station-front shopping complex (Tokyu Plaza Shibuya / Shibuya Fukuras) with an English Tabelog listing, but no source confirms an English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese will help.

places.name: うな重（うなしげ） (Unashige (Suidōbashi))
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional-style unagi (eel) specialist: the eel is basted in a house tare sauce, which in Japanese kabayaki cooking is almost universally soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or ingredient labeling was found in any source consulted (Tabelog, press coverage, blog visit reports). No mention of a dedicated fryer or GF-specific handling was found — likely not applicable since the eel is grilled, not fried, but cross-contact with the shared tare/dashi station cannot be ruled out. Diners with celiac disease should treat this as not gluten-free and confirm directly with staff (Japanese-language only, in person) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Every dish centers on grilled freshwater eel; no plant-based, vegetarian, or eel-free alternative was found in any menu description or press coverage. The shop's self-serve dashi broth station (used to turn the rice bowl into an ochazuke/hitsumabushi-style dish) is, per standard Japanese practice, very likely bonito/katsuo-based and not vegan. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町、千代田区（水道橋駅徒歩3分）
places.notes: Opened July 1 near Suidōbashi Station, this eel-rice-bowl shop (branded in Japanese simply as "うな重," pronounced "Unashige," and marketed as UNASHIGE) was created by restaurateur Hamano Kōichi (濱野浩一). Hamano previously spent years at the Global Dining restaurant group leading new- · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened July 1 near Suidōbashi Station, this eel-rice-bowl shop (branded in Japanese simply as "うな重," pronounced "Unashige," and marketed as UNASHIGE) was created by restaurateur Hamano Kōichi (濱野浩一). Hamano previously spent years at the Global Dining restaurant group leading new-store openings, then headed its planning and development division from 2003 to 2010, before going independent to run cafés and small lodging businesses. He founded the eel-shop concept as a standalone company aiming to make unagi (normally an expensive, special-occasion dish in Japan) into an everyday, fast-food-priced meal.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated concept is a "unaju fast-food shop": by systematizing operations and streamlining eel sourcing, the shop hand-bastes and grills eel to order in the traditional style but sells it from about ¥1,210-1,320 — far below typical unagi-restaurant prices — to bring eel to a broader, younger, everyday clientele (office workers and students in their 20s) rather than treating it as a once-a-year luxury.
chef_bio.specialty: Unajū / unadon — eel fillets basted in a house tare sauce and charcoal/grilled to order in-house, plus shirayaki (unsauced grilled eel) and a hitsumabushi-style option using free self-serve dashi broth poured over the rice.

anecdotes.text: Another reviewer titled their post "あらゆる面で鰻屋らしくない" ("unlike an eel restaurant in every way"), highlighting the touch-panel self-ordering system and fast-food-style operation as a break from traditional unagi-ya conventions.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 新潟地酒と旬肴 ぼんや 新宿三丁目店 (Niigata Jizake to Shunko Bonya (Bonya, Shinj)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is documented. This is a traditional izakaya: soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and bonito/kelp dashi run through most dishes, and the signature Tokamachi hegisoba noodles use funori seaweed as a binder but are conventionally milled with wheat flour blended into the buckwheat, so they cannot be assumed gluten-free. The Tochio-style deep-fried tofu (aburaage) is fried in oil of unknown dedication. Celiac diners should not consider this restaurant safe without direct, on-site confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is centered on fish (nodoguro sashimi/grilled/simmered), Echigo chicken, and other Niigata seafood and meat specialties. Broths and sauces (including the aburaage/deep-fried tofu dishes) likely rely on bonito (katsuo) dashi. No vegan menu, dish substitutions, or plant-based options are documented in any indexed source.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict: one set of listings shows Mon-Fri lunch 11:30-15:00 and dinner 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00), Sat/holidays 12:00 or 16:00-22:30, closed Sundays; another aggregator shows weekday hours as 16:00-24:00 and weekend/holiday 16:00-23:30. The lunch+dinner/Sunday-closed pattern was corroborated by two independent listing sources and is used for hours_week below.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku-Sanchome
places.notes: An izakaya branch (Shinjuku-Sanchome location) of a small Niigata-cuisine concept that imports fresh fish directly from the Joetsu coast of Niigata Prefecture and stocks around 60 varieties of Niigata sake, built around introducing Tokyo diners to Niigata's regional food and drin · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Niigata regional izakaya specializing in fish sourced directly from Niigata/Joetsu and Niigata sake
chef_bio.origin: Niigata Prefecture regional cuisine, brought to a Shinjuku-Sanchome izakaya setting
chef_bio.background: An izakaya branch (Shinjuku-Sanchome location) of a small Niigata-cuisine concept that imports fresh fish directly from the Joetsu coast of Niigata Prefecture and stocks around 60 varieties of Niigata sake, built around introducing Tokyo diners to Niigata's regional food and drink culture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Direct-from-Niigata sourcing of both fish and sake so Tokyo customers can experience Niigata's coastal and mountain regional specialties paired with local sake in one sitting
chef_bio.specialty: Nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) served as sashimi, grilled, or simmered, alongside Tokamachi-style hegisoba noodles (bound with funori seaweed) and Tochio-style deep-fried tofu (aburaage)

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese sake-and-regional-food izakaya with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, though online reservation platforms (Tabelog, AutoReserve) support English/browser translation for booking.

places.name: 小料理屋 さと (Koryoriya Sato)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented anywhere. This is traditional Japanese home cooking, which relies on wheat-containing soy sauce and typically bonito/kombu dashi. Notably, one of the kitchen's signature specialties is 車麩の煮物 — simmered kuruma-fu, a wheat-gluten product from the proprietress's native Niigata — meaning wheat gluten is a deliberate ingredient on the menu, not just an incidental seasoning. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation is documented. Obanzai-style Japanese home cooking typically includes vegetable-forward simmered/pickled dishes, but standard preparation uses bonito (katsuobushi)/kombu dashi and likely includes fish and meat dishes on the same counter spread, so nothing here can be assumed vegan without asking directly and in Japanese.
places.neighborhood: 西浅草 (Nishi-Asakusa), Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter restaurant tucked down a back alley in Nishi-Asakusa, run single-handedly by its proprietress (女将), who prepares every dish herself and lines the counter daily with an ever-changing spread of homemade obanzai (Japanese home-style side dishes). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 新潟県 (Niigata Prefecture) — the proprietress's hometown
chef_bio.background: A small counter restaurant tucked down a back alley in Nishi-Asakusa, run single-handedly by its proprietress (女将), who prepares every dish herself and lines the counter daily with an ever-changing spread of homemade obanzai (Japanese home-style side dishes).
chef_bio.philosophy: Health-conscious, from-scratch home cooking made with care for the body — comfort food rather than restaurant showmanship.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai built around her native Niigata's local cuisine, including simmered kuruma-fu (車麩の煮物, a braised wheat-gluten specialty of Niigata) alongside seasonal vegetable dishes.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe the feeling of walking in as being 'like coming home,' saying every dish carries genuine home-cooked warmth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free process documented; kitchen prepares a wheat-gluten (fu) dish as a signature item, indicating no separation of gluten-containing ingredients.

positives.text: Small, single-proprietress kitchen where every dish is made to order/by hand, which could in principle allow a dish to be discussed and adjusted if asked directly in Japanese — but this is not a documented policy, only an inference from the restaurant's small scale.

cultural_comfort.note: A one-woman counter restaurant with no English menu evidence and no dietary signage found; expect Japanese-only interaction, so bring translated dietary cards if you need GF/vegan accommodation.

places.name: 名代 富士そば 水道橋店 (Nadai Fuji Soba Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are a standard soba-shop blend that includes substantial wheat flour (multiple sources describe standard Fuji Soba branches as roughly 40% buckwheat / 60% wheat flour, unlike a special higher-buckwheat variant offered at one different branch). The company's own allergen chart lists both wheat (小麦) and buckwheat (そば) as allergens present even in the plain kake soba. Not suitable for celiac or wheat-avoiding diners.
places.vegan_detail: The base broth is fish-based: the company's own allergen chart lists mackerel (さば) and soy as allergens present in the plain kake soba, indicating a bonito/fish-derived dashi rather than a kombu-only vegan stock. No vegan-specific menu items or substitutions are documented.
places.hours_raw: 04:00–03:00 daily (L.O. 02:45); closed roughly 45 minutes from 3:00 AM per administrative guidance

The stall's format spread to Nishi-Ogikubo, Shinjuku, and Ikebukuro, and in 1972 th · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founder / Chairman of Daitan Group (parent company of Fuji Soba)
chef_bio.origin: First shop "Soba-sei" opened in Shibuya, Tokyo in 1966; formally incorporated as Daitan Food Co. in 1972
chef_bio.background: Fuji Soba traces back to a single standing-soba stall called "Soba-sei," opened in Shibuya in 1966 by Michio Tan, a former real-estate dealer who wanted a business with dependable daily cash flow. The stall's format spread to Nishi-Ogikubo, Shinjuku, and Ikebukuro, and in 1972 the operation was formally incorporated as Daitan Food Co. , rebranding as 名代 富士そば (Fuji Soba). It has since grown into one of Tokyo's largest standing-soba chains, with this Suidobashi branch being one of many locations across the city rather than an independent shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tan built the group around independently-run sister companies: branches, even near the same station, are often operated as separate subsidiaries so that each store stays competitive rather than functioning as one centrally managed chain.

anecdotes.text: Group founder Michio Tan started out in real estate but pivoted to food service because, unlike property deals, a noodle stall brings in cash every single day.
Daitan Group branches are deliberately run as separate, independently-accountable subsidiary companies, even when stores sit near the same station, to preserve an internal spirit of competition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fuji Soba's official allergen chart lists both wheat and buckwheat among the specified allergens even for the basic kake soba, and independent sources describe standard-branch noodles as roughly 40% buckwheat flour blended with 60% wheat flour.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a standard soba-tsuyu shop, the seasoning broth is soy-sauce based; soy is listed on the company's own allergen chart for the basic kake soba.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The plain kake soba broth already contains fish (mackerel) per the company's allergen chart, indicating a bonito/fish-based dashi rather than a kombu-only vegan stock; no vegan-specific broth or substitution is documented.

positives.text: The company publishes an official allergen reference chart covering its menu, offering more ingredient transparency than most fast standing-noodle shops.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-only ticket vending machine (often color-coded by dish type) that you hand to staff; there's no English menu, but the picture/color system makes it manageable without fluent Japanese.

places.name: 地鶏割烹 おはじき 新宿 (Jidori Kappo Ohajiki Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation was found in any source. The menu is built around karaage (wheat-battered fried chicken) and dishes that typically rely on soy sauce (commonly wheat-containing in Japan) for seasoning and dipping sauces. No mention of a dedicated fryer or wheat-free soy sauce. Not recommended for diners with celiac disease or a wheat allergy without calling ahead and confirming specifics directly with the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, options, or accommodations were found. This is a chicken specialty kappo restaurant centered on raw chicken sashimi, grilled skewers, liver pâté, karaage, and chicken hot pot — there is no indication of plant-based dishes, and dashi/broths in a restaurant of this style would be expected to contain bonito/chicken stock unless otherwise confirmed with staff.
places.hours_raw: Lunch (Mon-Fri only): 11:00-14:00 (no lunch service weekends/holidays). Dinner: 18:00-24:00 (daily). Fri & Sat late-night bar 'Yorimichi Gohan & BAR Ohajiki': 24:00-29:00 (i.e. midnight-5:00am).

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ohajiki Shinjuku is a hideaway-style kappo restaurant near Shinjuku Station's west exit built around Koshu jidori — a free-range chicken breed raised in Yamanashi Prefecture and known for firm texture and rich flavor. The restaurant is listed by the Yamanashi Prefecture tourism o · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Koshu, Yamanashi Prefecture (roots in Yamanashi's jidori chicken tradition)
chef_bio.background: Ohajiki Shinjuku is a hideaway-style kappo restaurant near Shinjuku Station's west exit built around Koshu jidori — a free-range chicken breed raised in Yamanashi Prefecture and known for firm texture and rich flavor. The restaurant is listed by the Yamanashi Prefecture tourism office as an official Tokyo outlet serving the region's jidori chicken, underscoring a direct sourcing relationship with Yamanashi producers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around showcasing the quality of Koshu jidori chicken across raw, grilled, and simmered preparations, paired with a curated selection of Yamanashi wines and sake from across Japan, inside a retro Showa-era interior meant to feel like an adult hideaway near the station.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh tori-sashi (raw chicken sashimi), charcoal-grilled chicken skewers, an original-recipe liver pâté (rebā pate) served with wine and sake, karaage fried chicken, and mizutaki chicken hot pot.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is registered on Yamanashi Prefecture's own tourism site as one of the prefecture's approved 'yamaaji' outlets in Tokyo serving Koshu jidori chicken.
The liver pâté is described as made from an original secret recipe, popular paired with wine and sake.

cultural_comfort.note: A local, Japanese-oriented hideaway restaurant near Shinjuku Station's west exit; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service was found, so diners without Japanese may want translation help.

places.name: 道玄坂マンモス (Dogenzaka Mammoth)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are wheat ramen noodles, and the house specialty noodle is made by kneading in extra wheat germ (胚芽麺) — more wheat-forward, not less. The pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce (typical for this style of tsukemen), and no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy-safe preparation was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is a rich pork-bone-and-seafood (魚介豚骨) tsukemen soup — not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based menu items, vegetable-only broth option, or vegan accommodation were found in any source. Kanji branding referencing 'vegepota' (ベジポタ) refers to a vegetable-enriched pork-bone broth style, not a vegan dish.
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka 2-chome, Shibuya (near Shinsen)
places.notes: Opened February 18, 2011 as the Shibuya branch/sister shop of Enji (えん寺), the well-known Kichijoji tsukemen restaurant credited with originating the rich 'vegepota' (vegetable + pork-bone) style of tsukemen broth that later spread across Tokyo. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened February 18, 2011 as the Shibuya branch/sister shop of Enji (えん寺), the well-known Kichijoji tsukemen restaurant credited with originating the rich 'vegepota' (vegetable + pork-bone) style of tsukemen broth that later spread across Tokyo.
chef_bio.specialty: Rich tsukemen (濃厚つけ麺): a thick pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth accented with yuzu citrus and menma, served with a choice of two house noodles — a standard thick chewy noodle or an unusual noodle kneaded with wheat germ (胚芽麺) for extra aroma and sweetness.

anecdotes.text: The shop's distinctive wheat-germ noodle (胚芽麺) is singled out by food writers as unusually fragrant and slightly sweet compared to standard ramen noodles.
Described by ramen bloggers as one of the most concentrated ('most rich') tsukemen broths in the Shibuya area, blending seafood and vegetable umami in the En-ji lineage style.

cultural_comfort.note: Small 11-seat counter-only local ramen/tsukemen shop with no confirmed English menu or signage — expect a typical Japanese neighborhood noodle-shop experience, ordering by pointing or basic Japanese.

places.name: SWING 水道橋店（スイング） (SWING Suidobashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated fryer was found in any source. Signature dishes (bara-tare pork belly, beef tendon omurice, beef tendon red wine stew, house sausage) rely on soy-sauce-based glazes, flour-based sauces/roux, and bread or rice pairings typical of yoshoku cooking, so wheat cross-contact should be assumed throughout the kitchen. Celiac diners should not rely on this spot without calling ahead (03-3515-7280) to ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around pork belly, beef tendon, and German/Japanese-style sausage, with beer as the featured drink pairing. No vegetarian or vegan dishes, plant-based substitutions, or dashi/bonito disclosures were mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for vegan diners without prior arrangement directly with the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 15:00–23:00; Sat, Sun & holidays 11:30–23:00 (may open from midday on weekdays during Tokyo Dome events); open year-round except around New Year's.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町 (Kanda-Misakicho), Chiyoda-ku, near Suidobashi Station
places.cuisine: 洋食・ダイニングバー
places.notes: SWING Suidobashi-ten is a small yoshoku (Japanese-style Western food) and craft-beer dining bar near Suidobashi Station and Tokyo Dome, run by owner-chef Ozaki Chieko. It is part of a small independently run SWING group of casual beer-and-yoshoku bars in the area. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: SWING Suidobashi-ten is a small yoshoku (Japanese-style Western food) and craft-beer dining bar near Suidobashi Station and Tokyo Dome, run by owner-chef Ozaki Chieko. It is part of a small independently run SWING group of casual beer-and-yoshoku bars in the area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Casual, comfort-food yoshoku plates (pork belly, beef tendon, house sausage) built to pair with an extensive list of German and Belgian import beers.
chef_bio.specialty: Stamina bara-tare — pork belly simmered in a sweet-and-savory brown sugar glaze

anecdotes.text: The signature Stamina Bara-tare set meal — pork belly glazed in a sweet-spicy brown sugar sauce — was created by owner-chef Ozaki Chieko after she was inspired by a pork belly dish she tried in Wakayama.
Another standout is beef tendon braised in red wine for over five hours, and 'Meister Togane' sausage, a Japanese-made sausage certified at Germany's IFFA Frankfurt International Sausage Exhibition.

cultural_comfort.note: All available menu and listing information is in Japanese with no evidence of an English menu, so non-Japanese-speaking visitors may want a translation app or local help.

places.name: 手打蕎麦 渡邊 (Teuchi Soba Watanabe (Hand-Made Soba Watanab)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, options, or protocol were found in any source. This is a hand-made soba shop, and soba at shops of this style is typically blended with wheat flour (a 100%-buckwheat "juwari" option was not confirmed here). Tempura batter is wheat-flour based, and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is very likely made with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. Not recommended for celiac diners without a directly-confirmed, dedicated preparation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or options were found in any source. As a traditional soba shop, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is almost certainly made with dashi that includes katsuobushi (bonito flakes), and tempura may be fried in oil shared across items. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct, dish-by-dish confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:30; closed Sunday (one source also lists public holidays as closed)
places.neighborhood: West Shinjuku (西新宿), near Shinjuku Station West Exit
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A hand-made soba (teuchi soba) and tempura shop that has operated near Shinjuku Station's West Exit for over 40 years, blogs describe it as a long-running neighborhood fixture rather than a modern or trend-driven restaurant. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: West Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: A hand-made soba (teuchi soba) and tempura shop that has operated near Shinjuku Station's West Exit for over 40 years, blogs describe it as a long-running neighborhood fixture rather than a modern or trend-driven restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes hand-made soba prepared on-site, with a glass-enclosed workspace at the front of the restaurant letting customers watch craftsmen roll and cut the noodles by hand.
chef_bio.specialty: Ten-zaru soba (cold soba noodles served with tempura, including shrimp, kabocha squash, carrot and nori tempura fried in sesame oil)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has a glass-walled soba-making area near the entrance where staff in white uniforms can be seen hand-rolling and cutting noodles, and the dining room is decorated with ikebana flower arrangements.
A visiting blogger described the ten-zaru's tempura as fragrant with sesame oil, with two pieces of plump shrimp alongside vegetable tempura, and the noodles as having a chewy, springy texture.

cultural_comfort.note: An old-school, cash-only local soba shop with no confirmed English menu or signage, so bring a translation app to communicate any allergy needs.

places.name: 浅草豆花大王 (Asakusa Tofa King (Asakusa Toka Daiō))
places.gf_detail: The signature douhua is made only from soy milk and a coagulant with no wheat in the base, and most syrup choices (brown sugar, brown-sugar ginger, peanut) and toppings (tapioca, red bean, mung bean, taro balls, herbal jelly) appear naturally wheat-free based on ingredient descriptions found. However, this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: the shop also serves congyoubing, a Taiwanese wheat-flour scallion pancake, prepared in the same space, so cross-contact is possible. No information was found on a dedicated fryer or a formal allergen protocol — diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about how the douhua is prepared alongside the pancake item.
places.vegan_detail: The soy-milk douhua base and most syrup/topping combinations (brown sugar syrup, tapioca, red bean, mung bean, taro balls, peanuts, herbal jelly) contain no animal products. A search-engine summary of the shop's Vegewel listing states the shop's sweets use no animal-derived ingredients apart from the condensed milk (練乳) drizzled on one shaved-ice item, and that vegan customers are welcome. The menu also includes the savory item congyoubing (scallion pancake), whose vegan status was not confirmed. Ask staff to point out any non-vegan items when ordering.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 12:00-20:00 (one source lists 20:01), Saturdays/Sundays/Holidays 11:00-19:00, closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku
places.notes: The shop's owner, Shida Akihisa (nicknamed "Dai-ō," meaning "the King," hence the shop's name), has a Taiwanese mother and spent time as a child in Taipei, particularly in the old downtown district of Wanhua near Longshan Temple — an area he describes as feeling like Taipei's own · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened April 2016 in Asakusa
chef_bio.background: The shop's owner, Shida Akihisa (nicknamed "Dai-ō," meaning "the King," hence the shop's name), has a Taiwanese mother and spent time as a child in Taipei, particularly in the old downtown district of Wanhua near Longshan Temple — an area he describes as feeling like Taipei's own Asakusa. He grew up eating douhua there and, after years of self-taught trial and error trying to recreate the authentic texture and flavor he remembered, opened Asakusa's first dedicated douhua specialty shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Shida's stated goal is to spread douhua in Japan in a form that suits Japanese palates, treating himself as something of a "missionary" for the dessert. Beyond the shop, he runs douhua-making classes for individuals and groups, and several of his students have gone on to open their own douhua-related shops elsewhere in Japan.
chef_bio.specialty: Douhua (豆花) — a silky, unsweetened soy-milk pudding served warm or cold, customized to order with a choice of syrup (brown sugar, brown-sugar ginger, or limited-batch peanut) and a pick of over ten toppings such as tapioca, red bean, mung bean, taro balls, and herbal jelly. The shop also serves Taiwanese-style shaved ice and the street snack congyoubing (scallion pancake).

anecdotes.text: A local Taito City tourism feature describes the shop as tucked away like a "hidden house" in Asakusa where visitors can experience the world of jiggly, silky douhua.

A Sanpo no Tatsujin (散歩の達人) feature is titled "The douhua missionary pursuing the taste he first encountered as a child in Taipei," tying the shop directly to Shida's childhood memories of Wanhua district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop also serves congyoubing (a wheat-flour Taiwanese scallion pancake) alongside the soy-based douhua, indicating the kitchen is not exclusively wheat-free even though the douhua itself contains no wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A search-engine summary of the shop's Vegewel restaurant listing indicates that aside from the condensed milk (練乳) used on one shaved-ice item, the shop's sweets use no animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Search-engine summaries citing the shop's Vegewel listing describe it as animal-product-free aside from the condensed milk on shaved ice, and note the shop welcomes vegan customers.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in dessert shop popular with both locals and Asakusa-area visitors; ordering involves picking a syrup and toppings, which is easy to navigate even with limited Japanese, though menus and signage are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 背脂ラーメン チャッチャ亭 (Seabura Ramen Chaccha-tei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found in any source. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles and the tare is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which contains wheat; no mention of a gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol. Not suitable for a celiac diner.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options found. The broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based and finished with rendered pork back fat, the signature element of the dish, and the shop's entire concept centers on that pork fat — there is no indication of a vegetable- or soy-based broth alternative.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00, reportedly open daily including Sundays (one older snippet listed 11:00-22:30, so confirm current hours before visiting)
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka / Shinsen, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on Dogenzaka in Shibuya in August 2023 by owner Manabu Kobayashi, taking over the site of the previous shop, Tokyo Seabura Ramen Menō (東京背脂らーめん 麺王), and rebranding it to put its signature back-fat technique front and center. The shop has since grown into a small local mini · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened on Dogenzaka in Shibuya in August 2023 by owner Manabu Kobayashi, taking over the site of the previous shop, Tokyo Seabura Ramen Menō (東京背脂らーめん 麺王), and rebranding it to put its signature back-fat technique front and center. The shop has since grown into a small local mini-chain, with a second location opening in Shimokitazawa in May 2026.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen insists on A-grade back fat, prized for its clean sweetness and lack of gaminess compared to cheaper grades, and refuses to serve fat that has lost its freshness. Because the back fat itself carries so much richness, the pork-bone broth is kept deliberately simple so extra seasoning doesn't fight the fat's own flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Seabura ramen — a pork-bone shoyu broth finished by straining and ladling a generous pour of slow-simmered pork back fat over the noodles just before serving.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from the crisp 'chaccha' sound the melted back fat makes as it is strained through a sieve and ladled over the ramen at the finish.
Owner Manabu Kobayashi specifically sources A-rank back fat for its sweetness and absence of off-odors, and won't plate fat that isn't fresh.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone shoyu (soy sauce) base, and soy sauce in Japanese ramen shops is standardly wheat-based unless stated otherwise; no gluten-free or wheat-free tare was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local ramen counter with a Japanese-only menu and no dedicated English signage found in any source — bring a translation app or be ready to point at photos.

places.name: バンコックコスモ食堂 (Bangkok Cosmo Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is documented in any source found. Thai cuisine here leans on rice (jasmine rice, some rice noodle dishes) which lowers baseline wheat exposure versus Japanese izakaya food, but soy sauce, oyster sauce, and other seasonings used in the kitchen are not confirmed wheat-free. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6261-1977) and ask about ingredients dish-by-dish rather than assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegetarian or vegan menu is documented. Thai home-style cooking of this kind typically relies on fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste (kapi) as base seasonings, so most dishes should be assumed non-vegan unless the kitchen confirms otherwise. Some vegetable-forward items (e.g., fresh spring rolls) may be adaptable on request, but this was not independently confirmed by any source found — ask staff directly and consider calling ahead.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:15-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 21:45), Monday-Saturday and public holidays; closed Sundays. (One secondary listing gives slightly different weekday hours of 11:00-17:00/17:00-23:00 — treat the times above, corroborated by Tabelog and Gnavi, as the more reliable baseline and confirm by phone if precision matters.)
places.neighborhood: Nishikanda (西神田), Chiyoda-ku — between Jimbocho and Suidobas
places.notes: Bangkok Cosmo Shokudo is run by a Thai cook from Khon Kaen Province in Thailand's Isaan region who trained at well-known restaurants in Thailand before coming to Japan. It opened in October 2016 as the newest member of a small family of Thai "shokudo" (Peanuts Shokudo, Pony Shoku · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Isaan region, Khon Kaen Province, Thailand
chef_bio.background: Bangkok Cosmo Shokudo is run by a Thai cook from Khon Kaen Province in Thailand's Isaan region who trained at well-known restaurants in Thailand before coming to Japan. It opened in October 2016 as the newest member of a small family of Thai "shokudo" (Peanuts Shokudo, Pony Shokudo, and Lalala are its sister restaurants), set in a retro, Showa-era storefront along Hakusan-dori near Jimbocho.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen insists that gapao "means nothing without the aroma of real holy basil," so it sources holy basil directly from Thai-run contract farms rather than substituting Japanese basil, aiming for the sweet-spicy-sour balance of true Isaan home cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Gai Gapao (ガイガパオ) — coarsely hand-minced chicken stir-fried with holy basil, served over jasmine rice with a fried egg

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is one of a small group of Bangkok-named shokudo (Peanuts, Pony, Lalala, and Cosmo) known for hiring genuine Thai cooks and sourcing specialty ingredients like contract-farmed holy basil for their gapao.
Opened October 2016 in a retro storefront themed around 'Asian chaos' on Jimbocho's Hakusan-dori.

positives.text: The kitchen is particular about ingredient sourcing — it uses genuine holy basil from Thai-run contract farms for its gapao dishes rather than a substitute, suggesting attentiveness to authentic, unadulterated flavors.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood Thai "shokudo" popular with local office workers and students, not a tourist-oriented spot — basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will help, though staff are used to explaining spice levels.

places.name: シズラー 新宿三井ビル店 (Sizzler Shinjuku Mitsui Building)
places.gf_detail: The Premium Salad Bar's large selection of whole fresh vegetables and fruit gives gluten-sensitive diners real, naturally GF choices, but it is a self-serve buffet: official menu descriptions confirm bread, pasta, and taco tortillas are set out on the same bar as the produce, dressings' ingredients are not itemized, and no dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or allergen labeling has been confirmed for this Japan location. Celiac diners should serve vegetable/fruit items first, ask staff about specific dressings, and treat cross-contact as likely rather than controlled.
places.vegan_detail: The salad bar's fresh vegetables, fruit, and some pasta/rice items give vegans real choices, but many of the deli salads, dressings, soups, and hot dishes share the same buffet and are not confirmed to be dairy-, egg-, or fish/bonito-free — Japanese buffets commonly use bonito-based dashi in soups and some dressings. No vegan-specific menu, labeling, or confirmation was found for this location, so check ingredients dish-by-dish.
places.hours_raw: 平日 (Mon-Fri): Lunch 11:00-15:00, then 15:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30) — effectively continuous 11:00-22:00. 土日祝 (Sat/Sun/holidays): Morning 8:00-10:00, then 10:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30) — effectively continuous 8:00-22:00.

places.notes: Sizzler began in 1958 when the Johnson family opened a steakhouse in Culver City, California, paring the menu down to two cuts of steak with salad and bread and serving it through a then-novel self-service format so a steak dinner could be affordable. The brand landed in Japan in · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: California, USA (Culver City, 1958); first Japan location, 1991
chef_bio.background: Sizzler began in 1958 when the Johnson family opened a steakhouse in Culver City, California, paring the menu down to two cuts of steak with salad and bread and serving it through a then-novel self-service format so a steak dinner could be affordable. The brand landed in Japan in 1991, and this Shinjuku Mitsui Building restaurant was Japan's very first Sizzler. The store closed in December 2023 for a full renovation and reopened in May 2025 with a 'catering party'-inspired layout built around a slimmer, easier-to-navigate salad bar.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on Sizzler's founding idea of making a steak dinner accessible through a self-serve, all-you-can-eat format centered on a big salad bar rather than a formal steakhouse experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Premium Salad Bar with 70+ rotating items — fresh vegetables, deli salads, soups, pasta, tacos and desserts — served alongside grilled steaks; the all-you-can-eat Cheese Toast is a house classic dating back to the original 1968 Sizzler Hollywood menu.

anecdotes.text: Sizzler's Cheese Toast first appeared in 1968 at the original Sizzler in Hollywood and has been served all-you-can-eat, just like the salad bar, at Sizzler locations worldwide ever since, including here.

This location was Sizzler's first restaurant in Japan when it opened in 1991; after an 18-month renovation the flagship reopened in May 2025 and the brand ran a year-long 'ALL-STAR REVIVAL FAIR' revisiting menu items from its 34 years in Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The self-serve Premium Salad Bar places wheat-containing items — bread, pasta, and taco tortillas — on the same buffet line as the fresh vegetables and fruit, using shared serving utensils, so cross-contact is likely for anyone strictly avoiding gluten.

Sizzler is a chain which typically means a high risk of cross-contact, and it is likely not possible to dine safely at this establishment if you have celiac disease or must avoid cross-contact; the Shinjuku Mitsui location is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease.

The Premium Salad Bar offers 70+ items always available including seasonal vegetables, fresh deli salads, soup, fruit, pasta, tacos, dessert and drinks — all set out on the same self-serve bar, i.e., wheat items (pasta, tacos/tortillas, bread) physically alongside produce.

EU/US 'gluten-free labeling' and Japan's 'allergy labeling' are based on different standards; gluten-free does not necessarily mean wheat-free — Japan has no unified gluten-free standard, so an allergen menu does not equal celiac-safe handling.

Salad bar dressings are numerous (5 dressings, 6 vinegars, 3 oils, plus mayonnaise) and not itemized for allergens at the bar; Japanese buffet soups/dressings commonly rely on bonito dashi and contain dairy/egg — relevant to hidden animal products for vegans and unlabeled gluten sources.

positives.text: With 70+ items including whole fresh seasonal vegetables and fruit, the salad bar gives diners plenty of naturally plant-based, unprocessed options to build a meal around without relying on prepared or sauced dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-run American-concept chain restaurant with a visual, point-and-serve salad bar that's easy to navigate with minimal Japanese, though menus and staff are not confirmed to be English-fluent at this specific location.

places.name: 道玄坂たまごけん (Dogenzaka Tamagoken)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation was found in any source. The chicken rice, demi-glace/hayashi sauce, and breaded katsu toppings are all conventionally made with wheat-based ingredients (soy sauce, flour-thickened sauce, breadcrumbs), so this is not considered safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct kitchen confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu was found. Dishes are built around egg, chicken, butter, dairy-based/demi-glace sauces, and meat toppings (including Wagyu katsu); there is no indication of a plant-based rice, sauce, or protein alternative.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・道玄坂（渋谷駅徒歩5分）
places.notes: This Shibuya Dogenzaka shop is the 10th location of Kanda Tamagoken, a Tokyo omurice (omelette rice) chain. It opened on Dogenzaka-dori in April 2025 with an open kitchen so guests can watch the omurice being made counter-side. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanda Tamagoken chain (10th location)
chef_bio.background: This Shibuya Dogenzaka shop is the 10th location of Kanda Tamagoken, a Tokyo omurice (omelette rice) chain. It opened on Dogenzaka-dori in April 2025 with an open kitchen so guests can watch the omurice being made counter-side.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated aim is to make proper, well-made omurice something anyone -- including solo diners -- can enjoy easily and casually, not just a special-occasion dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Fluffy, molten ("toro-toro") egg omurice over freshly pan-fried chicken rice, topped with tomato or hayashi demi-glace sauce; the Wagyu katsu omurice is a signature draw.

anecdotes.text: A food writer described eating the Wagyu katsu omurice at the Dogenzaka shop as making for a very happy, indulgent Shibuya meal.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern chain restaurant with an open kitchen and app-based mobile ordering ("注文くん"); menus and ordering are Japan-oriented and no English-speaking-staff or English-menu confirmation was found, though Tabelog offers an English listing page.

places.name: とり焼一 (Toriyaki Hajime)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional tare-style yakitori-ya — the glaze (tare) used on many skewers is typically soy-sauce based (wheat). Shio (salt-grilled) skewers may avoid the tare glaze, but no dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or cross-contamination statement was found on any source, and no English allergy information was located. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead (03-3288-0031) and ask specifically about tare ingredients and shared grill/cross-contact.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on chicken: skewer courses (yakitori), chicken-and-egg oyakodon, chicken ramen, and chicken ochazuke. No vegan or vegetarian options were found in any source; this is a chicken-focused specialty restaurant.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-14:30 (lunch), 17:00-23:00 (dinner); closed Sundays and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact, stylish yakitori restaurant tucked near Suidōbashi Station, built around the idea of letting guests enjoy quality grilled chicken and sake in a calm, black-toned interior — the kind of understated hideaway suited to a date or a quiet client dinner. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A compact, stylish yakitori restaurant tucked near Suidōbashi Station, built around the idea of letting guests enjoy quality grilled chicken and sake in a calm, black-toned interior — the kind of understated hideaway suited to a date or a quiet client dinner.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on selecting quality local/heritage chicken breeds and grilling each part of the bird individually, so diners can taste the difference between cuts rather than treating yakitori as a single uniform dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Skewer courses built from carefully sourced regional chicken breeds — jiyō-kei, Niwa black chicken (丹羽黒鶏), and Kyoto red jidori (京紅地鶏) — grilled part by part so each cut's character comes through; at lunch, the shop is known for an oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) that has been featured in magazines.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's lunchtime oyakodon (chicken and egg rice bowl) has been featured in magazines.
The concept of the shop is described as letting guests enjoy quality grilled chicken (焼鶏) and sake, using selected chickens such as jiyō-kei, Niwa black chicken, and Kyoto red jidori.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional neighborhood yakitori-ya with no evidence of English menus, English signage, or foreign-visitor accommodations — expect a Japanese-language-only experience.

places.name: こんごう庵 神保町店 (Kongoan Jinbocho)
