places.gf_detail: Hegisoba is traditionally bound with funori seaweed rather than egg or nagaimo, which in principle needs less wheat flour than some other soba styles, but this shop does not publish its soba-to-wheat-flour ratio, and standard izakaya tsuyu (dipping sauce) and other cooked dishes in Japan typically use wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free fryer, dedicated cookware, or celiac-specific protocol was found in any source. Treat as ask-only: call ahead and confirm ingredients/cross-contact directly before relying on this for a celiac-safe meal.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and pork-centered Niigata izakaya: signature items include hegisoba served with a standard (likely bonito/katsuo-based) tsuyu dipping sauce, tare-katsudon (pork cutlet), shrimp tempura, and fresh Niigata seafood dishes. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan-adapted hegisoba was found in any source, so it should not be assumed vegan-friendly beyond possibly a side vegetable dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri: Lunch 11:30–14:30, Dinner 17:00–23:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays: Lunch 11:30–15:30, Dinner 17:00–21:00
places.neighborhood: Jinbocho (神保町), Chiyoda-ku — one minute's walk from Jinbocho
places.cuisine: Niigata-style hegisoba soba and regional Ech
places.notes: Kongoan is a small Niigata-origin restaurant company whose Tokyo branches (including this Jinbocho location, a one-minute walk from Jinbocho Station exit A5) bring Niigata's signature hegisoba noodles and prefectural sake to the capital. The shop advertises fresh seafood and vege · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Niigata regional cuisine izakaya / soba restaurant
one of ~4 locations run by the Niigata-rooted company Kongoan (main store in Niigata, plus Tokyo branches in Jinbocho, Okachimachi and Yushima/Ueno)
chef_bio.origin: Niigata Prefecture (main store), with this being the Jinbocho branch in Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Kongoan is a small Niigata-origin restaurant company whose Tokyo branches (including this Jinbocho location, a one-minute walk from Jinbocho Station exit A5) bring Niigata's signature hegisoba noodles and prefectural sake to the capital. The shop advertises fresh seafood and vegetables shipped directly from Niigata rather than sourced locally in Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Niigata's food and drink culture — hegisoba, fresh Niigata seafood/vegetables and Echigo regional sake — for a Tokyo audience, pairing izakaya-style seasonal dishes with a hegisoba finish
chef_bio.specialty: Hegisoba — Niigata-style soba noodles bound with funori seaweed (instead of egg or nagaimo yam) and served pleated in small individual bundles on a large wooden box ("hegi"), giving a smooth, glossy, chewy texture

anecdotes.text: A lunchtime blog review praised the shop's tare-katsudon (sauce-glazed pork cutlet rice bowl) paired with hegisoba as a satisfying, good-value set (around ¥1,000–¥1,050), also calling out the shrimp tempura.
A food-walking blog visited specifically for the hegisoba and Niigata local sake, describing the pairing of Echigo sake flights with seasonal seafood/vegetable dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed; this reads as a traditional Japanese regional-cuisine izakaya best visited with a translation app or basic Japanese.

places.name: なんじゃもんじゃ (Nanjamonja)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu centers on okonomiyaki and monjayaki, both made from wheat-flour batter cooked on a shared teppan grill, so there is no gluten-free path here. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free batter were mentioned by any source; soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce used are standard (wheat-containing) varieties. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based course, or vegan labeling was found. In principle a vegetable-topping okonomiyaki (no meat/seafood) could be ordered, but the standard okonomiyaki/monjayaki sauces and dashi used in this style of cooking commonly contain bonito (katsuo) or other animal-derived stock, and this was not confirmed either way for this restaurant. Vegan diners would need to ask staff in detail about sauce and dashi ingredients before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Sources vary: some report weekdays 16:30-24:00 with Sat/Sun 13:00-16:00 and 16:30-24:00 (lunch break in between); one HotPepper-derived summary reports a flat 16:00-24:00 daily; another reports 18:00-24:00 weekdays and 17:00-24:00 Sun/holidays. Consistent across all sources: no regular closed days (無休), last food order 23:00, last drink order 23:30.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-Sanchome), Shinjuku
places.notes: Nanjamonja is one location in a small multi-branch okonomiyaki/monjayaki izakaya group (other branches exist in Nakano-Shimbashi and Hōnan-chō in Tokyo, plus locations in Nagoya, Osaka, and Toba). The Shinjuku-Sanchome branch sits on Suehiro-dōri and spans the 3rd and 4th floors  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Nanjamonja is one location in a small multi-branch okonomiyaki/monjayaki izakaya group (other branches exist in Nakano-Shimbashi and Hōnan-chō in Tokyo, plus locations in Nagoya, Osaka, and Toba). The Shinjuku-Sanchome branch sits on Suehiro-dōri and spans the 3rd and 4th floors of the Icchō Building, with tatami zashiki seating, sunken kotatsu tables, and a teppan counter, built around all-you-can-eat/all-you-can-drink okonomiyaki and monjayaki courses.
chef_bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat course featuring dozens of okonomiyaki and monjayaki varieties (roughly 70-80 types) grilled tableside on the teppan, alongside standard izakaya small plates.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki are both wheat-flour-batter dishes cooked on a shared teppan grill used for all orders, so cross-contact with gluten is essentially unavoidable; no gluten-free batter or dedicated equipment was found in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegetable-only or vegan course was identified; okonomiyaki/monjayaki are typically prepared with dashi-based sauces and shared teppan surfaces alongside meat and seafood items.

cultural_comfort.note: Listings and menus found are Japanese-only with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so bring a translation app or basic Japanese.

places.name: イタリアンチーズバル AND TABLE 渋谷店 (Italian Cheese Bar AND TABLE Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is advertised anywhere for this restaurant. The menu centers on pizza, pasta, and bread-adjacent cheese dishes (wheat-heavy), and none of the listing sites mention gluten-free options, a dedicated fryer, or allergen protocols. Diners with celiac disease should not assume safety here and would need to call ahead in Japanese and get explicit confirmation before relying on this venue.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is advertised. The food is built around cheese, cured/aged meats (roast beef, horse-meat carpaccio), and seafood-based pasta, with no mention of plant-based substitutions, hidden dashi/katsuo disclosure, or vegan-friendly dishes on any listing site found. Vegan diners should not expect suitable options without calling ahead, and even then accommodation is unconfirmed.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・センター街（渋谷駅徒歩3分）
places.cuisine: イタリアン・チーズバル
places.notes: Opened in 2021 on Shibuya's Center Street, this Italian cheese bar is run by a young owner-manager team rather than a large corporate chain. The kitchen is led by a chef with prior experience at established restaurants, cooking with seasonal ingredients. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2021 on Shibuya's Center Street, this Italian cheese bar is run by a young owner-manager team rather than a large corporate chain. The kitchen is led by a chef with prior experience at established restaurants, cooking with seasonal ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: A casual, date-friendly "cheese bar" concept blending authentic Italian technique (seafood pasta, cured meats) with playful fusion touches, served in a dim, café-like room meant to feel relaxed rather than formal.
chef_bio.specialty: Cheese-forward Italian small plates and pizza, all-you-can-eat cheese course, aged (jukusei) roast beef, and seared horse-meat carpaccio; crab cream pasta is frequently mentioned as a standout.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as run by a young owner and manager team focused on hospitality and "surprising guests while keeping their original passion."
Known for an all-you-can-eat cheese course alongside aged beef and horse-meat carpaccio, with a relaxed, softly lit interior suited to dates and small groups.

cultural_comfort.note: No evidence of an English menu or foreign-visitor-oriented service was found; this reads as a standard Japanese-language izakaya-style bar aimed at local diners.

places.name: ビストロ イルシーブ (Bistro Elusive)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free sauce/soy-sauce information was found in any source. As a French bistro, sauces are likely to use wheat-based roux/beurre manié and bread is likely served as part of the meal; cross-contamination risk in the kitchen is unconfirmed either way. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6281-3300) to discuss specific dishes before booking.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, hidden-dashi/bonito disclosure, or plant-based course was found in any source. The restaurant's menu and reviews center on meat (duck) and Toyosu-market seafood in an omakase/course format, suggesting limited built-in vegan options; a la carte vegetable dishes may exist but are unconfirmed. Vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神保町（神田猿楽町）
places.notes: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free sauce/soy-sauce information was found in any source. As a French bistro, sauces are likely to use wheat-based roux/beurre manié and bread is likely served as part of the meal; cross-contamination risk in the kitchen is unconfirm · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: シェフ（詳細経歴は未確認）
chef_bio.philosophy: 枠にとらわれない自由な発想で四季を感じさせる創作料理を提供し、農園の有機野菜、豊洲市場の天然魚介、丁寧に下処理した厳選肉を使い、素材の持ち味と組み合わせの妙を大切にする
chef_bio.specialty: スモーク仕立ての鴨料理（鴨のミニバーガー、ジューシーな鴨のメイン皿など）を中心とした季節の創作フレンチ

anecdotes.text: 路地の一角にある小さめのビストロで、静かな空気の中、スモークの香りが立つ鴨のミニバーガーや、柔らかくジューシーなメインの鴨など、魚も肉も種類豊富で、一皿ごとに満足感があると評された。一人でもハーフサイズで注文でき、グラスワインの提案も的確だったとの声もある。
農園から届く有機野菜、豊洲市場の天然魚介、丁寧に下処理した厳選肉を使用し、素材の持ち味と組み合わせの妙を大切にした、枠にとらわれない自由な発想の季節料理を提供する神保町のビストロ。

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations run through Japanese-language platforms (Tabelog, Ikyu, Autoreserve) and no English menu or English-language official presence was found, so Japanese ability or translation help is recommended.

places.name: バインセオサイゴン 東京ドームシティ店 (Banh Xeo Saigon Tokyo Dome City)
places.gf_detail: Several dishes are naturally based on rice rather than wheat (phở noodles, gỏi cuốn rice-paper spring rolls, and the bánh xèo batter itself, which is rice flour and turmeric based), which is typical of Vietnamese cuisine generally. However, no gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy/dipping-sauce information was found from the restaurant, and fried items (e.g. fried spring rolls) likely share cooking equipment with other menu items. Not verified safe for celiac disease — ask staff about nước chấm/nước mắm ingredients and frying setup before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan or vegetarian menu was found. Signature dishes (bánh xèo, gỏi cuốn, phở, hủ tiếu) are typically made with pork and/or shrimp and broths are usually meat-based; dipping sauces (nước chấm/nước mắm) are fish-sauce based and not vegan. Some vegetable-forward items may exist but were not confirmed. Vegans should ask staff directly about substitutions and hidden fish sauce.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidōbashi) / Tokyo Dome City
places.notes: Part of the Banh Xeo Saigon group of Ho Chi Minh-style Vietnamese street-food stalls in Tokyo (other branches include Shinjuku and Yurakucho). The Tokyo Dome City branch moved here from its original Ebisu location when the food hall opened. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Originally a street-food stall in Ebisu ("バインセオサイゴン屋台恵比寿"); relocated to Tokyo Dome City's newly opened FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall on June 24, 2024.
chef_bio.background: Part of the Banh Xeo Saigon group of Ho Chi Minh-style Vietnamese street-food stalls in Tokyo (other branches include Shinjuku and Yurakucho). The Tokyo Dome City branch moved here from its original Ebisu location when the food hall opened.
chef_bio.philosophy: Described by the operator as being thoroughly committed to authentic Ho Chi Minh City street-food flavor ("とことん本場ベトナムの味にこだわった本格ベトナム・ホーチミン屋台料理店"), aiming to recreate a casual Saigon street-stall atmosphere inside the food hall.
chef_bio.specialty: Bánh xèo — a large, turmeric-yellow rice-flour crepe filled with pork, shrimp and bean sprouts, wrapped in lettuce and herbs and dipped in nước chấm; also known for phở and gỏi cuốn (fresh rice-paper spring rolls) and the pork-broth noodle dish hủ tiếu.

anecdotes.text: The bánh xèo is prepared by a Vietnamese chef who trained in Ho Chi Minh City for more than 15 years before bringing the recipe to Japan.
Vietnamese customers have reportedly told the shop the bánh xèo tastes even better than what they ate back home in Vietnam.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in a large modern food hall inside Tokyo Dome City with an English-language listing page, but ordering and menu detail are primarily in Japanese so some phrase preparation helps.

places.name: すし善 (Sushi Zen)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated soy sauce, or celiac-safety information could be confirmed for this restaurant. As a traditional Edomae sushi counter, nigiri is typically brushed with tare/nikiri sauce and served with regular soy sauce, which usually contains wheat; cross-contact risk from shared soy sauce and shared prep surfaces has not been documented one way or the other. Celiac diners should call ahead (Japanese-language call likely needed) to ask specifically about wheat-free soy sauce and shared equipment before booking.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegetarian option, or plant-based accommodation was found for this restaurant. It is a traditional fish-focused Edomae sushi counter (nigiri course centered on raw and cured seafood); there is no indication vegan diners could be accommodated beyond possibly a few vegetable-only items on request, which is unconfirmed. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct advance confirmation.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya, near Namiki-bashi intersection)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, counter-only Edomae sushi restaurant in Shibuya where the chef reportedly goes to Toyosu Market each morning to select the day's fish personally. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small, counter-only Edomae sushi restaurant in Shibuya where the chef reportedly goes to Toyosu Market each morning to select the day's fish personally.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi (omakase counter course)

anecdotes.text: The chef is described by diners as personable and welcoming, making the counter comfortable even for first-time guests, and is known for personally sourcing seasonal seafood at Toyosu Market each morning.

cultural_comfort.note: A small traditional 10-seat sushi counter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service; reservations and some Japanese-language ability are recommended.

places.name: ラーメン豚山 サクラステージ店 (Ramen Butayama Sakura Stage)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are thick, chewy, wheat-based Jiro-style noodles (no gluten-free alternative found); the tare/broth is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat in Japan). No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or gluten-free noodle substitute was found in any source. The counter kitchen is small and shared, with no indication of separated prep for allergens. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based and finished with pork chashu and rendered pork fat/back-fat, a hallmark of the Jiro-style genre; tare is soy-sauce based. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, meat-free broth option, or plant-based substitute was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷区桜丘町（渋谷サクラステージ内、渋谷駅隣接）
places.notes: Butayama is part of Gift Holdings, the Tokyo ramen company built by Sho Tagawa, who trained at the noted Yokohama-style shop Ichirokuya before founding the Iekei chain Machida Shoten in 2008. Butayama grew out of Tagawa's personal love of Jiro-style ramen: its first shop opened i · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Chain restaurant operated by Gift Holdings Inc. (株式会社ギフトホールディングス)
chef_bio.origin: Machida, Tokyo (brand founded 2018)
chef_bio.background: Butayama is part of Gift Holdings, the Tokyo ramen company built by Sho Tagawa, who trained at the noted Yokohama-style shop Ichirokuya before founding the Iekei chain Machida Shoten in 2008. Butayama grew out of Tagawa's personal love of Jiro-style ramen: its first shop opened in Machida in July 2018, at a time when chain-expanding the notoriously idiosyncratic Jiro genre was almost unheard of. It has since grown into Japan's largest Jiro-inspired chain, with roughly 50 locations across nine prefectures; the Shibuya Sakura Stage shop opened December 24, 2025, as the brand's first location right by Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built to make intimidating Jiro-style ramen approachable for people who find the genre's original shops hard to navigate, especially women, families, and first-timers, through printed ordering guides at every table, a smaller portion option, and attentive service, without diluting the genre's hallmark volume and richness. The parent company's stated motto is 'ramen, as a gift to the world. '
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style ramen with thick, chewy all-wheat noodles, a semi-emulsified pork-bone and soy broth, tender braised pork, and a tall pile of cabbage and bean sprouts, customizable with garlic, extra vegetables, and oil ('mashimashi' add-ons); a smaller 125g noodle portion is also offered.

anecdotes.text: Butayama's first shop opened in Machida in July 2018 — at the time, chain-expanding a Jiro-inspired ramen concept was almost unprecedented. It has since grown into Japan's largest Jiro-style chain.

The chain was founded by Sho Tagawa, who trained at the Yokohama-style ramen shop Ichirokuya before opening the Iekei chain Machida Shoten in 2008; Butayama grew out of his personal enthusiasm for Jiro-style ramen.

Every table carries a printed guide to the Jiro-style ordering calls, a mini 125g noodle size is offered for smaller appetites, and staff provide paper aprons; the brand states on its official social media that it 'aims to be a shop that's easy for women and families with children to enter.'
The parent company's stated motto is 'ramen, as a gift to the world.'

cultural_comfort.note: The brand prints simplified ordering-call instructions at every table specifically to help newcomers and women navigate the intimidating Jiro-style ordering ritual, but there is no confirmed English menu, so some Japanese comprehension or willingness to point at the printed guide is still needed.

places.name: ＆スリランカTOKYO（旧：RスリランカTOKYO） (& Sri Lanka Tokyo (formerly R Sri Lanka Toky)
places.gf_detail: The curry base is coconut milk and spices served over rice (not bread/roti), and one secondary source describes the curry as not using wheat flour or oil in its preparation -- a promising sign for gluten-free diners. However, there is no confirmed information about whether soy sauce (often wheat-containing) is used in seasoning, nor any stated cross-contamination precautions or dedicated cooking equipment. Ask staff directly before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat-based curries -- the signature dish is a pork shoulder steak curry -- and no dedicated vegetarian/vegan curry set was confirmed in available sources. Sri Lankan curry preparations can involve fish-based stock or dried shrimp (not confirmed either way here), so vegans should call ahead (03-5280-7066) to ask whether a vegetable-only curry can be made without hidden animal-derived stock.
places.neighborhood: 神保町（神保町駅北側、千代田区神田神保町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The original Fukuoka shop was opened in 2018 by a Sri Lankan native in the Yakuin district. When the Sri Lankan manager ran into a visa problem and had to return home, a longtime regular customer, Takao Okuma, quit his salaryman job and bought out the restaurant -- including its  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: original Sri Lankan founder (2018, no longer involved)
chef_bio.origin: Yakuin, Fukuoka (original shop opened 2018 by a Sri Lankan native)
chef_bio.background: The original Fukuoka shop was opened in 2018 by a Sri Lankan native in the Yakuin district. When the Sri Lankan manager ran into a visa problem and had to return home, a longtime regular customer, Takao Okuma, quit his salaryman job and bought out the restaurant -- including its name -- to keep it running. A second Fukuoka branch ('Rスリランカ+') opened in Nakasu-Kawabata in February 2022, and the brand's first Tokyo branch opened in Jimbocho in October 2022 under the name 'RスリランカTOKYO,' later rebranded to '＆スリランカTOKYO. '
chef_bio.philosophy: A Japanese owner carrying forward a Sri Lankan friend's recipes and restaurant, adapting Sri Lankan spice curry to Japanese ingredients and palates rather than running it as a franchise copy.
chef_bio.specialty: 豚肩ロースステーキカレー (Pork Shoulder Steak Curry) -- roughly 200g of grilled pork shoulder steak on a Sri Lankan-style coconut-milk-and-spice curry made with about 7 spices imported from Sri Lanka, topped with red pepper.

anecdotes.text: The shop's original name, 'R,' is the initial of the Sri Lankan manager's late father -- chosen so that running the restaurant would feel like 'working together with his father in spirit.'
Okuma took over the whole restaurant, including its name, after quitting his corporate job when the Sri Lankan manager's visa situation forced him to return to Sri Lanka.
The Jimbocho branch opened in October 2022 as the brand's first Tokyo location, expanding from its Fukuoka roots.

positives.text: Curry is described as coconut-milk and spice based, served over rice rather than bread, and reportedly prepared without wheat flour or oil.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood curry shop primarily run in Japanese; menu and staff interaction are not confirmed to be English-friendly, though the dish itself (rice plus curry) is easy to point at and order.

places.name: 京都 茶寮翠泉 新宿店 (Kyoto Saryo Suisen Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: The house soba is marketed as 十割そば (juwari — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat used as a binder), including a matcha version, which is a promising sign for wheat-avoidance. However, no source found any statement from the shop about the soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) ingredients — Japanese soba tsuyu is typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce — nor about dusting-flour practices, shared equipment, or celiac-level cross-contamination controls. There is no evidence of a formal gluten-free menu, labeling, or staff allergy protocol. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the tsuyu and any dusting flour before ordering the soba, and should not assume the matcha sweets (warabimochi, parfaits, etc.) are wheat-free without asking, since ingredients like cookie/cracker garnishes are common in Japanese matcha parfaits.
places.vegan_detail: Hot/cold matcha drinks prepared with water rather than milk are likely vegan, but this was not explicitly confirmed. The signature warm warabimochi is made from warabi starch, matcha, and wasanbon sugar with no dairy mentioned, though it is typically served with sweetened accompaniments that may not be vegan. The soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi, as is standard for Japanese soba, which would make the soba dish non-vegan unless a special sauce is offered — this has not been confirmed either way. Matcha parfaits and lattes commonly include dairy cream/milk. No vegan menu, labeling, or staff accommodation policy was found from the shop itself.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30)、定休日なし（施設に準ずる）、予約不可
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (Tochomae Station area)
places.notes: Saryo Suisen is a Kyoto-born matcha specialty teahouse brand, with its Shinjuku shop sitting on the ground floor of the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building directly connected to Tochomae Station, near the Tokyo Metropolitan Government building. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Kyoto matcha teahouse / sweets shop, Tokyo (Shinjuku) branch
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (main honten in Takatsuji, Kyoto; this Shinjuku shop opened July 2020 as the brand's first Tokyo outlet)
chef_bio.background: Saryo Suisen is a Kyoto-born matcha specialty teahouse brand, with its Shinjuku shop sitting on the ground floor of the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building directly connected to Tochomae Station, near the Tokyo Metropolitan Government building.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is built around Uji matcha selected by Kyoto tea appraiser Haruhide Morita, and deliberately uses koicha-grade (thick-tea) matcha — the richer, more ceremonial grade usually reserved for formal tea ceremony — in its sweets and drinks rather than the thinner usucha grade most matcha cafes use.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly made warm warabimochi (出来立て温わらび餅) kneaded to order from genuine warabi starch, matcha and wasanbon sugar, and 100%-buckwheat matcha soba (十割抹茶そば) made in-house daily.

anecdotes.text: The shop's warm warabimochi has been featured on Japanese TV (Hirunandesu!) as a specialty best eaten within about 10 minutes of being made, while still warm and soft.
Matcha used across the brand is chosen by tea connoisseur Haruhide Morita, and the shop emphasizes serving koicha (thick tea) grade matcha rather than the usucha typically used in matcha desserts.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba dipping sauce (tsuyu) ingredients are not specified in any source found; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is made with wheat-containing soy sauce, but this has not been confirmed for this shop specifically.

positives.text: The house soba (including the signature matcha soba) is made in-house daily as 十割そば — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder in the noodle itself.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, tourist-adjacent café inside an office building right at Tochomae Station near the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building, so some visitor familiarity is likely, though no explicit English menu/staff confirmation was found — basic Japanese or pointing at photos should suffice.

places.name: 兆楽 道玄坂店 (Choraku Dogenzaka-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-centric Chinese diner: ramen noodles, hand-made gyoza wrappers, and fried rice are all wheat-based, and standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is used throughout. No source mentions gluten-free preparation, a wheat-free menu, or a dedicated fryer — fried items almost certainly share oil with breaded/battered and wheat-containing foods. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-forward Chinese diner built around pork and beef stir-fries and lard-fried rice; no dedicated vegan dishes, plant-based broth, or dashi-free preparation were confirmed in any source. Some vegetable side dishes may exist but are not confirmed to be free of meat-based stock, oyster sauce, or lard, and are likely cooked in shared woks with meat. Not recommended for vegans without on-site clarification with staff (Japanese needed).
places.hours_raw: 7:00〜24:00 (L.O. 23:30), reportedly open daily / no fixed closed day (one source noted a possible Monday closure — unconfirmed)
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (道玄坂) — about 2-3 minutes' walk from Shib
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Shibuya neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka) dating back to the 1950s. The original founder had no successor, so the current owner's father took over the shop in 1987 and later expanded it in 1995 by absorbing the space of an adjacent yakitori restaurant. This Dogenzaka bra · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A Shibuya neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka) dating back to the 1950s. The original founder had no successor, so the current owner's father took over the shop in 1987 and later expanded it in 1995 by absorbing the space of an adjacent yakitori restaurant. This Dogenzaka branch opened in 2002 in the newly completed Fujiyama-Kosan building, alongside the original Udagawa-cho location.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fast, filling, wallet-friendly Chinese comfort food served from early morning (7am) through late night, aimed at commuters, students, and Shibuya office workers alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Ruusu Chahan (ルースーチャーハン) — a loose, saucy pork-and-vegetable stir-fry served over fried rice; reportedly sold over 2,000 servings a month before the pandemic.

anecdotes.text: Founded in the mid-1950s by a restaurateur named Tachihashi; with no successor, the current owner's father took over the shop in 1987 and expanded it by taking over the neighboring yakitori shop's space in 1995.
The signature loose-style fried rice (ルースーチャーハン) reportedly sold more than 2,000 servings a month before the pandemic.
Choraku collaborated with Nissin's 'Ra-oh' instant noodle line on a shop-supervised Cantonese-style noodle product, reflecting its local cult following.

cultural_comfort.note: Old-school neighborhood diner with verbal seated ordering and cash-only payment; no English menu was found in any source, so some Japanese ability or pointing at photos/other diners' dishes helps.

places.name: ぴえもん (Piemon)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free pasta or menu options are mentioned anywhere. The restaurant's core identity is fresh-boiled wheat pasta ("yudetate pasta"), and no source discusses gluten-free substitutions, dedicated cooking equipment, or allergy accommodations. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or dedicated vegan options were found. The signature dishes (tarako cod-roe pasta, tuna pastas, Gorgonzola cream pasta, meat sauce) are all animal-based, and wafu-style pasta sauces in Japan commonly rely on bonito dashi or similar fish-based stock, though this was not explicitly confirmed for this restaurant. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct confirmation.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 22:00, some sources note close at 22:45); 土 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); 日・祝 定休日。不定期にディナー営業延長あり (occasional irregular dinner extension).
places.neighborhood: Kudanshita, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Piemon is a long-running neighborhood Italian pasta shop near Kudanshita Station, founded in 1980 (Showa 55) and still run more than 40 years later out of a renovated two-story former traditional house (kominka). Lunchtime, it operates as a dedicated fresh-boiled pasta counter an · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Casual Italian dinner and party venue
chef_bio.background: Piemon is a long-running neighborhood Italian pasta shop near Kudanshita Station, founded in 1980 (Showa 55) and still run more than 40 years later out of a renovated two-story former traditional house (kominka). Lunchtime, it operates as a dedicated fresh-boiled pasta counter and is known locally as a queue-forming spot; dinner service expands into pizza and a wide range of tapas alongside the pasta menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pasta is boiled to order and served in a distinctly Japanese-inflected style (wafu), pairing Italian technique with Japanese ingredients and flavor combinations rather than sticking to classic Italian sauces.
chef_bio.specialty: Wafu-style fresh pasta, with the tarako (cod roe) pasta and a rich Gorgonzola cream pasta as standout dishes; portions are selectable in three sizes (280g regular, 380g large, 480g extra-large).

anecdotes.text: Bloggers describe the tarako and shiso tuna pasta as well-balanced in saltiness, with a smooth mouthfeel, and note approvingly that it is not drowned in mayonnaise the way typical Japanese tuna pasta often is.
A regular describes returning for the plum-shiso tuna and tarako pastas, praising the al dente texture and the seaweed garnish that rounds out the dish.
The restaurant is noted as a renovated old machiya-style two-story building a one-minute walk from Kudanshita Station exit 7, used for everyday meals as well as parties and dates.

cultural_comfort.note: A local, Japanese-oriented neighborhood pasta shop with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking service, so bring basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: かっぺ亭 新宿思い出横丁本店 (Kappe-tei Shinjuku Omoide Yokocho Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. This is a traditional yakitori/motsu-nikomi izakaya: yakitori is typically served with a soy-based tare sauce (contains wheat) or salt, and the beef offal stew is a long-simmered miso-based sauce (miso and Japanese soy sauce commonly contain wheat) continuously topped up over time, making a wheat-free version of the house sauce essentially impossible to isolate. Skewers are grilled together over a shared binchotan grill, so cross-contact between tare-brushed and unsauced items is likely. Diners with celiac disease should not consider this restaurant safe without direct, in-person confirmation of ingredients — no such confirmation is available from published sources.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan accommodation was found in any source. This is a meat- and offal-centric izakaya (yakitori, liver sashimi, beef offal stew) with no plant-based dishes identified; the house sauces (miso-based nikomi broth, yakitori tare) are built around animal-derived stock and are not vegan. There is no indication of a vegetable/vegan menu section.
places.neighborhood: 新宿西口 思い出横丁 (Shinjuku Nishiguchi, Omoide Yokocho)
places.cuisine: 炭火焼き鳥・牛もつ煮込み居酒屋
places.notes: Kappe-tei's Shinjuku Omoide Yokocho honten is the flagship location of a yakitori/motsu-yaki izakaya brand operated by Takashige Food Service Co. (株式会社高繁フードサービス), a Shinjuku-founded (1952) restaurant company that also runs the yakiniku chain Gyū-Shige. This location sits in Omoid · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: kitchen/hall staff of a Shinjuku-based izakaya operator
chef_bio.background: Kappe-tei's Shinjuku Omoide Yokocho honten is the flagship location of a yakitori/motsu-yaki izakaya brand operated by Takashige Food Service Co. (株式会社高繁フードサービス), a Shinjuku-founded (1952) restaurant company that also runs the yakiniku chain Gyū-Shige. This location sits in Omoide Yokocho, the narrow postwar alley of small taverns just west of Shinjuku Station, and has undergone a renewal reopening under the company's 'kodawari sakaba' (particular-taste tavern) concept.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes same-day hand-prepared ingredients — skewers are strung by hand daily rather than pre-made, the offal stew is simmered for two or more days, and cuts of offal vary with each day's delivery rather than being standardized.
chef_bio.specialty: Three signature dishes: reba-teki-zashi (low-temperature-aged liver sashimi), binchotan-charcoal-grilled yakitori hand-skewered fresh from raw (not frozen) chicken daily, and gyu-motsu nikomi (beef offal stew) simmered in a miso-based broth that is continuously topped up ('tsugitashi') over years for a consistent flavor.

anecdotes.text: The beef offal stew uses whatever cuts come in with each delivery, simmered in a miso sauce that has been continuously replenished over the years to keep an unchanging taste.
Yakitori skewers are hand-strung fresh daily from raw chicken and grilled over strong binchotan charcoal, served with either a traditional tare sauce or aged salt.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Japanese-language alley tavern in tourist-frequented Omoide Yokocho — menus and ordering are primarily in Japanese, though staff are used to some foreign visitors given the location.

places.name: 足湯カフェ もみの気ハウス 渋谷道玄坂店 (Ashiyu Cafe Mominoki House Shibuya Dogenzaka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated-fryer information is published for this location. The light food menu (curry, tea-rice, sweets) likely uses standard curry roux and soy sauce, both common wheat sources, and cross-contamination has not been addressed publicly. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering or eating any food item; the herbal tea itself is not a wheat risk.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu is published. The site references Sri Lankan curry/tea-rice dishes and homemade sweets, but ingredients such as ghee, dairy, honey, or meat-based stock in the curry are not disclosed. The herbal tea served with the foot bath is plant-based, but full meals should not be assumed vegan without asking staff.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00, open year-round except New Year's holidays (年中無休・年末年始を除く); the ashiyu-cafe portion (foot bath + herbal tea, 45 min) is bookable within these hours
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya
places.notes: Mominoki House began in 1985 as an acupuncture and shiatsu practice in Japan, building up traditional Chinese five-element (in-yo gogyo) massage technique for over two decades. In its ongoing search for more effective wellness methods, the company encountered Ayurveda and in July · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Ayurveda salon and foot-bath cafe chain, est. 1985
chef_bio.origin: Founded in Japan in 1985
chef_bio.background: Mominoki House began in 1985 as an acupuncture and shiatsu practice in Japan, building up traditional Chinese five-element (in-yo gogyo) massage technique for over two decades. In its ongoing search for more effective wellness methods, the company encountered Ayurveda and in July 2004 opened its own Ayurvedic facility in Mount Lavinia, Sri Lanka, relocating it to Colombo in October 2006. The Sri Lanka operation trained around 100 staff in herbal-oil selection and use, dosha assessment, and traditional massage technique. Today the company partners with a Sri Lankan Ayurveda graduate school and has its treatments supervised by an in-house Ayurvedic doctor. The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is one of several Mominoki House 'ashiyu' (foot-bath) cafe and Ayurveda salon locations across Japan, combining a 45-minute herbal foot bath with a cup of Sri Lankan herbal tea, alongside optional Ayurvedic oil and shiatsu treatments.
chef_bio.philosophy: Holistic wellness rooted in traditional Sri Lankan Ayurveda, combining herbal treatments with a relaxing tea/foot-bath ritual rather than a conventional sit-down meal.
chef_bio.specialty: 45-minute herbal foot bath served with a cup of Sri Lankan herbal tea

cultural_comfort.note: Staff-facing signage and booking are mostly in Japanese, but the brand maintains an English Tabelog listing and bilingual social accounts, so basic greetings/simple English should get you through a visit.

places.name: 浅草 GYUUNA やどき (Asakusa GYUUNA Yadoki)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen list, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination policy was found on the official site or any directory. This is a grilled-eel and wagyu specialty restaurant; kabayaki tare (eel glaze sauce) is traditionally soy-sauce based and commonly contains wheat, though no source specific to this restaurant confirms its exact ingredients. Celiac diners should not assume safety and should ask directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or meat-free options were found on any source. The restaurant's entire concept is built around farmed eel and A5 wagyu/beef tongue, making it unsuitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 月〜日: 08:00〜22:00（料理L.O. 21:00、ドリンクL.O. 21:30）。8:00〜11:00は朝食メニュー限定提供。定休日なし（年中無休）。
places.neighborhood: 浅草・新仲見世 (Asakusa / Shin-Nakamise), Taito City
places.notes: Opened in July 2024 in the Shin-Nakamise shopping arcade just steps from Sensoji, the restaurant is run as a direct extension of its parent company's own eel farm in Yamanashi Prefecture, letting it serve domestically raised eel at unusually accessible prices. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 松本 彪雅 (Matsumoto Hyoga) — listed as head chef on directory profile
chef_bio.background: Opened in July 2024 in the Shin-Nakamise shopping arcade just steps from Sensoji, the restaurant is run as a direct extension of its parent company's own eel farm in Yamanashi Prefecture, letting it serve domestically raised eel at unusually accessible prices.
chef_bio.philosophy: Vertical integration from farm to table — because the eel is raised on the company's own farm using Yamanashi's clean spring water and ocean minerals, the restaurant can offer high-grade domestic unagi that would normally be expensive at a reasonable price.
chef_bio.specialty: Unagi kabayaki (grilled eel) made from the restaurant's own Yamanashi-farmed 'blue eel' (青うなぎ), served alongside A5-rank wagyu and beef tongue.

anecdotes.text: The eel served here is raised on the company's own farm in Yamanashi, using the prefecture's clear water and ocean minerals in an original aquaculture method; the top-grade 'blue eel' is hand-grilled and finished with a tare sauce that has reportedly been continuously replenished for over 100 years.
In May 2025 the restaurant's Mt. Fuji spring-water eel kabayaki (富士山湧水鰻蒲焼) won the Grand Prix at the Japan Food Selection awards.
The restaurant sits on Shin-Nakamise, the busy shopping street connecting to Sensoji, and pairs its eel with carefully selected A5 wagyu and beef tongue.

cultural_comfort.note: Located on Asakusa's busy, tourist-heavy Shin-Nakamise arcade with an English-language Tabelog listing and online reservation options, so some English support can be expected even without on-site confirmation of an English menu.

places.name: 麺処 竹川 (Mendokoro Takekawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen/ramen specialist using custom noodles from Murakami Seimen; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free prep, or allergen menu was found in any source, and the dipping sauce/tare is a standard soy-based broth (soy sauce typically contains wheat in Japan) with no stated wheat-free alternative.
places.vegan_detail: Every noodle dish is built on a chicken white broth (100% domestic chicken, simmered about 10 hours daily); the organic Yamaguchi vegetables are served as a topping/garnish on the chicken-broth tsukemen rather than as a standalone vegan preparation, and no plant-based broth or vegan menu item was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), lunch service only; closed Mondays. Limited to roughly 100 servings per day.
places.neighborhood: Shinsen, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop runs out of the same tiny space as an address-undisclosed, reservation-based chicken hot pot (mizutaki) restaurant; during lunch hours the space becomes Mendokoro Takekawa, serving additive-free chicken white broth tsukemen and ramen, before switching back to the hot pot · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Originally opened in Sangenjaya (Setagaya) in February 2015 as "Tsukemen Takekawa," then relocated to Maruyama-cho, Shibuya (near Shinsen Station) in August 2019.
chef_bio.background: The shop runs out of the same tiny space as an address-undisclosed, reservation-based chicken hot pot (mizutaki) restaurant; during lunch hours the space becomes Mendokoro Takekawa, serving additive-free chicken white broth tsukemen and ramen, before switching back to the hot pot restaurant in the evening.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept grew out of a wish to let more people taste the pesticide-free, organically grown vegetables shipped directly from the owner's own farm in Yamaguchi Prefecture, which are normally reserved for the evening mizutaki restaurant; the chicken white broth is made from 100% domestic chicken simmered daily for around 10 hours, with an emphasis on no additives.
chef_bio.specialty: Additive-free special chicken white broth tsukemen (無添加特製つけ麺), topped with pesticide-free organic vegetables (turnip, kabocha squash, potato, and other seasonal produce).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's entrance is hidden down an alley and is genuinely hard to find without a map app — one visiting blogger described spending nearly an hour searching for it before finally sitting down to eat.
Unusually for a ramen or tsukemen shop, all 22 seats are arranged as individual private rooms, so diners eat without other customers in view.
The noodles are a custom mid-thick straight noodle made exclusively for Takekawa by noodle maker Murakami Seimen.

cultural_comfort.note: The entrance is tucked down an alley and easy to miss without a map app, and the lunch service runs on a Japanese-style ticket/ordering flow with no indication of an English menu, so a little Japanese comfort helps.

places.name: 台湾豆乳大王 (Taiwan Tonyu Daiou (Taiwan Soy Milk King))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented anywhere in available sources. The signature savory soy milk soup is topped with a deep-fried wheat-dough stick (油條); the egg crepe (蛋餅) uses a wheat-flour wrapper; the pepper bun (胡椒餅) is a wheat-dough bun; and the braised pork rice (魯肉飯) is soy-sauce marinated (soy sauce in Japan is typically wheat-based). No GF-labeled items, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol were found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Only the plain soy milk drinks (豆漿/甜豆漿) look vegan-friendly if made without animal additives; the signature savory soy milk soup (鹹豆漿) is topped with shrimp, and the rest of the menu centers on braised pork rice (魯肉飯), chicken rice (鶏肉飯), and egg-based crepes (蛋餅) and pepper buns. No vegan-labeled dishes, no stated dashi/bonito avoidance, and no indication this is a vegetarian-oriented kitchen — it is a general Taiwanese breakfast shop, not a vegan or vegetarian specialist.
places.hours_raw: Most consistent report: Mon-Fri 8:00-14:00, closed Sat/Sun/public holidays, prepayment counter service, cash and cashless both accepted. One secondary aggregation suggested Mon-Wed 11:00-14:30, Thu-Fri 11:00-14:30 & 18:00-22:00, Sat 11:00-14:00 (closed Sun/holidays) — this could not be confirmed against a primary source and may reflect a different period or a mixed-up listing; call ahead to confirm.
places.neighborhood: Kudanshita / Kanda-Jinbocho, Chiyoda-ku
places.cuisine: Taiwanese breakfast / soy milk specialty sho
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 台湾豆乳大王 opened on May 11, 2020 on a side street between Kudanshita and Jimbocho stations in Chiyoda-ku, focused on Taiwanese-style soy-milk breakfast (早餐) fare rather than a full dinner menu. It has since drawn heavy media attention as one of Tokyo's go-to spots for authentic Taiw · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Taiwan (regional Taiwanese breakfast-shop style, 早餐店)
chef_bio.background: 台湾豆乳大王 opened on May 11, 2020 on a side street between Kudanshita and Jimbocho stations in Chiyoda-ku, focused on Taiwanese-style soy-milk breakfast (早餐) fare rather than a full dinner menu. It has since drawn heavy media attention as one of Tokyo's go-to spots for authentic Taiwanese breakfast, including a May 31, 2025 feature on Fuji TV's 'Ariyoshi-kun no Shojiki Sanpo,' where comedian Hiroyuki Ariyoshi praised the shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop presses its own soy milk fresh every morning and centers the menu on that daily-made soy milk, in the tradition of a Taiwanese 早餐店 (breakfast shop).
chef_bio.specialty: 鹹豆漿 (xián dòu jiāng / savory soy milk soup) made with soy milk pressed fresh each morning, and 魯肉飯 (braised pork rice)

anecdotes.text: On Fuji TV's 'Ariyoshi-kun no Shojiki Sanpo' (aired May 31, 2025), comedian Hiroyuki Ariyoshi tried the shop's food and said, 'I could have this every morning, it's so delicious.'

A June 2025 blog visit describes the savory soy milk soup as made with rich, freshly pressed soy milk topped generously with chili oil for a sour-spicy kick, and the braised pork rice as tender, sweet-savory pork over rice.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 新宿ダイアログ (Shinjuku Dialogue)
places.gf_detail: A gluten-free guide site lists this venue, stating that at least one of its rotating kitchens avoids gluten, dairy, white sugar, trans fats, GMO grains and food additives across its dishes and sweets — consistent with the macrobiotic, brown-rice-vegetarian style of the Wednesday guest kitchen. However, because the venue is shared among several independent tenants on different days, this standard has not been confirmed to apply uniformly to every operator, and no information was found on dedicated fryers, wheat content in soy sauce used on other days, or formal cross-contact protocols. Celiac diners should confirm directly with staff which kitchen is running that day and ask specifically about soy sauce, cross-contact, and shared prep surfaces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Historically known for a vegan "veggie taco rice" (ベジタコライス) made with soy meat (大豆ミート) instead of ground beef, plus vegetarian/vegan-friendly lunch boxes and organic drinks (azuki-bean amazake latte, house-made fermented juice). The venue is a rotating multi-tenant space — different independent cooks/pop-ups run the kitchen on different days — and the vegan taco rice was specifically tied to a Wednesday guest kitchen (reported as recently as late 2023/early 2024). Not every day's menu is confirmed fully vegan (some rotating tenants serve general Japanese/Asian home cooking that may include fish-based dashi or other animal products), so vegan diners should ask which kitchen is operating and check the dish list for that day.
places.hours_raw: General framework reported across multiple years: café service roughly 11:00-17:00 and bar service roughly 18:00-24:00 (midnight). This is a multi-tenant venue — a different independent café/kitchen operator runs the space on different days/timeslots (examples reported over time include ノムカフェ, f-on, Teru cafe/テルカフェ on Wednesdays serving the vegan taco rice, and more recently CAFE AmaLie and an Asian home-cooking kitchen "百福"). Because the operator (and sometimes exact hours/menu) rotates and could not be confirmed for the current week via a live source, exact hours for a specific visit day should be reconfirmed via the venue's Instagram (@shinjukudialogue) or Facebook page.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目
places.notes: Opened in spring 2019 in Shinjuku Sanchome as an SDGs-themed café and bar. Rather than a single fixed kitchen, the space is run day-to-day and time-slot by time-slot by a rotating cast of independent café and food operators, so visitors encounter different cooks, cuisines, and st · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in spring 2019 in Shinjuku Sanchome as an SDGs-themed café and bar. Rather than a single fixed kitchen, the space is run day-to-day and time-slot by time-slot by a rotating cast of independent café and food operators, so visitors encounter different cooks, cuisines, and stories depending on when they come.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Dialogue' reflects the founders' wish to create a place where conversation about the UN Sustainable Development Goals happens naturally over food and drink. Staff — including transgender team members — dress in their own clothes rather than uniforms, and the venue is deliberately run as an LGBTQ+-friendly space where visitors are invited to encounter a range of gender expressions and backgrounds.
chef_bio.specialty: A vegan 'taco rice' made with soy meat in place of ground beef — historically served by a Wednesday guest kitchen rooted in macrobiotic, brown-rice vegetarian cooking — alongside organic drinks such as an azuki-bean amazake latte and house-fermented enzyme juice.

anecdotes.text: 毎週水曜日に出店している「Teru cafe」がタコライスを提供しており、マクロビオティックをベースにした玄米菜食のお料理やスイーツを作っている（動物性食品・白砂糖・精製塩・化学調味料・保存料・着色料は一切不使用）。
ドリンクを注文するごとにコインが1枚渡され、それを「環境問題」や「貧困」と書かれた項目の箱に入れると、その社会問題に取り組んでいる団体に寄付される仕組みになっている。
店長やコックなど、スタッフ全員が私服で、トランスジェンダーを含め性別の枠にとらわれないカラフルな個性の人々が集まっており、訪れた人にはいろいろなジェンダーの在り方に触れてほしいと考えている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Venue is a shared space with independent tenants operating on different days; recent search results list current operators as CAFE AmaLie (Sat/Sun 12-17), アジア家庭料理・百福 (Mon/Tue/Wed 18:00-22:30, Asian home cooking), plus ノムカフェ (Sat), f-on (Sun/Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri), テルカフェ (Wed) — a different lineup than the 2019 macrobiotic-Wednesday kitchen the GF/vegan labels rely on.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch snippet aggregating tabelog.com/en/tokyo/A1304/A130401/13230852/ and Facebook (Asian kitchen cafe 百福)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Asian home cooking (アジア家庭料理・百福) is a Thai/ethnic-style operator; such cuisine standardly uses wheat-containing soy sauce, fish sauce (ナンプラー) and oyster sauce — an unmitigated gluten hazard and a hidden non-vegan (fish sauce/dashi) hazard on multiple days of the week.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch results for 百福 (tabelog/gnavi/hitosara listings describing it as アジア料理/エスニック)

gf_cross_contamination.text: 百福 posted an October menu revision notice (メニュー改定のお知らせ), indicating the current menu differs from what earlier sources documented.
gf_cross_contamination.source: m.facebook.com/momofuku.asian (10月の営業予定とメニュー改定のお知らせ)

gf_cross_contamination.text: un-gluten.com (あんグル), the GF guide, itself publishes an article titled 'おしょうゆがグルテンフリーと言われても除去を続けるべき理由' — i.e. even celiac-aware Japanese guides warn that soy sauce is a hazard; a listing on such a guide does not certify the whole rotating venue as GF.
HappyCow now categorizes Shinjuku Dialogue as 'Tokyo Coffee & Tea' rather than a restaurant, and no celiac/GF safety reviews for the venue could be surfaced on FindMeGlutenFree or HappyCow.

positives.text: Listed on a Tokyo gluten-free guide site, which states the venue's menu/sweets avoid gluten, dairy, white sugar, trans fats, GMO grains and food additives (attributed to its macrobiotic-style kitchen).

The Wednesday guest kitchen's dishes and desserts were reported to use no animal products (meat, fish, eggs, dairy, butter), no white sugar, no refined salt, no chemical seasonings, no preservatives, and no artificial coloring.

cultural_comfort.note: An LGBTQ+-friendly, diversity-themed community café/bar built around casual conversation ("dialogue"/対話) and SDGs topics; welcoming to newcomers, though no English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed.

places.name: 魚山亭 渋谷店 (Gyossantei Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation information was found in any source. The signature dish, chicken nanban, is deep-fried in a wheat-flour batter and served with sweet vinegar sauce and tartar sauce; other dishes (sashimi, grilled local chicken, Miyazaki beef, hiyajiru cold soup) likely rely on standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce and miso, which typically contain wheat. Not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. The restaurant centers on seafood, chicken, and Miyazaki beef; the specialty hiyajiru cold soup is miso/dashi-based (dashi in Miyazaki-style versions is commonly bonito-derived) and the signature chicken nanban is meat-based with a mayonnaise-based tartar sauce. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without direct, specific confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Listings vary: some sources give a uniform Tue-Sat schedule of 11:30-14:00 and 17:30-24:00 (Sunday closed); a more detailed breakdown found gives Mon 17:30-23:00 (no lunch), Tue-Fri 11:30-14:10 and 17:30-24:00, Sat 17:30-23:30 (no lunch), Sun closed. Recommend phone confirmation (03-5489-6350).
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂/神泉 (Dogenzaka/Shinsen), Shibuya
places.notes: Gyossantei's Shibuya branch traces back to the Nikko Group, a Miyazaki-based family business that ran a tonkatsu restaurant ('Niko/二幸') and a Miyazaki-cuisine restaurant called Gyosantei back home. The Tokyo branch opened in 1984 near Miyagizaka, moved to its current Bunkamura-do · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 川野祐嗣 (Yuji Kawano, current operator)
chef_bio.origin: 宮崎県 (Miyazaki Prefecture)
chef_bio.background: Gyossantei's Shibuya branch traces back to the Nikko Group, a Miyazaki-based family business that ran a tonkatsu restaurant ('Niko/二幸') and a Miyazaki-cuisine restaurant called Gyosantei back home. The Tokyo branch opened in 1984 near Miyagizaka, moved to its current Bunkamura-dori/Dogenzaka location in 2008, and today the founder's son runs it through the company Nisshusha (日州舎).
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on ingredients flown in directly from Miyazaki — fish and shellfish from the Hyuga-nada coast, organic vegetables from Aya town, Miyazaki beef and free-range local chicken — aiming to bring an authentic taste of Miyazaki hometown cooking to Shibuya.
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken nanban (チキン南蛮) — Miyazaki's signature dish of deep-fried chicken thigh dipped in sweet vinegar and topped with a housemade tartar sauce loaded with finely chopped aromatics — alongside hiyajiru (冷や汁), a cold miso soup with summer vegetables like cucumber and myoga, ladled over barley rice.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was opened in 1984 by the Nikko Group founder's family (who also ran a tonkatsu shop and a Gyosantei restaurant in Miyazaki); it started near Miyagizaka and relocated to its present Bunkamura-dori spot in 2008, and is now run by the founder's son, Yuji Kawano, via the company Nisshusha.

The Shibuya branch's chicken nanban is fried a bit harder and dipped more thoroughly in sweet vinegar than the original Miyazaki-airport shop's version, giving it a bolder flavor; the tartar sauce differs between the two locations.
Hiyajiru (cold miso soup with summer vegetables) poured over barley rice is served alongside the chicken nanban as a refreshing counterpoint to its rich, sweet-and-sour flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's signature chicken nanban is deep-fried in a wheat-flour batter; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or allergy-accommodation policy was found in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The specialty cold soup hiyajiru (冷や汁) is a miso-based dish traditionally made with dashi stock (commonly bonito-based) in Miyazaki-style preparations; no vegan-specific menu or substitution was found.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local izakaya serving Miyazaki home-style dishes; no evidence of an English menu, English-speaking staff, or foreign-tourist-oriented service was found in any source.

places.name: 濃厚海老つけ麺 ともやま (Noukou Ebi Tsukemen Tomoyama)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen specialty shop; the noodles are wheat-based and the dipping broth is built on a shrimp (and likely soy-sauce/wheat) base. No source mentions any gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol — assume not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dish is a shrimp-based dipping broth (with a chicken-broth alternative also offered) — both animal-based. No plant-based, vegetable-broth, or vegan option was found in any source, so this is not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 daily; closed days irregular (不定休)
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in spring 2024 just a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, this small tsukemen specialty shop built its identity around a single idea: a rich, shrimp-forward dipping broth mixed fresh in-house. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in spring 2024 just a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, this small tsukemen specialty shop built its identity around a single idea: a rich, shrimp-forward dipping broth mixed fresh in-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emulsifies its shrimp broth with a mixer on site to concentrate the shellfish aroma and body, aiming for a soup thick enough to fully coat the noodles with every dip.
chef_bio.specialty: Rich shrimp tsukemen (濃厚海老つけ麺) — thick, medium-straight noodles served with a viscous, emulsified shrimp dipping broth, finished with a half-boiled egg, scallion, bamboo shoots, and nori.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers highlight the broth's standout aromatic shrimp fragrance and its unusually thick, coating consistency, calling it a stand-out among Suidobashi's dense ramen/tsukemen scene.
The shop also offers a chicken-broth tsukemen option for guests who don't care for shrimp, and lets diners choose warm or cold noodles — a flexibility uncommon among tsukemen specialists.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local tsukemen counter shop with a Japanese-only menu; basic Japanese or pointing at photos should be enough to order.

places.name: plant more（プラント モア） (plant more (Plant More))
places.gf_detail: Multiple secondary sources (WE TABLE allergy-dining directory, DESSA, おかずテーブル blog) state plant more offers many gluten-free and dairy-free items with allergen information/plant-based markers shown on the menu, and that staff give explanations of allergen content on request. No source specifies whether soy sauce/dashi used in savory dishes is wheat-free, nor whether a dedicated fryer or gluten-free prep area is used — celiac-level diners should confirm these specifics with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Plant more's meal plates come in a 100%-plant-derived version (e.g. falafel with homemade tahini sauce, hummus, ratatouille) and all desserts (cakes, soft-serve sundaes) are made only with plant-derived ingredients with no dairy or eggs. However, the menu also includes meat and fish dishes for diners who prefer them, so it is not an exclusively vegan menu — look for the plant-based markers when ordering. No source confirms whether hidden animal-derived ingredients like katsuo dashi are used in any dishes.
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 / Lumine Shinjuku 1, directly connected to Shinjuku Stat
places.cuisine: プラントベース・ヴィーガンカフェ
places.notes: plant more opened on March 4, 2024, on the 6th floor of Lumine Shinjuku 1, directly connected to Shinjuku Station. It is a café brand created by CAFE COMPANY. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Café brand operated by CAFE COMPANY
chef_bio.origin: Operated by CAFE COMPANY, a Tokyo restaurant-brand operator
chef_bio.background: plant more opened on March 4, 2024, on the 6th floor of Lumine Shinjuku 1, directly connected to Shinjuku Station. It is a café brand created by CAFE COMPANY.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café's stated concept is to let people with different dietary styles and preferences share the same table together in a borderless way — the menu offers 100%-plant-derived plates alongside meat and fish dishes so everyone can choose according to preference.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based meal plates (e. g. falafel with homemade tahini sauce, hummus, ratatouille) and all-plant-derived desserts, including cakes and soft-serve sundaes.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger described plant more as having a natural, spacious atmosphere, discovered via an Instagram account that shares dairy-allergy-friendly restaurants; all desserts there are plant-based.
Menus display plant-based and allergen markings so that customers with allergies can order with more confidence.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Plant More advertised gluten-free options, but this primarily applies to desserts, while the meal options are not gluten-free. Some meal items are wheat-free but have a barley mix on top of the salads. Staff confirmed they use the same utensils and bowls to make meal items, so even if they remake a meal there is a high chance of cross contact. The paper menu says to note allergies with your order, but on the website ordering system there is no option to indicate allergies. This establishment is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease.
Staff are described as lovely and trying to be helpful, but are 'super uninformed with what's actually gluten free and what isn't,' and reviewers recommend avoiding if you're sensitive.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Plant more offers many gluten-free and dairy-free menu items, with staff providing explanations of allergen information for guests.

positives.text: All desserts (cakes, soft-serve sundaes) are made only with plant-derived ingredients and contain no dairy or eggs.
The menu displays plant-based and allergen markings so that allergy sufferers can order with more confidence.
Listed in WE TABLE's directory of Shinjuku restaurants with food-allergy accommodations.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside Lumine Shinjuku 1, a large shopping complex directly connected to Shinjuku Station, so basic navigation is easy for visitors; menus use allergen/plant-based icons, though English-language menu support is not specifically confirmed by sources.

places.name: そば処 春木屋 (Sobadokoro Harukiya (Soba-dokoro Harukiya))
places.gf_detail: No source mentions jūwari (100% buckwheat) or gluten-free noodles; as a standard traditional machi-soba shop, the soba is very likely a conventional nihachi-style blend containing wheat flour, and the tsuyu (dipping broth/curry broth) is soy-sauce based, which contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or cross-contamination protocol was found in any source — not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional soba tsuyu (dipping sauce/broth) is almost certainly dashi-based using katsuobushi (bonito flakes), as is standard for old-school machi-soba shops; the curry nanban broth also contains pork per blog description. No vegan-specific options, plant-based dashi substitutions, or vegan menu items are mentioned in any review or menu source found.
places.hours_raw: 火・水・金・土・日・祝: 11:30-19:30（月・木定休、不定休あり）※一部の古い情報源では昼のみ12:00-15:00との記載もあり、来店前に電話確認を推奨（03-3461-6624）
places.neighborhood: 松濤（神泉駅徒歩5分、Bunkamura近く）/ Shoto, near Shinsen Station and Bun
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, unassuming neighborhood soba shop tucked into the quiet backstreets of Shoto (松濤), between Shibuya's Bunkamura cultural complex and Shinsen Station. It has the well-worn, nostalgic look of an old-school machi-soba (town soba) shop — faded walls, hand-written menu boards, · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small, unassuming neighborhood soba shop tucked into the quiet backstreets of Shoto (松濤), between Shibuya's Bunkamura cultural complex and Shinsen Station. It has the well-worn, nostalgic look of an old-school machi-soba (town soba) shop — faded walls, hand-written menu boards, no background music, and a handful of four-person tables — and appears to be run by a couple, giving it a warm, homely feel that stands out in an increasingly fast-changing part of Shibuya.
chef_bio.specialty: Local reviewers single out the curry nanban soba (curry-flavored broth with pork and green onion) for its rich, thick broth that clings well to the thin noodles; classic kake soba and tempura zaru soba are also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: A Tokyo food blog described it as 'the machi-soba of Shoto,' noting the calm, unadorned dining room — no background music, five four-person tables — and praised the curry nanban broth for its rich, thick consistency that clings well to the thin soba noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school local soba shop with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff — expect a fully Japanese ordering experience typical of a Shitamachi-style neighborhood eatery.

places.name: 遠州屋 (Enshuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation was found in any source. As a classic izakaya serving miso/soy-seasoned simmered offal, grilled skewers, and sashimi with soy sauce, wheat exposure via shoyu and miso-based seasoning should be assumed; no information on a dedicated fryer or gluten-free substitutions was found. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is centered on beef/pork offal (motsu-nikomi, motsu-yaki) and fish sashimi; no vegan dishes, plant-based options, or vegan labeling were found in any source. Broths/seasonings likely include dashi and animal-based stock. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 16:00–23:00, Sat 15:30–22:00, Sun & public holidays: closed
places.neighborhood: Koishikawa, Bunkyo-ku (near Kasuga Station, close to the Sui
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in Showa 55 (1980) by an owner who had previously worked as the head of a company's accounting department (経理部長) before leaving to open his own tavern. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Founded in Showa 55 (1980) by an owner who had previously worked as the head of a company's accounting department (経理部長) before leaving to open his own tavern.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner buys fresh offal and seasonal fish at the market himself each morning; the miso-and-salt seasoning used for the signature motsu-nikomi is his own original recipe.
chef_bio.specialty: Motsu-nikomi (simmered beef and pork offal, especially the version made with tofu) and motsu-yaki (grilled offal), alongside sashimi platters of seasonal fish such as chutoro (medium fatty tuna), buri, octopus, aji, and sweet shrimp.

anecdotes.text: The owner, a former corporate accounting department manager, opened the shop in 1980 and devised the shop's miso-and-salt seasoned motsu-nikomi recipe himself, resulting in a mild dish with no unpleasant gaminess.

The first floor has table seating while a spacious tatami room upstairs accommodates larger groups, giving the shop a warm, hometown izakaya atmosphere for both casual lunches and lively evening gatherings.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, decades-old neighborhood izakaya with no indication of English menus, signage, or staff — best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese or with a translation app.

places.name: HealthyTOKYO CBD ショップ&カフェ 新宿マルイ本館 (HealthyTOKYO CBD Shop & Cafe Shinjuku Marui )
places.gf_detail: CBDスイーツは小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使用し、グルテンフリー・ラザニアやキッシュなど「ほとんどのメニュー」がグルテンフリーと案内されているが、「全メニュー」ではない点に注意。専用フライヤーの有無、醤油・だし等の小麦混入やクロスコンタミネーション対策についての具体的な情報源は見つからなかったため、セリアック病レベルの厳格な除去が必要な場合は事前に店舗へ直接確認することを推奨する。
places.vegan_detail: HealthyTOKYOは「100%ヴィーガン」を掲げる企業で、当店のカフェメニュー（ケーキ、パニーニ、ラザニア、キッシュ、ドリンク等）はすべて動物性食品不使用の植物性メニューとして提供されている。だし・かつお節等の隠れ動物性食品の使用は確認されていない（サイト上でも明確にヴィーガンと案内）。
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目・新宿マルイ本館地下1階
places.cuisine: ビーガン・カフェ・グルテンフリー
places.notes: HealthyTOKYOはCBD（カンナビジオール）とヴィーガン食を組み合わせた日本のパイオニア企業として、有機栽培のヘンプを使ったCBD製品と、すべて植物性のスイーツ・軽食を提供している。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: マイケル・ボブロフ（Michael Bobrov、HealthyTOKYO創業者・代表取締役）
roles: 創業者兼代表取締役社長
chef_bio.origin: 2011年設立。2016年に日本市場へCBDオイルを導入した先駆者的企業で、2018年には羽田空港内に日本初の完全ヴィーガン空港カフェ（世界初の空港内CBD販売店）を開設。新宿マルイ本館店はグループ最大規模の店舗として開業。
chef_bio.background: HealthyTOKYOはCBD（カンナビジオール）とヴィーガン食を組み合わせた日本のパイオニア企業として、有機栽培のヘンプを使ったCBD製品と、すべて植物性のスイーツ・軽食を提供している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「動物性食品の消費を減らすことは、私たちの健康はもちろん、環境や動物たちにも良い影響を与えられる」という考えのもと、日本におけるヴィーガン食の普及を目指し、洗練された落ち着いた空間でCBDフードを楽しんでもらうことを大切にしている。
chef_bio.specialty: CBD入りモンブラン（グルテンフリー・ヴィーガンのCBDクッキー土台に栗のペーストと生クリーム風のヴィーガンクリームをのせたシグネチャーケーキ、CBD20mg配合）

anecdotes.text: HealthyTOKYOは日本において「ヴィーガン」へのポジティブな理解を広めるため、毎年何万食ものヴィーガン料理を提供してきたと述べている。
新宿マルイ本館店はApple Storeの真下（地下1階）に位置し、グループ5店舗目・最大規模の店舗として2023年4月29日にオープンした。
店内はケーキやドリンクとともにCBDオイルなども扱い、都会の喧騒から離れてリラックスできるオアシスとしてデザインされている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: HealthyTOKYO is not a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease, with the establishment recommending people contact them directly to inquire about safety precautions.

Items are labeled as gluten free on the menu, and while they have some regular sandwiches available, the lasagnas are covered so cross contamination would be minimal — confirming gluten-containing items are prepared in the same cafe alongside GF items.
Establishment described as NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility; customers advised to contact directly about cross-contamination protocols and food preparation procedures.
Marketing/reviews confirm 'most' Shinjuku menu items are gluten-free (not all), e.g. the CBD Mont Blanc is GF and vegan — implying non-GF items also on offer.

positives.text: 店舗の全ての食品・ドリンクメニューが100%ヴィーガン（動物性食品不使用）で提供されている。
CBDクッキーやケーキ類は小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使用するなど、ほとんどのメニューがグルテンフリーで作られている。

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a major department store (Shinjuku Marui) with an English-language official website and international CBD-wellness branding, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate.

places.name: スケロク ダイナー (SUKE6 DINER (Sukeroku Diner))
places.gf_detail: Reviewer/listing sources tag the restaurant with a "gluten free options" label and mention a gluten-free buddha bowl, but this is a bread-and-charcuterie-forward diner with its own bakery (\"Manufacture\") on the 3rd floor baking fresh bread daily on-site — meaning wheat is handled throughout the kitchen. No source confirms a dedicated gluten-free prep area, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi practice. Celiac travelers should treat this as "ask and verify," not GF-safe by default.
places.vegan_detail: Vegan/vegetarian items are limited but present — sources mention a vegan buddha bowl, a soy/green-tea latte, and easy vegetarian options like quinoa-honey bread. As a Western-diner-and-bakery concept built around eggs, homemade charcuterie (bacon, sausage), and dairy-based dishes, the bulk of the menu is not vegan; no confirmation of dashi/katsuo/bonito use was found, but Japanese-style hidden animal stocks are less likely here than at a washoku restaurant given the Western-diner format. Ask staff to confirm ingredient specifics per dish.
places.neighborhood: 花川戸 (Hanakawado), Asakusa
places.notes: SUKE6 DINER opened in March 2015 on the corner of Sukeroku Yume-dōri, a cherry-tree-lined street along the Sumida River embankment in Asakusa — the street's name is where the restaurant's name comes from. The building is a renovated former footwear-wholesaler warehouse; the diner · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 代表 / オーナー (Representative, Epietriz Inc.)
chef_bio.background: SUKE6 DINER opened in March 2015 on the corner of Sukeroku Yume-dōri, a cherry-tree-lined street along the Sumida River embankment in Asakusa — the street's name is where the restaurant's name comes from. The building is a renovated former footwear-wholesaler warehouse; the diner occupies the 1st and 2nd floors, while the 3rd floor houses an in-house bakery workshop called "Manufacture" that bakes the bread served downstream and sells baked goods on weekends. It is run by Epietriz Inc. , which also operates a few other concept cafés in Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's guiding concept is "EAT GOOD" — food they would be happy to serve their own families, without chemical seasonings. Beyond sourcing organic/low-pesticide produce, the kitchen makes as much as possible in house: bacon, sausage and other charcuterie, salad dressing, ginger ale and lemonade are all handmade rather than bought in.
chef_bio.specialty: Avocado toast and the skillet-served English breakfast are among its best-known dishes, alongside a signature burger ("Sukeroku Burger") and a weekday morning set.

anecdotes.text: Owner Matsuura, who lives in Hayama, personally drives Miura-peninsula vegetables and local farm eggs to the restaurant most mornings; the shop also draws seasonal produce from contracted farms in Kyushu and from Epietriz's own farm in Yamanashi.

About 40% of the diner's customers are visitors from overseas; the bilingual (Japanese/English) menu and social posts reflect a stated aim of making the space welcoming to everyone regardless of nationality, age, or gender.
The diner's concept of hand-making processed foods and seasonings in-house, down to condiments, was covered as part of Epietriz's "EAT GOOD" philosophy at the restaurant's 2015 opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The diner operates its own on-site bakery ("Manufacture") baking fresh bread daily, meaning wheat flour is present and handled throughout the kitchen; no source confirms separated gluten-free prep.
Search of GF/celiac review channels surfaced no celiac corroboration of GF handling; the only GF artifact is a single 'Gluten-Free Omelets' photo on FindMeGlutenFree with no protocol described.

Venue described as serving 'natural yeast bread baked in the workshop on the third floor' (croissants, bagels, pain au chocolat) — confirms an on-site flour/wheat-saturated bakery integral to the kitchen, undermining any default GF-safe claim.

Described as 'a brunch and dinner place serving western food with few vegetarian and vegan options' — vegan is limited, not a core offering, consistent with 'options' ceiling and no vegan-dedicated handling.
Concept centers on in-house charcuterie, eggs, and bread ('EAT GOOD', additive-free charcuterie, farm eggs) — a wheat-, egg-, and meat-forward menu with no stated allergen/celiac protocol.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Described as a Western-food diner and bakery with only a few vegetarian and vegan menu items, rather than a vegan-focused kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guidance for visitors is to tell staff about vegetarian/vegan preferences and any allergies before choosing a menu item, rather than relying on menu marks alone.

positives.text: Menu carries English translations and vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free tags on select items (e.g., a gluten-free buddha bowl, a soy/green-tea latte), and the kitchen avoids chemical seasonings, making ingredients more transparent than a typical diner.
All charcuterie, dressings, and drinks (bacon, ginger ale, lemonade, etc.) are handmade in-house from named ingredient sources rather than bought pre-made, reducing hidden additive risk.

cultural_comfort.note: Bilingual (Japanese/English) menu, English-speaking staff, and roughly 40% international clientele make this an easy, low-friction stop for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.

places.name: 串徳（くしとく） (Kushitoku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The restaurant's entire concept is deep-fried, breaded (wheat flour/panko) kushiage skewers cooked in shared frying oil, almost certainly served with wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) dipping sauce/tsuyu.
