There is no indication ingredients or breading can be substituted. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegetable-only kushiage option, or plant-based accommodation was found in any source. The course is an omakase selection of roughly 30-35 skewers that includes meat and seafood as its specialty, and traditional kushiage tsuyu dipping sauce typically contains bonito-derived dashi. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation and course customization by the chef.
places.hours_raw: 17:30〜22:30 (L.O.); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya — near Shinsen Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kushitoku (串徳) is a kushiage (deep-fried skewer) specialty shop that traces its roots to 1978 in the Dogenzaka backstreets of Shibuya. It is now run by the founder's successor, a second-generation owner who works the counter personally, carrying forward the frying technique passe · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Kushitoku (串徳) is a kushiage (deep-fried skewer) specialty shop that traces its roots to 1978 in the Dogenzaka backstreets of Shibuya. It is now run by the founder's successor, a second-generation owner who works the counter personally, carrying forward the frying technique passed down from the shop's earlier years while adding his own refinements.
chef_bio.philosophy: A small, quiet, counter-only room built around watching the chef fry each skewer to order and talking with him directly, rather than a high-volume izakaya approach — the emphasis is on freshness, seasonality, and precision more typical of a sushi counter than a typical kushikatsu chain.
chef_bio.specialty: An omakase-style seasonal kushiage course — roughly 30 to 35 skewers per sitting, from classic staples to seasonal and limited items, coated in a light batter and fried in a house blend of three different oils to keep each ingredient's natural flavor and texture intact.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1978 in Shibuya's Dogenzaka area and has been carried on by a second-generation owner who maintains the original frying technique while introducing his own seasonal variations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source documented whether frying oil is shared across different ingredients (meat, seafood, vegetables) or whether any items are fried separately; the shop uses a blend of three oils but this refers to the type of oil, not fryer separation for allergen purposes.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional counter-only shop where the owner interacts directly with guests in Japanese; reservations are normally made by phone in Japanese, though one source noted international visitors have reserved via the shop's Instagram, so a translation app or Japanese-speaking companion is recommended.

places.name: 冨田書店 (Tomita Shoten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce were confirmed in any source. The menu includes wheat-containing items (pasta such as meatball spaghetti, castella pancakes, breaded/fried preparations), so cross-contact risk is likely. Plain charcoal-grilled meats and vegetables could be a safer option, but sauces and seasonings are unconfirmed — celiac diners should call ahead (03-6272-9758) and confirm specifics with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Seasonal, charcoal-grilled vegetables are part of the restaurant's concept and menu, but the overall menu is centered on wagyu beef, local chicken, and meat-based book-inspired dishes; no vegan menu, dashi/bonito-free labeling, or animal-stock-free assurances were found. Vegetables are likely grilled on the same charcoal grill as meat and may be seasoned with meat-based stock or sauces — confirm specifics with staff.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Sat/Sun/Holidays: through-service 11:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Some sources additionally report a weekday lunch service (approx. 11:30-14:30/15:00) and a Sunday closure — hours are inconsistently reported across platforms, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku (神田三崎町)

In 2017 the old bookshop was renovated into a charcoal-grill bistro by architect Kuma Taichi, son of renowned architect Kengo Kuma, who kept the wooden b · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: restaurant/bistro run by Nasuya, part of a small local hospitality group with a sister izakaya nearby
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (Kanda-Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku)
chef_bio.background: Tomita Shoten occupies a bookstore building from the early Showa era in Kanda-Misakicho, a short walk from Suidobashi Station. In 2017 the old bookshop was renovated into a charcoal-grill bistro by architect Kuma Taichi, son of renowned architect Kengo Kuma, who kept the wooden bookshelves and warm, mid-century atmosphere while turning the space into a restaurant. It is run by the local company Nasuya (owner Mitsufuji Kimihisa), which also operates the nearby motsu-dining spot Umakamon in Suidobashi.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept ties the space's former life as a bookstore to the menu: each table is given a picture book that doubles as part of the menu, and diners can order dishes inspired by scenes from beloved stories, paired with charcoal-grilled seasonal produce and meats and natural wine in a renovated, book-lined dining room.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled wagyu sirloin steak, and the restaurant's signature concept of recreating dishes that appear in famous books, manga, and anime (e. g. Sanji's crispy potato paille from One Piece, the meatball spaghetti from Lupin III: The Castle of Cagliostro, and the castella pancake from the picture book Guri and Gura), served alongside seasonal charcoal-grilled vegetables and a rotating natural wine list.

anecdotes.text: The building is a renovated early-Showa-era bookstore in Suidobashi/Misakicho, redesigned in 2017 by architect Kuma Taichi (son of Kengo Kuma), preserving a book-lined, mid-century interior.

Each table's menu takes the form of a picture book, and diners can order the actual dish described in that book's story — e.g. Sanji's crispy potato paille (One Piece), the meatball spaghetti from Lupin III: The Castle of Cagliostro, and a castella pancake inspired by Guri and Gura.
The restaurant is operated by Nasuya, a small Chiyoda-based hospitality company owned by Mitsufuji Kimihisa, which also runs the motsu-dining izakaya Umakamon near Suidobashi.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-concept restaurant (book-themed charcoal-grill bistro) with no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff, though it appears on English-language Tabelog and online reservation platforms; basic Japanese or a translation app is useful for describing dietary restrictions.

places.name: 郷土料理 くらわんか (Kyodo Ryori Kurawanka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated gluten-free preparation was found in any source. As a standard Japanese regional-cuisine izakaya, dishes are likely to use ordinary soy sauce (contains wheat), bonito/kombu dashi, and fried items (tempura-style preparations), which are not confirmed to be handled separately from other cooking. Celiac diners should not assume safety here without directly and explicitly confirming ingredients and preparation with staff in Japanese before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling was found. Regional Japanese cuisine of this type typically relies on bonito-based dashi and features meat/seafood specialties as the core of the rotating monthly menu, so most dishes are unlikely to be vegan by default. Vegetable-forward regional dishes may exist in a given month's rotation, but this would need to be confirmed with staff on a visit-by-visit basis, and dashi/broth bases should be assumed to contain fish unless stated otherwise.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 13:50) and 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30). Saturdays and public holidays: 15:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays, except that if the following Monday is a public holiday the restaurant opens Sunday and closes Monday instead.

The restaurant occupies the basement floors of the Daian Building, alongside a · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Rotating regional dishes sourced from across Japan, prepared in Shinjuku
chef_bio.background: An izakaya specializing in regional Japanese cuisine (郷土料理) located one minute from Shinjuku Station's Central East Exit, in operation for several decades under representative director Shinichi Yasuda. The restaurant occupies the basement floors of the Daian Building, alongside affiliated shops in the same building group.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is to let diners in Tokyo take a culinary tour of Japan without leaving Shinjuku, cycling the menu each month so that guests can sample specialties from a different prefecture or region alongside that region's own sake.
chef_bio.specialty: A monthly-rotating selection of traditional dishes drawn from different regions of Japan, served alongside regional sake, shochu, and awamori sourced directly from breweries nationwide, plus a wine list curated by the owner-sommelier. A limited daily lunch box called "Tamatebako Lunch" and a lunch set course (starters, sashimi, grilled dish, simmered dish, rice or cold udon, and dessert) are also offered.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant maintains direct relationships with sake breweries across the country, bringing in regional sake and shochu that it pairs with that month's rotating lineup of local specialty dishes.

Seating is arranged around named private and semi-private rooms styled after regional pottery traditions (Mashiko counter seats, Bizen table seats, Hagi and Imari/Karatsu private rooms), reflecting the restaurant's regional-Japan theme.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local izakaya aimed at Japanese diners, with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service, so some Japanese-language ability or a translation app will help.

places.name: 隠れや しぶ天 (Kakureya Shibuten (Shibu Ten))
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional tempura specialist: the batter is standard wheat flour and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is typically soy sauce (contains wheat) plus bonito dashi — both common gluten sources. No source found mentions a gluten-free batter, alternative flour, dedicated fryer, or any other celiac-safe accommodation, so it should not be considered safe for a strict gluten-free/celiac diet without direct (Japanese-language) confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: A specific all-vegetable tempura course is offered — 7 pieces of seasonal vegetables, fried to order, for ¥1,380 per person — introduced by the restaurant as being for vegan diners. This is a genuine, dedicated vegan option rather than an ad hoc substitution. However, no source confirms whether the accompanying dipping sauce/salt is free of bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, which is the most common vegan pitfall with Japanese tempura; vegan diners should ask for salt or a confirmed plant-based dip instead of the standard tentsuyu.
places.hours_raw: Lunch: Mon, Tue 11:30-14:30. Dinner: Mon-Fri 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Regular closed day: Sunday; a temporary notice also lists Saturdays and holidays as closed.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（渋谷駅より徒歩6分）

chef_bio.background: Tucked away on the 2nd floor above Shibuya's Miyamasu-zaka slope, near Aoyama Gakuin, this tempura bar bills itself as a 'kakureya' (hideaway) and has been operating in the same spot for around nine years.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, adult-oriented izakaya atmosphere — jazz playing, soft lighting — meant for lingering over course-by-course fried tempura rather than a quick meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable and seafood tempura paired with Japanese sake, umeshu, and other drinks.

anecdotes.text: The shop is described as sitting quietly above Miyamasu-zaka in Shibuya, near Aoyama Gakuin, with a jazz soundtrack and soft lighting meant to let solo diners, couples, and friends relax over tempura and drinks.

The restaurant offers a dedicated all-vegetable tempura course of seven pieces for ¥1,380 per person, introduced with the line 'we'd like vegans to enjoy this' — fried-to-order seasonal vegetables only.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated all-vegetable tempura course (7 pieces, ¥1,380 per person) explicitly aimed at vegan guests, using seasonal vegetables fried fresh to order rather than a modified version of a meat/seafood dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy, Japanese-language izakaya-style tempura bar aimed at local adult diners (jazz, low lighting); an English Tabelog listing exists but there's no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: アジアンダイニング&バー SAPANA 水道橋店 (Asian Dining & Bar SAPANA Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: Many curries are rice-based and could be gluten-free, but the restaurant also serves wheat naan, roti and other breads made in the same open kitchen, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or dedicated prep area is publicized. Diners with celiac disease should confirm specific dishes and cross-contact risk directly with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetarian set (samosa, spinach-cheese curry, vegetable curry, salad, naan or rice) is available, but curries commonly use ghee, butter, or dairy cream typical of Indian/Nepalese cooking, so strict vegans should confirm dairy-free preparation and any honey/animal-derived ingredients with staff before ordering. No dedicated vegan menu was found.
places.hours_raw: 24時間営業（年中無休）。ただし6:00〜9:00頃は清掃のため入店できない場合あり、深夜帯も営業していない場合がある。

In 2001 he opened his first two Asian restaurants in Iidabashi (Asian Kitc · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nepal
chef_bio.background: According to the Global SAPANA Group's company history, founder Bhuvan Adhikari was born in Nepal and moved to Japan after finishing high school, working jobs including dishwashing at an Indian restaurant. In 2001 he opened his first two Asian restaurants in Iidabashi (Asian Kitchen & Bar SAPANA and Iidabashi Prano), which grew into the multi-branch Global SAPANA Group that now includes this Suidobashi location.
chef_bio.specialty: Cheese naan made with an original cheese blend, tandoori chicken, and Indian/Thai-style curries with adjustable spice levels (mild to level 6).

anecdotes.text: Founder Bhuvan Adhikari moved from Nepal to Japan after high school and worked washing dishes at an Indian restaurant before opening his first SAPANA restaurants in Iidabashi in 2001.

positives.text: A vegetarian set is offered, including samosas, a spinach-cheese curry, a vegetable curry, salad, and a choice of naan or rice.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains an English-language corporate website and English Tabelog listing and serves a broad, multicultural clientele near Tokyo Dome/Suidobashi's business and student district, so English-speaking diners should find it easy to navigate.

places.name: 鶏そば かぐら屋 西新宿店 (Torisoba Kaguraya Nishi-Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option, allergen menu, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination protocol was found in any source. The noodles are wheat-based (custom-ordered from a Hokkaido-flour noodle maker), and the tare for the shoyu-style bowls and tsukemen is soy-sauce based — standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-containing unless specifically labeled otherwise, and no gluten-free labeling was found. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: All broths are chicken-bone based (paitan or clear chicken soup), and the tsukemen/shoyu tare additionally relies on katsuobushi (bonito flakes), saba-bushi (mackerel flakes) and niboshi (dried sardines) for dashi. No plant-based or vegetarian menu items were found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:30〜翌2:00 (日・祝は〜24:00) — some listings alternatively show 11:00〜23:00, 無休
places.neighborhood: West Shinjuku (Seibu-Shinjuku area), Shinjuku-ku
places.notes: Kaguraya built its name on a single idea: ramen made entirely from chicken, with no pork or beef anywhere in the broth. The Nishi-Shinjuku shop is one of several branches (Suidobashi, Ueno-Okachimachi and others) run under that same recipe and philosophy. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of the 鶏そば かぐら屋 (Tori Soba Kaguraya) chicken-ramen family, whose first shop opened near Suidobashi in January 2013
chef_bio.background: Kaguraya built its name on a single idea: ramen made entirely from chicken, with no pork or beef anywhere in the broth. The Nishi-Shinjuku shop is one of several branches (Suidobashi, Ueno-Okachimachi and others) run under that same recipe and philosophy.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen simmers large quantities of Oyama-brand chicken bones with vegetables, scallop shell and Hidaka kelp for roughly 12 hours, cracking the bones partway through to pull out marrow before re-filtering and re-emulsifying the broth, aiming for a smooth, chicken-only soup with no pork or beef backbone. Noodles are custom-ordered from Taisei Shokuhin, a noodle maker founded in 1917.
chef_bio.specialty: 濃熟鶏そば (Noujuku Tori Soba) — a rich, collagen-laden chicken paitan ramen

anecdotes.text: The concentrated white broth is made by slowly simmering whole Oyama chickens and bones with vegetables and kombu, then simmered again for 3-4 hours with chicken fat, with the bones cracked to extract marrow before a second filtering and emulsifying pass — about 12 hours total.
The tsukemen tare is built from chicken thigh simmered down with soy sauce, then layered with katsuobushi, saba-bushi, niboshi and konbu dashi, paired with thick Hokkaido-wheat noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-style and tsukemen tare are built on soy sauce combined with katsuobushi, saba-bushi, niboshi and konbu dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based and no gluten-free alternative was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood chicken-ramen shop near Seibu-Shinjuku Station with a compact counter-and-table layout; expect a Japanese-only ordering experience typical of small ramen shops, though it does list on Uber Eats/Demae-can for English-app-assisted delivery ordering.

places.name: 浅草 天彩 (Asakusa Amairo)
places.gf_detail: Tempura batter is traditional wheat-flour based, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based in standard Edo-style preparation (soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free batter, alternate flour, or dedicated fryer was mentioned by any source — not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on shrimp and conger-eel tempura rice bowls; vegetable tempura (shishito pepper, kabocha, shiitake) is fried in the same oil as seafood, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is traditionally dashi-based (bonito). No vegan-specific preparation, substitution, or dedicated frying setup was found in any source, so it should not be assumed vegan-safe.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–14:30 & 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:30–15:00 & 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00). One aggregator listed a single continuous 11:00–23:00 block with a 14:00–17:00 break instead — treat the split lunch/dinner service as the more consistently reported pattern.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2020 just off Nakamise-dori, a short walk from Kaminarimon Gate. The counter is run by two tempura cooks who each trained more than 30 years in Asakusa before opening their own shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2020 just off Nakamise-dori, a short walk from Kaminarimon Gate. The counter is run by two tempura cooks who each trained more than 30 years in Asakusa before opening their own shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen deliberately fries without sesame oil in its blend, aiming for a lighter, less heavy tempura while still keeping the classic Edo-mae style alive.
chef_bio.specialty: Tokusei Tendon (special tempura rice bowl) with conger eel (anago), two shrimp, a soft-set egg, and three seasonal vegetables (shishito pepper, kabocha squash, shiitake mushroom) over rice, alongside a larger 'mega tendon' version.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the tempura as fried fresh to order and coated in a well-balanced sweet-savory sauce, with plump shrimp and a finish that isn't too heavy or greasy.

cultural_comfort.note: Located steps from Kaminarimon Gate in a heavily touristed stretch of Asakusa, and reviews mention staff being welcoming to foreign visitors, though no dedicated English menu was confirmed.

places.name: 麺㐂 やしま 円山町店 (Yashima Maruyamacho-ten (Menki Yashima))
places.gf_detail: The noodles are traditional hand-made Sanuki udon, i.e. wheat flour — inherently not gluten-free, with no gluten-free noodle alternative documented anywhere. The dipping/pouring sauces are standard Japanese shoyu-based tsuyu, which conventionally contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free prep area, fryer, or noodle option is mentioned in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth used across the menu (including bukkake and tempura udon) is a bonito (katsuobushi) fish-based Kansai-style dashi — not vegan or vegetarian. No vegetable-only broth, plant-based dashi, or vegan menu option is documented in any available source.
places.hours_raw: Most consistently reported as 11:00-17:00 daily, closing earlier if the day's hand-made noodles sell out ("麺無くなり次第終了"). One source lists a more elaborate day-specific split (shorter Mon/Tue lunch-only hours, Wed-Fri lunch 11:30-16:00 plus dinner 18:00-21:00/21:30, weekends 11:30-21:30) — sources are not fully consistent, so treat hours as irregular and confirm same-day if possible.

It later moved to Matsuyama, then to Tokyo in the 1970s — first to Shibuya's Udagawa-cho, at a time when Sanuki-style udon was still largely unfamiliar in the Kanto region, then to Tomigaya · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Zentsuji, Kagawa Prefecture — the historic home of Sanuki udon
chef_bio.background: The shop traces back to a Sanuki udon shop founded in 1936 in Zentsuji, Kagawa Prefecture. It later moved to Matsuyama, then to Tokyo in the 1970s — first to Shibuya's Udagawa-cho, at a time when Sanuki-style udon was still largely unfamiliar in the Kanto region, then to Tomigaya. In 2013 it settled into the current Maruyama-cho location near Shinsen Station, now carried on by the fourth generation of the founding family.
chef_bio.philosophy: Continuing the hand-made noodle-making technique passed down across four generations since the shop's 1936 founding in Kagawa
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made Sanuki udon, served bukkake-style (noodles topped with a poured sauce) or with tempura, in a Kansai-style bonito dashi broth

anecdotes.text: The current Maruyama-cho shop is the latest stop in a lineage that runs from a 1936 Zentsuji (Kagawa) udon shop through Matsuyama, Shibuya Udagawa-cho, and Tomigaya, before settling here in 2013 under the fourth generation.
The interior has a retro, subculture atmosphere, decorated with vintage anime figurines and movie posters.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The udon broth is a Kansai-style dashi made with bonito (katsuobushi), used throughout the menu, including for tempura dipping sauces.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, locally-loved neighborhood udon shop with no documented English menu or foreign-visitor accommodations, though it is easy to point-and-order.

Not recommended for strict gluten-free diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff (in Japanese) of ingredient sourcing.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan accommodation was found in any source. The restaurant is built around seafood (sashimi, grilled fish/shellfish) and dashi-based oden, which almost certainly uses katsuobushi (bonito) stock throughout; not viable for vegan diners without a fully custom, pre-arranged order confirmed directly with staff.

The restaurant offers fully private-room seating for parties as w · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A charcoal-grill robatayaki izakaya operating as the Shinjuku flagship ("新宿本店") of a multi-branch robatayaki concept, located about a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's Keio New Line Exit 5 / Keio Mall Exit 3. The restaurant offers fully private-room seating for parties as well as open dining, built around a charcoal robata grill, fresh seafood, and Kyoto-style dashi oden.
chef_bio.specialty: 刺身の桶盛り — thick-cut sashimi heaped in a wooden tub, made from fish shipped in from Sado, Niigata, alongside seafood sourced from the Toyosu market; and 土鍋ご飯 — charcoal-finished clay-pot rice served in seasonal versions (e. g. salmon and salmon roe, charcoal-grilled mackerel, Wagyu sukiyaki-style).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature dish is 刺身の桶盛り: thick-cut sashimi from carefully selected fish, shipped directly from Sado in Niigata Prefecture, served heaped in a wooden tub for the table.
Its clay-pot rice (土鍋ご飯) course changes seasonally, with versions such as salmon-and-roe, charcoal-grilled mackerel, and Japanese-beef sukiyaki-style rice, cooked to order in individual pots.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free/gluten-free soy sauce alternative is advertised on any listing; as a standard robatayaki/oden izakaya, regular (wheat-containing) shoyu should be assumed throughout the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu, English-speaking staff, or foreign-visitor amenities were confirmed in any source; a Tabelog English-language listing page exists (auto-translated), but the restaurant itself appears to operate in Japanese, so visitors without Japanese should expect to rely on translation apps or picture menus.

places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen chart was found in any available source. The ramen uses standard wheat noodles, and the tare/flavor bases (soy-based tare, rayu chili oil, garlic mayu oil, basil-olive oil) are not documented as wheat-free. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Every ramen is built on a tonkotsu (pork bone) broth; no vegetarian, vegan, or plant-based broth alternative is documented in any source found.

In 2006 the company launched its tonkotsu (pork-bone) sub-brand, Butao, and this Shibuya location opened June 15, 2006 (renewed/reopened March 2, 2 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founder/Chef: Satoshi Ikuta (生田智志), originally from Fukuoka
chef_bio.origin: Shinjuku Golden Gai (2004) → Butao brand and this Shibuya shop (2006)
chef_bio.background: Ramen Nagi began in 2004 as a tiny 8-seat, one-day-a-week shop in Shinjuku's Golden Gai, founded by Fukuoka-born chef Satoshi Ikuta. In 2006 the company launched its tonkotsu (pork-bone) sub-brand, Butao, and this Shibuya location opened June 15, 2006 (renewed/reopened March 2, 2020). Butao later became an unexpected overseas hit as 'BUTAO' after opening a flagship in Hong Kong's Central district in 2010, and that overseas popularity helped cement its reputation back in Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim with the tonkotsu broth is to avoid the heavy, off-putting pork-bone odor common to the style while still delivering concentrated flavor, resulting in a broth that finishes lighter than its richness would suggest.
chef_bio.specialty: 豚王 (Butao) — a rich yet clean-finishing Hakata-style tonkotsu pork-bone ramen, simmered 10-12 hours, served alongside three creative variations: 赤王 (Akao, made with a house-made rayu chili-oil base and sweet aromatic vegetables), 黒王 (Kurao, roasted garlic mayu oil with black sesame and squid ink for deep umami), and 翠王 (Suio, a basil and olive-oil accented broth).

anecdotes.text: Butao's pork-bone ramen first broke out internationally as 'BUTAO' at a Hong Kong Central district location opened in November 2010, and that overseas success is credited with amplifying the brand's profile back home in Japan.
The shop at 渋谷区東1-3-1 first opened June 15, 2006, and was renewed with an updated tonkotsu recipe on March 2, 2020.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known, internationally recognized ramen chain (with overseas branches including Hong Kong) that draws many foreign visitors, but ordering is still via an in-store ticket vending machine typical of Japanese ramen shops.

places.name: ブックハウスカフェ (Book House Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-free preparation, or allergen accommodation are mentioned by the official site or any review/press source found. The signature dish is a roux-based curry (roux typically contains wheat flour) served with rice, plus pasta and bread sandwiches -- all likely to contain wheat, and there is no stated dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol. Assume this is not a safe option for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff, which could not be verified here.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan labeling, or plant-based substitution options are mentioned anywhere. The featured dishes are meat- and fish-based (beef curry, mentaiko/cod-roe pasta, tuna sandwich, egg sandwich), and there is no indication of dashi/bonito disclosure or vegan accommodation. Treat as not vegan-friendly unless confirmed directly with staff.
