A children's picture-book shop called "Bookhouse Jimbocho" occupied the same spot from 2007-2017 with bac · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanda Jimbocho, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The building has been in the Imoto family's book trade since her grandfather founded Kitazawa Bookstore here in 1902, later run as a foreign-language bookstore by her father. A children's picture-book shop called "Bookhouse Jimbocho" occupied the same spot from 2007-2017 with backing from major publishers, but closed in 2017 once it was felt to have fulfilled its cultural role. Yoshiko Imoto, who had raised her own children on visits to that shop, felt Jimbocho (Tokyo's famous book district) could not be allowed to lose its only picture-book specialist, and took over the business herself despite doubts from family, friends and neighboring booksellers. She reopened it as Book House Cafe on May 5, 2017, built on four pillars -- bookstore, cafe, events, and rentable gallery/event space -- to keep it financially sustainable.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep Jimbocho's only new-picture-book specialty store alive as a place where people connect over books, pairing over 10,000 picture books with a cafe, an evening picture-book bar ("Liliput"), and gallery/event space for readings and exhibitions.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef and cheese curry made with roux from a well-known Jimbocho curry shop, plus a house baked curry-bread and hayashi rice; it has been entered in the Kanda Curry Grand Prix.

anecdotes.text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, when business was in serious difficulty, Imoto posted a direct, crowdfunding-style appeal for support on the shop's own website, and customers rallied to help the store survive.
The shop occupies the site of the earlier publisher-backed picture-book store "Bookhouse Jimbocho" (2007-2017); Imoto took over rather than let Jimbocho lose its only picture-book specialist.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-language picture-book shop and cafe aimed at local families; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help most for ordering and asking about ingredients.

places.name: ぱん屋 (Pan-ya (Nishi-Shinjuku))
places.gf_detail: This is a conventional wheat-flour bakery specializing in naturally leavened (天然酵母) bagels and scones. No gluten-free bread, substitutions, or dedicated preparation area were mentioned in any source found, so it is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on dairy- and egg-rich items — ham & cheese scone sandwiches, honey blue cheese bagels, salted butter caramel, matcha red bean butter — with no vegan-labeled items, plant-based substitutions, or vegan menu callouts found in any source. A plain bagel exists on the menu but its exact ingredients (e.g. honey/malt in the boil) were not confirmed as vegan; best to ask staff directly if visiting.
places.hours_raw: 11:00~19:00頃（営業は火・水・金・土のみ、売り切れ次第終了。営業日・在庫はX(Twitter) @panya0411 で要確認）
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku) / near Nishi-Shinjuku Station
places.cuisine: 天然酵母パン・地元の小さなベーカリー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in November 2020 as a signless, word-of-mouth bakery tucked one street off Ome-kaido in Nishi-Shinjuku. With no storefront sign, it built its following almost entirely through regular customers and its X (Twitter) account, which the shop uses to announce each day's small-b · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in November 2020 as a signless, word-of-mouth bakery tucked one street off Ome-kaido in Nishi-Shinjuku. With no storefront sign, it built its following almost entirely through regular customers and its X (Twitter) account, which the shop uses to announce each day's small-batch stock, sometimes with same-day sell-out or schedule-change notices.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-shaped natural-yeast bagels, generously filled with rotating flavors (matcha red bean butter, honey blue cheese, salted butter caramel, zunda red bean, ham & cheese), plus made-to-order scone sandwiches on Tuesdays.

anecdotes.text: The shop has no sign and sits down an alley off Ome-kaido; it grew a devoted following purely by word of mouth and through its X (Twitter) account (run under the handle 'ぱん屋'), which posts each day's small-batch bread lineup and lets regulars reserve items ahead of pickup.
Reviewers describe the natural-yeast bagels as satisfyingly chewy ('mucchiri') and heavily filled, calling it a distinctive small bakery worth seeking out in the Nishi-Shinjuku office district.

cultural_comfort.note: There is no storefront signage and daily stock/schedule updates are posted only in Japanese on the shop's X (Twitter) account, so some Japanese ability (or a companion who can read it) helps you find the shop and know what is available that day.

places.name: チョンプー (CHOMPOO) (Chompoo)
places.gf_detail: The vegan-focused directory VegeVege lists CHOMPOO with a "GF" tag alongside V/VG/organic tags, and search summaries note the restaurant asks guests to report allergies and disliked ingredients in advance (same-day requests may not be accommodated). However, no source examined gave dish-level confirmation of which sauces are gluten-free — Thai cooking commonly uses soy sauce and oyster sauce, both of which can contain wheat — or confirmed a dedicated fryer/prep area. Celiac-level diners should call ahead (03-6455-0396) and confirm sauce ingredients directly.
places.vegan_detail: A distinct vegan Thai tasting course is available (6-8 dishes: multiple appetizers, Kao Yam herb salad, soup, a warm plant-based main such as vegan gapao/green curry, and dessert with herbal tea), made only with vegetables, fruit, beans and herbs — no fish sauce, oyster sauce, meat, egg or dairy in that course. This is a set course rather than freely substitutable a-la-carte dishes across the whole regular menu, so vegans dining outside the vegan course should still confirm individual dishes for fish sauce or oyster sauce, which are common in Thai cooking.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya PARCO 4F, Udagawa-cho / Center-gai area)
places.notes: Chef Kan Morieda trained at Tetsuya's in Sydney, at the Kyoto kaiseki restaurant Kogetsu (湖月), and at the molecular-gastronomy Tapas Molecular Bar inside the Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, before going independent in 2011 and later opening CHOMPOO in Shibuya PARCO in November 2019. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner-produced by chef Kan Morieda (森枝幹)
chef_bio.background: Chef Kan Morieda trained at Tetsuya's in Sydney, at the Kyoto kaiseki restaurant Kogetsu (湖月), and at the molecular-gastronomy Tapas Molecular Bar inside the Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, before going independent in 2011 and later opening CHOMPOO in Shibuya PARCO in November 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: An additive-free, MSG-free take on Thai food built on generous use of fresh herbs and spices — described by the chef's team as not 'Thailand's first Thai food' nor 'just spicy', but a comforting, deeply flavorful ('しみじみおいしい') style of Thai cooking, with colorful, photogenic plating.
chef_bio.specialty: カオヤム (Kao Yam) — a vividly blue butterfly-pea-rice herb salad that has become the restaurant's signature, widely shared on social media.

anecdotes.text: CHOMPOO opened in November 2019 on the 4th floor of Shibuya PARCO as one of the dining anchors of the rebuilt mall, produced by chef Kan Morieda (who also ran Shimokitazawa's Salmon and Trout).
The restaurant has run seasonal menu refreshes highlighting fresh herbs and spices for spring/summer, and collaborations such as a limited-time terrace pairing with Veuve Clicquot.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Restaurant listing guidance indicates food allergies and disliked ingredients should be communicated in advance, and requests made on the day of the visit may not be accommodatable.

positives.text: CHOMPOO offers a dedicated multi-course vegan tasting menu (6 to 8 dishes) made only from seasonal vegetables, fruits, beans and herbs with no animal products — including appetizers, a Kao Yam herb salad, soup, a warm main, and plant-based dessert with tea.
The restaurant is additive-free and MSG-free across its menu.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits inside the very tourist-trafficked Shibuya PARCO complex near Shibuya Scramble, so basic English support and card payment are likely, but the menu, reservation platforms (Ikyu, OZmall, Hot Pepper) and course descriptions found are all in Japanese.

places.name: PEACE TABLE 渋谷道玄坂店（ピーステーブル） (PEACE TABLE (Shibuya Dogenzaka))
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and no official allergen/cross-contamination statement was found. However, the plant-based, largely Italian/Ayurveda-influenced menu (no traditional soy-sauce/dashi-based Japanese cooking) reduces typical wheat-via-shoyu risk, and at least one diner reported being served fresh, soft gluten-free bread as part of a standard course, suggesting gluten-free substitutions are available. Celiac travelers should confirm cross-contamination handling directly with staff before dining.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely vegan kitchen (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs). Being an Ayurveda/Italian-leaning concept rather than a traditional Japanese kitchen, it does not rely on katsuo/bonito dashi. The set courses are built around vegetables, curry, and pasta, with a reviewer noting the meal was explicitly dairy-free — unusual and deliberate given Ayurvedic cooking often defaults to dairy (ghee).
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:30-21:00 (last seating around 19:30). Closed 2nd & 4th Saturdays and Sundays, plus irregular additional closures.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya Dogenzaka (渋谷道玄坂), 3-minute walk from Shibuya Statio
places.notes: According to restaurant-opening coverage, chef Tetsuo Ohira trained in cooking across roughly 20 countries and spent 8 years (from 2009) as owner-chef of a restaurant called "Lotus & Flowers One" before opening PEACE TABLE in Shibuya in 2020 as an all-vegan flagship. The restaura · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: According to restaurant-opening coverage, chef Tetsuo Ohira trained in cooking across roughly 20 countries and spent 8 years (from 2009) as owner-chef of a restaurant called "Lotus & Flowers One" before opening PEACE TABLE in Shibuya in 2020 as an all-vegan flagship. The restaurant was later renovated to build out a full Ayurveda program, developed with input from Dr. Kazuo Uemba, described in press coverage as a leading figure in Japanese Ayurveda.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name PEACE TABLE expresses the idea that people can share good food and a moment of peace regardless of nationality, religion or skin color; the restaurant's peace-sign logo doubles as the "V" of "vegan. "
chef_bio.specialty: A personalized Ayurveda-based vegan set course: guests fill out a dosha questionnaire (Vata / Pitta / Kapha) and are served a matching course of soup, bread, salad, main and dessert — for example a creamy mushroom-and-truffle pasta on the Kapha menu.

anecdotes.text: Guests are given a warming Agni drink to aid digestion while completing a dosha questionnaire (an English version is available), then receive a course tailored to their result — one visitor's Kapha-type meal came with a creamy mushroom truffle pasta and a gluten-free bread described as fresh and soft.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A visiting food blogger noted her Kapha-type set course came with gluten-free bread that was "super fresh and soft," though no formal cross-contamination or dedicated-equipment statement was found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Described as a fully vegan restaurant serving Ayurveda-style meals without dairy (notable since traditional Ayurvedic cooking often uses ghee/dairy), suggesting deliberate control over animal-derived ingredients.

staff_allergy_handling.text: On arrival, guests fill out a dosha (constitution) questionnaire — an English version is available — and staff/kitchen tailor the course to the guest's answers, which provides a natural touchpoint to flag allergies or gluten-free needs.

positives.text: 100% vegan, personalized menu system with an English questionnaire and a welcome digestive (Agni) drink before the meal.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language dosha questionnaire and personalized menu guidance are offered, making the concept accessible to non-Japanese-speaking diners even though it requires more explanation than an average restaurant.

places.name: 東京メルカート 東京ドームシティ店 (Tokyo Mercato (Tokyo Dome City / Suidobashi))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free pizza dough could be confirmed from any source. The signature dishes are wood-fired Neapolitan-style pizza and pasta, both wheat-based, and no source mentions gluten-free adaptations. Assume standard shared kitchen; not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. Every dish is served with a fresh vegetable salad side, and vegetable-topped pizzas/pastas exist on the menu, but cheese, butter, and animal stock are standard in the kitchen and no source confirms dairy-free preparation. Vegetarians could likely find something with cheese; strict vegans would need to ask staff carefully about hidden animal products.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (food last order 21:00, drink last order 21:00); last order may extend to 21:30 on Tokyo Dome event days
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidōbashi, Tokyo Dome City Meets Port 3F)
places.cuisine: イタリアン・ピッツァ・ワインバー
places.notes: TOKYO MERCATO is designed to recreate the feel of an Italian open-air market (mercato) inside Tokyo Dome City, combining a wood-fired pizzeria, an illy-brand espresso bar, and a sommelier-curated wine selection under one roof. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: pizza chef trained in Ischia, Southern Italy
chef_bio.origin: Part of the TOKYO MERCATO brand (operated by Aquaplannet/Carvino group), with this branch inside Tokyo Dome City's Meets Port building
chef_bio.background: TOKYO MERCATO is designed to recreate the feel of an Italian open-air market (mercato) inside Tokyo Dome City, combining a wood-fired pizzeria, an illy-brand espresso bar, and a sommelier-curated wine selection under one roof.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let guests experience the lively, market-like atmosphere and food culture of Italy in a single open, terrace-style space, suited to everything from casual meals to parties.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza (crispy outside, chewy inside), baked by a pizza chef trained on Ischia Island in Southern Italy, alongside seasonal pasta dishes.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's pizza chef trained on Ischia Island in southern Italy, a region known for its historic Neapolitan pizzerias, before bringing that wood-fired technique to the Tokyo Dome City location.

The space is built around the concept of a Mercato (Italian market), pairing the pizzeria with an illy espresso bar and sommelier-recommended wines so guests can sample multiple facets of Italian food culture in one visit.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits inside the Tokyo Dome City shopping/entertainment complex, a heavily tourist-trafficked area, so basic English signage and picture menus are likely, though full English service is not confirmed.

places.name: TEXMEX FACTORY 渋谷公園通り店（テクスメクスファクトリー） (TEXMEX FACTORY Shibuya Koendori)
places.gf_detail: Both gluten-free corn tortillas and wheat 'flower' tortillas are offered, so a corn-tortilla taco is possible in principle, but this is a busy all-you-can-eat buffet-style operation (Endless Tacos) where stations, grills and serving utensils are shared with wheat tortillas and other wheat-containing items. No dedicated gluten-free fryer, prep area, or celiac protocol has been documented anywhere found, so cross-contact risk is real and unconfirmed — not recommended for celiac-level sensitivity without direct confirmation from staff at time of visit.
places.vegan_detail: Weekday lunch and the regular taco line-up include a plant-based 'Veggie Meat' (ベジタブルミート) taco option alongside the meat tacos, and make-your-own fajitas let diners skip meat and load up on guacamole, pico de gallo and beans. However standard dishes are built around cheese, sour cream and meat, no dashi/bonito-free stock has been confirmed, and there is no dedicated vegan menu or certification — vegan diners should specify no cheese/sour cream and confirm ingredients with staff.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・公園通り（渋谷駅徒歩5分）
places.cuisine: メキシコ料理・タコス
places.notes: TEXMEX FACTORY opened in Shibuya on August 22, 2016 as a new Tex-Mex ('old-school Mexican culture' filtered through Texas-style flavors) casual dining restaurant and bar launched by major Japanese restaurant operator Watami Co., Ltd. The Koendori-area branch sits a short walk fro · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Watami Co., Ltd. — operating company (chain casual dining & bar concept)
chef_bio.background: TEXMEX FACTORY opened in Shibuya on August 22, 2016 as a new Tex-Mex ('old-school Mexican culture' filtered through Texas-style flavors) casual dining restaurant and bar launched by major Japanese restaurant operator Watami Co. , Ltd. The Koendori-area branch sits a short walk from Shibuya Station and has since become known for its lively, colorful interior and all-you-can-eat taco format.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept leans into a bold, playful take on Tex-Mex: vivid primary colors, a pink key color, skull motifs throughout, and interior decor imported directly from Mexico and Texas, aiming to transport diners into a fun, festive 'south of the border' atmosphere rather than pursue formal authenticity.
chef_bio.specialty: Endless Tacos — all-you-can-eat tacos, taco bowls and burritos with unlimited soup and drink-bar refills (weekday lunch), plus sizzling-iron-plate fajitas that diners wrap themselves in tortillas with guacamole, pico de gallo and sour cream.

anecdotes.text: A visitor report described lines forming before opening on weekdays in Shibuya for the ¥1,380 all-you-can-eat taco/taco-rice lunch, noting the festive pink-and-skull decor as a highlight of the visit.

A local Shibuya gourmet write-up described the restaurant as offering an authentic-feeling Mexican dining experience just a short walk from the station, citing the imported decor and taco/fajita/burrito line-up.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant serves both corn and wheat 'flower' tortillas side by side as part of an all-you-can-eat buffet-style taco service, with no mention anywhere of separate gluten-free prep, utensils, or stations.

positives.text: Corn tortillas (naturally gluten-free) are offered as an alternative to wheat 'flower' tortillas across the taco menu.
A plant-based 'Veggie Meat' taco/lunch option is listed alongside the meat-based tacos, giving vegetarians a menu item that doesn't require special requests.

cultural_comfort.note: A high-volume Tex-Mex chain restaurant a few minutes from Shibuya Station with an English-language Tabelog listing and a picture-heavy taco/fajita menu, so ordering should be manageable with basic gestures even without shared language.

places.name: シャガット（Chat Gatto） (Chat Gatto (Shagatto))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The restaurant's signature dish is a wheat-based egg pasta (tajarin), and bread/pasta appear to be central to the French-Italian menu, so this is not a safe option confirmed for celiac diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is meat- and fish-centric (Ehime charcuterie, wild boar roast, seafood marinades) with a vegetable side dish plate (野菜のそうざいプレート) also offered, but no dedicated vegan menu, and no information on whether stocks/sauces use dashi, bonito, or animal-based bases was found. Not confirmed safe for strict vegan diners without asking staff directly.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町（神保町駅徒歩3〜4分）
places.cuisine: フレンチ・イタリアン
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chat Gatto opened in 2016, run by a husband-and-wife chef duo: Takashi, who trained in Italian cooking, and Yoshiko, who trained in French cooking. The restaurant's name blends the French word "chat" and the Italian word "gatto" (both meaning cat), symbolizing the two chefs worki · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 薄田貴志・薄田宜子 (Usuda Takashi & Usuda Yoshiko)
chef_bio.origin: Owner Usuda Takashi is from the Imabari / Shimanami area of Ehime Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Chat Gatto opened in 2016, run by a husband-and-wife chef duo: Takashi, who trained in Italian cooking, and Yoshiko, who trained in French cooking. The restaurant's name blends the French word "chat" and the Italian word "gatto" (both meaning cat), symbolizing the two chefs working freely together across their two culinary traditions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Home-style French and Italian cooking centered on vin nature (natural wine) and ingredients shipped directly from Ehime Prefecture — vegetables and seafood from the owner's native Imabari/Shimanami area, plus local poultry, pork, venison and wild boar.
chef_bio.specialty: Tajarin, a traditional Piedmont-style egg pasta made with Ehime-prefecture eggs, served with gold sesame and anchovy

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is tucked in a quiet back alley between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations with only about 10 seats, giving it a hideaway feel — regulars recommend booking ahead.

The menu's tajarin pasta, a traditional dish from Italy's Piedmont region, is made with eggs sourced directly from Ehime Prefecture, along with vegetables, fish and meats from the owner's home region.
Menu highlights spotted by diners include Ehime vegetable and fish marinades, a charcuterie platter, roasted wild boar, and tajarin with gold sesame and anchovy.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, reservation-recommended neighborhood bistro in a back alley with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so a translation app or basic Japanese will help.

places.name: 煮干中華そば 鈴蘭 (Niboshi Chuka Soba Suzuran)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options identified. This is a wheat-noodle ramen/tsukemen shop; the broth is a "triple soup" of tonkotsu (pork bone), chicken, and niboshi (dried sardine) dashi, and shoyu-based tare/soy sauce (which typically contains wheat) is standard in this style of ramen. No mention anywhere of gluten-free noodles, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen-aware preparation; celiac/gluten-sensitive diners should assume high cross-contamination risk and avoid.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options identified. The broth is explicitly a "triple soup" built from pork bone (tonkotsu), chicken, and niboshi (dried sardine) dashi — hidden/base-level animal ingredients are unavoidable in every bowl, and no plant-based broth or dish is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-23:30 (Sundays 11:30-23:00), 無休 (no closed days)
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目駅 / 新宿区新宿
places.cuisine: つけ麺・煮干ラーメン
places.notes: Nibo-shi Chuka Soba Suzuran is a niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen specialty shop near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, run by the INGS restaurant group, which operates several Tokyo ramen brands including Raa Men Hayashida. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: operated by INGS Co., Ltd. (also known for らぁ麺はやし田 and other ramen brands)
chef_bio.background: Nibo-shi Chuka Soba Suzuran is a niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen specialty shop near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, run by the INGS restaurant group, which operates several Tokyo ramen brands including Raa Men Hayashida.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop aims for a niboshi broth with little of the bitterness (エグ味) that puts off people who usually dislike dried-sardine ramen, so the flavor stays refined, balanced, and clean-finishing despite its richness.
chef_bio.specialty: 濃厚煮干中華そば (rich niboshi ramen) and つけ麺 (tsukemen), made with a triple soup combining tonkotsu (pork bone), chicken, and dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi, topped with low-temperature-cooked rare chashu; noodles come in a choice of medium-thick curly or low-hydration thin styles.

anecdotes.text: The shop periodically runs a quantity-limited special, 特製炭火焼鯵煮干そば (charcoal-grilled horse mackerel niboshi soba), alongside its regular tsukemen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or gluten-free accommodations were found in any source; this is a conventional wheat-noodle ramen shop where cross-contact with wheat is presumed throughout (shared broth pots, shared noodle-boiling water, shoyu-based tare).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's ramen and tsukemen use a shoyu/tare-seasoned niboshi-tonkotsu-chicken soup, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature soup is a triple stock of pork bone (tonkotsu), chicken, and dried sardine (niboshi), with no vegetarian or vegan broth option mentioned anywhere.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard neighborhood ramen counter shop with no indication of English menus or English-speaking service, so expect Japanese-only ordering (likely point-and-order or simple counter service).

places.name: ラーメン二郎 新宿小滝橋通り店 (Ramen Jiro Shinjuku Kotakibashi-dori)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Jiro-style shop: thick house-made wheat noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare/broth, meaning wheat is baked into both the noodles and the soup itself. There is no gluten-free noodle substitute or separate GF menu, and the tiny open kitchen (shared vats, one ladle line) makes cross-contact effectively unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base and standard bowls carry large portions of chashu pork plus pork back fat; there is no vegetable or plant-based broth, and no vegan menu items are documented anywhere.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:00–22:30 (L.O.); Sun & public holidays 11:00–21:30 (L.O.)
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku)
places.notes: Opened February 8, 1999. It began life as one of the licensed "Jiro Foods System" (フーズ系) satellite shops operating under the Jiro name but outside the founder's direct lineage. After Ramen Jiro's trademark was formally registered in 2003, most of these satellite shops were forced · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Directly-managed Ramen Jiro branch (formerly an independently licensed "Foods-kei" satellite)
chef_bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Opened February 8, 1999. It began life as one of the licensed "Jiro Foods System" (フーズ系) satellite shops operating under the Jiro name but outside the founder's direct lineage. After Ramen Jiro's trademark was formally registered in 2003, most of these satellite shops were forced to rebrand or fold — leaving only the Kanda shop as a lingering Foods-kei holdout — and the Kotakibashi-dori shop transitioned to direct management under Jiro headquarters.
chef_bio.specialty: Standard Jiro-style ramen: thick wheat noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) pork-bone broth, piled with cabbage, bean sprouts, and thick-cut chashu, customizable via the shop's shouted "call" system (garlic, vegetables, pork fat, sauce). Unusually for a Jiro shop it also offers table seating and a cheaper "student ramen. "

anecdotes.text: During its years as a Foods-kei satellite the shop had a rocky reputation and was jokingly nicknamed "the worst Jiro" among devoted fans (ジロリアン); its standing has since recovered, with reports of the recipe improving further around 2017 and the noodles becoming noticeably thicker around 2019.
Unlike most Jiro shops, which are counter-only, this location has a 4-person and a 2-person table alongside its 12-seat counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Extremely small, single-line open kitchen where noodles, broth, and toppings for every order pass through the same equipment — no separated prep area exists for allergen-sensitive orders.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare/soup is described as a soy-sauce (shoyu) based, micro-emulsified broth with a pronounced soy sauce character — standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base simmered with pork, and standard bowls are topped with generous chashu pork and often pork back fat (abura); no plant-based broth or protein option is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: Ramen Jiro shops run on an unwritten etiquette and a shouted "call" system for customizing toppings (garlic, vegetables, oil, sauce) that can feel intimidating without some Japanese-language prep, though many foreign fans do fine with a quick primer beforehand.

places.name: 珈琲館 渋谷店 (Kohikan Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any source. The food menu includes clearly wheat-containing items (toast, sandwiches, hayashi rice served over rice but sauced with a flour-thickened demi-glace roux, Western-style cakes), so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff — and even then no gluten-free preparation process is documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-labeled dishes were found. Plain drip coffee, charcoal-roasted coffee, and tea are inherently vegan, but the food menu (sandwiches, toast, hayashi rice, Western cakes such as cheesecake and chocolate cake, matcha parfait) relies on dairy, egg, and/or meat, with no dashi/katsuo-free or plant-based alternative noted anywhere in the sources reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 8:00–20:00; Saturdays, Sundays & holidays 10:00–19:00 (closed only when the Shibuya Cross Tower building itself is closed)
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（渋谷クロスタワー内、渋谷駅東口すぐ）
places.notes: Kohikan is a long-running Japanese kissaten (traditional coffee shop) chain founded in 1970. In 2021 the original Kohikan Co. merged with Chatnoir (Café Veloce's parent) to form C-United, now part of the Colowide restaurant group. This Shibuya branch sits on the ground floor of S · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Japanese kissaten (coffee shop) chain, this location seats solo diners and groups on the ground floor of Shibuya Cross Tower
chef_bio.origin: 1970年創業の老舗喫茶チェーン「珈琲館」（現C-United株式会社／コロワイドグループ）
chef_bio.background: Kohikan is a long-running Japanese kissaten (traditional coffee shop) chain founded in 1970. In 2021 the original Kohikan Co. merged with Chatnoir (Café Veloce's parent) to form C-United, now part of the Colowide restaurant group. This Shibuya branch sits on the ground floor of Shibuya Cross Tower, a short walk from Shibuya Station's east exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's long-standing motto is "one cup of coffee, made with all our heart" (一杯のコーヒーに心をこめて), reflecting a full-service, unhurried kissaten style rather than a quick-turnover coffee bar.
chef_bio.specialty: Sumibi Kohi (charcoal-roasted coffee), brewed from beans roasted over premium Japanese binchotan charcoal, alongside the chain's original house blend and seasonal specialty coffees.

anecdotes.text: Kohikan built its identity around binchotan charcoal-roasted coffee and a wide, comfortable seating area meant to welcome solo customers and groups alike for a relaxed, old-school Japanese cafe experience.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard Japanese chain kissaten operating in Japanese with no evidence of an English menu; ordering and explaining dietary needs will likely require basic Japanese or pointing at the menu.

places.name: 関山米穀店 (Sekiyama Beikokuten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi has been documented anywhere. The tapas list includes breaded croquettes and pasta/risotto dishes that typically contain wheat. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm ingredient-by-ingredient with the chef before visiting; this is not a place with established gluten-free protocols.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on Spanish-style charcuterie (pâté de campagne, pork terrine), meat/fish mains, and pasta/risotto. A few vegetable-based tapas may exist among the 20-plus dishes, but no source confirms meat-, fish-, or dashi-free stocks and sauces. Vegan diners should ask specifically before ordering; this is not a vegan-oriented kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金・祝前日 17:00〜翌0:00、土・祝日 17:00〜23:00、日曜定休（祝日の扱いは変動あり）
places.neighborhood: 神田小川町 (Kanda-Ogawamachi), near Jimbocho/Ogawamachi/Awajicho
places.cuisine: Natural wine bar / Spanish-style tapas bistr
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Sekiyama cooked Italian and Spanish food and ran the wine izakaya Pont du Gard in Shintomicho before opening this bistro in Kanda-Ogawamachi in 2015. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Sekiyama cooked Italian and Spanish food and ran the wine izakaya Pont du Gard in Shintomicho before opening this bistro in Kanda-Ogawamachi in 2015.
chef_bio.philosophy: The wine list favors small producers from France, Italy, Spain and Japan, chosen for bottles that show the terroir and winemaker's vision without tiring the palate over a long evening.
chef_bio.specialty: Spanish-style tapas paired with a roughly 300-bottle all-natural wine list.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name comes from the rice shop his family ran in Ibaraki Prefecture from his grandfather's generation onward — a name he kept out of affection for that legacy even though the shop now pours wine, not rice.
Before opening this spot in 2015, Sekiyama had cooked Italian and Spanish food and run the wine izakaya Pont du Gard in Shintomicho.
The roughly 300-bottle natural wine list favors small producers from France, Italy, Spain and Japan, chosen for wines that show terroir and the winemaker's vision without tiring the palate.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, Japanese-run natural wine bistro with no English menu or English-language booking found; reserving by phone in Japanese (or via a Japanese-capable booking app) is recommended.

places.name: 上海飯店 (Shanghai Hanten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodations were found in any source. Signature items include gyoza (wheat-flour wrapper) and noodle dishes (wheat noodles), and standard Chinese-restaurant soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) is presumably used throughout. There is no mention of a dedicated fryer, allergy menu, or wheat-free substitutions. Treat as unsafe for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is meat- and seafood-centric (mapo eggplant in a meat-based sauce, garlic-chive meat stir-fry, gyoza, fried rice), with no vegan dishes, labeling, or menu section identified in any source. Vegetable-forward items likely still use meat stock, oyster sauce, or lard typical of machi-chuka cooking. Vegan diners would need to ask staff directly (in Japanese) about substitutions, with no guarantee of accommodation.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:30 daily including holidays (per one aggregator source; unverified against a second primary source)
places.neighborhood: 水道橋
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 上海飯店 is a long-running 町中華 (neighborhood Chinese diner) near Suidōbashi Station, said to trace back to around 1963 (Shōwa 38), identifiable by its red-and-yellow awning. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 上海飯店 is a long-running 町中華 (neighborhood Chinese diner) near Suidōbashi Station, said to trace back to around 1963 (Shōwa 38), identifiable by its red-and-yellow awning.
chef_bio.specialty: 麻婆ナス定食 (mapo eggplant set meal) — large eggplant pieces in a thick, spicy sauce — is repeatedly cited as the signature dish, alongside juicy gyoza and a garlic-chive-and-meat stir-fry set.

anecdotes.text: Described as a Shōwa-era (1960s-founded) town Chinese restaurant, with a distinctive red-and-yellow awning near Suidōbashi Station.
The dining room is set up with large tables, making it a popular choice for groups eating together, including after Tokyo Dome baseball games.
The mapo eggplant set meal is called out as the standout order, alongside gyoza, chilled Chinese noodles, and a garlic-chive meat stir-fry set.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional, local-regulars-style 町中華 diner with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help with ordering and any allergy questions.

places.name: らぁ麺 鳳仙花 (Ra-men Hosenka)
places.gf_detail: The ramen noodles are made from a blend of several wheat varieties (including whole wheat/全粒粉) chosen for chew and a high-hydration texture — there is no gluten-free noodle substitute documented anywhere. The signature broth is a shio (salt) tare rather than a heavy soy-sauce tare, which may reduce but does not eliminate wheat exposure risk from seasonings, and no dedicated gluten-free process, fryer, or cross-contamination protocol is documented. Because the core product is wheat noodles in a shared open kitchen, this is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The base broth is a kinmedai and madai fish-bone dashi (not a plant-based stock), and the standard bowl is topped with sous-vide pork shoulder chashu. No plant-based broth, noodle, or topping substitution is documented on any menu source found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00〜23:00（L.O.22:45）、無休（スープがなくなり次第終了の場合あり）
places.neighborhood: 新宿・歌舞伎町 (Shinjuku, Kabukicho)
places.notes: Ra-men Hosenka opened on July 26, 2018 in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, as a sister shop to the ramen brands 煮干中華そば鈴蘭 (Niboshi Chuka Soba Suzuran) and らぁ麺はやし田 (Ra-men Hayashida), all operated by the restaurant company INGS Co., Ltd. (founded 2009), which runs seven ramen shops plus the It · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: signature dish: kinmedai (golden-eye snapper) shio ramen
chef_bio.background: Ra-men Hosenka opened on July 26, 2018 in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, as a sister shop to the ramen brands 煮干中華そば鈴蘭 (Niboshi Chuka Soba Suzuran) and らぁ麺はやし田 (Ra-men Hayashida), all operated by the restaurant company INGS Co. , Ltd. (founded 2009), which runs seven ramen shops plus the Italian restaurant chain CONA. Within the INGS ramen family, each shop has its own broth identity — Suzuran is dried-sardine (niboshi) based, Hayashida is chicken based, and Hosenka is the group's seafood-based shop, built around kinmedai (golden-eye snapper).
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps no condiments on the tables, asking diners to taste the kinmedai broth exactly as the chef intends rather than adjust it themselves; the shop is also promoted as 無化調 (no added MSG), extracting its umami from the fish bones alone.
chef_bio.specialty: Kinmedai ramen (金目鯛らぁ麺) — a light, shio-style bowl made from a dashi of kinmedai and madai (sea bream) bones, extracted with careful temperature and time control to keep the broth delicate rather than heavy. The chashu is sous-vide pork shoulder loin served rare for a moist, tender texture, topped with two large, crisp menma shoots. For an extra ¥200, diners can finish the meal with kinmedai chazuke, pouring the remaining dashi over rice.

anecdotes.text: 店内には卓上調味料がなく、スープや具材へのこだわりが徹底されています (There are no condiments on the tables — the shop is thoroughly committed to its soup and toppings as prepared).
チャーシューは豚肩ロースを低温調理したレアタイプで、しっとりとしてやわらかな食感が特徴 (The chashu is sous-vide pork shoulder loin served rare, prized for its moist, tender texture).
鯛塩系のラーメン...最後に金目鯛茶漬けで二度オイシイ (A tai-shio style ramen finished, for an extra charge, with kinmedai chazuke for a second course).

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature broth is a kinmedai/madai fish-bone dashi and the standard topping is pork chashu, so the dish itself is built on fish and meat, not merely at risk of cross-contact.

positives.text: The shop advertises itself as 無化調 (no added MSG), sourcing its umami from kinmedai/madai bones alone rather than seasoning additives.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is by ticket machine, payment is cash-only, and no English menu is documented, so some comfort with Japanese-style ramen-shop customs (or willingness to point/gesture) is helpful.

places.name: 海鮮居酒屋 魚秀 渋谷宇田川店（UOHIDE） (Kaisen Izakaya Uohide Shibuya Udagawa-ten (U)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found in any source checked. As a seafood izakaya, sashimi and raw seafood items are naturally low in gluten, but shared soy sauce (typically wheat-based), dashi, tempura, and sauces should be assumed to contain wheat unless confirmed otherwise with staff. No information on a dedicated fryer or formal allergy protocol was found — celiac diners should ask directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or vegan-specific information was found in any source. This is a seafood-focused izakaya run by a fish wholesaler, so dashi/bonito-based stocks and fish are central to nearly all dishes; it should not be presented as vegan-friendly. Vegan diners would need to ask staff about plain vegetable side dishes and confirm no fish stock is used, with no guarantee of a suitable option.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sun, holidays, and days before holidays: 12:00–23:15 (food L.O. 22:45, drink L.O. 22:45). Lunch 12:00–15:00 / Dinner 15:00–23:15.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（宇田川町、渋谷駅近く）
places.notes: This Shibuya Udagawa-cho shop is one location of the small Tokyo seafood-izakaya group 魚秀 (UOHIDE), which is run directly by a fish wholesaler and operates a handful of seafood-focused restaurants around Tokyo (including branches in Kichijoji and Tsukiji) in addition to this Shib · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: seafood izakaya run directly by a fish wholesaler/fishmonger (鮮魚卸直営)
chef_bio.background: This Shibuya Udagawa-cho shop is one location of the small Tokyo seafood-izakaya group 魚秀 (UOHIDE), which is run directly by a fish wholesaler and operates a handful of seafood-focused restaurants around Tokyo (including branches in Kichijoji and Tsukiji) in addition to this Shibuya location, a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.specialty: Ochoko-don (おちょこ丼) — small rice bowls piled high with a variety of fresh seafood toppings — alongside seasonal sashimi platters and a rotating raw seafood 'yukhoe'-style tartare fair, sourced through the group's own fish wholesale line.

cultural_comfort.note: Standard Japanese-run seafood izakaya near Shibuya Station; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or translation help is advisable.

places.name: 水道橋すしわさび (Suidōbashi Sushi Wasabi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any official or review source found. As a sushi/izakaya spot, standard shoyu (contains wheat) is almost certainly used for dipping and glazes, and fried/tempura-style appetizers are on the menu. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should ask staff directly before ordering; nothing here should be assumed safe by default.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/bonito-free preparations were found in any source. As a fish-centric sushi bar, dashi and sauces should be assumed to contain katsuo (bonito) by default. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without prior direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 daily (food last order 22:00, drink last order 22:30); hours may vary during New Year, Golden Week, and Obon periods.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋駅 徒歩3分 / 文京区後楽（東京ドームシティ内）
places.notes: Operated by GYRO HOLDINGS, this is a casual all-counter sushi izakaya set inside a lively, neon-lit sports-and-entertainment food hall next to Tokyo Dome, built as a livelier spin-off of its quieter Jimbocho sister shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: sushi izakaya / sister branch of Jimbocho Sushi Wasabi
chef_bio.origin: Sister branch (4th location) of the original 神保町すしわさび (Jimbocho Sushi Wasabi), opened June 24, 2024 inside the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall at Tokyo Dome City.
chef_bio.background: Operated by GYRO HOLDINGS, this is a casual all-counter sushi izakaya set inside a lively, neon-lit sports-and-entertainment food hall next to Tokyo Dome, built as a livelier spin-off of its quieter Jimbocho sister shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: A pop, high-energy take on the neighborhood sushi bar — fresh market fish and chef-made original small plates served at a counter, meant for solo diners, casual meetups with friends, or a relaxed date, with big screens and neon signage around the food hall.
chef_bio.specialty: Bara chirashi (scattered sushi rice bowl) — the shop's signature dish, alongside hand-pressed nigiri sets (toro, seared, Hokkaido seafood) and seafood rice bowls at lunch.

anecdotes.text: 水道橋すしわさびは、神保町駅から徒歩2分ほどの「神保町 すしわさび」の4店舗目となる姉妹店。ネオン看板が目を引く全席カウンターの寿司酒場で、派手でポップな空間で味わう新感覚の寿司体験が特徴。夜はお酒と創作おつまみ、昼は海鮮丼やランチ握り、名物のバラちらしが楽しめる。

厳選鮮魚と絶品つまみ — casual sushi tavern offering sushi, good sake, and appetizers, featuring fresh fish sourced from the market and the chef's original creative small dishes; open 11:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drinks L.O. 22:30).

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, Japanese-language-first sushi izakaya inside a busy local food hall — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will get you through, but don't expect English-speaking staff or an English allergy menu.

places.name: 地鶏と焼酎 とりちゅう (Jidori to Shochu Torichu (Torichu — Free-Ran)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free tare, or celiac protocol was found in any source. As a yakitori/izakaya centered on tare-glazed grilled chicken, dishes very likely use wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) in the tare/marinades, and some menu items (e.g., tori-zuke nanban, described as marinated fried chicken) appear to be breaded/fried, raising cross-contamination risk in shared fryers. No source confirms a wheat-free option, dedicated fryer, or ingredient disclosure. Not recommended for strict gluten-free or celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around jidori chicken (grilled skewers, tataki, marinated fried chicken, chicken rice) with meat as the centerpiece of virtually every dish; rice dishes such as tori-meshi are cooked in chicken broth. No vegan or vegetarian menu, plant-based dishes, or accommodations were found in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:30), Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. food 22:00 / drink 22:30). Fri (and day before holidays): Lunch 11:30-15:00, Dinner 17:00-23:30. Sat: Lunch 11:30-14:30, Dinner 16:30-23:00. Closed Sundays and holidays.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 Suidobashi
places.notes: Part of the Sun Dining restaurant group, this Suidobashi izakaya is built around slow-reared free-range chicken from Kagoshima and a deep shochu list, bringing a Kyushu-style chicken-and-spirits izakaya experience near Tokyo Dome/Suidobashi Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Izakaya specializing in Kagoshima free-range chicken (jidori) and shochu
chef_bio.origin: Kagoshima / Amami, Kyushu — the chicken breed and the rice dish both trace to Kagoshima and Amami culinary traditions
chef_bio.background: Part of the Sun Dining restaurant group, this Suidobashi izakaya is built around slow-reared free-range chicken from Kagoshima and a deep shochu list, bringing a Kyushu-style chicken-and-spirits izakaya experience near Tokyo Dome/Suidobashi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Centers the menu on long-reared, high-quality jidori chicken paired with one of the larger izakaya shochu selections in Tokyo (roughly 200 labels, including about 30 ready-mixed shochu drinks), aiming to bring Kyushu chicken-and-shochu izakaya culture to central Tokyo
chef_bio.specialty: Chiran-dori (知覧どり), a Kagoshima free-range chicken raised roughly 450 days (far longer than typical broiler chicken), served as kurogayaki (black-seared grilled skewers) and tataki, alongside signature tori-zuke nanban (marinated fried chicken) and Amami-style tori-meshi chicken rice simmered daily in Chiran-dori broth

cultural_comfort.note: Menu, signage, and online reservations are geared toward Japanese-speaking guests with no confirmed English menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help.

places.name: ソバージュ (SOBA-JU)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are made from coarse-ground (hikigurumi) whole buckwheat flour, and at least one visitor described them as close to juuwari (100% buckwheat), but the restaurant's own materials do not state an exact flour ratio or confirm a wheat-free blend, so a two-eight (wheat-containing) mix cannot be ruled out. The signature broth is a mackerel-and-bonito dashi that, like most Japanese soba tsuyu, is very likely seasoned with ordinary (wheat-containing) soy sauce. There is no mention of a dedicated gluten-free prep area, dedicated boiling water, or wheat-free soy sauce. Celiac or severely gluten-sensitive diners should call ahead and ask directly about the buckwheat ratio and soy sauce ingredients before relying on this spot.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen's signature dishes are all built on animal ingredients: the standard soba broth is a mackerel-and-bonito (katsuo) dashi, and the lunchtime specialty is meat soba topped with duck, pork, or beef; the evening menu leans further into meat/offal izakaya dishes served with wine. No vegan-specific broth, noodle, or dish was found in any source, so this is not a workable option for vegan diners without a from-scratch off-menu request that the restaurant may not be able to accommodate.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Mon-Sat); Dinner 18:00-25:00/1:00am (Tue-Sat); Monday dinner and all day Sunday closed; hours may vary before public holidays
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.notes: SOBA-JU opened in Shinjuku Sanchome in August 2019 as part of a small local restaurant group (alongside the wine bar Margo), reimagining soba as a bar-friendly dish to pair with wine and sake. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: SOBA-JU opened in Shinjuku Sanchome in August 2019 as part of a small local restaurant group (alongside the wine bar Margo), reimagining soba as a bar-friendly dish to pair with wine and sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: A stylish, counter-only 'soba bar' concept that treats soba as a food to linger over with a glass of wine, rather than a quick lunchtime bite, while still keeping the noodles themselves the centerpiece.
chef_bio.specialty: Niku-soba (meat soba) with duck, pork, or beef, served over thick, chewy, coarse-ground (hikigurumi) buckwheat noodles in a broth built from a mackerel-and-bonito dashi blend, given a subtle kick from domestic sansho pepper and chili oil.

anecdotes.text: A visiting soba blogger described SOBA-JU's noodles as thick, full-grained, and close to juuwari (100% buckwheat), with a fragrant aroma and pronounced chewy texture, and specifically praised the beef soba (gyu-soba).

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free prep area, cookware, or boiling water is mentioned anywhere in available sources.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house broth is a mackerel-and-bonito dashi typical of soba tsuyu, which in Japan is almost always seasoned with standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce or tamari option was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core menu (meat soba, mackerel-bonito dashi broth, offal dishes at dinner) is built around fish and meat stock, so vegan diners cannot rely on shared broth or sauces being incidentally plant-based.

positives.text: The soba flour is a coarse whole-grain (hikigurumi) buckwheat, and one visiting blogger judged the noodles close to juuwari (100% buckwheat) with a fragrant, hearty texture.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-only neighborhood soba bar with counter seating and no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese or pointing/translation-app use will likely be needed.

places.name: 円山町わだつみ (Maruyamacho Wadatsumi)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic nihachi (二八) soba restaurant — the noodles are milled at roughly 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour as a binder, and the tempura is a wheat-flour batter, so neither the noodles nor the tempura are gluten-free. No dedicated fryer, gluten-free noodle option, or wheat-free menu is documented anywhere in available sources. Traditional soba tsuyu (dipping broth) is also typically built on regular (wheat-containing) shoyu. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: As a traditional soba-and-tempura restaurant, the dipping broth (tsuyu) is conventionally made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and the menu includes wagyu, tuna, and other animal-based dishes alongside seafood-inclusive tempura. No vegan menu, dashi substitution, or plant-based broth is documented in any source reviewed.
places.neighborhood: 円山町（Maruyama-chō）/ 神泉（Shinsen）, Shibuya
places.notes: Opened in May 2012 inside 'Maruyamachō Sanchō,' a Japanese wooden house more than 70 years old in Maruyama-chō, Shibuya — an area that once flourished as a geisha district. The restaurant's menu has since narrowed from a broader wagyu/tuna kaiseki concept toward hand-made soba an · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: soba and tempura chefs (individual chef not named in available sources)
chef_bio.background: Opened in May 2012 inside 'Maruyamachō Sanchō,' a Japanese wooden house more than 70 years old in Maruyama-chō, Shibuya — an area that once flourished as a geisha district. The restaurant's menu has since narrowed from a broader wagyu/tuna kaiseki concept toward hand-made soba and freshly fried tempura as its centerpiece.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant describes its cooking as layering a modern sensibility onto a foundation of Japanese tradition, set within a space meant to evoke the atmosphere of old Maruyama-chō's geisha-district era.
chef_bio.specialty: Nihachi (two-eight) soba, made from buckwheat sourced seasonally from carefully chosen regions, served alongside freshly fried tempura that guests can eat with rock salt or dip into the tsuyu broth.

anecdotes.text: Guests enter through a corridor from the street that leads into 'Maruyamachō Sanchō,' a century-old house whose hallways and fittings still carry traces of the shamisen music and lantern-lit geishas that once filled the neighborhood at night.

The dining space is split into a main house ('母屋') and a separate annex ('はなれ'), both built around counter seating so diners can watch the chefs' handiwork while a small courtyard garden changes with the seasons.
The restaurant's tempura is plated separately from the soba so it stays crisp, meant to be eaten first with a touch of rock salt before the noodles are dipped in the broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional hideaway soba-and-tempura restaurant inside a renovated century-old house; expect Japanese-language menus and reservation channels (TableCheck/Ikyu/AutoReserve in Japanese) rather than English signage or staff.

places.name: 鮨 美吟 (Sushi Bigin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented anywhere for this restaurant. Traditional Edomae sushi relies on nikiri (a soy-based glaze) and regular shoyu for dipping, both of which normally contain wheat, and none of the sources found mention a gluten-free alternative, a dedicated fryer, or celiac-aware handling. Diners with celiac disease or a serious gluten allergy should not assume safety here and should call ahead in Japanese before booking.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi restaurant built around raw, cured, and vinegared fish and shellfish; no vegan menu, substitutions, or plant-based omakase option is documented in any source found. Traditional Edomae preparations typically use bonito (katsuo) dashi in the tamagoyaki and other components, so it is not a workable option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Online reservation slots run from 17:30 through 24:00 (last bookable start time), Tuesday through Saturday, dinner service only; closed Sunday and Monday. Exact posted opening/closing times were not found independently of the reservation-slot list.
places.neighborhood: Jimbocho (Kanda-Jinbocho), Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in Jimbocho in August 2025, run by a chef who spent many years honing his craft in Edomae-style sushi before striking out on his own with this small counter. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Ichikawa (surname as referenced in restaurant listings; not independently verified via a fetched primary source)
chef_bio.background: Opened in Jimbocho in August 2025, run by a chef who spent many years honing his craft in Edomae-style sushi before striking out on his own with this small counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Described as blending traditional Edomae technique and carefully selected seasonal ingredients with the chef's own sensibility, aiming for a refined, beautifully presented, calm counter experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi (traditional vinegared, cured, and aged preparations rather than plain raw fish)

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note that the roughly ¥20,000 course delivers quality comparable to high-end Tokyo sushi-ya that typically charge over ¥30,000, calling it remarkable value for the craftsmanship shown.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, reservation-only traditional sushi counter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; booking is via phone or Japanese-language reservation platforms, so English-speaking diners may want help booking.

places.name: CAFE ZUKKE（カフェ ズッケ） (Cafe Zukke)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The signature dishes include wheat-based pizza dough and beef tongue stew typically served with bread; no information on soy sauce/dashi wheat content, shared fryers, or cross-contamination protocols was available. Do not assume celiac safety — call ahead if this matters.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-adapted dishes were found. The core menu is meat- and dairy-centric (beef tongue stew, hamburger, steak, cheese-topped pizza, dairy desserts); no information found on hidden dashi/katsuo use in savory dishes. Not recommended as a vegan option without direct confirmation.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 daily (food last order 22:00, drink last order 22:30); lunch service 11:00-15:00. Closed days: irregular (不定休).
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku Sanchome

The shop's name is said to come from an Italian expression meaning roughly 'cheer me up.' · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: CAFE ZUKKE is an independently run cafe/bar in an antique-furnished space in Shinjuku Sanchome, a 1-2 minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station exits C5, C6 and C8. The shop's name is said to come from an Italian expression meaning roughly 'cheer me up. '
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated house motto is "delicious and fun" (美味しくて楽しい) - creating an exciting, comfortable moment for guests in a relaxed antique-and-jazz atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Slow-braised beef tongue stew (タンシチュー) and handmade pizza (Margherita and Quattro Formaggi), alongside made-in-house desserts such as tiramisu and Basque cheesecake.

anecdotes.text: The owner is described as a tiramisu enthusiast who put particular care into perfecting the cafe's tiramisu recipe.

The interior is furnished with antique-style furniture, dark gray concrete-look walls and jazz played through curated vacuum-tube amplifiers/speakers, aiming for a calm 'hideaway' feel near Shinjuku Sanchome.
Signature beef tongue stew is made by slow-braising thick-cut beef tongue in red wine until very tender.

cultural_comfort.note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; expect a standard Japanese-language cafe experience, though ordering by pointing at photos should be manageable.

places.name: noki shibuya（ノキシブヤ） (Noki Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce information was found in any source. As an Italian restaurant the menu likely includes pasta and bread items containing wheat; gluten-free diners should call ahead and ask specifically about ingredients and cross-contact before dining, since nothing confirming safe handling was located.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around organic, low-pesticide vegetables directly sourced from contracted farms, with vegetable-forward grilled dishes, but the restaurant is not vegan or vegetarian — dinner courses also feature chicken (e.g., a cream-sauce chicken stew) and, as an Italian kitchen, likely uses dairy (cheese, cream) and possibly eggs. No dedicated vegan menu or vegan-substitution policy was found; vegan diners should ask staff which vegetable dishes can be prepared without meat, dairy, or eggs.

places.name: シリバラジ 水道橋店 (Sri Balaji Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: Much of the South Indian menu (dosa, idli, vada, sambar) is naturally rice- and lentil-based rather than wheat-based, and no wheat-centric North Indian items like naan or roti were found on the menu. However, no explicit gluten-free labeling, dedicated gluten-free kitchen, or cross-contamination protocol was found in available sources. Celiac or highly gluten-sensitive diners should ask staff directly about specific dishes, fryer sharing, and any wheat-containing condiments or batters before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: South Indian vegetarian staples (dosa varieties, idli, vada, sambar, vegetable korma, vegetarian meals/thali) make up a large part of the menu, giving vegetarians many options. However, South Indian cooking commonly uses ghee (clarified butter) and dairy (curd/yogurt) in batters and curries, and the restaurant also serves chicken dishes (tandoori chicken, chicken biryani, chicken curry), so it is not a dedicated vegan restaurant. Vegans should ask staff which dishes are prepared without ghee, dairy, or honey.

roles: chef (South Indian, trained in Tamil Nadu)
chef_bio.origin: Tamil Nadu, South India (kitchen team's background)
chef_bio.background: Sri Balaji (シリバラジ) began as a South Indian restaurant in Nakameguro and later opened this Suidobashi branch in 2015 in Nishikanda, a spot wedged between Suidobashi and Jimbocho stations. The restaurant takes its name from Sri Balaji, one of South India's most widely worshipped deities — regarded as a god of wealth — who also gives his name to a major Hindu temple.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to serve South Indian food 'exactly as it tastes locally,' without adapting flavors for Japanese diners, using spices and herbs imported directly from India.
chef_bio.specialty: Meals (South Indian thali-style set), dosa (rice-and-lentil crepes), and tiffin-style light dishes

anecdotes.text: The chef comes from South India and previously worked at a well-known hotel in Tamil Nadu; the restaurant imports its spices and herbs directly from India rather than substituting local ingredients.
The restaurant's name, Sri Balaji, refers to one of South India's most famous deities, worshipped as a god of wealth, and is also the name of a major Hindu temple in South India.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All lunch meal sets (Regular Meals ¥890, Chennai Meals ¥990, Sri Balaji Special Meals ¥1,200) come with 'ライス or ナン' (rice OR naan) plus papad — directly contradicting the GF label's claim that no naan/roti was found.
The Special Thali set includes 'プーリ or チャパティ' (poori OR chapati) alongside chicken/mutton/fish curry, rasam, chicken 65, papad, basmati rice, salad, curd, dessert — multiple wheat breads on the menu.
120-minute all-you-can-eat + all-you-can-drink banquet plan, ¥4,500, ~68 dishes including pakoda — buffet service with shared serving utensils is a major cross-contamination vector for celiac diners.

Reservation/allergy handling amounts only to a generic note: 'If you have allergies, please consult with us.' No gluten-free labeling, dedicated prep, or cross-contamination protocol is described anywhere.

positives.text: The kitchen uses spices and herbs imported directly from India and keeps recipes faithful to authentic South Indian preparation rather than adapting them for the Japanese palate, which can mean fewer substituted or hidden ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains an English-language official site and English Tabelog listing and is frequently reviewed by English-language food blogs and Tripadvisor, suggesting reasonable comfort for non-Japanese-speaking diners.

Celiac diners should call ahead to ask about ingredient specifics before visiting; no gluten-free accommodation could be confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: No standalone vegan menu was found. As a Chinese dim sum and hot pot restaurant, some vegetable-based dim sum items likely exist, but broths, sauces, and hot pot stock commonly contain animal-derived ingredients (e.g., chicken/pork stock, oyster sauce) unless specifically requested otherwise; this could not be confirmed either way from available sources. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about ingredients in the medicinal mushroom hot pot broth and individual dim sum fillings.

chef_bio.background: 168 Tenshin Yamucha & Yakuzen Nabe is the Shibuya Hikarie branch of Gozenbo (御膳房), a well-known Chinese medicinal-cuisine restaurant group with sister locations in Roppongi and Ginza. It opened on the 6th floor of Shibuya Hikarie in April 2022, bringing chefs recruited from mainland China to a mall setting one minute from Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on the Chinese principle of isshoku-dogen (医食同源) — 'medicine and food share the same source' — using organic Japanese produce and ingredients chosen for both flavor and wellness.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade dim sum (xiaolongbao soup dumplings, gyoza, shumai) and 'yakuzen' medicinal mushroom hot pot

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is the Shibuya Hikarie branch of Gozenbo, a longstanding medicinal-cuisine restaurant group based in Roppongi and Ginza, with dim sum made by chefs invited from mainland China.
The medicinal mushroom hot pot is nicknamed 'taberu esute' (an edible spa treatment) in Yunnan, China, where it has traditionally been eaten by women for its skin-beauty benefits.
Dim sum is handmade following the isshoku-dogen ('medicine and food, same source') philosophy, using organic Japanese ingredients selected by chefs trained at top mainland Chinese restaurants.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside Shibuya Hikarie, a major shopping complex directly connected to Shibuya Station with English-language reservation pages (Tabelog EN, TableCheck EN), so basic English/tourist accommodation is likely, though an English menu on-site is unconfirmed.

Ask staff to confirm ingredients for specific dishes.

Search-result summaries of a dancyu magazine feature describe her as the smiling proprietress (女将) of the shop, with the sign out front showing a woman cheerfully holding a giant naan and a beer mug.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is run as a family operation by Rami-san, her husband, and their son, serving deep-cut Newari Nepalese dishes alongside Japanese izakaya-style drinking food.

Lunch centers on naan-and-curry sets and dal bhat; dinner turns into an ethnic-cuisine izakaya featuring Newari specialties from Nepal's capital region, using more than 20 spices flown in directly from India and Nepal.
anecdotes.source: Google search summary of restaurant listings (akr8370252446.owst.jp; kanda-curry.com)

positives.text: Lunch dal bhat (rice, lentil soup, and vegetable/curry sides) is a traditional Nepalese staple that is often naturally vegetarian, though this specific restaurant's preparation and cross-contact practices were not confirmed.

chef_bio.background: Bánh Xèo Saigon is a small Vietnamese restaurant group (a sister branch also operates near Suidōbashi/Tokyo Dome City). The Shinjuku branch closed its previous 'Shinjuku Milo Road' location in spring 2024 and reopened a short distance away on June 10, 2024 in the Kawano Annex building, spread across two floors (6F/7F) with an upgraded interior and service level.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring authentic Saigon-style Vietnamese cooking to Shinjuku in a refined, modern-colonial dining space blending French elegance with Vietnamese casual sensibility.
chef_bio.specialty: Bánh xèo (large Vietnamese savory rice-flour crêpe), roasted duck, and fresh spring rolls

anecdotes.text: The restaurant relocated and reopened on June 10 with an upgraded interior and service, after previously operating on 'Shinjuku Milo Road' until that March, billed as authentic Vietnamese cuisine 'coming back' to the neighborhood.

The weekday lunch salad bar, with roughly 15 kinds of domestically grown vegetables plus fresh spring rolls, shrimp crackers and desserts, has made the restaurant especially popular with female diners.

positives.text: The menu includes a dedicated 'Mon Chay' (vegetarian/vegan) marked section distinct from the standard dishes.
Weekday lunch includes an all-you-can-eat salad bar of roughly 15 kinds of fresh, domestically sourced vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu and service appear primarily Japanese-oriented (with an English-language Tabelog listing existing), so basic Japanese, pointing at photos, or asking staff directly is helpful, especially for confirming allergen details.

No vegan menu, plant-based cutlet option, or dashi substitution was found in any source, and traditional Japanese sauces/dashi used here likely include bonito-derived stock. chef_bio.origin: Nihonbashi, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Katsukichi opened in 1962 near Korakuen/Tokyo Dome and has kept its retro Showa-era interior almost unchanged ever since. The founder, Yoshida Kichizaemon, was the son of Iwatani Shohei, a well-known Meiji-era tobacco magnate. The dining room is filled with roughly 2,000 antiques — Imari porcelain tableware and reclaimed wood and water pipes salvaged from the old Tamagawa Canal — giving it the feel of a small private museum.
chef_bio.philosophy: A craftsman's commitment to quality ingredients — branded pork, fresh breadcrumbs, and fresh oil — carried forward largely unchanged since the shop's founding over 60 years ago.
chef_bio.specialty: Tonkatsu made from select branded domestic pork fillet, hand-coated in fresh nama-panko breadcrumbs and slowly fried in fresh corn oil; also known for a steak served with a distinctive soy-sauce-based sauce rather than the usual demi-glace.

anecdotes.text: Novelist Yukio Mishima, who trained at a nearby gym in Korakuen, was a regular. According to a well-known story, when a steak he ordered wasn't cooked the way he liked, he sent it back untouched and left without eating — and his preference for soy sauce over the restaurant's original demi-glace sauce is said to be the origin of the soy-sauce steak that later became a house specialty.
Author Yasunari Kawabata, a close acquaintance of the founder, gifted the restaurant a calligraphy work reading '亥' (boar), which is still displayed on the restaurant's wood paneling today.

cultural_comfort.note: A historic, traditional Japanese tonkatsu restaurant with a retro Showa-era atmosphere and antique-filled dining room; no English menu was confirmed, so a little Japanese or pointing at the picture menu may help.

places.name: かめや 新宿店 (Kameya Shinjuku-ten (Kameya Shinjuku))
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners. Reporting on tachigui soba shops generally (including this one) notes that standing-counter soba noodles use a higher wheat-flour ratio than seated shops so the strands survive fast handling, meaning the soba itself carries significant wheat. The tempura batter is also wheat-based, and the concentrated tsuyu dipping broth is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle, batter, or sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere, and there is no indication of allergen-aware kitchen handling at this fast, open-counter operation.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegan diners. The signature ten-tama soba is topped with a soft-cooked hot-spring egg and tempura, and the house tsuyu broth is soy-sauce based and (as at essentially all traditional soba shops of this type) very likely built on bonito/katsuo dashi — no vegetarian or vegan broth alternative is mentioned in any source. No plant-based menu items or accommodations were found.

It is widely credited in Tokyo food writing as the birthplace of ten-tama soba — soba t · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Rooted in the old Ueno Ikenohata kappo restaurant Kameya Issuitei (亀屋一睡亭); opened as a stand in Shinjuku Nishiguchi Omoide Yokocho in Showa 46 (1971).
chef_bio.background: Kameya opened in 1971 as one of the early stalls in Shinjuku Nishiguchi Omoide Yokocho (the old 'Shomben Yokocho'), tracing its roots to the historic Ueno Ikenohata restaurant Kameya Issuitei. It is widely credited in Tokyo food writing as the birthplace of ten-tama soba — soba topped with tempura and a soft egg — and has since grown into a small group of standing soba counters across Tokyo (Kanda, Okachimachi, Shimbashi, Ginza) while its original Shinjuku counter keeps running nearly around the clock.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fast, no-frills soba built for standing-counter speed, carried by a deeply concentrated soy-based tsuyu broth the shop has refined for over 50 years.
chef_bio.specialty: 元祖天玉そば (Ganso Ten-tama Soba) — soba topped with freshly fried kakiage tempura and a soft hot-spring egg

anecdotes.text: Kameya is credited as the originator of ten-tama soba, topping kakiage tempura with a soft-cooked hot-spring egg instead of a raw one — a combination now considered a Tokyo standing-soba classic.
The Shinjuku shop has no doors or walls, built like an open-air stall — a look it has kept for more than 50 years since opening in 1971.
The shop's dark, richly concentrated soba tsuyu is a deliberate house style, giving even a hurried standing-counter meal deep, memorable flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standing (tachigui) soba shops like this one typically raise the wheat-flour ratio in their noodles so the strands hold up to fast prep and handling — the soba here is not a low-gluten or gluten-free product.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dishes are served in a deliberately concentrated, dark soy-sauce-based soba tsuyu (dipping/soup broth), the shop's defining house style.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature ten-tama soba is topped with a soft hot-spring egg (onsen tamago) and kakiage tempura over a non-vegan tsuyu broth.

cultural_comfort.note: Orders are placed and paid for verbally with staff at the counter — no ticket machine and no confirmed English menu — so a little Japanese or pointing at neighbors' bowls helps.

places.name: にょろ助 東南屋 浅草 (Nyorosuke Tatsumiya Asakusa)

Tempura is wheat-battered, and eel and hot-pot dishes in this style of cooking are typically finished with soy-sauce-based sauces/broths, though no source for this specific restaurant explicitly confirmed the tare or broth recipe. No gluten-free menu, wheat-free options, or dedicated fryer were mentioned anywhere. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: This is a specialist unagi (eel), dojō (loach hot pot), tempura, and sukiyaki restaurant — the entire menu is built around animal proteins and traditional Japanese cooking bases. No vegetarian or vegan menu, options, or accommodations were documented in any source found.
