chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (site southeast of Sensō-ji Temple)
chef_bio.background: The restaurant occupies a building that has kept its Shōwa-era (mid-20th-century) street-front appearance. It previously operated under the related name たつみ屋 (Tatsumiya) and reopened as にょろ助 東南屋 浅草 on November 11, 2006, run by the Tokyo restaurant group 際コーポレーション (Kiwa Corporation), which operates a small number of similarly branded 'Nyorosuke' eel/loach restaurants. The shop's name, 東南屋, literally 'southeast house,' refers to its position southeast of Sensō-ji Temple and is read 'Tatsumiya' as a nod to the old zodiac-direction name for that compass point.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeps old shitamachi (downtown Asakusa) dining traditions alive by serving unagi, dojō (loach), tempura, and sukiyaki together under one roof, in a room with sunken-kotatsu tatami seating reminiscent of an earlier era.
chef_bio.specialty: 地焼きうなぎ (jiyaki-style grilled eel) — eel grilled directly without pre-steaming, a Kansai-region technique that yields a crisp exterior, tender flesh, and more pronounced eel flavor than the steamed Kantō style; also known for どぜう鍋 (Asakusa-style loach hot pot), a downtown Edo-period commoners' dish served heaped with scallions.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's jiyaki grilling method — grilling the eel without pre-steaming — is a distinctly Kansai technique, said to give the eel a crisper bite, tender flesh, and stronger aroma than the more common steamed Kantō style.

The shop's name, Tōnan-ya (東南屋), refers to its location southeast of Sensō-ji Temple and is read 'Tatsumiya,' echoing the old zodiac-direction name for that compass point; the restaurant previously traded under the related name Tatsumiya before rebranding as にょろ助 東南屋 in 2006.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu and picture/model displays are available, and staff have been described by visitors as helpful with non-Japanese-speaking guests.

places.name: 渋谷 ガパオ食堂 (Shibuya Gapao Shokudo (Gapao Shokudo Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or dedicated fryer was confirmed in available sources. Thai cooking here relies heavily on oyster sauce and soy sauce (both typically contain wheat) as base seasonings, including reportedly in the signature Gapao Rice, so most dishes should be assumed to contain gluten unless kitchen staff confirm otherwise. One listing indicates the restaurant welcomes allergy inquiries, so celiac diners should call ahead (03-6455-1817) and ask specifically about wheat-free substitutions and cross-contact in the wok before dining.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu found. Thai dishes at this restaurant commonly use fish sauce (nam pla), oyster sauce, and other animal-derived seasonings as standard base ingredients (e.g., the signature Gapao Rice uses chicken and oyster sauce/fish sauce). Vegetable-forward Thai dishes may be orderable but are likely to contain hidden fish sauce or shrimp paste unless the kitchen is specifically asked to omit them; vegan diners should call ahead and confirm ingredient substitutions.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00), reported as open daily with no fixed closed days.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂/桜丘町・神泉 (Dogenzaka/Sakuragaoka-cho, near Shinsen Station)
places.notes: The Shibuya branch of Gapao Shokudo is led by a veteran Thai head chef from Thailand's northeastern Isan region, described in restaurant write-ups as one of Japan's ten most highly regarded Thai chefs ("Isaan cooking master") with roughly 40 years of culinary career and over 20 y · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Isan (Isaan), northeastern Thailand
chef_bio.background: The Shibuya branch of Gapao Shokudo is led by a veteran Thai head chef from Thailand's northeastern Isan region, described in restaurant write-ups as one of Japan's ten most highly regarded Thai chefs ("Isaan cooking master") with roughly 40 years of culinary career and over 20 years living and cooking in Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Focuses on authentic, home-style Isan/northeastern Thai cooking rather than a Japan-adapted version of Thai food, drawing on the chef's own regional roots.
chef_bio.specialty: Gapao Rice (fresh holy-basil chicken stir-fry with chili and oyster sauce over rice) — the restaurant's namesake signature dish

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as sitting up a small hill from Shibuya Station's west/south side — a cherry-blossom-pink, cute little eatery — and is a Thai Select-certified restaurant recognized for serving authentic Thai cuisine.
Alongside the namesake Gapao Rice, the menu highlights Larb Gai, a specialty dish of the chef's native Isan region.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as Gapao Rice are prepared with oyster sauce and fish sauce as base seasonings; oyster sauce and any soy sauce used typically contain wheat, and this has not been confirmed as gluten-free at this restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A restaurant listing states customers with allergies are welcome to contact the restaurant, implying staff will field allergy questions on request, though no formal allergen menu was found.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language Tabelog page and delivery listing (Uber Eats) exist, and staff reportedly welcome allergy questions, but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff on-site was found, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) is recommended.

places.name: トレボ (trevo (Trevo Coffee & Meals))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options were found in any source. The signature dishes are toast/bread-based Seasonal Plates and sandwiches (e.g. a truffle cheese "bikini sandwich"), implying standard wheat bread throughout with no rice-flour or GF alternative mentioned. Not suitable for celiac diners based on available information.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant, but offers some plant-based options: lattes can be made with oat, soy, or almond milk instead of dairy, and at least one source mentioned a vegan cheesecake and banana bread on the menu. The core Seasonal Plates and sandwiches (e.g., truffle cheese "bikini sandwich") contain dairy and are not vegan. No confirmation of hidden dashi/bonito use since this is a Western-style cafe menu, not Japanese cuisine.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (some sources report 11:00-17:00, LO 15:00-16:00); may close earlier once the day's seasonal plates sell out; no fixed weekly closed day, but business days vary — check Instagram (@trevo_3_trevo) before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Hongo, Bunkyo-ku (near Suidobashi Station / Tokyo Dome)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] trevo (トレボ) opened on May 8, 2022, tucked into a hard-to-find spot near Tokyo Dome, about a 1-5 minute walk from Suidobashi Station. It's a tiny hideaway cafe with just five two-person tables. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: trevo (トレボ) opened on May 8, 2022, tucked into a hard-to-find spot near Tokyo Dome, about a 1-5 minute walk from Suidobashi Station. It's a tiny hideaway cafe with just five two-person tables.
chef_bio.specialty: Monthly-rotating Seasonal Plate (シーズナルプレート) — a vegetable-packed toast/sandwich plate; the July-August corn butter toast version is locally famous and draws long lines before opening.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's seasonal plates, especially the summer corn butter toast, are popular enough that customers queue before the shop opens on weekdays.
Described as a 'hideaway cafe' (隠れ家カフェ) near Suidobashi in a hard-to-spot location by Tokyo Dome.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No information found on whether vegan drinks/sweets are prepared separately from dairy/egg-containing items in this small shared kitchen.

positives.text: Cafe lattes can be made with oat milk, soy milk, or almond milk instead of dairy.

cultural_comfort.note: Tiny 5-table cafe with no reservations and groups capped at 2 people per table; ordering and any allergy questions will likely need to be handled in Japanese.

places.name: いけす無門 新宿 (Ikesu Mumon Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The menu includes fried items (天ぷら盛り合わせ / tempura assortment, フライ / fried dishes such as 子持ち昆布のフライ) prepared in shared fryers, and sashimi is normally served with standard shoyu, which typically contains wheat. Celiac diners should not assume any dish is safe without asking staff directly and should be cautious of cross-contact from the shared tempura/fry station and wheat-based soy sauce.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan labeling, or plant-based dishes were found in any source. The restaurant's identity is built entirely around live seafood (activelyswimming squid, fish, spiny lobster, abalone) plus black-pork/beef shabu-shabu, and dashi/broths used in shabu-shabu and set courses are very likely fish- or bonito-based. Vegan diners have essentially no confirmed safe options here beyond possibly plain rice or a side vegetable, and would need to negotiate directly with staff with no guarantee of accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat: 11:30-14:30 (lunch), 17:00-23:00 last order 22:00 (dinner). Sun & holidays: 11:30-14:30 (lunch), 16:30-22:30 last order 21:30 (dinner).
places.neighborhood: Kabukicho, near Seibu-Shinjuku Station (西武新宿駅), Shinjuku
places.notes: いけす無門 新宿 is operated by Eiwa Group (永和グループ), a Tokyo restaurant company also known for its 月亭 shabu-shabu brand. The Shinjuku Kabukicho location is built around a giant in-house fish tank (いけす) at the center of the dining room, with counter seats overlooking it on the first floor · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Live seafood (活魚) specialty restaurant, part of Eiwa Group
chef_bio.background: いけす無門 新宿 is operated by Eiwa Group (永和グループ), a Tokyo restaurant company also known for its 月亭 shabu-shabu brand. The Shinjuku Kabukicho location is built around a giant in-house fish tank (いけす) at the center of the dining room, with counter seats overlooking it on the first floor and private tatami/horigotatsu rooms upstairs and in a waterfall-accented basement floor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's concept centers on 活魚 (live fish) cooked to order: fish, spiny lobster and abalone are sourced daily from regions across Japan and kept alive in the tank until a customer orders, so the seafood served is caught and prepared within minutes rather than pre-cut.
chef_bio.specialty: 泳ぎいか (live squid sashimi) — squid kept swimming in the tank until ordered, then hand-cut to order by the chef.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's official site notes that freshly caught live squid has a distinctive semi-translucent appearance rather than plain white, and that this freshness is what gives it its characteristic sweetness and texture — a point of pride the restaurant highlights as its signature dish.

cultural_comfort.note: All located sources (official site, Tabelog, Gnavi, Hotpepper, Ikyu, Hitosara) are Japanese-language; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found, though online reservation systems used are standard tourist-accessible platforms.

places.name: カフェ アンド マクロビ (Cafe and MACROBI)
places.gf_detail: No source found any explicit statement about gluten-free preparation, soy sauce wheat content, shared fryers, or cross-contamination protocols. Macrobiotic cooking is typically grain- and vegetable-centric (brown rice based), which may reduce wheat exposure compared to an average cafe, but this is not confirmed gluten-free practice — celiac diners must ask staff directly about ingredients (especially seasonings/sauces) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: This is a macrobiotic (plant-forward) cafe, not a dedicated vegan restaurant: secondary sources describe the menu as including some animal-derived items such as bonito flakes (katsuobushi), whitebait (shirasu), and eggs alongside vegetable-heavy daily lunch plates (shredded daikon, purple cabbage with nuts, curry-style vegetables, brown rice). Vegan diners should ask specifically about dashi/bonito and egg use in any given dish rather than assuming the macrobiotic label means vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30), closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Shibuya 2-chome (near Omotesando Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The cafe is supervised by macrobiotic cooking instructor Mie Nakamae, with dishes prepared by her apprentices/students. It has a set-back entrance from the street giving it a hidden, hideaway feel. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Mie Nakamae (menu supervisor / macrobiotic cooking instructor)
roles: macrobiotic cooking instructor / menu supervisor: Mie Nakamae
chef_bio.origin: Opened in December 2015 near Omotesando Station in Shibuya 2-chome
chef_bio.background: The cafe is supervised by macrobiotic cooking instructor Mie Nakamae, with dishes prepared by her apprentices/students. It has a set-back entrance from the street giving it a hidden, hideaway feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasis on seasonal, farm-fresh vegetables — produce is sourced from farmers who sell at the Aoyama Farmers Market — prepared in the macrobiotic style under an instructor's guidance.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-changing vegetable lunch plate (980 yen on weekdays) featuring shredded daikon radish, purple cabbage with greens and nuts, curry-flavored vegetables, leafy greens, and brown rice

anecdotes.text: The cafe also hosted workshops on topics such as cooking, makeup, English conversation, and soap making, giving it a community-gathering-space character beyond just serving food.
Vegetables used in the daily lunch plate come from farmers who exhibit at the Aoyama Farmers Market, and the dishes are prepared with care by the instructor's apprentices.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward daily lunch plates using produce sourced from Aoyama Farmers Market growers, prepared under a macrobiotic cooking instructor's supervision.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-language service could be confirmed from available sources, so expect a Japanese-only experience and consider calling ahead (03-6805-1946) to confirm current hours before visiting.

places.name: 網焼きジンギスカン 羊肉酒場 悟大 水道橋店 (Amiyaki Jingisukan Yōniku Sakaba Godai — Sui)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen labeling, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination protocol was found in any source. Jingisukan is typically served with a soy-sauce-based tare dipping sauce (soy sauce generally contains wheat in Japan) and meat is grilled on shared open-flame grills, so cross-contact risk is likely high. Celiac diners should not rely on this spot without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered entirely on grilled Hokkaido lamb and other meat/izakaya dishes; no vegan dishes, plant-based substitutes, or vegan menu were found in any source. Dashi/broth composition for side dishes was not documented, so hidden animal-derived stock (katsuo/bonito) cannot be ruled out.
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi (Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku)
places.notes: Opened in March as a new concept from the Daishō Group (the company behind the Shōya izakaya chain), this Suidōbashi shop sources its lamb directly from Niku no Yamamoto, a long-established butcher in Chitose, Hokkaido. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Hokkaido-lamb-focused jingisukan izakaya, part of the Daishō restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Hokkaido (lamb sourcing)
chef_bio.background: Opened in March as a new concept from the Daishō Group (the company behind the Shōya izakaya chain), this Suidōbashi shop sources its lamb directly from Niku no Yamamoto, a long-established butcher in Chitose, Hokkaido.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than the typical jingisukan hot-pot method, the shop net-grills the lamb over an open flame, which drains excess fat and reduces gaminess so the natural flavor of the meat comes through.
chef_bio.specialty: Tokujō katarosu (特上肩ロース) — a premium raw lamb shoulder-roast cut, yielding only about 700g per lamb, grilled tableside.

anecdotes.text: A food writer's visit described working through a full course of jingisukan cuts and drink pairings, highlighting the shoulder roast as the standout dish and the retro Shōwa-tavern atmosphere with sports on large TV screens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No specific cross-contamination information was published; meat is grilled directly on open-flame nets shared across orders, which is a plausible cross-contact pathway for allergen-sensitive diners though not explicitly documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Jingisukan is conventionally served with a soy-sauce-based tare dipping sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free tare or soy sauce alternative was confirmed for this location.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, Japanese-oriented chain izakaya near Suidōbashi Station with no confirmed English menu; phone or Japanese-language app reservation is the norm, though extensive cashless/credit payment options ease the visit.

places.name: 焼きあご塩らー麺 たかはし 新宿本店 (Yakiago Shio Ramen Takahashi, Shinjuku Main )
places.gf_detail: Noodles are wheat-based — a hand-kneaded style made from premium center-cut wheat flour co-developed with a Kyoto noodle maker — and the tare (sauce base) is built on Sekigahara tamari soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated allergen protocol was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is built on pork bone and chicken carcass stock plus fish-based dashi (grilled flying fish and dried sardines/niboshi); no vegetable- or plant-based broth option was found. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Sources vary somewhat: Mon-Thu roughly 11:00-23:00/23:15 (some sources say to 24:00); Fri roughly 11:00 to ~2:00-3:00 the next morning; Sat roughly 10:00 to ~2:00-3:00 the next morning; Sun/holidays roughly 10:00-23:00/23:15.

Wanting to bring that hometown flavor to Tokyo, she founded the company in 2011 and, with no prior experience in the ramen industry, opened the f · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 新潟県 (Niigata Prefecture)
chef_bio.background: Founder and president Takahashi Yuka grew up in Niigata eating a local specialty: ramen made with yakiago (grilled flying fish) dashi. Wanting to bring that hometown flavor to Tokyo, she founded the company in 2011 and, with no prior experience in the ramen industry, opened the first shop as a two-person, husband-and-wife operation in Myogadani in 2012. Business was slow at first, but after the shop relocated/expanded to a spot in Kabukicho, Shinjuku in February 2015, it became an instant hit — the store is widely credited with popularizing yakiago (grilled flying fish) ramen as a nationwide trend when the ingredient was previously little known. The brand celebrated its 10th anniversary in February 2025 and has since grown into a small multi-location company.
chef_bio.philosophy: Recreate and share a nostalgic, hometown Niigata flavor — yakiago-based ramen — with Tokyo diners, elevating a regional ingredient that was largely unknown in the capital before this shop popularized it.
chef_bio.specialty: 焼きあご塩らー麺 (Yakiago Shio Ramen) — a salt ramen built on a broth of pork bone and chicken carcass stock combined with grilled flying fish (yakiago), dried sardines (niboshi), and kelp, finished with a proprietary flavored oil that concentrates the grilled-fish aroma. The noodles are a hand-kneaded style made from premium center-cut wheat flour co-developed with a long-established Kyoto noodle maker, and the tare (sauce base) uses Sekigahara tamari soy sauce paired with a generous amount of dried sardines.

anecdotes.text: Takahashi started the company in 2011 with no ramen-industry background, opening a small husband-and-wife shop in Myogadani in 2012 that struggled with slow business before finding success after moving into Kabukicho in 2015.
The Kabukicho shop is credited with sparking the modern 'yakiago (grilled flying fish) ramen' boom in Tokyo, and the brand marked its 10th anniversary in February 2025.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare (sauce base) is made with Sekigahara tamari soy sauce combined with a large amount of dried sardines (niboshi) as the signature flavor component.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature broth is built on pork bone and chicken carcass stock combined with grilled flying fish (yakiago), dried sardines, and kelp — meat- and fish-based throughout, with no vegan broth alternative found.

cultural_comfort.note: Small Japanese-style counter ramen shop in Kabukicho; expect Japanese-language menu and ordering norms, though the shop is well known and has drawn international visitors and English-language press coverage.

places.name: うさぎ（神泉のらぁめん屋 うさぎ） (Usagi)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles are the only noodle option, and the shoyu (soy sauce) broths are the traditional Japanese kind, which is almost always wheat-brewed, so the base broth is not gluten-free. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy-safe process was mentioned in any source — not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dishes are built on whole-chicken broth with ground pork toppings; no plant-based broth, noodle, or menu option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Lunch Mon–Sat 11:30–15:00; Dinner Mon–Fri 18:00–22:00; closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Shinsen, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained at the well-regarded ramen shop Menya Yu (麺屋雄) in Ichinoe before striking out on his own and opening Usagi as a tiny counter shop in Shinsen in May 2007. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The owner trained at the well-regarded ramen shop Menya Yu (麺屋雄) in Ichinoe before striking out on his own and opening Usagi as a tiny counter shop in Shinsen in May 2007.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen built on a whole-chicken (marutori) broth, alongside a broth-less "shirunashi" tantan-men with adjustable Sichuan-pepper numbing and chili heat.

anecdotes.text: The owner is the younger brother of the acclaimed hip-hop producer Nujabes, and the shop plays his music throughout service as a quiet tribute.
Usagi opened in Shinsen in May 2007 after the owner trained at the respected ramen shop Menya Yu in Ichinoe; it has since spun off sister shops including Kousagi in Otsuka and Usatan in Itabashi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is built on a traditional shoyu (soy sauce) broth; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed from both wheat and soybeans, so the broth is not gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic hole-in-wall ramen counter that uses a Japanese ticket-vending machine to order, with no English menu found online, so some comfort with ticket-machine ordering helps.

places.name: 串だおれ 水道橋店 (Kushidaore Suidobashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a deep-fried skewer (kushikatsu) specialist: skewers are breaded in wheat flour and panko before frying, the signature dipping sauce is a Worcestershire/tonkatsu-style sauce that typically contains wheat, and the Kansai-style oden broth commonly relies on soy sauce and dashi that may contain wheat-based seasonings. No gluten-free menu, wheat-free batter option, or dedicated fryer was mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan labeling, or plant-based accommodation was found for this location. The menu centers on meat, seafood, and fried skewers plus Kansai-style oden (broth commonly made with katsuo/bonito dashi and fish-based items like chikuwa/hanpen). Some vegetable skewers (e.g., eggplant, mushroom, lotus root) may be orderable, but the batter, shared fryer, and sauce ingredients are unconfirmed, so vegan diners would need to ask staff directly and should not assume a fully plant-based preparation.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金・祝前日 15:00〜24:00、土・日・祝日 12:00〜23:00（年中無休）※一部情報源では平日15:00〜23:00または15:00〜24:30とする表記もあり、詳細は要確認
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町 (Suidobashi Station East Exit, ~1-3 min walk)
places.cuisine: 串揚げ・串カツ・関西風おでん・居酒屋
places.notes: Kushidaore is a Tokyo-based kushikatsu (deep-fried breaded skewer) izakaya chain with multiple branches across the city (Shimbashi honten, Suidobashi, Ichigaya, Gotanda, Shimokitazawa, Kanda, and others), built around bringing Osaka-style kushikatsu culture to Tokyo at very low p · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain operated by Kings Know Co., Ltd.)
chef_bio.background: Kushidaore is a Tokyo-based kushikatsu (deep-fried breaded skewer) izakaya chain with multiple branches across the city (Shimbashi honten, Suidobashi, Ichigaya, Gotanda, Shimokitazawa, Kanda, and others), built around bringing Osaka-style kushikatsu culture to Tokyo at very low prices.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated concept is "安くてウマイは当たり前" ("cheap and delicious is a given") - serving a wide variety of skewers and cheap drinks in a lively, no-frills izakaya setting; like traditional Osaka kushikatsu shops, the classic "no double-dipping the sauce" (ソースの二度づけ禁止) rule applies.
chef_bio.specialty: Kushikatsu (deep-fried breaded skewers of meat, seafood and vegetables) starting from around 50-80 yen per skewer, served alongside Kansai-style oden and yakitori.

anecdotes.text: 串だおれ operates under the concept "安くてウマイは当たり前" (cheap and delicious is a given), centering the menu on kushikatsu and Kansai-style oden with kushikatsu priced as low as ~50-80 yen per skewer.
The chain has multiple Tokyo locations including a Shimbashi main store, Suidobashi, Ichigaya, Gotanda, Shimokitazawa, Hatchobori and Kanda branches.

cultural_comfort.note: A boisterous, budget Japanese izakaya chain with menus and service oriented to a local/Japanese-speaking clientele; no evidence of English menus or foreign-visitor-specific accommodation was found.

places.name: 肉汁水餃子 餃包 新宿店 (Nikujiru Suigyoza Gyopao Shinjuku-ten (Juicy)
places.gf_detail: The core menu is a wheat-wrapper dumpling restaurant (boiled gyoza skins are wheat-based), and standard Japanese soy sauce used for dipping typically contains wheat unless otherwise stated — no gluten-free soy sauce or dedicated allergen protocol was confirmed. However, the restaurant has specifically promoted a gluten-free chicken chimaki (glutinous rice wrapped in bamboo leaf) as a menu item for gluten-sensitive guests. Because the kitchen is centered on wheat-dumpling production, cross-contact risk for a celiac-level allergy is likely high even when ordering the chimaki; no dedicated fryer or separate prep area was confirmed in available sources.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan-adapted dishes, or plant-based broth options were found in available sources. The signature dish and most of the menu center on black-pork-filled dumplings and meat-based broth. Treat as not vegan-friendly absent further confirmation.
places.hours_raw: Two conflicting sets reported: (a) Rakuten Gurunavi listing: daily 12:00-23:45, no closed days; (b) source citing official site: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30), Sat/Sun/Holidays 15:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Using set (b) as likely more current/authoritative (official-site sourced).
