In July 2020 the brand opened this Shinjuku br · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Roppongi, Tokyo (original flagship location)
chef_bio.background: Gyopao (餃包) began as a water-dumpling specialty izakaya at Roppongi Crossing, where it became the No. 1-ranked restaurant in Tokyo on TripAdvisor in its category and won TripAdvisor's Travelers' Choice award for two consecutive years. In July 2020 the brand opened this Shinjuku branch about a two-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's southeast/central-east exits, bringing the Roppongi store's signature dumplings to Shinjuku for the first time.
chef_bio.philosophy: Described by the restaurant itself as a fusion of Japanese izakaya drinking culture and Taiwanese Chinese cooking, built entirely around perfecting one dish rather than offering a broad menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Nikujiru Suigyoza (肉汁水餃子) — plump boiled dumplings packed with black-pork filling that burst with hot broth when bitten into, similar in effect to xiaolongbao but served izakaya-style. Notably, the menu has no yaki-gyoza (pan-fried dumplings) at all; the boiled, broth-filled dumpling is the sole signature.

anecdotes.text: The original Roppongi restaurant is ranked No.1 in Tokyo on TripAdvisor for water-dumpling specialty restaurants and also has the most Google reviews in Tokyo in its category.
The Shinjuku branch opened in July 2020, a one-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, opening up Roppongi's previously exclusive menu to Shinjuku for the first time.
A gluten-free chicken chimaki (glutinous-rice dumpling wrapped in bamboo leaf) has been promoted on the blog as a recommendation popular with female customers specifically because it is gluten-free.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free prep area, fryer, or allergen protocol is confirmed; the kitchen's primary output is wheat-wrapper boiled dumplings, so cross-contact risk for a strict celiac diet is likely significant even when ordering the gluten-free chimaki item.

FindMeGlutenFree's Shinjuku directory returns no listing or celiac review for this dumpling shop, despite covering the area — there is no independent celiac-grade evidence that anyone has eaten here safely.

Aggregator coverage states the shop labels 餃子ソーセージ, 鶏肉チマキ, 油淋鶏 (deep-fried chicken) and 麻婆豆腐 as gluten-free. Yurinchi and mapo tofu conventionally use wheat soy sauce and wheat-flour coating/deep frying, contradicting the shop's unconfirmed GF-soy-sauce and no-dedicated-fryer status — indicating the 'GF' label reflects recipe wheat, not celiac cross-contact safety.

All gluten-free promotion originates from a single source — the restaurant's own 'おすすめ グルテンフリーの鶏肉チマキ' marketing blog — with no protocol, ppm, or dedicated-prep disclosure; secondary blogs only restate this shop-authored claim.

The shop is explicitly a 肉汁水餃子専門店 (juicy boiled water-dumpling specialty restaurant) whose entire production line is wheat wrappers, boiled in shared water — a structurally high cross-contact environment for celiac.

positives.text: The restaurant has proactively promoted a gluten-free chicken chimaki on its own blog as a recommendation for gluten-sensitive guests, indicating at least some staff awareness of gluten-free demand.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant sits in a heavily touristed pocket of central Shinjuku two minutes from Shinjuku Station and has an English-language Tabelog reservation page, but the official website and in-store materials appear to be Japanese-only, so basic English support rather than a fully English-run experience should be expected.

places.name: 本格タイ料理バル プアン 渋谷店 (Puan Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination information was found in any source consulted. As a standard Thai izakaya, dishes likely rely on soy sauce and other wheat-containing condiments in curries, noodles (e.g., pad thai sauce), and stir-fries. Not verified safe for celiac diners — ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu, vegan labeling, or staff vegan-accommodation policy was found in any source consulted. As a general Thai bar/izakaya, some vegetable-forward dishes exist (e.g., stir-fried morning glory, vegetable dishes), but standard Thai seasonings used throughout the menu (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste) are not confirmed to be omitted or substituted on request. Vegan diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering; do not assume any dish is vegan by default.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂／渋谷 (Dogenzaka, Shibuya)
places.notes: Puan Shibuya is the third branch of a small Thai restaurant group (Spice Kitchen Co., Ltd.) that began in Sangenjaya, where the original shop was built inside a renovated 60-year-old traditional Japanese house. The Shibuya branch opened a one-minute walk from Shibuya Station's In · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Thai chef-run Thai bar/izakaya, third branch of a small Thai restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Thailand (dishes prepared by veteran Thai chefs)
chef_bio.background: Puan Shibuya is the third branch of a small Thai restaurant group (Spice Kitchen Co. , Ltd. ) that began in Sangenjaya, where the original shop was built inside a renovated 60-year-old traditional Japanese house. The Shibuya branch opened a one-minute walk from Shibuya Station's Inokashira Line ticket gate, following an earlier second branch near Gakugei-Daigaku Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "Puan" (プアン) means "friend" or "companion" in Thai, reflecting the restaurant's stated aim of pairing warm, friendly hospitality with authentic Thai cooking and Thai drinks (Thai beer, wine, and whisky highballs).
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-rolled fresh spring rolls, tom yum kung noodle soup, and a shrimp-and-avocado green curry are cited as signature dishes.

anecdotes.text: "プアン"とはタイ語で友人・仲間を意味し、本場のベテランタイ人シェフの作るタイ料理と、タイビール、タイワイン、タイウィスキーを使ったハイボールなど様々なタイのお酒が用意されている。3号店となる渋谷店は渋谷駅井の頭線改札徒歩1分にオープン。
三軒茶屋本店は、茶沢通り沿いにあり、築60年の古民家をリノベーションして設計したおしゃれな店内と凄腕タイ人シェフが作る絶品タイ料理が魅力。

cultural_comfort.note: Reservation/menu pages are Japanese-first (HotPepper, Gnavi, official site) though Tabelog and Uber Eats list an English store name, suggesting basic but not full English accessibility.

places.name: 欧風カレー ボンディ 神保町本店 (Bondy (Jimbocho Main Store))
places.gf_detail: The signature 欧風カレー is built on a classic French-style brown sauce: a roux made from butter and wheat flour, simmered for hours with beef/pork stock, wine, and vegetables/fruit. Every curry on the menu shares this wheat-flour-thickened base, and no gluten-free preparation or substitution is offered. Not suitable for celiac disease or strict gluten-free diets.
places.vegan_detail: The sauce base is rich with butter and dairy and is built on beef or pork stock; there is no vegan menu, no plant-based stock option, and no indication the kitchen offers a dairy/meat-free version of the curry. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians avoiding animal stock.
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda City (near Jimbocho Station / Suidob
places.notes: In his twenties Murata traveled to France, ostensibly to study art, and ended up spending several years there. He worked in a friend's restaurant, where he became captivated by the depth and technique of classic French brown sauce (sauce espagnole/demi-glace). Bringing that sauce · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Koichi Murata (founder, 1973); now run by second-generation owner Shinsuke Murata
chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1973 (Showa 48) in Kanda-Jimbocho by Koichi Murata, who had previously run an earlier curry shop called Indira in Takashimadaira, Tokyo.
chef_bio.background: In his twenties Murata traveled to France, ostensibly to study art, and ended up spending several years there. He worked in a friend's restaurant, where he became captivated by the depth and technique of classic French brown sauce (sauce espagnole/demi-glace). Bringing that sauce-making tradition back to Japan and folding it into curry produced what he named '欧風カレー' (European-style curry).
chef_bio.philosophy: Murata summed up his curry as 'the aroma of India, the taste of Europe' — rather than a straightforward Indian-style curry, the sauce is built like a French brown sauce: butter-and-flour roux simmered for hours with beef or pork stock, wine, and fruit and vegetables cooked down slowly in butter, giving a rounder, less fiery, dairy-rich flavor with sweetness balancing the spice.
chef_bio.specialty: European-style beef or pork curry, served with a whole boiled potato and a pat of butter presented separately on its own plate before the curry arrives.

anecdotes.text: Murata spent several formative years in France in his twenties; what began as an art-study trip turned into an apprenticeship of sorts in a friend's restaurant kitchen, where he fell for the depth of French brown sauce and later built Bondy's curry around it.

Bondy is credited as the Jimbocho curry shop that first served a whole boiled potato alongside the curry. In an interview, second-generation owner Shinsuke Murata explained his father served the potato first, echoing the 'first course' convention of the French meals he had trained on.

Before founding Bondy, Murata had run an earlier, lesser-known curry shop called 'Indira' in Takashimadaira, Tokyo — part of the winding path from Itabashi to Jimbocho that curry historians trace for the shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All curries share one wheat-flour-and-butter brown-sauce base (roux simmered with meat stock, wine, fruit and vegetables) — there is no separate gluten-free sauce or preparation area.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curry's richness comes from generous butter and dairy plus beef/pork stock cooked into the single shared sauce base; no vegan menu or dairy/meat-free substitution was found.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, no-reservation, old-school local institution — ordering is straightforward from a picture-friendly menu, but there is no particular English-language accommodation.

places.name: 舎鈴 新宿センタービル店 (Sharin — Shinjuku Center Building)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles are used, and the chain's own allergen sheet lists wheat (小麦) among ingredients used across its menu. Sharin's allergen disclosure explicitly states it cannot guarantee against secondary cross-contamination and that cooking equipment/tableware are not kept separate by dish. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe process is indicated anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: The tsukemen dipping sauce and ramen soup are built from a fish-and-meat double stock (seafood dashi layered with pork/chicken bone broth), and the tare is soy-sauce based. There is no vegetarian or vegan broth/noodle option indicated anywhere in the brand's materials — this is a straightforward meat-and-fish-based tsukemen specialist.
places.neighborhood: 都庁前 / 西新宿 (Tochomae / Nishi-Shinjuku)
places.notes: Sharin was created by Matsufuji Foods, the company behind the celebrated Rokurinsha tsukemen shop, with the idea of building a tsukemen you could realistically eat every single day rather than just as an occasional indulgence. This branch operates inside the basement food court o · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Sister brand of the famous Rokurinsha (六厘舎) tsukemen shop, launched by Matsufuji Foods Co.
chef_bio.background: Sharin was created by Matsufuji Foods, the company behind the celebrated Rokurinsha tsukemen shop, with the idea of building a tsukemen you could realistically eat every single day rather than just as an occasional indulgence. This branch operates inside the basement food court of the Shinjuku Center Building skyscraper.
chef_bio.philosophy: Where Rokurinsha's tsukemen leans thick and heavy, Sharin deliberately dials the richness back so the dipping sauce stays craveable without being fatiguing — the stated goal is noodles "you could eat every day and never get tired of. "
chef_bio.specialty: 創業つけめん (Sōgyō Tsukemen / "Founding Tsukemen") — a revival of a recipe Rokurinsha served for only one month back at its 2005 opening and later brought back as a legendary 'phantom' dish; it pairs a rich double soup of chicken-and-pork stock with seafood dashi, sharpened with a soy-based tare and a house spice blend.

anecdotes.text: The founding tsukemen recipe was served for just one month in 2005 at Rokurinsha's original Osaki shop and gained cult status as a 'phantom' menu item before Sharin brought it back; it has since sold over 200,000 bowls chain-wide.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sharin's chain-wide allergen notice states that individual sensitivity to allergens varies, that it cannot guarantee a customer won't react, that ingredients can pick up trace allergens during sourcing or in-kitchen prep, that secondary cross-contamination cannot be fully prevented, and that cooking tools and tableware are not dedicated per dish.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A chain-wide printable allergen list (covering items such as shrimp, crab, walnut, wheat, buckwheat, egg, dairy, peanut and more) is published by the brand for customers to check ingredients themselves; there is no branch-specific note about staff proactively managing allergy requests.

cultural_comfort.note: Self-service ticket-machine ordering (buy a ticket, sit, get called by number) is picture-driven and manageable without Japanese, but no English menu or English-speaking staff is confirmed.

places.name: haishop cafe 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店 (haishop cafe Shibuya Scramble Square)
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. The cafe has appeared in local blog roundups of gluten-free-friendly cafes in Shibuya (e.g., FruOats Magazine), suggesting some menu items may be wheat-free (smoothies, some deli items), but no official gluten-free menu, ingredient list, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination policy was found. Soy sauce/dashi wheat content and shared-equipment risk are unconfirmed — celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe explicitly markets itself as a sustainable/ethical cafe with roughly 80% of its menu vegan or vegetarian: soy-meat vegan burger, vegan tartare bagel sandwich, all-vegan soft-serve ice cream, seasonal fresh smoothies, and fair-trade coffee. It is not a 100%-vegan kitchen (the remaining ~20% of items are presumably non-vegan), so vegans should still confirm individual items; no specific mention of hidden dashi/katsuo(bonito) in vegan-labeled dishes was found, which is reassuring but not independently verified per dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sun (including holidays): 10:00–21:00
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷スクランブルスクエア7F, directly connected to Shibuya Stati
places.notes: haishop cafe is run by Innovation Design Inc., founded by Tarō Ishizeki. The company opened its first omiyage (souvenir) shop and restaurants in 2019, then pivoted fully toward sustainability in early 2020 after the founder saw how normalized sustainable living had become while v · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (brand also has a Yokohama location)
chef_bio.background: haishop cafe is run by Innovation Design Inc. , founded by Tarō Ishizeki. The company opened its first omiyage (souvenir) shop and restaurants in 2019, then pivoted fully toward sustainability in early 2020 after the founder saw how normalized sustainable living had become while visiting New York. Partnerships with local farmers working to reduce food loss, and a confectionery workshop that employs people with disabilities, shaped the brand's approach. The Shibuya Scramble Square branch opened on the 7th floor in September 2022, directly connected to Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is "solving social issues through food": ingredients are chosen with attention to environment, sourcing, and social impact, and the cafe welcomes vegan and vegetarian diners with a menu that is roughly 80% vegan. Customers who bring their own tumbler or container get 100 yen off drinks.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-meat vegan burger and a vegan tartare bagel sandwich, alongside all-vegan soft-serve ice cream and seasonal fresh smoothies.

anecdotes.text: haishop cafe's operator, Innovation Design, was reported as the first in Japan to receive a three-star rating from the Sustainable Restaurant Association (Japan) for its sustainability practices, around the time the Shibuya Scramble Square branch opened with its 80%-vegan menu.

The brand's shift to a sustainability-first business came after the founder's trip to New York, where sustainable living felt normalized; back in Japan at the start of 2020 the company decided to reorient the business around sustainability.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shop press release confirms only ~80% of the menu is vegan/vegetarian, meaning it is NOT a fully vegan kitchen and ~20% of items are non-vegan — individual vegan items must be verified.

HappyCow listing for Haishop Cafe is a general vegan/vegetarian directory entry; it surfaces no celiac rating, no gluten-free tag, and no cross-contamination review — i.e., no celiac-grade corroboration exists.
FindMeGlutenFree Shibuya City celiac/gluten-free listings do not include a dedicated, celiac-reviewed entry for haishop cafe, so the app's 'options' GF claim has no independent celiac support.
The cafe's GF reputation traces only to a non-celiac lifestyle blog roundup of 'healthy sweets' cafes, which does not assess gluten-free ingredient sourcing or cross-contamination.

positives.text: Roughly 80% of the food and drink menu is vegan, including a soy-meat vegan burger, a vegan tartare bagel sandwich, and all-vegan soft-serve ice cream.
Ingredients are selected with attention to environment, sourcing, and social impact as part of the cafe's core concept.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside Shibuya Scramble Square, a major shopping complex directly connected to Shibuya Station and heavily used by international visitors, so some English menu signage and tourist-accustomed staff are likely, though this wasn't independently confirmed.

places.name: カフェ・トロワバグ (Cafe Trois Bagues)
places.gf_detail: All signature dishes are built on wheat bread — gratin toast (bread with a flour-based white sauce and cheese), a mackerel-and-vegetable curry toast, and ogura butter toast. No gluten-free bread, alternative flour, or dedicated GF preparation was mentioned in any source, so this is not a safe option for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on dairy- and egg-rich toast dishes (butter, cheese and flour-based white sauce in the gratin toast) and includes fish (mackerel in the curry toast); no vegan-labeled dishes or substitutions were found in any source. Coffee itself is vegan, but the food menu is not.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 10:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); Sat & holidays 12:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku (1 min walk from Jimbocho Station
places.cuisine: 喫茶・カレートースト・昭和レトロ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1976 (Showa 51) in the basement of the Tomita Building near Jimbocho Station, in the heart of Tokyo's famous secondhand-bookstore district. The founding couple's daughter, Miwa Tokuko, is now the second-generation owner, carrying on the taste and atmosphere passed down  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Opened in 1976 (Showa 51) in the basement of the Tomita Building near Jimbocho Station, in the heart of Tokyo's famous secondhand-bookstore district. The founding couple's daughter, Miwa Tokuko, is now the second-generation owner, carrying on the taste and atmosphere passed down from her parents for nearly half a century.
chef_bio.philosophy: A family-run, old-fashioned kissaten (Showa-retro coffee shop) philosophy centered on preserving the original recipes and unhurried atmosphere of the founders rather than chasing trends.
chef_bio.specialty: Gratin toast (グラタントースト), the signature dish since the shop's founding; also known for a mackerel-and-vegetable curry toast sandwich and, more recently, an ogura (red bean) butter toast served with coffee.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, Trois Bagues, is French for "three rings" (trois = three, bague = ring) — a direct translation of the owner family's surname Miwa (三輪, literally "three wheels/rings"), and is also meant to evoke the connection between customer, staff, and coffee cup.
Second-generation owner Miwa Tokuko continues to run the cafe, having inherited it from her parents who opened it in 1976.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic old-school Japanese kissaten with a Japanese-only menu and no particular English support, though its long-running fame in the bookstore district means walk-in visitors are common.

places.name: グリーン イタリアン トルチャ (Green Italian TORCIA)
places.gf_detail: Vegewel's restaurant guide (which screens specifically for vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free-friendly restaurants) lists this location as having a gluten-free menu option. No source found confirming a dedicated gluten-free fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or a formal cross-contamination protocol — treat as 'ask before ordering' for strict celiac safety rather than a fully dedicated GF kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Runs a full, clearly labeled vegan menu alongside its standard Italian menu — soy-meat bolognese, a plant-based 'Zero Meat' burger, vegan pizza, vegan pasta, and vegan desserts (Mont Blanc, strawberry cake) — all free of meat, egg, dairy, and refined white sugar. A 3-course all-vegan lunch set has been reported by reviewers. No specific statement found about hidden bonito/katsuo dashi in the non-vegan menu items, so vegan diners should stick to items explicitly marked 'Vegan.'
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (Lunch 11:00-15:00 L.O.; Tea time 15:00-17:00; Dinner 17:00-22:00, L.O. 21:00). Follows Isetan Shinjuku Main Building's store hours/holidays.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome), Shinjuku
places.cuisine: Natural/modern Italian with an extensive par
places.notes: Opened inside Isetan Shinjuku's main building as a rebrand/renewal of the long-running CHAYA Macrobiotics restaurant at that location, Green Italian TORCIA pairs a conventional modern Italian menu with a parallel, clearly-labeled vegan menu built on macrobiotic principles (no mea · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 店長 大之木さん (Store Manager Onoki-san)
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan — CHAYA Macrobiotics brand
chef_bio.background: Opened inside Isetan Shinjuku's main building as a rebrand/renewal of the long-running CHAYA Macrobiotics restaurant at that location, Green Italian TORCIA pairs a conventional modern Italian menu with a parallel, clearly-labeled vegan menu built on macrobiotic principles (no meat, egg, dairy, or refined white sugar in the vegan dishes).
chef_bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic-inspired 'natural Italian' cooking meant to let vegans, vegetarians and omnivores sit at the same table together, each ordering what suits them, in a warm 'gathered around the torchlight' atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-meat bolognese with soy-milk vegan cheese and the Zero Meat plant-based burger are the most-mentioned vegan mains; vegan Mont Blanc and strawberry cake are the standout desserts.

anecdotes.text: Store manager Onoki-san: 'Through Italian food that anyone can feel at home with, we hope this can become a place where all kinds of people can gather around the same table' — explaining why the restaurant pairs its modern Italian menu with an extensive vegan menu.
The name TORCIA is Italian for 'torch,' chosen because the owners wanted to create the warm, welcoming feeling of people gathered around a hearth or torchlight to share a meal.

The restaurant is operated by CHAYA Macrobiotics, which renewed its long-running 'CHAYA Macrobi Isetan Shinjuku' concept into Green Italian TORCIA, keeping the macrobiotic, egg/dairy/meat/refined-white-sugar-free approach for its vegan dishes while adding a full modern Italian menu alongside it.

positives.text: Listed on Vegewel's restaurant guide as offering a gluten-free menu option, and reviewers note being glad a gluten-free menu is available.
All vegan dishes are made without meat, egg, dairy, or refined white sugar, and are clearly marked 'Vegan' on the menu, so vegan diners can identify safe dishes easily.
The restaurant is designed so vegan and non-vegan diners can eat together, each ordering from the appropriate part of the menu, which reviewers highlight as useful for mixed dietary groups.

cultural_comfort.note: Located on Isetan Shinjuku's dedicated restaurant floor with English-language coverage from multiple vegan travel blogs (itravelforveganfood, HappyCow), online reservation systems, and vegan items clearly marked in Japanese and English on the menu.

places.name: ホームズパスタ 渋谷本店 (HOME'S PASTA Shibuya Main Store)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free pasta or menu accommodation is mentioned anywhere in the restaurant's official site, press coverage, or blog reviews. The entire menu is built around wheat-flour spaghetti (fresh pasta), so celiac or gluten-avoidant diners should not plan to eat here without calling ahead, and even then options are unlikely.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu options are documented. The restaurant's signature and flagship dishes (e.g., "絶望のスパゲッティ") are meat-sauce and cream-based, and general menu descriptions found in sources reference meat sauce, bacon, and cream throughout — no plant-based soup base or dashi/katsuo-free option was identified.
places.neighborhood: 宇田川町 / 渋谷駅徒歩5分
places.cuisine: パスタ・スープパスタ専門店
places.notes: 1986年に渋谷・宇田川町で創業した、日本におけるスープスパゲッティのパイオニア店。創業シェフ本田氏の「本当においしいスープスパゲッティを食べてもらうためのこだわり」を守り続けてきた。2026年2月、老朽化に伴い一時休業（長年腕を振るった料理長も引退）、同年4月28日にリニューアルオープンした。現在は東京・大阪・名古屋・岡山など全国に展開するスープスパゲッティ専門チェーンの本店にあたる。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 渋谷区宇田川町
chef_bio.background: 1986年に渋谷・宇田川町で創業した、日本におけるスープスパゲッティのパイオニア店。 創業シェフ本田氏の「本当においしいスープスパゲッティを食べてもらうためのこだわり」を守り続けてきた。 2026年2月、老朽化に伴い一時休業（長年腕を振るった料理長も引退）、同年4月28日にリニューアルオープンした。 現在は東京・大阪・名古屋・岡山など全国に展開するスープスパゲッティ専門チェーンの本店にあたる。
chef_bio.philosophy: 本当に美味しいスープスパゲッティを届けることへのこだわりを創業以来守り続けている
chef_bio.specialty: 絶望のスパゲッティ（Zetsubo no Spaghetti / "Despair Spaghetti"）— じっくり煮込んだ自家製ミートソースに生クリームとオリーブを合わせ、ニンニクと唐辛子を効かせた濃厚ミートクリームスープパスタに、炒めたキノコとベーコンをトッピング

anecdotes.text: 看板メニュー「絶望のスパゲッティ」は1994年、新宿新店舗開業時の新メニュー開発から誕生。導入当初は全く売れず苦戦したが、メディア関係者が食べて絶賛したことから注文が増え、行列ができるほどの一番人気メニューに成長した。
2026年2月2日、老朽化のため渋谷本店は一時休業。長年キッチンを担ってきた料理長も引退し、同年4月28日にリニューアルオープンした。

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known, high-traffic local chain flagship near Shibuya Station with an all-Japanese menu and no confirmed English support — approachable with basic Japanese or pointing at the menu, but not set up for allergy conversations in English.

places.name: こってりらーめん せきやけ 水道橋店 (Kotteri Ramen Sekiyake Suidōbashi)
places.gf_detail: Uses standard wheat ramen noodles; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation was mentioned in any source reviewed. Both the shoyu and miso tare are conventional soy-sauce-based preparations typical of Naritake-style ramen, so wheat exposure should be assumed throughout the dish. Not appropriate for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broths are pork-bone (tonkotsu-style) and back-fat based, and the shoyu/miso tare are conventional soy-based preparations; no vegan options, plant-based broth, or substitutions were found in any menu or review source. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict somewhat: weekday hours reported as 11:00-20:00 (with some earlier listings showing 11:00-23:00), weekend/holiday hours reported as 11:00-15:00; one source also reports the shop closed Saturdays and Sundays entirely. A more recent report notes hours may extend into the evening depending on Tokyo Dome events, announced via the shop's official X (Twitter) account. Confirm current hours directly before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋駅・千代田区神田三崎町
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sekiyake began as an independent offshoot of Naritake, the popular Chiba back-fat ramen chain, opening its original shop in Shin-Koiwa (新小岩) before this Suidōbashi branch was added a short walk from the station's east exit. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Suidōbashi branch of こってりらーめん せきやけ, an independent spin-off of the well-known Chiba back-fat ramen shop こってりらーめん なりたけ (Naritake)
chef_bio.origin: Shin-Koiwa, Tokyo (original Sekiyake location); this Suidōbashi shop is the later branch
chef_bio.background: Sekiyake began as an independent offshoot of Naritake, the popular Chiba back-fat ramen chain, opening its original shop in Shin-Koiwa (新小岩) before this Suidōbashi branch was added a short walk from the station's east exit.
chef_bio.specialty: Kotteri (rich) miso or shoyu ramen loaded with pork back fat (背脂) over medium-thick wavy noodles

anecdotes.text: Diners can freely adjust the amount of pork back fat and the spice level in the miso ramen, and noodle portions up to 'medium' plus rice refills are free — a generous, customizable style associated with the Naritake ramen lineage.

cultural_comfort.note: Order and pay in cash at a ticket vending machine near the entrance; little to no English support, so knowing basic ramen ordering terms (broth type, noodle firmness, fat amount) helps.

places.name: 火鍋 小肥羊 新宿西口店 (Hinabe Xiaofeiyang Shinjuku Nishiguchi-ten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or process was identified. The build-your-own dipping sauces (soy-based ponzu, fermented tofu, miso-style sauce, oyster sauce) typically contain wheat, and the shared hot pot is used table-side alongside other diners' orders which may include wheat-based items (dumplings, noodles), so cross-contact is likely. Celiac diners should ask staff carefully about sauce ingredients and request a broth eaten without the standard condiments.
places.vegan_detail: Despite the health-oriented herbal branding, both the white (白湯) and spicy mala (麻辣) broths are built on chicken extract, beef extract, and bonito (fish) extract, so the hot pot broth itself is not vegan or vegetarian. Vegetables and tofu can be added to the pot, but they cook in the same animal- and fish-derived broth as the meat and seafood, and common condiments (oyster sauce, fermented tofu with possible non-vegan components) are offered as defaults.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku West Exit (新宿西口)
places.notes: Xiaofeiyang (小肥羊) is a well-known Chinese hot pot chain built around a 'medicinal herbal hot pot' concept; the Shinjuku West Exit branch is one of several Tokyo-area locations operating under this brand, about three minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station's west exit. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Chinese hot pot specialty chain restaurant
chef_bio.origin: Chain originating in China, operated in Japan under the Xiaofeiyang/Hinabe brand
chef_bio.background: Xiaofeiyang (小肥羊) is a well-known Chinese hot pot chain built around a 'medicinal herbal hot pot' concept; the Shinjuku West Exit branch is one of several Tokyo-area locations operating under this brand, about three minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station's west exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rooted in the idea of 医食同源 (food and medicine share the same origin): the broths are steeped with herbs and aromatics such as garlic, goji berry, ginseng, angelica root (当帰) and Sichuan pepper, with a dedicated in-house 'soup master' (鍋師) overseeing the recipe.
chef_bio.specialty: Herbal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) with a choice of two broths — a mild white broth (白湯スープ) or a spicy mala broth (麻辣スープ) — each simmered with dozens of Chinese medicinal ingredients, served with a build-your-own selection of dipping sauces (scallion sauce, sesame-adjacent condiments, fermented tofu, ponzu, chili oil, etc. ).

anecdotes.text: 唐辛子や花椒、当帰や良姜など、数十種類の漢方食材から抽出した秘伝のエキスがたっぷりと含まれている。
The white soup base is made with chicken extract, beef extract powder, and bonito extract in addition to the herbal blend, crafted under a dedicated 'pot master' recipe used chain-wide.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The white and spicy mala hot pot broths are made with chicken extract, beef extract, and bonito (fish) extract as their base, so the broth itself is not vegan or vegetarian even when only vegetables and tofu are added to it.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language Tabelog listing and broad payment support (major credit cards, PayPay, Alipay) suggest some accommodation for overseas visitors, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: タイ料理研究所 渋谷店 (Thai Food Laboratory (Thai Ryori Kenkyujo) —)
places.gf_detail: Many Thai dishes (steamed rice, rice noodles, most curries) are naturally wheat-free, but sauces and soy sauce used in the kitchen may contain wheat gluten, and no dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contact protocol was found in available sources. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm specific dishes and sauces with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Standard Thai menu includes vegetable and tofu-based dishes, but curries, stir-fries, and sauces commonly rely on fish sauce, oyster sauce, or shrimp paste (kapi) unless specifically requested without; no dedicated vegan menu or vegan certification was found. Ask staff to confirm ingredients per dish.
places.neighborhood: 桜丘町, Shibuya
places.notes: Operated by Shibuya-based restaurant group SSCY, with sister Thai Food Laboratory locations in Shimokitazawa and Fuchu; dishes are made by cooks trained in Thailand, including hotel kitchens. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Cooks trained in Thailand, including hotel kitchens
chef_bio.background: Operated by Shibuya-based restaurant group SSCY, with sister Thai Food Laboratory locations in Shimokitazawa and Fuchu; dishes are made by cooks trained in Thailand, including hotel kitchens.
chef_bio.philosophy: Stated focus on ingredients, cooking technique, atmosphere, and hospitality, aiming to introduce diners to both familiar and regional Thai cooking styles.
chef_bio.specialty: Course menus that range from beginner-friendly Thai classics to lesser-known Isaan (northeastern Thai) regional dishes

anecdotes.text: Offers three course menus, from dishes suited to Thai-food beginners to advanced regional dishes from northeastern Thailand (Isaan), prepared by cooks who trained in Thailand.
Positioned by its parent company as 'Thai Food Laboratory,' a Thai cuisine specialist with locations in Shibuya and Shimokitazawa.

cultural_comfort.note: Located a few minutes from Shibuya Station with an English-language company webpage, but an English menu or English-speaking staff at this specific branch was not confirmed — reasonably tourist-adjacent but not guaranteed easy for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 日本料理 一会 (Nihon Ryori Ichie)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. As a traditional dashi- and soy-sauce-based kaiseki restaurant, standard shoyu (which typically contains wheat) and tempura-style items are likely on the menu. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should call ahead (03-3868-2889) before booking to confirm ingredient handling, since this is a fully custom, one-party-per-day omakase where the kitchen could plausibly accommodate a request but has no documented gluten-free protocol.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented anywhere. The restaurant's identity is explicitly built around traditional dashi (multiple sources emphasize its dedication to broth), which in standard kaiseki preparation is katsuobushi (bonito)-based, making it very unlikely to be vegan-friendly without extensive advance customization. No evidence of a kombu-only or vegan dashi alternative was found.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-22:00, nightly, completely reservation-only (one party seated per day)
places.neighborhood: Hongo, Bunkyo-ku (near Suidobashi Station)
places.notes: This kaiseki restaurant relocated to Hongo (near Suidobashi) from its original location in Hiroo, Shibuya-ku. As "Hiroo Ichie," the predecessor establishment held a Michelin star for eight consecutive years before the move. It now operates as a completely reservation-only restaur · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Relocated from Hiroo, Shibuya-ku, where it was known as Hiroo Ichie.
chef_bio.background: This kaiseki restaurant relocated to Hongo (near Suidobashi) from its original location in Hiroo, Shibuya-ku. As "Hiroo Ichie," the predecessor establishment held a Michelin star for eight consecutive years before the move. It now operates as a completely reservation-only restaurant that serves only one party per day.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner personally prepares every dish, with what multiple sources describe as an uncompromising focus on dashi (broth) as the foundation of the cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase kaiseki built around house-made dashi, with seasonal set menus that have featured suppon (soft-shell turtle) dishes and bincho-charcoal grilled items.

anecdotes.text: A repeat diner wrote: "ここの出汁が、すごく好きでした。10年振りの再訪ですが、この点は、変わりません" ("I've always loved the dashi here — even after 10 years away, that hasn't changed").

cultural_comfort.note: A completely reservation-only, one-group-per-night restaurant reached by phone (03-3868-2889); no indication of English menus or English-speaking service, so non-Japanese-speaking guests should expect to arrange details via a Japanese speaker or hotel concierge.

places.name: Salad Cafe 京王百貨店新宿店 (Salad Cafe Keio Department Store Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: This is a weighed-salad deli counter (量り売り) in the basement food hall of Keio Department Store, not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. Dressings and prepared sides commonly use soy sauce or soy-sauce-based seasonings, which contain wheat, and salads are displayed and scooped in an open counter format where cross-contact between trays is likely. No dedicated gluten-free process, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free labeling was confirmed in available sources — celiac diners should ask staff to check ingredient labels per item before choosing.
places.vegan_detail: The counter offers 15-18 rotating types of vegetable-forward salads and deli sides sold by weight, plus sandwiches, bento, and a Shinjuku-exclusive appetizer platter, so some individual salads may be vegetable-only. However, the shop is not vegan-focused: many salads and sides commonly include egg, mayonnaise, ham, or other animal ingredients (the parent company, Kenko Mayonnaise, is a mayonnaise/dressing maker), and dashi or bonito-based seasonings are common in Japanese-style salads. Ingredient composition varies daily; vegan diners should check the posted ingredient/allergy labels for each item or ask staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 10:00-20:30, Sun and public holidays 10:00-20:00
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku, Keio Department Store / Shinjuku Station Wes
places.notes: Salad Cafe is a weighed-salad ('ryōrigai') deli shop brand operated by Salad Cafe Inc., a group company of the long-established Japanese seasoning and mayonnaise maker Kenko Mayonnaise Co., Ltd. The brand runs salad and deli counters inside department stores and shopping malls ac · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Salad Cafe Inc. (subsidiary of Kenko Mayonnaise Co., Ltd.)
chef_bio.origin: Kenko Mayonnaise Co., Ltd. group (Japan)
chef_bio.background: Salad Cafe is a weighed-salad ('ryōrigai') deli shop brand operated by Salad Cafe Inc. , a group company of the long-established Japanese seasoning and mayonnaise maker Kenko Mayonnaise Co. , Ltd. The brand runs salad and deli counters inside department stores and shopping malls across the Kanto and Kansai regions. This Shinjuku location opened on November 22, 2022 in the basement food hall of Keio Department Store's Shinjuku store, near Morozoff, opening the same day as a second new shop, Shinjuku Takano's Keio Shinjuku Salad shop, in the same building.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's stated concept is 'サラダのある毎日で、お客様の笑顔と健康をサポートします' — supporting customers' smiles and health through everyday salads — with an emphasis on vegetable-forward, colorful, health-oriented prepared foods.
chef_bio.specialty: Weighed, made-to-order salads sold by weight (15-18 rotating varieties including deli-style sides), with a Shinjuku-store-exclusive appetizer platter and a popular Japanese-style onion-slaw potato salad (和風オニスラポテトサラダ)

anecdotes.text: Salad Cafe 京王百貨店新宿店 opened November 22, 2022, launching alongside a second new store, Shinjuku Takano's Keio Shinjuku Salad shop, in the same basement food hall of Keio Department Store Shinjuku.
The Shinjuku store's line-up centers on 15-18 kinds of weighed salads and deli items, plus bento, sandwiches, and a platter exclusive to this location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese-style salads and deli sides at Salad Cafe commonly use soy-sauce-based dressings and seasonings, which typically contain wheat; no wheat-free dressing option was confirmed for this location.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Salad Cafe's parent operations state a commitment to providing accurate allergy and nutritional information for its products so customers can make informed choices.

positives.text: The shop offers a wide daily rotation of 15-18 vegetable-based salads and deli items sold by weight, so diners can visually select and portion exactly what they want rather than ordering a fixed set dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A self-service, weigh-and-pay department-store deli counter where you point to what you want, so it's easy to navigate with little Japanese, though ingredient details are mainly in Japanese.

places.name: 台南担仔麺 新宿本店 (Tainan Tami Shinjuku Honten (Tainan Danzaime)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is wheat-based Taiwanese ramen-style noodles (担仔麺) served in a chicken-and-pork-bone broth; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation was mentioned in any source found. Soy sauce use in the broth/toppings is likely (standard for this dish) but not explicitly confirmed either way.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is meat/seafood-centric Taiwanese small plates; the signature 担仔麺 is built on chicken-and-pork-bone broth topped with a proprietary meat miso (rousō/minced pork). No vegan, vegetarian, or plant-based options, and no mention of dashi/bonito transparency, were found in any source.
places.cuisine: 台湾料理 / 台湾ラーメン・担仔麺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 台南担仔麺 新宿本店 is a long-running Taiwanese restaurant in Kabukicho (multiple sources describe it as roughly 30 years in business), styled after a Tainan night-market food stall, marked by a red lantern sign. It is one of two Shinjuku-area locations of the same small operation. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tainan, Taiwan (dish origin); restaurant based in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: 台南担仔麺 新宿本店 is a long-running Taiwanese restaurant in Kabukicho (multiple sources describe it as roughly 30 years in business), styled after a Tainan night-market food stall, marked by a red lantern sign. It is one of two Shinjuku-area locations of the same small operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let guests casually enjoy the atmosphere of a Taiwanese night-market food stall, offering over 90 kinds of Taiwanese small plates alongside the signature noodle bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: 担仔麺 (tanshimen/tami) — a small bowl of noodles in a light chicken-and-pork-bone broth topped with the restaurant's original slow-cooked meat miso (rousō)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant recreates the feel of a Tainan night-market food stall with red-lantern signage in Kabukicho, and serves its signature 担仔麺 in a small bowl of chicken-and-pork-bone broth with a proprietary meat miso, alongside more than 90 varieties of Taiwanese small plates.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservation and info channels found (Hotpepper, Gnavi, LINE, Tabelog EN listing) are Japanese-first with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: 大衆すし酒場 ろんじ (Taishu Sushi Sakaba Ronji)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitutions, or dedicated-fryer information appears in any source. The menu centers on tempura (wheat-flour batter) and nigiri sushi, which in standard izakaya practice use regular wheat-containing shoyu; nothing found confirms tamari, a gluten-free soy sauce option, or a separate fryer for tempura versus other fried items. Celiac diners should treat this as not gluten-free-safe absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu options, dashi/broth substitutions, or plant-based accommodations are mentioned anywhere. This is a fish- and meat-centric sushi/tempura izakaya (sushi, wagyu-butter eel, tempura) with no indication of vegan awareness.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 / 渋谷センター街・公園通り
places.cuisine: 寿司・天ぷら・居酒屋
places.notes: 大衆すし酒場 ろんじ opened February 26, 2026 in an alley behind Dogenzaka, near Shibuya Mark City. It was co-produced by Tokyo restaurant operator For You Group (株式会社フォーユー, 35 stores nationwide) and Yokohama-based Dynac, marking For You's return to izakaya operations after the company piv · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Chain izakaya operated by For You Group in partnership with Dynac
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (For You Group, headquartered in Toshima-ku), produced jointly with Yokohama izakaya operator Dynac
chef_bio.background: 大衆すし酒場 ろんじ opened February 26, 2026 in an alley behind Dogenzaka, near Shibuya Mark City. It was co-produced by Tokyo restaurant operator For You Group (株式会社フォーユー, 35 stores nationwide) and Yokohama-based Dynac, marking For You's return to izakaya operations after the company pivoted toward soba (十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷) and tonkatsu (とんかつ わか葉) concepts during the pandemic.
chef_bio.philosophy: Catchphrase '味は本格、価格は大衆' — authentic quality at mass-market prices — built around an escalating drink-price system (390/290/190 yen for 1st/2nd/3rd-plus drinks) aimed at customers in their 30s-50s.
chef_bio.specialty: Nigiri sushi and tempura from 99 yen a piece (squid, octopus from 99 yen; salmon 165 yen; scallop 209 yen), with a wagyu-butter eel nigiri (286 yen) as a signature higher-end item.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'ろんじ' is sushi-kitchen insider slang, borrowed as a nod to the trade — inspired directly by Dynac's popular 'Taishu Sushi Sakaba Menoji' in Yokohama's Noge district.
For You Group had shifted away from izakaya operations after COVID-19 toward soba and tonkatsu restaurants; teaming up with Dynac on Ronji represents its renewed push back into the izakaya business.

cultural_comfort.note: No source mentions an English menu or foreign-language support; it reads as a standard Japanese-language, locals-oriented budget izakaya chain.

places.name: ビストロ アリゴ (Bistro Aligot)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated prep found in any source. It is a classic French bistro with unlimited baguette refills as a stated draw, and sauces in this style are typically roux/flour-thickened. The open kitchen and shared cookware make cross-contact very likely. Celiac diners should not treat this as a safe option without calling ahead and getting explicit confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat- and cheese-centered classical French bistro (game dishes/ジビエ, braised meatloaf, roast chicken thigh, and the namesake aligot — mashed potato whipped with cheese). No vegan menu, vegan-marked items, or plant-based substitution options turned up in any source, and the cuisine style (butter, cream, cheese, animal stocks) makes it a poor fit for vegan diners without advance arrangement.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町
places.notes: Bistro Aligot occupies a roughly 65-year-old traditional Japanese house on a back alley in the used-bookstore district of Jimbocho — the building formerly housed an ice shop (氷屋), and its old sign was kept as part of the renovation. It opened as a hearty ('骨太') bistro concept run · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: chef: 村田シェフ (Murata, head chef)
operating company: 夢屋 (Yumeya), representative director 小林研
chef_bio.background: Bistro Aligot occupies a roughly 65-year-old traditional Japanese house on a back alley in the used-bookstore district of Jimbocho — the building formerly housed an ice shop (氷屋), and its old sign was kept as part of the renovation. It opened as a hearty ('骨太') bistro concept run by the Yumeya restaurant group, with chef Murata bringing classical French bistro training to the kitchen.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids frozen ingredients and pre-made products, building each dish by hand ('手仕事') in the classical bistro style.
chef_bio.specialty: Aligot — potato mashed and whipped with cheese into a stretchy, elastic texture, a specialty of France's Auvergne region — served alongside unlimited baguette refills.

anecdotes.text: 築65年の日本家屋を、元氷屋の看板を残したまま改装し「骨太ビストロアリゴ」として再生してオープンした。
冷凍商材を使わず、既製品を使わず、手仕事の温かみのある料理を一つ一つ作り上げることにこだわっている。
「昭和」の古民家で骨太フレンチを愉しめる神保町の名店として紹介されている。

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood-feel bistro in an old Japanese house with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff, so confident but ordinary Japanese-language comfort is expected to navigate the menu smoothly.

places.name: つけ麺 五ノ神製作所 (Tsukemen Gonokami Seisakusho)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen specialty shop — the noodles are standard wheat-flour noodles and no gluten-free noodle substitute was found in any source. The soy-sauce-based tare/broth almost certainly contains wheat as well. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation area, or allergen accommodation was mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The tsukemen broth is built on a pork-bone base concentrated with simmered shrimp, and the shop specializes exclusively in shrimp/pork-based dipping noodles. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, substitutions, or plant-based broth options were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 daily (some sources list 11:00-21:30 with last order 21:00); reported as no fixed closed day (無休)
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 / 渋谷区千駄ケ谷 (Shinjuku Sanchome / Sendagaya, Shibuya-ku)
places.notes: Operated by 株式会社五ノ神製作所 (Gonokami Seisakusho Co., Ltd.), a small Tokyo ramen-group company that also runs the original Itsuki shop plus related meat and seafood-focused offshoots, and has expanded the shrimp-tsukemen concept to additional domestic outlets and overseas locations in · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Shrimp-tsukemen specialty shop, one of several brands run by Gonokami Seisakusho Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: Grew out of らーめん いつ樹 (Ramen Itsuki), a ramen shop originally based in Ome City in western Tokyo; the shrimp-tsukemen concept was spun off into its own specialty brand, with this Shinjuku Sanchome shop as a flagship location of that brand.
chef_bio.background: Operated by 株式会社五ノ神製作所 (Gonokami Seisakusho Co. , Ltd. ), a small Tokyo ramen-group company that also runs the original Itsuki shop plus related meat and seafood-focused offshoots, and has expanded the shrimp-tsukemen concept to additional domestic outlets and overseas locations in Taiwan.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach is to take ingredients and byproducts that would otherwise be discarded and turn them into ramen/tsukemen, pushing to extract maximum shrimp flavor into the soup base.
chef_bio.specialty: 海老つけ麺 (Ebi Tsukemen / Shrimp Dipping Noodles) — a rich, pale-orange dipping broth built on a pork-bone base concentrated with luxuriously simmered sweet shrimp and a proprietary shrimp powder, served with thick wheat noodles; menu variations include Ebi Miso Tsukemen and Ebi Tomato Tsukemen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for its tagline inviting customers to "drink shrimp" (エビを飲む), reflecting how concentrated the shrimp flavor is in the dipping broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular, often-queued tsukemen counter shop with an English-language Tabelog listing (suggesting some foreign-visitor traffic), but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — basic Japanese or pointing at photos/menu items is advisable.

places.name: バンコクスパイス 新宿店 (Bangkok Spice Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or dedicated-fryer information was found on any source. As a Thai restaurant, sauces (soy sauce, oyster sauce) and some prepared items may contain wheat; safety for celiac diners cannot be confirmed and should be checked directly with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based labeling was found. Traditional Thai dishes here typically use fish sauce and shrimp paste (kapi) by default, so most items are not vegan as served without modification; vegans should ask staff which dishes can be adapted.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (behind Yodobashi Camera, near Shinjuku Stati
places.notes: Part of the Thai Garden / Bangkok Spice group of Thai restaurants, the Shinjuku branch is an officially certified "Thai Select" restaurant, a designation awarded by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce to establishments recognized for serving authentic Thai cuisine. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Part of the Thai Garden / Bangkok Spice group of Thai restaurants, the Shinjuku branch is an officially certified "Thai Select" restaurant, a designation awarded by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce to establishments recognized for serving authentic Thai cuisine.
chef_bio.philosophy: Aims to serve the true taste of Thailand -- built on umami depth rather than spice and sourness alone -- inside a stylish, resort-like hideaway dining room.
chef_bio.specialty: An extensive menu of more than 100 Thai dishes including desserts, spanning classics like tom yum goong alongside specialties such as crab claws in tamarind sauce (kung thot sauce makham) and shrimp-paste fried rice (khao khluk kapi).

anecdotes.text: Bangkok Spice Shinjuku is listed as an officially certified Thai Select restaurant by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce, a program recognizing restaurants abroad for authentic Thai cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: Booking and site content are in Japanese via mainstream reservation platforms (Hotpepper, Gnavi), with no confirmed English menu, so a bit of Japanese or gesture-based ordering will help.

places.name: 自然派ワインとフランス郷土料理 シュシュ (Chouchou (Shizenha Wine to France Kyodo Ryor)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, options, or allergy protocol were found in any source. The concept centers on roughly 15 kinds of house-baked wheat bread served unlimited alongside the meal, plus classic French sauces/roux that typically use wheat flour, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, advance confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegetarian options, or plant-based accommodation were found in any source. The menu is centered on game meats (Ezo venison, duck, wild boar), daily fresh seafood (including a bouillabaisse), and French sauces that commonly contain animal-derived stock/butter; no evidence the kitchen offers a vegan tasting or substitution track.
places.hours_raw: Lunch: weekdays 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), no lunch service Saturdays/Sundays/holidays. Dinner: 17:30-24:00 (L.O. 23:00). Closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: 神泉 (Shinsen), Shibuya — Sakuragaoka-cho
places.cuisine: French regional cuisine / bistro, natural wi
places.notes: Owner-chef with roughly two decades of experience in French cuisine at well-regarded restaurants in Tokyo before opening this bistro in the Shinsen/Shibuya area. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanagawa Prefecture (per one profile snippet)
chef_bio.background: Owner-chef with roughly two decades of experience in French cuisine at well-regarded restaurants in Tokyo before opening this bistro in the Shinsen/Shibuya area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pairs French regional cooking with natural ('vin nature') wines chosen from producers the chef knows personally, aiming to make natural wine approachable even for guests with little wine knowledge; sources fish daily from Toyosu market and bakes roughly 15 kinds of bread in-house each morning to accompany meals.
chef_bio.specialty: Roasted Ezo venison (エゾ鹿のロースト); the bouillabaisse (crispy-skinned sea bream, prawn, monkfish) is also cited as a signature dish.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Chouchou' comes from the French word for 'favorite/darling,' reflecting the chef's aim to make the restaurant a beloved neighborhood spot for natural wine lovers.

The restaurant is built around daily fresh fish from Toyosu market, game dishes (venison, duck, wild boar), and a natural wine list (cited variously as roughly 800-1000+ bottles across sources) that the chef helps guests navigate.

cultural_comfort.note: A reservation-oriented natural-wine bistro aimed mainly at domestic diners; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese (or a translation app) will help.

places.name: ワインビストロ トミーグリル (Wine Bistro Tommy Grill)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-avoidance accommodation was mentioned in any source found. The signature hamburg steak and other Western-style dishes typically rely on breadcrumbs and flour-thickened sauces, and no information confirms soy sauce, dashi, or sauce ingredients as wheat-free. Treat as not celiac-safe absent direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were mentioned in any source. The restaurant's identity centers on wine pairings with grape-fed beef and seasonal fish (旬魚); there is no indication of dashi/katsuo transparency or vegan substitutions. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct confirmation.
places.hours_raw: Lunch (Mon–Fri) 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner (Mon–Sat) 18:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); Closed Sundays and public holidays

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tommy Grill began roughly 70 years ago as a Western-style (yoshoku) restaurant with roots near Kanda. Current owner Masaki Nakatomi is the third generation to run it, having inherited the restaurant through his wife's family; he earned a sommelier qualification before taking over · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Tommy Grill began roughly 70 years ago as a Western-style (yoshoku) restaurant with roots near Kanda. Current owner Masaki Nakatomi is the third generation to run it, having inherited the restaurant through his wife's family; he earned a sommelier qualification before taking over and relaunched the shop as a wine-focused bistro, marking his 10th year at the helm as of 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner-sommelier Nakatomi personally selects roughly 120 bottles of wine, alongside a broad by-the-glass list, to pair with seasonal fish and beef dishes in a casual bistro setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hamburg steak (ハンバーグ) made with Australian 'budougyu' (grape-fed beef) — cattle raised on feed blended with grape pomace left over from winemaking, prized for its balance of lean meat and fat.

anecdotes.text: About 70 years after the restaurant's founding as a Kanda-area yoshoku shop, third-generation owner Masaki Nakatomi — who married into the family and became a certified sommelier before taking over — relaunched it as a wine bistro, reaching his 10th year running the shop in 2024.
The signature hamburg steak uses Australian grape-fed beef ('budougyu'), and the owner curates about 120 bottles of wine plus a wide glass-pour list to go with seasonal fish and meat dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking-staff info was found; this reads as a neighborhood bistro best visited with at least basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: めんめん・かめぞう (Menmen Kamezou)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles are used, with free kaedama (wheat noodle refills) as a signature feature of the shop; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented in any source. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional Hakata-style tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen shop; the broth is pork-bone based (sources describe it as a lighter 魚介豚骨 fish-and-pork-bone style), so it is not vegan and no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-19:00 (one source states 11:00-19:00), closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: 神保町 / 西神田 (Jinbocho / Nishikanda, Chiyoda-ku) — near Jinboch
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen counter shop in the Nishikanda/Jinbocho area, in operation since around 2007. It has long operated under a yellow side-panel sign advertising 'new Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen' (新博多とんこつラーメン), located just off Hakusan-dori next to a taiyaki shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen counter shop in the Nishikanda/Jinbocho area, in operation since around 2007. It has long operated under a yellow side-panel sign advertising 'new Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen' (新博多とんこつラーメン), located just off Hakusan-dori next to a taiyaki shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: A no-frills, old-fashioned neighborhood ramen counter — Showa-retro in feel — prized by regulars for its unpretentious, friendly atmosphere as much as for the food.
chef_bio.specialty: Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen with thick-cut chashu, ajitsuke tamago (flavored soft-boiled egg), and a free kaedama (noodle refill) included with the bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run single-handedly by an older ('初老の') male owner, with a Showa-retro interior and a friendly, convivial atmosphere shared with regular customers.
The shop remade its signage after being featured on the TV program 'Adobake Tengoku' (出没！アド街ック天国).
The ramen has a lighter fish-and-pork-bone (魚介豚骨) character rather than a heavy classic Hakata tonkotsu, with fine, springy noodles and free kaedama.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic old-school, Japanese-only counter ramen shop run solo by an older owner-chef, with no English signage or menu found — a purely local, no-frills experience.

places.name: 農 おおはし (Nou Ohashi (No Ohashi))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was documented in any source. The menu includes tempura (wheat-flour batter, likely a shared fryer) and standard Japanese seasonings, which typically contain wheat-based shoyu and dashi. This is a vegetable-forward izakaya, not a gluten-free-oriented restaurant — celiac diners should call ahead and ask about specific ingredients and fryer use rather than assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is vegetable-forward (seasonal vegetable salads, vegetable tempura, vegetable stir-fries) but is not a vegan restaurant — it also serves meat (wild boar, farmer's meat-and-vegetable stir-fry) and seafood (garlic shrimp) dishes, and traditional dashi/sauces used in Japanese cooking commonly contain bonito or other animal-derived stock. No dedicated vegan menu, marking, or policy was found in any source — vegans should ask staff directly which dishes and sauces are plant-based.
places.neighborhood: Kabukicho, Shinjuku

The restaurant describes itself as a bridge between farmers and diners: it runs its own agriculture and livestock operation, sources seasonal vegetables d · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nakatsugawa, Gifu Prefecture (Ohashi Farm)
chef_bio.background: 農おおはし is a producer-run izakaya connected to Ohashi Farm (大橋農園), an actual working farm in Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture. The restaurant describes itself as a bridge between farmers and diners: it runs its own agriculture and livestock operation, sources seasonal vegetables directly from the family farm, and frames its mission around raising the standing of producers and giving diners traceability they can trust.
chef_bio.philosophy: Build a direct line from a real farm to the plate — showcasing seasonal, farm-grown vegetables in simple preparations (salads, tempura) alongside creative cooked dishes, paired with a curated list of organic wine, sake, and shochu.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable tempura (旬の野菜天ぷら) and vegetable-forward dishes such as colorful seasonal salads and a farmer's meat-and-vegetable stir-fry

anecdotes.text: The Ohashi family's farming story goes back to wartime hardship, when the current farm's founder's grandmother, Masako Ohashi, began growing a fiery heirloom chili called ajime pepper (あじめこしょう) — a variety cultivated in the same corner of Gifu for roughly 400 years. She called it a kind of 'magic powder,' saying even a pinch could turn a poor wartime meal into something delicious; the farm still grows it today alongside corn, broccoli, garlic, and sweet potatoes that supply the Tokyo restaurant.

positives.text: The restaurant's own description emphasizes fresh, seasonal vegetables shipped directly from its affiliated Ohashi Farm in Gifu, forming the base of colorful salads, vegetable tempura, and other vegetable-centered dishes, which gives naturally plant-forward options on the menu even though it is not a dedicated vegan restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu, English-language site, or English-speaking-staff confirmation was found in any source, so go in expecting a Japanese-only experience.

places.name: イタメシ食堂 アペロ 渋谷道玄坂店 (Itameshi Shokudo Apero Shibuya Dogenzaka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodations were found. Signature dishes include a doria (bechamel/rice/pasta bake) and breaded, fried whole lobster tail (frit), both wheat-based preparations; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol was mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. The menu centers on cured meats (14-month aged Slovenian prosciutto), lobster, and cream/cheese-based dishes like Milan-style doria, none of which are plant-based, and no mention of dashi/katsuo substitution or plant-based alternatives was found.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-15:00 (lunch) & 17:00-23:00 (dinner); Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30-23:00, per current listing aggregators. Note: the July 2025 opening press release described different, later dinner-only bar hours (Mon-Sat 17:00-29:00 last order 28:00, Sun 17:00-23:00 last order 22:00) with no lunch service, suggesting hours have since changed to add lunch and/or sources conflict.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dōgenzaka), Shibuya
places.notes: Opened July 10, 2025 in Shibuya's Dōgenzaka nightlife district by restaurant company Eatwing Inc., pairing sommelier Shinsuke Yamada with chef Akito Hashimoto (ex-Ginza L'écrin, ex-'Ore no Italian'). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Shinsuke Yamada — proprietor, sommelier-certified at age 23, formerly executive vice president at the 'Ore no' restaurant group
Akito Hashimoto — chef, previously worked at Ginza L'écrin and led the kitchen at 'Ore no Italian'
chef_bio.background: Opened July 10, 2025 in Shibuya's Dōgenzaka nightlife district by restaurant company Eatwing Inc. , pairing sommelier Shinsuke Yamada with chef Akito Hashimoto (ex-Ginza L'écrin, ex-'Ore no Italian').
chef_bio.philosophy: Reinterprets nostalgic 'itameshi' (Japanese-Italian fusion) comfort-food dishes from the late-1980s to 1990s Itameshi boom with a modern, 'neo-itameshi' twist, served alongside craft sours, sangria, and natural wines.
chef_bio.specialty: Neo-Milan Doria (a reinvented take on classic Milan-style doria) and whole lobster tail fritto; also 14-month aged Slovenian prosciutto with melon.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Apero' is drawn from the Italian aperitivo culture — the owners wanted guests to casually enjoy a drink amid the energetic nightlife atmosphere of Shibuya's Dōgenzaka.
The restaurant was originally positioned as a late-night Italian bar open until 5am (last order 4am) on weeknights, reflecting its 'enjoy until late night' concept at launch.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-language service was confirmed in available sources; treat as a Japanese-language dining experience unless confirmed otherwise on arrival.

places.name: 油そば 春日亭 渋谷店 (Abura-soba Kasugatei Shibuya Ten)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are made with 100% Hokkaido wheat flour and there is no gluten-free noodle alternative identified. The tare (sauce) is a blend of several soy sauces from Kyushu, which typically contain wheat. As a wheat-noodle specialty shop with no gluten-free options found, this is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options were identified. The base tare/stock is chicken-and-pork-bone broth simmered for two days, and there is no indication of a plant-based broth, noodle, or topping alternative on the menu. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners as currently documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-24:00; Sun & holidays 11:00-22:00; open daily (無休) except closed during the New Year holiday period
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (道玄坂 / near Shibuya Scramble crossing)
places.notes: Kasugatei is a Tokyo-born abura-soba (oil noodle) specialty chain that began at its Ikebukuro honten and has since grown into a multi-location chain across Tokyo (and beyond, including Nagoya and Niigata). This Dogenzaka shop is the original Shibuya branch, run as part of the Kas · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Ikebukuro, Tokyo (chain founded 2009)
chef_bio.background: Kasugatei is a Tokyo-born abura-soba (oil noodle) specialty chain that began at its Ikebukuro honten and has since grown into a multi-location chain across Tokyo (and beyond, including Nagoya and Niigata). This Dogenzaka shop is the original Shibuya branch, run as part of the Kasugatei Group.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated commitment (kodawari) centers on noodles made with 100% Hokkaido-grown wheat flour, paired with a secret tare blended from several Kyushu soy sauces, backed by a chicken-bone and pork-bone stock simmered for two days to concentrate its flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu abura-soba, and the shop's popular Torimen (chicken-and-pork) abura-soba — noodles served oil-and-sauce-coated rather than in broth, meant to be tossed tableside with vinegar, rayu chili oil and garlic before eating.

anecdotes.text: In spring 2025 Kasugatei announced it was suspending PayPay and other cashless payment options at several locations — including its Ikebukuro flagship, this Shibuya (Dogenzaka) shop, and its Akihabara and Shibuya Center-gai branches — citing the burden of card-processing fees on a roughly 1,000-yen average ticket, and asked customers to "please pay in cash wherever possible."

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature tare is a secret blend of several soy sauces sourced from Kyushu, and the noodles are made with 100% Hokkaido wheat flour — a standard wheat-based soy-sauce noodle shop with no gluten-free noodle or sauce alternative identified.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tare and stock base are built on chicken-bone (torigara) and pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock simmered for two days, so the standard dishes are not vegetarian or vegan; no plant-based broth or noodle option was found.

cultural_comfort.note: Typical ticket-vending-machine (食券) ordering system common to Japanese ramen/abura-soba shops; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app or pointing at the photo menu is recommended.

places.name: SACYA（サクヤ）渋谷スクランブルスクエア店 (SACYA Shibuya Scramble Square)
places.gf_detail: Plain matcha and matcha lattes are naturally gluten-free (no reported wheat-based syrups or additives). The Shibuya-exclusive matcha cookie sandwich is made with rice flour and marketed as gluten-free as well as vegan. However, other bakery items such as the signature matcha Basque cheesecake are wheat-based, and there is no confirmed dedicated gluten-free prep line — the counter appears to handle both wheat-containing and rice-flour items, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Celiac-level diners should confirm with staff before ordering baked goods.
places.vegan_detail: Matcha lattes can be made with soy or oat milk instead of dairy, and the sakura-shaped matcha cookie sandwich (Shibuya-exclusive) is fully plant-based. Default items such as the matcha Basque cheesecake and the pink-foam "kinuchino" use dairy unless a substitution is requested. As a matcha/tea café with no savory food menu, there are no dashi/bonito-based items to worry about.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-21:00 (reported daily hours; specific weekday variation not confirmed)
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（Shibuya Scramble Square B2F, directly connected to Shibuy
places.notes: SACYA is a matcha specialty café that began in Daikanyama (the original shop opened in 2023 under the name SOPHIA COFFEE and was rebranded to SACYA the following year). This Shibuya Scramble Square location opened July 28, 2025 inside the Tokyu Food Show Edge food hall on B2F, di · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Daikanyama, Tokyo (original SACYA location); this Shibuya Scramble Square store is the brand's second shop
chef_bio.background: SACYA is a matcha specialty café that began in Daikanyama (the original shop opened in 2023 under the name SOPHIA COFFEE and was rebranded to SACYA the following year). This Shibuya Scramble Square location opened July 28, 2025 inside the Tokyu Food Show Edge food hall on B2F, directly connected to Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand name SACYA comes from Konohanasakuyahime, the Japanese mythological goddess associated with the origin of sakura (cherry blossoms). SACYA presents itself as a 'modern teahouse,' reinterpreting traditional Japanese tea culture through a contemporary, visually driven café format.
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha made from Yame-grown tea leaves supplied by Hoshino Seichaen, a Fukuoka tea garden that has produced matcha since 1989. Signature items include a 'kinuchino' (a cappuccino-style matcha drink topped with pink foam) and colorful matcha lattes such as strawberry matcha and lavender matcha latte; matcha is whisked to order at the counter in front of customers.

anecdotes.text: The Shibuya store's exclusive menu includes a vegan, gluten-free sakura-shaped matcha cookie sandwich made with rice flour.
SACYA uses Yame matcha from Hoshino Seichaen, one of Kyushu's early matcha producers dating to 1989.
The name SACYA is derived from Konohanasakuyahime, the goddess said to be the origin of the word sakura.

positives.text: Offers a Shibuya-exclusive vegan and gluten-free matcha cookie sandwich made with rice flour, plus soy milk and oat milk substitute options for matcha lattes.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 淺野屋（浅野屋） (Asanoya)
places.gf_detail: The shop serves nihachi soba (二八蕎麦), i.e. noodles made from roughly 80% buckwheat and 20% wheat flour, so the soba itself contains gluten. The dipping tsuyu is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat) and the tempura items (gobo-ten burdock tempura, tonpura pork cutlet) are coated in wheat flour batter and fried in the shared kitchen. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific preparation is mentioned in any source — not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth/tsuyu for both hot and cold soba is built on bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, and the menu includes meat and fish soba-mae dishes such as tonpura (pork loin tempura). No vegan or dashi-free preparation is mentioned in any source, so this is not suitable for vegan diners without prior arrangement (which is unconfirmed).
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00 & 17:00-20:00; Sat 11:00-14:00; closed Sun and public holidays
