places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1925 (Taishō 14) as a noren-wake (independent branch) of the historic Asanoya soba lineage in Kanda, whose original shop traces back to 1872 (Meiji 5). Founder Kobayashi Kiichi opened this Sarugakuchō branch after receiving the noren (shop name and blessing) from the m · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanda, Tokyo — traditional Edo-style soba lineage
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1925 (Taishō 14) as a noren-wake (independent branch) of the historic Asanoya soba lineage in Kanda, whose original shop traces back to 1872 (Meiji 5). Founder Kobayashi Kiichi opened this Sarugakuchō branch after receiving the noren (shop name and blessing) from the main Asanoya, and the shop has operated at the same Suidōbashi-area address for a century.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeps the unhurried, old-fashioned atmosphere of a classic Tokyo soba-ya, serving soba-mae (small dishes meant to be enjoyed with sake before the noodles) alongside hand-prepared tempura, in a calm room with both table and tatami seating.
chef_bio.specialty: Gobo-ten (burdock-root tempura) and tonpura (pork loin deep-fried in tempura batter)

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature gobo-ten burdock tempura is cut using a 'hyōshigiri' technique the cook mastered during his apprenticeship, giving the tempura an unusually hearty, crunchy bite that has become the dish diners order it for.
For roughly 15 years the shop has served itawasa (sliced fish cake with wasabi) made with Suzuhiro kamaboko, and the owners say customers regularly compliment its texture and flavor.

cultural_comfort.note: A century-old neighborhood soba shop with no English menu found in sources — comfortable for diners with some Japanese dining experience, but not set up for English-only visitors.

places.name: 月島もんじゃ おこげ 浅草 (Tsukishima Monjya Okoge Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free batter, or dedicated GF preparation was found in any source. Monjayaki and okonomiyaki batter is wheat-flour based by nature, sauces and soy sauce used in Japanese teppan cooking typically contain wheat, and the dish is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside other wheat-containing orders, creating cross-contamination risk. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian-specific menu was found. The shop's specialty dishes are seafood-forward (mentaiko/pollock roe, squid ink), and traditional monjayaki broth is typically made with bonito (katsuo) or other fish-based dashi, plus Worcestershire-style sauces that often contain fish extracts. Vegetable-only monjya could potentially be ordered, but no confirmation of a dashi substitution or animal-product-free broth was found in any source.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku - about 5 minutes' walk from Sensoji
places.cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き・鉄板焼き
places.notes: This Asakusa shop is a branch of the 月島もんじゃ おこげ (Tsukishima Monjya Okoge) group, which traces its roots to a Tsukishima monjayaki restaurant run by a seafood wholesale company founded in Meiji 4 (1871). The Asakusa branch opened on March 4, 2022, about a 1-2 minute walk from Asak · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: monjayaki/okonomiyaki restaurant, part of a small Tsukishima-origin monjayaki group
chef_bio.origin: Tsukishima, Tokyo (the historic monjayaki district)
chef_bio.background: This Asakusa shop is a branch of the 月島もんじゃ おこげ (Tsukishima Monjya Okoge) group, which traces its roots to a Tsukishima monjayaki restaurant run by a seafood wholesale company founded in Meiji 4 (1871). The Asakusa branch opened on March 4, 2022, about a 1-2 minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6 and roughly 5 minutes from Sensoji, and quickly became popular with lines forming from opening day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Because the parent company is a longstanding seafood wholesaler, the kitchen leans on seafood ingredients for its monjya and okonomiyaki. Skilled staff grill the monjayaki tableside for guests, so first-timers who have never cooked monjayaki themselves can still enjoy it without doing the work.
chef_bio.specialty: 明太もちもんじゃ (mentaiko mochi monjayaki) - a pollock-roe-and-mochi monjayaki that has become the shop's signature, alongside other seafood-forward monjya such as ika-sumi (squid ink) monjya.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened March 4, 2022, and has drawn steady lines of customers since its opening day.
The signature 明太子もちもんじゃ (mentaiko-mochi monjya) sells for about ¥1,618 and is frequently singled out by visitors as the must-order dish.
The shop's roots trace back to a Tsukishima monjayaki restaurant operated by a seafood wholesale company founded in Meiji 4 (1871), which is why seafood dishes are recommended.

positives.text: Skilled staff grill the monjayaki tableside for guests, which some reviewers note makes it approachable for first-timers, though this addresses cooking skill rather than allergen safety.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular tourist-adjacent monjayaki spot near Sensoji where staff cook tableside for beginners, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found in sources — expect a mostly Japanese-language experience.

places.name: 渋谷更科 (Shibuya Sarashina)
places.gf_detail: The shop's noodles are described consistently across sources as 二八そば (nihachi / "two-eight") — an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat-flour blend — meaning the soba itself contains wheat gluten and is not gluten-free. No 十割 (juwari, 100% buckwheat) noodle option was found in any menu source. Japanese soba tsuyu (dipping/broth sauce) is standardly made with wheat-containing shoyu; no gluten-free tsuyu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol could be confirmed for this shop. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan menu, vegan-adapted dashi, or any vegan accommodation at this shop. Traditional soba tsuyu in Japan is standardly made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, which is not vegan; nothing found indicates this shop uses a kombu/shiitake-only vegetarian dashi instead. Vegan diners should call ahead (03-3461-0335) to ask whether a plain noodle order or modified sauce is possible before visiting.
places.hours_raw: Most-cited: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30), Sun & holidays 11:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). One older/conflicting source lists Mon-Sat 11:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), Sun 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Sources disagree — call 03-3461-0335 to confirm current hours.

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An 80-plus-year-old neighborhood soba shop tucked in the basement at the very entrance of Shibuya Center-gai, just past the Scramble Crossing. chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: An 80-plus-year-old neighborhood soba shop tucked in the basement at the very entrance of Shibuya Center-gai, just past the Scramble Crossing. Despite sitting in one of the busiest, loudest corners of Tokyo, the shop keeps a hushed, Showa-era dining room down a narrow staircase — a deliberate contrast that regulars and food writers alike remark on.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen selects wheat and buckwheat flour by season, choosing whichever is 'most delicious at that time of year,' and adjusts the water ratio in the dough daily to account for changing weather and humidity — a craft the shop attributes to accumulated years of the noodle-makers' experience and intuition rather than a fixed recipe.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made futsu (two-eight ratio) Sarashina-style buckwheat noodles, including the house specialty sankensoba (三根そば) and classic mori/zaru soba, alongside donburi and udon dishes.

anecdotes.text: Located at the entrance of Center-gai in the basement of a building across from Mizuho Bank, the shop is often described as an island of calm below the crowds and noise of one of Shibuya's busiest streets.
The shop has been serving its Sarashina-style noodles for over 60-80 years, adjusting flour selection and dough hydration daily according to the season and weather.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Noodles are two-eight (nihachi) soba — roughly 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour — so the noodle itself contains gluten; standard soba tsuyu also relies on wheat-based shoyu.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, local, Japanese-language-first basement soba shop with no confirmed English menu or vegan/GF signage — diners with dietary restrictions should be prepared to explain their needs in Japanese or via translation app.

places.name: ビストロ ユウ (Bistro U (Bisutoro Yū))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found on Tabelog, the restaurant's Instagram, or food-media coverage. As a French bistro featuring roux-based sauces, bread service, and confit/fried preparations, wheat exposure across the kitchen should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise by phone.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based labeling, or dashi/bonito-avoidance information was found in any source. The restaurant is explicitly marketed as "肉&ワイン カジュアルフレンチ" (Meat & Wine Casual French), a meat-centric concept, so a full vegan meal is unlikely to be available without prior arrangement; vegetarians/vegans should call ahead to check for any adaptable vegetable-forward dishes.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区 水道橋
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 店長の川邊優太さんはソムリエ資格を持ち、水道橋駅から徒歩1分の地下1階でこのカジュアルフレンチビストロを営んでいる。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 店長の川邊優太さんはソムリエ資格を持ち、水道橋駅から徒歩1分の地下1階でこのカジュアルフレンチビストロを営んでいる。
chef_bio.specialty: 低温調理のコンフィ製法で仕上げる豚肉のグリル（ロースト前にオイルでじっくり火入れするコンフィ仕立て）

anecdotes.text: ランチは全7種類のメニューがあり、メインに加えてライスまたはパン、スープ、ソフトドリンクが付いて1,200円という、コストパフォーマンスの高さで知られる。
ディナーではソムリエ資格を持つ川邊さんが世界各国から集めた50種類以上のワインやビールをグラス単位で提供し、ビストロフレンチと共に楽しめる。

cultural_comfort.note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service; ordering will likely require Japanese or pointing at photos.

places.name: 間借りカレー シモカワスパイス (Magari Curry Shimokawa Spice)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free policy or ingredient statement from the shop was found. Spice curries here are rice-based rather than roux-thickened, which is favorable, but wheat exposure is unconfirmed for shoyu/marinades used in dishes like the pork vindaloo, and there is no stated dedicated fryer or cross-contamination protocol. Ask staff directly about wheat in sauces/marinades before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No formal vegan menu or claim was found. The shop's core menu (butter chicken curry, pork vindaloo) is meat-based and not vegan. However, the current yearly theme (2025) is a vegetable-forward "W-vegetable curry" featuring dishes such as jackfruit rendang and Assam taro curry, which are plant-based in concept but not confirmed free of dashi, fish sauce, or ghee. Vegan diners should ask staff which items are fully plant-based on the day they visit.
places.hours_raw: 水〜金 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:30)、土 11:30-14:30、日・月・火定休。間借り営業のため出店日・時間は変動あり、Instagramで要確認
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Shinjuku Station West Exit
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A one-person "magari" (borrowed-kitchen) spice curry pop-up that takes over a Nishi-Shinjuku bar for lunch service a few days a week. The owner (who posts as @high007 / @shimokawa.spice) also runs occasional pop-ups elsewhere in Tokyo (Koenji) and makes research trips abroad, inc · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A one-person "magari" (borrowed-kitchen) spice curry pop-up that takes over a Nishi-Shinjuku bar for lunch service a few days a week. The owner (who posts as @high007 / @shimokawa. spice) also runs occasional pop-ups elsewhere in Tokyo (Koenji) and makes research trips abroad, including to India, to study regional curry traditions.
chef_bio.philosophy: "Ichinen-ichi-curry" (one curry per year): rather than a fixed menu, the shop spends a full year deeply refining a single spice-curry concept before moving to the next, treating each year's dish as a dedicated study.
chef_bio.specialty: A rotating yearly "signature" curry built around one theme mastered per year — butter chicken (2021), pork vindaloo (2022), sashimi-fish coconut curry (2023), rendang (2024), and a vegetable-forward "W-vegetable curry" (2025, e. g. jackfruit rendang and Assam taro curry) — usually served alongside a rotating daily curry, plus a mixed "3-curry forest bowl. "

anecdotes.text: A blog post title notes the owner traveling to India in January for curry research ("1月は店主インドへ"), reflecting the shop's practice of sourcing techniques directly from source regions each year.
The shop's core concept of mastering one new curry theme per year (butter chicken, pork vindaloo, fish-coconut curry, rendang, vegetable curry) is described across independent write-ups.

cultural_comfort.note: A very local one-person pop-up whose schedule and updates are posted only in Japanese on Instagram, with no confirmed English menu or signage — best visited with some Japanese support or translation help.

places.name: ヴェジィーター（Vegeater） (Vegeater)
places.gf_detail: No source found confirming gluten-free buns or a gluten-free menu; this is a 100%-vegan (not gluten-free) concept and the burger buns appear to be standard wheat buns. Diners with celiac disease should not assume safety here and should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan concept: no animal-based ingredients and no soy-meat substitute, using an original bean-based patty instead. The signature Pulled Jack Burger (¥1,500) uses jackfruit, coleslaw, daikon sprouts and a bourbon BBQ sauce alongside the bean patty. No specific mention was found of hidden dashi/bonito risk since the whole menu is built vegan from the ground up, but exact sauce/condiment ingredient lists were not independently verified.
places.hours_raw: 情報源により差異あり：（Hotpepper系）月〜日 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:30) / 17:00-23:00 (L.O.22:30)。（ぐるなび系）平日・土 11:30-14:00 / 17:30-23:00。ハンバーガーはランチのみ提供、ディナーで食べる場合は予約時に要事前連絡。営業日の詳細はInstagramでの確認が推奨されている。
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（東、渋谷駅東口より徒歩約10分）
places.notes: Vegeaterは渋谷駅東口から徒歩約10分の場所にある、大豆ミートを使わず動物性食材を一切使用しない100%ヴィーガンのハンバーガー専門店。数種の豆をベースにした自家製の厚みのあるオリジナルパティを使用し、卵・乳製品不使用のバンズで仕立てている。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: ヴィーガンハンバーガー専門店
chef_bio.background: Vegeaterは渋谷駅東口から徒歩約10分の場所にある、大豆ミートを使わず動物性食材を一切使用しない100%ヴィーガンのハンバーガー専門店。 数種の豆をベースにした自家製の厚みのあるオリジナルパティを使用し、卵・乳製品不使用のバンズで仕立てている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 大豆ミートに頼らず、複数の豆を組み合わせた独自レシピのパティでボリュームと食べ応えを追求し、動物性食材ゼロでも満足感のあるハンバーガーを提供することを掲げている。
chef_bio.specialty: プルドジャックバーガー（豆ベースのオリジナルパティに加え、ジャックフルーツ、コールスロー、貝割れ大根、バーボンBBQソースを合わせた看板メニュー、¥1,500）

anecdotes.text: 「食べ応え充分なヴィーガンバーガー！」と評され、プルドジャックバーガーの厚みのあるパティとジャックフルーツの組み合わせが紹介されている。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 店は動物性食材を一切使用しない100%ヴィーガン業態で、大豆ミートも使わずオリジナルの豆ベースパティを使用しているとされる。

positives.text: 動物性食材不使用・大豆ミート不使用を明記した100%ヴィーガン専門店。

cultural_comfort.note: There is an English-language Tabelog listing and an English/Japanese-mixed Instagram presence, but menu and staff communication appear primarily Japanese, so basic Japanese greetings will help.

places.name: 追分だんご本舗 新宿本店 (Oiwake Dango Honpo Shinjuku Honten)
places.gf_detail: Dango here are rice-flour based (not wheat), and the anko (red bean paste) is normally wheat-free, but the shop's mitarashi sauce is a standard sweet soy-sauce glaze — ordinary Japanese shoyu typically contains wheat, and no gluten-free soy sauce, dedicated allergen protocol, or GF menu labeling was found in any source. No dedicated-fryer confirmation either way was found. Anyone with celiac disease should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Plain dango, anko-based sweets, and basic mitsumame/anmitsu components (rice dumplings, red beans, agar, syrup) are typically made without animal products, but the menu also includes items like cream anmitsu (dairy whipped cream) and other seasonal additions that are not vegan. No dedicated vegan menu, labeling, or staff confirmation was found in any source — vegan diners should ask about specific ingredients, especially anything involving cream or dairy toppings.
places.hours_raw: 物販: 10:30〜19:00（毎日）／喫茶室: 12:00〜18:00（L.O.17:30）、土・日曜日・祝日は11:30から。生菓子完売の場合は営業時間内でも早く閉店することがある。
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（Shinjuku Sanchome）
places.cuisine: 甘味処・和菓子・喫茶
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop's roots trace back to 1455, when the warlord Ota Dokan, while building Edo Castle, held a moon-viewing party at Takaido and was presented with hand-formed dango by a local family — he loved them so much he kept requesting them. The name 'Oiwake' (追分) refers to the fork i · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 和菓子店・喫茶室 (wagashi shop and tea room)
chef_bio.origin: 1947年（昭和22年）創業。起源はさらに古く、1455年に太田道灌が高井戸で中秋の名月の宴を催した際、地元の名族から献上された手作りの団子を大いに気に入り、その後も所望したという言い伝えに遡る。
chef_bio.background: The shop's roots trace back to 1455, when the warlord Ota Dokan, while building Edo Castle, held a moon-viewing party at Takaido and was presented with hand-formed dango by a local family — he loved them so much he kept requesting them. The name 'Oiwake' (追分) refers to the fork in old Naito-Shinjuku where the Koshu Kaido and Ome Kaido roads split, a busy travelers' crossroads. In the summer of 1947, just after the war, founder Fujii Toemon opened a small shop called 'Yanagiya' in Shinjuku Sanchome; having heard the old story of Oiwake dango from town elders, he set out to revive its taste on the very spot where the roads once met, and the business grew into today's Oiwake Dango Honpo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Dough, red bean paste, and beans are all hand-prepared fresh in the shop's own kitchen every morning before the dango are made and sold the same day.
chef_bio.specialty: Otona no Mitarashi (大人のみたらし, "Adult's Mitarashi") — dango glazed in the shop's mitarashi sauce blended with sansho pepper and fresh shichimi spice, giving the classic sweet-salty soy glaze a warmer, spicier edge.

anecdotes.text: 太田道灌が江戸城築城中、高井戸で中秋の名月の宴を開いた際、地元の名族が手づくりの団子を献上し、道灌がその味をいたく気に入って以後も所望した、という室町時代の逸話が「追分だんご」の起源とされる。
終戦間もない1947年夏、新宿三丁目に「やなぎ家」という白い暖簾を掲げたのが現在の追分だんご本舗の始まり。創業者・藤井藤右ヱ門が町の古老から追分だんごの由来を聞き、新宿追分の地でその味を復活させようとしたという。
名物「大人のみたらし」は山椒と生七味を効かせた甘辛いタレが特徴で、起源は室町時代にまで遡ると紹介されている。

cultural_comfort.note: A historic, primarily Japanese-oriented neighborhood wagashi shop and tea room — no confirmed English menu, though it sits in the very central, tourist-frequented Shinjuku Sanchome area.

places.name: 浅草おにぎりはるちゃん (Asakusa Onigiri Haruchan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation were found in any source. Fillings include soy-sauce-marinated egg yolk (soy sauce typically contains wheat) and sides include karaage (fried chicken, typically flour/wheat-battered) and tonjiru (miso soup, miso can contain wheat/barley depending on type) — meaning cross-contact with wheat is likely throughout the kitchen. Plain salted rice balls may be closer to gluten-free by ingredient list alone, but shared prep surfaces/utensils are not confirmed safe. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan claims found. The menu is centered on fish and meat fillings (salted salmon, mentaiko/cod roe) and animal-based sides (tonjiru pork soup, karaage fried chicken); dashi/stock ingredients for the soup are not detailed but Japanese tonjiru conventionally uses katsuo (bonito) or niboshi dashi. A plain salt-only onigiri might be achievable by request, but no dedicated vegan option, ingredient list, or staff accommodation process was confirmed in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-22:00 daily (per multiple listing aggregators); some sources note irregular/no-fixed closed day (不定休) rather than a guaranteed daily schedule — call ahead to confirm.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in April 2024 on Kokusai-dori in Asakusa, a short walk from the Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station. The shop occupies a building styled like an old Japanese house, with shoji-paper windows, wooden nameplate menus, and a hinoki (cypress) counter, aiming for a warm, nostalgic a · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in April 2024 on Kokusai-dori in Asakusa, a short walk from the Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station. The shop occupies a building styled like an old Japanese house, with shoji-paper windows, wooden nameplate menus, and a hinoki (cypress) counter, aiming for a warm, nostalgic atmosphere. The shop's name, "Haruchan," is the name of the proprietress's own mother — the onigiri are presented as her mother's home-style recipe.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighborhood onigiri counter built around the idea of Japanese soul food made fresh to order: rice balls hand-shaped to order using carefully sourced ingredients, served either to eat in or take away.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-shaped onigiri (rice balls) made with Niigata Uonuma-grown Koshihikari rice and Ariake-sea nori, offered in 20+ varieties (e. g. mentaiko, salted salmon, chirimen-zansho, soy-marinated egg yolk) with fillings and toppings developed under a food researcher's supervision; sides include tonjiru (pork miso soup) and karaage fried chicken.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name "Haruchan" comes from the proprietress's mother's name, positioning the onigiri as a literal taste of her mother's cooking.
The interior deliberately uses shoji windows, wooden nameplate-style menus, and a hinoki counter to create a warm, distinctly Japanese, old-house feel just steps from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or protocol was found in any source; the menu's karaage (fried chicken) and soy-sauce-based fillings suggest wheat is present in the kitchen, creating cross-contact risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: One listed filling is 卵黄しょうゆ漬け (soy-sauce-marinated egg yolk), and standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat unless specifically labeled tamari/gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core menu is built around fish (salmon, mentaiko/cod roe), egg, and meat-based sides (tonjiru pork soup, karaage fried chicken), so shared rice, utensils, and cooking surfaces are not confirmed free of animal-product contact even for a plain rice ball.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, Japanese-only neighborhood onigiri counter with no English menu confirmed in any source, so some pointing/simple Japanese may be needed to order.

places.name: 麺屋 我ガ 渋谷二丁目中央店 (Menya Gaga Shibuya Nichome Chuo)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-noodle tonkotsu ramen shop; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy menu was found in any source. The tonkotsu broth itself is pork-bone based, but the shop's tare (seasoning base) and the aged spicy chili sauce are not confirmed wheat-free, and shoyu-based seasonings common to this style typically contain wheat. Celiac diners should not consider this a safe option.
places.vegan_detail: Every listed dish, including the vegetable-topped versions (e.g. moyashi/bean-sprout ramen, negi ramen), is built on the same 21-hour-simmered pork bone (tonkotsu) broth — there is no vegetable or plant-based broth alternative mentioned in any source. The gyoza and most toppings (chashu pork, ajitama egg) are also non-vegan. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-22:30, no regular closed days
places.neighborhood: Shibuya Ward, Shibuya 2-chome (near Miyamasuzaka, between Sh
places.notes: Menya Gaga began in Ogori, Fukuoka in 2013 after its founder learned the recipe directly from Ichiran's founders, Sadayuki and Keiko Nakahara, reportedly opening on the same site where an early-generation Ichiran shop had once stood. The group expanded within Fukuoka (Ogori, Kuru · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Fukuoka-origin ramen group; Tokyo/Shibuya branch of Menya Gaga
chef_bio.origin: Ogori City, Fukuoka Prefecture (original honten founded 2013)
chef_bio.background: Menya Gaga began in Ogori, Fukuoka in 2013 after its founder learned the recipe directly from Ichiran's founders, Sadayuki and Keiko Nakahara, reportedly opening on the same site where an early-generation Ichiran shop had once stood. The group expanded within Fukuoka (Ogori, Kurume, Tenjin) and to Bangkok, Thailand, before opening this Shibuya 2-chome shop on February 2, 2026 as its first Tokyo location.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated aim is to preserve and carry forward the pork-bone ramen flavor inherited from Ichiran's founders: a broth simmered over 21 hours for concentrated umami and a creamy, odor-free mouthfeel, paired with a distinctive aged spicy sauce blending chili peppers and Aomori garlic. The Shibuya shop's interior pairs counter seating aimed at solo diners with calligraphy of four-character philosophical phrases (e. g. 行雲流水, 'drifting like clouds, flowing like water') and original jazz playing in the background.
chef_bio.specialty: Ajitama ramen (味玉らーめん) — tonkotsu ramen topped with a seasoned soft-boiled egg; the shop's best-selling item

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's Tokyo debut was widely reported as carrying on the taste directly transmitted by Ichiran founders Nakahara Sadayuki and Keiko; the Ogori honten dates to 2013 and the group also runs shops in Kurume, Tenjin, and Bangkok.

Coverage of the Shibuya opening describes handmade water gyoza made by a chef with Chinese dim sum credentials, and a soup 'simmered for 21 hours or more, pursuing a creamy mouthfeel without any odor while maintaining concentrated umami and sweetness from pork bone.'
A blog write-up of the Shibuya shop describes the interior's counter seating for solo diners, wall calligraphy of philosophical four-character phrases, and jazz playing in the background.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-only ticket vending machine typical of Ichiran-lineage shops, and no English menu or allergy/dietary accommodation has been confirmed by any source.

places.name: 鶏味座YATAI (Torimikura YATAI)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free option was mentioned in any source found. The karaage (fried chicken) is a standard Japanese-style deep-fried item, typically coated in wheat flour/starch, and the signature oyako-don's tare (simmering sauce) is a proprietary sauce that in standard Japanese cooking is soy-sauce based (soy sauce normally contains wheat) — no gluten-free alternative was identified. Treat as not celiac-safe absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options were identified. The signature dish is chicken and egg based (oyako-don), and the brand's core tare/broth recipe is described as built on bonito, mackerel, and horse-mackerel dashi (fish stock), making it unsuitable for vegan diners. No plant-based menu items were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-21:00 (daily, food-court hours)
places.neighborhood: 文京区後楽 (Tokyo Dome City / Suidōbashi)
places.cuisine: 親子丼・から揚げ・鶏料理
places.notes: 鶏味座YATAI is the Tokyo Dome City food-court (GO-FUN) branch of Torimikura (鶏味座), a chicken-focused restaurant brand operated by Will International (ウイル・インターナショナル), whose full-service flagship restaurant is in Aoyama, Minato-ku. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Food-court branch of the Torimikura (鶏味座) chicken-specialty chain
chef_bio.origin: Part of the wider 鶏味座 (Torimikura) brand, whose original restaurant is in Aoyama, Minato-ku
chef_bio.background: 鶏味座YATAI is the Tokyo Dome City food-court (GO-FUN) branch of Torimikura (鶏味座), a chicken-focused restaurant brand operated by Will International (ウイル・インターナショナル), whose full-service flagship restaurant is in Aoyama, Minato-ku.
chef_bio.philosophy: Across its locations the brand's stated focus is on treating chicken and egg with particular care — the tare (simmering sauce) the chicken cooks in and the texture of the egg finish — alongside crispy karaage and craft beer, sold in a fast, counter-service format at this food-court location.
chef_bio.specialty: 究極の親子丼 (Ultimate Oyako-don) — chicken lightly simmered in the shop's original warishita sauce and topped with a carefully finished, silky egg

anecdotes.text: The brand's oyako-don technique, as described for the flagship store, involves simmering Tokyo jidori chicken in a tare built on bonito, mackerel, and horse-mackerel dashi, then topping it with egg folded to a silky finish — the same 'ultimate oyako-don' concept is sold at this Tokyo Dome City stall.

At the Tokyo Dome City food court, the shop is described as offering the 'ultimate oyako-don' (chicken lightly simmered in an original sauce, finished with carefully prepared egg), karaage, and craft beer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature oyako-don is finished with the shop's original tare (simmering sauce); no gluten-free or wheat-free tare/soy sauce was mentioned in any source, and Japanese tare of this kind is typically soy-sauce based (soy sauce normally contains wheat).

vegan_cross_contact.text: The brand's signature broth/tare is described (for the technique used across its stores) as built on bonito, mackerel, and horse-mackerel dashi, meaning fish stock underlies core menu items such as the oyako-don.

cultural_comfort.note: It's an order-at-counter food-court stall inside a major tourist complex (Tokyo Dome City), so basic Japanese or pointing at photos should be enough, though no English menu was confirmed by any source.

places.name: 東京ギョーザスタンドウーロン 新宿店 (Tokyo Gyoza Stand Oolong Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: This is a gyoza specialty restaurant — wrappers, dumpling fillings, noodle dishes (中華そば), and typical Chinese-izakaya seasonings (soy sauce, sauces) are wheat-based by nature. No gluten-free wrapper option, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu was found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners without prior direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: Signature dishes are pork-based fried and boiled gyoza plus general izakaya fare; no vegan menu, plant-based dumpling option, or dashi/bonito-free alternatives were identified in any source reviewed.
places.neighborhood: 新宿 - 新宿駅東口 歌舞伎町

chef_bio.origin: Tachikawa (original shop), expanded to Tokyo Station Gransta, Nihonbashi, and Shinjuku
chef_bio.background: Part of the Tokyo Gyoza Stand Oolong group, which began as a popular gyoza shop in Tachikawa and later expanded into Tokyo Station's Gransta mall and Nihonbashi before this Shinjuku 3-chome branch opened on April 30, 2026, one minute on foot from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'neo machi-chuka' (new-style neighborhood Chinese) izakaya concept — casual town-Chinese cooking made approachable for a quick meal or a few after-work drinks
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-wrapped pan-fried gyoza (焼餃子) and water-boiled gyoza (水餃子), including cilantro and shiso variations, paired with an original house craft beer brewed with oolong tea leaves

anecdotes.text: The gyoza recipes were developed in collaboration with a Michelin-experienced chef and a Chinese dim sum specialist, served alongside 'Uron Ale,' a house craft beer brewed using oolong tea leaves sourced from China's Fujian province.
The Shinjuku 3-chome branch opened April 30, 2026 as a 'neo machi-chuka' izakaya with pan-fried gyoza as its signature dish, also offering water-boiled, cilantro, and shiso gyoza varieties.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, casual chain izakaya right by Shinjuku Station's southeast exit; ordering is straightforward with a simple, visual gyoza-and-beer menu, but no confirmed dedicated English menu.

places.name: ファラフェルブラザーズ 渋谷パルコ店 (Falafel Brothers Shibuya PARCO)
places.gf_detail: The falafel itself is chickpea-based and made without a wheat-flour binder, and a gluten-free-friendly rice plate is offered as an alternative to the pita/bread sandwiches. However, the restaurant has not published confirmation about wheat in its sauces/dressings, shared-fryer use, or kitchen cross-contamination practices, so this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen — celiac and severely gluten-sensitive diners should confirm specifics with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is a fully vegan concept: all menu items (falafel wraps/sandwiches, salad bowls, rice plates, sweets) are plant-based, with no meat, fish, dairy, egg, or honey. Sources describe the brand as using organic ingredients and holding kosher certification, which lowers the likelihood of hidden animal-derived additions like fish-based dashi, though exact sauce/dressing ingredient lists were not independently verified from a fetchable primary source.
places.hours_raw: 月〜木 11:00-21:30、金・土 11:00-22:00（日曜は平日と同様の時間帯という情報もあり、渋谷PARCOの営業時間に準ずるため変動あり）

chef_bio.origin: Israeli street food, brought to Japan
chef_bio.background: Falafel Brothers was founded in 2017 by Israeli entrepreneur Amnon Agassy together with Japanese chef Daisuke Tsukahara. The first shop opened on Imoaraizaka in Roppongi, a neighborhood with a large foreign-resident community that already knew falafel, before the brand grew into a small Tokyo group with additional locations in Ebisu, Otemachi, Harajuku and the Shibuya PARCO branch (7th floor).
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing casual, plant-based Israeli street food to Japan in an accessible, fully vegan form, using organic ingredients where possible.
chef_bio.specialty: Falafel pita wraps/sandwiches made from hand-formed chickpea falafel, plus rice-bowl plates and vegan sweets

anecdotes.text: The brand's first shop opened in 2017 on Roppongi's Imoaraizaka, a district chosen for its concentration of foreign residents already familiar with falafel, before the concept expanded toward a broader Japanese audience.
Falafel Brothers describes itself as bringing an easy, casual taste of Israeli food and vegan cooking to Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Celiac review states it is 'not recommended for celiacs,' that you must be 'very, very explicit about cross contamination,' the food preparer 'didn't know anything about Celiac disease,' and cross-contamination is 'highly likely' — there is a lot of bread and no separate prep area, with hands filling pita bread and then going into the same bowl of salad used to make the rice plates.
Listing states this establishment is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease.
Shop and aggregator listings market 'gluten-free menu' items and GF/sugar-free vegan sweets but publish no cross-contamination, shared-fryer, or allergen-protocol information.

positives.text: The entire menu is plant-based/vegan (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs), and the brand is described as using organic ingredients and being kosher-certified, reducing risk of hidden animal-derived ingredients such as dashi or bonito for vegan diners.
The falafel is chickpea-based with no wheat-flour binder, and the rice-plate menu option is noted as popular among gluten-free diners.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside Shibuya PARCO, a major tourist-facing shopping complex, the shop is described as offering English-language menu support, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate.

places.name: ビオカフェ (BiOcafe)
places.gf_detail: Offers a monthly-changing dedicated gluten-free lunch plus standing gluten-free items such as a veggie burger with a bun made from rice flour and konjac flour (no wheat) and gluten-free karaage. However, the same lunch service includes a self-serve salad buffet with homemade (wheat) bread and soup in the same area, and no source confirms a dedicated fryer or full wheat-free kitchen separation, so this is well-suited to gluten-sensitive diners but celiac-level guests should confirm cross-contact handling directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Vegan-friendly dishes are available on request/menu, including a vegan veggie burger, brown rice pasta, and lasagna, built on the same organic, pesticide-free vegetable base used throughout the menu. No source directly confirms whether dashi/bonito stock is avoided across all dishes, so vegans should ask specifically which items are prepared without fish-derived dashi.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (Udagawa-cho / Spain-zaka), near Shibuya Station
places.notes: BiOcafe describes itself as one of Japan's first organic cafe-restaurants built around 100% pesticide-free, chemical-fertilizer-free vegetables sourced from a small group of farmers chosen for sharing the owner's commitment to health and beauty. The concept is summed up on the of · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: BiOcafe describes itself as one of Japan's first organic cafe-restaurants built around 100% pesticide-free, chemical-fertilizer-free vegetables sourced from a small group of farmers chosen for sharing the owner's commitment to health and beauty. The concept is summed up on the official site as "食べてキレイになる" ("Eat to become beautiful").
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around organic, additive-free vegetables and recipes reworked to be gluten-free, vegetarian, and vegan where possible, so that health-conscious and diet-restricted guests can eat freely; the cafe also pours organic Italian wines chosen personally by the owner.
chef_bio.specialty: Gluten-free veggie burger — a bun made from rice flour and konjac flour (no wheat) filled with plant-based "veggie meat" and all plant-based sauces — alongside a monthly-changing gluten-free lunch course and gluten-free karaage.

anecdotes.text: Lunch guests are served an all-you-can-eat salad bar of organic Bio vegetables together with homemade bread, soup, and detox water alongside the gluten-free/vegan main dishes.
Described in 2025 coverage as a cafe-and-bakery on Spain-zaka that's especially popular with vegan diners in Shibuya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The lunch service pairs gluten-free/vegan mains with a self-serve salad buffet that also includes homemade (wheat) bread and soup in the same buffet area, a potential cross-contact point for celiac-level guests.

Celiac review reports the restaurant refuses to serve celiacs because they make everything with the same equipment, and gluten-free options were in the same case as the normal cakes so the cross-contamination risk was extremely high; the establishment is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease.

Japan has no strict gluten-free standard and no mandatory cross-contamination labeling; rice-flour products can be processed on the same line as wheat, so celiacs must confirm cross-contact directly rather than rely on a GF menu label.

BiOcafe's menu includes animal-derived dishes (eel, egg, chicken) alongside vegan items, and no public source specifies whether katsuo/bonito or other animal dashi is used, so vegan status of individual dishes is unconfirmed.

positives.text: Vegetables used across the menu are 100% pesticide-free and grown without chemical fertilizer, sourced from farmers chosen for sharing the cafe's health-focused standards.

The gluten-free veggie burger replaces the wheat bun with one made from rice flour and konjac flour and uses only plant-based sauces and "veggie meat," making it fully suitable for a vegan diner avoiding wheat.
Runs a standing monthly-changing healthy lunch explicitly marketed as gluten-free, vegetarian, and vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern organic cafe in central Shibuya that draws international vegetarian/vegan visitors, but menus and service appear to be primarily in Japanese, so basic Japanese or translation help is useful.

places.name: 九月堂 (Kugatsudo)
places.gf_detail: The ramen and tsukemen use standard wheat noodles, and the shoyu (soy sauce) tare is soy-sauce based. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu option is mentioned by the shop or in any press coverage found — not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Both ramen options ("assari"/light and "kotteri"/rich) use a double stock — an animal stock of pork bone, chicken, aromatic vegetables and black soybeans, combined with a bonito (katsuo) and dried-sardine (niboshi) seafood stock. The tsukemen dipping sauce is similarly meat/fish-based. No vegetable-stock, plant-based broth, or vegan menu item is described in any source found — not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 daily on days open (or until soup sells out); closed Sunday. Note: multiple 2026 announcements on the official site describe adjusted/shortened hours in some months — check the official site or X (@Kugatsudo) for the current schedule.
places.neighborhood: Jinnan, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2009 in Shibuya's Jinnan neighborhood. According to ramen-industry coverage, the owner trained for years at AFURI and ZUND-BAR — ramen shops affiliated with the celebrated Nakamuraya lineage — before striking out on his own with a chemical-seasoning-free (無化調) shoyu ram · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: owner's wife: pastry chef (formerly at ZUND-BAR), makes the shop's desserts
chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Opened in 2009 in Shibuya's Jinnan neighborhood. According to ramen-industry coverage, the owner trained for years at AFURI and ZUND-BAR — ramen shops affiliated with the celebrated Nakamuraya lineage — before striking out on his own with a chemical-seasoning-free (無化調) shoyu ramen concept.
chef_bio.philosophy: "Gentle ramen for the body" (体に優しいらーめんを) — no MSG or artificial seasonings, relying on natural ingredients for umami.
chef_bio.specialty: Assari (light) and Kotteri (rich) shoyu ramen, and tsukemen with thick, chewy noodles — each built on a double stock combining a full day's worth of simmered pork bone, chicken, aromatic vegetables and black soybeans with a bonito and dried-sardine seafood stock.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained extensively at AFURI and ZUND-BAR, sister shops connected to Nakamuraya, developing his soup-making craft before opening Kugatsudo.
The owner's wife, previously a pastry chef at ZUND-BAR, hand-makes the shop's desserts, giving Kugatsudo its identity as a combined 'ramen and sweets' cafe.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, independently run neighborhood ramen shop with a Japanese-language menu and no confirmed English signage or English-speaking staff in any source found.

places.name: 新宿古民家 十徳 (Shinjuku Kominka Juttoku)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or celiac-safety information was found in any source. Standard izakaya risk factors apply: soy sauce (wheat), tempura batter (wheat, and likely a shared fryer), and dashi/sauces that may contain wheat-based ingredients. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead (03-6321-4385) and confirm specific dishes and fryer use rather than assuming safety.
places.vegan_detail: As a standard izakaya, most cooked dishes rely on bonito-based dashi and typical seasonings, and the menu centers on sashimi and pork shabu-shabu; some individual vegetable dishes (tempura, salads, tofu-based items) may be vegetable-only but are not confirmed vegan-prepared (broth, sauces, and shared fryers likely contain animal products). No dedicated vegan menu or vegan certification was found — best to ask staff dish-by-dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-23:00 (lunch 11:30-15:00); closed Saturday and Sunday

places.notes: The restaurant occupies a Showa-era kominka (old private house) in Nishi-Shinjuku, one block from Shinjuku Station's west exit, with its original early-Showa building fabric largely intact rather than gutted for a modern fit-out. It is one of several Tokyo branches run under the  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: owner/operator under the 酒処十徳 (Sake-dokoro Juttoku) regional-izakaya brand
chef_bio.background: The restaurant occupies a Showa-era kominka (old private house) in Nishi-Shinjuku, one block from Shinjuku Station's west exit, with its original early-Showa building fabric largely intact rather than gutted for a modern fit-out. It is one of several Tokyo branches run under the 十徳 (Juttoku) name, a group built around sourcing regional Japanese sake and countryside cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's stated focus is on hunting down jizake (local, small-brewery sake) directly from breweries and tasting events, pairing it with regional home-style cooking, and extending that same care to the ingredients, cooking, room, and service — omotenashi hospitality built around sake discovery.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuroton (premium black pork) shabu-shabu and sashimi platters, served alongside a rotating lineup of dishes drawn from regional Japanese cuisine (郷土料理) — described in listings as a 'tour of Japan's local flavors. '

anecdotes.text: The cellar holds 70-plus types of sake, shochu, and umeshu, including sought-after labels like Dassai, Hiroki, and Jikon, sourced by staff visiting breweries and regional tasting events; brewers themselves are said to occasionally stop by the shop.
A food blogger described it as a quiet, easy-to-get-a-seat izakaya tucked on a back street near Shinjuku's west exit, good for a quick, unhurried drink.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood izakaya with an English-language Tabelog listing but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app is a good idea.

places.name: ネパリコ 渋谷店 (Nepalico Shibuya (Nepalico Dal Bhat & Cafe))
places.gf_detail: No source confirms dedicated gluten-free protocols. The vegetarian Dal Bhat set (dal lentil soup, rice, vegetable tarkari, two achar pickles) is naturally free of wheat and does not rely on Japanese gluten carriers like soy sauce or dashi, since this is a Nepali kitchen, not a Japanese one. However, the broader menu includes wheat-containing items, and there is no stated dedicated fryer or confirmed avoidance of cross-contact in the shared kitchen — ask staff before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetarian Dal Bhat set (no meat curry, swapped for extra achar/pickles) is regularly offered at both lunch and dinner. It is not confirmed vegan: Nepali curries commonly use ghee (clarified butter) and dairy is common in Nepali cooking, and no source states the vegetarian set is dairy-free. Treat as vegetarian-friendly with vegan needing direct confirmation from staff.
places.neighborhood: Sakuragaoka-cho, Shibuya (behind Cerulean Tower, near Nampei
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka backstreet in 2010 by a Nepalese owner-chef known to regulars as Padam-san, Nepalico set out to serve genuine Nepali home cooking rather than a Japanese-ified curry-shop version, in a small dining room decorated with photos and crafts from Nepal. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nepal
chef_bio.background: Opened in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka backstreet in 2010 by a Nepalese owner-chef known to regulars as Padam-san, Nepalico set out to serve genuine Nepali home cooking rather than a Japanese-ified curry-shop version, in a small dining room decorated with photos and crafts from Nepal.
chef_bio.philosophy: To introduce ordinary Japanese diners — not just curry enthusiasts — to the real, comforting flavor of Nepali family cooking: mild, spiced with imported Nepali spices, and closer to a home-cooked set meal than a restaurant curry.
chef_bio.specialty: Dal Bhat — the everyday Nepali meal of dal (lentil soup), bhat (rice), tarkari (vegetable or meat curry) and achar (pickle); ordered at lunch it comes with free refills of the dal soup and rice.

anecdotes.text: Owner Padam-san runs the restaurant with the aim of introducing everyday Japanese diners, not curry specialists, to authentic Nepali home cooking.
The lunchtime Dal Bhat set comes with free refills of the dal soup and a free rice size-up, plus a small salad and mini chai.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No published information on dedicated gluten-free prep areas or cross-contamination controls; ask staff directly.

The vegetarian dal bhat set is described as dal soup, rice, three tarkari, three achar, sag, and papad — the fried papad wafer sits directly on the shared plate, an unconfirmed-composition wheat/cross-contact vector no source addresses.

Search across FindMeGlutenFree, HappyCow, Tripadvisor and the shop site returned no celiac/coeliac-specific review or any statement of GF handling for Nepalico Shibuya; 'gluten-free' appears only as a generic property of dal bhat as a dish.
Japanese allergen search confirms no gluten-free or wheat-allergen accommodation information is published for the store; users are told to phone the shop directly — i.e., no verified protocol exists.

The claim that the vegetarian set is dairy-free/vegan came only from an aggregated search summary, not any cited primary source; Nepali dal/tarkari/sag commonly use ghee, leaving vegan status unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No published information confirming whether the vegetarian Dal Bhat is prepared without ghee/dairy or shares cookware with meat curries; ask staff directly.

positives.text: The Dal Bhat vegetarian set (dal soup, tarkari, two achar, rice) replaces the meat curry with extra vegetable/pickle sides, and at lunch the dal soup and rice are refilled free of charge.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, independent Nepali-owned neighborhood restaurant popular with Japanese office workers at lunch; the menu and staff are geared to Japanese speakers, but the cuisine and owner are Nepali-immigrant background, so simple English or pointing at the menu photos should get by.

places.name: ナンハウス (Naan House (Suidobashi))
places.gf_detail: Naan is a wheat-flour flatbread and is not gluten-free; rice is offered as an alternative side. No dedicated gluten-free menu, cross-contamination protocol, or wheat-free practice has been documented for this restaurant, so travelers with celiac disease should not assume curries are safe without asking the kitchen directly, especially given the small, single-kitchen setup shared with naan-making.
places.vegan_detail: The menu includes vegetable curry and dal (lentil) curry, which are naturally plant-forward, but Indian-Nepalese kitchens commonly finish curries with ghee, butter, or cream — this has not been confirmed one way or the other for this location, so vegans should ask specifically whether a dish is made without dairy/ghee before ordering.
places.neighborhood: Suidobashi / Kudan-shita (千代田区神田三崎町)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Multiple local listings describe Nan House Suidobashi as a small, family-run Indian-Nepalese curry shop staffed by a Nepalese chef, part of a wider pattern of Nepalese-run curry houses common around Tokyo train stations. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nepal
chef_bio.background: Multiple local listings describe Nan House Suidobashi as a small, family-run Indian-Nepalese curry shop staffed by a Nepalese chef, part of a wider pattern of Nepalese-run curry houses common around Tokyo train stations.
chef_bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat naan served alongside a choice of curries (including vegetable and dal/lentil curry)

anecdotes.text: Described online as a cozy, homey Indian curry spot near Suidobashi Station where naan refills are free.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or foreign-language service has been confirmed for this location; expect a Japanese-language ordering experience typical of a neighborhood curry shop.

places.name: 麦ノ音 (Mugi no Oto)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi information was found in any source. The venue's core draw is craft beer (wheat/barley-based) and house-made pork sausages/bacon; nothing suggests gluten-free awareness or accommodation. Celiac diners should not assume safety here without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu was found in any source. The menu centers on house-made pork sausages, smoked bacon and other meat dishes designed to pair with craft beer and natural wine; nothing indicates plant-based options or awareness of hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., dashi/katsuo).

It shares its building/space concept with an attached reservation-only sushi counter (へいらっしゃい) and a concept · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 麦ノ音 (Mugi no Oto) opened March 14, 2019 in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, as a live-music dining bar ("mixed restaurant") combining craft beer, natural wine, and house-made sausage. It shares its building/space concept with an attached reservation-only sushi counter (へいらっしゃい) and a concept cafe (ひみつきち屋さん).
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Watanabe said he wanted to offer dining-out experiences unique to a restaurant setting — live musician performances, craft beer, and house-made sausage — and expressed hope that overseas visitors would enjoy it too.
chef_bio.specialty: "Ratzi's Organic Sausage" — a handmade sausage sourced from Sausage Fest, a Bib Gourmand (Michelin Guide) restaurant in Kumamoto run by Czech chef Ladislav Hajžman, who spent eight years as manager of the Dutch embassy residence in Tokyo before turning to sausage-making. It is seasoned simply with just salt, pepper, garlic, and herbs, and served alongside smoked bacon and six rotating craft beer taps.

anecdotes.text: The signature dish, Ratzi's Organic Sausage, comes from Kumamoto restaurant Sausage Fest, helmed by Czech chef Ladislav Hajžman, a former eight-year manager of the Dutch embassy residence in Tokyo turned sausage maker; the sausage is kept simple with only salt, pepper, garlic and herbs.
On opening, owner Watanabe said he wanted to create restaurant-only experiences — live music, craft beer and house-made sausage — and hoped overseas tourists would enjoy it as well.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern Kabukicho dining bar geared toward adults with live music and online reservation platforms (Hotpepper, Ikyu); the owner has expressed hope that overseas visitors enjoy it, but no English menu or English-speaking-staff claim was confirmed, so basic Japanese or gestures may be needed.

places.name: AWkitchen figlia 渋谷スクランブルスクエア (AWkitchen figlia Shibuya Scramble Square)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free pasta option, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source. The restaurant's signature offering is housemade fresh wheat pasta, so celiac-safe dining here cannot be confirmed and should not be assumed; anyone with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was confirmed. The restaurant's fresh pasta is typically egg-based, and its signature bagna cauda dish is traditionally made with a dairy/anchovy-based warm sauce, so it should not be assumed vegan-friendly without asking. Some vegetable-forward appetizers and salads may be adaptable, but this was not confirmed from any source.

"Figlia" is Italian for "daughter," and this location — the brand's daughter outpost — opened on the 13th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square in 2019, moving the concept from its original Ao · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Italian restaurant, part of the AWkitchen family of vegetable-focused pasta restaurants
chef_bio.origin: Omotesando/Aoyama, Tokyo (original AWkitchen concept, founded 2004)
chef_bio.background: AWkitchen began in 2004 in Omotesando/Aoyama as a restaurant built around eating vegetables. "Figlia" is Italian for "daughter," and this location — the brand's daughter outpost — opened on the 13th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square in 2019, moving the concept from its original Aoyama roots into the new landmark tower directly connected to Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's guiding idea is pairing housemade fresh pasta with an abundance of vegetables sourced from a nationwide network of contracted farmers, changing seasonally so guests taste different vegetable combinations through the year.
chef_bio.specialty: AW Farm Bagna Cauda (農園バーニャカウダ) — a signature dish of vegetables from the restaurant's partner farms served with a warm dipping sauce, paired with a rotating selection of handmade fresh pasta.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's farm bagna cauda has become widely shared on social media and is considered the signature draw of the Shibuya Scramble Square location.
AWkitchen's original Aoyama/Omotesando shop, the concept's birthplace, was known as a beloved 'restaurant where you eat vegetables' before the brand expanded to Shibuya, Ebisu and other locations.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside Shibuya Scramble Square, a major international tourist landmark, so basic English navigation is likely, but no restaurant-specific English menu or English-speaking-staff confirmation was found.

places.name: おっこん (Okkon)
places.gf_detail: The okonomiyaki batter is wheat-flour based and the signature dishes are built directly around soba or udon wheat noodles shipped from Hiroshima; the finishing sauce is a thick Worcestershire-style okonomiyaki sauce, which typically contains wheat. No source mentions any gluten-free substitution, rice-noodle option, or celiac accommodation. This is not a suitable choice for a strict gluten-free or celiac diet.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork, egg, and an okonomiyaki sauce typical of the style (which commonly includes meat/seafood-derived extracts such as oyster or fish); no vegan-specific dish, dashi substitution, or plant-based menu item was found in any source.
