roles: pro-wrestling mask craftsman and referee (owner's side vocation)
chef_bio.origin: 文京区後楽（水道橋駅東口徒歩1分）
chef_bio.background: Okkon is a small, deeply personal Hiroshima-okonomiyaki counter run by an owner who moonlights as a professional-wrestling mask craftsman and match referee. The basement shop, opposite the Tokyo Dome Hotel, doubles as a shrine to two passions: the walls and stairwell are covered in pro-wrestling posters and hand-made masks, and the room is decked out in Hiroshima Toyo Carp memorabilia, making it a magnet for Carp fans after a Tokyo Dome game.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built with cabbage, bean sprouts, pork and soba or udon noodles shipped in from Hiroshima, griddled crisp and finished with a thick sauce and a heavy scattering of scallions — the nikutama-soba (肉玉そば) and nikutama-udon (肉玉うどん), sometimes topped with squid tempura, are the most talked-about orders.

anecdotes.text: Featured in the TV segment 'なりゆき街道旅' with actress Mayumi Wakamura visiting the Suidobashi shop to sample the Hiroshima okonomiyaki.
The owner is known in the pro-wrestling world as a mask-maker and referee, and the shop's decor and clientele reflect that double life alongside its Hiroshima Carp fandom.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature okonomiyaki sauce is a thick, Worcestershire-style sauce and the dishes are built around wheat-flour batter and wheat soba/udon noodles from Hiroshima, so wheat is a core, unavoidable ingredient rather than an optional condiment.

cultural_comfort.note: A very local, character-driven okonomiyaki counter themed around pro wrestling and the Hiroshima Carp, with no English menu confirmed in any source, though it does appear on a couple of English-language booking listings.

places.name: KIELO COFFEE ASAKUSA (Kielo Coffee Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: Multiple sources (Akiba Keizai grand-opening release, independent cafe blogs) state KIELO COFFEE's homemade sweets lineup includes a gluten-free option made without wheat flour, alongside a vegan option — this appears to be a brand-wide practice also mentioned for the original Akihabara shop, not something confirmed specifically photographed/verified at the Asakusa branch. The same kitchen also bakes wheat-based items (e.g. the signature Nordic cinnamon roll), and no source describes separate GF prep equipment, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contact protocols — celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Reported to include at least one homemade vegan (no animal product) pastry as a standard menu item, alongside regular dairy-based drinks and pastries. This is a coffee-and-pastry menu (no Japanese cooked-food menu), so hidden dashi/bonito is not a relevant concern here; however, standard espresso drinks likely default to dairy milk unless a plant-based milk substitution is requested, and no source confirms whether plant milk is offered.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays: 8:00-18:00 / Weekends & holidays: 8:00-19:00
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草・雷門 (Kaminarimon, Asakusa)
places.notes: KIELO COFFEE began in Akihabara in 2019 after founder Yoshiike traveled to Finland and was struck by the Finnish sense of peace of mind, work-life balance, and the daily ritual of the coffee break. He felt Japan lacked the culture that produced that kind of happiness, and set out · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in Akihabara, Tokyo in 2019 by Yoshiike, inspired by a personal trip to Finland.
chef_bio.background: KIELO COFFEE began in Akihabara in 2019 after founder Yoshiike traveled to Finland and was struck by the Finnish sense of peace of mind, work-life balance, and the daily ritual of the coffee break. He felt Japan lacked the culture that produced that kind of happiness, and set out to build coffee shops that embody it. The brand has since grown to several Tokyo locations, including a dedicated vegan/gluten-free sister cafe (Be green by KIELO COFFEE) in Hongo. KIELO COFFEE ASAKUSA opened August 15, 2025, a short walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate, pairing the brand's Nordic sensibility (soft wood tones, a central communal table) with Japanese design touches like shoji screens.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Kielo means 'lily of the valley' in Finnish, chosen to express the hope that happiness will return to everyday life; the brand's stated mission is to bring Finland's unhurried 'coffee break' culture into Japanese daily life.
chef_bio.specialty: Light-roast, single-origin specialty coffee (roughly ten in-house-roasted origins) and a Nordic cinnamon roll, alongside carrot cake and other homemade pastries.

anecdotes.text: Founder Yoshiike was drawn to Finland's sense of peace and quality of life during a visit there, and felt that the culture behind that happiness was exactly what Japan lacked — leading him to open KIELO's first shop in Akihabara in spring 2019.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source describes a separate gluten-free prep area or equipment; the same kitchen bakes wheat-based pastries (e.g. the Nordic cinnamon roll) alongside the wheat-flour-free sweet, so cross-contact risk is unconfirmed either way.

positives.text: Sweets are described as all homemade, with a gluten-free (no wheat flour) option and a vegan (no animal product) option specifically called out as part of the regular lineup — a deliberate dietary accommodation rather than an incidental one.

cultural_comfort.note: Brand operates an English-language official site and an English Tabelog listing, and the cafe sits in the heart of the very tourist-facing Kaminarimon/Sensoji area.

places.name: 光醤 (Hikari Hishio)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles in a tonkotsu-shoyu (soy sauce) broth; no gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or any celiac-specific accommodation was found in any source. Not recommended for gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base, and the standard bowl includes non-vegan toppings (ajitsuke tamago soft-boiled egg, chashu pork). No vegan or vegetarian menu items, broth, or substitutions were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:00〜翌7:00、無休 (10:00 to 7:00 the next morning, open every day)
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (near Shibuya Station west/Inokashira-lin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 光醤 is one of a small cluster of Shibuya ramen shops (alongside らーめん金伝丸 and 男旭山) run by the same local operator out of the Dogenzaka/Shibuya Mark City area, rather than a national chain. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: owner/operator (name not published in sources found)
chef_bio.background: 光醤 is one of a small cluster of Shibuya ramen shops (alongside らーめん金伝丸 and 男旭山) run by the same local operator out of the Dogenzaka/Shibuya Mark City area, rather than a national chain.
chef_bio.philosophy: Build the bowl around genuinely good shoyu (soy sauce) rather than treating shoyu as an afterthought to the pork-bone broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Hakata-style tonkotsu-shoyu ramen (thin noodles, adjustable firmness) with unlimited free kaedama noodle refills

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, 光醤 (Hikari Hishio — 'light' + hishio, an old word for a fermented seasoning), is said to come from the owner being struck by how beautiful fresh soy sauce looked catching the light at a shoyu brewery, which inspired him to try to make what he considered Japan's best tonkotsu-shoyu ramen.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, counter-style Japanese ramen shop with no evidence of an English menu — comfortable for diners with basic Japanese or ramen-shop-ordering experience, less so for those needing English support.

places.name: 肉汁うどん武讃 (Nikujiru Udon Busan)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu is built around udon, i.e., wheat-flour noodles (both the Sanuki-style noodle and the housemade Musashino-style flat "ichi-tanki" noodle), so there is no gluten-free noodle option. The dipping broth (tsuyu) is also almost certainly seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-based, adding a second source of gluten. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dishes are "nikujiru" (meat-broth) udon featuring pork as a core ingredient, and the dipping broth is likely built on a bonito/katsuo-based dashi in addition to the pork. No vegan or vegetarian menu item, substitution, or plant-based broth option was found in any source describing the menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00, 17:30-21:30, daily, no regular closed days
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Ogawamachi, Chiyoda (Ogawamachi/Jimbocho area)
places.notes: Busan is a newer concept from 株式会社のみもの (Nomimono Co., Ltd.), a Tokyo restaurant group best known for quirky specialty-noodle concepts such as "Curry is a Beverage" and "Why Put Chili Oil in Soba?". The shop opened on March 30, 2026 in Kanda-Ogawamachi, taking over the space that  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Udon specialty shop under the Nomimono restaurant group
chef_bio.background: Busan is a newer concept from 株式会社のみもの (Nomimono Co. , Ltd. ), a Tokyo restaurant group best known for quirky specialty-noodle concepts such as "Curry is a Beverage" and "Why Put Chili Oil in Soba? ". The shop opened on March 30, 2026 in Kanda-Ogawamachi, taking over the space that had previously housed the group's own "Why Put Chili Oil in Soba? Kanda-Ogawamachi" branch.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "Busan" (武讃) is a portmanteau of Musashino (武蔵野) and Sanuki (讃岐), reflecting the shop's premise of fusing Tokyo's rustic, home-style Musashino udon with the smoother, more refined noodle texture of Sanuki udon from Kagawa — an East-meets-West "hybrid udon" mashup.
chef_bio.specialty: Nikujiru udon (肉汁うどん) — a dipping-style udon that pairs smooth, firm Sanuki-style noodles with a hearty, vegetable- and pork-laden dipping broth in the Musashino udon tradition.

anecdotes.text: For the two-day opening event on March 30-31, 2026, everyone who ordered a main noodle dish received a free deep-fried chikuwa (fish cake) tempura.

A diner described the noodles as slightly thin, soft Sanuki-style udon eaten in a hearty Musashino-style dipping broth, noting that the leftover udon-yu (noodle cooking water) is kombu (kelp) water, which tastes like kenchin-jiru when used to dilute the remaining broth.

One reviewer noted the dipping broth is so packed with pork and vegetables (eggplant, negi, shiitake) that there's barely room left to dip the noodles, and that a single thick "himokawa" flat noodle is included on top for fun.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine at the entrance, and no English menu or signage has been reported by visitors.

places.name: しゅぞう (Shuzo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi were mentioned in any source. The signature dishes (chicken nanban is breaded and deep-fried with a wheat-based tartar sauce; teishoku sets rely on standard soy sauce and dashi) point to wheat throughout the menu, with no evidence of substitutions. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from the shop by phone.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and fish teishoku (chicken nanban, pork shabu-shabu, salmon sashimi, whitebait rice bowls) with standard dashi (likely bonito-based). No vegetarian or vegan option, dashi substitution, or plant-based mention appears in any source, so it should be treated as not accommodating for vegan diners without calling ahead.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 17:30-23:00 (Mon-Fri); closed Sat, Sun, and public holidays
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (Shinjuku Nishiguchi), Shinjuku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on March 16, 2015 by an owner couple who took over an existing izakaya space in the Nishi-Shinjuku backstreets, a few minutes' walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, and had it redesigned around reclaimed old-house timber. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened on March 16, 2015 by an owner couple who took over an existing izakaya space in the Nishi-Shinjuku backstreets, a few minutes' walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, and had it redesigned around reclaimed old-house timber.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the idea of 'banshaku to banmeshi' (an evening drink and an evening meal) - a small, unpretentious neighborhood counter where someone can drop in alone for a drink or settle in at a table with company.
chef_bio.specialty: Shuzo teishoku (the daily changing set meal) - rotating home-style dishes such as crispy chicken nanban with housemade tartar sauce, a shirasu (whitebait) rice bowl, pork shabu-shabu, or salmon sashimi, served with miso soup and a choice of rice portion.

anecdotes.text: The interior was redesigned by Nagano-based Sansui-sha using 'kobitsu' - reclaimed pillars and beams salvaged from century-old dismantled farmhouses - worked into the existing layout of the space to give the small room a warmer, wood-lined feel.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, Japanese-only neighborhood counter spot with no English menu or site found, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help when ordering.

places.name: 尾張屋 本店 (Owariya Honten (Owariya Main Store))
places.gf_detail: No source confirms a 100%-buckwheat (juwari/生粉打ち) noodle, a gluten-free menu, or a dedicated gluten-free fryer here, despite specific searches for these terms. The restaurant markets its noodles as made from premium domestic (Shinshu-centered) soba flour, but does not state the buckwheat-to-wheat ratio, and traditional soba shops commonly use a wheat binder (nihachi-style) unless juwari is explicitly advertised. The tempura batter is standard wheat-flour batter, and the soy-based tsuyu is likely to contain wheat via shoyu, though this specific fact was not independently confirmed for this shop. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-3845-4500) or ask staff in person about the buckwheat ratio and kitchen handling before ordering — do not assume this is a safe gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: The signature tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) is built on bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, per the restaurant's own description of its "trio" of broth, tempura, and noodles — this is an animal-based stock used throughout the menu. No vegan-adapted broth or dish was found advertised anywhere, so vegan and vegetarian diners should not expect accommodation without directly and skeptically confirming with staff.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-20:00 (L.O.), Thursdays 11:30-16:00; closed Fridays
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草 (Asakusa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1860 (Man'en 1, the last year of the Edo period) on Kaminarimon-dori near Sensoji Temple, Owariya has been run by the same family for six generations across more than 160 years, serving Asakusa locals and Sensoji pilgrims. According to the shop's own account, the name  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kaminarimon-dori, Asakusa, Taito-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1860 (Man'en 1, the last year of the Edo period) on Kaminarimon-dori near Sensoji Temple, Owariya has been run by the same family for six generations across more than 160 years, serving Asakusa locals and Sensoji pilgrims. According to the shop's own account, the name comes from Shinmon Tatsugoro — the Edo-era fire brigade chief who also served as a gatekeeper of Sensoji — who suggested a name evoking the vigorous 'spread of a shrimp's tail' when the founder left firefighting to open the restaurant. The shop displays a 'Sensoji Goyo' (Official Purveyor to Sensoji Temple) sign, reflecting its long-standing ties to the temple.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its aim as 'a flavor you don't tire of even eating it every day,' built on what it calls a trio of essentials: a bonito-based tsuyu, shrimp tempura fried in specially ordered sesame oil, and firm noodles made from top-grade domestic (Shinshu-centered) buckwheat flour.
chef_bio.specialty: Tempura soba (天ぷらそば) topped with two oversized shrimp tempura, fried in specially sourced sesame oil, so large they spill over the edge of the bowl

anecdotes.text: The novelist Nagai Kafu, a devoted Asakusa regular, is said to have eaten here every single day for a year. He always took the same reserved back seat, and the proprietress was instructed to serve him kashiwa nanban (chicken soba) without even taking his order once tea was poured. He ate in near-silence and left payment in coins lined up neatly along the edge of the table before leaving.

A photograph of Kafu eating at the restaurant — taken secretly by a fan, since the famously camera-shy writer disliked being photographed — was later given to the shop and still hangs inside as a memento of his patronage.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, tourist-frequented shop steps from Sensoji with an English-language Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.

places.name: 博多串焼き・野菜巻き工房 渋谷宮益坂のごりょんさん (Hakata Kushiyaki / Yasaimaki Kobo Shibuya Mi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The menu centers on grilled skewers glazed with tare (soy-based sauce), steamed gyoza (wheat dumpling wrappers), and motsunabe hot pot — all standard vehicles for wheat via soy sauce and dashi bases. There is no mention of a dedicated fryer, gluten-free soy sauce, or allergy protocol. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused izakaya whose signature dish is vegetables wrapped around domestic pork belly and grilled — the vegetables function as a wrapping for pork, not a standalone vegan dish. The rest of the menu (motsunabe offal hot pot, other pork/chicken skewers) is similarly meat-centered, and Japanese izakaya sauces/stocks routinely use bonito-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu, labeling, or options were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–23:30 daily (some listings show 17:30–24:00); no regular closed day (無休)
places.neighborhood: 宮益坂/渋谷 (Miyamasuzaka, Shibuya)
places.cuisine: 博多串焼き・野菜巻き / Hakata-style grilled skewers wi
places.notes: Goryon-san is operated by Basix Co., Ltd., a Tokyo restaurant group led by representative director Hiroshi Iwasawa (known as "Gan-san") that also runs the atmospheric izakaya brands Teyan-tei, Jomon, and Meatman. The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka branch opened on April 19 in a deliberatel · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Operated by Basix Co., Ltd., under representative director Hiroshi Iwasawa
chef_bio.origin: Hakata (Fukuoka) izakaya cuisine
chef_bio.background: Goryon-san is operated by Basix Co. , Ltd. , a Tokyo restaurant group led by representative director Hiroshi Iwasawa (known as "Gan-san") that also runs the atmospheric izakaya brands Teyan-tei, Jomon, and Meatman. The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka branch opened on April 19 in a deliberately low-visibility spot — reached via a stairway down to a basement level with no prominent signage — reflecting the group's stated approach of building each shop's individuality around food and hospitality rather than location, signage, or a manual-driven service style.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name, "Goryon-san," is a term of affection used in Hakata for a household's proprietress — a woman admired for her charm, virtue, kindness, and cooking skill during Hakata festivals. The restaurant frames its hospitality and cooking around that warm, home-style image.
chef_bio.specialty: Yasai-maki-gushi (野菜巻き串) — skewers of fresh vegetables wrapped in domestic pork belly and grilled; other signature items include steamed gyoza and Hakata motsunabe (offal hot pot).

anecdotes.text: The shop was deliberately opened on a quiet side street with no signage, reached down a basement stairway, as part of parent company Basix's philosophy of pursuing each shop's individual appeal through food and hospitality rather than location or manuals.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard Japanese-language izakaya chain outlet with no evidence of an English menu, picture menu, or foreign-visitor-oriented service — expect to order in Japanese or by pointing.

places.name: 酒場 たかや (Sakaba Takaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised anywhere. The hand-cut soba is not described as 十割 (100% buckwheat) in any source, so it should be assumed to contain wheat flour as a binder in the traditional nihachi style; the dipping sauce/seasonings are standard izakaya shoyu-based preparations. No mention of a dedicated fryer or any celiac-safe handling. Not recommended for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation is mentioned in any source. The menu centers on charcoal-grilled yakitori (chicken), grilled fish, and soba; typical izakaya dashi (bonito-based) and shoyu are used in seasoning. The one dish that could plausibly be plant-based — soba eaten with salt instead of dipping sauce — has not been confirmed as free of animal-derived ingredients in the noodle itself. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: 月(Mon): 17:00-23:30 (dinner only) / 火〜金(Tue-Fri): 11:45-14:00 (lunch), 17:00-23:30 (dinner) / 土・祝日(Sat/holidays): 17:30-23:30 / 日(Sun): 定休日 (closed)
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: Opened in February 2024 a few minutes from Suidōbashi Station's east exit, run as one of roughly 100 restaurants produced by the Osaka-based SASAYA Holdings restaurant group. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in February 2024 a few minutes from Suidōbashi Station's east exit, run as one of roughly 100 restaurants produced by the Osaka-based SASAYA Holdings restaurant group.
chef_bio.philosophy: Buckwheat is sourced by region and season for the hand-cut soba, paired with charcoal-grilled yakitori made from a rare chicken breed and a rotating list of 40-plus types of junmai sake.
chef_bio.specialty: "Takaya soba" — dark, coarsely-ground hand-cut buckwheat noodles served with natural salt from the Shirakami mountain range in place of the usual dipping sauce, so the buckwheat's own flavor comes through.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature dish is a dark, coarsely-ground hand-cut soba eaten with Shirakami-range natural salt instead of tsuyu dipping sauce, letting diners taste the noodle itself.
Described as a hideaway sake bar ("隠れ家的日本酒酒場") built around hand-cut soba, seasonal small plates, and sake from across Japan.

cultural_comfort.note: A locally-oriented sake and soba izakaya with no confirmed English menu or foreign-language signage; reservations are typically made by phone or via Japanese booking platforms.

places.name: フォーハノイ屋台33 ルミネエスト新宿店 (Pho Hanoi Yatai 33 Lumine Est Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: Pho itself is made from rice noodles, which are naturally wheat-free, and the menu includes 33 pho variations. However, no gluten-free certification, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free-soy-sauce statement was found; the same kitchen also produces wheat-based items (banh mi, fried spring rolls), so cross-contact risk for a strict celiac diet is unconfirmed and should be asked about directly.
places.vegan_detail: Menu includes a vegetable-based pho made without meat, and Tabelog tags the restaurant as offering vegan options. Table condiments (nuoc mam fish sauce, chili sauce, herb vinegar) appear to be self-added rather than built into the broth, which helps, but no source confirms the vegetable pho's stock is entirely free of fish sauce, dashi, or other animal-derived seasoning, so vegan diners should double-check the broth base with staff.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (last order around 21:30), reported hours follow Lumine Est Shinjuku's building hours; one source lists 11:00–21:45 (L.O. 21:15) — minor variation across listings, not clearly day-specific
places.neighborhood: 新宿南口・新宿駅東口 (Shinjuku Station area, inside Lumine Est Shinjuk
places.notes: Opened April 8, 2022 on the 8th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, billed by its operators as Japan's first restaurant built around a 33-variety pho menu. It's one location of a small Tokyo pho chain that also has branches in Nakano, Suginami and Kichijoji, and it sits about a 2-minut · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: branch of a small multi-location Tokyo pho chain
chef_bio.origin: Vietnam (Hanoi street-food concept)
chef_bio.background: Opened April 8, 2022 on the 8th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, billed by its operators as Japan's first restaurant built around a 33-variety pho menu. It's one location of a small Tokyo pho chain that also has branches in Nakano, Suginami and Kichijoji, and it sits about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's east side.
chef_bio.philosophy: Furniture and decor were imported directly from Vietnam to recreate the bright, colorful feel of a Hanoi street stall (yatai) inside a Shinjuku department-store food floor, pairing typical pho and bun with com ga, coconut-sweet-potato curry, congee sets, banh mi and spring rolls.
chef_bio.specialty: 33 different pho varieties, plus a signature whole coconut ice cream served scooped from the shell (a mango version is also offered)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's most talked-about item is a whole coconut ice cream scooped out of the coconut shell, with a mango variation also on the menu.
Marketed at launch as Japan's first restaurant format centered on a 33-item pho noodle lineup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: 特製スープは鶏ガラと豚骨、玉ねぎ、生姜、ベトナム調味料を長時間煮込んで旨味を抽出したもの — the house broth is chicken bone AND pork bone simmered with onion, ginger and Vietnamese seasonings, i.e. a shared animal-based stock; no meat-free vegan broth is documented.

各フォーメニューにはハーフの生春巻き、揚げパン、追い野菜、小さなデザートが付きます — every pho set is served with a half fresh spring roll, deep-fried wheat bread (揚げパン), extra vegetables and a small dessert, placing fried wheat and a spring roll on the celiac's own dish by default.

Menu confirmed to include banh mi, 生春巻き (fresh spring rolls) and fried items produced in the same kitchen; no gluten-free, allergen, dedicated-fryer, or wheat-free seasoning statement found on the shop site or any listing.

General Vietnamese-pho gluten guidance notes soy sauce, hoisin and fish sauce commonly used with pho can contain gluten — relevant because this shop's broth uses unspecified 'Vietnamese seasonings' with no wheat-free assurance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tabelog lists the restaurant's services as including both allergen accommodations and vegan options.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Restaurant is tagged on Tabelog for allergen accommodation, but the specific scope (which allergens, how requests are handled) was not confirmed from the sources available.

positives.text: A vegetable-based pho made without meat is offered as a regular menu item, positioned as a lighter option among the 33 pho varieties.

Condiments including chili sauce, herb vinegar and nuoc mam (fish sauce) are set out at the table for diners to add themselves rather than being mixed into dishes beforehand, giving diners some control over fish sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: A mall-floor restaurant right at Shinjuku Station with a Tabelog English listing and card/QR payment, so it's easy for travelers, though an on-site English menu is not confirmed.

places.name: POTA PASTA 渋谷道玄坂店（ポタ パスタ 渋谷道玄坂店） (POTA PASTA Shibuya Dogenzaka)
places.gf_detail: All noodles are a proprietary fresh wheat pasta developed using Sanuki-udon kneading and aging techniques applied to durum wheat — this is a wheat-based product with no gluten-free noodle alternative found in any source. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or preparation process was mentioned anywhere. Not appropriate for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The two signature dishes highlighted in press coverage are an egg-and-cream carbonara and an A4–A5 wagyu bolognese, both animal-product-based; egg content of the house noodle itself is not documented. No vegan menu, plant-based substitution option, or vegan labeling was found in any source. A strict vegan would need to ask staff directly about a plain tomato-based option, with no guarantee of accommodation.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-16:00, 17:00-22:00 (some listings show a continuous 11:30-22:00, L.O. 21:30); 無休 (open daily, no fixed closed days)
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya — about a 1-minute walk from Shibuy
places.notes: Before founding POTA PASTA, Ebato ran a Sanuki-udon specialty shop. He applied udon noodle-making techniques — 'fumikomi' (treading/kneading) and 'jukusei' (aging) — to durum wheat to develop a proprietary fresh pasta noodle, aiming to bring authentic hand-made-style pasta down t · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 江波戸千洋 (Ebato Chihiro), representative of parent company Echigoya Co.
chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo — opened 2016 next to Shibuya Mark City
chef_bio.background: Before founding POTA PASTA, Ebato ran a Sanuki-udon specialty shop. He applied udon noodle-making techniques — 'fumikomi' (treading/kneading) and 'jukusei' (aging) — to durum wheat to develop a proprietary fresh pasta noodle, aiming to bring authentic hand-made-style pasta down to fast-food prices in a market he felt was stuck at roughly 1,000 yen per plate because dried pasta takes too long to cook for fast table turnover.
chef_bio.philosophy: Standardize every step — the fresh noodles and sauces are made off-site and flash-frozen, then simply boiled and combined to order — so that any staff member can consistently produce good pasta at very low prices (dishes historically started around 330–560 yen), making 'real' fresh pasta an everyday, fast-casual option near Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.specialty: Kiseki no Bolognese ("Miracle Bolognese") made with A4–A5 grade Japanese wagyu, and the shop's signature Ultimate Carbonara (究極のカルボナーラ) with a rich egg-and-cream sauce

anecdotes.text: Ebato developed the thick, chewy fresh pasta using know-how from his earlier Sanuki-udon shop, applying udon kneading and aging techniques to durum wheat as a proprietary product.

POTA PASTA was designed from the menu-development stage to fully standardize cooking so that any staff member makes the same dish — including a fully manualized method for the creamy egg-and-cream carbonara sauce.
The shop offers both thick and thin house-made noodles; the thick noodle is prized for its extra-chewy, edged mochi-mochi texture, the thin for how well it carries sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine with photo menus and no reservations needed, so pointing and basic numbers get you through even without English, but don't expect English-speaking staff for detailed allergy questions.

places.name: カリーライス専門店エチオピア 本店 (Curry Rice Specialist Ethiopia (Honten / Mai)
places.gf_detail: Multiple independent food-writer accounts describe the housemade curry roux as prepared without wheat flour — it is thickened naturally by long-simmering a vegetable (celery, onion) and spice puree rather than using a flour-based roux, which is a positive sign for those avoiding wheat. However, this is not an official allergen disclosure from the restaurant: there is no confirmed information on whether other seasonings (e.g., soy sauce) contain wheat, and no published cross-contamination protocol. Those with celiac disease should ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: All standard curry varieties — including the vegetable curry (野菜カリー) and bean curry (豆カリー) — appear to share a common base built by simmering the vegetable-spice paste with meat stock/extract, according to multiple independent food-writer descriptions of the cooking process. No dedicated vegan menu item or plant-based-broth option has been identified, so this is not a safe choice for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho / Kanda-Ogawamachi, Chiyoda-ku — 3 min walk f
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ethiopia started life in 1988 as a coffee-and-curry café. Its Ethiopian coffee blend proved so popular with customers that the shop took the name 'Ethiopia' for itself, even though the curry served is Indian-style rather than Ethiopian. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened in 1988 (Showa 63) in Kanda-Jimbocho, one of Tokyo's most curry-competitive neighborhoods.
chef_bio.background: Ethiopia started life in 1988 as a coffee-and-curry café. Its Ethiopian coffee blend proved so popular with customers that the shop took the name 'Ethiopia' for itself, even though the curry served is Indian-style rather than Ethiopian.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner-chef Kenji Suzuki has kept the 1988 recipe unchanged, insisting on made-from-scratch spice blending and fresh vegetables rather than a store-bought curry roux — an unwavering commitment to not compromising on the curry's quality or flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Curry rice built on a housemade, flour-free roux — a large batch of celery-and-onion paste sautéed with spices and simmered for about three days until the vegetables melt down into a naturally thick, silky sauce, finished with 12 spices. Diners choose from chicken, beef, shrimp, bean, or vegetable curry, and can dial the spice level up in steps (famously as high as 70x), which has made it a rite of passage for Tokyo spice enthusiasts as well as a lunchtime staple for office workers and students in the Jimbocho/Kanda area.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1988 as a coffee-and-curry café; its name comes from an Ethiopian coffee blend that was popular with early customers.
The curry base is a large batch of celery-and-onion paste cooked with spices and rested about three days, so the vegetables dissolve into a naturally thickened, flour-free roux.
Spice levels can be ordered far beyond standard, with regulars working their way up toward a legendary '70x' heat level over repeated visits.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Official site carries a per-menu allergen chart listing wheat and soybeans as allergens present: 'ALL MENU such as shrimp, wheat, eggs, dairy, cashew nut, beef, soybeans, chicken, banana, pork, yam, lamb, apple, gelatin, sesame, are all displays as follows' — confirming wheat IS present somewhere in the menu, so a wheat-flour-free roux does not equal gluten-free.
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://www.ethiopia-curry.com/ (allergen chart, quoted via search snippet)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking process confirmed as vegetable-spice paste simmered with meat extract over ~3 days ('肉のエキスでじっくり煮込み'), and this base underlies the vegetable curry and bean curry — confirms vegan 'no' and shows the base is a stock-based sauce that may also carry soy-sauce-derived wheat.

General Japanese curry allergen guidance: soybean listing in a curry base commonly reflects soy sauce or Worcestershire sauce used as a hidden seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and is not gluten-free — a real, unmeasured gluten vector here.

positives.text: The curry roux is made without wheat flour, thickened naturally from a long-simmered puree of celery, onion, and other vegetables with 12 spices rather than a store-bought roux.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, very popular independent curry shop with a Japanese-language menu and no confirmed English-language support, but simple to navigate (order by curry type and spice level) and well used to first-time and international visitors given its fame.

places.name: ビストロ コロリス (Bistro COLORIS)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation area, or gluten-free statement has been documented. As a classic French bistro, dishes commonly involve roux-thickened sauces and house-baked bread served alongside courses, both of which typically contain wheat. Diners with celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity should call ahead (03-5389-1441) and confirm precautions directly with the chef before booking.
places.vegan_detail: Course menus are built around fish and meat mains with butter- and cream-based French sauces; a vegetable salad with house-made dressing typically appears as a starter, but no vegan menu, dedicated vegan course, or hidden-animal-ingredient disclosure (e.g., in stocks or sauces) has been documented. Vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly to discuss what, if anything, can be adapted.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat and days before public holidays: Lunch 12:00-14:30 (food L.O. 12:30, drink L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30-21:00 (food L.O. 19:00, drink L.O. 20:00). Closed Sundays and public holidays (private party bookings can extend outside these hours).
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku / Seibu Shinjuku Station area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Kawase Aya trained at a culinary school in the United Kingdom, traveled extensively through France to eat and learn regional cooking, and later honed her craft at French restaurants associated with renowned sommelier Shinya Tasaki as well as at Hotel Okura Nihonbashi before  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Chef Kawase Aya trained at a culinary school in the United Kingdom, traveled extensively through France to eat and learn regional cooking, and later honed her craft at French restaurants associated with renowned sommelier Shinya Tasaki as well as at Hotel Okura Nihonbashi before opening her own bistro.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the idea that guests should be able to "eat plenty of vegetables" alongside carefully chosen wine, served with warm, unpretentious hospitality rather than formality.
chef_bio.specialty: Colorful, vegetable-forward seasonal French bistro cuisine, with sauces, salad dressings, bread and desserts all made in-house by the chef rather than bought in from suppliers.

anecdotes.text: The name "Coloris" plays on the French word for color/hue and on the chef's own name Aya (彩, which itself means "color"), reflecting the vividly colorful plates she creates.
Guests enter through an arched doorway into a small red-and-ivory dining room decorated like a storybook interior, which has made the bistro a popular choice for birthdays and anniversaries.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-language service has been documented; this is a small neighborhood French bistro run day-to-day in Japanese, so non-Japanese-speaking diners should be prepared to point at a menu or use a translation app, especially for allergy questions.

places.name: ラーメン二郎 新宿歌舞伎町店 (Ramen Jiro Shinjuku Kabukicho)
places.gf_detail: Ramen is built entirely around wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare; there is no gluten-free noodle substitute, no dedicated gluten-free prep area, and no indication the kitchen accommodates gluten-free requests. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based, finished with pork fatback/lard and topped with fatty chashu pork; there is no vegetable-broth or plant-based option. Only the vegetable toppings (cabbage, bean sprouts, garlic) are themselves plant-based, but the dish as served is not vegan or vegetarian.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-翌2:30 (11:30am-2:30am next day), closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho, Shinjuku)
places.notes: Ramen Jiro traces its roots to a yatai (street stall) started around 1968 by Takami Yamada near Keio University in Mita, Minato-ku, which grew into the original honten (main shop) and, later, a network of independently run 'chokkei' (direct-lineage) branches trained in the founde · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Mita, Minato-ku, Tokyo (birthplace of the original Ramen Jiro honten)
chef_bio.background: Ramen Jiro traces its roots to a yatai (street stall) started around 1968 by Takami Yamada near Keio University in Mita, Minato-ku, which grew into the original honten (main shop) and, later, a network of independently run 'chokkei' (direct-lineage) branches trained in the founder's method. The Kabukicho shop opened on 1997-08-11 as a company-operated ('foods-kei') branch, and was uniquely permitted to keep the 'Jiro' name after the trademark was formally registered in 2003 (when other non-lineage branches had to drop it). In October 2016 it relocated to its current Kabukicho 2-chome address under new management and converted to full chokkei (direct-lineage) status, with an owner-chef trained in the Jiro method.
chef_bio.specialty: Jiro-style tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: thick, chewy flat noodles in a pork-bone and soy-sauce broth, topped with two slices of fatty chashu pork, garlic, and a towering mound of cabbage and bean sprouts, customizable via the shop's 'call' system (amount of garlic, vegetables, fat, and sauce strength).

anecdotes.text: Of the original company-run 'foods-kei' branches (which also included Shimbashi, Kanda, and Toranomon), the Kabukicho shop was the sole holdout allowed to keep operating under the 'Jiro' name after the 2003 trademark registration, until it converted to a true direct-lineage (chokkei) shop in 2016.
Staying open into the small hours (last order around 2:30am), the shop has become known as a late-night refuge in Kabukicho, drawing nightlife workers such as hosts and hostesses after their shifts.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/tare is described as a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone and soy-sauce) base; standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based, and no wheat-free tamari alternative is mentioned anywhere.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu and ticket machine are Japanese-only and ordering follows an unwritten 'call' etiquette (specifying garlic/vegetable/fat amounts) that first-time and non-Japanese-speaking diners generally need to learn from a guide beforehand.

places.name: シャンティ スープカリー（渋谷店） (Shanti Soup Curry (Shibuya))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or allergen chart was found for this location. The curry soup itself is broth-based rather than roux-thickened, but seasonings likely include soy sauce (typically wheat-containing in Japan), and naan (wheat) is offered as a side alongside rice. No information was found on shared fryers, cross-contamination protocols, or a gluten-free-safe soup base. Guests needing strict gluten avoidance should ask staff directly about the specific soup and seasoning ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The signature soups are built on chicken stock enriched with pork back fat, so the standard broth is not vegan. Some vegetable-heavy curry preparations exist, and the menu has been described as accommodating to vegetarians, but no vegan-specific broth, dashi-free option, or confirmation of animal-free stock was found. Vegans should ask staff directly whether any vegetable curry is made without meat stock, dashi, or animal fat.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（道玄坂／渋谷マークシティ付近）
places.cuisine: スープカレー・野菜料理
places.notes: Founded by an owner surnamed Uemura, who traveled to Hokkaido to sample the region's soup curry, fell in love with the dish, and opened a Sapporo-style soup curry shop in Tokyo in November 2003 — reportedly the second such shop to expand from Sapporo into the Kanto region. The Sh · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Sapporo, Hokkaido style, brought to Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded by an owner surnamed Uemura, who traveled to Hokkaido to sample the region's soup curry, fell in love with the dish, and opened a Sapporo-style soup curry shop in Tokyo in November 2003 — reportedly the second such shop to expand from Sapporo into the Kanto region. The Shibuya branch later relocated a short distance within Dogenzaka (to the basement of the Shibuya Dai-ichi Building near Shibuya Mark City) after the building housing its original location was torn down for area redevelopment.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Shanti is Sanskrit for 'peace and tranquility. ' The company's stated purpose is to make people around the world happy through a single bowl of soup curry.
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken and vegetable soup curry — a broth built from 20-plus spices and medicinal herbs, enriched with pork back fat for body, served with meltingly tender slow-cooked chicken and colorful vegetables (taro, paprika, and others); spice level is adjustable across more than 40 steps, and a milder, clearer 'Saigon' broth is offered alongside the original.

anecdotes.text: Owner Uemura discovered soup curry on a trip to Hokkaido and was so captivated that he opened what is described as Tokyo's second Sapporo-style soup curry restaurant in November 2003.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No confirmation found on whether soy sauce or other seasonings used are wheat-free; standard Japanese shoyu (which contains wheat) is likely used unless confirmed otherwise by staff.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward curry options (e.g. taro, paprika, and other vegetables) exist alongside the signature chicken soup curry, and at least one review characterized the restaurant as offering 'a good choice for vegetarians and meat eaters.'

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and ordering is simplified by a numbered spice-level system, making it approachable for non-Japanese speakers, though there is no dedicated English allergy/ingredient guide.

places.name: スープデリ (Soup Deli)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free roux, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. This is a Western-style curry and beef-stew shop; curry roux and stew bases of this type are typically wheat-thickened and often use ordinary (wheat-containing) soy sauce. Celiac or gluten-avoidant diners should not assume safety here and would need to ask staff directly, though no evidence suggests staff are set up to accommodate such requests.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were identified. The shop's known dishes (beef stew, curry with meat) are animal-based, and no source mentioned vegetable-only or dashi/bonito-free preparations. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation, and even then likely limited.
