This is a Western-style curry and beef-stew shop; curry roux and stew bases of this type are typically wheat-thickened and often use ordinary (wheat-containing) soy sauce. Celiac or glut · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: カレー・洋食・デリのお店 / 夜はダイニングバー
chef_bio.specialty: ビーフシチュー(beef stew) — 定番の人参とじゃがいも、大ぶりでじっくり煮込んだ牛肉がゴロゴロ入り、ワインでまろやかに仕立てたシチュー。 ランチには喫茶店風の特製カレーライスやお弁当も人気。

anecdotes.text: 手描き風の看板に描かれたおじさんの似顔絵に惹かれて入店したという常連客のレビューがあり、個人経営らしい親しみやすい佇まいが伝わる。
ランチには手作りのお弁当もあり、テイクアウトにも対応していると紹介されている。
昼は喫茶店風の特製カレーライスを提供し、夜になるとバーに姿を変える二面性のある店として紹介されている。

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu, website, or English-language service was found in any source; expect a Japanese-only, locally-oriented small shop.

places.name: そば処 信州屋 新宿南口店 (Shinshuya Shinjuku Minamiguchi-ten (Soba-dok)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option or 100%-buckwheat (juwari) soba is mentioned by any source; the shop's own description only says the noodles are made in-house from stone-ground Shinshu soba flour via an extrusion noodle machine, with no claim of being wheat-free. Tempura (a signature item, fried fresh to order) is wheat-battered, and the tsuyu dipping sauce in standard tachigui soba shops of this type is soy-sauce (wheat-containing) and bonito-dashi based. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or option is mentioned by any source found. As a standard soba-ya, the tsuyu (dipping/soup sauce) is very likely bonito (katsuo) dashi-based per typical practice at this style of shop, and no plant-based dashi alternative is advertised. Not confirmed vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 7:30-23:30、土・日・祝 7:30-23:00（無休）※出典によりわずかに終了時刻の記載が異なる（23:00〜23:30の幅）
places.neighborhood: 新宿駅南口 / 新宿区新宿3丁目
places.notes: 信州屋は、大正2年（1913年）創業の老舗企業クマガイコーポレーション株式会社が展開するそば専門店ブランドで、新宿南口店は新宿駅南口・新宿三丁目エリアの店舗。石臼で挽いた信州（長野）産そば粉を使い、押出式の製麺機で自家製麺を作り、注文ごとに茹でたてを提供する立ち食い・着席兼用スタイルの店。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 立ち食い・着席兼用のそば専門店（チェーン店舗）
chef_bio.origin: 長野県（信州）産そば粉を使用
chef_bio.background: 信州屋は、大正2年（1913年）創業の老舗企業クマガイコーポレーション株式会社が展開するそば専門店ブランドで、新宿南口店は新宿駅南口・新宿三丁目エリアの店舗。 石臼で挽いた信州（長野）産そば粉を使い、押出式の製麺機で自家製麺を作り、注文ごとに茹でたてを提供する立ち食い・着席兼用スタイルの店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「打ちたて、茹でたて、揚げたて」をモットーに、安心・安全でおいしいそばを、忙しいビジネスパーソンにも手早く提供する。 器には有田焼（唐子文様）を用いるなど、立ち食いそばながら質にもこだわる。
chef_bio.specialty: 打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたてのもりそば／かけそばと、目の前で揚げるかき揚げ天ぷら。 季節限定で根付き天然せりそばなどの限定メニューも登場する。

anecdotes.text: 季節限定の「根付き天然せりそば」（鰹出汁の温つゆに天然の根付きせりと豚バラ肉）が、立ち食いそば屋の水準を超えていると評された。
冷しかき揚げそば（430円)など、コストパフォーマンスの良いメニューと、コシのある麺の食感が実食レビューで評価されている。

positives.text: Tempura and soba are made fresh to order in-house (stone-ground soba flour, extrusion noodle-making, boiled to order) rather than pre-made, per the operator's own description of its process.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a fast, order-at-the-register tachigui-style soba shop aimed at local office workers with no ticket machine (pay after ordering) and no confirmed English menu, so some basic Japanese or pointing at a photo menu will help.

places.name: カールヴァーン トウキョウ (CARVAAN TOKYO)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, certification, or cross-contamination protocol was found in any available source. Mediterranean/Arabian cuisine here includes wheat-based items (pita-style bread, grain-based sides), so gluten exposure is likely on several dishes even where they are vegan/halal. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten sensitivity should call ahead or ask staff in person to confirm ingredients and kitchen handling before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Select menu items are explicitly marked vegan and halal — including falafel, hummus, and tahini/harissa-style dips, plus a vegan version of the chicken curry — but the overall menu is meat-inclusive (lamb, chicken, merguez sausage, tagines), so it is not a vegan restaurant. Vegan diners should confirm dish-by-dish with staff. No mention of hidden dashi/bonito was found in any source; as this is Mediterranean/Arabian rather than Japanese cuisine, that specific risk is less likely but was not explicitly documented either way.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / Afternoon Tea 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:00) / Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Weekends & holidays: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / Afternoon Tea 14:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:00) / Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). One later search snippet cited slightly different lunch hours of 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 14:30) daily with dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00) — treated as the same overall continuous 11:00-21:00 operating window. No regular closed day found in any source.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷)
places.notes: CARVAAN TOKYO is the Shibuya branch of the CARVAAN restaurant group (operated by FAR EAST Inc.), which also runs locations in Hanno and Yokohama. It opened in November 2019 on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, the skyscraper directly connected to Shibuya Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Shibuya branch of the CARVAAN restaurant group
chef_bio.origin: Mediterranean/Arabian cuisine, with a brand concept tracing food and drink history back to ancient Mesopotamia
chef_bio.background: CARVAAN TOKYO is the Shibuya branch of the CARVAAN restaurant group (operated by FAR EAST Inc. ), which also runs locations in Hanno and Yokohama. It opened in November 2019 on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, the skyscraper directly connected to Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The CARVAAN brand concept traces the shared origins of food and drink to ancient Mesopotamia, where wheat cultivation, bread-baking and beer fermentation began, and frames the restaurant as a way to reflect that layered history and culture through Mediterranean and Arabian cooking adapted with umami for Japanese palates.
chef_bio.specialty: Mediterranean/Arabian dishes such as hummus, falafel, merguez sausage, tagine, and makbous (spiced rice)

anecdotes.text: The interior is designed as 'an exotic, non-everyday Arabian space overlooking Tokyo's skyline,' with 13th-century Mamluk Sultan Baibars-style brass lamps hanging from the ceiling and antique decorative plates and wall lamps, evoking an Egyptian palace.
CARVAAN TOKYO opened November 1, 2019 inside the newly built Shibuya Scramble Square, introduced in the opening press release as a Mediterranean/Arabian restaurant.
Almost 90% of the menu is marked halal/Muslim-friendly, and reviewers have singled out dishes like the burger as standouts for Muslim diners.

positives.text: CARVAAN TOKYO is certified as a Muslim-friendly shop by the Islamic Center of Japan; the kitchen uses naturally derived, halal-certified ingredients imported from around the world and states dishes contain no chemical seasonings, alcohol, or pork-derived ingredients.
Specific menu items are explicitly labeled vegan and halal, including the falafel, hummus and tahini-based dips, and a vegan version of the chicken curry.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available and the restaurant is Muslim-friendly certified by the Islamic Center of Japan, so staff are accustomed to international and tourist diners.

places.name: いろり庵きらく そば 水道橋店 (Iroriann Kiraku Soba - Suidobashi Branch)
places.gf_detail: Per the chain's own published allergen advisory, soba noodles are boiled in the same pot as udon and Chinese-style (ramen) noodles, both wheat-containing, so even a plain kake soba carries direct cross-contact. The tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) is soy-sauce based and prepared in a shared kitchen alongside wheat, soy, and mackerel-containing items; the advisory explicitly warns that allergens not listed as direct ingredients can still contaminate dishes via shared equipment. The noodle's exact flour ratio (whether 100% buckwheat) could not be independently confirmed. Overall: not safe for celiac or severe wheat-allergic diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-labeled items or plant-based broth alternative were found in any available source. The chain's tsuyu is a conventional soy-sauce based broth typical of Japanese tachigui soba shops (traditionally dashi built on bonito/katsuobushi), and tempura batter typically contains egg. Without an on-site confirmed vegan option, treat as not vegan-friendly.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町（水道橋駅東口）
places.cuisine: 立ち食いそば・うどん
places.notes: いろり庵きらくは、JR東日本グループのJR東日本クロスステーション フーズカンパニーが首都圏の駅構内・駅前で展開する立ち食いそばチェーン。水道橋店はJR水道橋駅東口改札外に直結する形で2022年7月18日にオープンした。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: JR東日本クロスステーション フーズカンパニーが運営する立ち食いそばチェーン「いろり庵きらく」の水道橋店
chef_bio.background: いろり庵きらくは、JR東日本グループのJR東日本クロスステーション フーズカンパニーが首都圏の駅構内・駅前で展開する立ち食いそばチェーン。 水道橋店はJR水道橋駅東口改札外に直結する形で2022年7月18日にオープンした。
chef_bio.philosophy: 生の蕎麦を店舗で都度茹で上げて提供するスタイルにこだわり、そばとの相性を追求した特製つゆを使う、というのがチェーン共通のコンセプトとして案内されている。
chef_bio.specialty: 茹でたての生そばと、注文を受けてから揚げる自家製かき揚げなど各種天ぷらをのせたそば・うどん。

anecdotes.text: 水道橋店は、そばを自動で茹で上げてすすぐ「そばロボット」を導入したいろり庵きらくの5店舗目となった。1時間あたり約150食分の茹で上げが可能で、茹で時間は約100秒とされる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Udon and Chinese-style (wheat) noodles are boiled in the same pot as the soba noodles, so those with wheat allergy or celiac disease need to be careful even with a plain soba order.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu, which contains soy sauce, is prepared in a shared kitchen alongside items containing wheat, soy, and mackerel; the chain's allergen chart notes that allergens not listed as direct ingredients can still be introduced via shared equipment/facilities.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain (excluding a couple of named branches) publishes a company-wide allergen chart covering its menu and directs customers with concerns to check it or ask staff.

cultural_comfort.note: Order at a self-service ticket machine (typically Japanese-only or minimal English) and eat standing or at a quick counter; no table service and little English support should be expected.

places.name: はせ川 (Hasegawa)
places.gf_detail: Tonkatsu here is breaded in wheat panko and deep-fried to order; no gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free sauce option was found in any source. Traditional tonkatsu sauce and dipping sauces are also typically wheat/soy-based. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers entirely on pork tonkatsu and fried pork/seafood skewers (kushiage); no vegetarian or vegan dishes, or accommodation for plant-based diners, were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sundays and public holidays
places.neighborhood: Hanakawado, Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-run tonkatsu counter tucked in Asakusa's Hanakawado district, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station's north exit. The current generation keeps it a neighborhood institution: the owner and his son work the fryer while his wife runs the front of house, serving he · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small, family-run tonkatsu counter tucked in Asakusa's Hanakawado district, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station's north exit. The current generation keeps it a neighborhood institution: the owner and his son work the fryer while his wife runs the front of house, serving hearty set meals at old-school prices to a mostly local, regular clientele.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built around specially made panko and carefully sourced pork, fried using a low-temperature technique meant to keep thick-cut cutlets tender all the way through rather than just crisp on the outside. Alongside the classic tonkatsu sauce, the shop offers its own sesame-paste dipping sauce as a second way to eat the cutlet.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut rosu katsu (pork loin cutlet) teishoku - a noticeably thick loin cutlet fried low and slow for tenderness, served with rice, miso soup, shredded cabbage, and pickles.

anecdotes.text: A 2010 visitor blog described leaving "fully satisfied" after the thick-cut loin cutlet set, crediting the shop's low-temperature frying technique for keeping the thick meat tender.
A 2021 review titled it a spot with 'satisfyingly thick tonkatsu' worth the visit at everyday prices.
A TripAdvisor reviewer called it 'really good Tonkatsu and well worth the money,' noting a generous portion for around ¥1,050-1,350 and that staff manage a little English with visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: An old-school Asakusa counter shokudo with a Japanese-only feel — a review noted staff can manage a little English and gestures, but do not expect an English menu.

places.name: ウダガワ 成ル (Udagawa Naru)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi option is documented anywhere. As a traditional izakaya/teppanyaki restaurant, the soy sauce and dashi used in the signature dashimaki tamago and other dishes almost certainly contain wheat (regular shoyu), and the teppan grill is shared across all dishes, creating cross-contact risk. Anyone with celiac disease should call ahead and ask detailed questions before dining; do not assume any dish is safe by default.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based accommodation is documented. The signature dashimaki tamago is an egg dish made with traditional dashi (typically bonito/katsuobushi-based), and the broader izakaya/teppanyaki menu centers on meat and seafood. No source mentions a vegan or vegetarian option, kombu-only dashi, or substitutions. Strict vegans should not assume any accommodation without calling ahead.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-24:00 daily per most listings (one source lists 17:00-23:00); some sources note 不定休 (irregular closures) with New Year holidays (12/31-1/3) off.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（センター街・公園通り沿い、宇田川町）
places.notes: 「成ル」はエイジアキッチンの第1号店として1999年に渋谷駅近くで開業したが、渋谷駅南口の再開発に伴い2013年8月に一度閉店。同年11月10日、渋谷・宇田川町（センター街裏手）に場所を移し、鉄板焼きと日本酒の店「ウダガワ 成ル」としてリニューアルオープンした。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: エイジアキッチン（Asia Kitchen Co.）系列店
chef_bio.background: 「成ル」はエイジアキッチンの第1号店として1999年に渋谷駅近くで開業したが、渋谷駅南口の再開発に伴い2013年8月に一度閉店。 同年11月10日、渋谷・宇田川町（センター街裏手）に場所を移し、鉄板焼きと日本酒の店「ウダガワ 成ル」としてリニューアルオープンした。
chef_bio.specialty: 名物のだし巻き玉子（卵焼き）。 卵の種類・だしの濃さ（あっさり／甘め）・トッピングを選んで自分好みにカスタマイズできる一皿で、店の看板メニューとなっている。

anecdotes.text: エイジアキッチンの第1号店だった「成ル」は渋谷駅南口再開発のため2013年8月に閉店し、同年11月に宇田川町で鉄板焼き・日本酒の店として生まれ変わった。
名物のだし巻き玉子は卵の種類、だしの濃さ、トッピングを選んで自分好みに仕上げられる。

cultural_comfort.note: Primarily Japanese-language listings and menu; an English Tabelog page exists but no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese ability or translation help is useful.

places.name: 洋飲食 (Yoinshoku (Yō-Inshoku))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodations were mentioned in any available source. The menu is built around wheat-heavy yōshoku staples — napolitan pasta, curry with a flour-based roux, and likely breaded items typical of this style — making it unsuitable for a gluten-free or celiac diet as currently documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly: the signature dish is a butter-cooked omurice with chicken rice and often ham, and the rest of the menu centers on hamburg steak, curry, and napolitan pasta made with meat and dairy. No plant-based or vegetarian options were identified in any available source.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区 水道橋・神保町 (Nishikanda, between Suidobashi and Jimbocho st
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in July 2021 in Nishikanda, between Suidobashi and Jimbocho stations. The owner-chef is from Nepal and trained at Musashiya, a well-known old-school yōshoku (Western-style) restaurant in Shimbashi, before striking out on his own with this eight-seat counter shop. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nepal
chef_bio.background: Opened in July 2021 in Nishikanda, between Suidobashi and Jimbocho stations. The owner-chef is from Nepal and trained at Musashiya, a well-known old-school yōshoku (Western-style) restaurant in Shimbashi, before striking out on his own with this eight-seat counter shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name, Yōinshoku, is a coined word blending yōshoku (Western food) and inshoku (food and drink). It carries forward the butter-forward, showa-era yōshoku style learned at Musashiya while adding the chef's own playful, spice-leaning touches, such as a curry-spiked dry-curry omurice.
chef_bio.specialty: Omurice — a two-egg omelette laid over butter-scented chicken rice, served with a side of napolitan pasta and salad.

anecdotes.text: "ネパール人が営むユニークな洋食店。新橋のむさしやチルドレンですが、メガ盛りもあり、いかにも学生やリーマン受けしそう！" (A unique yōshoku shop run by a Nepali chef — a 'child' of Shimbashi's Musashiya, with hefty portions that appeal to students and office workers.)
A blog visit describes the butter aroma as reminiscent of Musashiya, while noting the omurice and chicken rice have become their own distinct dish under this chef.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat local counter shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho popular with students and office workers; no English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found, so be ready to order in Japanese or by pointing at photos.

places.name: 三国一 西口店 (Sangokuichi Nishiguchi-ten (Shinjuku West Ex)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's identity is built around hand-made wheat udon noodles, freshly pressed twice daily at their own nearby noodle factory — there is no gluten-free noodle substitute mentioned anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce (which typically contains wheat) and wheat-based dashi/seasonings are used throughout the menu. Not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional udon specialist known for meat-topped and dashi-based bowls (pork, beef, miso-braised, salad udon with mayonnaise-based dressing). No vegan-specific menu, plant-based dashi, or vegetarian labeling was found in any source; the broth is presumed to be conventional bonito/katsuo-based dashi as is standard for this style of shop.
places.neighborhood: 新宿西口 (Shinjuku Nishiguchi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sangokuichi traces back to 1954, when founder Hashimoto Ken went independent and opened a small (about 6-tsubo) shop near Shinjuku's east exit, first selling bags, then converting it into a ramen shop the following year. In 1965 the shop changed business again to become an udon r · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo (founded 1954/opened as udon shop in 1965)
chef_bio.background: Sangokuichi traces back to 1954, when founder Hashimoto Ken went independent and opened a small (about 6-tsubo) shop near Shinjuku's east exit, first selling bags, then converting it into a ramen shop the following year. In 1965 the shop changed business again to become an udon restaurant. The name '三国一' ("best of the three kingdoms") comes from a trip: returning by shinkansen from a Sanuki-udon tasting tour in Shikoku, the founder saw Mt. Fuji through the train window — a peak visible from three provinces (Shizuoka, Kanagawa, Yamanashi) — and named the shop after it. The company now runs three shops around Shinjuku Station (east exit, west exit, and the Island Tower location), all still hand-making their own noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything made in-house: noodles are hand-pressed twice daily at the company's own nearby noodle-making facility and delivered fresh, and the dressing, mayonnaise, and dashi used across the menu are all made from scratch on site.
chef_bio.specialty: Salad udon (サラダうどん) — cold udon topped with fresh vegetables, tuna or meat, and a house-made mayonnaise-based dressing

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from the founder spotting Mt. Fuji — visible across three provinces — from a shinkansen window while returning from a Sanuki-udon eating trip in Shikoku.

Salad udon, now a common menu item at udon shops across Japan, is said to have originated here roughly 40 years ago when the previous-generation owner invented it during a health-food-conscious era; it became the shop's signature best-seller.
A food writer compared the shop's cult following and hearty, no-frills style to Ramen Jiro, calling it 'the Jiro of udon.'

cultural_comfort.note: An old-school, mostly local udon shop near Shinjuku Station West Exit; a Tabelog English page and delivery-app listing exist, but basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.

places.name: シャーク コーヒー (SHARK COFFEE) (Shark Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Sweets and light food are curated by an organic consultant specifically to be gluten-free, additive-free and mostly vegan, including gluten-free cakes, pizza, hot dogs, curry bread, rice-flour carrot cake, and gluten-free melon pan. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, dedicated fryer, or formal cross-contamination protocol was documented in any source found, so those with celiac disease should confirm specific items with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Sweets and light food are largely vegan (curated by an organic consultant), including vegan carrot cake, baked cheesecake, coconut-raspberry pie, and lemon cake, and the chai latte is honey-free. However, the menu is not exclusively vegan — items such as Greek yogurt (dairy) also appear — so vegan diners should check individual dishes rather than assume the whole menu is animal-free.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-19:00（L.O.18:30）、定休日なし [via Retty/aggregator listings]
places.neighborhood: 宇田川町 / 渋谷PARCO周辺
places.cuisine: ヴィーガンカフェ・オーガニックコーヒー
places.notes: SHARK COFFEE opened in April 2023 on the Shibuya Park-dori side near Shibuya PARCO, LINE CUBE SHIBUYA and Yoyogi Park, inside a ground-floor space (Shibuya Homes 1F) that gives it an unusually spacious, sofa-lined layout for central Shibuya. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (opened 2023)
chef_bio.background: SHARK COFFEE opened in April 2023 on the Shibuya Park-dori side near Shibuya PARCO, LINE CUBE SHIBUYA and Yoyogi Park, inside a ground-floor space (Shibuya Homes 1F) that gives it an unusually spacious, sofa-lined layout for central Shibuya.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café's stated concept is "からだにいいものを気軽に" — enjoying things that are good for the body, casually. It pairs specialty coffee (a single organic house blend, 100% organic beans) with sweets and light food curated by an organic consultant to be gluten-free, largely vegan and additive-free.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan carrot cake (made with rice flour), vegan coconut-raspberry pie, gluten-free melon pan, and the house organic-bean specialty coffee and plant-milk lattes.

anecdotes.text: The café is dog-friendly throughout the space except the sofa seating, and has become known among Tokyo cafe-hoppers as a rare large, calm, pet-welcoming spot in the middle of Shibuya.
Its drinks lean fully organic — including a honey-free chai latte and kombucha — alongside the standard organic-bean coffee menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: FindMeGlutenFree listing states the establishment is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease; recommends contacting the establishment directly about safety precautions.

Menu and directory data show dairy items (Greek yogurt; cafe latte with dairy option, oat/rice/almond only as +charge alternatives), confirming the menu is not exclusively vegan and not a controlled allergen-free environment.

All corroborating sources describe GF menu variety ('so many GF and vegan options,' 'wonderful assortment') but none document cross-contamination handling, a dedicated fryer, or staff allergen training — no celiac-grade handling evidence exists.

positives.text: Sweets and food are curated by an organic consultant specifically for gluten-free, vegan, and additive-free needs.
Food menu includes gluten-free cakes, pizza, hot dogs, curry bread, and rice-flour carrot cake.
Coffee and most drinks (including chai and kombucha) are made from 100% organic ingredients, with organic plant-milk options for lattes.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu, but ordering is straightforward café-style (point at displayed cakes/drinks) and payment is cashless card/QR only, which is easy for foreign visitors.

places.name: 大衆居酒屋 とりいちず 水道橋店 (Taishū Izakaya Toriichizu Suidōbashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is advertised. The signature bone-in chicken and grilled skewers are finished with a proprietary tare sauce, which in standard izakaya cooking is soy-sauce based and contains wheat. The karaage (fried chicken) is coated in a spiced flour breading and cooked in a shared fryer alongside other fried items, creating cross-contamination risk. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-centric izakaya with no vegetarian or vegan menu items identified. The house specialties (bone-in chicken, mizutaki chicken hot pot, chicken-skin skewers, karaage) are all meat-based, and the signature broth is made from chicken bones. No plant-based dashi, broth, or dish substitutions were found in any official or listing source.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町（水道橋駅東口 徒歩2分）
places.notes: Toriichizu is a nationwide Japanese chicken-focused izakaya chain operated by FS.shake Co., Ltd., with roughly 100 branches across Japan; this branch sits two minutes on foot from Suidōbashi Station's east exit in the Jimbōchō/Kanda-Misakichō office district. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Toriichizu is a nationwide Japanese chicken-focused izakaya chain operated by FS. shake Co. , Ltd. , with roughly 100 branches across Japan; this branch sits two minutes on foot from Suidōbashi Station's east exit in the Jimbōchō/Kanda-Misakichō office district.
chef_bio.specialty: Bone-in chicken (骨付鳥) marinated in a house recipe and finished with a proprietary tare sauce (spice level selectable from mild to extra-hot), and a mizutaki chicken hot pot slow-simmered over 8+ hours from chicken bones with no added MSG.

anecdotes.text: The chain's signature kawa-kushi (grilled chicken-skin skewers) are dusted table-side with a 'secret spicy powder' unique to Toriichizu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Karaage and other fried items are coated in a wheat-flour-based spiced breading and cooked in a shared kitchen fryer used for multiple menu items; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or process is advertised.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's bone-in chicken and grilled skewers are finished with a proprietary tare (sauce); tare at Japanese izakayas is soy-sauce based and contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature mizutaki hot pot broth is slow-simmered from chicken bones for 8+ hours; no vegetable or plant-based broth alternative is listed.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-language chain izakaya with picture-menu ordering typical of large casual chains; no English menu, vegan signage, or foreign-visitor accommodation was found in any listing.

places.name: すごい煮干ラーメン凪 新宿ゴールデン街店本館 (Sugoi Niboshi Ramen Nagi — Shinjuku Golden G)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle or menu option was found in any source. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the tare (seasoning sauce) is a spiced sardine/soy-based blend that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. No mention of a dedicated allergy-safe fryer or gluten-free accommodation exists in the sources reviewed, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The core broth is built on niboshi (dried baby sardines/anchovies) plus an additional iwashi (sardine) extract in the tare, which is the shop's signature and defining flavor — this is inherently fish-based and cannot be made vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based broth, noodle, or topping alternative was found in any source checked.
places.hours_raw: 24時間営業・年中無休 (24 hours a day, open every day)
places.neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho) / 新宿ゴールデン街, near 新宿駅東口 and 新宿三丁目駅
places.notes: Ramen Nagi began in 2004 when founder Satoshi Ikuta and ramen-loving friends started serving noodles once a week out of a borrowed, tiny Golden Gai space with no money, no signature flavor, and no customers. The shop's name, Nagi (凪, meaning a windless calm sea), was chosen becau · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded 2004 in a tiny 4-tsubo, 8-seat rented space in Shinjuku Golden Gai
chef_bio.background: Ramen Nagi began in 2004 when founder Satoshi Ikuta and ramen-loving friends started serving noodles once a week out of a borrowed, tiny Golden Gai space with no money, no signature flavor, and no customers. The shop's name, Nagi (凪, meaning a windless calm sea), was chosen because the founders felt they had nothing to start with and wanted to create their own wind from a calm sea. Word of mouth grew the weekly pop-up into a full-time shop, and lines eventually stretched over 100 people deep outside this same Golden Gai alley. This location is the original honkan (main store) where the entire Nagi niboshi-ramen chain began, and it has since grown into a chain of 40+ locations in Japan and dozens more overseas.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nagi's ethos is built around pushing niboshi (dried fish) ramen to its most concentrated, umami-heavy extreme, and around the shop's scrappy, community-built origin story in Golden Gai's tiny alleys.
chef_bio.specialty: Sugoi Niboshi Ramen — a intensely rich broth built from dried niboshi (baby sardines/anchovies), sourced from producers across Japan, combined with an original wide "ittan-men" noodle and a spiced sardine-based tare aged for several days.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was chosen at a low point: with no money, no signature taste, and no customers, the founders decided to make their own "wind" from Nagi, a windless calm sea.
In 2015, this Golden Gai shop briefly collaborated with insect-food advocate Yuta Shinohara on a limited 100-bowl "cricket ramen," using dried cricket for dashi alongside the usual niboshi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is a spiced sardine (iwashi)-based sauce; standard Japanese ramen tare of this type is soy-sauce based and contains wheat, though no source explicitly confirmed wheat content for this specific shop's tare.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, tourist-visited Golden Gai landmark with an English Tabelog listing and ticket-vending-machine ordering, but menus and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 串揚げ かたやま (Kushiage Katayama)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, allergy menu, or dedicated fryer were found in any source. All skewers are coated in a wheat-flour/panko batter and deep-fried together in shared oil; the tartar and oroshi-ponzu sauces used across the 10-sauce tasting are soy-sauce (wheat) based. This restaurant is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is overwhelmingly meat- and seafood-based (shrimp, clams, crab, wagyu beef, pork belly, chicken tsukune); no vegan-labeled dishes or accommodations were found. Even skewers featuring vegetables are typically wrapped with or served alongside meat (e.g., asparagus wrapped in meat) and fried in the same shared oil as animal products. Sauces are not confirmed dashi/bonito-free. Not recommended for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: 西浅草（浅草・つくばエクスプレス浅草駅近く）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 夫婦二人で営む串揚げ専門店。ご主人が浅草橋の人気串揚げ店「串衛門」で串揚げの味に感銘を受け、その技術のもとで修行を積んだのち、より多くの人にその魅力を伝えたいという思いから西浅草に自分たちの店を構えた。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 西浅草（浅草橋の名店「串衛門」で修行）
chef_bio.background: 夫婦二人で営む串揚げ専門店。 ご主人が浅草橋の人気串揚げ店「串衛門」で串揚げの味に感銘を受け、その技術のもとで修行を積んだのち、より多くの人にその魅力を伝えたいという思いから西浅草に自分たちの店を構えた。
chef_bio.philosophy: 専門店であっても堅苦しくなく、気軽に楽しんでほしいという想いで営業している
chef_bio.specialty: 串揚げ10串コース ― 旬の魚介・肉・野菜をバランスよく揚げた10本の串を、タルタルソースなど10種類のオリジナルソースで味わう

anecdotes.text: 店主は浅草橋の専門店「串衛門」で食べた串揚げに感銘を受け、同店で修行を積んだ後に独立して西浅草に開店した。
専門店とはいえ夫婦2人で営む店で、気軽に楽しんでほしいというのが店主たちの想い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's signature sauce lineup includes oroshi ponzu, a soy-sauce-based dressing, alongside original tartar sauces used across its 10-sauce tasting.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, intimate husband-and-wife counter restaurant in a quiet residential corner of Nishi-Asakusa; expect Japanese-only signage and menus with limited English support, though online reservation platforms (Tabelog, AutoReserve) allow bookings.

places.name: 国際秘羊館 (Kokusai Hiyoukan)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth, with a soy-sauce-based tare typical of the style; no gluten-free noodle, broth, or preparation alternative is mentioned anywhere in coverage of this restaurant, and the kitchen is not set up to avoid wheat cross-contact.
places.vegan_detail: The lunch ramen is built entirely around a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and the dinner menu centers on grilled lamb (jingisukan) with dishes like lamb tartare/sashimi; no plant-based broth, noodle, or protein alternative is mentioned anywhere in available coverage.
places.hours_raw: Weekday lunch (ramen only): 11:00-14:00. Weekday dinner (jingisukan): 16:00-04:00. Saturday: 16:00-23:30 (L.O. 22:00), jingisukan only, no lunch ramen service. Closed Sundays and Dec 31 - Jan 3.
places.neighborhood: Shinsen / Dogenzaka, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 国際秘羊館 opened in Shibuya's Dogenzaka/Shinsen area in June 2025 as a 'creative jingisukan' restaurant serving herb- and spice-marinated grilled lamb (its name is a playful pun on 秘宝館, a type of curio museum). Starting February 2026, the jingisukan chef, Yahiro, began offering a sep · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nagasaki
chef_bio.background: 国際秘羊館 opened in Shibuya's Dogenzaka/Shinsen area in June 2025 as a 'creative jingisukan' restaurant serving herb- and spice-marinated grilled lamb (its name is a playful pun on 秘宝館, a type of curio museum). Starting February 2026, the jingisukan chef, Yahiro, began offering a separate weekday-only lunch menu of old-fashioned Kyushu tonkotsu ramen, recreating the Nagahama-style ramen-stall bowls he remembers eating growing up in Nagasaki.
chef_bio.philosophy: A jingisukan (spiced, herb-marinated grilled lamb) specialist by night who uses the quiet weekday lunch hours to recreate the nostalgic roadside tonkotsu ramen of his Nagasaki youth
chef_bio.specialty: Kyushu-style (Nagahama/Hakata) tonkotsu ramen served only on weekday lunches, made with a pork-head-based broth continually replenished (yobimodashi method) alongside a spine/knuckle stock for a rich, emulsified, lightly funky ('kusa-uma') pork-bone flavor without excess gaminess

anecdotes.text: The lunch ramen was born as a side project by the jingisukan chef: he set out to reproduce the funky-but-delicious ('kusa-uma') pork-bone ramen from old Nagahama ramen stalls, simmering a pork-head-based stock and continually replenishing it with backbone/knuckle stock (a traditional 'yobimodashi' method) so the broth turns rich and lightly emulsified without becoming too gamey.
Reviewers describe being surprised that a jingisukan specialty shop could turn out ramen of this quality, calling it a genuine hidden find rather than a side-business afterthought.

cultural_comfort.note: A very local, characterful spot (playful pun name, jingisukan-by-night/ramen-by-day setup) with no confirmed English menu or signage, so some comfort navigating Japanese-only ordering is helpful.

places.name: シズラー新宿東宝ビル店 (Sizzler Shinjuku Toho Building)
places.gf_detail: The all-you-can-eat salad bar (70+ items: seasonal vegetables, fruit, deli salads, soups, pasta, taco fixings) offers many naturally gluten-free choices to build your own plate, and general reporting on the Sizzler Japan chain (not confirmed for this specific branch) describes an allergen information sheet covering Japan's 28 legally designated allergens, available from staff on request, plus other branches reportedly labeling allergens near the buffet. However, wheat-containing items are also on the bar and grill menu (croutons, pasta, curry roux, tacos, breaded/fried items), buffet serving utensils are shared across dishes, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer or prep area has been confirmed for this location. Celiac diners should ask staff directly for the current allergen list before eating.
places.vegan_detail: The buffet salad bar has many vegetable-, fruit-, and legume-based items that can be combined into a plant-based plate, and taco/rice/pasta stations allow build-your-own combinations. However, soups, curries, dressings, and some prepared sides commonly use dashi (fish stock), meat stock, or dairy, and no dedicated vegan menu, labeling, or vegan-specific preparation has been confirmed for this branch. The restaurant's core menu is a steak-and-grill concept, so the strongest vegan options are limited to what can be assembled at the salad bar itself.
places.hours_raw: Breakfast buffet 6:30–10:30 (L.O. 10:00); regular hours 11:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30), daily.
places.neighborhood: 歌舞伎町・新宿東宝ビル内（ホテルグレイスリー新宿1F）, 新宿区 — near JR Shinjuku Station
places.notes: Sizzler Shinjuku Toho Building opened June 3, 2025 on the 1st floor of the Shinjuku Toho Building — the same tower that houses Hotel Gracery Shinjuku and the Godzilla Head, just outside Kabukicho. It was Sizzler's first brand-new store opening in five years and became its 11th lo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Salad bar & grill restaurant, part of the Sizzler chain (operated in Japan by Royal Holdings Co., Ltd.)
chef_bio.origin: Sizzler is a U.S.-founded steak/salad-bar chain; this branch is run by Japanese operator Royal Holdings.
chef_bio.background: Sizzler Shinjuku Toho Building opened June 3, 2025 on the 1st floor of the Shinjuku Toho Building — the same tower that houses Hotel Gracery Shinjuku and the Godzilla Head, just outside Kabukicho. It was Sizzler's first brand-new store opening in five years and became its 11th location in Japan. A breakfast buffet service for both hotel guests and walk-in diners was added starting June 4, 2025.
chef_bio.specialty: The Sizzler Salad Bar: an all-you-can-eat, self-serve spread said to include 70+ items — seasonal vegetables, house-made deli salads, soups, fruit, pasta, taco fixings, and mini desserts — served alongside grilled steak and chicken mains.

anecdotes.text: This was Sizzler's first new store opening in five years and its 11th location in Japan, opening June 3, 2025 in Shinjuku.

Press coverage described the new Shinjuku Toho Building location as offering a calm, natural-wood interior with sofa seating and a terrace, aimed at both hotel guests of Gracery Shinjuku and outside visitors.

A visitor blog noted that, despite expecting hotel-buffet-style lines, the salad bar was surprisingly uncrowded, with mostly female diners lingering over the salad bar to chat — describing it as popular for girls'-night-out style visits.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The salad bar is a shared self-serve buffet where guests use common serving utensils across all dishes, so cross-contact between gluten-containing and gluten-free items is possible even though many items are naturally gluten-free.

Sizzler Shinjuku Mitsui Building (a different, nearby branch) is described as 'not a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease' — no branch has celiac-grade confirmation, including this one.

Chain-level assessment: 'As a chain restaurant, Sizzler typically means a high risk of cross-contact, and it is likely not possible to dine safely at this establishment if you have celiac disease or must avoid cross-contact.' A manager reportedly said only 'a couple of items in the salad bar' are gluten-free and those are 'subject to cross contamination,' and reviewers 'wouldn't trust the salad because of so much cross contamination.'

Salad-bar croutons are wheat-flour bread fried in oil; unpackaged buffet dishes carry no labeling obligation and even trace exposure can trigger symptoms, so wheat-allergic/celiac diners must confirm cross-contamination directly with staff — i.e. the buffet is a documented cross-contact hazard, not a safe self-serve GF environment.

The Toho Building branch opened in early June 2025 (JR Shinjuku ~5 min); searches for branch-specific allergen/wheat/vegan/dashi accommodation returned no confirmation of GF handling or vegan prep for this location.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soups, curries, and dressings on the buffet may contain dashi, meat stock, or dairy, and are served from the same buffet line as vegetable dishes, so strict vegans should check ingredients item by item rather than assume the whole bar is plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: General reporting on the Sizzler Japan chain describes staff providing an allergen-removal menu and a list covering 25+ designated allergen items on request, though this describes chain-wide practice rather than a confirmation specific to the Shinjuku Toho Building branch.

A Tripadvisor review of a different Tokyo Sizzler branch (Setagaya) reported staff bringing an allergy-information folder when asked, suggesting this is a chain-standard practice, though not independently confirmed for this Shinjuku location.

positives.text: The salad bar is described as offering 70+ items of fresh seasonal vegetables, fruit, and made-to-order components, giving diners with dietary restrictions substantial control over what goes on their own plate.

cultural_comfort.note: A major hotel-based chain restaurant (inside Hotel Gracery Shinjuku) catering heavily to domestic and international/tourist guests, with online reservation booking and a self-explanatory buffet format that needs little Japanese to navigate.

places.name: ベーカリー サンチノ 渋谷店 (Bakery Santino Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: Despite the "rice-flour bread" branding, the dough is a blend of rice flour (Niigata Sado-Island Koshihikari + Kumamoto rice flour) with a small amount of wheat gluten deliberately added — reported at roughly 20% of the flour blend — to help the dough rise and hold texture. Food blogs covering the shop explicitly state it is "not completely gluten-free" (完全グルテンフリーではありません). This is a normal working bakery (not a dedicated gluten-free facility), so cross-contact with wheat-based products is also expected. Not appropriate for celiac disease or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan line or vegan-labeled items were found in available sources. General coverage of Santino's pastry case notes butter-rich items such as croissants, implying dairy is used in at least part of the lineup. Whether the signature rice-flour breads themselves contain eggs/dairy was not confirmed — ask staff before purchasing if avoiding animal products.
places.hours_raw: 10:00〜21:00（施設営業時間に準ずる）／10:00-21:00 daily, following the mall's operating hours
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (Shibuya Scramble Square 1F, directly connected to S
places.cuisine: 米粉パン・ベーカリー
places.notes: Santino is a rice-flour-bread concept produced by Akimasa Sugikubo, the owner-chef of the well-known Tomigaya/Yoyogi-Koen bakery "365日" (365 Nichi, opened 2013) and head of Ultra Kitchen Inc. This Shibuya branch is inside the ecute EDITION food hall on the 1st floor of Shibuya Sc · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Santino is a rice-flour-bread concept produced by Akimasa Sugikubo, the owner-chef of the well-known Tomigaya/Yoyogi-Koen bakery "365日" (365 Nichi, opened 2013) and head of Ultra Kitchen Inc. This Shibuya branch is inside the ecute EDITION food hall on the 1st floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, which opened November 1, 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "Santino" evokes the idea of "[someone]'s hometown" / a producer's home region — the shop's concept centers on tracing where its rice flour and other ingredients come from, spotlighting specific regional producers through bread.
chef_bio.specialty: コシヒカリ生食パン — a very moist, chewy "nama" (soft-crust) milk bread made from a blend of Sado Island (Niigata) Koshihikari rice flour and Kumamoto rice flour.

anecdotes.text: The dough's moist, chewy texture comes from blending Sado-Island (Niigata) Koshihikari rice flour with Kumamoto rice flour; a small amount of wheat gluten is mixed in to help the dough rise, so the shop is described as 'not completely gluten-free' — recommended as a gentle entry point for people new to rice-flour bread rather than as a celiac-safe option.
Santino is produced by Akimasa Sugikubo, the chef behind the popular Yoyogi/Tomigaya bakery '365日'.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bread's dough is a blend of rice flour and a small amount of intentionally-added wheat gluten (~20% of the flour blend) for texture and rise — this is a general-purpose bakery producing rice-and-wheat-gluten breads together, not a dedicated gluten-free facility, and it is explicitly described as 'not completely gluten-free.'

positives.text: The shop is well regarded as an approachable option for people curious about rice-flour bread's moist texture, though coverage is careful to note it is not a strict gluten-free product.

cultural_comfort.note: Self-service bakery counter (pick tray/tongs, pay at register) inside a major train-station shopping complex — pointing and paying works fine without Japanese, but ingredient and allergy details are likely Japanese-only, so ask staff directly if you have celiac disease or a wheat allergy.

places.name: GARB pintino（ガーブ ピンティーノ） (Garb Pintino)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation process was found. As a French bistro, standard dishes are likely to include wheat-based elements (bread, roux/sauces, pastry, breading). The restaurant's general practice is that guests with allergies or ingredients they cannot eat should call in advance so the kitchen can review options; no information on a dedicated fryer or wheat-free alternatives was found. Celiac diners should treat this as a call-ahead-and-verify situation, not a gluten-free-safe kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-specific process was confirmed. French bistro cooking of this style typically relies on butter, cream, and meat- or fish-based stocks, and no evidence of plant-based substitutions or a vegan track was found in available sources. Vegan diners would need to call ahead to ask what, if anything, the kitchen can adapt.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (food LO 14:00); Dinner 17:30-23:00 (food LO 20:30, drink LO 22:30). Sat: Lunch only 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:00); dinner closed. Sun: closed. Dinner reported open on public holidays.

The Jimbocho branch relocated in 2015 into the Terrace Square building in Kanda-Nishikicho (its original site had been near Tokyo Tower), taking over · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: GARB pintino is one location in the GARB series of casual French restaurants run by the Osaka-founded Balnibarbi restaurant group. The Jimbocho branch relocated in 2015 into the Terrace Square building in Kanda-Nishikicho (its original site had been near Tokyo Tower), taking over a bright, open space with 6-meter ceilings that today mixes a bar counter, lounge seating, private rooms and a terrace.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated concept is to let guests "casually enjoy food and drink in their own style, without pretension" — a relaxed take on French cooking built around seasonal, domestically sourced meat, vegetables and fish chosen for freshness and origin, finished with French technique.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guidance associated with the restaurant's booking listings states that customers with food allergies or ingredients they cannot eat should call the restaurant in advance so staff can confirm what can be accommodated.

positives.text: Reports associated with the restaurant's listings note that when guests informed the restaurant of allergies or disliked ingredients ahead of time, the accommodation was handled attentively.

cultural_comfort.note: All located information is in Japanese with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so it is best to call ahead in Japanese (or have someone call) to discuss any allergy or dietary needs before booking.

places.name: ディヴァーノ ワインダイニング 新宿 (Divano Wine Dining Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free-specific information (dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, wheat in dashi/tempura, or cross-contamination protocols) was found in any source consulted. The menu leans Italian/French with seasonal-ingredient plates and meat/game mains, which may involve wheat-based pasta, roux, or breading. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten intolerance should call ahead and confirm specific dishes before dining; this cannot be recommended as a safe celiac choice without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based tasting course, or vegan-specific accommodations were mentioned in any source found. The restaurant's identity centers on natural-wine pairing with seasonal-ingredient cooking that includes a signature beef red-wine stew and game (gibier) dishes, i.e. it reads as a meat-forward wine-dining spot rather than a vegetable-forward one. Vegan or vegetarian diners should call ahead to ask what can be modified.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri: 18:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30); Sat, Sun, holidays: 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays

chef_bio.background: The restaurant's owner holds a sommelier qualification and personally selects the natural-wine (vin nature) list served at the restaurant, from beginner-friendly bottles to rare, sought-after labels for enthusiasts.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Divano' means 'sofa' in Italian, reflecting the restaurant's casual-yet-refined all-sofa-seating concept; the food and wine program is built around pairing seasonal, carefully sourced ingredients with organically grown, naturally vinified wines chosen personally by the owner-sommelier.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef braised in red wine (牛肉の赤ワイン煮込み) and game (gibier) dishes, paired with the sommelier's natural-wine selections

anecdotes.text: オーナーソムリエが自ら厳選したこだわりのナチュラルワインは、ビギナーから上級者までが満足できるとっておきのものを豊富にご用意しており、オーガニック（有機栽培）のブドウを使い、ワインの醸造においても自然な製法にこだわった生産者のワインです。(The owner-sommelier personally selects an extensive range of prized natural wines, satisfying beginners and connoisseurs alike, from producers who use organically grown grapes and commit to natural winemaking methods.)
「ディヴァーノ(Divano)」はイタリア語でソファーという意味。グリーン/レッド/ブラックなどカラフルなデザインのソファーがあり、白を基調とした内装。('Divano' is Italian for sofa; the restaurant has colorful green/red/black sofa seating in a white-toned interior.)

cultural_comfort.note: Japanese-language phone/web reservation system with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese ability or translation help is useful.

places.name: ミラン・ナタラジ 渋谷店 (Milan Nataraj Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: The soy-meat (大豆ミート) Nataraj curry is described by the restaurant/reviewers as completely gluten-free, and rice can be substituted with brown rice; menus use pictograms to flag wheat-containing dishes. However, the popular naan is made with natural yeast and domestic (regular) wheat flour, so it is NOT gluten-free, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free kitchen zone is documented — curries are simmered in a shared kitchen where wheat-based naan dough is also handled. Celiac-level diners should confirm cross-contact directly with staff before ordering; this is a solid options-friendly spot, not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is vegetarian by design (no meat or fish is used anywhere in the kitchen), and a substantial vegan-labeled selection is available including soy-meat curries and a vegan naan made with komatsuna greens (no egg or dairy). Note that some standard menu items do contain dairy (ghee, cream, yogurt) per traditional Indian preparation, so vegan diners should check pictogram labels or ask staff for the vegan-marked dishes specifically. No katsuo/bonito dashi is used since the cuisine is Indian-style, not Japanese-style broth-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30; takeout/delivery accepted until 22:30), year-round except Dec 30 16:00 - Jan 1

places.notes: Nataraj opened Japan's first vegetarian Indian restaurant in Takadanobaba in September 1989, founded by a Gujarati-born vegetarian who moved to Tokyo and wanted to introduce authentic vegetarian Indian cooking to Japan. The chain grew to locations in Ogikubo (1990), Minami-Aoyama · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded by Kanhaiyalal N. Buddhadev (originally from Gujarat, India) together with his Japanese wife Masako Saikawa
chef_bio.background: Nataraj opened Japan's first vegetarian Indian restaurant in Takadanobaba in September 1989, founded by a Gujarati-born vegetarian who moved to Tokyo and wanted to introduce authentic vegetarian Indian cooking to Japan. The chain grew to locations in Ogikubo (1990), Minami-Aoyama (2000), Ginza (2002), and the Shibuya branch (Milan Nataraj), which opened in September 2015 a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nataraj cooks all curries from vegetables, legumes, and plant proteins with no chemical seasonings, preservatives, artificial colorings, or additives, aiming to bring genuinely natural, additive-free vegetarian food to Japan.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable and soy-meat curries built from organically farmed produce, served with naan baked in an Indian clay tandoor oven imported from India; signature vegan naan is kneaded with komatsuna greens.

anecdotes.text: The company runs its own organic farms in Minami-Boso (Chiba) and Akeno (Yamanashi), growing the vegetables used seasonally in its restaurants without pesticides or herbicides, using only organic fertilizer.
Nataraj is credited as the restaurant that started Japan's vegetarian curry movement, tracing back to its 1989 Takadanobaba opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or fryer is documented; naan made with regular wheat flour is prepared in the same kitchen as the curries, so cross-contact is possible for wheat-sensitive/celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most curries are dairy-based per traditional Indian recipes (ghee, cream, yogurt), so vegan diners must rely on menu pictogram labeling or ask staff to identify the vegan-safe dishes rather than assuming the whole menu is vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Menu items are labeled in both Japanese and English with pictograms indicating vegan status and allergens (wheat, egg, dairy, nuts), letting diners self-screen dishes at a glance.

positives.text: The soy-meat Nataraj curry is completely gluten-free, and rice can be substituted with brown rice.
All curries are prepared vegetarian, using vegetables, legumes, and soy meat with no meat or fish, and no chemical seasonings, preservatives, or artificial additives.
The restaurant sources vegetables from its own pesticide-free organic farms in Chiba and Yamanashi.

cultural_comfort.note: Menus are presented in Japanese and English with pictogram labeling for vegan/vegetarian and allergen (wheat, egg, dairy, nuts) status, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking diners to navigate.

places.name: 寿司の惑星 水道橋 (Sushi Planet Suidobashi (Sushi no Wakusei Su)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce (tamari), or dedicated fryer is mentioned in any source reviewed. As a standard Edomae sushi and tempura izakaya, the tempura batter is presumably wheat-flour based and shared with other fried items, and standard shoyu (which typically contains wheat) is the likely condiment. Celiac diners should not consider this GF-safe without asking staff directly, and language may be a barrier.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based accommodations are mentioned anywhere. The restaurant's identity centers on fresh tuna and white fish nigiri and tempura (likely egg-based batter); traditional izakaya cooking of this kind typically relies on katsuo (bonito) dashi in broths and sauces, which is not vegan. No sources indicate any vegetarian or vegan-friendly items.

It sits just south of Suidobashi Station, near Tokyo Dome. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of a small independent chain (Musashi-Kosugi honten + Suidobashi)
chef_bio.background: 寿司の惑星 (SUSHI PLANET) is a small Tokyo sushi-and-tempura izakaya chain; the Suidobashi shop opened in May 2025 as a sister location to the group's original Musashi-Kosugi restaurant. It sits just south of Suidobashi Station, near Tokyo Dome.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as bringing old-school Edomae tradition — red-vinegar rice, fish chosen with the toppings in mind — to a casual, affordable izakaya setting rather than a formal sushi-ya.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri made with akazu (red vinegar) shari, with pieces starting around ¥75, alongside made-to-order tempura

anecdotes.text: Tempura here goes beyond the usual shrimp and vegetables to include playful items like cheese-stuffed sausage, simmered daikon, and even a 'dessert tempura.'
Tuna and white fish are sourced fresh daily, and every piece of sushi uses akazu red-vinegar shari for a more traditional Edomae flavor.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu and service appear to be Japanese-language first (izakaya-style operation); a Tabelog English listing exists but no English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed.

places.name: タムジャイサムゴー 新宿中央通り店（譚仔三哥） (TamJai SamGor Shinjuku Chuo-dori)
places.gf_detail: The signature 米線 (mixian) noodles are made only from rice and water and are marketed by the brand and delivery platforms explicitly as gluten-free, so the core noodle dish is a strong option for gluten-avoiders. However, the menu also includes clearly wheat-containing items (pork wontons, likely-battered/fried chicken wings, etc.) prepared in the same kitchen, and no source confirms a dedicated gluten-free fryer, gluten-free-certified soup bases, or wheat-free soy sauce — soup bases and seasonings have not been confirmed free of wheat-derived soy sauce. Treat as noodle-safe but not confirmed celiac-safe; ask staff about specific soup-base ingredients if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan menu item, vegan broth, or vegan certification. Toppings and soup bases lean meat/seafood (pork wontons, clams, fish balls per delivery listings, chicken-wing side dish), and Hong Kong mixian soup bases typically use meat or bone stock, though this specific shop's stock ingredients were not directly confirmed. A vegetable-topped noodle bowl (noodles reduced or swapped for lettuce) is available, but it is not confirmed that the soup base itself is meat/dashi-free. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about the soup base ingredients.
