It traces back to the original Tam Jai Yunnan Mixian founded in Hong Kong in 1996 by two brothers; after a 2008 family split, the third brother ("SamGor"/三哥) and sixth brother kept operating their branches as Ta · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Japan flagship/debut location of the Hong Kong chain, operated in Japan by Toridoll Holdings
chef_bio.origin: Hong Kong
chef_bio.background: TamJai SamGor (譚仔三哥) is a Hong Kong rice-noodle (mixian) soup chain. It traces back to the original Tam Jai Yunnan Mixian founded in Hong Kong in 1996 by two brothers; after a 2008 family split, the third brother ("SamGor"/三哥) and sixth brother kept operating their branches as TamJai SamGor, developing it into one of Hong Kong's most popular noodle-soup chains (three-time Michelin Bib Gourmand recipient there). Both the original Tam Jai and TamJai SamGor were later acquired by Japan's Toridoll Holdings, which brought TamJai SamGor to Japan for the first time, opening this Shinjuku Chuo-dori shop as the brand's Japan debut store on March 31, 2022.
chef_bio.philosophy: Build a bowl from rice noodles, a choice of original soup bases, ten spice levels, and a wide range of fresh vegetable and protein toppings — the store markets over a million possible combinations, with an emphasis on the signature flavor profile described as "fragrant, numbing, spicy, hot, fresh" (香麻辛辣鮮).
chef_bio.specialty: Customizable 米線 (mixian) rice-noodle soup — choose soup base, spice level (1-10), and toppings; a vegetable-topped version with noodles reduced or swapped for lettuce is offered as a lighter option.

anecdotes.text: This Shinjuku Chuo-dori shop was TamJai SamGor's first store in Japan, opening March 31, 2022, billed as the Hong Kong No.1-popularity noodle brand's Japan landing.

TamJai SamGor split from the original Tam Jai Yunnan Mixian in a 2008 family divide between the founding brothers; both chains were eventually bought by Japan's Toridoll Holdings, which unified them under Tam Jai International.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen/fryer is confirmed; the menu includes wheat-containing items (e.g. pork wontons) made in the same kitchen as the rice-noodle soups.

FindMeGlutenFree listing for TamJai SamGor (Shinjuku City): staff advised that noodles are made with wheat so no dishes are ok, and the chicken and other meat has sauces that are not suitable for gluten-free diets; establishment is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease.

Documented TamJai SamGor soup base is water + pork-bone (or chicken) broth + chicken powder + evaporated milk + peppercorn oil + chili — none confirmed wheat-free, and contains dairy; not a vegan/GF-safe base.

Japanese gluten-free guidance surfaced in search: TamJai uses gluten-free rice noodles but 'most menu items contain wheat components,' and soy sauce/seasonings typically contain wheat, so wheat-allergic diners must confirm with staff — no celiac protocol documented.

Brand/delivery 'gluten-free' labeling (Uber Eats store names, brand site) applies to the mixian ingredient only; no dedicated fryer, allergen certification, or wheat-free tare/broth is claimed for celiac handling.

Vegetarian mushroom base and vegetarian toppings are described for the international/HK menu, not confirmed for the Japan/Shinjuku branch, whose menu leans pork wontons, clams, fish balls and chicken wings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu toppings include pork wontons, clams, and (per delivery listings) fish balls and fried chicken wings, indicating soup bases and shared prep are not run as a vegan kitchen even when a vegetable-topped noodle bowl is ordered.

positives.text: The signature 米線 (mixian) rice noodles are made from just rice and water, so they are naturally gluten-free — this is called out directly in the store's own delivery-platform listing title.
A vegetable-forward option exists: diners can reduce the noodle portion or substitute lettuce for noodles, topped with a variety of fresh vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering means choosing a soup base, spice level, and toppings from a large combination menu, which is easier with a little Japanese or a translation app even though the concept (build-your-own noodle soup) is intuitive.

places.name: 地どり屋つかだ 渋谷 (Jidoriya Tsukada Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated wheat-free preparation, or allergy protocol was found on any source checked (official site, Tabelog, aggregator listings). As a traditional jidori/yakitori specialist, sauces and tare are typically soy-sauce based (soy sauce commonly contains wheat in Japan), though this was not explicitly confirmed for this restaurant. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about tare vs. salt (shio) preparation and shared-grill cross-contact before ordering — nothing here should be assumed safe without asking.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-specialty restaurant built entirely around jidori (local chicken): chicken sashimi and tataki, charcoal-grilled skewers, and a chicken-bone-broth hot pot are the signature dishes. No vegan dishes, substitutions, or accommodations were found in any source; vegetable side dishes/salads appear on the course menu but the restaurant's core offering and broths are chicken-based throughout.
places.hours_raw: Dinner 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00), daily; irregular holidays (some listings also show Sat/Sun/Holiday opening at 13:00, which could not be fully reconciled across sources)
places.neighborhood: Shibuya, Dogenzaka
places.notes: Jidoriya Tsukada is a flagship redefinition of AP Company's long-running Tsukada Farm chicken-izakaya brand, launched under the concept '違いのわかる大人のための塚田農場' (Tsukada Farm for adults who can tell the difference). The company positioned Shibuya as its model district, opening this sto · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 保坂 翔馬 (Hosaka Shoma), listed as head chef on Hitosara
chef_bio.origin: Opened June 20, 2020 by AP Company (operator of the Tsukada Farm / 塚田農場 izakaya chain) as a flagship 'jidoriya' concept store in Shibuya
chef_bio.background: Jidoriya Tsukada is a flagship redefinition of AP Company's long-running Tsukada Farm chicken-izakaya brand, launched under the concept '違いのわかる大人のための塚田農場' (Tsukada Farm for adults who can tell the difference). The company positioned Shibuya as its model district, opening this store alongside several other 'Tsukada'-branded chicken specialists in the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chickens are raised on the company's own farms for two to three times longer than standard broilers, with an emphasis on low-stress rearing to bring out deeper umami and sweetness in the meat; the restaurant pairs bold charcoal-grilling with low-temperature cooking and raw preparations to showcase the ingredient from multiple angles rather than treating it as a single-dish izakaya staple.
chef_bio.specialty: Miyazaki Jitokko (みやさき地頭鶏) and Kurosatsuma (黒さつま鶏) jidori chicken — breeds said to make up roughly 1% of chicken eaten in Japan — served as charcoal-grilled skewers, chicken sashimi and tataki (breast/thigh), and a chicken-bone-broth hot pot (shabu-suki), with rare cuts featured alongside the standard grilled repertoire

anecdotes.text: AP Company's press release describes the store as a strategic flagship ('戦略拠点') built around a U-shaped open counter where guests watch the charcoal grilling up close, intended to move the chicken-izakaya format upmarket for an adult clientele.

Coverage of AP Company's Shibuya expansion notes the company clustered two Tsukada Farm locations and five 'Tsukada'-branded specialty spinoffs (including this one) in Shibuya as a proving ground for the rebrand.

cultural_comfort.note: Traditional Japanese izakaya-style course dining with Japanese-language menus and booking systems (Ikyu, Ebica, Hitosara); a Tabelog English page exists but no English-speaking staff or English menu was confirmed.

places.name: 寄合 (Yoriai)
places.gf_detail: Menu centers on deep-fried items (aji-fry, tonkatsu, karaage, menchi-katsu), which are conventionally breaded in wheat flour/panko and likely fried in a shared fryer; sauces and any soy-sauce-based seasoning are typically wheat-containing. No gluten-free menu items, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodations were found in official or review sources.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is built around fried fish and meat set meals (horse mackerel aji-fry, chicken karaage, yūrinji chicken, menchi-katsu); no vegetarian or vegan set meals were identified in available sources, and dashi/sauces likely contain bonito/fish-derived ingredients.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Monday-Saturday; closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Kanda Misakichō, Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yoriai is a teishoku (set-meal) restaurant a few minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station, known for hearty, generously portioned lunch sets and a lively, welcoming proprietor who serves diners at the counter. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Yoriai is a teishoku (set-meal) restaurant a few minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station, known for hearty, generously portioned lunch sets and a lively, welcoming proprietor who serves diners at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh horse mackerel (aji-fry) set meal made with domestic mackerel shipped daily from Toyosu market, served with rice from an Akita farm (one free refill, or a large portion on request).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant offers more than 10 varieties of hearty Japanese set meals, with rice from an Akita farm that diners can have refilled once for free or upgraded to a large serving.
Its most popular set meal is fried horse mackerel (aji-fry) made with fish shipped fresh from Toyosu market.

A visiting food blogger described the yuzu-pepper fried chicken (yūrinji) set as juicy karaage topped with plenty of scallion and a well-balanced sweet-savory sauce, served by an energetically welcoming owner.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local counter-style set-meal shop with no evidence of an English menu; pointing at photos of dishes or using a translation app is recommended.

places.name: 炭火焼食堂 こがね屋 (Sumibi Yaki Shokudo Koganeya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation was found in any source. As a traditional Japanese grilled-fish/izakaya restaurant, dishes very likely involve soy sauce (contains wheat) in marinades and dipping sauces, and shared grill/kitchen equipment. No information on dedicated fryers or cross-contamination protocols was found. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. This is a fish- and meat-centric charcoal-grill/izakaya restaurant; dashi and cooking bases likely include bonito/fish stock. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:30), Dinner 17:30-23:00 (LO 22:00). Sat: Dinner 17:00-22:00 (LO 21:00), Lunch 11:30-14:30. Closed Sundays and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Seibu-Shinjuku Station
places.cuisine: 焼き魚・串焼き・居酒屋
places.notes: A charcoal-grill set-meal restaurant (定食屋) in Nishi-Shinjuku, operated by Humax Corporation, specializing in yakizakana (grilled fish) teishoku for lunch and izakaya-style charcoal grilling at night. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A charcoal-grill set-meal restaurant (定食屋) in Nishi-Shinjuku, operated by Humax Corporation, specializing in yakizakana (grilled fish) teishoku for lunch and izakaya-style charcoal grilling at night.
chef_bio.specialty: Toro-saba shioyaki teishoku (fatty mackerel salt-grilled set meal) and salmon belly (sake harasu) grilled over charcoal

anecdotes.text: Lunch specials in the ¥680-¥980 range feature grilled fish that bloggers describe as thick, juicy, and freshly grilled to order, including toro-saba, sake harasu, aji, shima-hokke, toro-nishin, sanma, and gin-zake depending on season.

cultural_comfort.note: A local Japanese-style set-meal/izakaya spot with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff — expect a Japanese-only experience.

places.name: 豆腐料理 空野 渋谷 (Tofu Ryori Sorano Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found. The restaurant's standard soy sauce (used e.g. with the fresh tofu-skin sashimi) is ordinary Japanese shoyu, which typically contains wheat. One vegan-friendly menu item is explicitly 'wheat gluten skewers' (fu), which contains gluten and is unsuitable for celiac diners despite being plant-based. TripAdvisor lists a generic 'gluten-free options' tag but no restaurant-sourced detail backs this up, and no mention of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls was found. Celiac or severe gluten-avoidant diners should ask staff directly about tamari availability and preparation methods for each dish rather than relying on menu tags.
places.vegan_detail: Vegan diners have several genuinely plant-based options — cold silken tofu, fresh tofu-skin (yuba) sashimi, and tofu gelato all reviewed favorably by vegan reviewers — plus a wheat-gluten (fu) skewer dish. However, most of the broader menu (tempura, carpaccio, salads, and other izakaya-style dishes) is not vegetarian, dashi/bonito use elsewhere on the menu is unconfirmed, and one review found a 'vegetarian'-labeled dish actually contained chicken. Vegan guests should verbally confirm each dish with staff rather than trusting menu markings, and expect portions of the vegan items to be small.
places.neighborhood: 桜丘（渋谷駅より徒歩約5分）
places.notes: Sorano is a tofu-specialty restaurant operated by FOODGATE Co., Ltd., a Tokyo restaurant group that also runs 'Gohan-ya Ichishin Daikanyama' and 'Udon Yamanaga.' The Shibuya shop (previously known as 'Sora no Niwa') sits beside the JR tracks a short walk from Shibuya Station's so · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of a small Tokyo/Osaka tofu-restaurant group (FOODGATE Co., Ltd.), not a single-founder shop.
chef_bio.background: Sorano is a tofu-specialty restaurant operated by FOODGATE Co. , Ltd. , a Tokyo restaurant group that also runs 'Gohan-ya Ichishin Daikanyama' and 'Udon Yamanaga. ' The Shibuya shop (previously known as 'Sora no Niwa') sits beside the JR tracks a short walk from Shibuya Station's south exit, and is one of several sister branches (Ebisu, Osaka-Minamisenba) built around the same house-made-tofu concept.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated priority is tofu made without additives or coagulants, pairing traditional tofu-making knowledge with newly created tofu-centered dishes rather than treating tofu as a side note to a standard izakaya menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Sorano Tofu (空野豆腐) — silken tofu coagulated tableside from unadulterated soy milk (no additives or chemical coagulants), made from organic Hokkaido Tokachi soybeans pressed to a rich 14-degree soy-milk density; also known for kinu-age (delicately fried silken tofu) and fresh yuba (tofu skin) sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The tableside tofu order sets over about twenty minutes into a dense, creamy curd with a deeper soybean flavor than supermarket tofu — reviewers recommend ordering it first thing.
One diner called it 'the most memorable restaurant in Tokyo,' praising the calm, unhurried atmosphere built around tofu craftsmanship.
The interior features a small water feature with swimming carp and a mezzanine of semi-private seats, giving the space a modern-wa, unhurried feel distinct from typical Shibuya dining.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A 'wheat gluten skewers' dish (made from fu, i.e. wheat gluten) is offered as a vegan-friendly menu item — it is plant-based but contains gluten, so it is not a safe option for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners despite appearing on 'vegan' recommendation lists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Fresh tofu-skin sashimi is served with standard soy sauce (shoyu), wasabi, and sudachi — no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A reviewer asked staff whether a 'vegetarian'-marked menu item was vegan; staff initially said yes, but on double-checking it turned out the dish actually contained chicken — indicating menu dietary labels are not fully reliable and should be verbally reconfirmed per dish.

positives.text: Reviewers describe a 'fantastic selection of vegan and vegetarian food' with staff willing to identify vegan-suitable dishes when asked, and an English menu that makes communicating dietary needs easier.
Core tofu dishes — cold silken tofu, fresh tofu-skin sashimi, and tofu gelato — are naturally vegan and were specifically praised by vegan diners on abillion.

cultural_comfort.note: Reviewers report staff who speak English and an English-language menu is provided, making ordering and asking about dietary needs manageable for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: つじ田 水道橋店 (Tsujita Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen specialist — the dipping noodles are wheat-based (no rice or gluten-free noodle alternative found), and the soy-based tare/seasoning almost certainly contains wheat as is standard for shoyu. No gluten-free menu, allergen substitution, or dedicated preparation was found in any source checked; celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: The signature tsukemen broth is built on pork bone (tonkotsu) simmered with animal fats plus a seafood dashi of bonito flakes, sardine flakes, and kombu — no vegan or vegetarian version is offered, and the broth is not adaptable to remove animal ingredients.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区 水道橋駅 4分
places.notes: Yudai Tsujita worked at an izakaya after high school, opened his own izakaya at age 22, later sold it, then trained in ramen-making from around age 23 before opening the original Tsujita ramen shop in Kanda-Ochanomizu in 2005 at age 26. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto, born 1978
chef_bio.background: Yudai Tsujita worked at an izakaya after high school, opened his own izakaya at age 22, later sold it, then trained in ramen-making from around age 23 before opening the original Tsujita ramen shop in Kanda-Ochanomizu in 2005 at age 26.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tsujita is widely regarded as the pioneer of 'noukou' (rich) tonkotsu-seafood tsukemen in Tokyo, built on an exacting selection of dozens of ingredients and a continual pursuit of a better bowl even after growing into a multi-branch brand (expanding to Osaka, Kanagawa, Saitama, Fukuoka, and even Los Angeles).
chef_bio.specialty: 濃厚特製つけ麺 (Rich Special Tsukemen) — thick wheat noodles dipped in a rich broth combining pork-bone tonkotsu with bonito, sardine, and kombu seafood dashi, topped with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, bamboo shoots, and nori.

anecdotes.text: Tsujita created its own 'tsukemen etiquette': eat the noodles plain at first, add sudachi citrus at the one-third mark, then black shichimi pepper for the final third, finishing with a soup-splitting (wari-soup) to enjoy the broth on its own.
The shop opened in Kanda-Ochanomizu in 2005 and is credited with helping ignite Tokyo's modern tsukemen boom before growing into a multi-city, multi-branch operation.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, order-and-queue tsukemen counter shop with Japanese-only ambiance typical of Tokyo ramen culture; no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff, though ordering is simple and picture-menu-friendly.

places.name: シンちゃんラーメン (Shin-chan Ramen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options or accommodations are mentioned anywhere in sourcing. This is a standard shoyu-leaning chuka soba shop: house-made wheat noodles are the entire point of the dish, and the soup is finished with a soy-based tare (sources describe a "light-colored but distinctly soy-flavored" broth), which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing shoyu rather than tamari. No dedicated gluten-free noodle, broth, or fryer was found, and celiac diners should assume this is not a safe option.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are indicated. The broth is a pork-based clear soup (豚清湯スープ) and the signature dishes are built around thick chashu pork; there is no mention of a plant-based broth, noodle, or topping substitution in any source, so vegan diners should assume there is nothing suitable here.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00-23:00、土・日・祝 11:00-21:00（不定休あり）※一部情報源では金・土24時間営業との記載もあり、要確認
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.notes: シンちゃんラーメン is part of Tokyo's "chan-kei" (ちゃん系) ramen genre — a family of small counter shops each named after a person's nickname (Shin-chan, Haru-chan, Ecchan, etc.) associated with the ramen company 株式会社凪スピリッツジャパン (Nagi Spirits Japan), which also runs the well-known "Nagi" nibo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: シンちゃんラーメン is part of Tokyo's "chan-kei" (ちゃん系) ramen genre — a family of small counter shops each named after a person's nickname (Shin-chan, Haru-chan, Ecchan, etc. ) associated with the ramen company 株式会社凪スピリッツジャパン (Nagi Spirits Japan), which also runs the well-known "Nagi" niboshi ramen chain. The Shinjuku 4-chome shop opened around June 2022, a one-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station and about three minutes from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, tucked in a back alley away from the main Shinjuku bustle.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated approach is to avoid gimmicks and keep things fundamentally simple — a pork-based broth and honest, straightforward ingredients rather than a complicated recipe, which is characteristic of the back-to-basics "chan-kei" style.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba (中華そば) — thin, flat house-made noodles in a pork-based clear broth (豚清湯スープ) with a soy-forward but light-colored tare, piled high with thick, freshly hand-sliced chashu, menma, and scallion. The chilled dipping-noodle version, mori chuka with extra chashu (もりチャーシュー), is also a signature order.

anecdotes.text: The chashu is sliced fresh to order at a live kitchen counter in front of guests, so each bowl gets pork that was just cut rather than pre-sliced and held.
The soup is served piping hot and filled to the brim, with thick-cut, tender chashu, menma, and scallion on flat, chewy house-made noodles; free rice and pickled cucumber are offered on the side.
シンちゃんラーメン belongs to the same "chan-kei" family of shops (e.g. Haru-chan Ramen, which has been listed in the Michelin guide) run by 株式会社凪スピリッツジャパン, the company behind the niboshi ramen chain "Nagi."

cultural_comfort.note: A small Japanese-language counter ramen shop with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the ticket/menu) will help.

places.name: 叶屋（手打ちうどん叶屋） (Kanoya (Handmade Udon))
places.gf_detail: Serves hand-made wheat-flour udon noodles (Sanuki-style, firm 'tsuru-shiko' texture) — udon is inherently wheat-based, and no gluten-free noodle alternative, gluten-free menu, or dedicated prep process is mentioned in any source. The dashi/tsuyu broth is a traditional Japanese preparation that would conventionally use wheat-containing soy sauce. No allergy protocol or cross-contamination information could be confirmed. Not recommended for celiac or strictly gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The dashi broth is built from Rishiri kombu combined with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and iriko (dried sardines) — a fish-based broth used throughout the menu — and no plant-based dashi or vegan option was found in any source. Tempura and other toppings likely also include animal ingredients. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri approx. 11:30–19:30 (one source lists 11:00 start); Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:30–20:00; closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, Taito City — on Denbouin-dori (伝法院通り), a few minute
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in November 2005 on Denbouin-dori (伝法院通り), a shopping street near Sensoji Temple in Asakusa. It is an independently run, hand-made udon shop (operated as Yugen-gaisha Udon Kanoya / 有限会社饂飩叶屋), just a few minutes' walk from Sensoji. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in November 2005 on Denbouin-dori (伝法院通り), a shopping street near Sensoji Temple in Asakusa. It is an independently run, hand-made udon shop (operated as Yugen-gaisha Udon Kanoya / 有限会社饂飩叶屋), just a few minutes' walk from Sensoji.
chef_bio.philosophy: The dashi broth is built from scratch using Rishiri kelp, quality bonito flakes (katsuobushi), and dried sardines (iriko), with no added chemical seasoning (no MSG), aiming for a refined, Kansai-influenced depth of flavor rather than a shortcut broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made udon noodles with a firm, springy 'tsuru-shiko' (つるしこ) texture in the Sanuki style, served hot or cold across roughly twenty menu variations, including tempura-topped bowls and set meals.

anecdotes.text: Located on Denbouin-dori a few minutes' walk from Sensoji Temple, the shop is known as a popular, often-queuing udon spot offering around twenty types of hot and cold udon plus tempura-topped bowls.
The restaurant opened in November 2005 and makes Sanuki-style firm udon, with dashi from Rishiri kombu and quality bonito/iriko, without chemical seasoning, for a refined Kansai-style flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free noodle alternative or dedicated gluten-free prep process was found in any source; the shop's entire menu centers on hand-made wheat udon.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house dashi is made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and iriko (dried sardines) alongside kombu, and is used as the base for the broth across the menu; no plant-based dashi option was found.

positives.text: The broth is made from scratch with Rishiri kelp, bonito, and dried sardines, with no added chemical seasoning (no MSG) — a plus for diners avoiding additives, though it is not a GF/vegan accommodation.

cultural_comfort.note: A local, independently-run udon shop on a busy tourist street near Sensoji; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its location on a heavily-touristed street near the temple means simple ordering is generally manageable with pointing/photos.

places.name: まぜそば 渋谷 チョップス (Mazesoba Shibuya Chops)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen/mazesoba noodles and a soy-sauce-based (shoyu) tare are used throughout the menu; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe process was mentioned in any source. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The mazesoba tare and toppings rely on pork back fat, chicken oil (鶏油), and a soy-based sauce likely containing animal-derived dashi elements typical of the genre; no vegetarian or vegan menu option was found in any available source.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷区宇田川町 (Shibuya Marui / scramble crossing area)
places.notes: Chops grew out of Yoyogi's iekei ramen shop 'Samurai' (侍), which opened the original 'Mazesoba Tamani Ramen Chops' in Yoyogi in December 2021. The Shibuya branch — a one-minute walk from Shibuya Station's A3 exit, right by Marui and the scramble crossing — opened in 2022 as a sec · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: second location of Yoyogi's 'Samurai' iekei ramen group
chef_bio.origin: Yoyogi, Tokyo (opened as a second location of the Yoyogi original)
chef_bio.background: Chops grew out of Yoyogi's iekei ramen shop 'Samurai' (侍), which opened the original 'Mazesoba Tamani Ramen Chops' in Yoyogi in December 2021. The Shibuya branch — a one-minute walk from Shibuya Station's A3 exit, right by Marui and the scramble crossing — opened in 2022 as a second location, reinterpreting Jiro-inspired mazesoba for a busy Shibuya crowd.
chef_bio.philosophy: Take the heavy, garlic-forward DNA of Jiro-style ramen and adapt it into a bowl light enough to work as an everyday, quick meal in the middle of Shibuya rather than an occasional indulgence.
chef_bio.specialty: Mazesoba (soy-based or spicy 'karami'), Jiro-inspired but built to be lighter than a full Jiro bowl, topped with garlic, chicken oil (鶏油) and, at the table, a seasoned crunchy fried-batter topping called 'Chops Bomb' for a mid-bowl texture and flavor change.

anecdotes.text: Covered on SUSURU TV (episode 2402) trying the mazesoba in a large ('大') portion, describing it as a 'gutsy' new Shibuya opening.
A food blogger described it as 'Jiro-style but not too heavy — a bowl that works in Shibuya,' noting it channels Jiro's context while dialing back the weight to suit an urban lunch/dinner crowd.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mazesoba tare is described as soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which in standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari alternative was mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a touch-panel food-ticket machine with photo-labeled menu items, so a first-timer can point-and-order, though the garlic/oil/topping customization prompts are Japanese-only.

places.name: チャルモゴッソヨ 東京ドームシティ店 (Charu Mogossoyo Tokyo Dome City)

Celiac or strict gluten-avoiding diners should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering; this cannot be confirmed safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-specific information was found. Vegans should ask staff about substitutions; a fully plant-based meal is not confirmed available.

places.notes: 「チャルモゴッソヨ（잘 먹었어요）」は韓国語で「よく食べました」という意味。2025年11月13日、東京ドームシティ内のフードホール「FOOD STADIUM TOKYO」内にオープンした韓国酒場で、「映え×健康志向の韓国ダイニング」をコンセプトに掲げている。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 韓国料理チェーン（ワールドリカーインポーターズ株式会社／ZATOグループ運営）
chef_bio.background: 「チャルモゴッソヨ（잘 먹었어요）」は韓国語で「よく食べました」という意味。 2025年11月13日、東京ドームシティ内のフードホール「FOOD STADIUM TOKYO」内にオープンした韓国酒場で、「映え×健康志向の韓国ダイニング」をコンセプトに掲げている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「よく食べました」という店名の通り、写真映えする韓国料理と、マッコリやチャミスルなどの韓国のお酒を気軽に楽しめる“韓国酒場”として、東京ドームシティを訪れる人々に賑やかな食体験を届けることを目指している。
chef_bio.specialty: サムギョプサル（豚バラ肉の炭火焼き）とビビンバ

anecdotes.text: 店名の由来は韓国語で「よく食べました」を意味する「잘 먹었어요」。定番のサムギョプサルやビビンバ、韓国のお酒（マッコリ、チャミスルなど）とともに、SNS映えする“推しカラードリンク”などフォトジェニックなメニューも提供している。
2025年11月13日オープン。ランチ限定のサムギョプサル定食（1,080円）は、ご飯・チャプチェ・キムチ・ごぼう・ナムル・サンチュ・韓国のりが付く構成。

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a major tourist-oriented entertainment complex (Tokyo Dome City) that publishes an English-language shop directory, so basic English navigation is likely, though no dedicated English/allergy menu for this specific stall was confirmed.

Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation was found. The restaurant's identity centers on goat sashimi and other goat/pork dishes, plus champuru and other Okinawan preparations that typically use pork, katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and other animal products. Not suitable for vegan diners.

It is run by a master known to regulars as "Miyachan," who is originally from Okinawa and, according to reviews, previously trained as a French chef before opening this Okin · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Okinawa
chef_bio.background: Gusuku is a small owner-run Okinawan izakaya tucked in a zakkyo (mixed-use) building in Shinjuku Sanchome. It is run by a master known to regulars as "Miyachan," who is originally from Okinawa and, according to reviews, previously trained as a French chef before opening this Okinawan-focused spot.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant aims to bring "uchinaa taimu" (Okinawan time) — a slow, unhurried pace — into the middle of fast-moving Shinjuku, giving guests a calmer, more intimate izakaya experience than typical Tokyo Okinawan restaurants.
chef_bio.specialty: Goat sashimi (yagi-sashi), a rare specialty even among Okinawan restaurants in Tokyo, served when stock allows; also yagi-nabe (goat hotpot with goat offal), mimigā (pig ear), and champuru stir-fries.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the food as curing their "Okinawa-loss" (homesickness for Okinawa), calling it authentic, home-region-quality cooking.
Unlike many bustling Okinawan izakaya in Tokyo, reviewers note Gusuku is quiet enough to relax and enjoy a slow-paced meal.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a local, Japanese-language izakaya with karaoke and no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so non-Japanese-speaking diners should expect a fully local experience.

Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking to confirm what, if anything, can be modified — this listing cannot promise safety.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan option, or vegan-specific accommodation was found in any source. The restaurant's identity and signature dish (foie gras monaka) are built around foie gras and other animal-based French classics (game/jibier dishes are also mentioned), so it should not be presented as vegan-friendly without direct confirmation from the restaurant.

chef_bio.origin: Japan (trained in Paris and Corsica, France)
chef_bio.background: Chef-owner Katsuya Otsuka went to France at 24, spending roughly two and a half years training in Paris (including Michelin-starred kitchens) before finishing with six months on Corsica. Back in Tokyo he refined his craft at respected houses such as Roquabrousse in Aoyama, becoming a head chef at 29 and opening his own restaurant, Ludique, in Asakusa's Kannonura backstreets in 2016.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name is French for "playful," and that spirit shows in Otsuka's inventive starters, but the cooking underneath is described as solid, classical French technique rather than overwrought or experimental.
chef_bio.specialty: Foie gras monaka — a crisp wafer 'sandwich' amuse-bouche filled with smooth foie gras mousse, layered with apple compote and yuzu jam, served since the restaurant's opening and considered its signature bite.

anecdotes.text: Ludique opened in 2016 in Asakusa's quieter Kannonura (behind Sensoji) area as a small, counter-driven French restaurant with 15 seats, 7 of them facing the open kitchen.
The foie gras monaka amuse has been on the menu since day one and is what many guests remember most.
Otsuka trained roughly three years in Michelin-starred kitchens in Paris and Corsica before working at Roquabrousse in Aoyama, Tokyo.

cultural_comfort.note: A small (15-seat) chef's-counter French restaurant run day-to-day in Japanese with course-menu dining etiquette; no English menu or English-speaking-service confirmation was found, so first-time foreign visitors should expect a primarily Japanese experience and may want to reserve via Tabelog's English booking page.

places.name: 大勝軒まるいち 渋谷店 (Taishoken Maruichi Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are handmade in-house from wheat flour, water, egg and kansui (lye water) — there is no gluten-free noodle alternative. The dipping broth and any shoyu-based tare almost certainly contain wheat-derived soy sauce, typical of this style of tsukemen. No gluten-free menu, preparation area, or accommodation was found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, broth, or protein substitute was found. The dipping broth (the core of every tsukemen bowl) is made from pork bone, chicken carcass, and dried fish stock; standard toppings include pork chashu and a seasoned soft-boiled egg. This is not a vegan-friendly kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-22:30, 年中無休 (open every day of the year)

The founder trained directly under Yamagishi (sources describe him as on · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Direct lineage of Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken, the birthplace of modern tsukemen under 'ramen god' Kazuo Yamagishi
chef_bio.background: Opened in Kita-Akabane in July 2007, just four months after Kazuo Yamagishi's legendary Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken — the shop widely credited with popularizing tsukemen (dipping noodles) — closed its doors. The founder trained directly under Yamagishi (sources describe him as one of Yamagishi's very last apprentices) and opened this shop to carry the technique forward; the chain has since grown to a handful of Tokyo locations including this Shibuya branch.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving and carrying forward the noodle-making technique and hospitality spirit of the original Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken after its 2007 closure.
chef_bio.specialty: Tsukemen (dipping noodles) — thick, handmade wheat noodles made from flour, water, egg and kansui, served alongside a rich dipping broth simmered from pork bone, chicken carcass, vegetables and dried fish, topped with grilled chashu pork and a soft-boiled egg

anecdotes.text: The shop's first location opened in Kita-Akabane in July 2007, only four months after Kazuo Yamagishi's original Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken closed, founded by one of his last apprentices to keep the recipe and spirit alive.

The Shibuya branch first opened in March 2016 nearby, closed briefly in January 2017 for a relocation, then reopened later that year at the Udagawa Building spot it occupies today near Shibuya Hikarie.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are handmade from wheat flour, water, egg, and kansui (lye water) — this is a traditional wheat-based tsukemen shop with no gluten-free noodle alternative mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is simmered from pork bone, chicken carcass, vegetables, and dried fish, and standard bowls come topped with pork chashu and a soft-boiled egg — there is no vegan broth or protein option described.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic no-frills Tokyo tsukemen counter shop with no confirmed English menu — expect to order in Japanese or by pointing, and know upfront that the broth and noodles are not gluten-free or vegan.

places.name: 石釜 ベイクブレッド 茶房 タムタム (Ishigama Bake Bread Sabō Tamu Tamu (TAM TAM))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, flour substitution, or dedicated gluten-free preparation was found in any source. The entire menu is built around wheat: stone-oven baked bread, toast, and the signature thick hotcakes/pancakes. In a bakery environment centered on wheat flour and a shared stone oven, cross-contamination risk for celiac diners would be very high. Not recommended for strict gluten-free/celiac needs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu items, egg/dairy substitutions, or plant-based alternatives were found in any source. The signature hotcake, French toast, and stone-oven curry all appear to be conventional egg-, butter-, and milk-based preparations. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

Tamura Nobuyuki naturally took over the family business and gradually shifted its focus to coffee and new food offerings. When the building needed reconstruction due to age, he closed for about a year, then par · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (born December 3, 1957)
chef_bio.background: The building has housed a family-run tea house (kissaten) since 1973. Tamura Nobuyuki naturally took over the family business and gradually shifted its focus to coffee and new food offerings. When the building needed reconstruction due to age, he closed for about a year, then partnered with a designer to rebuild the space and rethink the menu, reopening on September 20, 2014 as the modern stone-oven café "石釜bake bread 茶房 TAM TAM. "
chef_bio.philosophy: Business consultant Isao Endo and Hitotsubashi University emeritus professor Ikujiro Nonaka featured Tamura and TAM TAM's stone-oven hotcake as a case study in Endo's book on how an everyday dish like the pancake can become the basis of a distinctive, thriving business.
chef_bio.specialty: 石釜焼きホットケーキ — a very thick stone-oven-baked hotcake/pancake, crisp on the outside and fluffy inside from the oven's radiant heat; also stone-oven French toast and stone-oven curry.

anecdotes.text: Management consultant Isao Endo, motivated after a well-known Kanda pancake shop closed in 2012, went searching for outstanding pancake shops nationwide; this led him to profile Tamura Nobuyuki and TAM TAM's stone-oven hotcake alongside Hitotsubashi professor Ikujiro Nonaka in his book on business lessons from pancakes.

The café occupies a tea house that has operated at the same Jimbocho spot since 1973; after the building was rebuilt, the owner took about a year off before reopening the space in its current stone-oven concept form on September 20, 2014.

cultural_comfort.note: No source indicates an English menu, English-speaking staff, or English signage; this reads as a traditional Japanese neighborhood café experience.

places.name: 天兼 (Tenkane)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, rice-flour batter, or allergy-accommodation information was found for this restaurant. As a traditional Edomae tempura specialist, the batter is presumed wheat-flour based and the dipping sauces (tentsuyu) and dashi are presumed to use standard soy sauce (which typically contains wheat) and bonito-based stock; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free options were mentioned in any source reviewed. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation by phone.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is a fixed-course tempura specialist featuring shrimp, fish, and other seafood tempura, with dashi presumed to be bonito (katsuobushi) based per standard Edomae tempura practice. No vegan course, substitution option, or plant-based menu was found in any source reviewed. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:15), Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00), Monday-Saturday; one source describes Sundays/holidays as dinner-only (~17:00-21:30). Closed Wednesdays, with at least one source specifying only the 2nd, 4th, and 5th Wednesdays of the month are closed. Course-menu only (no a la carte).

places.notes: Founded in Meiji 36 (1903) by Kenichi Ishinabe as a tempura stall (yatai) in Yotsuya Iga-cho, Tokyo. The business later grew into a full restaurant behind the Mitsukoshi department store in Shinjuku, and was rebuilt in its current Nishi-Shinjuku location, inside the Odakyu Halc b · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Yotsuya Iga-cho, Tokyo (original 1903 stall location)
chef_bio.background: Founded in Meiji 36 (1903) by Kenichi Ishinabe as a tempura stall (yatai) in Yotsuya Iga-cho, Tokyo. The business later grew into a full restaurant behind the Mitsukoshi department store in Shinjuku, and was rebuilt in its current Nishi-Shinjuku location, inside the Odakyu Halc building near Shinjuku Station's west exit, when the building was completed in 1961.
chef_bio.philosophy: A family business now in its fourth generation, committed to preserving the techniques and style of traditional Edomae tempura passed down since the restaurant's founding over 120 years ago.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style (traditional Tokyo) tempura, served as a fixed course meal (course-only menu, no a la carte)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant traces its roots to a tempura yatai (street stall) opened in 1903 by founder Kenichi Ishinabe in Yotsuya; the current premises were completed in 1961 after the postwar redevelopment that produced the Odakyu Halc building.
Today the restaurant is run by fourth-generation owner Jin Ishinabe, continuing over a century of family-run Edomae tempura tradition just steps from Shinjuku Station's west exit.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No product-level confirmation was found on whether the tentsuyu dipping sauce uses standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce or a gluten-free alternative; as a traditional Edomae tempura restaurant it should be assumed to use standard soy sauce unless confirmed otherwise by phone.

cultural_comfort.note: A formal, century-old course-only tempura restaurant with private tatami seating; no English menu or English-language service was confirmed in available sources, so some Japanese ability or reservation assistance is recommended.

places.name: 韓国料理 Korean Dining 彩（だいにんぐ彩） (Korean Dining Sai)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was mentioned on any source checked. Korean seasonings used across the menu (gochujang, soy-based sauces) are typically wheat-based, and dishes like jjigae hot pots and cheese dakgalbi likely involve shared cooking surfaces/pots. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering; nothing here should be assumed safe without confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant offers a fresh vegetable bibimbap (生野菜のせビビンバ), but this is a general Korean home-cooking menu, not a vegan or vegetarian establishment — it also serves bulgogi, sundubu jjigae, budae jjigae, samgyetang, and cheese dakgalbi. Jjigae/soup bases and bibimbap toppings likely include meat, egg, or seafood-derived stock unless specifically requested otherwise, and no vegan-specific accommodation is advertised.

chef_bio.background: A small, family-run Korean home-cooking (韓国家庭料理) restaurant tucked into a quiet residential corner of Hanakawado, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station, known among neighborhood regulars for hearty, vegetable-forward meals.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes carefully selected ingredients, unhurried preparation, and cooking technique built up over years of experience, aiming to give guests a relaxed, home-style dining experience.
chef_bio.specialty: 生野菜のせビビンバ (fresh vegetable bibimbap) and 石焼きビビンバ (sizzling stone-pot bibimbap)

anecdotes.text: Local Asakusa guides recommend it specifically for its generous, vegetable-packed bibimbap lunch set.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood Korean home-cooking spot with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — basic Japanese or pointing at the handwritten/blackboard menu will help.

places.name: 渋谷横丁 北海道食市 (Shibuya Yokocho Hokkaido Shokuichi)

The menu is centered on wheat-based items — ramen noodles, gyoza dumplings — plus soy-sauce-seasoned Hokkaido izakaya dishes (jingisukan, donburi), and Japanese soy sauce/shoyu typically contains wheat. No dedicated fryer, wheat-free noodle option, or allergy protocol is mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan option found in any source. The menu is meat- and seafood-driven (grilled lamb jingisukan, pork bowl, Hokkaido seafood donburi, chicken dishes), and Hokkaido izakaya broths/sauces commonly use dashi or bonito-based seasoning. No plant-based menu items or substitutions are indicated anywhere.

places.notes: This stall sits inside Shibuya Yokocho, a roughly 100-meter indoor alley of about 19 stalls on the south side of RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK, built by Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho to recreate the atmosphere of a regional Japanese yokocho drinking alley, bringing craftspeople and spe · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Hokkaido specialty izakaya stall within a multi-region yokocho food hall
chef_bio.origin: Hokkaido regional food concept, part of the Shibuya Yokocho food-alley complex
chef_bio.background: This stall sits inside Shibuya Yokocho, a roughly 100-meter indoor alley of about 19 stalls on the south side of RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK, built by Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho to recreate the atmosphere of a regional Japanese yokocho drinking alley, bringing craftspeople and specialties from Hokkaido down to Kyushu and Okinawa together in one place. This particular stall is dedicated to Hokkaido, and specifically Tokachi-area, food and drink culture.
chef_bio.specialty: Sapporo-style miso ramen, Hokkaido seafood rice bowl (kaisendon), jingisukan (grilled seasoned lamb), Tokachi-style pork bowl (butadon), and Tokachi gyoza dumplings

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, all-Japanese-style yokocho izakaya stall inside a busy Shibuya food hall; no confirmed English menu or staff English support was found, so basic Japanese food vocabulary helps.

As a standard Taiwanese eatery, dishes such as gyoza/dumpling wrappers, soy sauce, and fried items should be assumed to contain wheat; cross-contamination risk is likely high given the small, busy kitchen. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff (Japanese needed).
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu options, labeling, or accommodations are mentioned in any source. This is a meat- and seafood-heavy home-style Taiwanese menu (pork dumplings, meatballs with scallop, etc.); dishes very likely use meat/bonito-based stocks throughout. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without extensive kitchen customization, which was not indicated as available.
