It has a lived-in, casual izakaya feel with Chinese-language chatter that regulars say makes it feel like  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small, no-frills Taiwanese home-cooking (台湾家庭料理) restaurant just steps from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, identifiable by its green exterior and red Chinese lanterns. It has a lived-in, casual izakaya feel with Chinese-language chatter that regulars say makes it feel like a trip to Taiwan itself, and it gets especially busy after events let out at nearby Tokyo Dome.
chef_bio.specialty: Water-boiled dumplings (水餃子) with a soft, springy skin flavored with chives, ginger, and sesame oil, and meatballs (肉団子) with a fine-textured, juicy filling studded with scallop for extra umami; crispy pan-fried gyoza are also a favorite.

anecdotes.text: JR水道橋駅西口の改札を出てすぐのところにある台湾家庭料理店で、緑の外壁に紅い中国提灯が特徴。中国語が飛び交い、台湾にいるような雰囲気がある。
東京ドームでのイベント後に賑わう、水道橋駅から徒歩数分の台湾料理店。

cultural_comfort.note: A local, cash-only neighborhood spot with no English menu or online booking evident; basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will be needed to order and to ask about ingredients.

places.name: カナル 新宿 （CANARD） (Canaru Shinjuku (Restaurant CANARD))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation process is mentioned in any available source. As traditional French home cooking, the menu (duck confit, meat and fish mains, cream/butter-based sauces, bread service) relies heavily on wheat — sauces are typically roux-thickened and bread accompanies meals — so cross-contact and hidden wheat risk should be assumed high. Celiac diners should not rely on this restaurant without calling ahead, and even then should expect limited or no safe options.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat and fish (duck confit is the namesake dish — "canard" means duck in French) with butter- and cream-based French sauces; no vegan, vegetarian, or plant-based options, and no mention of dashi/bonito use since this is French rather than Japanese cuisine, but animal-based stocks and butter are standard throughout. No vegan accommodation was found in any available source.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（Shinjuku Sanchome）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The restaurant is run as a small family business; the owner's husband is French, and the French home cooking she learned for her own family became the basis of the restaurant's menu. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The restaurant is run as a small family business; the owner's husband is French, and the French home cooking she learned for her own family became the basis of the restaurant's menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Casual, unpretentious French home cooking (家庭料理) in generous portions, drawing on recipes from across France's regions, served in a relaxed bistro atmosphere rather than a formal grand-maison setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Duck confit (鴨のコンフィ) — fittingly, "canard" is French for duck, the restaurant's namesake

anecdotes.text: Housed in a basement space down a Shinjuku alley, the restaurant is described as a hidden bistro-style spot about five minutes' walk from Isetan Shinjuku, popular for its hearty, home-style French cooking and warm, homey atmosphere — including for birthday celebrations.
A blogger described CANARD as a Shinjuku-Sanchome spot with generous French home cooking and a friendly, chatty madame running the room.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, 19-seat neighborhood bistro run by a Japanese owner with a French husband; no English menu or English-language service was confirmed, so basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will help.

places.name: しゃぶしゃぶ れたす 渋谷センター街店 (Shabu-shabu Retasu Shibuya Center-gai)
places.gf_detail: The finishing-course menu explicitly lists a gluten-free Chinese noodle alongside the regular Chinese noodle, pho, egg porridge, and cheese risotto options, so a specific GF starch option exists. However, no source confirms whether the 8 dipping sauces (tare) or any of the 8 broths are made with wheat-free soy sauce, and no allergy/cross-contamination policy could be found. Each guest's shabu-shabu is cooked in their own individual pot (not a shared communal pot), which may reduce cross-contact risk versus a shared hot pot, but this was not explicitly stated as an allergy safeguard by the restaurant. Celiac diners should confirm tare/broth ingredients with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dashi across the restaurant's broths is bonito(katsuobushi)-based, not plant-based, and the specialty dish (aged beef tongue) is meat. No source confirms a dashi-free or fully vegetable-based broth among the 8 options, or a vegan-labeled tare. Vegetables are a major focus (about 20 farm-direct varieties, sold on a fresh vegetable platter) and could be eaten raw/plain, but a vegan diner would need to ask staff which broth (if any) and sauces avoid fish and animal products.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-22:00 daily (L.O. food 21:30, last entry 21:00)
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 宇田川町（センター街）
places.notes: Shabu-shabu Retasu built its concept around "one person, one pot" (一人一鍋) — rather than sharing a communal hot pot, every guest gets their own individual pot so they can pick their own broth, dipping sauce, and pace. The Shibuya Center-gai branch sits two minutes from Shibuya Stat · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of the "Shabu-shabu Retasu" chain, which opened its first shop in Nakameguro, Tokyo in November 2017 before expanding to Shibuya, Sapporo, Musashi-Kosugi and Susukino.
chef_bio.background: Shabu-shabu Retasu built its concept around "one person, one pot" (一人一鍋) — rather than sharing a communal hot pot, every guest gets their own individual pot so they can pick their own broth, dipping sauce, and pace. The Shibuya Center-gai branch sits two minutes from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit in a wood-toned dining room.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's stated commitment is to a "drinkable dashi" — an additive-free stock made from five kinds of carefully selected bonito flakes — paired with eight broth styles and eight dipping sauces so each diner can compose the meal to their own taste rather than following a fixed course.
chef_bio.specialty: Individually-portioned shabu-shabu built around fresh vegetables sourced directly from contract farms (around 20 varieties) paired with aged beef tongue, finished with a choice of noodles including a gluten-free Chinese-noodle option.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant describes offering 8 types of broth and roughly 50 menu items centered on fresh vegetables, with finishing options spanning Chinese noodles, a gluten-free Chinese noodle, pho, egg porridge, and cheese risotto.

The concept page frames the restaurant's core promise as an additive-free, bonito-based "drinkable" dashi made from five selected types of katsuobushi, alongside 20 varieties of vegetables from contract farms.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Each guest is served their own individual shabu-shabu pot rather than sharing one communal pot with the table, which is the core '一人一鍋' (one person, one pot) concept of the restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant's signature dashi is described as an additive-free stock made from five kinds of selected bonito (katsuobushi) flakes, used across its broths.

positives.text: A gluten-free Chinese noodle is offered as one of the finishing-course choices, alongside regular noodles, pho, egg porridge, and cheese risotto.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern casual chain restaurant popular with young, mixed-language Shibuya crowds; basic Japanese or pointing at the picture-based menu should be enough, though no English menu was confirmed.

places.name: 炭火串焼ヒヨク之トリ 水道橋店 (Sumibi Kushiyaki Hiyoku no Tori Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. Traditional yakitori tare (glaze) is soy-sauce based and contains wheat; even shio (salt-only) skewers share the same binchotan grill as tare-basted skewers, so cross-contact is likely. Diners with celiac disease should not assume safety here without calling ahead and should expect no dedicated gluten-free accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling found. This is a chicken-skewer specialty restaurant; the menu is meat-centric, and no confirmation of vegan-suitable side dishes, dashi-free preparations, or bonito/katsuo-free options was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 17:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:20); Sat/Sun/Holidays: 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:20). Some sources note a shift to irregular (不定休) closed days — confirm current schedule before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi / Kanda-Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku (near Tokyo Dome)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hiyoku no Tori opened its Suidobashi branch in July 2020 as the second location of its acclaimed Azabu Juban yakitori restaurant, bringing the same charcoal-grilling craft to diners near Tokyo Dome. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Second branch of the Azabu Juban original, which has repeatedly been named to Tabelog's Top 100 Yakitori Restaurants in Japan (2018, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2024).
chef_bio.background: Hiyoku no Tori opened its Suidobashi branch in July 2020 as the second location of its acclaimed Azabu Juban yakitori restaurant, bringing the same charcoal-grilling craft to diners near Tokyo Dome.
chef_bio.philosophy: Skilled grillers cook each skewer individually over binchotan to bring out the chicken's full flavor, pairing the food with original sours (including a whole-lemon sour) designed to complement grilled chicken; a strong ventilation system is emphasized so guests don't leave smelling of smoke.
chef_bio.specialty: Kōshū Shingen-dori (a prized branded chicken from Yamanashi) grilled skewer-by-skewer over top-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal; the restaurant has also run limited menus featuring the rare "Tokyo Shamo" heritage game fowl.

anecdotes.text: For its first anniversary, the Suidobashi branch ran a limited membership/course offering that let guests try both the rare 'Tokyo Shamo' heritage chicken and its usual Kōshū Shingen-dori side by side.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All skewers, whether salt (shio) or sauce (tare) based, are grilled on the same shared binchotan charcoal grill, so cross-contact between wheat-containing tare and other skewers is likely; no dedicated gluten-free grilling process was found in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's tare glaze for yakitori is a traditional soy-sauce-based sauce, which in standard Japanese yakitori preparation contains wheat.

positives.text: The restaurant highlights a strong ventilation/smoke-extraction system for the dining room, though this addresses odor/comfort rather than food allergens.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood yakitori counter aimed mostly at local and Japanese-speaking diners, with menus and staff interaction primarily in Japanese, though online reservation platforms (Hotpepper, Ikyu, Autoreserve) offer some English-adjacent booking support.

places.name: D.I.Y. salad & delicatessen（ディーアイワイ サラダ アンド デリカテッセン） (D.I.Y. Salad & Delicatessen)
places.gf_detail: The shop is marketed (via its operator's brand page and a Shinjuku wheat/dairy/egg-free dining roundup) as offering gluten-free, refined-sugar-free and additive-free items, and the build-your-own format lets diners skip bread, croutons or breaded toppings. However, this is a shared deli counter also handling meat, bread-adjacent items and prepared dressings, and no source itemizes which dressings contain soy sauce or other wheat-derived ingredients, or describes dedicated equipment/cross-contact protocols. Celiac diners should ask staff directly which toppings and dressings are wheat-free before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The chopped-salad, build-your-own format allows diners to construct a fully plant-based bowl from vegetable, grain and legume toppings, and the shop is described in dining roundups as vegan-accommodating. Exact dressing recipes (e.g., whether any contain honey, dashi, or other animal-derived ingredients) are not published online, so vegan diners should confirm dressing ingredients with staff at the counter.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 daily (hours follow LUMINE Shinjuku LUMINE 1's operating hours; closures are irregular and follow the building's holiday schedule)
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (LUMINE 1 B2F, directly at Shinjuku Station s
places.notes: D.I.Y. SALAD & DELICATESSEN is a made-to-order chopped-salad and deli counter tucked into the basement food floor of LUMINE 1 at Shinjuku Station, run by the ADemotion delicatessen brand group (Cardenas Co.). It was built around the idea of letting time-pressed Shinjuku office wo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: D. I. Y. SALAD & DELICATESSEN is a made-to-order chopped-salad and deli counter tucked into the basement food floor of LUMINE 1 at Shinjuku Station, run by the ADemotion delicatessen brand group (Cardenas Co. ). It was built around the idea of letting time-pressed Shinjuku office workers and shoppers assemble their own bowl — base, protein, vegetables, toppings and dressing — rather than choosing from a fixed menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Positioned as a health-conscious, nutrient-dense alternative to typical station fast food — the shop markets its food as gluten-free-friendly, refined-sugar-free and additive-free, aimed at health-minded women and commuters wanting a customizable, vegetable-forward meal on the go.
chef_bio.specialty: The 'Super D. I. Y. Special' build-your-own chopped salad, piled with a large volume of vegetables and a choice of meat or deli toppings.

anecdotes.text: A Shinjuku-area lifestyle magazine profiled it as a hugely popular, nutrient-packed salad specialty shop inside LUMINE Shinjuku, highlighting its healthy positioning and popularity with local shoppers.
A food blogger who visited for lunch described it as a chopped-salad specialist inside LUMINE Shinjuku offering a substantial vegetable-based lunch with build-your-own toppings and dressings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source describes a dedicated gluten-free prep area; the counter also prepares meat and other deli items alongside salads, so cross-contact for gluten and other allergens has not been independently confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Store listings note the shop accommodates vegan diners and allergy-related requests alongside its gluten-free-friendly, additive-free positioning, but do not describe a formal allergy protocol.

positives.text: Marketed as offering gluten-free, refined-sugar-free and additive-free food and drink options for health-conscious customers.
The build-your-own ('D.I.Y.') format lets diners choose every topping and dressing themselves, making it easier to avoid specific ingredients compared to a fixed-menu restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: A mall food-hall counter inside LUMINE Shinjuku with an English-language Tabelog listing and simple point-and-build ordering, so basic English/pointing should get you through, though menu boards and dressing ingredient details are likely Japanese-only.

places.name: ラーメン龍の家 新宿小滝橋通り店 (Ramen Tatsunoya Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol is mentioned in any source. Noodles are standard wheat noodles and the tonkotsu broth/shoyu-based tare very likely contain wheat (soy sauce). Not a safe choice for celiac or wheat-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every broth on the menu is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based, and the shop's signature dish is offal (motsu) tsukemen. No plant-based broth, vegetarian, or vegan menu items are mentioned in any source found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00, closed Tuesdays (open on public holidays); some secondary listings give a later close (up to 23:30 or midnight) so exact closing time should be confirmed on arrival
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku / West Shinjuku)
places.cuisine: Tonkotsu ramen and tsukemen with pork offal
places.notes: Ramen Tatsunoya (龍の家) was founded in Kurume, Fukuoka in 1999 by staff who had trained at Hakata's famed Ippudo. The Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori branch began life in September 2007 as a stall in the Tobu Department Store Ikebukuro ramen food court before relocating to its current roa · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Tonkotsu ramen and tsukemen specialist, first Kanto branch of the Kurume-founded Tatsunoya chain
chef_bio.origin: Kurume, Fukuoka — birthplace of tonkotsu ramen
chef_bio.background: Ramen Tatsunoya (龍の家) was founded in Kurume, Fukuoka in 1999 by staff who had trained at Hakata's famed Ippudo. The Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori branch began life in September 2007 as a stall in the Tobu Department Store Ikebukuro ramen food court before relocating to its current roadside spot on Otakibashi-dori on July 11, 2009 — the brand's first standalone Kanto shop. The chain has since grown to about a dozen locations across Kyushu, Tokyo, and overseas, and this Shinjuku shop sits among the neighborhood's ramen heavyweights (Ramen Jiro, Ichiran, Menya Musashi, Mongoru Tanmen Nakamoto).
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than following the traditional Kurume 'yobimodoshi' method of continuously topping up the tonkotsu stock, Tatsunoya uses a 'torikiri' approach, cooking down and using up each day's broth in full. The brand describes its style as 'Neo Kurume ramen' — noodles 'born in Hakata, raised in Kurume. '
chef_bio.specialty: Tsukemen motsu — offal (motsu) dipping noodles in a pork-bone base, an item exclusive to this Tokyo shop — alongside the shop's core tonkotsu ramen made from pork bones simmered into a rich, mild-flavored soup.

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori shop opened in its current location on July 11, 2009, after starting as a 2007 department-store food-court stall in Ikebukuro.
Tatsunoya was founded in Kurume 26-27 years ago by ramen makers who trained at Hakata's Ippudo, and has grown to roughly 12 shops across Kyushu, Tokyo and overseas.
The Otakibashi-dori shop is described as expressing 'the spirit of Kurume, without resting on tradition' through its signature tsukemen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tare/seasoning is shoyu (soy-sauce) based tonkotsu, which in standard Japanese ramen preparation contains wheat; no wheat-free tare or noodle alternative is documented for this shop.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine typical of Japanese ramen shops, but the location is well used to international visitors and often has lines of inbound tourists.

places.name: 季節割烹 勝味 (Kisetsu Kappo Katsumi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free labeling, or dedicated preparation was mentioned in any source found. As a traditional kappo restaurant, dishes very likely use standard shoyu (contains wheat), dashi (often bonito-based, sometimes with mirin/soy), and possibly tempura or breaded items, with no indication of a gluten-free alternative or dedicated fryer. Celiac diners should not assume safety and would need to ask directly (in Japanese) before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-friendly claim found in any source. The restaurant's identity centers on daily fresh fish from the market (sashimi platters, simmered kinki, seafood-based izakaya-style dishes), and dashi in traditional kappo cooking is typically bonito (katsuo)-based, so hidden animal-derived ingredients should be assumed in soups, simmered dishes, and sauces unless a diner confirms otherwise directly with staff.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 / 神泉 (Dogenzaka / Shinsen, Shibuya)
places.notes: Katsumi was founded in 1976 (Showa 51) by the current owner's father as a neighborhood kappo restaurant near Dogenzaka-ue. Tsuda Katsuyuki, born in 1975, grew up watching his father cook and naturally set his sights on becoming a chef himself. He trained at Kaiseki Kawabata and H · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 東京都生まれ (Tokyo)
chef_bio.background: Katsumi was founded in 1976 (Showa 51) by the current owner's father as a neighborhood kappo restaurant near Dogenzaka-ue. Tsuda Katsuyuki, born in 1975, grew up watching his father cook and naturally set his sights on becoming a chef himself. He trained at Kaiseki Kawabata and Hanagoyomi in Roppongi before joining the family restaurant, and became the second-generation owner in 2009 when his father retired.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tsuda goes to Tsukiji himself every morning to hand-select fish, and the kitchen emphasizes simple, ingredient-forward washoku built on traditional kappo fundamentals rather than novelty.
chef_bio.specialty: きんき煮付 (simmered kinki/thornhead rockfish) — the restaurant's most popular dish, prepared in an old-fashioned, gently sweet-savory style; the lunchtime oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) is also a well-known specialty.

anecdotes.text: 津田勝幸は1975年に東京都生まれ。料理人をしている父親の後ろ姿を見て育ち、自然と料理の道を志した。懐石川端と六本木の花ごよみにて修業した後、実家である季節割烹 勝味に入店した。2009年に父親である先代の隠居と共に二代目店主に就任した。
毎日店主が築地に出向き、目利きを働かせて仕入れた鮮度の良い魚を6〜7品盛り合わせた豪華な一皿を提供している。一番人気は「きんき煮付」で、昔ながらの優しい味が名物料理となっている。
昭和51年創業の歴史ある味を800円から提供 — restaurant opened in Showa 51 (1976) and is known for its long-standing, affordable lunch dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood kappo restaurant run by a Japanese-speaking family; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found, so first-timers should expect a Japanese-only experience.

places.name: 渋谷西村フルーツパーラー 道玄坂店 (Shibuya Nishimura Fruit Parlor Dogenzaka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodation was found. The signature fruit sandwiches are made with regular wheat bread, and parfaits often include cake, cookie, or wafer elements; menu allergen labels for several items list 小麦 (wheat) alongside 乳・卵・アーモンド (dairy, egg, almond). Not celiac-safe without direct staff consultation, and no dedicated gluten-free process or fryer was documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu items or plant-based cream substitutes were found. Parfaits rely on dairy whipped cream and ice cream; fruit sandwiches use dairy whipped cream between slices of bread. Fresh fruit toppings are plant-based, but every composed dish documented includes dairy (and some include egg/almond).
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka, Shibuya, near Scramble Crossing)
places.cuisine: フルーツパーラー・パフェ・フルーツサンド
places.notes: Nishimura Fruit Parlor traces back to 1910 (Meiji 43), when it began as a high-end fruit shop in Kagomachi (present-day Koishikawa, Bunkyo Ward). The Dogenzaka shop opened in 1935, and the fruit parlor on its 2nd floor was added the following year, 1936. Today the 1st floor house · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Long-established family fruit merchant and parlor
chef_bio.origin: Kagomachi (Koishikawa), Tokyo, 1910; Dogenzaka location since 1935
chef_bio.background: Nishimura Fruit Parlor traces back to 1910 (Meiji 43), when it began as a high-end fruit shop in Kagomachi (present-day Koishikawa, Bunkyo Ward). The Dogenzaka shop opened in 1935, and the fruit parlor on its 2nd floor was added the following year, 1936. Today the 1st floor houses the flagship fruit shop selling premium seasonal fruit for gifts and personal use, while the 2nd-floor parlor serves fruit parfaits and fruit sandwiches, one minute's walk from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, just past the Scramble Crossing.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen prizes each fruit at its seasonal peak, changing the menu roughly seven times a year so parfaits and sandwiches always feature fruit at its best.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit sandwich (フルーツサンド) and fruit parfaits built around whichever fruits are in peak season

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1910 as a high-end fruit shop; the Dogenzaka store opened in 1935 with the parlor added in 1936. The menu is changed about seven times a year to serve fruit at its most delicious moment.
The 'Special Seasonal Fruit Sandwich,' combining Tochiotome strawberries with mixed fruit across two sandwich types, is a signature item at roughly 1,880 yen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or process was documented; wheat-containing bread and cake components are used throughout the parfait and sandwich menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy whipped cream and ice cream are core components of virtually every parfait and fruit sandwich, with no vegan cream substitute identified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Menu listings note allergens per dish (e.g. 乳・小麦・卵・アーモンド — dairy, wheat, egg, almond), but no specific gluten-free or vegan accommodation protocol beyond standard allergen labeling was found.

positives.text: Fresh, high-quality seasonal fruit is the centerpiece of every dish, giving naturally gluten-free and plant-based components (the fruit itself) even though prepared dishes are not GF/vegan overall.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available and the spot is a well-known tourist stop right off Shibuya Scramble Crossing, but staff English is limited, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.

places.name: 讃岐うどん大使 水道橋麺通団 (Suidobashi Mentsudan (Sanuki Udon Ambassador)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour Sanuki udon specialist — the noodles themselves are made from wheat, and no gluten-free noodle, rice-noodle substitute, or allergen menu was found in any source. Tempura sides (self-served from a shared counter) are fried in a shared fryer alongside wheat-battered items, and shoyu-based dipping sauces are standard. No dedicated fryer, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or staff allergy protocol was found. Not suitable for a celiac or wheat-allergic diner.
places.vegan_detail: The house dashi is iriko-based (dried sardines/anchovies), used as the base for the hot and cold kake broths that most menu items rely on. No vegan or dashi-free option, plant-based menu, or vegan labeling was found in any source. Dry/tossed styles like bukkake or zaru with soy-based tare might reduce but not eliminate animal-derived ingredients (shoyu-based tare can itself contain bonito extract), and this was not confirmed either way — not recommended for a strict vegan diner without asking staff directly (Japanese-only environment).
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 10:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:15) ／ 土日祝 10:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30)、不定休（older sources cite 10:00-23:30 as historical hours, possibly outdated）
places.neighborhood: 文京区 本郷 (Suidobashi / across from Tokyo Dome City)
places.notes: Suidobashi Mentsudan opened in July 2009 as the second Tokyo branch of the Mentsudan lineage — the group whose 1990s guerrilla udon-hunting guidebooks (published out of Kagawa Prefecture) is credited with sparking Tokyo's Sanuki udon boom. The manager, a Kagawa native, worked at  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Managed by Shinji Yamanaka (山中信二), a native of Kagawa Prefecture and former member of the original "Mentsudan" (麺通団) group
chef_bio.origin: Kagawa Prefecture (the birthplace of Sanuki udon)
chef_bio.background: Suidobashi Mentsudan opened in July 2009 as the second Tokyo branch of the Mentsudan lineage — the group whose 1990s guerrilla udon-hunting guidebooks (published out of Kagawa Prefecture) is credited with sparking Tokyo's Sanuki udon boom. The manager, a Kagawa native, worked at the original Nishi-Shinjuku "Tokyo Mentsudan" before opening "Kichijoji Mentsudan" in 2007 and then this Suidobashi shop, located in the business district across from Tokyo Dome City, in 2009.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the real, no-frills self-service Sanuki udon experience to Tokyo — order your noodles, help yourself to tempura and sides from the counter, and pay at the register at the end, just as at the honke (originator) shops in Kagawa.
chef_bio.specialty: Kake udon finished with iriko (dried-sardine) dashi poured from a self-serve tap on the wall, and the shop's popular mentai kama-tama udon (mentaiko and raw egg tossed with hot noodles).

anecdotes.text: Diners pour their own iriko dashi from a tap mounted on the wall before eating, part of the shop's deliberately rustic, self-service Sanuki-style setup.

The shop is the second Tokyo offshoot of the original "Mentsudan" group, opened in July 2009 by a Kagawa-native manager who had previously run the Nishi-Shinjuku Tokyo Mentsudan and the Kichijoji branch.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop's signature broth is iriko dashi, made from dried sardines/anchovies — a fish-based stock used throughout the menu, not a vegan option.
vegan_cross_contact.source: search summary of http://ss-foodlabo.com/restaurant/restaurant_detail.php?id=93 and related listings

cultural_comfort.note: This is a self-service Sanuki udon shop: order and receive your noodles first, help yourself to tempura and side dishes from the counter, then pay at the register — the multi-step flow can be confusing on a first visit, but pointing at the picture menu works fine.

places.name: 串焼き石川 (Kushiyaki Ishikawa)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free protocol or menu was found. As a tare-based kushiyaki shop, the standard basting sauce is soy-sauce based (contains wheat), and tsukune (chicken meatballs) commonly use a wheat-based binder. Ordering skewers shio (salt-grilled, no tare) would reduce but not eliminate wheat exposure, since the same charcoal/electric grill and tare-brushing tools are shared across items. Celiac-level diners should ask staff directly and treat this as a use-caution, not a confirmed-safe, option.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around Oyama-dori chicken (sashimi and grilled skewers); no vegetarian or vegan dishes were identified in any source. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Regularly Mon-Sat 17:30-23:30 (L.O. food 22:30, drinks 23:00); closed Sundays, and closed on Mondays that fall on a public holiday
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (West Shinjuku), near Shinjuku Station west e
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, second-floor hideaway a few minutes from Shinjuku Station's west exit, done up in a Japanese folk-craft (mingei) style with a counter plus a few tables — the kind of unassuming local spot regulars duck into after work. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Kushiyaki (grilled skewer) izakaya specializing in Oyama-dori chicken
chef_bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: A small, second-floor hideaway a few minutes from Shinjuku Station's west exit, done up in a Japanese folk-craft (mingei) style with a counter plus a few tables — the kind of unassuming local spot regulars duck into after work.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around a single high-quality chicken breed (Oyama-dori) and careful skewer-by-skewer grilling rather than a sprawling menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Oyama-dori (大山鶏) chicken sashimi served with wasabi, alongside skewers such as breast with wasabi, plum-shiso, cheese, or yuzu-kosho, plus thigh, negima, tsukune, and skin — grilled using a combination of charcoal and electric heat to bring out the chicken's flavor.

anecdotes.text: Skewers start from around 150 yen — inexpensive for the Shinjuku area — with a typical dinner running about 4,000-4,999 yen.
The restaurant is known for its chicken sashimi from Oyama-dori chicken, dressed simply with wasabi, as its signature dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and no evidence of English-speaking service, though its Tabelog listing does have an English page for browsing before you go.

places.name: 弁天 (Benten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodles or dedicated preparation are mentioned anywhere. This is a traditional Edo-style soba-ya; the noodles are standard soba (not confirmed as 100%/juwari buckwheat), the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce based, and the menu includes tempura. No source mentions a gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation — not a safe choice for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on seafood and meat: clam (hamaguri) soba, oyster (kaki) nanban, herring (nishin) soba, chicken-based mujina soba, and pork katsu dishes. Broths are bonito (katsuobushi) dashi-based. No vegan options, substitutions, or dashi alternatives are mentioned in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun 11:30–21:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Wednesdays. Note: following the March 2026 renovation/reopening, some recent listings describe hours simply as "11:30 until sold out," so the fixed closing times above may no longer be exact.

It closed temporarily for seismic reinforcement and renovation work and reopened in March 2026, continuing to serve the same backstreet clientele it always has. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood soba shop in Asakusa's Kannon-ura backstreets, founded in 1950. It closed temporarily for seismic reinforcement and renovation work and reopened in March 2026, continuing to serve the same backstreet clientele it always has.
chef_bio.philosophy: A longtime regular describes the shop's ethos as unpretentious: soba here is everyday comfort food, best enjoyed simply — soba shochu cut with soba-yu (noodle cooking water) alongside a few small dishes, finished with a plate of noodles, rather than a ceremonious affair.
chef_bio.specialty: Hamaguri seiro — clam dipping soba with plump, briny hamaguri clams. Originally offered only in spring, it proved so popular that it became a year-round fixture. Seasonal specialties include kaki nanban (oyster soba) in winter and nishin soba (herring soba).

anecdotes.text: A writer recalls being taken to Benten by his grandfather and describes it as a tiny, spotlessly kept shop seating only about a dozen people, where the ritual is soba-yu-wari (soba shochu diluted with the noodles' cooking water) with small dishes before the soba itself — soba, he writes, 'shouldn't be eaten pretentiously, just as everyday comfort food.'
The clam dipping soba (hamaguri seiro) began as a spring-only special but became so popular with regulars that it is now served year-round.
Founded in 1950, the shop closed for seismic reinforcement and renovation work and reopened on March 5, 2026.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local soba-ya with no confirmed English menu and cash-only payment — an authentic neighborhood experience rather than a tourist-oriented one.

places.name: チャオタイ 渋谷道玄坂店 (Chao Thai Shibuya Dogenzaka)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or celiac-safety protocol was found. As a Thai kitchen, many dishes use rice noodles (e.g., pad thai) which are naturally wheat-free, but soy sauce and other seasonings used in Thai-Japanese cooking can contain wheat, and there is no confirmation of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Anyone with celiac disease should ask staff directly about specific dishes and sauces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. A vegetable-forward pad thai and vegetable/salad dishes (e.g., papaya salad) exist on the menu, but standard Thai cooking here relies on fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste for flavoring, and these are not confirmed to be omitted or substituted. Vegans should ask staff to confirm that fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste are excluded from a specific dish before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); no fixed closed day

It has since grown into a small group of Thai restaurants (Yokohama, Ginza, Shibuya Dogenzaka, Sh · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1993 in Yokohama's Isezaki-chō district; the Shibuya Dōgenzaka shop is one of several branches the group now runs across the Kanto region.
chef_bio.background: Chao Thai opened in Yokohama in 1993, at a time when Thai food was still largely unfamiliar in Japan, and became one of the pioneering authentic Thai restaurants in the Kanto region. It has since grown into a small group of Thai restaurants (Yokohama, Ginza, Shibuya Dogenzaka, Shibuya Axsh and others).
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant aims to serve Thai food authentic enough that Thai customers themselves recognize it as 'the real thing,' with Thai cooks preparing the food in a spacious dining room used for everything from casual meals to parties and banquets.
chef_bio.specialty: Pad Thai (including an egg/shrimp version and a vegetable-forward version) and the multi-course 'Chao Thai Course,' plus Tom Yum Kung, green curry, and papaya salad (som tam).

anecdotes.text: Chao Thai holds Thai Select certification, a mark awarded by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce to restaurants outside Thailand that meet its standards for authentic Thai cuisine.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known Shibuya Thai restaurant with an English-language Tabelog listing and delivery presence, so some English/tourist accommodation is likely, though staff English ability isn't independently confirmed.

Celiac diners should not assume safety here and would need to inquire directly in Japanese before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused kappo/izakaya centered on sashimi and dishes such as hamo (pike conger) shabu-shabu, suppon (soft-shell turtle) hot pot, and octopus shabu-shabu; no vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. Dashi is very likely bonito/katsuo-based, as is standard for this style of Japanese cuisine. Not recommended for vegan diners.

chef_bio.origin: Aogashima, Izu Islands, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The owner was born and raised on Aogashima, a remote volcanic island in the Izu chain with a population of roughly 160 people. The restaurant opened in Nishi-Shinjuku on March 24, 2014, bringing island food and drink to the mainland for people who cannot easily make the difficult boat/helicopter trip to Aogashima itself.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithfully reproduces Aogashima and wider Izu/Ogasawara island home cooking in Tokyo, using seafood, vegetables, salt and shochu sourced from the islands rather than mainland substitutes.
chef_bio.specialty: Aogashima and Izu-island regional dishes such as simmered sea turtle, muro-aji (mackerel scad) fish cutlet, and ashitaba (island celery) with tuna mayonnaise, alongside the island's rare 'ao-chu' shochu.

anecdotes.text: The owner's family runs a minshuku (guesthouse) back on Aogashima and is also involved in producing the island's rare 'ao-chu' shochu, which the restaurant pours in Tokyo.

The restaurant is described as a way to experience Aogashima's isolated island culture and cuisine without leaving West Shinjuku, complete with an aerial photo of the island marking the building entrance.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally-run izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and standard izakaya service style; English support is not documented, so some Japanese phrasebook or translation help is useful.

places.name: クンバ ドゥ ファラフェル (Kuumba du Falafel)
places.gf_detail: Falafel itself is chickpea-based and naturally gluten-free, but the signature dish is a sandwich served in housemade wheat pita bread. Search summaries mention a falafel plate (salad, grilled vegetables, fava-bean dumplings) as a menu option that could avoid the pita, but this was not independently confirmed, and no source addresses shared fryer oil, cross-contact during pita baking, or wheat in the sauces. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6416-8396) and confirm specifics before relying on this as a safe gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's signature falafel sandwich and falafel itself are consistently described across sources as 100% plant-based (chickpea falafel, hummus, roasted eggplant, fresh vegetables in housemade pita). A vegan menu section including an organic soy ice cream and vegan/gluten-free cupcakes is also mentioned, suggesting a vegan-first menu design. As a Middle Eastern concept (not Japanese), there is low inherent risk of hidden dashi/bonito, though this was not explicitly confirmed by the restaurant itself.

chef_bio.background: Shibuya's Shinsen neighborhood tucked-away vegan falafel specialty shop; the storefront is unmarked and covered in ivy, giving it a hideaway feel that regulars find largely by word of mouth rather than signage.
chef_bio.specialty: Falafel pita sandwich — housemade pita packed with freshly fried chickpea falafel, roasted eggplant, hummus, and a generous mix of fresh vegetables, arranged so the cross-section looks like a bouquet of flowers.

anecdotes.text: サンドイッチはまるで野菜のブーケのように彩りよく盛り付けられ、見た目は華やかだが食べ応えも十分とレポートされている。
看板もなく蔦に覆われた外観で、知らないと店だと気づかずに通り過ぎてしまうほどの隠れ家的な店構えと紹介されている。
ファラフェルはひよこ豆を主原料とした中東風コロッケで、100%植物性・低カロリーのヴィーガンフードとして紹介されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source found addressing whether falafel is fried in a fryer shared with wheat-breaded items; not confirmed either way.

positives.text: Falafel (the core protein) is chickpea-based and inherently free of animal products and gluten before assembly into the wheat-pita sandwich.

cultural_comfort.note: The shop has no visible signage and an ivy-covered, easy-to-miss storefront, so finding it and navigating the menu likely requires some comfort reading Japanese or asking staff, even though the concept (falafel sandwiches) is internationally familiar.

places.name: アタクカフェ (Atacu Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No source confirms gluten-free preparation or a dedicated fryer. The signature tacoraise and curry dishes use teriyaki, gochujang, Caesar, and curry sauces that commonly contain wheat-based soy sauce or roux, and shared kitchen equipment should be assumed. Ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan menu or vegan-specific preparation. Salad bowls appear vegetable-heavy, but dressings, the tacoraise sauces (teriyaki, curry, Caesar, gochujang), and soups may contain dashi, bonito, dairy, or other animal-derived ingredients. Vegans should ask staff about individual dish ingredients rather than assume any dish is plant-based.

It is run solo by owner Ataku Tomohiko (安宅智彦) — the café's name is widely understood to derive from his surname. He has a varied food-industry background spanning Japanese cuisine and pastry work, and han · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: atacu cafe opened in 2023 on a quiet street near the Jimbocho intersection. It is run solo by owner Ataku Tomohiko (安宅智彦) — the café's name is widely understood to derive from his surname. He has a varied food-industry background spanning Japanese cuisine and pastry work, and handles cooking, sweets, and coffee himself.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café centers on vegetable-forward, colorful dishes made with fresh produce, paired with a rotating case of seasonal handmade desserts. The weekly-changing lunch set (soup, a side, dessert, and coffee) is designed to keep the short daily menu from feeling repetitive.
chef_bio.specialty: 旅するチーズタコライス ("Traveling Cheese Tacoraise") — a taco rice bowl served with four original sauces meant to evoke different countries: Japanese teriyaki, Korean gochujang, French Caesar, and Indian curry.

anecdotes.text: A local Tokyo walking-guide feature describes the shop's simple, clean storefront near the Jimbocho intersection and profiles the signature four-sauce 'Traveling Cheese Tacoraise,' plus its generous, colorful salad bowls and dessert case.
The proprietor is identified as 安宅智彦 (Ataku Tomohiko), who runs the small counter-and-table café alone.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, solo-owner neighborhood café that likely operates with a Japanese-only menu and no English signage, but the simple counter/case ordering style is easy to navigate by pointing even without Japanese fluency.

places.name: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 神保町店（十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷 神保町店） (Juwari Soba Sagatani, Jimbocho Branch)
places.gf_detail: The soba noodles are 十割そば — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat used as a binder — stone-milled in-house daily, so the plain soba itself is inherently wheat-free. However, this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: tempura and kakiage toppings are coated in wheat-flour batter and fried in the same kitchen, so cross-contact is possible for anyone ordering those toppings or sharing fryer equipment. No shop-specific information was found confirming whether the tsuyu (dipping sauce) uses a wheat-free soy sauce; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is typically made with regular wheat-containing shoyu. Diners with celiac disease or a severe wheat allergy should order plain soba without tempura and ask staff directly about the tsuyu before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu items, vegan dashi, or vegan accommodations were found in any source. Traditional Japanese soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and no shop-specific evidence suggests this location deviates from that norm, so the standard menu should be assumed non-vegan unless confirmed otherwise with staff.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町（神保町駅より徒歩1〜2分）
places.notes: This branch is one of many locations of the 十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷（さがたに） chain, operated by 株式会社フォーユー (Foryou Group), which runs stone-mill soba shops across Tokyo (Jimbocho, Kanda-Ogawamachi, Shibuya, Hamamatsucho, Tamachi, Shinjuku, Ariake and more). No single founder or personal-history nar · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Stone-mill soba makers (in-house team, part of the Sagatani/Sagataniグループ chain operated by 株式会社フォーユー / Foryou Group)
chef_bio.background: This branch is one of many locations of the 十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷（さがたに） chain, operated by 株式会社フォーユー (Foryou Group), which runs stone-mill soba shops across Tokyo (Jimbocho, Kanda-Ogawamachi, Shibuya, Hamamatsucho, Tamachi, Shinjuku, Ariake and more). No single founder or personal-history narrative was found for this branch specifically — the story that is documented is the company's production method.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated approach is 挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて — freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried. Whole buckwheat groats are ground on a stone mill inside the shop (visible near the entrance), and the flour is used the same day to make 十割そば (soba made from 100% buckwheat flour, with no wheat used as a binder), aiming to serve high-quality soba at everyday, affordable prices rather than as a luxury item.
chef_bio.specialty: 十割そば (jūwari soba) — soba noodles made from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat binder, milled in-house daily; served as simple mori-soba or with tempura/kakiage toppings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and kakiage items are coated in wheat-flour batter and cooked in the same in-house kitchen as the soba; there is no evidence of a separate gluten-free fryer or prep area.

To eat this 100% buckwheat soba gluten-free you should use wheat-free soy sauce or a wheat-free mentsuyu, because regular soy sauce/broth contains wheat — implying the shop's default tsuyu is not gluten-free.
Jimbocho menu centers on wheat-battered fried toppings: 天もりそば (kakiage tempura), とり天そば (chicken tempura), fried fresh in-store — shared-fryer wheat cross-contact.

The 'Celiac heaven. 100% gluten free dedicated kitchen' and 'they change the flour to rice for frying' reviews on FindMeGlutenFree geolocate to a Roppongi, Minato-ku address — a different restaurant, so they do not corroborate Sagatani Jimbocho's handling.
Japanese allergy guidance stating soy sauce need not be avoided applies to wheat ALLERGY (fermentation degrades protein), not celiac disease — standard shoyu remains disqualifying for celiac GF.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No shop-specific confirmation was found on whether the dipping sauce (tsuyu) uses a wheat-free soy sauce; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is typically made with regular wheat-containing shoyu, so this should not be assumed safe without asking staff.

positives.text: The soba noodles are genuinely 十割そば — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder — which is unusual even among specialty soba shops, and the buckwheat is stone-milled in-house daily for freshness.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language self-service ticket machine near the entrance (pay by cash or transit IC card such as Suica); no English menu was confirmed, so a little Japanese or menu-reading confidence helps.

places.name: 喫茶堂 (Kissadō)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information, cross-contamination policy, or allergen menu was found in any source. The signature drinks (fruit tea, fruit/mango jasmine tea, juice) do not obviously contain wheat, but this was not confirmed by the venue, and nothing is known about syrups, toppings, or any food items that might be served alongside the drinks. Ask staff directly before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan labeling or menu information was found. The core drink menu (fruit tea, mango jasmine tea, juice) appears plant-based on its face, but milk-tea variants, honey-based sweeteners, or dairy toppings were not ruled out, and nothing about kitchen practices is confirmed. Ask staff before ordering if avoiding animal products is essential.

chef_bio.background: Despite its old-fashioned Japanese name ("kissa-dō"), this is actually a newer, bright Taiwanese-style tea café tucked on the second floor of a building near Suidōbashi Station, just off Hakusan-dori.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit tea and juice series, especially the mango jasmine tea, with customizable sweetness and ice levels

anecdotes.text: A reviewer called it 'a bright, clean Taiwanese tea café' with counter seating that looks out over Hakusan-dori while calm piano music plays, though they felt the chairs could be more comfortable.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small counter-order tea café with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) will help when ordering.

places.name: ハヌリ 渋谷本店 (Hanuri Shibuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free protocol was found in any source. As a traditional Korean yakiniku restaurant, sauces and marinades (ganjang/soy sauce, gochujang, house yangnyeom sauce) commonly contain wheat, and shared grills mean cross-contact with wheat-containing marinades is likely. Grilled meats ordered plain and unmarinaded, plus simple vegetable sides, may be the safest bets, but this must be confirmed with staff — celiac-safety is not established.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on grilled meats (samgyeopsal, wagyu yukhoe, galbi) and meat/seafood hot pots (nakkopse), so it is not vegan-oriented. Refillable vegetable sides (salad, grilled kimchi, wrapping vegetables) offer some plant-based bulk, but broths, house sauces, and banchan may contain fish-based dashi, fish sauce, or animal products not confirmed vegan. Vegan diners should ask staff item-by-item rather than assume any dish is vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-24:00, continuous service (no afternoon break); food last order 23:00, drink last order 23:30. Happy hour on drinks (draft beer, highball, lemon sour, ¥329) daily from lunch until 19:00. Open every day, including New Year holidays.

places.notes: Hanuri Shibuya Honten is a Korean yakiniku and traditional Korean restaurant tucked in the basement of the A2 Building on Bunkamura-dori, right next to Shibuya 109 and Mega Donki, about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station. It has been certified an "Excellent Korean Restaurant" ( · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Korean yakiniku and traditional Korean restaurant
chef_bio.origin: South Korea
chef_bio.background: Hanuri Shibuya Honten is a Korean yakiniku and traditional Korean restaurant tucked in the basement of the A2 Building on Bunkamura-dori, right next to Shibuya 109 and Mega Donki, about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station. It has been certified an "Excellent Korean Restaurant" (優秀韓食レストラン) by Korea's Korean Food Foundation in a joint inspection with the Hattori Nutrition College, a certification given to restaurants judged to serve authentic, high-quality Korean cuisine.
chef_bio.philosophy: Government-certified commitment to authentic, traditional Korean cooking rather than a Japanese-style adaptation, served in an accessible yakiniku/izakaya format with private and semi-private rooms.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuroge wagyu beef toro yukhoe (Korean-style raw beef tartare), hand-cut naengmyeon (cold buckwheat noodles) in beef-bone broth, and the restaurant's original nakkopse — a spicy hot pot of octopus, beef tripe and shrimp.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant holds the Korean government's "Excellent Korean Restaurant" certification, awarded after an on-site audit by the Korean Food Foundation and Hattori Nutrition College for authenticity of its Korean cuisine.
Its original nakkopse (spicy octopus, tripe and shrimp hot pot) has been noted by fans of the TV series Solitary Gourmet (Kodoku no Gurume).
The house special seasoned sauce (yangnyeom) along with free-refill grilled kimchi, negi salad, house miso and wrapping vegetables are highlighted as signature touches for the samgyeopsal.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward sides — grilled kimchi, negi (scallion) salad, house miso and lettuce/perilla wrapping leaves for samgyeopsal — are described as free refills, giving vegetarian/vegan diners extra plant-based options to fill up on alongside the meat menu.

cultural_comfort.note: Very close to Shibuya Station with an English Tabelog listing and online reservation platforms (Hot Pepper), but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff — expect basic accommodation for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: いもや (Imoya)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-batter tempura counter: the fixed teishoku set is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce and miso soup are dashi-based and very likely contain wheat soy sauce. No gluten-free preparation, alternative batter, or dedicated fryer is mentioned in any source found — not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The fixed tempura set includes shrimp and squid, and the accompanying miso soup and tentsuyu dipping sauce are dashi-based (traditionally bonito/katsuo). No vegan or vegetable-only option, and no vegan accommodation, was found mentioned in any source — not suitable for vegan diners as currently understood.
places.hours_raw: Reported as Mon–Fri approx. 11:30–14:15 & 17:00–18:15 (one source lists 11:30–14:00 & 17:00–18:00); Sat approx. 11:30–14:15, lunch only, no dinner; closed Sun, public holidays, and some Saturdays. Sources disagree slightly on exact minutes — call ahead or arrive early since this tiny counter can sell out.

The original Jimbocho honten (main shop) t · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded 1959 (Showa 34) in Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The shop traces back to founder Saburo Miyata, born in the Taisho era, whose family had earlier sold daigaku-imo (candied sweet potato) — which is why the shop was named 'Imoya' ('potato shop') even though it switched to serving tempura. The original Jimbocho honten (main shop) that Miyata opened in 1959 became a neighborhood institution, packing lunchtime lines of publishing-house editors, salarymen and students onto its famous 7-seat counter before it permanently closed on March 31, 2018.
chef_bio.philosophy: No-frills, fast-fried tempura at old-Tokyo teishoku-ya prices, served at a worn wooden counter with a deliberately retro Showa-era feel rather than a polished dining-out experience
chef_bio.specialty: Ebi Ten Teishoku (shrimp tempura set meal) — a fixed tempura set of shrimp, squid, kisu (sillago), pumpkin and shungiku (chrysanthemum greens) served with rice and miso soup

anecdotes.text: Founder Miyata's family had sold daigaku-imo (candied sweet potato) before he opened the tempura shop in 1959 — the shop kept the 'imo' (potato) name even after the switch to tempura.

The original honten ran for roughly 60 years on just 7 counter seats, with lines forming at lunchtime, before closing for good on March 31, 2018; several branch shops (noren-wake) continued the recipes afterward.

cultural_comfort.note: A small old-school Japanese counter shop with a short, simple two-item menu (no confirmed English signage), so ordering is easy even without Japanese, but don't expect English-language allergy conversation.

places.name: 半笑 (Hanwarai)
places.gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are a ni-hachi (2:8) blend — 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour used as a binder (tsunagi) — confirmed by multiple soba-specialist sources describing the noodle-making. This means the noodles contain wheat/gluten by default; no 100%-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option was found in any source. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) for the signature dish is also soy-sauce based (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, bonito), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No information was found on a dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free noodle preparation area. Not safe for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners as currently documented.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated vegan shojin soba is offered as a menu item, with a broth made from kombu (kelp) and dried shiitake mushroom rather than bonito/katsuobushi, giving a plant-based umami base. However, this appears to be one specific menu item rather than a vegan-adapted full menu — most other dishes (including the signature tempura oroshi soba) use a bonito-based dashi/tsuyu. No information found on whether shared cooking equipment (e.g., the same tempura fryer or soba-boiling water) is used between the vegan dish and animal-based dishes.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷スクランブルスクエア12F, Shibuya Scramble Square 12F)
places.notes: 半笑 (Hanwarai) is a more casual sister concept of the Michelin-listed soba restaurant 玉笑 (Tamawarai) in Jingūmae, opened on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square. The interior, designed by architect Kengo Kuma with earthen walls by a master plasterer, is built to feel like a h · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: soba restaurant, sister shop of Michelin-listed Tamawarai
chef_bio.origin: Sister restaurant of 玉笑 (Tamawarai), a soba restaurant in Jingūmae regularly listed in the Michelin Guide Tokyo
chef_bio.background: 半笑 (Hanwarai) is a more casual sister concept of the Michelin-listed soba restaurant 玉笑 (Tamawarai) in Jingūmae, opened on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square. The interior, designed by architect Kengo Kuma with earthen walls by a master plasterer, is built to feel like a hidden back-alley soba shop tucked inside a Shibuya skyscraper.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carries forward Tamawarai's soba craftsmanship in a more accessible, everyday format, while trying to accommodate a wider range of guests — including adding a vegan shojin soba option to the menu.
chef_bio.specialty: 名物 天おろしそば (tempura oroshi soba, about ¥2,200) — grated daikon soba topped with shrimp tempura served dramatically balanced on the bowl's lid. The noodles are a ni-hachi (2:8) blend made from pesticide-free buckwheat sourced from Hokkaido, Ibaraki, and Nagano, stone-ground with some husk left in for a robust flavor.

anecdotes.text: The vegan soba's broth is made from kombu and dried shiitake mushroom rather than bonito flakes, giving a gentle but deep umami flavor without any animal products.
Despite sitting on the 12th floor of a shopping-complex skyscraper, the roughly 12-seat counter space is designed to feel like a quiet, hidden back-alley soba shop.

The soba is stone-ground from pesticide-free buckwheat grown in Hokkaido, Ibaraki, and Nagano, with the shrimp-tempura oroshi soba served with the tempura balanced on the bowl's lid as the signature presentation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba is made as a ni-hachi (2:8) blend, meaning wheat flour is mixed directly into the noodle dough as a binder — this is a structural gluten content issue, not just a cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi/tsuyu for the signature dish is described as bonito-based with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: WebSearch synthesis, no single primary source page successfully fetched

positives.text: A vegan shojin soba is offered with a broth made from kombu and dried shiitake mushroom instead of bonito flakes, explicitly to accommodate diverse dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside the very international Shibuya Scramble Square complex and welcoming to solo diners, but the restaurant itself is a traditional Japanese soba counter with no confirmed English menu, so basic Japanese or pointing/translation help is useful.

places.name: 桂庵 水道橋店 (Katsuraan Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional soba/udon shop; soba noodles are typically wheat-blended (not gluten-free) and the tsuyu dipping sauce/broth is made with standard wheat-based soy sauce. No source mentions gluten-free noodles, a dedicated fryer, or any gluten-aware preparation — assume standard wheat exposure throughout, including in the tempura used for tenjū bowls.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is built around donburi (katsudon, oyakodon, curry rice, toridon, tenjū) and soba/udon in what is standard bonito (katsuobushi)-based dashi broth. No vegan menu items, plant-based dashi, or vegan accommodations are documented in any source — assume hidden animal-derived dashi throughout.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 11:00-21:00 | Sat: 11:30-21:00 | Sun/Holidays: 11:30-17:00 (one secondary aggregator lists weekday close as 22:00 instead of 21:00)
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神保町・水道橋 (Nishi-Kanda, near Jimbōchō/Suidōbashi stations)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual, quick-service soba and udon shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho known locally not just for its noodles but for its rice-bowl (donburi) side menu. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A casual, quick-service soba and udon shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho known locally not just for its noodles but for its rice-bowl (donburi) side menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Donburi rice bowls served alongside soba/udon — katsudon (pork cutlet bowl), curry rice, oyakodon (chicken and egg bowl), toridon (chicken bowl), and tenjū (tempura rice bowl)

anecdotes.text: Rocket News24's long-running 'standing soba wandering' column profiled this shop specifically because its donburi rice bowls were unusually good for a soba shop, and interviewed the owner about why he puts so much effort into the rice-bowl side of the menu rather than just the noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A local, no-frills quick-service noodle shop with no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff — comfortable for those with basic Japanese or pointing at photos.

places.name: Sensing Touch of Earth（センシング・タッチ・オブ・アース） (Sensing Touch of Earth)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer is described anywhere. Plain specialty coffee (French press/espresso) and hand-whisked matcha are naturally wheat-free, but baked goods, affogato toppings, and the craft beer on the menu are not gluten-free, and no source addresses cross-contamination. Celiac guests should confirm with staff before ordering beyond plain coffee or matcha.
places.vegan_detail: No page confirms plant-milk options (soy/oat/almond) for the matcha lattes or espresso drinks, and no vegan-labeled food items were found. Matcha and black coffee are inherently plant-based, but lattes may default to dairy milk unless a substitute is requested and confirmed available. Baked sweets, affogato, and ice cream are not confirmed dairy-free.
places.neighborhood: 浅草・合羽橋 (Kappabashi), Asakusa, Taito-ku
places.cuisine: Specialty Cafe - organic matcha lattes, spec
places.notes: Sensing Touch of Earth opened on Kappabashi Dōgu-gai (the famous kitchenware-supply street) in Asakusa as a specialty coffee and organic matcha café, with an interior-design showroom on the second floor. It is operated by Budscene Inc. (founded 2009), whose representative is Yūic · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kappabashi kitchenware district, Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Sensing Touch of Earth opened on Kappabashi Dōgu-gai (the famous kitchenware-supply street) in Asakusa as a specialty coffee and organic matcha café, with an interior-design showroom on the second floor. It is operated by Budscene Inc. (founded 2009), whose representative is Yūichi Namiki; the space itself was designed by a firm that normally builds music studios and soundproof rooms, which is why acoustics, lighting, and fixtures were treated with unusual care for a café.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café is built around three ideas — pursuit of a high-quality space, a sense of appropriateness, and food/drink craft — aiming to be a place where visitors experience the room itself (calm jazz and classical music through professional audio equipment) rather than just grabbing a coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-poured specialty coffee (French press) from Foret Coffee, a self-roaster in Shinano Town, Nagano Prefecture, using single-origin beans from Ethiopia, Guatemala and Brazil, plus matcha drinks made by a barista whisking organic matcha to order one cup at a time.

anecdotes.text: The shop names its French-press coffee flights "morning," "afternoon," and "evening," choosing beans and roast profiles to suit the time of day they're meant to be drunk.
Seasonal limited drinks such as a matcha-and-strawberry koji (rice-malt) latte are released periodically using the café's organic matcha.

positives.text: Plain hand-whisked organic matcha and French-press specialty coffee are simple, wheat-free beverages with no obvious hidden gluten source.

cultural_comfort.note: The café draws many foreign visitors and has an English name and Tabelog English listing, but no source confirms an English menu or fluent English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese greetings will help.

places.name: CONROW 渋谷道玄坂（コンロウ） (CONROW Shibuya Dogenzaka)
places.gf_detail: No source mentions gluten-free options, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce. As a Thai pub menu, dishes like Pad Thai and stir-fries likely use soy sauce and oyster sauce (both typically wheat-containing in Japan), and fried items are not confirmed to use a separate fryer. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm ingredients directly rather than assume any dish is safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source. Thai cuisine at this pub-style restaurant relies heavily on fish sauce (nam pla) and likely shrimp paste across curries, salads, and stir-fries; vegan diners should not assume any dish is plant-based without asking staff directly, and there is no confirmation of a katsuo/bonito-free option for broths.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Food L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:30-23:30 (Food L.O. 22:30, Drink L.O. 23:00). Sat/Sun/Holidays: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Food L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:00-23:30 (Food L.O. 22:30, Drink L.O. 23:00). (One older listing showed a shorter dinner window ending 22:00; the 23:30 close appears in more recent listings.)
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂・円山町 (Dogenzaka / Maruyama-cho), near Shinsen Station, Sh
places.notes: CONROW is a Thai food pub tucked in the basement of a building on the Dogenzaka hill above Shibuya, near Shinsen Station. It positions itself less like a typical formal Thai restaurant and more like an easygoing Thai-style pub, serving reasonably priced small plates alongside Tha · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Thailand (via a Shibuya Thai-pub group with a sister branch in Ebisu)
chef_bio.background: CONROW is a Thai food pub tucked in the basement of a building on the Dogenzaka hill above Shibuya, near Shinsen Station. It positions itself less like a typical formal Thai restaurant and more like an easygoing Thai-style pub, serving reasonably priced small plates alongside Thai beer, whisky, and cocktails so groups can share several dishes over drinks. It has a sister location, CONROW Ebisu.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's own description emphasizes affordable small-plate portions so diners can sample a wide range of authentic Thai dishes in one sitting, in a stylish, pub-like setting that intentionally breaks from typical Thai-restaurant decor.
chef_bio.specialty: Gai Yang (Thai-style roasted/grilled chicken) is repeatedly singled out by reviewers as the standout dish, alongside Som Tam (green papaya salad), Pad Thai, Tom Yum seafood soup, and a Thai-style pizza topped with cilantro and cheese.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger described the Gai Yang as tender and well-seasoned with crispy skin, and praised the staff's lively, upbeat energy during a dinner visit.
A review on Thai Hyper (thaich.net) highlighted that all the staff were personable and made for a pleasant dining experience.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu, listings, and service appear to be primarily in Japanese with no confirmed English-language menu or support, so some Japanese ability or translation help is useful.

places.name: 和伊わい亭 (Waiwaitei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac-specific protocol is documented anywhere. As an Italian restaurant, pasta and bread (wheat) are core menu items, and Japanese-Italian fusion sauces commonly rely on soy sauce (wheat) and possibly dashi. There is no indication the kitchen is set up to prevent cross-contamination for celiac-level sensitivity. Diners must call ahead and discuss specific dishes/ingredients directly with staff before assuming safety.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on fish (especially sardines), lamb, and a signature hamburg steak (beef/meat-based), so it is not vegan-friendly by design. No dedicated vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. Some pasta or vegetable side dishes could potentially be adapted on request, but sauces may contain butter, cream, meat/fish stock, or dashi -- none of this is confirmed. Vegan diners should call ahead to ask what, if anything, can be modified.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat Lunch 11:30-16:00 (LO 15:00); Mon-Fri Dinner 17:30-24:00 (LO 23:00); Sat Dinner 17:30-23:00 (LO 22:00); Closed Sunday, with occasional irregular closures and Sunday reservations available on request.
places.neighborhood: Nishikanda (西神田), Jimbocho/Suidobashi border, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2006 near Suidobashi Station, the restaurant is run by an owner-chef who spent years working across a wide range of culinary genres before settling on a Mediterranean-rooted Italian style for this shop, described in local write-ups as a neighborhood casual Italian bar ( · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2006 near Suidobashi Station, the restaurant is run by an owner-chef who spent years working across a wide range of culinary genres before settling on a Mediterranean-rooted Italian style for this shop, described in local write-ups as a neighborhood casual Italian bar (bal) rather than a formal ristorante.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef is described as carefully sourcing ingredients and serving only dishes he is personally satisfied with, fusing 'wa' (Japanese) taste sensibilities into an Italian-Mediterranean menu -- reflected in the restaurant's name, which combines 和 (wa, Japanese) and 伊 (i, Italy).
chef_bio.specialty: Sardine (iwashi) dishes are the house's most popular seafood item, alongside lamb chops and a signature oversized hamburg steak (特大ハンバーグ); the kitchen blends genres freely, pairing Mediterranean technique with flavors adjusted to Japanese palates.

anecdotes.text: A Suidobashi-area walking-guide feature describes the current lunch service as generously portioned and health-conscious, highlighting the owner's attentive, warm service toward guests.

cultural_comfort.note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; this reads as a neighborhood spot oriented toward Japanese-speaking regulars, so first-timers should be prepared to order in Japanese or use a translation app.

places.name: 地鶏 海鮮 十割そば だししゃぶ 日日 新宿本店 (Jidori Kaisen Juwari Soba Dashi Shabu Nichin)
places.gf_detail: The soba itself is juwari (十割) — 100% stone-ground buckwheat with no wheat flour used as a binder — which is unusual and noteworthy for celiac diners. However, no information was found confirming whether the dipping tsuyu, dashi shabu broth, or soy sauce used are wheat-free, and cross-contamination in a kitchen that also prepares tempura-style and other wheat-containing dishes has not been confirmed. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free protocol, so diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the tsuyu/soy sauce ingredients and shared cooking equipment before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-and-seafood-focused izakaya-soba restaurant: jidori (free-range chicken) dishes and seafood (海鮮) are core menu items, and the signature "dashi shabu" and soba tsuyu are dashi-based, which in Japanese cooking is almost always made with bonito (katsuobushi), a hidden animal ingredient. No vegan-specific menu, substitutions, or plant-based dashi option was found in available sources.
places.hours_raw: 16:00～翌4:00（料理L.O. 翌3:00、ドリンクL.O. 翌3:30）年中無休（年末年始・夏季休暇あり）
places.neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho), Shinjuku — near Seibu-Shinjuku Station
places.notes: Nichinichi is a late-night soba specialist sitting at the seam between Kabukicho's entertainment district and its hotel quarter, staying open from 4pm until 4am to serve the neighborhood's night-shift crowd, drinkers, and after-hours diners. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Nichinichi is a late-night soba specialist sitting at the seam between Kabukicho's entertainment district and its hotel quarter, staying open from 4pm until 4am to serve the neighborhood's night-shift crowd, drinkers, and after-hours diners.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its menu around stone-milled buckwheat and carefully prepared bonito-based dashi, pairing rustic soba craft with izakaya-style chicken, seafood, and hot pot for a menu meant to carry a night out from early evening into the early morning.
chef_bio.specialty: Juwari soba (100% stone-ground buckwheat noodles, made without wheat flour binder, blended with a fragrant dark buckwheat flour) served alongside their signature Satsuma Tsuyu Shabu (dashi-based dipping shabu-shabu) and Hakata jidori free-range chicken dishes.

anecdotes.text: Described as a night-focused soba restaurant standing at the boundary of Kabukicho's entertainment area and its hotel district, known for its Satsuma Tsuyu Shabu made with rich dashi broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's own descriptions state the soba tsuyu is made with dashi from thick-cut Satsuma honkarebushi (薩摩本枯本節, dried bonito) roasted over binchotan — confirming bonito dashi in the tsuyu (non-vegan) and that the sauce is a kaeshi/soy-sauce base, which standard shoyu makes wheat-containing.
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://gcft102.gorp.jp/ (via WebSearch summary of 日日 新宿本店 tsuyu description)

gf_cross_contamination.text: General soba-tsuyu facts confirm 'soy sauce comprises a significant portion' of tsuyu and kaeshi is the soy-sauce base of restaurant tsuyu — i.e., the dipping sauce is inherently wheat-risk unless a special wheat-free shoyu is used, which is not advertised here.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch results for 十割そば 日日 そばつゆ 醤油 かえし 小麦

gf_cross_contamination.text: Course examples for the Satsuma dashi-shabu explicitly include a tempura assortment (天ぷら盛り合わせ) finished with itasoba — wheat batter and shared-fryer cross-contamination in the same kitchen.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch results (日日 新宿本店 course menu; hotpepper.jp/strJ001153728, gcft102.gorp.jp)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No result for 日日/Nichinichi soba appears on FindMeGlutenFree or HappyCow celiac listings for Shinjuku/Tokyo — no independent celiac-grade evidence of any gluten-free handling exists.
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://www.findmeglutenfree.com/jp/shinjuku-city/dinner (search returned no listing for this shop)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant is a jidori/kaisen izakaya (chicken, seafood, sake) with dashi-shabu as its signature; searches for グルテンフリー/アレルギー返し return no allergen accommodation or GF protocol for this location.

positives.text: The signature soba is juwari (十割そば) — 100% buckwheat made with stone-ground flour and no wheat flour binder — which is a naturally wheat-free noodle base, unlike standard soba blended with wheat flour.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-language accommodation was confirmed in available sources, so Japanese-language ability or a translation app will help when ordering or asking about ingredients.

places.name: 純天然だしラーメン アートマサシヤ (Art Masashiya (Junten-nen Dashi Ramen))
places.gf_detail: No source (Tabelog reviews, food blogs, magazine features, or business listings) mentions gluten-free noodles, a wheat-free alternative, or any celiac/allergy accommodation. The ramen appears to use standard wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-based tare, which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing. Despite being marketed as "純天然だし" (pure natural dashi) and 無添加 (no chemical additives), that branding refers to avoiding MSG/artificial seasoning, not to being gluten-free. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners without direct confirmation from the shop.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is built from seafood and chicken dashi (bonito flakes / katsuobushi and dried sardines / niboshi, plus chicken), topped with a house-rendered lard-based aroma oil — not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based broth or noodle option is mentioned in any available source.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun, and holidays/pre-holidays: 11:30-15:00. Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Minamidaira-cho, Shibuya (near Shinsen Station / Shibuya Sta
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kanegae was a longtime staff member/executive at the Tokyo select-shop apparel company BEAMS before leaving the fashion industry to teach himself ramen-making. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Okinawa (previous shop) / Tokyo (current location)
chef_bio.background: Kanegae was a longtime staff member/executive at the Tokyo select-shop apparel company BEAMS before leaving the fashion industry to teach himself ramen-making.
chef_bio.philosophy: A conviction that food made purely from natural ingredients, without chemical additives, nourishes both body and spirit — he wanted to spread that idea after leaving the apparel world.
chef_bio.specialty: Junten-nen dashi ramen — a completely no-chemical-seasoning (無化調), 100% natural-ingredient ramen built on a seafood-and-chicken dashi (bonito, dried sardine, chicken) finished with a house-rendered lard-and-sesame aroma oil and a hint of rayu chili heat.
