anecdotes.text: Kanegae first opened a ramen shop called Shima Ramen Masashiya in Naha, Okinawa in 2008, naming it 'Masashi' after the owner of a guesthouse (minshuku) he loved staying at during his time in Okinawa.

In 2011 he and his wife moved the business to Tokyo, opening in the current Minamidaira, Shibuya location under the name Art Masashiya — the 'Art' chosen in tribute to Art Coffee Salon, the kissaten (retro coffee shop) that had operated in the same space for more than 20 years before them.
The shop keeps much of the original kissaten's interior intact, giving the ramen counter a nostalgic, Showa-era coffee-shop atmosphere rather than a typical ramen-shop look.

cultural_comfort.note: A hidden, retro-café-turned-ramen-counter with a cash-only Japanese ticket vending machine and no English menu, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps, though the counter staff are described as friendly to newcomers.

places.name: ガッツォ (Gazzo)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free pasta/bread substitution, or dedicated fryer was found on the restaurant's own pages or major booking sites. The kitchen is pasta- and bread-forward (Italian) and uses a shared charcoal grill and open counter kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. Sauces lean on imported spices/herbs/citrus rather than Japanese soy-based sauces, which may reduce (but not eliminate) hidden-wheat risk from shoyu/dashi common at Japanese-style restaurants; this was not explicitly confirmed either way. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm specific dishes and cooking surfaces.
places.vegan_detail: GAZZO's identity is built around a butcher-shop/charcoal-grilled-meat concept ('spice x butcher's'), with organic vegetables used as accompaniments rather than as a standalone vegan menu. No vegan-specific dishes, plant-based substitutions, or confirmation of meat-stock-free sauces were found. Vegan diners should call ahead to ask which vegetable dishes can be made without meat, butter, or animal-based stock.
places.hours_raw: Lunch Mon-Fri 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner Mon-Thu & Sat 18:00-21:30 (L.O.), Fri 18:00-22:00 (L.O.); Sunday/holidays normally closed but can open irregularly for pre-booked parties of 10+.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区三崎町（水道橋駅より徒歩3分）
places.notes: The chef trained originally as a dolce (pastry) craftsman before shifting to charcoal-grilled Italian cooking, which shows up in the restaurant's habit of presenting handmade cakes to guests celebrating birthdays and anniversaries. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: "Tecchan" (てっちゃん), referred to on the restaurant's own materials as the 'good-mood chef'
chef_bio.background: The chef trained originally as a dolce (pastry) craftsman before shifting to charcoal-grilled Italian cooking, which shows up in the restaurant's habit of presenting handmade cakes to guests celebrating birthdays and anniversaries.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated house motto is 'Umai! ga seikai' ("Delicious is the only right answer") — cooking meat simply over charcoal to let the ingredient quality speak, rather than heavy technique or formal fine-dining pretension.
chef_bio.specialty: Whole cuts of carefully selected meat (from contracted domestic farms) grilled simply over charcoal and finished with dozens of imported spices, herbs, and citrus, alongside organic-vegetable side dishes.

anecdotes.text: The chef, a former pastry craftsman, personally makes and presents handmade cakes to guests dining for a birthday or anniversary.
The restaurant frames its cooking as 'spice x butcher's,' grilling whole cuts of contract-farm meat over charcoal and finishing them with spices, herbs, and citrus gathered from around the world.

positives.text: The kitchen sources organic vegetables and uses fresh, traceable contract-farm meat, and cooking relies mainly on charcoal grilling plus imported spices/herbs/citrus rather than heavy sauces, which may make it easier to identify and discuss individual allergens with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed online; phone reservations (03-6661-2150) and ordering are expected to be in Japanese, so a translation app is recommended.

places.name: 手打蕎麦 ふじや (Teuchi Soba Fujiya)
places.gf_detail: The shop offers 十割そば (juwari, 100% buckwheat noodles with no wheat flour in the noodle itself) as an upgrade from its standard 二八そば (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat blend), so a wheat-free noodle option exists. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, fryer, or boiling water is documented; the shop also serves wheat-battered tempura and likely uses standard soy-sauce-based tsuyu, and both juwari and nihachi noodles are hand-made in the same space. This is not confirmed celiac-safe — cross-contact risk is real and unmanaged as far as published sources show; ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, dashi ingredients, or vegetarian accommodations are documented for this shop. Traditional soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is conventionally bonito/katsuobushi dashi-based, and nothing in available sources indicates a plant-based alternative here.
places.hours_raw: 11:30〜売り切れまで（14時前後）、17:30〜21:00 L.O.（土曜・日曜・祝日休み）
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku / West Shinjuku), adjacent to Tokyo Metr
places.cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦・十割蕎麦・天ぷら
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop traces its lineage to Kashiwagi Fujiya (柏木ふじ家), a Nishi-Shinjuku soba shop founded in 1933. After that original shop closed, its fourth-generation successor — who had trained at Tokyo shops recognized with a Michelin Bib Gourmand, including Soba Tajima in Hiroo and Honji · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The shop traces its lineage to Kashiwagi Fujiya (柏木ふじ家), a Nishi-Shinjuku soba shop founded in 1933. After that original shop closed, its fourth-generation successor — who had trained at Tokyo shops recognized with a Michelin Bib Gourmand, including Soba Tajima in Hiroo and Honjinbo in Shinbashi — opened this reincarnation, Teuchi Soba Fujiya, in December 2019, later moving to its current Nishi-Shinjuku Daikyo Building location to refine the hand-made soba further.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything from milling the domestically-sourced buckwheat on a stone mill to hand-cutting the noodles is done in-house without machines, and noodles are boiled to order just before serving for the best texture and aroma.
chef_bio.specialty: 十割そば (juwari soba, 100% buckwheat noodles), hand-ground on a custom stone mill and hand-cut without machines, served alongside freshly fried tempura.

anecdotes.text: The owner personally hand-grinds buckwheat with a special stone mill and hand-cuts every noodle without machines, a discipline carried over from training at Michelin Bib Gourmand-listed Tokyo soba shops.

The restaurant is the fourth-generation continuation of the old Nishi-Shinjuku shop Kashiwagi Fujiya (founded 1933); the current owner reopened it under the new name in December 2019 after training at Soba Tajima (Hiroo) and Honjinbo (Shinbashi).

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's menu structure offers a 100% buckwheat (juwari) noodle alongside its standard wheat-blended (nihachi) noodle, but no dedicated gluten-free prep area, fryer, or cooking water is documented, and tempura is wheat-battered.

汁は良質な本節からとった出汁とかえしを合わせています (The tsuyu combines dashi taken from quality honbushi/dried bonito with kaeshi) — confirms both a non-vegan bonito dashi AND a wheat-soy-sauce kaeshi in the sauce served with all noodles, including juwari.
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://soba-fujiya.jp/ (shop's own description, quoted in web search snippets)

gf_cross_contamination.text: ベースの蕎麦は二八そばですが、十割そばに変更することも可能です (The base soba is nihachi/80-20 wheat blend, but it can be changed to juwari) — the default product contains wheat; juwari is only an upgrade swap made in the same hand-cutting operation.

Restaurant offers hand-made 100% buckwheat soba AND crispy wheat-battered tempura in the same small shop; multiple listings describe no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no allergen-handling information.

General guidance: 'many dried and fresh versions of soba noodles in Japan are nonetheless mixed with wheat' and soba shops routinely use wheat-soy tsuyu and shared boiling water — celiacs are advised to confirm directly; no source documents Fujiya doing any of this safely.

positives.text: A wheat-free (100% buckwheat) noodle option is a standing menu item rather than a special request, which is a meaningful GF-aware baseline compared to shops offering only wheat-blended soba.

cultural_comfort.note: Traditional local soba craftsman's shop with an all-Japanese menu and no confirmed English signage or menu, though modern cashless payment (card, e-money, QR pay) is accepted.

places.name: ターリー屋 渋谷並木橋店 (Thali-ya Shibuya Namikibashi)
places.gf_detail: The signature format centers on wheat naan baked fresh in-house with free refills, but rice can be substituted as the base, which removes the naan itself from the meal. However, no information was found confirming the curries are made without a wheat-based roux/thickener or wheat-containing soy sauce, and the open kitchen actively rolls and bakes wheat naan throughout service, creating a real flour cross-contamination risk. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free process, so this is not a safe default choice for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: The set-meal format lets you pick 2 curries from a rotating list that typically includes a vegetable curry alongside chicken, keema, and mutton options, but the vegetable curry is not confirmed to be free of ghee, butter, or cream, which are common in Japanized Indian curry gravies. No dedicated vegan menu, labeling, or preparation process was found for this location, so vegan diners should ask staff about dairy/ghee in the specific curry before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 並木橋交差点
places.notes: Thali-ya (ターリー屋) is one of Japan's largest Indian-curry chains, launched in 2002 in Tokyo and since expanded to roughly 44 shops across Tokyo, Kanagawa, Chiba, and Osaka. This Shibuya branch sits right at the Namikibashi intersection on Meiji-dori, on the walk from Shibuya Statio · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: chain restaurant / branch of a Tokyo-founded Indian curry chain
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain founded 2002)
chef_bio.background: Thali-ya (ターリー屋) is one of Japan's largest Indian-curry chains, launched in 2002 in Tokyo and since expanded to roughly 44 shops across Tokyo, Kanagawa, Chiba, and Osaka. This Shibuya branch sits right at the Namikibashi intersection on Meiji-dori, on the walk from Shibuya Station toward Ebisu.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain built its identity by moving away from the idea that Indian food in Japan means only 'curry rice,' instead creating a distinct 'Indian teishoku (set meal)' category — one-plate meals meant to fill you up, with unlimited naan refills and free large-portion rice.
chef_bio.specialty: The ターリー屋定食 (Thali-ya set meal): a choice of two curries served with tandoori items and freshly baked naan (with free refills) or rice, in generous, filling portions.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger described the shop's cramped, narrow interior as part of its charm, and singled out the tandoori set meal as tasting notably good.
The shop is noted for a dedicated takeout counter where customers can order curry-and-naan or curry-and-rice bento boxes directly from outside.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-run chain Indian-curry shop primarily oriented to local diners; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service for this branch, though the picture-friendly curry-and-naan set format is easy to point-and-order even without Japanese.

places.name: 立ち食い蕎麦 噛む。 (Tachigui Soba Kamu. (Standing Soba "Chew."))
places.gf_detail: This is a conventional standing-soba shop, not a gluten-free or buckwheat-only (juwari) specialist — its signature noodle is explicitly described as an extra-thick, wheat-blended "wasshiwashi" style, and traditional soba tsuyu/dipping sauce at this type of shop is wheat-based shoyu tare. No gluten-free noodle, sauce, or dedicated-fryer accommodation was found in any source. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's specialty is a meat dipping soba with a choice of beef, pork, or chicken, and no vegan-specific menu items were found in any source. Standing-soba tsuyu at shops of this type is typically built on bonito (katsuo)-based dashi. Not recommended for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho, between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations alo
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened May 7, 2025 on Hakusan-dori between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, the shop quickly drew attention from Tokyo's tachigui-soba (standing soba) enthusiast community for noodles far thicker and chewier than the typical standing-soba norm. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Standing soba shop specializing in extra-thick, chewy noodles
chef_bio.background: Opened May 7, 2025 on Hakusan-dori between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, the shop quickly drew attention from Tokyo's tachigui-soba (standing soba) enthusiast community for noodles far thicker and chewier than the typical standing-soba norm.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's own name, Kamu (嚙む, "to chew"), states its concept outright: noodles thick and firm enough that eating them means real chewing rather than the usual soba slurp, which reviewers say releases more of the buckwheat's aroma and flavor with each bite.
chef_bio.specialty: Meat dipping soba (nikutsuke soba) — choice of beef, pork, or chicken piled over extra-thick 'wasshiwashi' noodles, alongside tempura soba and a limited-time soba breakfast (6:00-9:00).

anecdotes.text: The regular price for the meat dipping soba is 980 yen; for the opening days of May 7-10, 2025 it was offered at a 300-yen discount (680 yen).
Reviewers describe the noodles as 'wasshiwashi' style, thick with strong chew, calling it true to the shop's chew-focused name.
The interior is a chic, black-toned, clean design meant to feel calm enough that women dining alone are comfortable there.

cultural_comfort.note: Standing-only counter shop that orders via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine (shokken), typical of tachigui-soba stalls, with no English menu reported.

places.name: 玄蕎麦しんがり 浅草本店 (Gensoba Shingari Asakusa Honten)
places.gf_detail: See gf_detail field.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around animal proteins (wagyu beef, shrimp tempura, horse sashimi) and the standard tsuyu dipping sauce for the soba is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi, per general soba-restaurant convention and the restaurant's own menu descriptions. No vegan option, dashi substitution, or plant-based dipping sauce was found in any available source, so this cannot be recommended as vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: 10:00〜22:00（L.O. 21:00）の通し営業。休日前日（金曜日を除く）は〜22:30まで延長。無休。
places.neighborhood: 浅草・新仲見世通り（浅草寺近く）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 浅草寺に近い新仲見世通り沿いで営業する玄蕎麦（皮ごと挽いた蕎麦粉を使う蕎麦）の専門店。株式会社ココチーノが運営し、埼玉県草加市にもう一店舗を持つ小規模な2店舗展開。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 蕎麦専門店（十割玄蕎麦・天ぷら）
chef_bio.origin: 浅草・新仲見世通り（浅草寺近く）で営業する玄蕎麦専門店。埼玉県草加市にもう1店舗を構える小規模2店舗展開。
chef_bio.background: 浅草寺に近い新仲見世通り沿いで営業する玄蕎麦（皮ごと挽いた蕎麦粉を使う蕎麦）の専門店。 株式会社ココチーノが運営し、埼玉県草加市にもう一店舗を持つ小規模な2店舗展開。
chef_bio.philosophy: つなぎを使わない完全十割にこだわり、蕎麦粉を自家製粉、麺は注文が入ってから打つ。 蕎麦つゆに添える辛味大根も店主自らが栽培したものを使うなど、自家生産・自家製粉・打ちたてを軸にした蕎麦作りを掲げている。
chef_bio.specialty: 完全十割玄蕎麦 — つなぎ粉を一切使わず、国産玄蕎麦粉100%だけで打った蕎麦。 新潟県・長野県の契約農家から仕入れた在来種の玄そばを店主自身が石臼で挽き、注文後にその都度製麺するという打ちたてにこだわる。

anecdotes.text: 店主自ら新潟県・長野県の契約農家から仕入れた在来種のみの玄そばを石臼で挽き、蕎麦つゆに添える辛味大根も自家栽培している。
その時期最高の国内産玄蕎麦粉を100%使い、注文後に製麺する完全十割蕎麦を、浅草寺・浅草駅近くの新仲見世通り沿いで通し営業（10:00〜22:00）で提供している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba is 完全十割 (100% domestic whole buckwheat flour, no wheat binder), confirmed across multiple sources, but tempura using a wheat-flour batter is prepared in the same kitchen and no dedicated fryer or gluten-free handling protocol was confirmed.
Celiac reviewer notes that while the soba noodles are 100% buckwheat, the broth/tsuyu isn't gluten free; a customer who asked to have a meal made without soy sauce was told no.

Review content surfaced via aggregator listing: staff made sure not to include soya (often containing wheat) in the soup for one celiac guest — indicating accommodation is ad hoc and inconsistent, not a documented protocol.

Shop's own menu lists shrimp tempura, large-shrimp tempura, and kakiage (wheat-battered fried items) alongside 十割 soba, confirming an on-premises wheat fryer and cross-contamination exposure; no GF/allergen accommodation stated.
Menu items include 黒毛和牛すき玄蕎麦 (kuroge wagyu), 海老天 (shrimp tempura), 馬刺し (horse sashimi), 湯葉刺し (yuba) — an animal-protein-centric menu with conventional katsuo-dashi tsuyu, no vegan option found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirmed whether the tsuyu (dipping sauce) uses a wheat-free soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and this has not been verified as gluten-free at this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on animal proteins (wagyu beef, shrimp tempura, horse sashimi) and the tsuyu dipping sauce is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi; no vegan-adapted dish or dashi substitution was found in available sources.

positives.text: The base soba noodle is a genuine 100% buckwheat (juwari) product milled in-house from single-origin buckwheat and cut fresh per order, rather than a machine-made 2:8 blend that always contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits directly on Asakusa's tourist-heavy Shin-Nakamise shopping street near Sensoji, so some visitor familiarity is likely, but no English menu or multilingual staff was confirmed in available sources.

places.name: 老麻火鍋房 (Raoma Hinabebou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, certification, or explicit wheat-free claim was found. The broths themselves (pork-bone/chicken-carcass base with Chinese herbs) do not have confirmed wheat content, but Chinese hot pot dipping sauces and seasonings commonly contain wheat-based soy sauce or bean pastes, and this was not addressed on any source checked. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the composition of the dipping sauce, broth seasonings, and any shared cooking surfaces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Both signature broths (the mild white broth and the numbing mala broth) are made from an 8-hour pork-bone and chicken-carcass stock, so neither is vegan or vegetarian regardless of which vegetables are chosen. Over 30 vegetable varieties are kept in stock and could be ordered as ingredients, but no vegetable-only or vegan broth option, and no vegan menu, was found in any source checked.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 円山町
places.notes: A medicinal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) specialty restaurant in Shibuya's Maruyama-cho nightlife district. The kitchen simmers its own stock in-house for about 8 hours from a pork-bone and chicken-carcass base with vegetables, without additives, artificial coloring, or chemical seasoning, the · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Medicinal hot pot specialty restaurant (薬膳火鍋専門店)
chef_bio.background: A medicinal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) specialty restaurant in Shibuya's Maruyama-cho nightlife district. The kitchen simmers its own stock in-house for about 8 hours from a pork-bone and chicken-carcass base with vegetables, without additives, artificial coloring, or chemical seasoning, then builds two broths from it: a mild white (白湯) broth and a numbing-spicy mala broth infused with 26 kinds of Chinese medicinal herbs. Vegetables used are stated to be domestically sourced (Japan-grown), and the restaurant keeps 30-plus vegetable varieties in stock so a single hot pot meal can cover a day's worth of vegetables.
chef_bio.philosophy: Positions itself around 医食同源-style thinking — food as medicine — aiming to let guests eat a hot pot that is genuinely nourishing for the body rather than just spicy or indulgent, explaining each herb used as the pot is prepared tableside.
chef_bio.specialty: Herbal medicinal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) built on an 8-hour pork-and-chicken bone stock infused with 26 Chinese herbs, served as a choice of mild white broth or numbing mala broth.

anecdotes.text: A local food feature described late nights in Shinsen as good value for hot pot, noting the bold fragrance of Chinese medicinal herbs and urging readers to experience "the real thing" (本物を体感せよ) at 老麻火鍋房.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found; service appears to be standard Japanese-language hospitality, so bringing basic Japanese or a translation app is advisable for communicating dietary needs.

places.name: ちょいバル Bocado (Choi Baru Bocado)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations were mentioned in any source. The menu leans on classic Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, likely wheat-containing) and includes breaded fried items such as アジフライ (fried horse mackerel) and たこの唐揚げ (fried octopus), almost certainly coated in wheat flour/panko and cooked in a shared fryer. Celiac diners should not assume any dish is safe without asking staff directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific dishes or labeling were found. The venue is fish-forward (seared bonito, grilled mackerel, fried horse mackerel, octopus) and side items such as grilled camembert contain dairy. Vegetable-only or dashi-free preparations are not indicated anywhere, and traditional Japanese dashi (typically fish/bonito-based) is likely used in soups and sauces. Vegans should assume very limited to no suitable options without direct confirmation from staff.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区、水道橋駅路地裏（三崎町）
places.cuisine: シーフード・イタリアン
places.notes: Formerly operating under the name "なぎさ食堂" (Nagisa Shokudo), the spot rebranded as "ちょいバル Bocado" while staying at the same back-alley address near Suidobashi Station. It runs a dual concept: a fish-set-meal lunch counter by day and a Spanish-tapas-leaning fish bar by night. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Formerly operating under the name "なぎさ食堂" (Nagisa Shokudo), the spot rebranded as "ちょいバル Bocado" while staying at the same back-alley address near Suidobashi Station. It runs a dual concept: a fish-set-meal lunch counter by day and a Spanish-tapas-leaning fish bar by night.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily rotating grilled fish teishoku with barley rice at lunch; at night, seared bonito (かつおのたたき), grilled mackerel with salt, mushroom ajillo, and fried horse mackerel (アジフライ) with plum-paste tartar sauce.

anecdotes.text: During lunch the restaurant serves a different grilled fish with barley rice and a daily miso soup as a healthy set meal, with free large portions; in the evening it becomes a casual bar with glasses of wine from 380 yen alongside seasonal carpaccio and fresh fish dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A local back-alley fish bar near Suidobashi Station with no evidence of English menu or English-speaking staff — expect a Japanese-only experience.

places.name: 博多もつ鍋 前田屋 新宿東口店 (Hakata Motsunabe Maedaya Shinjuku Higashiguc)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's own brand allergen listing for its motsunabe (used for the same product line sold via mail order) declares wheat, soy, beef, pork, and sesame. The broth is soy-sauce/miso based, and motsunabe meals are traditionally finished with wheat-based chanpon noodles. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct kitchen confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu revolves around a beef/pork offal hot pot with a meat-based (motsu) broth; no vegetable-broth, plant-based, or vegan-labeled option was found in any source reviewed. Not suitable for vegan diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant, and no such confirmation exists in available sources.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30 (food L.O. 14:00) / Dinner 17:00-24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30); open daily, no closed days reported
places.neighborhood: 新宿東口・歌舞伎町 (Shinjuku Higashiguchi/Kabukicho)
places.cuisine: 福岡・博多もつ鍋・郷土料理
places.notes: Maedaya is a motsunabe (beef offal hot pot) specialist that built a devoted following in Hakata, Fukuoka, known there for long queues outside its shops. In February 2026 the brand opened its first-ever Tokyo location, this branch, just steps from Shinjuku Station's east side. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Hakata, Fukuoka
chef_bio.background: Maedaya is a motsunabe (beef offal hot pot) specialist that built a devoted following in Hakata, Fukuoka, known there for long queues outside its shops. In February 2026 the brand opened its first-ever Tokyo location, this branch, just steps from Shinjuku Station's east side.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps the motsunabe broth intentionally light and clean so the natural sweetness and umami of the carefully chosen offal comes through on its own, rather than being masked by a heavy sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Motsunabe hot pot made with hand-selected A5-grade Japanese wagyu beef offal in a light, refined miso-based broth, finished with garlic chives and cabbage from contracted farms; the shop is also known for a sesame-marinated kanpachi (yellowtail) starter.

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku Higashiguchi branch marks Maedaya's first-ever Tokyo location, following years of popularity and queues at its original Hakata, Fukuoka shops.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The Maedaya motsunabe product's declared allergens (wheat, soy, beef, pork, sesame) and the traditional wheat-based chanpon-noodle finish mean the dish as normally served is not gluten-free; no dedicated gluten-free process was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce/miso base, and the brand's own allergen labeling for this motsunabe includes wheat and soy, indicating standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce and miso are used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around beef and pork offal hot pot with a meat-based broth; no vegetable or plant-based broth alternative was found in any source, so the kitchen does not appear set up for vegan dining.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found; expect a Japanese-language menu and ordering experience typical of a Hakata-style izakaya/hot pot shop.

places.name: クワサル（浅草茶寮 Kuwasaru） (Kuwasaru (Asakusa Saryō Kuwasaru))
places.gf_detail: No source describes gluten-free accommodation. This is a standard soba-kappō restaurant, not a gluten-free-oriented one: whether the noodles are 100% buckwheat (jūwari) or a wheat-blended style (nihachi) is not stated in any source found, the dipping sauce/broth is traditionally soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat) and dashi is likely bonito-based, and there is no mention of a dedicated fryer or separate cooking water for gluten-free orders. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should call ahead (03-6802-4936) and confirm noodle composition and broth ingredients before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: No source indicates a vegan menu or vegan accommodation. As a soba-kappō restaurant built around Iwate regional cooking, the soba dipping broth (tsuyu) and many side dishes are likely to use bonito (katsuobushi) dashi and may include fish or meat, and no plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned anywhere. Vegan diners would need to ask in advance whether a kombu-only or vegetable-based broth can be prepared and which regional dishes are meat/fish-free.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays: approx. 12:00-22:00 (some listings show 12:00-23:00 with food/drink last order 22:00); closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 観音裏 (Kannon-ura, Asakusa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained at the now-closed soba-kappō restaurant Akasaka Takegami before opening this Asakusa shop, cooking with ingredients, buckwheat, and sake brought in from his native Iwate Prefecture. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Iwate Prefecture (owner's home region)
chef_bio.background: The owner trained at the now-closed soba-kappō restaurant Akasaka Takegami before opening this Asakusa shop, cooking with ingredients, buckwheat, and sake brought in from his native Iwate Prefecture.
chef_bio.philosophy: A soba-kappō approach that pairs quietly excellent noodles with a changing lineup of Iwate country cooking and Iwate sake, in a small, low-key backstreet setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba made from Iwate buckwheat flour (the shop keeps two different batches on hand, so the character of the noodles varies by the day), served alongside Iwate regional home-style dishes.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, Kuwasaru, comes from an Iwate dialect expression meaning roughly 'the chopsticks keep moving' — a nod to food you can't stop eating.
The owner hails from Iwate and trained at the since-closed soba-kappō restaurant Akasaka Takegami before striking out on his own in the Asakusa backstreets.
Two different lots of Iwate buckwheat flour (including grain from the Yabukawa area) are kept in rotation, so the soba's flavor and texture shift from day to day.

cultural_comfort.note: A small backstreet counter-and-table restaurant in the Kannon-ura area with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service — comfortable for visitors used to ordering in Japanese or with a translation app.

places.name: スタミナラーメン鬼山 渋谷店 (Stamina Ramen Oniyama (Shibuya))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options are mentioned anywhere. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles (Jiro-style, thick-cut), and the tare/soup relies on soy sauce (shoyu) and pork backfat. No alternative noodle, dedicated prep area, or celiac-safety statement was found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are indicated anywhere. The signature dish is built on pork-based stamina toppings (garlic, pork backfat, ginger), and the broth/tare tradition it's modeled on (Takeoka-style) is typically fish/pork based; no plant-based alternative was found.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷区道玄坂 (Dogenzaka, Shibuya) — near Shibuya Mark City / Inoka
places.cuisine: スタミナラーメン・背脂系
places.notes: The shop is an homage to "すず鬼" (Suzuki-Oni), the famed late-night stamina ramen served at 鶏こく中華すず喜 near Mitaka Station, created by that shop's owner. Suzuki-Oni itself is a hybrid dish combining toppings from Chiba's cult "Arilan Ramen," a soup style modeled on Chiba/Uchibo's reg · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Operated by 株式会社INGS (multi-brand ramen operator)
chef_bio.origin: Opened June 26, 2025 by 株式会社INGS (INGS Inc.), the company behind the popular light-broth ramen chain らぁ麺はやし田; this is a rare foray into rich, stamina-style ramen for the company.
chef_bio.background: The shop is an homage to "すず鬼" (Suzuki-Oni), the famed late-night stamina ramen served at 鶏こく中華すず喜 near Mitaka Station, created by that shop's owner. Suzuki-Oni itself is a hybrid dish combining toppings from Chiba's cult "Arilan Ramen," a soup style modeled on Chiba/Uchibo's regional "Takeoka-style" ramen, and Jiro-inspired thick noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Stamina Ramen (garlic, pork backfat, and ginger) — also served as spicy stamina ramen, stamina abura-soba (oil noodles), and stamina tsukemen (dipping noodles), priced roughly ¥950-¥1,000.

anecdotes.text: The recipe combines three distinct regional Chiba/Tokyo ramen traditions into one bowl: Arilan Ramen's toppings, Takeoka-style soup, and Jiro-style noodles — a tribute to Suzuki-Oni in Mitaka.
INGS describes the shop as a departure from its usual light-broth ramen lineup, built specifically around a heavier garlic/backfat/ginger "stamina" style.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-only counter ramen shop with no indication of English menus or signage; ordering assumes basic Japanese or menu-photo pointing.

places.name: 新潟カツ丼タレカツ 神保町本店 (Niigata Katsudon Tarekatsu Jimbocho Honten ()
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is a pork cutlet coated in wheat-flour panko breading and deep-fried, then dipped in a soy-sauce-based tare sauce — both the breading and the tare introduce wheat/gluten. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated fryer was found in any source; this is not a suitable choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a pork-cutlet specialty shop; even the "vegetable" fillet-katsu bowl (yasai hire-katsudon) is served together with fried pork cutlet rather than as a standalone vegan dish, and side dishes such as miso soup likely use bonito-based dashi. No vegan-adapted dish or vegan accommodation was found in any source.
places.neighborhood: 神保町 (Suidobashi/Jimbocho border, Chiyoda-ku)
places.notes: Tarekatsu's cooking style traces back to Niigata City, where in the early Showa era Michitaro Komatsu, founder of the original Niigata shop Tonkatsu Taro, began dipping thin, freshly-fried pork cutlets in a sweet-savory soy sauce tare instead of folding them into egg as in Tokyo- · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Original flagship shop of the Tarekatsu chain (opened June 2007)
chef_bio.origin: Niigata City, Japan — home of the tare-katsudon style
chef_bio.background: Tarekatsu's cooking style traces back to Niigata City, where in the early Showa era Michitaro Komatsu, founder of the original Niigata shop Tonkatsu Taro, began dipping thin, freshly-fried pork cutlets in a sweet-savory soy sauce tare instead of folding them into egg as in Tokyo-style katsudon. That regional dish became a Niigata soul food, and this Jimbocho shop — opened in June 2007 as the very first Tarekatsu location — brought it to Tokyo, a few minutes' walk from Suidobashi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pork cutlets are pounded thin before frying so they stay light and easy to bite through, then dipped whole in a sweet-soy tare rather than being simmered in egg — the defining trait of Niigata-style katsudon as opposed to the Tokyo style.
chef_bio.specialty: Yasai hire-katsudon (vegetable and fillet-cutlet rice bowl), the shop's most popular dish, alongside the classic tare-katsudon and the double-tier nidan-mori katsudon

anecdotes.text: Reviewers originally from Niigata comment that the tare seasoning and frying at this Tokyo shop live up to the hometown version ("新潟人も認める美味さ").
The vegetable fillet-katsu bowl (yasai hire-katsudon) is noted as the shop's most popular menu item.

cultural_comfort.note: A small 12-seat counter-only local katsudon shop with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff — comfortable for diners who can manage basic Japanese ordering.

places.name: イル ラート (Il Lato)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented in any source found. The restaurant's signature offerings include handmade wheat pasta, and seafood dishes are likely to use soy sauce (shoyu, typically wheat-containing) and dashi-based sauces. Because this is a small open-kitchen omakase restaurant, cross-contamination risk for a shared kitchen is likely but unconfirmed. Celiac diners should call ahead in Japanese to discuss specific needs before booking; do not assume any dish is safe without direct confirmation from the chef.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation is documented. The restaurant is built around an omakase-style course centered on seafood, meat, handmade pasta, and dashi/bonito-based sauces, which is structurally difficult to adapt to a fully plant-based meal. Vegan diners should not expect standard accommodation and would need to arrange a heavily customized meal in advance by direct, specific request — success is not guaranteed.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.notes: Chef Furui trained in Italy's Piedmont region, then spent nine years as head chef of Osteria Oriera in Shinjuku Sanchome, where the restaurant earned a Tabelog Award eight years running. He went on to lead Ristorante Pegaso in Minami-Aoyama for two years before opening Il Lato as · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 古井繁規 (romanization varies across sources: "Shigeki Furui" appears most often, also seen as "Shigehiro Furui")
chef_bio.origin: Piedmont, Italy (culinary training)
chef_bio.background: He went on to lead Ristorante Pegaso in Minami-Aoyama for two years before opening Il Lato as his own restaurant in February 2021.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "Il Lato" means "side" or "facet" in Italian, reflecting the chef's approach of drawing out the many different sides and expressions of each seasonal ingredient.
chef_bio.specialty: Seafood-driven Italian courses built on ingredients from Toyosu Market — handmade pasta alongside precision-grilled fish and meat.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, meaning "side" in Italian, is meant to capture the chef's philosophy of revealing the many facets of seasonal ingredients through his cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations run through Japanese-language platforms (Ikyu, Gurunavi, Hitosara, TableCheck) and a Japanese phone line, with no evidence of an English menu, so diners without Japanese may want a hotel concierge or reservation site to book ahead.

places.name: グリル グランド (Grill Grand)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodation could be found anywhere. The signature dishes are built on wheat: the house demi-glace sauce is a classic French-style roux-based sauce simmered about two weeks, the crab cream croquette is breaded and deep-fried (shared fryer, no indication of a dedicated fryer), and omurice/beef stew are served with this same wheat-thickened sauce. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, labeling, or accommodation found. The restaurant's identity is built around meat- and dairy-heavy French-yōshoku technique: the demi-glace sauce base uses beef tendon, beef bones and chicken bones; the beef stew is slow-braised beef; omurice contains egg and butter; the crab cream croquette contains dairy cream, crab, and egg-based breading. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 火〜土 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00)。日・月定休。※一部の古い情報源では平日夜21:30までとの記載もあり、来店前の電話確認を推奨。
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Grill Grand opened in 1941, when Asakusa's Kannonura backstreet was a lively hanamachi (geisha district); the restaurant became a favorite of geisha and often catered Western dishes for the neighborhood's ryōtei (traditional restaurants). It has stayed in the same family for thre · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 坂本良太郎 (Sakamoto Ryōtarō) — 3rd generation chef
roles: Front of house/management: Sakamoto Shōichi and 2nd-generation parents
chef_bio.origin: Founded 1941 by 坂本友治郎 (Sakamoto Yūjirō), who trained in French cuisine at the Sannō Hotel before opening this Western-style (yōshoku) restaurant in Asakusa.
chef_bio.background: Grill Grand opened in 1941, when Asakusa's Kannonura backstreet was a lively hanamachi (geisha district); the restaurant became a favorite of geisha and often catered Western dishes for the neighborhood's ryōtei (traditional restaurants). It has stayed in the same family for three generations: the founder's grandson, Ryōtarō, now cooks while his older brother, Shōichi, and their parents (2nd generation) run the front of house.
chef_bio.philosophy: The demi-glace sauce is treated as the soul of the kitchen — simmered for about two weeks from beef tendon, beef and chicken bones, aromatic vegetables, tomato and red wine to build layers of umami; the beef for the stew is braised roughly seven hours until it falls apart.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef stew simmered for hours until fork-tender, and the signature 特製オムライス (tokusei omuraisu) — omurice finished with a choice of the house demi-glace or a ketchup sauce. Their crab cream croquette has won gold seven years running in the Croquette Grand Prix's cream category.

anecdotes.text: As teenagers, the two brothers who now run the restaurant had a good-natured cabbage-shredding contest in the kitchen; their father (the 2nd generation owner) judged that the elder brother, Shōichi, lacked a cook's touch, which is when Shōichi decided he'd focus on managing the restaurant instead, leaving the cooking to his brother Ryōtarō.
The restaurant is a well-known local landmark 'just behind Sensō-ji' and has been recommended in the Michelin Guide Tokyo Bib Gourmand selection.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or fryer is indicated anywhere; the crab cream croquette (a breaded, deep-fried item) is one of the restaurant's signature dishes, implying a shared fryer used for wheat-breaded foods.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local yōshoku restaurant with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — order in Japanese or with a translation app, though its Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition means occasional non-Japanese visitors are not unusual.

places.name: 黄金の塩らぁ麺 ドゥエ イタリアン 東急プラザ渋谷店 (Due Italian – Tokyu Plaza Shibuya (Ougon no )
places.gf_detail: Do not rely on this shop for a celiac-safe meal. It offers a 'healthy noodle' swap advertised with gluten-free language, but per menu sourcing it is actually a half wheat-flour / half konjac-noodle blend — it still contains wheat gluten. The soy-sauce ramen (醤油らぁ麺) uses conventional wheat-based shoyu, and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, separate fryer, or allergen protocol. Someone with celiac disease or a severe wheat allergy should treat this as an unsafe option.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house broth is chicken-bone based with kelp/scallop dashi, and the signature dishes are built around dairy (cream cheese, Gorgonzola) and cured pork (prosciutto). No plant-based broth or protein alternative is documented.
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (inside Tokyu Plaza Shibuya, 6F, at Shibu
places.notes: Chef Kazuo Ishizuka ran six Italian restaurants over roughly 25 years before meeting legendary ramen craftsman Minoru Sano (佐野実), which led him to cross over into ramen and fuse his Italian training with the craft. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Main branch (honten) is in Ichigaya, Chiyoda-ku; this is the Tokyu Plaza Shibuya branch of a small Tokyo ramen chain
chef_bio.background: Chef Kazuo Ishizuka ran six Italian restaurants over roughly 25 years before meeting legendary ramen craftsman Minoru Sano (佐野実), which led him to cross over into ramen and fuse his Italian training with the craft.
chef_bio.philosophy: A self-described 'Cheese × Ramen' fusion concept — treating each bowl like a compressed Italian-inflected full course, only possible from a chef who has worked both sides of Italian cooking and ramen craft.
chef_bio.specialty: Ramen Fromage (らぁ麺フロマージュ) — noodles finished with a rich, Gorgonzola-style cream cheese, plus the signature golden salt ramen (黄金の塩らぁ麺) built on a chicken-bone broth enriched with kelp and scallop dashi. Menus also mix in Italian touches like prosciutto (生ハムフロマージュ).

anecdotes.text: Ishizuka previously operated six Italian restaurants before meeting ramen master Minoru Sano and pivoting to create his own ramen brand blending Italian and ramen technique.
The Due Italian brand's Ichigaya honten was selected as a Michelin Guide Tokyo Bib Gourmand pick and has appeared in the Michelin Guide Tokyo for three consecutive years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's 'healthy noodle' is marketed with gluten-free language but is actually a half wheat-flour / half konjac-noodle blend, meaning it still contains gluten and is unsafe for celiac diners despite the framing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 醤油らぁ麺 (soy-sauce ramen) is made with conventional shoyu, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature golden broth is built from chicken bones (鶏ガラ) simmered with kelp and scallop, and dishes are finished with cream cheese, Gorgonzola, and prosciutto — there is no vegan or vegetarian broth option.

positives.text: A noodle-swap option (part-konjac) is available for those wanting a lower-carbohydrate bowl, though it is not a genuine gluten-free option.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside the international Tokyu Plaza Shibuya mall right at Shibuya Scramble, with photo-driven ramen menus that are easy to point-and-order from, but no confirmed English-speaking staff or English-language menu.

places.name: うどん 丸香 (Udon Maruka)
places.gf_detail: This is a specialist wheat-udon shop — the noodles themselves are made from wheat flour with no gluten-free alternative offered. Popular toppings (chicken tempura, chikuwa fish-cake tempura) are deep-fried, almost certainly in a shared fryer with wheat-battered items, and the broth is a soy-sauce-based dashi (Japanese soy sauce is typically wheat-containing). No gluten-free accommodation was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is an iriko (dried sardine)/mixed dried-fish dashi with kombu, so the base soup is not vegan. Popular toppings such as chicken tempura and chikuwa (fish cake) tempura are also animal-derived. No plain-dashi or vegetable-broth alternative was found in any source, so this shop cannot be recommended for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00-16:00, 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:30); Sat 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 14:30); closed Sun, national holidays, and New Year's; may close earlier on any day if the noodles sell out
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Ogawamachi, Jimbocho, Chiyoda City
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Maruka was opened in 2003 by a Kagawa-prefecture (Takamatsu) native who left a career in advertising/sales-promotion planning to become an udon craftsman. Before opening his own shop he trained at Yamakoshi, a Kagawa institution often credited as one of the birthplaces of kamaage · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kagawa Prefecture (Takamatsu), Japan
chef_bio.background: Maruka was opened in 2003 by a Kagawa-prefecture (Takamatsu) native who left a career in advertising/sales-promotion planning to become an udon craftsman. Before opening his own shop he trained at Yamakoshi, a Kagawa institution often credited as one of the birthplaces of kamaage-style udon.
chef_bio.philosophy: To bring Kagawa's everyday soul food — Sanuki udon as it is actually eaten at home, not a showcase dish — to Tokyo, with every bowl timed and boiled so it reaches the customer at its freshly-made peak.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made Sanuki udon, especially kamaage-style noodles served straight from the cooking water, alongside classics like kake and niku (meat) udon and hand-fried toppings such as chikuwa and chicken tempura.

anecdotes.text: The shop is often mistaken for a decades-old institution, but it was actually founded about 20 years ago (2003) by a former ad-agency employee who quit his corporate job to pursue udon full time.

The owner calculates the timing of every order — how much noodle dough to boil and for how long — so that each bowl is served at its ideal just-cooked moment, treating this attentive service as part of recreating Kagawa's 'everyday udon' experience.
Before opening Maruka, the Takamatsu-born owner trained at Yamakoshi, a Kagawa shop regarded as one of the originators of kamaage-style udon.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, cash-only neighborhood udon shop with no English menu, no online reservations, and frequent lines out the door — come ready to queue and point at the menu.

places.name: ラーメンまこと屋 渋谷センター街本店 (Ramen Makotoya Shibuya Center-gai Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option or dedicated wheat-free menu was found. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the signature beef-bone broth and shoyu-based seasonings are prepared in a shared kitchen alongside wheat products. The only reduced-carb option found ("糖質オフ麺") is a low-sugar noodle made by combining starch with regular wheat flour — it is not gluten-free. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature and default broths are all beef-bone (gyukotsu) based, and other soup variants use seafood/beef combinations — no plant-based or vegetable broth option was identified. Shoyu-based seasonings likely include bonito/fish-derived dashi components typical of Japanese ramen. Not suitable for vegans; no vegetarian broth alternative was found in available sources.
places.hours_raw: 24時間営業・年中無休 (24-hour operation, open every day of the year)
places.neighborhood: Udagawa-cho, Shibuya (Shibuya Center-gai, ~3-5 min walk from
places.notes: Ramen Makotoya is a beef-bone (gyukotsu) ramen chain that grew out of a single shop opened in Fukushima-ku, Osaka in 1999 by Makoto Food Service Co. The company has since expanded to roughly 100 locations in Japan and abroad. The Shibuya Center-gai shop opened in February 2024 as · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 24-hour beef-bone ramen chain shop (Kanto flagship branch)
chef_bio.origin: Osaka (founded in Fukushima-ku, Osaka, October 11, 1999)
chef_bio.background: Ramen Makotoya is a beef-bone (gyukotsu) ramen chain that grew out of a single shop opened in Fukushima-ku, Osaka in 1999 by Makoto Food Service Co. The company has since expanded to roughly 100 locations in Japan and abroad. The Shibuya Center-gai shop opened in February 2024 as the chain's push into the Kanto region, reportedly around its 81st store, with a stated goal of 200 locations by 2030.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's catchphrases are "a ramen shop that makes families happy" and "you can see smiles through the steam" — the company emphasizes handmade preparation and mostly domestic (Japanese-sourced) ingredients rather than a dietary-focused approach.
chef_bio.specialty: 牛骨白湯ラーメン (creamy beef-bone tonkotsu-style ramen) — each store simmers roughly 30kg of beef bones (about six 5kg bones) per batch in large pressure pots to make the signature milky broth. The Shibuya branch also has a store-limited Taiwan-style abura soba (mazesoba).

anecdotes.text: The shop's spicy miso condiment is a proprietary blend of several spices and fruit juices aged into house miso, and its garlic oil is fried in three separate stages at different times and temperatures before being blended together.

The Shibuya Center-gai location opened February 23, 2024, becoming one of the chain's Kanto-region outposts as the Osaka-born chain expanded eastward, and offers a store-exclusive Taiwan-style mazesoba not found at other branches.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu displays a QR code linking to a full allergen chart covering the eight major mandatory allergens (egg, dairy, wheat, buckwheat, peanut, shrimp, crab, walnut) plus soy, beef, chicken and pork, though a blogger who visited with an egg/dairy-allergic child still recommended confirming specifics with staff in person before ordering.

positives.text: Provides an in-store QR-code allergen chart at the table/register covering major allergens rather than leaving customers to guess ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via an outdoor touch-panel ticket machine that supports English and Chinese, and the shop accepts credit cards and QR-code payments, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.

places.name: 冨多葉 (Futaba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodations are mentioned in any source. As a traditional soba-ya, the menu is built around soba/udon noodles, tempura, and katsudon (breaded, fried pork cutlet) — dishes that in standard Japanese preparation typically involve wheat (in noodles, tempura batter, and soy-sauce-based tsuyu) and are not celiac-safe without direct confirmation from the shop.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian dishes are identified in any source. The menu centers on pork katsudon and soba/udon served in what is typically a bonito (katsuobushi)-based dashi at traditional soba shops of this style — no plant-based options or dashi substitutions are mentioned anywhere found.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00〜17:00、土日祝定休 (Mon-Fri 11:00-17:00; closed Sat, Sun, and public holidays)
places.neighborhood: 神田猿楽町（神保町駅・水道橋駅間）、千代田区
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop stands on a spot that had housed a soba restaurant called Kagaya from the late Meiji into early Taisho era. Futaba's predecessor owner trained at Kokura-an, a soba house in Nihonbashi Hon-cho, then bought out Kagaya in Taisho 5 (1916) and reopened it under the new name F · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Kazuo Tomiyama (owner), with son Toshiyuki also active in the shop
chef_bio.origin: Kanda-Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The shop stands on a spot that had housed a soba restaurant called Kagaya from the late Meiji into early Taisho era. Futaba's predecessor owner trained at Kokura-an, a soba house in Nihonbashi Hon-cho, then bought out Kagaya in Taisho 5 (1916) and reopened it under the new name Futaba (冨多葉) in May of Taisho 6 (1917). The restaurant has continued on the same spot for more than a century, including rebuilding after the shop was lost in the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and again in air raids during World War II.
chef_bio.specialty: Katsudon (pork cutlet rice bowl) alongside classic soba and udon; close to half of today's customers order the ¥980 katsudon rather than soba.

anecdotes.text: The shop traces back to a soba restaurant called Kagaya on this spot from the late Meiji into early Taisho era; Futaba's predecessor owner trained at Kokura-an in Nihonbashi Honcho before buying Kagaya in Taisho 5 (1916) and reopening it as Futaba in May of Taisho 6 (1917).
The restaurant has kept operating at the same location through more than a century, including rebuilding after the shop was destroyed in the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and in WWII air raids.

Current owner Kazuo Tomiyama served for many years in the local Kanda-Sarugakucho neighborhood association (youth section, crime-prevention section, vice-chair), and his son Toshiyuki has taken on a vice-chair role in the association's youth section as well, keeping the shop closely tied to the surrounding community.

Although it is a century-old soba shop, close to half of today's customers order the katsudon rather than soba, and the ¥980 katsudon is often singled out for its careful cooking and old-fashioned, retro flavor.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a very traditional, local soba-ya with no English menu or signage indicated in any source found — expect an all-Japanese ordering experience typical of an old neighborhood shop.

places.name: オタコ（シフォンケーキの店 otaco） (otaco (Otako) — Chiffon Cake Shop otaco)
places.gf_detail: No wheat flour is kept anywhere in the shop, and no baking powder is used — every chiffon cake is made from a 100% domestic rice-flour batter that is leavened solely by whipped egg foam, so there is no shared-kitchen wheat cross-contact for the cakes themselves. This is a single-product cake specialist, not a full-meal venue, so there is nothing else on offer to worry about (no soy sauce, dashi, or fried items to check).
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The standard and plain chiffon cakes contain fresh eggs (Okukuji brand), milk, and condensed milk as core ingredients — eggs are essential to the recipe since whipped egg foam is what makes the cake rise (no baking powder is used). No egg-free or dairy-free version was found advertised.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-18:00, closed Sunday and Monday; also closes early/for the day when sold out and posts occasional irregular closures on its shop calendar
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, Kannon-ura (behind Sensoji, on Kototoi-dori)
places.notes: otaco opened in September 2010 in Asakusa's Kannon-ura backstreets, along Kototoi-dori just north of Sensoji Temple. The shop's name comes from the nickname of the owner's young son. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (opened September 2010)
chef_bio.background: otaco opened in September 2010 in Asakusa's Kannon-ura backstreets, along Kototoi-dori just north of Sensoji Temple. The shop's name comes from the nickname of the owner's young son.
chef_bio.philosophy: Marketed as "anshin sweets" (reassuring, safe sweets) meant to be enjoyed every day without worry — made with body-friendly ingredients (unrefined kibi sugar, domestic rapeseed oil, fresh Okukuji eggs) and no wheat flour or baking powder anywhere in the kitchen, letting the rice flour's naturally moist, mochi-like texture and gentle sweetness stand on its own.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour chiffon cake — a plain version plus cocoa and rotating seasonal flavors, sold as a whole cake or by the single slice for eating on the go

anecdotes.text: Every cake is handmade one at a time in the shop's kitchen by the owner, known to regulars as "otaco mama."
The shop's name otaco comes from the pet nickname of the owner's son.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No wheat flour is kept anywhere on the premises, and no baking powder is used in the chiffon cakes — the batter is a 100% rice-flour base leavened only by whipped egg foam.

positives.text: Highlighted by gluten-free-focused bloggers as a reassuring option for people with wheat allergies because the entire kitchen is flour-free rather than just offering a token gluten-free item.

cultural_comfort.note: It's a simple point-and-pay takeout counter selling one type of product, so basic greetings and numbers are enough to order even without shared language.

places.name: 元祖 うな鐡 (Ganso Unatetsu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free tare, or dedicated-fryer information was found for this restaurant. The kitchen is a traditional eel-skewer grill counter with a soy-based dipping tare typical of kabayaki-style unagi, and no source mentions any gluten-free accommodation. Celiac or wheat-allergic diners should assume the tare and any accompanying items contain wheat and should not rely on this restaurant without asking staff directly.

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1957 in Shibuya's Dogen-zaka district, the shop was built around a simple idea: eel (unagi), long considered a luxury food in Japan, should be something ordinary people could enjoy casually and affordably. Instead of the formal, pricier kabayaki-box style served at many unagi restaurants, the founders built the menu around charcoal-grilled eel skewers — served almost like yakitori — so diners could order a few at a time.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep unagi cuisine unpretentious and accessible — grill it simply over binchotan charcoal, sell it by the skewer rather than only as a formal set meal, and let regulars drop in without a reservation the way they would at a neighborhood yakitori counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Kushiyaki eel skewers grilling nearly every part of the fish over Kishu binchotan charcoal (roughly 400°C) — including kurikara (a coiled belly-meat skewer), hire (fin), tsumaki, tanshaku, kimo (liver), kabuto (head), baramimi, and rebaa. The kurikara skewer is said to have originated at this shop.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant serves eight distinct cuts of eel skewer — kurikara, hire, tsumaki, tanshaku, kimo, kabuto, baramimi, and rebaa — grilled over Kishu binchotan charcoal, using nearly the whole fish rather than only the fillet.
Kurikara, a skewer made by coiling belly meat, is said to have originated at this shop and remains one of its signature orders.
The shop opened in 1957 with the concept of making eel, then seen as a luxury item, accessible to everyone through skewer-style service.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, counter-style local eel-skewer shop with a Japanese-language menu and no reservations, run first-come-first-served — approachable but easiest for diners with some comfort ordering in Japanese or pointing at a picture menu.

chef_bio.background: A direct-from-producer seafood izakaya (産直市場) near Suidōbashi Station specializing in oysters sourced straight from growers in Miyagi, with additional fresh catches from Hokkaido and Iwate.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sources oysters directly from Miyagi producers and only serves oysters that meet the prefecture's strict raw-consumption ('生食用') quality standards, prized for their rich flavor and large meat.
chef_bio.specialty: 殻付き牡蠣 (shell-on grilled oysters) from Miyagi, served alongside fresh sashimi platters (tuna, toro-salmon, scallop) and a lunchtime 牡蠣三昧定食 (oyster feast set meal).

anecdotes.text: 名物の殻付かきは、潮流と森の恵みが育む宮城の豊かな海で育った牡蠣は、味が濃厚で身も大きいのが特徴。宮城県は牡蠣の名産地のため厳しい規格基準が設けられており、その基準をクリアした質のよい『生食用』の牡蠣だけを提供しています。

roles: Water-dumpling specialty restaurant, Shinjuku East Exit branch of the Gyopao (餃包) chain
chef_bio.origin: Roppongi, Tokyo (original Gyopao location); this is the Shinjuku East Exit branch
chef_bio.background: Gyopao's water-dumpling recipe traces back to the founder's Taiwanese father and Kumamoto-born mother — the dumplings blend the flavor of his father's home-town xiaolongbao with his mother's Kumamoto dago-jiru broth. The brand's direct roots go back to a dumpling restaurant the father opened in 2004. After the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake pushed the company to the brink of closing almost all of its shops, the family opened the original Gyopao in Roppongi as a last stand, betting everything on water dumplings alone. This Shinjuku East Exit branch opened in July 2024 as a station-front sister location, directly connected to the Shinjuku Subnade underground arcade, about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's East Exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company deliberately narrowed its menu to water dumplings only, dropping the pan-fried gyoza that once made up 80% of sales in order to become a true specialist. The menu is kept largely unchanged over time, with an emphasis on in-restaurant hospitality and service that customers can't get from delivery.
chef_bio.specialty: 肉汁水餃子 (juicy meat-filled water dumplings) — thin-skinned boiled dumplings that burst with broth similar to xiaolongbao, served with broths such as chicken paitan or hot-pot style preparations

anecdotes.text: After the 2011 earthquake put the company on the verge of bankruptcy, closing nearly every other shop, the founder staked everything on opening the original water-dumpling specialty shop in Roppongi.
The company chose to stop selling pan-fried gyoza — which made up 80% of sales — in order to commit fully to water dumplings as a specialist shop.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains a dedicated English-language website and sits directly connected to the Shinjuku Subnade underground arcade steps from Shinjuku Station's East Exit, making it easy to find for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.

roles: Chain tsukemen (dipping noodle) specialty restaurant, one of ~40+ nationwide branches
chef_bio.origin: Mita, Minato-ku, Tokyo (original 2008 honten)
chef_bio.background: Mita Seimenjo was founded in 2008 at its original honten in Mita, Minato-ku, Tokyo, as a dedicated tsukemen (dipping noodle) shop. It grew into one of the drivers of Tokyo's 'rich pork-bone and seafood' tsukemen boom, expanding to roughly 40+ locations nationwide plus delivery service across many prefectures by the 2020s. This Suidobashi/Tokyo Dome-mae location is one of those branch shops, a short walk from Tokyo Dome and Suidobashi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's stated goal is 'to make tsukemen an everyday, familiar food' (つけ麺を身近な日常食にする), pursued through a three-part broth built from animal stock (pork bone and chicken), vegetable stock, and a blended fish dashi (katsuobushi, mackerel, and flying-fish stock).
chef_bio.specialty: Noko Tonkotsu Gyokai Tsukemen (濃厚豚骨魚介つけ麺) — a rich pork-bone-and-seafood dipping noodle bowl served with the chain's proprietary thick square-cut wheat noodle

anecdotes.text: At the original Mita honten, a single small noodle-making machine kept on the third floor — the source of the shop's name ('seimen' = noodle-making) — was used for the trial-and-error process that produced the chain's signature rich pork-bone-and-seafood tsukemen recipe.

The chain blends animal broth (pork bone/chicken), plant-based vegetable stock, and a three-way fish dashi (katsuobushi, sababushi, agobushi) into its signature tsukemen soup, paired with an original thick 'kaku straight No. 12' noodle.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, fast-paced neighborhood tsukemen chain shop near Tokyo Dome with no English menu or dietary-accommodation signage found — comfortable for diners who can navigate ordering in Japanese.

Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten sensitivity should not assume safety here without direct, in-person confirmation, and even then caution is warranted given the shared kitchen and standard seasonings.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken (charcoal-grilled yakitori) and the chicken-and-egg oyakodon, with dashi appearing to be bonito (katsuo)-based. chef_bio.background: Established as a specialty chicken restaurant on Meiji-dori near Namiki Bridge, a short walk from Shibuya Station's east/new south exit. The shop occupies a two-story standalone building and has built its reputation in Shibuya as a go-to spot for oyakodon lovers, drawing lines at lunch and being featured on television for its egg dish.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to sourcing specific, traceable ingredients (named chicken breed and named egg producer) rather than generic supermarket chicken and eggs, applied consistently across both the lunchtime oyakodon and the evening charcoal-grilled yakitori menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) made with Chiba-raised free-range 'Akadori' chicken and eggs from Iwai, Ibaraki. The rice bowl is built in two stages — a fluffy egg layer topped with a second, deep-orange, extra-rich yolk — giving the dish its signature glossy, creamy texture.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's rich, creamy egg oyakodon has been featured on television programs and is repeatedly named among Shibuya's top oyakodon spots by local food bloggers and 'best of' round-ups.

Diners describe the oyakodon as deceptively simple-looking but built around a deliberate two-step egg technique (fluffy whites first, rich yolk poured on top) that produces its trademark glossy, golden appearance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The oyakodon sauce and yakitori tare are traditional soy-sauce (shoyu) based preparations; no wheat-free or gluten-free soy sauce alternative was found advertised.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local yakitori-and-oyakodon shop with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app or pointing at photos will help when ordering.

Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

chef_bio.origin: Tainan, Taiwan (dish origin); restaurant founded in Tokyo in 1986
chef_bio.background: Opened in 1986 near Suidobashi Station, this restaurant was one of the first in Tokyo to specialize in Tainan-style dan zai noodles at a time when authentic regional Taiwanese cooking was still rare in Japan. Four decades later it remains one of the longest-running Taiwanese specialty restaurants in the Suidobashi/Jimbocho area.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant recreates the feel of a Tainan night market, decorating the dining room with lanterns and serving dishes in small, shareable portions in the way dan zai noodles are traditionally eaten alongside several other small plates in Tainan.
chef_bio.specialty: 担仔麺 (dan zai noodles) — a small bowl of clear pork broth topped with slow-simmered minced pork and cilantro, traditionally paired with 魯肉飯 (braised pork rice) and other Tainan-style small plates.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as offering a night-market atmosphere with lanterns, serving small-plate Tainan cuisine meant to be shared the way locals in Tainan eat dan zai noodles alongside several other dishes.

Now in its fifth decade of business, the Suidobashi location is noted as one of Tokyo's original Taiwanese restaurants, credited with introducing authentic Tainan flavors to Japan when such regional Taiwanese cooking was still uncommon.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-established local Taiwanese noodle restaurant near Suidobashi/Jimbocho with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service; basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help when ordering or asking about ingredients.

The ramen noodles are standard wheat noodles, and no source states the shoyu/soup bases are wheat-free. This is a vegan-focused shop, not a gluten-free one — not confirmed safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The shop is fully plant-based: soup bases (shoyu, shio, miso, white "vege shiro" broth), soy-meat chashu, burdock-root chips, gyoza, and rice-bowl toppings all avoid meat, fish, eggs, and dairy as the shop's core concept — this is a dedicated vegan ramen kitchen, not a vegan-option add-on. No mention of bonito/katsuo dashi anywhere in sourced material.

places.notes: veJin was developed in partnership with Fuji Oil Co., using Fuji Oil's soy-processing technology to create "Prime Soy Meat" for the chashu topping and a plant-based dashi built on Fuji Oil's proprietary MIRACORE ingredient platform, so the shop could offer a rich, tonkotsu-style  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: operating company: D.K International Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: D.K International Co., Ltd. (headquartered in Osaka), represented by Masahiro Iwatani, opened veJin as a dedicated 100%-plant-based ramen concept inside FOOD HALL BLAST! TOKYO on November 22, 2023.
chef_bio.background: veJin was developed in partnership with Fuji Oil Co. , using Fuji Oil's soy-processing technology to create "Prime Soy Meat" for the chashu topping and a plant-based dashi built on Fuji Oil's proprietary MIRACORE ingredient platform, so the shop could offer a rich, tonkotsu-style white broth without any animal products.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated concept is "kind to the earth and body" (地球とカラダにやさしい) — built to widen ramen options for people who avoid meat, fish, eggs, or dairy, and to introduce Japan's ramen culture to a global, plant-based-curious audience.
chef_bio.specialty: Vege Shiro (white broth) ramen in shoyu, shio, and miso styles, topped with soy-meat chashu and burdock-root chips, plus vegan gyoza and a chashu rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: veJin was founded with the aim to "expand food choices for people who cannot eat animal-derived dishes for various reasons, let them enjoy ramen, a Japanese food culture, and share it with the world."
The shop uses Fuji Oil's newly developed "Prime Soy Meat," engineered to combine meat-like fiber and chew with a smooth mouthfeel that was previously hard to achieve in plant-based chashu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop's entire menu is 100%-plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy are used in any dish or broth on site, which removes animal-product cross-contact for vegan diners (though wheat allergens are not addressed).

positives.text: Broths, chashu (soy meat), and toppings are all formulated to be free of meat, fish, egg, and dairy as the shop's core concept, rather than as a special off-menu accommodation.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern food-hall ramen counter aimed partly at an international, plant-based-curious crowd, with an English Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff.

chef_bio.background: Bistro Kojiya is a small, casual French bistro tucked in a quiet alley off the main Shinjuku streets, close to Shinjuku-Sanchome Station. According to its booking-site listings, the chef trained at well-known restaurants, including establishments overseas that hold Michelin stars, before opening this intimate 16-seat spot.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its menu around seasonal ingredients, refreshing the full course lineup roughly once a month rather than running a static menu.
chef_bio.specialty: A monthly-changing, seasonal chef's-choice (omakase) French course, often featuring a double-main format with both a meat and a fish course; the April-May 2026 menu, for example, ran caprese, turnip velouté, salmon, duck breast, and cherry pie for dessert.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as an upscale-casual bistro in a quiet Shinjuku alleyway, popular for occasions like women's lunch parties, pairing its course menu with an all-you-can-drink selection of around 10-15 wines chosen by the chef.
A visitor blog post recounts a women's-group lunch course with a free-flow wine option at Bistro Kojiya in Shinjuku-Sanchome.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, chef-driven French bistro geared toward Japanese diners (course-only menu, reservation booking sites in Japanese); no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff.

places.name: らーめん はやし (Hayashi)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-flour ramen noodles with no gluten-free substitute offered. The shoyu-based broth almost certainly uses conventional wheat-containing soy sauce, and no gluten-free or allergy accommodations are mentioned in any source. Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is made from pork bone and fish (double soup), and there are no vegetarian or vegan menu options. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:30〜15:30（スープが終わり次第終了）日・祝定休、夏季休業あり
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂（渋谷マークシティ近辺）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] According to blog accounts, the owner once served as an assistant priest at his family's temple in Hyogo before spending about a decade working the kitchen of a dining bar in Takadanobaba, Tokyo. He then taught himself how to build ramen soup from scratch before opening this shop · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Hyogo Prefecture
chef_bio.background: According to blog accounts, the owner once served as an assistant priest at his family's temple in Hyogo before spending about a decade working the kitchen of a dining bar in Takadanobaba, Tokyo. He then taught himself how to build ramen soup from scratch before opening this shop with his wife in November 2003.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop keeps things deliberately small and simple: a three-item menu, lunch-only hours, and closing the moment the soup runs out rather than stretching it to serve more bowls.
chef_bio.specialty: Additive-free (無化調) shoyu ramen built on a double soup of pork bone and fish, with a lesser-known off-menu shio (salt) version also available.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run as a husband-and-wife team from a 10-seat counter, open only for a roughly four-hour lunch window and closing for about a month each summer because of the heat.

cultural_comfort.note: The menu is Japanese-only and limited to just three simple items (ramen, ramen with egg, ramen with roast pork), and it's cash-only counter seating with no reservations, but ordering is straightforward since choices are minimal.

places.name: 熊魚菴 たん熊北店 東京ドームホテル店 (Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise — Tokyo Dome Hotel)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu was found. The restaurant's own allergy notice (via its reservation platform) states that guests with food allergies, including wheat, must call the restaurant directly rather than note it in an online booking comment field; it explicitly warns that dishes are cooked in a shared kitchen using shared cooking and washing equipment, so trace cross-contamination is possible, that its dishes are not a complete allergy-accommodation menu, and that the restaurant may decline to serve guests with severe allergy symptoms for safety reasons. As a traditional Kyoto-kaiseki/tempura restaurant, tempura batter is standard wheat flour and soy sauce used in dashi and sauces is very likely wheat-based unless specifically substituted — celiac guests should call ahead and confirm case-by-case rather than assume any dish is safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or shōjin-style menu was found for this branch. As a formal Kyoto-kaiseki, tempura and teppanyaki restaurant, dashi (stock) is very likely made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and other fish/meat-based elements are standard across courses (teppanyaki is built around Kobe beef; a suppon/softshell-turtle course is a headline dish). Vegan diners should call ahead and discuss substitutions case-by-case; a fully plant-based meal cannot be assumed available.
places.neighborhood: 文京区後楽 (Suidōbashi / Tokyo Dome area)
places.notes: Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise traces its lineage to a small counter-style kappo restaurant called Tankuma, opened in 1928 on the banks of Kyoto's Takase River by Kumasaburo Kurisu. The restaurant's fuller name, Kumagyoan (熊魚菴), was bestowed later by Heiko Hirata, then head priest of K · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Kyoto-cuisine, tempura, teppanyaki and sushi restaurant inside Tokyo Dome Hotel, operated by Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: Kyoto
chef_bio.background: Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise traces its lineage to a small counter-style kappo restaurant called Tankuma, opened in 1928 on the banks of Kyoto's Takase River by Kumasaburo Kurisu. The restaurant's fuller name, Kumagyoan (熊魚菴), was bestowed later by Heiko Hirata, then head priest of Kyoto's Tenryu-ji temple, drawing on a classical Chinese phrase for rare delicacies. The present operating company, Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise Co. , Ltd. , was established in May 1993 and now runs a group of Kyoto-cuisine restaurants nationwide; the Tokyo Dome Hotel branch is one of its Tokyo locations, occupying a large fourth-floor dining floor with separate Kyoto-cuisine, tempura, teppanyaki and sushi kitchens, plus private rooms including a tea room, and views over the Tokyo Dome.
chef_bio.specialty: A5 Kobe beef teppanyaki courses, and a softshell-turtle (suppon) kaiseki course served as nabe, chawanmushi, tempura and rice porridge, offered in a strictly limited run of about 10 servings a day.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name Kumagyoan (熊魚菴) was given by Heiko Hirata, head priest of Kyoto's Tenryu-ji temple, based on a classical Chinese expression for the finest delicacies.
The Tokyo Dome Hotel branch offers a suppon (softshell turtle) kaiseki course — served as a hot pot, chawanmushi, tempura and rice porridge — limited to about 10 servings per day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dishes are prepared in a kitchen shared across the restaurant's full menu, using shared cooking and washing equipment, so trace amounts of allergens (including wheat) may be present even in dishes without wheat as a listed ingredient.
gf_cross_contamination.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with food allergies or disliked ingredients (seafood, meat, dairy, buckwheat, wheat, etc.) are asked to contact the restaurant directly by phone rather than note it in the online reservation comment field, so staff can discuss options before the visit.
staff_allergy_handling.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)
