staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant states its dishes are not a complete allergy-accommodation menu and that it may decline to serve guests with severe allergy symptoms, for the guest's own safety.
staff_allergy_handling.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)

positives.text: The restaurant proactively publishes an allergy-handling policy and invites direct phone consultation before the visit, rather than ignoring the topic.
positives.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)

cultural_comfort.note: As a large international hotel restaurant (Tokyo Dome Hotel) with English-language pages on the hotel's own site and English-language online reservation via TableCheck, it is set up to serve English-speaking guests, though allergy/dietary discussion is directed to a phone call rather than handled online.

places.name: 浅草 ひら山 (Asakusa Hirayama)
places.gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat flour binder, but the dipping sauce/tsuyu and most course dishes use wheat-containing soy sauce, and courses include tempura. The restaurant has stated it is unable to modify its set courses to accommodate gluten allergies or gluten-free requests; guests wanting to avoid wheat are advised to reserve à la carte and choose dishes themselves — there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation or verified allergy-safe process, so this is not suitable for guests requiring strict celiac-level safety without further direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant has explicitly stated it cannot accommodate vegetarian, vegan, or halal orders: the dashi/broth and most dishes contain animal-derived ingredients (bonito-based dashi, meat/seafood), and menu items cannot be modified to remove them.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 17:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Monday and Tuesday
places.neighborhood: Tawaramachi / Nishi-Asakusa (Kappabashi area), Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hirayama Shu trained at the celebrated Ryōgoku soba restaurant Hosokawa (ほそ川) before honing his skills further and opening his own shop in Nishi-Asakusa in November 2021. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Hirayama Shu trained at the celebrated Ryōgoku soba restaurant Hosokawa (ほそ川) before honing his skills further and opening his own shop in Nishi-Asakusa in November 2021.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to "freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled": whole buckwheat groats brought in from Fukui are hulled and stone-ground each morning, then immediately kneaded and hand-cut without any resting or aging period, to keep the buckwheat's aroma and flavor at their peak.
chef_bio.specialty: Juwari soba — handmade noodles made from 100% stone-ground buckwheat with no wheat flour binder, cut fresh daily.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in November 2021, founded by a soba craftsman who trained at the renowned Hosokawa in Ryōgoku before striking out on his own near Tawaramachi Station.
Buckwheat groats are peeled and ground on a stone mill fresh every morning, then kneaded and cut immediately — the dough is never left to age, in pursuit of the purest buckwheat taste and aroma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Course menus include tempura and dishes seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce; the restaurant is unable to modify set courses for gluten-free or gluten-allergy requests. Guests seeking to avoid wheat are told to book à la carte and pick dishes themselves.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's course menus use soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Due to the ingredients and broth used (including animal-derived dashi/soup), the restaurant is unable to accommodate vegetarian, vegan, or halal orders or modify dishes for these diets.

staff_allergy_handling.text: For gluten-free dining, the restaurant recommends reserving the à la carte menu so guests can select individual dishes themselves, rather than offering a modified or verified allergy-safe course.

positives.text: The signature soba noodles are handmade from 100% stone-ground buckwheat with no wheat flour binder (juwari soba), milled fresh each morning.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains an English-language site (asakusa-hirayama.com/en) and is bookable via English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck, byfood), but menus and in-person service are primarily Japanese-oriented.

places.name: 福田屋 (Fukudaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soba option was mentioned in any source. As a traditional soba shop, the noodles are very likely a wheat-buckwheat blend (not 100% juwari buckwheat), and the dipping sauce/broth (tsuyu) is a dashi-and-shoyu (soy sauce, wheat-containing) base per menu descriptions. Tempura is fried in shared oil alongside other items. Celiac diners should not consider this a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff, which could not be verified here.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu or accommodations were found. The standard tsuyu/broth used for hot and cold soba is dashi-based (typically bonito/katsuo), which is not vegan. Vegetable tempura, cold eggplant, and natto dishes appear on the menu and could suit vegetarians, but none were confirmed as prepared with a vegan (non-bonito) broth or separate fryer oil.
places.hours_raw: Most sources report lunch service 11:00-14:30 (or in some listings 11:00-15:30), closed Sundays and public holidays. One synthesis of listings suggests evening service (previously roughly 17:00-20:30/22:00 on weekdays per Hot Pepper/Jalan) was discontinued around April 2024, leaving lunch-only hours; this could not be independently confirmed and sources disagree.
places.neighborhood: Dogen-zaka, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run soba noodle shop founded in 1952 on a narrow backstreet off Dogen-zaka, just a few minutes from Shibuya Station. Over more than 70 years it has watched the neighborhood transform around it, staying a steady lunchtime fixture for office workers, longtime regulars, and · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: A family-run soba noodle shop founded in 1952 on a narrow backstreet off Dogen-zaka, just a few minutes from Shibuya Station. Over more than 70 years it has watched the neighborhood transform around it, staying a steady lunchtime fixture for office workers, longtime regulars, and soba enthusiasts. Guests climb a staircase to reach a bright, unexpectedly spacious dining room with a small garden (tsuboniwa) visible through the windows — often described as a quiet oasis just off the busy Dogen-zaka strip.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen favors fresh, seasonal ingredients without excess — aiming for clean, straightforward, health-conscious soba rather than heavy or elaborate preparations.
chef_bio.specialty: Stone-ground (ishiusu-biki), fragrant thin soba noodles, served in dishes such as kakiage-soba (tempura fritter soba) and seasonal specials like cold eggplant soba (hiyashi-nasu soba) and 'su-gomori soba' served heaped in a large bowl.

anecdotes.text: Described by local food bloggers as a hidden gem down a Dogen-zaka side street, where portions are generous and toppings (like the kakiage tempura) are notably large.
Reviewers note the dining room's calm, dignified atmosphere — a bright room with a garden view reached via a staircase, a contrast to the gritty street below.
Called a beloved old-school soba spot that has drawn a cross-generational local following for decades.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hot soba is served in a dashi- and shoyu-based broth, and cold soba comes with a shoyu-based dipping sauce (tsuyu) — both traditional preparations that contain wheat via soy sauce.

positives.text: Menu includes vegetable tempura, cold eggplant (nasu), and natto dishes alongside the standard soba, giving some plant-forward options on the menu (though broths are traditionally dashi-based).

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, Japanese-language-first neighborhood soba shop with no indication of an English menu, though it has been covered in a few English-language Tokyo food blogs for visitors comfortable ordering in Japanese or by pointing at a menu.

places.name: 満点籠 まんてんろう (Mantenrou)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's entire identity centers on wheat-wrapped dumplings (xiaolongbao and pan-fried shengjianbao), so wheat is unavoidable in the signature dishes. Standard soy sauce dipping condiments used with xiaolongbao in this style of cooking typically contain wheat as well. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated cooking equipment was mentioned in any source reviewed.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around meat- and seafood-filled dumplings (pork, shrimp, crab roe) and other Shanghainese dishes; no vegetarian or vegan xiaolongbao variety or other plant-based dish was identified in any source. Broths and sauces in this style of Shanghainese cooking commonly use animal-based stock.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区小川町 (小川町駅・神保町駅周辺)
places.cuisine: 中華・小籠包・上海料理
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The dim sum chef is from Shanghai and previously trained/worked at a well-known Tokyo hotel kitchen before opening this shop in Kanda Ogawamachi in 2026. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shanghai, China
chef_bio.background: The dim sum chef is from Shanghai and previously trained/worked at a well-known Tokyo hotel kitchen before opening this shop in Kanda Ogawamachi in 2026.
chef_bio.philosophy: Dumplings are wrapped only after an order comes in and steamed immediately on high heat, a technique meant to keep moisture from softening the wrapper while sealing in the meat broth; the kitchen also emphasizes domestically sourced (Japan-grown) ingredients for the fillings.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-wrapped xiaolongbao (soup dumplings) made to order in four varieties (plain, shiitake, shrimp, crab roe), plus pan-fried shengjianbao and scallion-oil noodles.

anecdotes.text: Because the xiaolongbao are wrapped to order and steamed the moment they're formed, the skin stays taut rather than becoming soggy, and the broth trapped inside the filling stays hot and juicy when the dumpling is bitten into.
The Shanghai-born owner-chef also serves shengjianbao (pan-fried soup dumplings) and scallion-oil noodles alongside the signature xiaolongbao.

cultural_comfort.note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; this reads as a standard local neighborhood restaurant best visited with a translation app or this guide's help.

places.name: ごちそう焼むすび おにまる 西新宿店 (Gochisou Yaki Musubi Onimaru Nishi-Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: Rice itself is naturally gluten-free, but many of the signature items use a soy-sauce-based glaze (e.g., the "kaeshi shoyu" tuna mayo and several other flavors), and standard Japanese soy sauce/kaeshi is typically wheat-based; mayo-based fillings in Japan (commonly Kewpie brand) may also contain barley. No dedicated gluten-free menu, labeling, or preparation protocol was found in any source, and there is a tempura shrimp item ("海老天むす") suggesting a shared fryer. Celiac travelers should ask staff directly about ingredients per item rather than assume any flavor is safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or plant-based labeling was found. The great majority of the roughly 20 daily rice-ball flavors contain fish (salmon, katsuobushi/bonito dashi, tuna, shirasu), meat (pork), egg, cheese, or mayonnaise. A plain salted rice ball is implied to exist as a base option (separate from the ~20 specialty flavors), which could be vegan, but this was not confirmed as dashi-free or explicitly marketed as vegan by the shop.
places.hours_raw: 平日7:30〜21:00、土日祝9:00〜21:00、年中無休 (Weekdays 7:30–21:00, weekends/holidays 9:00–21:00, no closed days)
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Shinjuku Station west exit
places.cuisine: おにぎり・焼きおむすび
places.notes: Onimaru is a specialty "gochisou yaki musubi" (deluxe grilled rice ball) chain. The Nishi-Shinjuku shop, which opened April 21, 2024, was the brand's first Tokyo location, chosen for its spot a short walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: takeout counter with limited outdoor bench seating
chef_bio.origin: Aichi-based rice-ball chain expanding nationally (Nagoya area origin), with this shop marking its Tokyo debut.
chef_bio.background: Onimaru is a specialty "gochisou yaki musubi" (deluxe grilled rice ball) chain. The Nishi-Shinjuku shop, which opened April 21, 2024, was the brand's first Tokyo location, chosen for its spot a short walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around a hand-selected large-grain rice variety called "Koshihougyoku," cooked in a large kettle for texture, then finished by grilling over bincho charcoal for fragrance; the shop offers roughly 20 rotating rice-ball varieties made fresh through the day, aimed at quick takeout for commuters (weekday opening from 7:30am).
chef_bio.specialty: Gochisou yaki musubi — a bincho-charcoal grilled rice ball stuffed with a fluffy, housemade rolled omelet (tamagoyaki).

anecdotes.text: Opened April 21, 2024 as the Onimaru brand's first Tokyo store, located near Shinjuku Station's west exit.
The shop stocks about 20 varieties of rice balls, including a salmon musubi made with a seven-type dashi blend and a tuna mayo finished with a traditional simmered (kaeshi) soy sauce.
The signature item is a grilled rice ball filled with a fluffy, housemade rolled omelet (tamagoyaki).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several signature onigiri, such as the tuna mayo and salmon rice balls, are finished with a traditional "kaeshi" simmered soy sauce, a style of Japanese soy sauce that typically contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple walk-up takeout counter with a visible display case of rice balls, so ordering by pointing works fine even without Japanese, but no English menu or GF/vegan signage was found.

places.name: 鶏そば・ラーメン Tonari (Torisoba Ramen Tonari)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen/tsukemen shop with no mention of gluten-free noodles or a separate preparation area. The signature tare is made from "Shichifuku" white soy sauce (shiro shoyu), a wheat-based seasoning, and the broth is finished with bonito. No gluten-free options, dedicated equipment, or allergy protocol were found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core broths are chicken-bone based and finished with bonito (katsuo) dashi; the oyster ramen adds shellfish. No plant-based broth, dashi swap, or vegetable-only bowl was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-21:00 (may close earlier once the day's soup sells out); closed Sundays and public holidays
places.neighborhood: Shinsen / Maruyama-cho, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tonari is a small counter ramen shop in the back streets of Shinsen (Shibuya), run by the same operator behind a neighboring oyster bar. The head chef, trained in Japanese cuisine, spent time studying light, clear-broth ramen shops around Tokyo before building the restaurant's si · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Head chef trained in traditional Japanese cuisine
chef_bio.background: Tonari is a small counter ramen shop in the back streets of Shinsen (Shibuya), run by the same operator behind a neighboring oyster bar. The head chef, trained in Japanese cuisine, spent time studying light, clear-broth ramen shops around Tokyo before building the restaurant's signature chicken soba.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids synthetic seasoning entirely. The base broth simmers chicken bones, gizzards, and skin from Misakura-branded chicken with kombu dashi and a light touch of garlic, ginger, and scallion for about two hours, finished with a whisper of bonito. The tare is made with 'Shichifuku,' said to be Japan's only certified organic white soy sauce (shiro shoyu), built up with dried shiitake mushroom and dried shrimp using a technique borrowed from soba-dipping-sauce making rather than chemical dashi powder.
chef_bio.specialty: White-soy-sauce chicken soba (concentrated chicken broth with a pale, clear tare) and an oyster ramen made with oyster broth and oyster soy sauce, topped with oyster meat and oyster wontons — drawing ingredients from the operator's sister oyster bar next door. The menu also rotates in tsukemen, abura-soba, maze-soba, and weekly specials built around other proteins such as red sea bream or sweet shrimp.

anecdotes.text: The head chef, trained in Japanese cuisine, researched light and clear broth ramen shops across Tokyo before developing Tonari's signature chicken soba.

The chicken soba's clear broth is simmered for two hours from Misakura chicken bones, gizzards and skin with kombu dashi, finished with a subtle bonito note, and paired with a tare made from Japan's only certified organic white soy sauce, 'Shichifuku,' enriched with dried shiitake and dried shrimp instead of chemical seasoning.

Alongside the chicken soba, Tonari serves an oyster ramen combining oyster broth and oyster soy sauce with oyster meat and oyster wontons, using ingredients shared with the operator's neighboring oyster bar.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is built from 'Shichifuku,' a certified organic white soy sauce (shiro shoyu) — a wheat-based product — with no gluten-free substitute mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house broths are chicken-bone based and finished with bonito (katsuo) dashi; no vegetable-only broth or plant-based bowl was identified.

positives.text: The kitchen states it avoids chemical seasoning (no MSG), instead building umami from dried shiitake mushroom, dried shrimp, and slow-simmered chicken bones.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat neighborhood ramen counter with a Japanese-language menu and no indication of English signage or staff — approachable if you can point at menu items, but not set up for non-Japanese-speaking tourists.

places.name: ガーデンテラス (Garden Terrace)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or gluten-free cake line was found in official or press sources. As a French-style hotel patisserie, cakes and baked goods are conventionally wheat-based; there is no mention of a dedicated gluten-free fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or gluten-free preparation area. Celiac or gluten-sensitive diners should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu items are advertised. This is a French-style patisserie serving butter-, cream-, and egg-based cakes and traditional afternoon tea sets, so vegan diners should ask staff directly about any dairy/egg-free substitutions before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 受付時間 10:00〜20:00、定休日なし (Reception hours 10:00-20:00, no closed days; no confirmed last-order time found)
places.neighborhood: Suidobashi / Koraku (Tokyo Dome Hotel lobby, Bunkyo-ku)
places.notes: Garden Terrace is the ground-floor lobby lounge of the Tokyo Dome Hotel, a large international hotel adjacent to Tokyo Dome and directly by Suidobashi Station, which opened in June 2000 as part of the Tokyo Dome City entertainment complex. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Garden Terrace is the ground-floor lobby lounge of the Tokyo Dome Hotel, a large international hotel adjacent to Tokyo Dome and directly by Suidobashi Station, which opened in June 2000 as part of the Tokyo Dome City entertainment complex.
chef_bio.philosophy: Designed around an 'urban oasis' concept, the lounge is a light-filled, water-and-greenery-bordered space where guests can relax over tea and cake while watching the courtyard fountain through floor-to-ceiling windows.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal whole cakes and afternoon tea sets made by the hotel's pastry team, also available for takeout

anecdotes.text: For the hotel's 26th anniversary, the pastry team created a special anniversary cake called "Jardin de Fruits" (ジャルダン・ド・フリュイ).

cultural_comfort.note: As the lobby lounge of a large international hotel next to Tokyo Dome, it is set up for foreign guests with an easy, walk-in, English-friendly atmosphere.

places.name: 北海道名物らー麺 えびそば 一幻 新宿店 (Hokkaido Meibutsu Ramen Ebisoba Ichigen Shin)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are wheat-based with no gluten-free substitute offered. The shoyu (soy sauce) tare and broth contain wheat, and the ebi-tempura topping is wheat-battered and fried. No dedicated gluten-free items, dedicated fryer, or allergen-free procedure was found. Not safe for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is built from pork bone/back fat and simmered shrimp heads with a house-made shrimp oil; noodles and tare also contain wheat/egg. No vegetarian or vegan menu items were found. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 daily (official site lists no fixed weekly closed day; some secondary listings mention Wednesday closures)
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Seibu-Shinjuku Station
places.notes: Ebisoba Ichigen began in Sapporo in 2003, founded by Masahiko Maeda after training at other ramen shops. He built the concept around a shrimp-based ramen unlike the miso/shio/shoyu norm of Hokkaido ramen, and the brand has since expanded to New Chitose Airport, Kansai, and Tokyo  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Sapporo, Hokkaido (founded 2003)
chef_bio.background: Ebisoba Ichigen began in Sapporo in 2003, founded by Masahiko Maeda after training at other ramen shops. He built the concept around a shrimp-based ramen unlike the miso/shio/shoyu norm of Hokkaido ramen, and the brand has since expanded to New Chitose Airport, Kansai, and Tokyo (including this Shinjuku shop, 3 minutes' walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station).
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's identity is built entirely around extracting maximum flavor from shrimp: heads are simmered for hours, then finished with a house-made shrimp oil and back fat stir-fried in a wok to build a rich, aromatic shrimp broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Ebisoba (shrimp ramen) in shio, miso, or shoyu tare, with a choice of thick or thin noodles and three broth-intensity levels (そのまま/ほどほど/味わい)

anecdotes.text: Every day, large quantities of sweet-shrimp heads are slow-simmered in big stockpots; the extracted umami is then finished with a proprietary shrimp oil and back fat stir-fried in a Chinese wok to create the shop's signature rich shrimp broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Broth, tare (shoyu/miso/shio), and noodles are all wheat/gluten-containing standard ramen components; no gluten-free process identified.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu ramen's noodles, soup, and ebi-ten topping all contain wheat, per the retail product's allergen listing for the same recipe line.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-fat/pork-bone and shrimp based; no vegetarian or vegan broth option identified.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a self-service ticket vending machine (mostly Japanese), but the counter is small and welcoming, and staff are used to walk-in customers near Seibu-Shinjuku Station.

places.name: 正ちゃん (Shōchan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodations are mentioned anywhere in the sourcing. The signature nikomi stew is simmered in a soy-based sauce (Japanese soy sauce/shoyu almost always contains wheat) that has reportedly been continuously topped up since 1951, meaning any wheat content is baked into the base and cannot be removed per-order. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are indicated in any sourcing. The signature and essentially only notable dish is a beef stew simmered with beef tendon in a meat-based, soy-sauce broth carried forward for decades; there is no evidence of vegetable-only or dashi-free preparations. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 12:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Mon & Tue, though the shop sometimes opens irregularly on those days and hours can shift with weather/stock (stall-style operation); some sources report weekend hours as early as 9:00.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, Hoppy Street (ホッピー通り), near Asakusa Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shōchan started as one of the yatai (open-air food stalls) that lined Asakusa's postwar Hoppy Street, opening in 1951 (Shōwa 26). It became a fixed shopfront roughly four decades later but kept its stall-style, half-outdoor seating and its original simmering pot of beef stew, top · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: small family-run izakaya / postwar food stall lineage
chef_bio.background: Shōchan started as one of the yatai (open-air food stalls) that lined Asakusa's postwar Hoppy Street, opening in 1951 (Shōwa 26). It became a fixed shopfront roughly four decades later but kept its stall-style, half-outdoor seating and its original simmering pot of beef stew, topped up continuously ever since (a "tsugitashi" nikomi sauce).
chef_bio.philosophy: An old-school Asakusa drinking-stall spirit: cheap, simple, unpretentious dishes (many under ¥500) meant to be eaten quickly at the counter or an outdoor tent table while drinking Hoppy, with the same pot of sauce carried forward for decades rather than remade from scratch.
chef_bio.specialty: Gyu-nikomi — beef tendon, konnyaku (devil's tongue jelly) and tofu slow-simmered for hours in a sweet-savory, soy-based sauce that has been continuously replenished since the shop opened; also served over rice as gyu-meshi.

anecdotes.text: David Bowie is said to have visited the shop incognito, and locals still say 'if you come to Hoppy Street, go to Shōchan.'

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, tourist-visited stop on Hoppy Street with an English-language Tabelog listing and reportedly an English menu, but it is an old-school local stall where Japanese is the working language and no dietary accommodation should be assumed.

places.name: ガシラ 渋谷店 (Gashira Shibuya-ten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. As a traditional Japanese seafood izakaya, dishes very likely use wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi, and the menu includes fried items (tempura-style preparations are typical of this genre) with no indication of a dedicated fryer. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm ingredient-by-ingredient; this is not a restaurant that can be assumed safe without direct staff confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. This is a fish-centric seafood izakaya where the core concept is fresh daily catch prepared by a chef at the counter; dashi and other stocks should be assumed to contain bonito/fish unless confirmed otherwise. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya
places.notes: Gashira Shibuya bills itself as a "fish shop with a chef in it" (料理人のいる魚屋) — a seafood-focused izakaya tucked on a side street off Dogenzaka, a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station. Fish bought fresh each morning is displayed at the counter and the menu changes daily depending  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Gashira Shibuya bills itself as a "fish shop with a chef in it" (料理人のいる魚屋) — a seafood-focused izakaya tucked on a side street off Dogenzaka, a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station. Fish bought fresh each morning is displayed at the counter and the menu changes daily depending on what came in.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself less as a conventional restaurant and more as a fishmonger's counter where a chef prepares the day's catch to order, letting the seasonal fish itself set the menu rather than a fixed menu of dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: Chef's daily 5-piece sashimi selection (刺身5点盛り) made from whatever fish was landed that morning

anecdotes.text: Local blog coverage describes it as a restaurant serving delicious fish tucked quietly away on a small side street off Dogenzaka.
Listing copy notes the shop takes pride in offering fresh fish, including varieties not commonly found at other restaurants, at reasonable prices.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used or available; as a conventional izakaya it should be assumed standard wheat-containing shoyu is used throughout unless staff confirm otherwise.

cultural_comfort.note: A local Japanese-style seafood izakaya near Shibuya Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service — diners with dietary restrictions should be prepared to explain them in Japanese or via translation.

places.name: 北ノ醤油チーホー 水道橋店 (Kita no Shoyu Chi-Ho (Suidobashi))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options are mentioned anywhere. The signature bowl uses standard wheat ramen noodles (thick, wavy Sapporo-style) and a soy-sauce (shoyu) based broth, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. No dedicated fryer, allergen menu, or gluten-free substitution was found in any source — assume this is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option is mentioned. The signature ramen is pork-based (minced pork stir-fried with garlic, pork back fat for richness), and no plant-based broth or noodle option was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00, no listed closed days (定休日:なし) per most sources; one source referenced irregular holidays (不定休)
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町 (Kanda Misaki-cho), Suidobashi
places.notes: Opened March 25, 2026 as the first physical restaurant produced by SUSURU, one of Japan's best-known ramen YouTubers, whose channel is built around eating a bowl of ramen every single day. The concept reportedly grew out of a ramen event he took part in at Tokyo Racecourse in Feb · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened March 25, 2026 as the first physical restaurant produced by SUSURU, one of Japan's best-known ramen YouTubers, whose channel is built around eating a bowl of ramen every single day. The concept reportedly grew out of a ramen event he took part in at Tokyo Racecourse in February 2026. The shop is run by the company andfoods, which also operates the ramen shop "Ore no Ikiru Michi. " SUSURU had turned down repeated offers to open his own restaurant before finally committing to this one, and the shop drew lines of 50+ people from its opening day.
chef_bio.philosophy: A YouTuber's personal, hands-on take on "serious" shoyu ramen — bringing the intensity and obsessive daily ramen-eating culture of his channel into a physical shop rather than opening a generic tie-in restaurant.
chef_bio.specialty: 北ノ醤油ラーメン (Kita no Shoyu Ramen) — a Sapporo-style bowl with thick, wavy noodles in a rich soy-sauce broth, topped with garlic-fried minced pork and bean sprouts stir-fried in an iron pan for a toasty, addictive aroma, finished with pork back fat for sweetness.

anecdotes.text: SUSURU had repeatedly turned down offers over the years to open his own restaurant before finally deciding to open this one.
The shop's opening day drew a queue of more than 50 people.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is built on a shoyu (soy sauce) broth; no gluten-free or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source, so standard wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth and toppings are pork-based (minced pork stir-fried with garlic, pork back fat), with no vegetarian or vegan preparation mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A hype local ramen counter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service — expect to order in Japanese and likely wait in line, since the shop is a social-media/YouTuber sensation.

places.name: 新宿 屋台苑 (Shinjuku Yataien)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found for this complex. As a yakitori/yakiniku/izakaya venue, tare glazing sauces and shoyu (both typically wheat-based) are standard preparation, and no gluten-free protocol or dedicated cooking equipment has been documented. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat- and seafood-centered yakitori/yakiniku/shellfish complex; no vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/bonito-free preparations were found in any source. Standard izakaya dashi (typically bonito/fish-based) should be assumed unless a stall confirms otherwise.
places.hours_raw: 11:00〜翌5:00（年中無休、営業時間は変更となる場合あり／各店舗までご確認ください）— sources vary; one listing mentions Wednesday closures and another mentions 24-hour operation for a sub-unit, unconfirmed
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku 3-chome, near JR Shinjuku Station East Exit
places.cuisine: Yakitori, yakiniku, shellfish and ramen yata
places.notes: Opened August 1, 2022, on the former site of the long-loved Shinjuku fruit stand 'Hyakkaen,' just steps from Shinjuku Station's east exit. It was built as part of a 'station-front yatai revival' project by producer Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho, aiming to recreate the casual, o · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Neo-yatai (food stall) dining complex produced by Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho, the Tokyo hospitality group behind Ebisu Yokocho and Shibuya Yokocho
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (Shinjuku), part of a Tokyo/Saitama/Chiba/Guam-wide chain of 'yokocho' and yatai dining complexes produced by the same company
chef_bio.background: Opened August 1, 2022, on the former site of the long-loved Shinjuku fruit stand 'Hyakkaen,' just steps from Shinjuku Station's east exit. It was built as part of a 'station-front yatai revival' project by producer Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho, aiming to recreate the casual, open-air food-stall culture that once stood in front of Japanese train stations before it disappeared amid Tokyo's redevelopment.
chef_bio.philosophy: Revive the informal 'ekimae yatai' (station-front stall) drinking culture of old Japan, giving travelers and commuters a retro, Showa-era open-air spot to gather for a quick skewer and a drink.
chef_bio.specialty: Asabiki (morning-fresh) yakitori from the in-house stall Hotei, served alongside shellfish from Kaidō, wagyu yakiniku from Shinjuku-en, and spicy noodles from Karamen — four stalls under one open-sided roof.

anecdotes.text: The complex was built on the site of the long-running Shinjuku fruit shop Hyakkaen, reusing the location's history as a neighborhood gathering point.
Described in the press as a retro, smoking-allowed Showa-style izakaya that transformed the well-known former fruit-shop site into a stall-lined drinking spot.
The complex groups four independent stall concepts — shellfish stall Kaidō, wagyu yakiniku stall Shinjuku-en, spicy noodle stall Karamen, and morning-fresh yakitori stall Hotei — under one roof.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, smoking-permitted retro-Showa izakaya complex aimed at a local/Japanese crowd; no English menu or English-speaking-staff confirmation was found, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board will help.

places.name: RICE HACK Gluten-free Bakery（ライスハック グルテンフリーベーカリー） (RICE HACK Gluten-free Bakery)
places.gf_detail: Dedicated 100% gluten-free bakery — all breads, baguettes, curry bread, and pizza are made with Japanese rice flour instead of wheat flour, developed specifically so people with celiac disease or wheat allergy can eat safely alongside family members without allergies. No wheat-flour products are baked on site. Specific allergen details for individual items (egg, dairy, soy sauce ingredients) are not documented in available sources, so celiac visitors should still confirm with staff for any item with a filling or glaze.
places.vegan_detail: Multiple vegan-friendly items are available, notably a vegan marinara pizza that reviewers single out; the bakery is popular with vegan and vegetarian visitors seeking gluten-free options. However, not every item is vegan — some baked goods (e.g. certain enriched breads or fillings) may contain egg or dairy — so vegan diners should ask staff about specific products before buying. No documented use of hidden dashi/katsuo (bonito) was found, but this has not been explicitly confirmed for every item.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00-15:00; Tue-Wed closed; Thu-Sun 11:00-17:00 (hours reported as somewhat variable due to small single-staff operation and selling out of items)
places.neighborhood: 神宮前 (Jingumae, near Meiji-Jingumae Station / Cat Street, Har
places.notes: RICE HACK Gluten-free Bakery was produced by Setsuko Otsuka, known in Japan as "米粉の母" (the "Mother of Rice Flour"), a leading figure in the country's rice-flour industry. The bakery opened in the Jingumae/Omotesando area of Shibuya on November 12, 2022, under the company Rice Ter · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: RICE HACK Gluten-free Bakery was produced by Setsuko Otsuka, known in Japan as "米粉の母" (the "Mother of Rice Flour"), a leading figure in the country's rice-flour industry. The bakery opened in the Jingumae/Omotesando area of Shibuya on November 12, 2022, under the company Rice Terrace Inc. (株式会社ライステラス), which also runs the adjoining Onden House café and rice-flour cooking classes.
chef_bio.philosophy: Otsuka's push to perfect gluten-free rice-flour bread grew out of a desire to let families eat the same bread together even when one member has a wheat allergy or celiac disease; years of development went into rice-flour bread that stays moist rather than the dry, crumbly texture typical of gluten-free bread.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% gluten-free rice-flour baguette and curry bread (カレーパン), plus rice-flour pizza — the vegan marinara pizza is frequently singled out by reviewers.

anecdotes.text: Otsuka's motivation traced back to a family in which one child could eat bread while a younger sibling with a wheat allergy could not — she wanted to make it possible for the whole family to eat the same bread together, and after years of research completed a 100% gluten-free rice-flour bread.

The bakery's rice-flour bread is described as moist and soft rather than dry and hard even after cooling — a common complaint about gluten-free bread — and many first-time customers reportedly become repeat buyers on the spot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: RICE HACK is a bakery dedicated to 100% gluten-free rice-flour products; no wheat flour is used in its breads, which were developed specifically so people with celiac disease or wheat allergy can eat safely.

positives.text: Reviewers on international gluten-free travel platforms describe it as a must-visit bakery in Tokyo for people with celiac disease, highlighting the rice-flour baguette and a vegan marinara pizza.
Listed as a dedicated gluten-free bakery on multiple English-language celiac/gluten-free directories.

cultural_comfort.note: Featured in several English-language gluten-free/celiac travel guides and popular with overseas visitors, but the bakery operates primarily in Japanese with no confirmed English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 庄や 水道橋店（本家店） (Shoya Suidobashi (Honke / "the original stor)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol was found for this location. As a standard izakaya it relies on wheat-based soy sauce, tempura batter, and other wheat-containing seasonings across most cooked dishes, so cross-contamination risk should be assumed high; celiac diners would need to ask staff directly and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-adapted dishes were found. The restaurant centers on fresh sashimi and chef-prepared seafood/meat dishes, and traditional bonito-based dashi likely underlies most cooked and simmered items, so genuinely vegan options are unlikely without a special, unconfirmed request to staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sun (including holidays): 11:30–14:00, 17:00–23:00 (food last order 22:00); open year-round with no scheduled closed days
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidobashi), Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: This exact location is the birthplace of the entire Shoya izakaya chain. Founder Taira Tatsu began with a small yakitori stall near Ikegami Honmon-ji temple in 1968; as that business grew, he opened the first-ever "Shoya" here in Kanda-Misakicho in 1973 (Showa 48), a one-minute w · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Founder: Taira Tatsu (平辰), originally from Sado Island, Niigata
chef_bio.origin: Kanda-Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku — founding location of the nationwide Shoya chain, opened in 1973
chef_bio.background: This exact location is the birthplace of the entire Shoya izakaya chain. Founder Taira Tatsu began with a small yakitori stall near Ikegami Honmon-ji temple in 1968; as that business grew, he opened the first-ever "Shoya" here in Kanda-Misakicho in 1973 (Showa 48), a one-minute walk from Suidobashi Station. A plaque still hangs at the entrance marking it as the chain's "birthplace" (発祥の地).
chef_bio.philosophy: Conceived as "an oasis in the urban desert" — home-style cooking and service meant to feel wrapped in maternal warmth, prepared by hand by resident chefs (itamae)
chef_bio.specialty: Generous fresh sashimi platters, the restaurant's signature since it opened, plus the clam butter (あさりバター) dish that Shoya itself originated

anecdotes.text: A sign at the entrance reads: "Founded in Showa 48 (1973), Shoya took its first breath in this place. We continue to ask for your patronage, together with our main store, into the future" — commemorating the site as the chain's birthplace.

cultural_comfort.note: A large, well-known nationwide chain right by the station exit with picture-menu ordering and card payment, but no confirmed English menu, so a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: アイスの沼 浅草 伝法院通り店 (Aisu no Numa Asakusa Denboin-dori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims, allergen list, or wheat-avoidance information were found for this shop in any source checked. As a gelato/ice specialty shop, base gelato is often naturally free of wheat, but the 40+ flavor lineup likely includes cookie, wafer, or biscuit mix-ins and cones, and scoops are typically served from open, shared cases with shared scoops — a cross-contamination risk for celiac diners. Nothing confirms a dedicated gluten-free process. Ask staff which specific flavors and cones are wheat-free before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, dairy-free flavor list, or plant-based claim was found for this shop. Gelato is traditionally milk/cream-based; the large flavor lineup may include fruit-forward sorbetto-style flavors that are naturally dairy-light, but none were confirmed as vegan (egg, honey, or dairy solids are common even in fruit gelato). Ask staff which flavors, if any, are made without dairy or egg.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 9:30-17:00; Saturdays, Sundays and holidays 9:30-18:00 (per opening announcement); closed-day policy not confirmed.
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草（伝法院通り、浅草駅より徒歩約5分）
places.cuisine: ジェラート・かき氷専門店
places.notes: Aisu no Numa ("Ice Cream Swamp") is the first directly-operated retail shop opened by Rex Very, a company that has long wholesaled premium gelato to restaurants and retailers across Japan. The Denboin-dori shop opened on 18 July 2025, with an interior that blends traditional Japa · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: flagship/direct-operated store of a gelato wholesaler
chef_bio.origin: First flagship store of gelato wholesaler Rex Very, opened July 2025 on Denboin-dori, Asakusa.
chef_bio.background: Aisu no Numa ("Ice Cream Swamp") is the first directly-operated retail shop opened by Rex Very, a company that has long wholesaled premium gelato to restaurants and retailers across Japan. The Denboin-dori shop opened on 18 July 2025, with an interior that blends traditional Japanese motifs with a modern, playful look to suit its location on one of Asakusa's classic shopping streets.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's guiding idea is "nani yori mo sozai" (ingredients above all) — building each gelato flavor around the character of a specific, often regional, ingredient rather than standard mix formulas.
chef_bio.specialty: Rotating lineup of 40+ gelato and ice flavors built around single ingredients, including a Super Premium strawberry gelato made with 60% Fukuoka Amaou strawberry juice and pulp, and a peanut gelato made with premium Yachimata-grown peanuts from Chiba.

anecdotes.text: The shop's concept is described as wanting customers to "fall into a swamp" (numaru) of outstanding ice cream, built entirely around maximizing the quality of its ingredients.

Signature items include a Super Premium Amaou strawberry gelato (¥800, limited quantity) made with 60% strawberry juice/pulp from Fukuoka, and a Premium peanut gelato (¥650) using Chiba's Yachimata-grown peanuts.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source addresses cross-contamination practices for this shop; gelato is typically scooped from open cases with shared scoops, a common cross-contact concern for celiac customers, but this shop's specific handling is unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No source confirms whether dairy-based and any non-dairy/fruit-based flavors are scooped with separate equipment.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: みちのり (Michinori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation was mentioned in any source found. As a Japanese-Asian fusion izakaya, dishes are likely to involve soy sauce, dashi, and fried items that typically contain wheat; diners with celiac disease should not assume safety and should ask staff directly (note: Japanese-language ability likely needed, see cultural_comfort_note). This is a general omnivore restaurant, not a gluten-free-focused establishment.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or vegan-specific accommodation was found. Signature dishes are meat- and seafood-based (avocado-tuna yukhoe, herb-grilled chicken, cream-sauce salmon steak), and as a Japanese-Asian izakaya, hidden dashi/katsuo (bonito) in sauces and broths is likely. Vegetable-forward small plates may exist given the "homemade herb" cooking style, but this could not be confirmed. Vegans should ask staff about ingredients dish-by-dish.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-16:00, Dinner 16:00-23:00, reported open daily including weekends/holidays (one source instead lists Lunch 12:00-16:00 / Dinner 16:00-23:30 — see caveats)
places.neighborhood: 宇田川町 (Udagawa-cho), Shibuya
places.notes: Opened in Udagawa-cho, Shibuya in October 2024 as the sister restaurant of the Shoto-area izakaya "Michi Nakimichi" (ミチナキミチ). The space, a former café, was DIY-renovated into a green-and-wood natural-toned izakaya & bar spanning the 3rd and 4th floors of the Shin Tokyo Building,  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Chef reported to hail from Nagasaki, Kyushu
chef_bio.background: Opened in Udagawa-cho, Shibuya in October 2024 as the sister restaurant of the Shoto-area izakaya "Michi Nakimichi" (ミチナキミチ). The space, a former café, was DIY-renovated into a green-and-wood natural-toned izakaya & bar spanning the 3rd and 4th floors of the Shin Tokyo Building, functioning like a café by day for lunch and transforming into a full Asian-style izakaya by night.
chef_bio.philosophy: Homemade, herb-forward cooking that blends Japanese technique with Asian/ethnic flavors (described locally as "和なエスニック料理"), built around fresh vegetables and seafood, with an emphasis on visually striking plating and Kyushu-sourced ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Avocado and tuna yukhoe (avocado to maguro no yukke) and herb-grilled chicken are cited as signature dishes; a cream-sauce salmon steak is also frequently mentioned.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe it as a hidden-gem Asian creative izakaya near Shibuya Station, with an Instagram-worthy DIY green-and-wood interior and standout dishes like the avocado-tuna yukhoe and herb-grilled chicken.
Described as a sister restaurant to the popular izakaya Michi Nakimichi, run by a Kyushu (Nagasaki)-born izakaya cook who applies local Kyushu ingredients to a Japanese-inflected ethnic-food concept.

cultural_comfort.note: Listings and reservations run through Japanese-language platforms (Tabelog Japan, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Yahoo Map, AutoReserve); an auto-translated English Tabelog page exists but no dedicated English-language service was confirmed, so diners without Japanese should expect a Japanese-only in-restaurant experience.

places.name: 炭火焼干物定食 しんぱち食堂 水道橋店 (Sumibiyaki Himono Teishoku Shinpachi Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source for this location or the chain generally. The teishoku format bundles grilled fish with miso soup, pickles, and often a dipping sauce; Japanese soy sauce and some pickles/marinades commonly contain wheat, and nothing indicates ingredient substitution or cross-contact controls. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct, in-person confirmation from staff, and even then reliability is uncertain given the absence of any published allergen information.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around grilled dried fish, and the miso soup is made with traditional Japanese dashi, which is typically bonito (katsuobushi)-based and not vegan. No vegetarian or vegan set meal, dashi substitution, or plant-based option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners as currently documented.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町（千代田区）、水道橋駅西口近く
places.notes: Shinpachi Shokudo is a Japanese set-meal (teishoku) chain built around charcoal-grilled dried fish (himono). The brand's stated mission is to "restore and evolve Japan's everyday food culture," pairing carefully sourced grilled fish with attention to rice quality, house-made dash · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Japanese set-meal (teishoku) chain restaurant, counter/table seating arranged in a U-shape around an open kitchen
chef_bio.origin: Chain brand (57+ locations nationwide as of 2026), now part of the Skylark Group restaurant company; this Suidobashi branch opened around April 2020.
chef_bio.background: Shinpachi Shokudo is a Japanese set-meal (teishoku) chain built around charcoal-grilled dried fish (himono). The brand's stated mission is to "restore and evolve Japan's everyday food culture," pairing carefully sourced grilled fish with attention to rice quality, house-made dashi-based miso soup, and pickles from established, traditional producers.
chef_bio.philosophy: "Protecting a value that is one of a kind" — fish is grilled to order on an in-house-developed charcoal grill after the order is placed, with sourcing regions for the dried fish carefully chosen; the brand frames itself as reviving traditional Japanese home-style cooking (gohan, miso shiru, tsukemono) at accessible prices.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled dried fish set meals — the menu features more than 20 kinds of grilled fish (salmon, mackerel, sardine, aji, etc. ), grilled to order on a proprietary charcoal grilling setup rather than pre-cooked, alongside a signature unagi (eel) donburi made with eel from a hundred-year-old wholesaler.

positives.text: Dried fish is grilled to order over charcoal rather than fried, and the menu emphasizes simple preparations (salt-grilled or dashi-based) rather than heavily sauced dishes, which may simplify identifying lower-risk items — though this has not been confirmed with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, high-turnover Japanese set-meal chain with picture-menu teishoku boards; basic Japanese or pointing at menu photos is enough to order, but no English menu was confirmed.

places.name: すっぽん 田一（でんいち） (Suppon Denichi (Minami-Shinjuku))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or wheat-free accommodation is advertised. The signature karaage (fried turtle) is coated in kuzu (arrowroot) starch rather than wheat flour, which is a favorable sign, but the course also includes hot pot and dipping preparations typical of Japanese haute cuisine that commonly use wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) and bonito-based dashi; no statement about gluten-free soy sauce, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination handling was found. Diners needing strict gluten avoidance should call ahead and confirm specifics with staff.
places.vegan_detail: This is a specialty suppon (soft-shell turtle) restaurant — every course centers on turtle meat (hot pot, karaage, liver sashimi) alongside egg-based chawanmushi and dashi-based broths. There is no vegetarian or vegan course offered, and nothing in the published menus suggests any plant-based option exists.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-14:00, Dinner 17:00-22:00. Sat & holidays: Lunch 12:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00 (Saturday/holiday lunch reservations must be made by 18:00 the previous day). Closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: 南新宿 (Minami-Shinjuku)
places.cuisine: すっぽん料理・日本料理
places.notes: A specialty Japanese course restaurant built entirely around suppon (soft-shell snapping turtle), located steps from Minami-Shinjuku Station on the south side of Shinjuku Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A specialty Japanese course restaurant built entirely around suppon (soft-shell snapping turtle), located steps from Minami-Shinjuku Station on the south side of Shinjuku Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant sources 'Hagakure suppon' from farms across Japan that the owners visited personally, raised without pesticides or herbicides in EM-bacteria-treated water and fed only pesticide-free vegetable feed — quality said to command roughly double the price of standard farmed suppon.
chef_bio.specialty: すっぽん革命コース (Suppon Kakumei course) — kuzu-starch-fried turtle karaage, liver sashimi salad, chawanmushi, suppon hot pot, and a finishing rice porridge (zōsui)

anecdotes.text: Its signature sourcing story: turtles are raised in specially selected farms using microorganism (EM)-treated water and pesticide-free feed, described by the restaurant as yielding a noticeably higher-grade 'Hagakure suppon' than typical farmed turtle.

Course descriptions note the karaage (fried turtle) is coated in kuzu (arrowroot) starch rather than a standard batter, served alongside liver sashimi, chawanmushi, hot pot and a closing rice porridge as part of the multi-course meal.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, reservation-only Japanese course restaurant with semi-private table rooms; expect Japanese-language service and no English menu, so it helps to confirm dietary needs by phone (or with a Japanese-speaking helper) before your visit.

places.name: 酒さかな ずぶ六 (Sakasakana Zuburoku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or GF preparation protocol was found in any source. This is a traditional seasonal-fish izakaya built around simmered, grilled and dried fish dishes with soy-sauce-based seasoning, so wheat (via shoyu) should be assumed present in most cooked dishes and glazes. Some plain grilled or raw fish items may be naturally lower-risk, but nothing has been verified as celiac-safe — ask staff carefully about individual dishes and sauces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is oriented around daily fresh wild fish, homemade dried fish (自家製干物), and pure rice sake pairings; roughly 35 dishes are offered and fish dominates, with fish-based dashi assumed throughout. No vegan dishes or plant-based accommodations were found in available sources — not a suitable choice for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 観音裏 (Asakusa Kannonura)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2017 in the Kannonura backstreets behind Sensoji Temple, the shop quickly built a following among Tokyo food and sake enthusiasts and went on to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2017 in the Kannonura backstreets behind Sensoji Temple, the shop quickly built a following among Tokyo food and sake enthusiasts and went on to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "ずぶ六" is an old Edo-period term for someone who is thoroughly, happily drunk — the shop's aim is for the food and sake to be good enough that guests end up exactly that way.
chef_bio.specialty: Homemade dried fish (自家製干物) and daily fresh wild fish dishes paired with pure rice (junmai) sake served at room temperature or gently warmed.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, 'ずぶ六,' is an old term for someone completely drunk — reflecting the shop's goal of food and sake good enough to leave guests wonderfully so.
The owner commutes in from Saitama and personally selects the day's fish at Adachi Market before service, then prepares roughly 35 dishes a day including handmade dried fish.
The shop pours only junmai (pure rice) sake — about 10 served chilled and 15 at room temperature or warmed — in 90/120/150ml measures so guests can taste widely.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional counter-seat izakaya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service — best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese or using a translation app; reservations are recommended given the very limited seating.

places.name: 元祖油堂 宮益坂店 (Ganso Aburado Miyamasuzaka)
places.gf_detail: The dish is inherently wheat-based: the house noodle is a custom wheat noodle and the tare (sauce) is soy-sauce based, both containing gluten. The chain's own allergen chart lists wheat (小麦) as present in the noodle dish along with egg and dairy. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated gluten-free process is documented, so this is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The standard bowl uses a chicken/pork-style soy tare and toppings such as egg yolk and Parmesan-style cheese; no vegan or vegetarian broth, noodle, or topping option is documented on the official site or in reviews, so this is not a vegan-friendly choice as served.
places.neighborhood: 宮益坂 (Miyamasuzaka), Shibuya
places.notes: Part of a specialty abura-soba brand built around a custom house noodle and a secret sweet-savory soy tare finished with flavored oil. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Abura soba (oil noodle) specialty restaurant chain location
chef_bio.origin: Ganso Aburado is a Tokyo-born abura soba (oil noodle / brothless ramen) chain run by Gift Holdings, with branches across Shibuya, Ebisu, Shinjuku, Kitasenju, Tamachi, Kashiwa, Sendai and Yokohama; this Miyamasuzaka branch opened October 23, 2025 right by Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.background: Part of a specialty abura-soba brand built around a custom house noodle and a secret sweet-savory soy tare finished with flavored oil.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's stated approach is to let diners build their own bowl: a custom-developed wheat noodle for smooth texture and chew, paired with a rich soy-based tare and flavored oil, then finished at the table with a wide array of condiments so each customer can adjust the flavor to their own taste.
chef_bio.specialty: Abura soba (oil noodles) tossed table-side in the house tare and seasoned oil, topped with egg yolk and Parmesan-style cheese, with a large 225g noodle portion as the standard size.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger wrote that compared to other oil-soba shops this location isn't overly greasy, with a mild soy-sauce flavor that lets the richness of the egg yolk and Parmesan-style cheese come through, paired with thick, chewy noodles.
Another visitor noted the shop offers a large (225g) noodle portion, an 880-yen bowl with a drink bar included, and more than ten table-side flavor-changing condiments, popular with younger diners.
A local blogger described the Miyamasuzaka branch as having a stylish, women-friendly interior.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chain's official allergy information chart lists wheat (小麦), egg, and dairy as allergens present in the oil-noodle dish, consistent with the soy-based tare and standard wheat noodle used chain-wide.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain publishes a printed allergen chart per menu item, noting that sensitivity varies by individual and the chart is meant as a reference rather than a guarantee against reactions.

cultural_comfort.note: Casual counter-seat noodle shop with a picture-friendly menu and table condiments to self-customize the noodles; a little Japanese (or pointing) helps for ordering toppings and mix-in flavors, but it's an easy walk-in experience.

places.name: HATONOMORI 水道橋（マトン焼肉専門店 HATONOMORI～ハトノモリ～水道橋） (HATONOMORI Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: The menu centers on grilled fresh mutton and lamb served with a proprietary secret tare dipping sauce; no gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or allergen disclosure was found in any source. Japanese tare/dipping sauces are typically soy-sauce (shoyu) based and commonly contain wheat, and the tabletop grill is shared across all diners' orders, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Celiac diners should call ahead to ask specifically about the tare's ingredients and any shared cooking surfaces before dining.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated fresh-mutton-and-lamb grill restaurant built entirely around meat; no vegetarian or vegan dish, side, or menu accommodation was found in any source reviewed.
places.hours_raw: 月～土 ディナー 17:00～19:00（L.O.18:00、ドリンクL.O.18:30）。日曜日・祝日は予約による貸切・イベント対応のみ。
places.neighborhood: 水道橋（水道橋駅より徒歩3〜4分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in June 2020 in a renovated old Japanese house (kominka) near Suidobashi Station, HATONOMORI was launched after its founder happened to taste fresh, never-frozen mutton in Nagano Prefecture and wanted to bring that experience to Tokyo diners. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: founder/owner (name not disclosed in available sources)
chef_bio.origin: Iida City, Nagano Prefecture (source of the fresh mutton and the secret tare recipe)
chef_bio.background: Opened in June 2020 in a renovated old Japanese house (kominka) near Suidobashi Station, HATONOMORI was launched after its founder happened to taste fresh, never-frozen mutton in Nagano Prefecture and wanted to bring that experience to Tokyo diners.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop argues that mutton's reputation for a strong, unpleasant smell comes from frozen meat; fresh mutton shipped directly (never frozen) has a delicate flavor, soft texture, and natural sweetness that doesn't need to be masked with rice or heavy seasoning.
chef_bio.specialty: 生マトンジンギスカン — fresh (never-frozen) mutton and lamb grilled tableside on a jingisukan dome, dipped in a secret tare sauce that is a reproduction of a famous restaurant's recipe from Iida, Nagano.

anecdotes.text: The founder was captivated by mutton eaten by chance in Nagano and decided to open a Tokyo restaurant built entirely around fresh, never-frozen mutton shipped directly from Iida City, launching HATONOMORI in a renovated old folk house near Suidobashi Station in June 2020.
A reviewer described the lamb as tender and delicious eaten rare, though found the accompanying garlic-forward tare sauce strong enough to overshadow the meat's quality.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu, English signage, or English-speaking-staff mentions were found in any source, so expect a Japanese-language-only experience.

places.name: てっちゃん 新宿店 (Tetchan Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The menu centers on horumon (offal) skewer-stew, tare-glazed Genghis Khan lamb, and grilled beef tongue — dishes that typically rely on shoyu- and tare-based sauces (soy sauce commonly contains wheat) and shared grill/stew equipment, so cross-contact risk should be assumed high and celiac diners should not treat this as a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. The restaurant's entire identity and signature dishes are built around animal offal, beef tongue, and lamb, with no evidence of a vegetable-forward alternative; assume this is not workable for a vegan diner without prior direct arrangement with the restaurant.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome), Shinjuku
places.notes: Tetchan Shinjuku is a Showa-retro-styled meat izakaya (肉酒場) near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, built around carefully prepped horumon (beef/pork offal), grilled beef tongue and Genghis Khan-style grilled lamb, served in a nostalgic, unpretentious drinking-den atmosphere. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Tetchan Shinjuku is a Showa-retro-styled meat izakaya (肉酒場) near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, built around carefully prepped horumon (beef/pork offal), grilled beef tongue and Genghis Khan-style grilled lamb, served in a nostalgic, unpretentious drinking-den atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes thorough, careful preparation of offal to draw out its natural flavor, paired with a deliberately old-fashioned, low-cost izakaya feel so regulars can linger over cheap drinks in a retro Showa-era setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh horumon (offal) that is carefully cleaned and prepped before slow simmering into kushi-nikomi (skewer stew); steam-grilled Genghis Khan lamb cooked with beer; pepper-crusted grilled beef tongue.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers a 2-hour all-you-can-drink plan for ¥777 and glasses of alcohol priced under ¥200 regardless of how many are ordered, positioned as cheaper than a convenience store and one of the lowest prices in the area.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-language service was confirmed in any source, so this reads as a standard Japanese-language neighborhood izakaya best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese or by pointing at photos.

places.name: ビストロ デ マニラ (Bistro de Manila)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce (tamari), or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source. The signature dish, adobo, is braised in a soy-sauce-and-vinegar sauce (soy sauce contains wheat), and other dishes (e.g., stir-fried bihon noodles) are prepared in a shared, small (19-seat) kitchen with no indication of allergen segregation. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, detailed confirmation from staff in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu was found. Filipino home-style cooking typically relies on meat, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste; the one plant-forward item found in reviews (bihon noodles with stir-fried vegetables, ~1,050 yen) is not confirmed to be free of fish/oyster sauce or animal stock. Vegan diners would need to ask staff directly in Japanese and should not assume any dish is vegan by default.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金・土 18:00〜27:00（L.O.）、日曜定休・不定休あり（新型コロナ等の影響で変更の可能性あり、来店前に要確認）
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草（つくばエクスプレス浅草駅周辺）
places.cuisine: フィリピン料理・洋食
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] フィリピン出身のシェフが浅草3丁目交差点近くで営む、東京では珍しいフィリピン料理専門の洋食店。深夜から早朝まで営業しており、遅い時間の食事を求める客に本場の味を提供している。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: シェフ（フィリピン出身）
chef_bio.origin: フィリピン
chef_bio.background: フィリピン出身のシェフが浅草3丁目交差点近くで営む、東京では珍しいフィリピン料理専門の洋食店。 深夜から早朝まで営業しており、遅い時間の食事を求める客に本場の味を提供している。
chef_bio.specialty: アドボ（豚肉または鶏肉を酢と醤油ベースのたれで甘酸っぱく煮込んだフィリピンの国民食）

anecdotes.text: アドボは酸っぱさが控えめに調整されており、日本人客にも食べやすい味わいで、肉が非常に柔らかいと評判。
深夜から早朝まで営業する珍しい業態で、深夜料金のメニュー設定がある。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found on dedicated gluten-free preparation, allergen labeling, or cross-contamination controls; kitchen is small (19-seat restaurant) and shared for all dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature adobo dish is braised in a soy-sauce-and-vinegar sauce; standard Japanese/Filipino soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free (tamari) alternative was mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: Small, independent, cash-only late-night spot with no confirmed English menu or website beyond a Facebook page — phone reservations and ordering are likely Japanese-only, so bring basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: TENCUPS（テンカップス） (TENCUPS)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, rice-flour options, or allergy accommodations are mentioned in any source found. The menu centers on hand-made bakery items (croissants, cookies, cake) which are typically wheat-based, and the evening Asian fusion dishes may use soy sauce or other wheat-containing condiments. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not assume safety here without direct confirmation from staff, and no dedicated gluten-free preparation area or fryer was identified.
places.vegan_detail: A vegan banana cake is confirmed as a standing menu item, showing some plant-based options exist, but there is no evidence of a broader vegan menu or systematic labeling. The evening Asian fusion dishes and cocktail-lounge menu likely include animal-derived ingredients (dashi, fish sauce, honey, dairy) that are not called out as avoidable, so vegan diners should ask about individual dishes beyond the known vegan items.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya Shin-Minamiguchi (新南口), Shibuya
places.cuisine: Café / Asian-inspired fusion cuisine and coc
places.notes: TENCUPS opened on the ground floor of the Tokyu Stay Shibuya Shin-Minamiguchi hotel as a café-by-day, cocktail-lounge-by-night concept. Its name is drawn from the tarot card "Ten of Cups," which symbolizes harmony and connection between people. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: TENCUPS opened on the ground floor of the Tokyu Stay Shibuya Shin-Minamiguchi hotel as a café-by-day, cocktail-lounge-by-night concept. Its name is drawn from the tarot card "Ten of Cups," which symbolizes harmony and connection between people.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the "Ten of Cups" idea of nurturing connection — through loved ones, music, and food that engages the senses — the space is meant to be a gathering point for people to share culture and build relationships, not just a place to eat.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made bakery items and café drinks (including a vegan banana cake and pandan latte) by day; Asian fusion small plates and natural-wine cocktails as a music lounge from evening.

anecdotes.text: TENCUPS takes its name and concept from the tarot card "Ten of Cups," aiming to be a place where food, music, and human connection intertwine across all times of day.
The café offers hand-made bakery items and original café drinks from morning, transitioning into a music lounge with cocktails and Asian fusion cuisine from 6pm, with DJ and live events at night.

positives.text: A vegan banana cake is listed as a standing menu item alongside items like pandan latte, indicating at least some intentional plant-based options are offered rather than being incidental.

cultural_comfort.note: Set inside an international hotel (Tokyu Stay) near Shibuya Station, so basic English is likely manageable, but detailed allergy conversations should be confirmed in simple Japanese or via translation app.

places.name: まぜそば七 (Mazesoba Shichi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options were found. The noodles are standard wheat chuka-men and the signature tare is a shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) base aged about a week; no dedicated gluten-free noodle, sauce substitution, or allergen protocol is mentioned in any source. Not suitable for celiac diners without further direct confirmation from the shop, which is unlikely given the single-recipe, counter-only format.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu options were found. The shop's signature dish is topped with charcoal-grilled pork chashu, and the aromatic oil and tare are built around meat/umami-forward flavor with no indication of a plant-based broth or hidden-dashi-free option.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-23:30, no fixed closed days (定休日なし)
places.neighborhood: Sakuragaoka-cho, Shibuya (2-3 min walk from Shibuya Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is a continuation of "油そば 大黒屋" (Abura Soba Daikokuya), which originally operated under the JR train tracks near Shibuya Station. When that spot had to relocate due to the Shibuya Station redevelopment, the owners reopened nearby in Sakuragaoka-cho as まぜそば七, carrying the  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (formerly a guardrail/underpass stall near Shibuya Station)
chef_bio.background: The shop is a continuation of "油そば 大黒屋" (Abura Soba Daikokuya), which originally operated under the JR train tracks near Shibuya Station. When that spot had to relocate due to the Shibuya Station redevelopment, the owners reopened nearby in Sakuragaoka-cho as まぜそば七, carrying the original recipe forward under a new name and sign marked with the character 七 (seven).
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is built around balancing three housemade elements: an aromatic oil made from garlic and ten kinds of aromatic vegetables, a shoyu-based tare aged for about a week, and a small amount of soup stirred together with the noodles. Their housemade rayu (chili oil) uses seven kinds of spices, echoing the shop's name.
chef_bio.specialty: Abura-soba / mazesoba (oil-based mixed noodles) topped with charcoal-grilled chashu pork

anecdotes.text: ガード下の【油そば 大黒屋】が移転し、現在の『七』が目印の【まぜそば七】に生まれ変わりました。もともとは渋谷駅ガード下にて営業しており、移転して『まぜそば七』としてオープンした。
自家製香味油に厳選した唐辛子など七種類のスパイスを加えて作った自家製のラー油を使用。にんにくや10種類の香味野菜からつくった自家製香味油・1週間寝かせて作った醤油ダレ・少量のスープのバランスにこだわった油そばが特徴。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is a shoyu (soy sauce)-based sauce aged for about a week; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari or GF alternative is mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local counter-only noodle shop near Shibuya Station; expect a Japanese-language menu and a fast-paced, order-at-counter local ramen-shop atmosphere rather than English-friendly tourist service.

places.name: 鶏そば かぐら屋 (Torisoba Kaguraya (Suidōbashi Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations found. The shop serves standard wheat ramen noodles, and its signature broths are seasoned with shoyu (soy sauce, e.g. a flying-fish/tobiuo shoyu tare) which in Japan is almost always wheat-based unless specified otherwise. No mention of gluten-free noodles, tamari, or an allergy protocol was found in any source; not suitable for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option found. Every headline menu item (Assari Torisoba, Nōjuku Torisoba, tobiuo-shoyu chicken soba, tomato-salt chicken soba, tsukemen) is built on a chicken-bone (paitan) broth, so there is no plant-based broth alternative described anywhere in the sources checked.
places.hours_raw: Reported as 11:00-22:30 daily by one aggregated source describing the main shop; another source lists 11:00-23:00 weekdays/Saturday and 11:00-23:00 Sunday for what appears to be the same shop at a prior address. No closed days (無休) reported by any source.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 / Suidōbashi
places.notes: The Suidōbashi shop is the original flagship of Torisoba Kaguraya, which opened in the early 2010s near JR/Toei Suidōbashi Station and later moved a short distance to its current building (near Exit A6) around 2019. It has since grown into a small tori-paitan (chicken white broth · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The Suidōbashi shop is the original flagship of Torisoba Kaguraya, which opened in the early 2010s near JR/Toei Suidōbashi Station and later moved a short distance to its current building (near Exit A6) around 2019. It has since grown into a small tori-paitan (chicken white broth) ramen specialist group with several branches around Tokyo (including Shinjuku and Uguisudani).
chef_bio.philosophy: Long, careful simmering of chicken and vegetables to extract maximum umami and collagen into a silky, not-too-heavy paitan broth, offered alongside lighter and flavor-variant versions so diners can choose their preferred richness.
chef_bio.specialty: Nōjuku Torisoba (濃熟鶏そば) — a rich, collagen-laden golden chicken paitan broth simmered slowly with chicken bones and vegetables; the menu also includes a lighter clear-broth Assari Torisoba, a flying-fish (tobiuo) shoyu chicken soba, a tomato-salt chicken soba, and tsukemen dipping noodles.

anecdotes.text: The Suidōbashi shop is described as the honten (main/original shop) of the Kaguraya group, situated about a 1-3 minute walk from Suidōbashi Station's A6 exit, with the group's history page noting its growth from this single location into a multi-branch chain.
A food blogger described the Nōjuku Torisoba as a 'silky, deeply matured chicken soba' prized for its umami-rich collagen broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard Japanese neighborhood ramen shop near a busy station exit; expect a Japanese-language menu and typical counter-ramen ordering etiquette, with no confirmed English support.

places.name: 麺堂にしき 新宿歌舞伎町店 (Mendo Nishiki Shinjuku Kabukicho)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop: noodles are fine straight low-hydration wheat noodles from Mikawaya Seimen, and the tare is a duck-dashi shoyu (soy sauce), which conventionally contains wheat. No source mentions a gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free menu, or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is built on duck (鴨) and seafood/fish (魚介) dashi, and chashu (pork) is a standard topping — the ramen is not vegan and no plant-based/vegan menu variant was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 18:00〜翌13:00、年中無休（食べログ等、直近の複数情報源より）。開店当初（2021年）の一部情報源には11:00-15:00/17:00-23:30・火曜定休の記載もあるが、現行の情報とは一致せず古い可能性が高い。
places.neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho), near Seibu-Shinjuku Station
places.notes: 麺堂にしき is a small duck-broth ramen shop chain that began at a Kanda-Ogawamachi location before opening this Kabukicho branch as its second shop (2号店) on March 17, 2021. In spring 2025 the Kabukicho shop's lease ended and the team temporarily relocated operations to a new address o · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanda-Ogawamachi (original shop), expanded to Shinjuku Kabukicho in 2021
chef_bio.background: 麺堂にしき is a small duck-broth ramen shop chain that began at a Kanda-Ogawamachi location before opening this Kabukicho branch as its second shop (2号店) on March 17, 2021. In spring 2025 the Kabukicho shop's lease ended and the team temporarily relocated operations to a new address on Shokuan-dori (Hyakunincho), opening a sister store there in April 2025.
