The Kabukicho location itself was revived and reopened at the same Kabukicho address on August 27, 2025, and has been operating there since, with customer reviews confirmed as recently as January 2026.
chef_bio.specialty: 鴨だし醤油ラーメン (duck dashi shoyu ramen) — a soy-sauce ramen built on a broth that draws out the umami of duck and seafood, paired with distinctive toppings; the shop uses fine, straight, low-hydration noodles from Mikawaya Seimen for a chewy bite, along with thick-sliced chashu. A 山椒ラーメン (Sichuan pepper) variation is also offered.

anecdotes.text: When the original Kabukicho shop closed in spring 2025 after its lease ended, the owner posted on the shop's X/Twitter account: "材料が無くなったので営業終了させていただきました…来てくださった沢山のお客様のおかげでこの場所で4年続けることが出来ました 本当にありがとうございます" ("We've closed because we ran out of ingredients... thanks to the many customers who came, we were able to keep this location going for 4 years. Thank you truly.") — before relaunching nearby.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is a duck-dashi shoyu (soy sauce) tare; conventional Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-speaking-staff signage was found in any source, so expect a Japanese-only ordering experience typical of a small Kabukicho ramen counter.

places.name: 浅草うなな (Asakusa Unana)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional kabayaki-style eel shop: the eel rice balls, liver skewers, and rice bowls are all basted with a soy-sauce based sweet glaze (tare) while grilling. No gluten-free or wheat-free tare, dedicated allergy menu, or allergy accommodation was mentioned in any source found. Assume wheat exposure through the glaze and confirm directly with staff before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated eel (unagi) specialty shop — every dish centers on grilled eel (rice balls, liver skewers, kabayaki, and unaju rice bowls). There are no vegan or vegetarian menu items.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri approx. 9:30–17:30, Sat/Sun/Holidays approx. 9:30–18:20; closed Wednesdays. (Some sources list slightly different times, e.g. 10:00–17:00 weekdays / 10:00–18:15 weekends — treat as approximate and expect early sell-out closures.)
places.neighborhood: Asakusa, near Hanayashiki amusement park
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded by owner Nishibori-san as a takeout-focused specialty shop; the shop is credited as the originator of Asakusa's now-popular charcoal-grilled eel rice ball (unagi yaki-onigiri). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Founded by owner Nishibori-san as a takeout-focused specialty shop; the shop is credited as the originator of Asakusa's now-popular charcoal-grilled eel rice ball (unagi yaki-onigiri).
chef_bio.philosophy: Serve quality domestic eel (mainly from Miyazaki) at prices well below typical unagi specialty restaurants, so more people can enjoy it casually while walking around Asakusa.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled domestic eel rice ball (国産鰻の焼きおにぎり)

anecdotes.text: The shop's founder, Nishibori-san, came up with the idea of an eel-topped grilled rice ball so people could enjoy domestic eel more casually; he chose the Asakusa location for its warm, friendly shitamachi (old-downtown) character and the crowds that gather there.
The menu was developed together with a veteran eel craftsman, using domestic eel (mainly from Miyazaki) and Tochigi-grown Koshihikari rice, carefully grilled over high-heat binchotan charcoal.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is in Japanese, sometimes via a numbered ticket system when queues form, with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — a translation app or pointing at photos will help.

places.name: With Green 道玄坂通店 (WithGreen Shibuya Dogenzaka-dori)
places.gf_detail: Bowls are vegetable-forward and customizable (choose base, toppings, protein, dressing), so a wheat-free combination is plausible, but the chain's own allergen chart lists wheat, soy, and apple as allergens found in the finishing sauces/dressings poured over the salads (後かけのタレ), and the chain notes possible cross-contamination and advises asking staff about honey/allergen content per item. No dedicated gluten-free prep line or fryer was confirmed. Celiac diners should ask staff which specific dressings are wheat-free before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetable-only bowl can likely be assembled by skipping the default meat/gizzard/chicken topping and choosing a plant-based dressing (a lemon dressing is marked vegetarian/vegan-compatible on the chain's menu at some branches), but standard bowls default to animal protein, and dashi/animal-stock use in dressings is not fully confirmed. Ask staff to confirm a specific bowl is fully vegan before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-21:00 daily (eat-in last order 20:30)
places.neighborhood: Shibuya Dogenzaka (渋谷道玄坂通), near Shinsen Station
places.cuisine: Salad bowls, healthy lunch sets, build-your-
places.notes: Part of the WithGreen salad bowl chain, founded in Tokyo in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi (武文智洋) and grown to roughly 54 locations nationwide by its 10th anniversary in 2026. This Dogenzaka-dori shop opened on August 24, 2023 as the chain's 22nd store overall and its 3rd in the S · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Fast-casual, made-to-order salad bowl counter (order at register, eat-in or takeout)
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain founded 2016)
chef_bio.background: Part of the WithGreen salad bowl chain, founded in Tokyo in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi (武文智洋) and grown to roughly 54 locations nationwide by its 10th anniversary in 2026. This Dogenzaka-dori shop opened on August 24, 2023 as the chain's 22nd store overall and its 3rd in the Shibuya area, occupying the 1st floor of the Dogenzaka-dori mixed-use building about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station's A2 exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the slogan '一杯で主食になるサラダ' (a salad that becomes your whole meal in one bowl) - domestically grown Japanese vegetables (used exclusively except in cases like typhoon-related shortages) are combined with a customer's choice of protein and dressing so a salad can stand in as a full lunch rather than a side dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Gizzard salad (砂肝サラダ) - the chain's best-selling bowl, alongside build-your-own salad bowls with a choice of over 10 dressings

anecdotes.text: WithGreen's concept is a salad that becomes your main meal in one bowl, founded in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi and grown to roughly 54 locations nationwide by its 10th anniversary in 2026.

The Dogenzaka-dori branch opened August 24, 2023 as the chain's 22nd store and its 3rd in the Shibuya area, on the 1st floor of the Dogenzaka-dori building about 2 minutes from Shibuya Station's A2 exit.
The chain's signature gizzard salad (砂肝サラダ) has sold over 1 million bowls company-wide since launch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The chain's allergen chart flags wheat, soy, and apple as allergens present in the finishing sauces/dressings, and notes possible cross-contamination between menu items; customers are advised to check with in-store staff for specifics.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several of the chain's dressings are soy/wheat-based finishing sauces (後かけのタレ) that are called out specifically as containing wheat and soy allergens.

vegan_cross_contact.text: At least one dressing (a lemon-based dressing) is labeled vegetarian/vegan-compatible on the chain's menu at other WithGreen branches (Kagurazaka, Aoyama); it is not confirmed whether this exact labeling is posted at the Dogenzaka-dori branch.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain directs customers with allergen concerns, including questions about honey use and potential cross-contamination, to ask in-store staff directly rather than relying solely on printed charts.

positives.text: Salads are built to order from a wide choice of over 10 dressings and toppings, using domestically grown Japanese vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is counter-service with a build-your-own format (pick base, toppings, dressing) shown on Japanese menu boards; basic Japanese or pointing at photos will get you through, no confirmed English menu.

places.name: 食堂新 (Shokudo Arata)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodations were found in any source. The signature mackerel stew is simmered in a tamari-based soy sauce (たまり醤油) rather than standard koikuchi shoyu, but this has not been confirmed wheat-free and is not marketed as a GF measure. The menu also includes breaded, deep-fried items (aji fry / fried horse mackerel), which virtually guarantees wheat exposure and shared-fryer cross-contact. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian offerings were found — the menu centers entirely on mackerel, horse mackerel, chicken, and other seafood dishes, with dashi/fish-based sauces used throughout (e.g., the tamari-based simmering liquid for the signature dish). Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00〜14:30 (L.O.) ／ 土・日・祝 定休 (Hot Pepper's listing separately shows 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-22:00 with Saturdays sometimes open — unreconciled, see caveats)
places.neighborhood: Jimbocho / Kanda-Sarugakucho (神田猿楽町), Chiyoda-ku — near Suid
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hirano-san started out running a mobile kitchen-car (food truck) specializing in mackerel dishes, drawn to the close, direct customer interaction that street food allows. He decided to go independent during the COVID-19 pandemic, saying he was partly inspired to start his own bus · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Hirano-san started out running a mobile kitchen-car (food truck) specializing in mackerel dishes, drawn to the close, direct customer interaction that street food allows. He decided to go independent during the COVID-19 pandemic, saying he was partly inspired to start his own business after watching the Korean drama "Itaewon Class" (梨泰院クラス). In July 2023 he opened a fixed shop in Jimbocho/Kanda-Sarugakucho.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carried over from his food-truck days: keep the counter small and the connection with customers close, building the menu around a slow-cooked signature dish rather than a large fixed menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Saba no kuro-ni (鯖の黒煮) — mackerel simmered for two days in a tamari-based sauce until the bones soften enough to eat whole.

anecdotes.text: When the shop first opened it sold only about 35 meals a day; business picked up after Hirano-san began offering a daily fried-horse-mackerel (aji fry) special alongside the mackerel stew. The menu has since grown to roughly 15 items centered on chicken and seafood.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature saba kuro-ni is simmered for two days in a tamari-based soy sauce (たまり醤油) rather than standard shoyu; this has not been confirmed as wheat-free by the restaurant and should not be assumed safe for celiac diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, fast-paced counter-seat neighborhood shokudo near Jimbocho Station catering mostly to local office workers and students; expect a Japanese-only menu and no particular English support.

places.name: 十味や (Toomiya (Tomiya))
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop where the noodles themselves are the core product; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific menu was found in any source. Miso and soy-based seasonings likely contain wheat. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu items or accommodations were identified in any source. As a traditional miso ramen shop, the broth and standard toppings (pork chashu, soft-boiled egg, stir-fried vegetables) are animal-based, and dashi bases typical of this style commonly include bonito. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–22:00, daily including holidays (無休)

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1971 under the original name "Ishikarigawa Ramen," one of the early Tokyo shops to introduce Hokkaido-style miso ramen to the city. The shop was rebranded to its current name, 十味や, in 2002. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 1971 under the original name "Ishikarigawa Ramen," one of the early Tokyo shops to introduce Hokkaido-style miso ramen to the city. The shop was rebranded to its current name, 十味や, in 2002.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "十味" ("ten flavors") comes from an original ten-ingredient spice blend the shop developed itself — including chili, white sesame, Sichuan pepper, garlic, ginger, and dried shrimp — used to season its miso ramen and give it a distinctive, layered flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: 濃厚合わせ味噌ラーメン (rich combined-miso ramen), alongside white miso, red miso, and spicy miso variations, served with house-made wavy noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name '十味' comes from a self-developed ten-ingredient spice blend (chili, white sesame, Sichuan pepper, garlic, ginger, dried shrimp, and others) used to season the ramen.
A local ramen writer described it as a miso ramen shop 'loved for 53 years' that regulars can eat every day, noting the free large-noodle portions and brisk counter service.
A food writer's essay on the shop is titled 'Miso ramen you can eat every day / Shinjuku west exit's Tomiya,' describing it as a neighborhood staple near Shinjuku's west exit.

cultural_comfort.note: Orders are placed at a Japanese-only ticket vending machine and paid in cash, with no English menu identified, though the simple ramen-counter format is easy to follow even without fluency.

places.name: セウフ (C'est ouf)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation is mentioned in any source found. As a French bistro, dishes likely involve wheat-based roux, breading, or bread service by default; no information exists on soy sauce ingredients, cross-contamination handling, or celiac-level precautions. Ask staff directly before ordering; do not assume any dish is safe without confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or explicit vegan accommodation is mentioned in any source found. As a butter/cream/wine-forward French bistro, standard preparations likely include animal-based stock, butter, and cream by default; nothing was found about hidden dashi/bonito (less relevant for French cuisine, but also unconfirmed either way). Vegan diners should ask staff directly what can be modified.
places.hours_raw: 15:00–23:00 daily, food last order 21:30 (per Tabelog search listing); irregular closures announced on Instagram
places.neighborhood: Shibuya, Sakuragaoka-cho (near Shinsen Station, behind Cerul
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Tomohiro Kumagai trained for about ten years at Towers Restaurant Cucagne inside the Cerulean Tower Tokyu Hotel in Shibuya, working his way up from banquet cooking to the hotel's signature French dining room 150 meters above the street. In 2021 he won the Grand Prize at the  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Chef Tomohiro Kumagai trained for about ten years at Towers Restaurant Cucagne inside the Cerulean Tower Tokyu Hotel in Shibuya, working his way up from banquet cooking to the hotel's signature French dining room 150 meters above the street. In 2021 he won the Grand Prize at the Rougié Recipe Competition and placed second at the APGF U-29 French Cuisine Competition, and in 2022 he was selected as one of 16 rising 'focus chefs' for Diner's Club France Restaurant Week, Japan's largest French food event. He opened C'est ouf in June 2025 together with owners Kenta and Kota Sanda, the brothers behind the Shibuya izakaya Otonage and the shochu bar Achoo, in the same basement as their daytime cafe Cherry Pick Hills.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing hotel-trained French technique down to street level: instead of a fixed tasting course, Kumagai serves his cooking as a la carte small plates at a standing counter built around an open kitchen, paired with natural wine, so guests can drop in without a reservation and eat and drink as much or as little as they like.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, C'est ouf, is French slang for 'amazing!' or 'crazy!' — a nod to the surprise of finding serious hotel-caliber French cooking inside an unpretentious, reservation-free standing bar.

C'est ouf opened on June 29, 2025 in the basement of the same Sakuragaoka-cho building as Cherry Pick Hills, the daytime cafe also run by the Sanda brothers, who are known in Shibuya for the izakaya Otonage and shochu bar Achoo.

Kumagai spent roughly ten years cooking at Towers Restaurant Cucagne in the Cerulean Tower Tokyu Hotel before going independent, and has placed in national French-cuisine competitions including the Rougié Recipe Competition and APGF U-29.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu, English-speaking staff, or English website was found; the no-reservation standing-bar format is straightforward to walk into, but ordering and any dietary conversation will likely require Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: 曾さんの餃子 (Sou-san no Gyoza (Suidobashi))
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is pan-fried gyoza in a wheat wrapper, and Chinese-style seasoning (wheat-based soy sauce, likely shared fry surfaces for fried items like the gyoza and oil-poached chicken) is standard across the menu. No gluten-free wrapper, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu was mentioned in any source checked. This is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu was found in any source. The menu is meat-centric (pork gyoza, mapo tofu with ground meat, lu rou fan/braised pork rice, oil-poached chicken), and even the plainer-looking side dishes (cold tomato, salt-dressed cabbage) are not confirmed to be free of animal-derived seasoning (Chinese kitchens commonly use chicken powder/stock or oyster sauce even in vegetable sides). Not recommended for vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri & day before holidays: 11:00-15:30, 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:30, drink L.O. 21:30). Sat, Sun & holidays: 11:00-16:59, 17:00-20:00 (food L.O. 19:00, drink L.O. 20:00).
places.neighborhood: 西神田 (Nishi-Kanda), near Suidobashi / Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Before striking out on their own, the couple worked the lunch shift at Gyopao's Roppongi crossing flagship. Wanting to take on the challenge of running their own restaurant at least once in their lives, and believing the Gyopao-style dumpling deserved a wider audience, they inves · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: ザーチイ夫妻 (Za Chi couple)
chef_bio.origin: Myanmar-born couple; independently opened this shop in September 2018 as a sister branch of the 35+-year Roppongi dumpling institution "肉汁水餃子 餃包" (Gyopao), where they had previously worked lunch service.
chef_bio.background: Before striking out on their own, the couple worked the lunch shift at Gyopao's Roppongi crossing flagship. Wanting to take on the challenge of running their own restaurant at least once in their lives, and believing the Gyopao-style dumpling deserved a wider audience, they invested roughly 8 million yen of their own money to open the Suidobashi shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: To bring the Gyopao dumpling tradition beyond Roppongi and prove, through their own shop, that an immigrant-run neighborhood restaurant could succeed on its own terms.
chef_bio.specialty: Juicy pan-fried gyoza (焼き餃子) made with 100% Kagoshima black pork, alongside home-style Chinese dishes like lu rou fan (braised pork rice), oil-poached chicken, and mapo tofu.

anecdotes.text: Sales rose steadily from the September 2018 opening through November, dipped in December, then recovered from January 2019 and hit their original target by April 2019 -- recouping the roughly 8 million yen initial investment in about a year.

cultural_comfort.note: A local, Japanese-language neighborhood restaurant with online booking via major Japanese platforms (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gurunavi); no confirmed English menu was found, though it does appear on Tabelog's English-language listing.

places.name: 茶庭 然花抄院 渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店 (Chatei Zen Kashoin Shibuya Hikarie ShinQs)
places.gf_detail: The signature "然" castella contains wheat flour, egg, and milk and is baked fresh in-house daily; no gluten-free menu items, substitutions, or allergy protocols were found in any source. Plain matcha usucha is naturally wheat-free, but no dedicated gluten-free preparation area was confirmed and cross-contact risk in the shared confectionery kitchen is unaddressed — not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature castella and most confections/parfaits contain egg and/or dairy (milk, cream); no dedicated vegan menu, ingredient substitutions, or plant-based labeling were found. A plain bowl of matcha (usucha) alone would likely be vegan, but this was not confirmed as an advertised standalone option.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya) — 渋谷ヒカリエ5F、渋谷駅直結
places.cuisine: 甘味処・日本茶カフェ
places.notes: 然花抄院（Zen Kashoin）は、大正創業のカステラ専門店・長崎堂が2009年に立ち上げた京都発の菓子・茶ブランド。京都室町本店は築300年の京町家を改装した店舗で、渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店は2012年4月オープンのブランド初の東京店。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 京都・室町 (Kyoto Muromachi)
chef_bio.background: 然花抄院（Zen Kashoin）は、大正創業のカステラ専門店・長崎堂が2009年に立ち上げた京都発の菓子・茶ブランド。 京都室町本店は築300年の京町家を改装した店舗で、渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店は2012年4月オープンのブランド初の東京店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「花を愛でる心でお客様をもてなす」という京都本店由来のおもてなしの精神を、渋谷の茶庭でも継承している。
chef_bio.specialty: 『然』カステラ ― 京都で丹波黒豆を食べて育った鶏の卵黄のみを使い、紙焼きで毎日店内工房から届けられる出来立てのカステラ

anecdotes.text: 渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店は2012年4月にオープンした、然花抄院ブランド初の東京出店で、京都の風情を楽しめる茶庭として展開している。
看板商品の『然』カステラは卵黄のみを使用し、卵・砂糖・小麦粉・牛乳・水飴で作られる紙焼きカステラ。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature castella and most confections are made with wheat flour in the same shop kitchen used for all items; no dedicated gluten-free prep area or allergy protocol was found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Castella is made with egg yolks and milk; parfait and kakigori menu items include dairy toppings. No vegan-specific menu, ingredient substitution, or preparation process was found.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a major Shibuya department store (ShinQs at Hikarie) directly connected to Shibuya Station, with an English-language Tabelog listing and heavy tourist foot traffic.

places.name: レストラン はやしや (Hayashiya (Restaurant Hayashiya))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free options were found in any source. The menu is built around wheat-based dishes: omurice (rice wrapped in egg, but sauces built on wheat roux/demi-glace), breaded pork cutlets (wheat flour and panko breading), and soy-sauce-based preparations. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu items were identified. The restaurant's specialties (omurice, katsu, hamburger steak, beef stew, offal stew) are all egg- and/or meat-based, and sauces likely rely on meat stock (demi-glace). Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sun, holidays, and pre-holidays: 10:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:15)
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku (Shinjuku San-chome / east exit of Shinjuku Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hayashiya is a long-running yoshoku (Japanese-Western) diner in a Shinjuku Station-adjacent building, preserving the look and menu style of a Showa-era department store dining hall, with counter seats overlooking the Kabukicho streets below. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1949 (Showa 24) by Kobayashi Heizo, whose family shop name was 'Hayashiya.' He later reversed the characters of his given name Heizo (平三) to create 'Sanpei,' the name behind the restaurant's signature Sanpei Rice.
chef_bio.background: Hayashiya is a long-running yoshoku (Japanese-Western) diner in a Shinjuku Station-adjacent building, preserving the look and menu style of a Showa-era department store dining hall, with counter seats overlooking the Kabukicho streets below.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeps a full roster of old-fashioned yoshoku dishes -- omurice, beef stew, hamburger steak, and even offal stew rarely found at Western-style restaurants -- alive in something close to their original postwar form.
chef_bio.specialty: Omurice (omelette rice) and the revived 'Sanpei Rice' -- ketchup rice topped with a breaded pork cutlet folded in egg and finished with demi-glace sauce.

anecdotes.text: Because the founder's name (Heizo, reversed to 'Sanpei') coincidentally matched that of the famous rakugo comedian Hayashiya Shohei (林家三平), the restaurant asked him to appear in a commercial with the catchphrase 'cheap, delicious, so sorry about that' -- and he became a regular customer afterward.
The signature Sanpei Rice -- katsu folded in egg over ketchup rice with demi-glace -- was revived on the menu after a 45-year absence.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu is centered on wheat-flour-based demi-glace sauces and panko-breaded cutlets; no wheat-free soy sauce or GF sauce alternative was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or English-language official presence was found, so pointing at photos of dishes (omurice, Sanpei Rice) may help non-Japanese-speaking visitors order.

places.name: 天ぷら福岡 (Tempura Fukuoka)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional tempura counter; no source mentions a gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce. Standard tempura batter is wheat-flour based and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is conventionally made with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce and bonito dashi. Not verified safe for celiac diners — no gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish- and shellfish-forward tempura restaurant; menus include eel and other seafood tempura alongside vegetables, and the frying oil/dashi appear shared across all items. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or dedicated vegan fryer is documented by any source found.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa 2 (Kappabashi area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner spent 33 years cooking at a Japanese restaurant in New York before returning to Japan and opening this small tempura counter in the Kappabashi tool-town district of Nishi-Asakusa in March 2011 — the same month as the Great East Japan Earthquake, a dramatic start for the · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: wife and three daughters help run the counter
chef_bio.origin: New York, USA (owner-chef ran a Japanese restaurant there for 33 years before returning to Japan)
chef_bio.background: The owner spent 33 years cooking at a Japanese restaurant in New York before returning to Japan and opening this small tempura counter in the Kappabashi tool-town district of Nishi-Asakusa in March 2011 — the same month as the Great East Japan Earthquake, a dramatic start for the new shop. He and his wife, together with their three daughters, have run the counter as a family ever since.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built around taihaku gomayu — a refined, unroasted sesame oil the owner wanted to fry with in New York but couldn't justify importing from Japan at the time; opening the shop in Asakusa finally let him cook with the oil the way he'd always intended.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable and fish tempura fried in taihaku gomayu (太白胡麻油), unroasted white sesame oil

anecdotes.text: 天ぷら福岡（From New York）は、ニューヨークで33年間日本食レストランで腕を磨いた店主が、2011年3月に浅草かっぱ橋に開店した天ぷら専門店です。開店予定のその時に東日本大震災が日本を襲い、それからはご夫婦と娘さん3人で力を合わせて営業を続けてきました。
太白胡麻油を使用しており、ニューヨーク時代は日本からわざわざ持っていかなければならずコスト的に合わなかったため、浅草に来てようやく使用できるようになった。
英語が堪能なご夫婦であることから、店内には外国人客も多く訪れており、ザンビア、ドイツ、オーストラリアなどからのお客様も来店している。
夜のコースはジャズミュージシャンにちなんだボブコース、コルトレインコース、マイルスコースの3種類があり、落ち着いたジャズが流れる天ぷら屋として知られる。

positives.text: The English-speaking owner and his wife ran a Japanese restaurant in New York for 33 years and are experienced at hosting international guests, including visitors from Zambia, Germany, and Australia.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner and his wife are fluent English speakers who ran a restaurant in New York for over 30 years and now regularly host international guests from around the world.

places.name: 渋谷 和牛焼肉 USHIHACHI 極 (Shibuya Wagyu Yakiniku USHIHACHI Kiwami)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free tare (dipping sauce), or celiac protocol was found in any source. As a tabletop-grill yakiniku restaurant, plain salt-grilled cuts of meat and some vegetables could plausibly avoid wheat if ordered without the standard soy-sauce-based tare, but this has not been confirmed by the restaurant. The shared grill and shared kitchen mean cross-contact with wheat-containing sauces is likely; celiac diners should call ahead and clarify carefully rather than assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: This is a wagyu-focused yakiniku restaurant; no vegan menu, plant-based tare, or vegan protocol was found in any source. Some grilled vegetable side dishes likely exist, but they would share the grill with meat and sauces may contain dashi/animal-based stock. Not a reliable option for vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–15:30, 17:00–23:00 (last order for food and drink 22:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00–23:00 (last order 22:30)
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya
places.notes: USHIHACHI 極 (Kiwami) is the premium-line Shibuya branch of the USHIHACHI wagyu yakiniku brand (operated under GYRO HOLDINGS, which also runs a sister branch in Ueno). The restaurant buys whole, carefully selected kuroge (black-haired) Japanese wagyu cattle rather than pre-cut who · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Yakiniku restaurant (part of the USHIHACHI/GYRO HOLDINGS group)
chef_bio.background: USHIHACHI 極 (Kiwami) is the premium-line Shibuya branch of the USHIHACHI wagyu yakiniku brand (operated under GYRO HOLDINGS, which also runs a sister branch in Ueno). The restaurant buys whole, carefully selected kuroge (black-haired) Japanese wagyu cattle rather than pre-cut wholesale meat, using the full animal to keep quality high while controlling cost.
chef_bio.philosophy: The 'whole-animal purchase' model is presented as the restaurant's core idea: buying a full animal and using every cut lets the kitchen serve rare and premium parts that are hard to find elsewhere, at a price meant for everyday dining rather than only special occasions.
chef_bio.specialty: Toro-cut wagyu seared for three seconds on one side, USHIHACHI-style raw beef yukhoe, and salt-grilled ox tongue

anecdotes.text: The signature three-second-seared toro cut is marketed as having a repeat-order rate said to exceed 90% among diners who try it.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits directly by the Shibuya Scramble Crossing in a heavily tourist-trafficked area and has an English-language Tabelog listing, but day-to-day menus and staff communication appear to be primarily Japanese.

places.name: 大衆IZAKAYAエイト 水道橋店 (Taishu Izakaya Eight - Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer was found in any source. As a standard izakaya, dishes (tempura, fried items, simmered dishes) very likely use wheat-based soy sauce and shared fryers; sauces and dashi are not confirmed wheat-free. Celiac diners should not assume safety and should ask staff directly (best done via a Japanese allergy card) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/katsuobushi (bonito) disclosure was found. As a seafood-centric izakaya, the great majority of dishes are likely to contain fish or fish-derived dashi even in seemingly vegetable dishes; vegans should ask specifically about dashi ingredients or avoid unless staff can confirm a fish-free preparation.
places.neighborhood: Suidobashi, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer was found in any source. As a standard izakaya, dishes (tempura, fried items, simmered dishes) very likely use wheat-based soy sauce and shared fryers; sauces and dashi are not confirmed wheat-free. Celiac diners should not assume safety and · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

cultural_comfort.note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; this is a standard Japanese-language izakaya best visited with a translation app or phrasebook.

places.name: 宮崎風土 くわんね (Miyazaki Fūdo Quwanne)
places.gf_detail: Signature dishes (chicken nanban, karaage) are wheat-battered and deep-fried, and the menu is built on traditional Miyazaki miso- and soy-based preparations (hiyajiru cold soup, tare sauces) that are almost certainly made with ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce/miso. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free option was mentioned by any source found — not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person kitchen confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on Miyazaki jidori chicken (grilled and fried), chicken nanban, katsuo (bonito) rice bowls, and hiyajiru cold soup made with niboshi (dried sardines) and miso-based dashi. No vegan dishes, substitutions, or dashi alternatives were mentioned in any source.
places.neighborhood: 新宿駅南口 (Shinjuku Station South Exit)
places.notes: Opened April 28, 2018 as the restaurant corner of the Miyazaki Prefecture antenna shop 'Shinjuku Miyazaki-kan KONNE' inside Shinjuku Southern Terrace, right by Shinjuku Station's South/New South Exit. It is operated by A Pei Company, which also runs the nationwide Tsukada Farm iz · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Miyazaki Prefecture, Kyushu
chef_bio.background: Opened April 28, 2018 as the restaurant corner of the Miyazaki Prefecture antenna shop 'Shinjuku Miyazaki-kan KONNE' inside Shinjuku Southern Terrace, right by Shinjuku Station's South/New South Exit. It is operated by A Pei Company, which also runs the nationwide Tsukada Farm izakaya chain.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Kuwanne' is a warm Miyazaki dialect phrase meaning roughly 'please, eat up' — chosen to express a wish for guests to enjoy sun-blessed Miyazaki ingredients in a good mood.
chef_bio.specialty: Chicken nanban — Miyazaki-style fried chicken in sweet vinegar with tartar sauce

anecdotes.text: Manager Honma Yuta, then 31, had spent six and a half years working at the Tsukada Farm chicken chain before being put in charge of this new-concept restaurant. He kept refining the chicken nanban recipe until it won the gold award in the Chicken Nanban category at the 11th Japan Karaage Grand Prix in April 2020.
The restaurant's interior is finished with cedar wood from Obi, Miyazaki, to bring the scent and feel of the prefecture's forests into the dining room.

cultural_comfort.note: Sits inside a prefectural antenna-shop dining hall in the high-traffic Southern Terrace complex next to Shinjuku Station, so it is used to casual and tourist foot traffic, but no English menu or allergy-communication support was confirmed in any source.

places.name: 中華麺店 喜楽 (Chuka Menten Kiraku)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-flour ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu, contains wheat) based pork-bone and seafood broth; no gluten-free noodle substitution or dedicated gluten-free preparation was mentioned in any source. Gyoza (pan-fried dumplings) also contain wheat wrappers. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood (gyokai) based, with pork char siu, gyoza, and other meat-based toppings standard across the menu; no vegetarian or vegan menu items or substitutions were found in any source consulted.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-20:30, closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・道玄坂（百軒店）／神泉
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1952 in Shibuya's old Hyakken-dana entertainment alley just off Dogenzaka, this family-run noodle shop has served the same unfussy soy-sauce ramen recipe for more than 70 years, outlasting the neighborhood's transformation from the postwar era into modern Shibuya. The c · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 1952 in Shibuya's old Hyakken-dana entertainment alley just off Dogenzaka, this family-run noodle shop has served the same unfussy soy-sauce ramen recipe for more than 70 years, outlasting the neighborhood's transformation from the postwar era into modern Shibuya. The current tile-fronted building dates to 1985, replacing the original wooden storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen deliberately avoids trends and sticks to a classic soy-based broth, which longtime fans describe as comforting and unchanging - 'like plain rice' - through the decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Moyashi wantan-men (bean sprout wonton ramen) - plump, generously filled wontons and crisp bean sprouts in a soy-based pork-bone and seafood broth topped with fragrant charred scallions; it reportedly makes up 70-80% of all orders.

anecdotes.text: Regulars sometimes order just the wontons on their own, without noodles, in a large portion - a sign of how central the filling is to the shop's identity.
The shop has been named among Ramen Walker's best ramen shops in Tokyo and has been featured in television specials about long-loved neighborhood noodle shops.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing; no wheat-free tamari or gluten-free soy sauce option was found in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic, cash-only Showa-era neighborhood noodle shop with counter seating and no confirmed English menu - approachable with a little Japanese or pointing at the (mostly noodle) menu.

places.name: セイロンドロップ (Ceylon Drop)
places.gf_detail: Sri Lankan curries here are rice-based (turmeric rice) which is naturally gluten-free, but the menu also includes clearly wheat-containing items (paratha, egg roti), and there is no statement anywhere found about a gluten-free menu, dedicated cookware, or cross-contamination controls. Sauces/curries may use soy sauce or other wheat-derived seasonings in ways not disclosed. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should ask staff directly about specific curry ingredients and preparation before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetable curry is part of the standard lunch/dinner plate alongside the chicken curry, and dal-style vegetable curries are common in Sri Lankan cooking, but no page found explicitly labels any dish as vegan. Pol sambol (the coconut relish typically served alongside the curries) traditionally can include dried Maldive fish flakes in Sri Lankan cooking, which would not be vegan; whether this shop's version is fish-free was not confirmed in any source. Vegetarians/vegans should ask staff to confirm the vegetable curry and pol sambol are made without fish, shrimp paste, or ghee/butter.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区西神田 (Suidōbashi/Jimbōchō area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ceylon Drop was opened as a Sri Lankan tea specialty shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho by an owner who grew up on a tea estate in Sri Lanka. The shop imports single-origin, Sri Lanka Tea Board-certified teas through a shortened supply chain directly from auction-participating estates · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Vel Karunamoorthy (ヴェル・カルナモルティ)
chef_bio.origin: Sri Lanka (born on a tea plantation, associated with the Nuwara Eliya tea-growing region)
chef_bio.background: Ceylon Drop was opened as a Sri Lankan tea specialty shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho by an owner who grew up on a tea estate in Sri Lanka. The shop imports single-origin, Sri Lanka Tea Board-certified teas through a shortened supply chain directly from auction-participating estates, alongside serving home-style Sri Lankan food through lunch, tea time, and dinner.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the freshest possible Ceylon tea and authentic Sri Lankan home cooking to Tokyo by sourcing directly from tea estates and auction-linked distributors, minimizing the distance between plantation and cup.
chef_bio.specialty: The Sri Lanka Plate — turmeric rice served with a chicken curry and a separate vegetable curry, plus mizuna greens and pol sambol (coconut relish), finished with a complimentary cup of single-origin Ceylon tea and a sweet dessert such as kiri pani.

anecdotes.text: 店主はスリランカの茶園生まれで、現地の紅茶文化を紅茶ファンに直接届けることをコンセプトにしている（スリランカ政府紅茶庁認定の紅茶を最小限の流通ルートで仕入れ）。
店長のヴェル・カルナモルティさんがスリランカから直接仕入れした紅茶を販売し、ランチ・ティータイム・ディナーで本格的なスリランカ料理も提供している。

positives.text: The standard curry set (Sri Lanka Plate) pairs a chicken curry with a separate vegetable curry on the same plate, giving those avoiding meat a plant-based curry option without a special request.

cultural_comfort.note: A small independent Japanese-run/Sri Lankan-owner shop with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, though the cuisine itself (rice and curry) is easy to point at.

places.name: 小料理屋 わるくない (Koryoriya Warukunai)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process is documented anywhere. This is a traditional Japanese small-plates restaurant built around dashi, simmered fish, and handmade appetizers; standard soy sauce (contains wheat) and dashi are used in cooking (e.g., the signature tamagoyaki is dashi-based). Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should ask the chef directly about ingredients dish-by-dish before ordering — no dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol was found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or dedicated vegan items are documented. The kitchen is built around fish (simmered/cooked fish dishes are a specialty) and dashi is used throughout, including in the signature tamagoyaki — traditional dashi typically contains katsuobushi (bonito flakes), so hidden animal-derived stock should be assumed in most cooked dishes unless the chef confirms otherwise. Some seasonal vegetable dishes from contracted farms exist on the menu, but none were documented as prepared vegan (dashi-free).
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays 17:00-24:00 (food last order 23:00, drinks last order 23:30); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（Shinjuku Sanchome）
places.cuisine: 日本料理・小料理・地元居酒屋
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2020, this small counter-only Japanese eatery near Shinjuku Sanchome Station was built around an L-shaped counter in a clean, simply refined space meant to give adults a calm, unhurried spot to enjoy seasonal Japanese cooking and sake. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2020, this small counter-only Japanese eatery near Shinjuku Sanchome Station was built around an L-shaped counter in a clean, simply refined space meant to give adults a calm, unhurried spot to enjoy seasonal Japanese cooking and sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant is built around a comforting, unpretentious washoku (Japanese home-style cooking) concept, pairing seasonal dishes with seasonal Japanese sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Simmered and carefully prepared fish dishes, handmade otsumami (small appetizers), a dashi-rich tamagoyaki (Japanese rolled omelet), and potato salad, alongside seasonal vegetables sourced from contracted farms.

anecdotes.text: The name わるくない ('not bad') expresses the owner's hope that every guest leaves the meal feeling it was more than satisfying — genuinely 'not bad at all.'
The chef's carefully simmered fish and handmade appetizers are paired with seasonal vegetables from contracted farms, served at an L-shaped counter alongside a rotating selection of Japanese sake.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, local counter-only izakaya-style restaurant with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese ability (or a translation app) will help when ordering.

places.name: サンソウカフェ (Sansou Cafe)
places.gf_detail: Menu includes dedicated gluten-free sweets, and the signature enzyme brown rice (koso genmai) dishes/onigiri are naturally grain-based. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, dedicated fryer, or confirmation of wheat-free soy sauce was found — celiac diners should confirm cross-contamination and seasoning ingredients with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Offers vegan-friendly dishes including a vegan curry and vegan-suitable enzyme brown rice onigiri, alongside non-vegan menu items (the cafe also serves wine and beer). No confirmation found on whether dashi/bonito-based stock is used elsewhere on the menu; vegan diners should verify specific dishes with staff.
places.hours_raw: Wed/Thu 11:00-16:00; Fri/Sat 11:00-16:00 and 18:00-22:00 (evening cafe); closed Mon, Tue, Sun
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Kannon-ura area), near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Stat
places.cuisine: Organic health-food cafe centered on enzyme
places.notes: According to the cafe's own concept page, the name and philosophy of SANSOU CAFE trace back to the founders' family history running a mountain inn (ryokan) and hotel business; the cafe carries forward the predecessor's guiding idea of wanting to give guests 'a time to truly relax · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: cafe founded by owners with a family background in ryokan/inn hospitality
chef_bio.background: According to the cafe's own concept page, the name and philosophy of SANSOU CAFE trace back to the founders' family history running a mountain inn (ryokan) and hotel business; the cafe carries forward the predecessor's guiding idea of wanting to give guests 'a time to truly relax from the heart. '
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe describes its cooking as 'kihonshoku' (basic, foundational eating) — using seasonal ingredients and carefully chosen seasonings, minimizing additives, and favoring easy-to-digest preparations built around enzyme brown rice so that both body and mind can settle naturally.
chef_bio.specialty: Koso genmai (enzyme-fermented brown rice) served as the centerpiece of the menu, alongside gluten-free sweets and a vegan curry.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's name and hospitality concept were chosen to carry on a predecessor's belief, from the family's ryokan/hotel background, in creating a genuine space to relax from the heart through food.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Extent of allergy accommodation can vary by situation, so guests with concerns are advised to consult staff directly when visiting.

positives.text: The cafe minimizes food additives and trans fats and builds its menu around easily digestible dishes centered on enzyme brown rice.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found; site and listings appear to be Japanese-only, though the location is well within the tourist-trafficked Asakusa area.

places.name: 鳥竹総本店 (Toritake Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional yakitori/unagi shop whose signature dish is basted in a shoyu-based tare that has been continuously topped up (never fully replaced) since 1963 and used across the shared grill and sauce pot for essentially all skewer and unagi orders — wheat-based soy sauce contact is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free menu, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated prep/fryer setup is advertised in any source found.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on charcoal-grilled chicken skewers and unagi (eel) glazed in a shared savory sauce; there is no vegetarian or vegan menu, and the sauce and broths are meat/fish-derived. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (next to Shibuya Mark City)
places.notes: Founded in 1963 (Showa 38) next to what is now Shibuya Mark City, a two-minute walk from Shibuya Station. A casual, long-running yakitori and unagi izakaya now led by a third-generation proprietress referred to locally as the shop's 'kanban okami' (signboard proprietress), who mo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1963 (Showa 38) next to what is now Shibuya Mark City, a two-minute walk from Shibuya Station. A casual, long-running yakitori and unagi izakaya now led by a third-generation proprietress referred to locally as the shop's 'kanban okami' (signboard proprietress), who moves between the kitchen and the floor running the business.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames itself as preserving 'taishu no aji' — the everyday, unpretentious taste of the common people — keeping portions large and prices accessible rather than chasing refinement.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature dish is 'o-gushi yakitori' (large-skewer yakitori): a single oversized skewer stacked in five layers — thigh meat, scallion, shishito pepper, and breast meat — basted in the shop's own sauce and grilled over charcoal.

anecdotes.text: The basting sauce (tare) has never been fully replaced since the restaurant opened in 1963 — it is only topped up. With over 1,000 skewers grilled a day, the shop's own materials estimate that roughly 21.9 million skewers' worth of chicken essence has enriched the sauce over more than 60 years, giving it its deep, savory character.
A feature on the shop describes its five-layer o-gushi yakitori and the continuously-replenished tare as the reason the tavern has comforted generations of Shibuya regulars.

In March 2026 the company opened a takeout stand at Tokyo Dome offering its signature large-skewer yakitori and heritage sauce to baseball spectators, extending the brand beyond its original Shibuya shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic, order-as-you-go Japanese izakaya where menus and service are primarily in Japanese, though it has been drawing more international visitors in recent years.

places.name: スパイスカリー はちわん 神保町店 (Spice Curry Hachiwan (Jimbocho))
places.gf_detail: The shop's central concept, repeated across its own site and food-media coverage, is curry made without wheat flour — using vegetable and spice umami plus Japanese dashi (mackerel, bonito) and miso instead of a flour-thickened roux, which directly addresses the main wheat source in most Japanese curry. However, whether the soy sauce used contains wheat, whether any fried items share oil/a fryer with wheat-containing foods, and general kitchen cross-contamination protocols are not documented in the sources available — celiac diners should confirm these specifics with staff before ordering, especially regarding the rotating side-dish buffet, whose ingredients change daily and are not itemized online.
places.vegan_detail: Curries are built on Japanese dashi described as mackerel- and bonito-based, plus miso, so the signature preparations are not vegan. It is unconfirmed whether any of the three daily rotating curries is a dashi-free, fully plant-based option, or whether the side-dish buffet items are vegetable-only — vegan diners should ask staff about that day's specific ingredients before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00〜15:00（L.O.14:30）、土日祝休み（一部情報源では日曜のみ休みとの記載あり／要確認）
places.neighborhood: Nishikanda, Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: Hachiwan is a spice-curry specialty shop whose Jimbocho branch opened on November 18, 2025, roughly midway between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations (about a 4-minute walk from Jimbocho Station's Exit A2). Coverage of the opening describes it as produced by a chef with a backgroun · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Second location of the Hachiwan brand (sister shop at Toyosu Senkyakubanrai)
chef_bio.background: Hachiwan is a spice-curry specialty shop whose Jimbocho branch opened on November 18, 2025, roughly midway between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations (about a 4-minute walk from Jimbocho Station's Exit A2). Coverage of the opening describes it as produced by a chef with a background at a members-only yakiniku restaurant in Nishi-Azabu, who set out to build a genuinely wheat-free curry using Japanese pantry staples instead of a flour-based roux. It is the second Hachiwan location, alongside a sister shop at Toyosu Senkyakubanrai.
chef_bio.philosophy: No wheat flour in the curry base: the shop leans on vegetables, spices, and Japanese dashi/miso to build depth and umami, aiming for what it calls '本格' (authentic, proper) curry that happens to be gluten-free rather than a diet product.
chef_bio.specialty: A rotating lineup of three curries a day (single or two-curry combination), built on Japanese dashi rather than a wheat-flour roux — for example a mackerel-and-bonito-dashi coconut curry — served with a self-serve buffet of seasonal side dishes.

anecdotes.text: A diner posted about trying the mackerel-and-bonito-dashi coconut curry with the free side-dish buffet, calling it a clear hit and predicting the shop would become popular.
A food blogger's write-up on a Jimbocho-area curry crawl centers on the generous self-serve side-dish buffet as the highlight of a visit to this branch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shop concept and all media coverage confirm the curry is built on mackerel (鯖) and bonito (かつお) dashi plus miso — the GF story is only about omitting the flour roux, and says nothing about soy sauce, which in Japanese cooking overwhelmingly contains wheat and is unconfirmed here.

The offering includes a self-serve 副菜ビュッフェ (side-dish buffet: achar pickles, vegetables, soup, and 'spicy chicken'), an inherent cross-contamination vector whose items are not documented as wheat-free; 'spicy chicken' may be fried/karaage.

No FindMeGlutenFree or HappyCow celiac review of Hachiwan Jimbocho exists; searches for glutened/negative celiac reports returned nothing — consistent with a Nov-2025 opening and no independent celiac verification of handling rather than proven safety.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch (findmeglutenfree.com/jp/tokyo/curry; multiple JP-language searches for 体調/グルテン/混入)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only explicit GF/no-wheat-flour claim originates from the shop's own site; media pieces repeat that concept, so there are not two INDEPENDENT sources of real GF handling as required for a 'high' rating.

positives.text: The curry base is made without wheat flour, using vegetable, spice, and Japanese dashi umami instead of a flour roux — the shop's stated central concept.

cultural_comfort.note: A small lunch-only curry counter aimed at the local Jimbocho office crowd, with no confirmed English menu, English signage, or online reservation in English — expect Japanese-only ordering.

places.name: 新宿立吉 (Shinjuku Tatsukichi)
places.gf_detail: Kushi-age skewers — including the vegetable ones — are coated in wheat panko breadcrumbs and fried in a single shared oil at the counter; the coating itself contains gluten and there is no separate gluten-free fryer, batter, or menu. The composition of the restaurant's secret house dipping sauce is not published and likely contains wheat-based soy sauce or Worcestershire-style sauce. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: About 40 of the roughly 150 skewer ingredients are vegetables (asparagus, garlic, etc.), giving vegans real menu choices, but they are breaded and fried in the same shared oil as meat and seafood skewers, and the ingredients of the house dipping sauce are not published, so animal-derived seasoning (e.g. dashi/bonito) and oil cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Best to tell staff about dietary restrictions when seated and ask specifically which skewers and the sauce are free of animal products.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-sanchome)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1975 as a single Shinjuku Sanchome counter shop, Tatsukichi grew into a small local kushi-age specialty group (Shinjuku honten, Akihabara, Hibiya, and — opened for its 2025 50th anniversary — the 'Happourou' branch back at its original Shinjuku Sanchome site) while kee · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shinjuku Sanchome, Tokyo — opened its first shop on February 4, 1975
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1975 as a single Shinjuku Sanchome counter shop, Tatsukichi grew into a small local kushi-age specialty group (Shinjuku honten, Akihabara, Hibiya, and — opened for its 2025 50th anniversary — the 'Happourou' branch back at its original Shinjuku Sanchome site) while keeping its original recipes. In April 2024 the operating company was acquired as a subsidiary of restaurant group TRIPLETS Inc.
chef_bio.philosophy: An all-you-can-eat 'until you say stop' course format built around a teppan-style open counter, where the cook fries and serves each skewer individually in front of the guest rather than batch-frying.
chef_bio.specialty: Kushi-age (deep-fried skewers) fried one at a time to order at an open counter, using an original fine-panko coating and a secret in-house dipping sauce; roughly 40 vegetable varieties (asparagus, garlic, etc. ) among about 150 total ingredients offered.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened its first location on February 4, 1975 (Showa 50) and marked its 50th anniversary in 2025 by opening a new 'Happourou' branch back at the original Shinjuku Sanchome site.
Kushi-age is fried and served skewer by skewer right at the counter in front of guests, continuing until the customer says 'stop.'
In April 2024, TRIPLETS Inc. made the company operating Shinjuku Tatsukichi's three kushi-age shops in Tokyo a subsidiary.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushi-age skewers — meat, seafood, and vegetable alike — are coated with the restaurant's original panko breadcrumb and fried in the same oil at the open counter, so there is no separate gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable skewers are fried in the same shared oil, at the same counter, as the meat and seafood skewers.

positives.text: Roughly 40 of the restaurant's approximately 150 skewer ingredients are vegetables (e.g. asparagus, garlic), a wider vegetable selection than at most kushi-age shops.

cultural_comfort.note: The menu and the "keep frying until you say stop" counter format are run in Japanese with no confirmed English menu, so some Japanese (or a translation app) will help when specifying dietary exclusions.

places.name: 焼き鳥 陀らく (Yakitori Daraku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated-equipment information was found for this restaurant in any source checked. It is a traditional yakitori counter serving an omakase course of skewers and sauces that in Japanese yakitori commonly include wheat-based soy sauce (tare) alongside salt (shio) preparations, but this restaurant's specific sauce composition (including the fruit-based glazes reviewers mention) was not documented in gluten terms. Because the course is fixed and chef-directed with no printed menu, a celiac diner would need to contact the restaurant directly in advance to discuss ingredients and cross-contact — this cannot be confirmed as safe from available sources.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-omakase-only counter: there is no printed menu, the course is built entirely around rare cuts of chicken (breast, thigh, drumstick, knee meat, etc.) served with fruit-based sauces (e.g. green apple, mango), and it traditionally closes with a minced-meat rice dish (soboro gohan). No vegetarian or vegan course, substitution policy, or plant-based option was found in any source, and the omakase-only, chef's-choice format leaves little room for a meaningful vegan meal here.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂／渋谷
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] According to multiple independent reviews, the owner-chef of Daraku has an unusual background outside the yakitori trade — he previously worked designing accessories before opening this counter in Shibuya. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: According to multiple independent reviews, the owner-chef of Daraku has an unusual background outside the yakitori trade — he previously worked designing accessories before opening this counter in Shibuya.
chef_bio.philosophy: There is no printed menu — the chef serves whatever cut is best that day in a chef's-choice sequence until the guest says stop, and he pairs the meal with sake and wine chosen purely for how well they suit the food rather than by brand name.
chef_bio.specialty: An omakase-only course built around rare, hard-to-find cuts of chicken (e. g. knee meat, drumstick) sourced without loyalty to a single chicken brand, selecting whichever region's bird best suits a given part; skewers are paired with unusual sauces such as green apple and mango, and the course traditionally closes with a soboro (minced chicken) rice dish.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe Daraku as a hideaway in the middle of Shibuya where a rare chicken cut you can't get elsewhere is always on offer, served from a quiet, second-floor counter away from the crowds.

The owner-chef, who has an unusual prior career designing accessories, chooses chicken by part rather than by brand, seeking out whichever producer's bird is best suited to each specific cut, and pairs the food with carefully chosen sake and wine rather than famous-name bottles.

cultural_comfort.note: A small reservation-only omakase counter with no fixed menu and no confirmed English support — a little Japanese (or a hotel/concierge booking) will make the visit much smoother.

places.name: 手打そば大庵 (Teuchi Soba Daian)
places.gf_detail: The soba here is nihachi-style: stone-milled domestic buckwheat hand-kneaded with roughly 20% wheat flour as binder — not 100% buckwheat (juwari), so the noodles themselves contain gluten. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) and the tempura batter used for tenpura seiro are standard preparations that typically rely on wheat-based ingredients. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe accommodations were found in any source, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba specialty restaurant, not a vegan or vegetarian-focused establishment. The menu features duck confit, binchotan-charcoal-grilled chicken, tamagoyaki (egg), and a sommelier-curated wine/sake list; traditional soba tsuyu is conventionally made with bonito (katsuo) dashi. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi option, or vegan labeling was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 平日11:30-14:00(L.O.)・17:00-22:00(L.O.)、土曜12:00-22:00(L.O.)、日祝~21:30(L.O.)、月曜は17:00-22:00(L.O.)のみ（ランチ休み）。年中無休（年始・夏期旧盆を除く）。※情報源により細部の表記に差異あり、来店前に要確認。
places.neighborhood: 新宿東口 (Shinjuku East Exit)
places.notes: Opened in 2001 just outside Shinjuku Station's east/central-east exit as a hand-made soba specialty restaurant. The shop mills its own domestic (Hokkaido) buckwheat in-house with a stone mill, then hand-kneads, hand-rolls and hand-cuts the noodles fresh each day, keeping the whea · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner: Yasuda Shin'ichi (安田眞一), a certified senior sommelier
chef_bio.background: Opened in 2001 just outside Shinjuku Station's east/central-east exit as a hand-made soba specialty restaurant. The shop mills its own domestic (Hokkaido) buckwheat in-house with a stone mill, then hand-kneads, hand-rolls and hand-cuts the noodles fresh each day, keeping the wheat-flour binder to roughly 20% to preserve the buckwheat's natural aroma and flavor.
chef_bio.philosophy: Minimal binder, all-manual noodle-making (hand-kneaded/hand-rolled/hand-cut daily) to let the stone-milled buckwheat's own flavor come through, paired with seasonal Japanese small dishes and a sommelier-selected list of wine and sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut stone-milled soba (seiro / inaka seiro / tenpura seiro), served alongside dishes such as duck confit and binchotan-charcoal-grilled jidori chicken.

anecdotes.text: Just inside the entrance on the right you can watch the buckwheat being stone-milled in-house, with the soba-making station further back where the noodles are hand-kneaded, hand-rolled and hand-cut every day.
Positioned as a spot for relaxed dinners or business entertaining just steps from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, known for dishes like Omi duck confit alongside its soba.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are made with a stone-milled buckwheat dough that includes roughly 20% wheat flour as a binder, so all standard soba served here contains gluten — there is no all-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option identified.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional soba specialty shop, the tsuyu dipping sauce follows standard shoyu-based preparation; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free tsuyu alternative was mentioned in any source found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu includes duck confit and charcoal-grilled chicken, and traditional soba tsuyu is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi; no vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan labeling was found.

cultural_comfort.note: An upscale, Japanese-oriented soba-and-sake restaurant with counter, table and tatami-room seating and online booking, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service — comfortable for visitors with some Japanese or a translation app, less so for zero-Japanese diners with dietary questions.

places.name: 餅屋半兵衛 浅草 (Mochiya Hanbei Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: The mochi itself is not gluten-free: according to the shop's own product description, a small amount of premium Japanese wheat flour is mixed in to help the mochi keep its soft texture until the next day. This makes the core product unsuitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners; no dedicated gluten-free line, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocol was found.
places.vegan_detail: No explicit vegan claim was found from the operator. The core ingredients described (mochi rice, small amount of wheat flour, kinako/roasted soybean flour, red bean paste, kuromitsu black sugar syrup, matcha, grated daikon) are plant-based on their face, but red bean paste (anko) can sometimes include non-vegan additives depending on preparation, and this has not been confirmed either way. Treat as unconfirmed rather than verified vegan.
places.hours_raw: 9:00〜17:00（売り切れ次第終了）／定休日：水曜日 — "9:00-17:00, closes early once sold out; closed Wednesdays"
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito City — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Stat
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The mochi shop occupies the ground floor of a multi-story building in the heart of Asakusa devoted to hands-on Japanese culture: mochi pounding on the 1st floor, a spot to eat mochi with matcha while viewing art on the 2nd floor, and calligraphy, tea ceremony and a sake bar on th · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: mochi shop / wagashi counter within a Japanese-culture experience venue
chef_bio.origin: Opened in Asakusa in 2025 by Sumapura, a company based in Kimitsu City, Chiba Prefecture that runs a multi-floor "Japanese culture experience" venue.
chef_bio.background: The mochi shop occupies the ground floor of a multi-story building in the heart of Asakusa devoted to hands-on Japanese culture: mochi pounding on the 1st floor, a spot to eat mochi with matcha while viewing art on the 2nd floor, and calligraphy, tea ceremony and a sake bar on the floors above.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around letting visitors experience mochi-making as a living craft rather than just buying a packaged sweet — a hands-on mochi-pounding experience (about 1,000 yen per person, includes kinako mochi) is offered alongside the shop counter.
chef_bio.specialty: "Living mochi" (生きてる餅) — rice pounded fresh daily in a traditional wooden mortar and mallet, served plain or with a choice of up to about ten toppings such as kinako (roasted soybean flour), sweet red bean paste, kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), matcha, grated daikon, and creative options like caramel nuts.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer who visited on April 5, 2026 described enjoying freshly pounded mochi served in zenzai (sweet red bean soup).
A blogger described it as a "living mochi" specialty shop where visitors can also try mochi-pounding themselves.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found on cross-contamination handling; the shop is not positioned as gluten-free and mixes wheat flour directly into the mochi dough.

cultural_comfort.note: The shop has a full English-language website and English-language Tabelog listing, so English-speaking visitors can look up menu and toppings in advance even without staff English ability confirmed.

places.name: ワンダーケバブ (Wonder Kebab (Hikarie-mae))
places.gf_detail: Menu is pita sandwich, tortilla wrap, or ケバブ丼 (kebab-over-rice bowl) — all ¥600. The rice bowl skips the wheat pita/tortilla, but no source confirms a wheat-free sauce recipe, dedicated prep surface, or fryer at this small stand, so cross-contact with the bread-based items is likely. Not verified safe for celiac disease — ask staff directly.
places.vegan_detail: Meat options are limited to chicken, beef, or a mix — no tofu, seitan, or vegetable-only kebab was found in the menu, reviews, or shop listings, so there is no vegan protein option here.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 渋谷（ヒカリエ前）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small stand-style Turkish kebab counter just steps from Shibuya Hikarie, near Shibuya's east exit / Miyamasuzaka. It keeps long, no-day-off hours (8am to 11pm) and runs mostly as a grab-and-go takeout spot for the after-work and late-night crowd. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small stand-style Turkish kebab counter just steps from Shibuya Hikarie, near Shibuya's east exit / Miyamasuzaka. It keeps long, no-day-off hours (8am to 11pm) and runs mostly as a grab-and-go takeout spot for the after-work and late-night crowd.
chef_bio.specialty: ケバブ丼 (kebab rice bowl) — grilled chicken and/or beef kebab served over rice with cabbage, tomato, and a spiced mayo-ketchup sauce

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is simple — point at a photo menu and pick your meat (chicken/beef/mix) and sauce (mild/hot/yogurt/teriyaki) — but this is a small local stand where staff may not speak much English, so a few basic Japanese words help.

places.name: 肉丸酒場 水道橋店 (Nikumaru Sakaba Suidobashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer statement was found. As a churrasco/izakaya serving meat sushi (vinegared rice, likely soy-based tare/sauces) and grilled skewers, wheat exposure via soy sauce and shared marinades/grills should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise; not suitable for celiac-level strictness without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based substitution options were found. The restaurant's identity is entirely meat-focused (churrasco skewers, meat sushi, roast beef, often as all-you-can-eat courses), so it is not a realistic option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 火～日 16:00～24:00（料理L.O. 23:30、ドリンクL.O. 23:30）／月曜定休（3月・4月・12月、東京ドームイベント開催時は営業）
places.neighborhood: 千代田区水道橋（神田三崎町）
places.notes: A private-room izakaya near Suidobashi Station built around Brazilian-style churrasco (charcoal-grilled skewered meat) crossed with Japanese meat-sushi (肉寿司) small plates, aimed at group parties and company banquets rather than solo dining. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A private-room izakaya near Suidobashi Station built around Brazilian-style churrasco (charcoal-grilled skewered meat) crossed with Japanese meat-sushi (肉寿司) small plates, aimed at group parties and company banquets rather than solo dining.
chef_bio.specialty: Seared meat sushi (炙り肉寿司), roast beef, and charcoal-grilled churrasco skewers, often served as part of all-you-can-eat/all-you-can-drink course menus.

cultural_comfort.note: The Tabelog listing has an English reservation page, but the venue's own site, menu, and in-restaurant service appear to be Japanese-only, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help.

places.name: 萬馬軒 新宿西口本店 (Manbaken Shinjuku Nishiguchi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles (thick domestic wheat noodles from Mikawaya Seimen) and a soy-sauce-based secret miso tare; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated GF preparation area, or allergen-friendly menu was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free/celiac diet.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is built on pork bone and chicken stock, and the signature bowls are topped with thick-cut chashu (braised pork); no plant-based broth, vegetarian, or vegan menu option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners as currently documented.
places.hours_raw: 全日 11:00～23:00（定休日なし、臨時休業の可能性あり）
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku Nishiguchi (Nishi-Shinjuku), Shinjuku Station area
places.notes: Manbaken is a Tokyo miso-ramen specialist tracing back to 1988, when it was founded in Meguro by Eiji Hori. Over more than three decades the brand built a following for its rich pork-and-chicken broth blended with a secret miso tare. In autumn 2025 the then four-store chain was a · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: founder: Eiji Hori (1988, original Meguro shop)
current operator: Garden Group Co., Ltd. (since Nov 2025 acquisition)
chef_bio.origin: Meguro, Tokyo (1988)
chef_bio.background: Manbaken is a Tokyo miso-ramen specialist tracing back to 1988, when it was founded in Meguro by Eiji Hori. Over more than three decades the brand built a following for its rich pork-and-chicken broth blended with a secret miso tare. In autumn 2025 the then four-store chain was acquired by restaurant operator Garden Group, which set out to preserve the original recipe while professionalizing operations and growing the brand. The Nishi-Shinjuku Honten location, opened February 13, 2026 a few minutes from Shinjuku Station's west exit, was launched as the brand's flagship 'next chapter' store — its fifth location overall and third in the Shinjuku area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Under its current operator, the stated aim is to carry forward more than 30 years of miso-ramen recipe and technique while improving store operations, with a mid-term goal of becoming the leading station-front miso-ramen brand across greater Tokyo.
chef_bio.specialty: 濃厚味噌らーめん (rich miso ramen) — pork-bone and chicken broth emulsified with a secret blended miso tare, served with thick domestic wheat noodles (from Mikawaya Seimen). The signature bowl is the Negi Miso Chashu Men, topped with thick-cut braised chashu pork.

anecdotes.text: On its February 13, 2026 opening day, the Nishi-Shinjuku Honten offered its miso ramen at a special opening price of 600 yen (tax included).

The store was billed by its operator as the flagship marking 'the next chapter of the Manbaken brand' — the brand's fifth location and the third in the Shinjuku area, positioned to catch business, tourist, and inbound demand near Shinjuku Station.
Manbaken was acquired by Garden Group (TSE: 274A) in a cash deal of about 200 million yen, taking over operation of the brand's four existing directly-run stores from November 1, 2025.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The miso tare/soup base is described as combining pork-bone and chicken stock with a secret soy-sauce blend, indicating standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is used; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A short walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit in a high-traffic tourist/business corridor its operator explicitly targeted for inbound visitors, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: ひょうたんなべ 雷門店 (Hyotannabe Kaminarimon-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found on the official site, Tabelog, or other listings. The oden broth and general Japanese-izakaya seasoning conventions typically rely on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and the menu also includes tempura, so cross-contamination risk should be assumed high; celiac diners should not treat this as a safe option without asking staff directly, and even then confirmation may be difficult given no confirmed English support.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dashi is built on bonito (katsuobushi) and chicken stock, and most oden ingredients are fish-based (tsumire, satsuma-age, hanpen) or meat-based. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi option, or vegan labeling was found on any source, so this is not a viable option for vegan diners as currently documented.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa / Kaminarimon (浅草雷門)
places.notes: A homemade-oden izakaya tucked 30 seconds' walk from Asakusa's Kaminarimon (Thunder Gate) — one of the neighborhood's most recognizable landmarks. The kitchen builds its oden broth from Rishiri kelp, bonito, and chicken, simmering more than 30 kinds of ingredients including handm · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A homemade-oden izakaya tucked 30 seconds' walk from Asakusa's Kaminarimon (Thunder Gate) — one of the neighborhood's most recognizable landmarks. The kitchen builds its oden broth from Rishiri kelp, bonito, and chicken, simmering more than 30 kinds of ingredients including handmade tsumire (fish balls) and satsuma-age (fried fish cake) alongside oden staples like daikon, potato, egg, and hanpen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around a slow-simmered, kombu-and-bonito dashi and a large rotating lineup of oden ingredients, paired with a curated selection of Japanese sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Tomato-cheese oden — a dashi-soaked tomato topped with melted cheese for a milky twist on the classic hotpot — served alongside sashimi, ankimo (monkfish liver), and tempura.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits just 30 seconds on foot from Kaminarimon, the iconic gate at the entrance to Asakusa's Sensoji temple complex, making it a popular stop for visitors exploring the area.
Its oden broth is described as kombu- and bonito/chicken-based with over 30 types of ingredients, including handmade tsumire and satsuma-age, plus a signature tomato-cheese oden.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood oden izakaya aimed primarily at Japanese-speaking guests; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found beyond automated translation on the Tabelog English listing.

places.name: ケバブカフェ (Kebab Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification was found. Kebab plates (grilled meat, rice, salad) could work for a gluten-free diet if the pita/flatbread wrap is skipped, but marinades, sauces, and whether the grill/prep area avoids cross-contact with bread have not been confirmed. Ask staff directly (English is spoken) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetarian dishes (salads and dips) are offered alongside the halal kebab menu, but Turkish dips/sauces often contain yogurt, and no source confirmed which specific dishes are fully vegan (dairy- and honey-free). Ask staff before ordering if strict vegan avoidance is required.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Turkish kebab shop on Dogenzaka run by a Turkish owner-chef, cooking in the casual style of a Turkish neighborhood lokanta (corner eatery) rather than a formal restaurant menu. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Turkey
