chef_bio.background: A small Turkish kebab shop on Dogenzaka run by a Turkish owner-chef, cooking in the casual style of a Turkish neighborhood lokanta (corner eatery) rather than a formal restaurant menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the everyday flavors of a Turkish street-corner lokanta to Shibuya, with a bilingual menu so both locals and visitors can order comfortably.
chef_bio.specialty: Kebab plates and kebab sandwiches (chicken, lamb, beef), Iskender kebab, and baklava

anecdotes.text: Kebab varieties are created by a Turkish chef with the taste of a Turkish street-corner cafeteria (lokanta), and the shop uses a different type of bread for its kebabs than other kebab spots in Tokyo.
The restaurant is run by a sole owner-chef described as extremely friendly with great service; because the shop is narrow, only the rear seats can accommodate groups larger than four.

positives.text: The menu has both English and Japanese, and the staff can speak English, so ordering and asking about ingredients is not a problem.
The restaurant is certified halal and also lists vegetarian dishes such as salads and dips alongside its kebab-focused menu.

cultural_comfort.note: The menu is bilingual in English and Japanese and staff can communicate in English, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking diners to order and ask questions.

places.name: ブリスクスタンド水道橋（BRISKSTAND SUIDOBASHI） (Brisk Stand Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free bun or dedicated wheat-free option is documented. The signature buns are described as made from whole-grain (unbleached) wheat flour, and no allergen/gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination information was found in any sourced material. Treat as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian or plant-based patty/burger option was found; every burger described uses a 100% Japanese-beef patty. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available sourcing.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町 (Kanda Misakicho), Suidobashi, Chiyoda-ku
places.cuisine: ハンバーガー・グルメバーガー
places.notes: BRISK STAND is a Japanese gourmet-burger brand built around its original "kitta yatsu" (the cut one) burger; after winning the Gourmet Burger Japan Championship 2025 it expanded rapidly nationwide. The Suidobashi shop (BRISKSTAND SUIDOBASHI, subtitled "Burger & Bar") opened July  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Japan (nationwide chain, Tokyo branch)
chef_bio.background: BRISK STAND is a Japanese gourmet-burger brand built around its original "kitta yatsu" (the cut one) burger; after winning the Gourmet Burger Japan Championship 2025 it expanded rapidly nationwide. The Suidobashi shop (BRISKSTAND SUIDOBASHI, subtitled "Burger & Bar") opened July 26, 2025, a 1-2 minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station near Tokyo Dome, and was reported as roughly the brand's 14th store.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's catchphrase is "和食にかぶりつけ" ("bite into Japanese cuisine") — burgers built with a Japanese culinary sensibility, using whole-grain-flour buns and domestic beef, in a wood-toned "wa-modern" interior.
chef_bio.specialty: "切ったやつ" (Kitta Yatsu, "the cut one") — a 100% Japanese-beef patty burger that is cut and double-seared on a hot griddle so the meat's juices soak into the cut surfaces of the bun and patty, finished with house-made sweet stewed onions.

anecdotes.text: BRISK STAND won first place in the JAPAN BURGER CHAMPIONSHIP 2025 gourmet burger competition before opening this Suidobashi location.
The Suidobashi shop opened as roughly the 14th BRISK STAND location, styled as a 'wa-modern' space blending the calm of a traditional restaurant with the casualness of a burger stand.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern Western-format burger-and-bar concept with an English brand name and picture-friendly menu, so ordering is straightforward even with minimal Japanese, though no confirmed English-language menu was found.

places.name: 俺の割烹 炉ばた 新宿 (Ore no Kappo Robata Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer information, or wheat-free statement was found in any source. As a robatayaki izakaya, tare/glaze sauces, ponzu, and dipping sauces typically used on grilled skewers and hotpot items are usually soy-sauce (wheat) based, and shared grill surfaces are likely used across meat, seafood, and any breaded items. Celiac diners should ask staff in detail about individual dish sauces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is centered on grilled game meat and seafood; no vegan menu, dashi ingredients, or plant-based accommodation statement was found. Robatayaki restaurants of this type often grill some vegetables, but that has not been confirmed here, and traditional broths/hotpot bases in this style of cooking commonly use bonito (katsuobushi) or other animal-based dashi. Vegan diners should ask in detail before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 Kabukicho, Shinjuku
places.notes: Occupies the Ishizuka Building space in Kabukicho that was previously home to the game-meat izakaya 炉とマタギ (Ro to Matagi). That shop closed for renovation in spring 2025, and the space reopened on June 25, 2025 as 俺の炉ばた under the 俺の ("Ore no") restaurant group. On January 14, 2026 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Robatayaki izakaya operated by 俺の株式会社 (Ore no Co., Ltd.)
chef_bio.origin: Part of 俺の株式会社, a Tokyo restaurant group whose model is built around staffing kitchens with chefs trained in high-end/Michelin-caliber settings and selling their cooking at a fraction of typical fine-dining prices.
chef_bio.background: Occupies the Ishizuka Building space in Kabukicho that was previously home to the game-meat izakaya 炉とマタギ (Ro to Matagi). That shop closed for renovation in spring 2025, and the space reopened on June 25, 2025 as 俺の炉ばた under the 俺の ("Ore no") restaurant group. On January 14, 2026 it was folded into the group's 俺の割烹 (Ore no Kappo) line and renamed 俺の割烹 炉ばた 新宿, its current name.
chef_bio.philosophy: The 俺の group's stated approach is to put skilled cooks on ingredient-forward menus with a very high food-cost ratio (reportedly over 60%), covering the economics through fast table turnover rather than high per-dish margins.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal robatayaki of game meat (jibier) — Ezo venison, wild boar, duck and lamb, including a four-game-meat platter (肉四種盛り合わせ) — cooked live at two grill stations, an open-hearth 原始焼き and a mesh-grill 網焼き, for a smoky, tableside-theater style of service.

anecdotes.text: The location reopened in the Ishizuka Building in Kabukicho on June 25, 2025 as "Ore no Robata Shinjuku" after previously operating as 炉とマタギ.

The shop is grouped under 俺の株式会社's 俺の炉ばた concept, described as the group's first Japanese-style (和) concept in 11 years, combining a primitive open-hearth grill and a mesh grill so guests can watch high-grade seafood and game meat cook.

cultural_comfort.note: Has an English-language Tabelog listing and online table booking, and sits in a heavily tourist-trafficked part of Kabukicho, but no English menu or bilingual-staff claim was found, so a few basic Japanese food/allergy words will help.

places.name: 鳥せん (Torisen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific information were found in any source. The confirmed menu is built around yakitori with soy-sauce-based tare glaze, kamameshi, and duck nabe served with housemade ponzu — tare and ponzu in traditional Japanese cooking are typically soy-sauce-based and therefore contain wheat, and there is no indication this kitchen offers a wheat-free alternative. Treat as not gluten-free safe without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options or accommodations were found in any source. The confirmed specialties (yakitori, chicken kamameshi, duck nabe) are entirely meat-based, and the duck hot pot broth is explicitly katsuobushi (dried bonito) based — meaning even the soup base is not vegan/vegetarian. Not recommended for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 火〜日・祝日・祝前日 17:00〜22:00（料理L.O. 22:00、ドリンクL.O. 22:00）、月曜定休（月曜が祝日の場合は翌火曜定休）
places.neighborhood: Kannon-ura, Asakusa, Taito, Tokyo
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific information were found in any source. The confirmed menu is built around yakitori with soy-sauce-based tare glaze, kamameshi, and duck nabe served with housemade ponzu — tare and ponzu in traditional Japanese coo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — in business roughly 46 years as of sources found
chef_bio.philosophy: A nostalgic, old-Tokyo neighborhood eatery in the backstreets behind Sensoji (the 'Kannon-ura' district) where regulars return as much to see the owner and his wife as for the food.
chef_bio.specialty: Tori kamameshi (chicken clay-pot rice, one of 11 kamameshi varieties on the menu), yakitori, and kamo nabe (duck hot pot) with a housemade ponzu and katsuobushi-based broth

anecdotes.text: 鳥せんは浅草で30年以上続く老舎で、人情味あふれる店主と奥さんの人柄が温かく、お二人に会いに来店する常連のお客様も多い。
自慢の鶻鳩や常連のお客様に人気の釜めしはもちろん、焼鳥やカジキマグロ大トロステーキなどの焼物も多数ご用意されております。人気の釜めしは全部で11種類で、特に人気な釜めしはプリップリの鳥をふんだんに使った鳥釜めしです。

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school neighborhood izakaya run by a local couple with a regulars-first atmosphere; no evidence of an English menu or foreign-visitor-oriented service, so basic Japanese or a translation app is recommended.

places.name: 旭川成吉思汗 大黒屋 新宿駅東口店 (Asahikawa Jingisukan Daikokuya Shinjuku Stat)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or allergen chart was found. The chain's signature tare (dipping sauce) is soy-sauce-based; a retail version of the same "Daikokuya-supervised" Jingisukan sauce lists soy sauce and wheat among its ingredients, so the standard dipping sauce is not gluten-free. Menu items such as cold noodles (冷麺) and possibly the tsukune (meatballs) likely contain wheat. Plain salt-grilled lamb and vegetables ordered without the tare may be lower-risk, but this restaurant uses a shared open grill with no confirmed dedicated gluten-free cooking area, so cross-contact is likely. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-5990-2927) and ask specifically about the tare ingredients and grill-sharing practices before eating.
places.vegan_detail: This is a lamb-grill restaurant with no vegan menu found. Side dishes such as a daikon-and-almond salad, grilled vegetables, and ramen/cold-noodle salads exist, but sauces and dressings likely include bonito (kelp/bonito extract appears in the chain's sauce ingredients) and other animal-derived seasonings, so even the vegetable sides are not confirmed vegan-safe. Vegan diners would need to ask staff in detail about each dressing/sauce and should expect very limited, unconfirmed options in a restaurant built around meat grilling.
places.neighborhood: 新宿駅東口 Shinjuku Station East Exit
places.notes: Daikokuya Jingisukan began in Asahikawa, Hokkaido in 2002 and became a queue-forming local favorite there before opening its first Tokyo branch just outside Shinjuku Station's East Exit on November 21, 2024, on the 10th floor of the Shinjuku Aoki Building. The interior incorporat · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Jingisukan (Hokkaido-style grilled lamb) restaurant, Tokyo branch of an Asahikawa-founded chain
chef_bio.origin: Asahikawa, Hokkaido
chef_bio.background: Daikokuya Jingisukan began in Asahikawa, Hokkaido in 2002 and became a queue-forming local favorite there before opening its first Tokyo branch just outside Shinjuku Station's East Exit on November 21, 2024, on the 10th floor of the Shinjuku Aoki Building. The interior incorporates Ainu-inspired patterns and offers window seats with night views over Shinjuku.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes air-flown, genuinely fresh (non-frozen) lamb, kept in a dedicated lamb refrigerator held below 0°C, as a point of difference from most Jingisukan restaurants that use frozen meat.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh, never-frozen raw lamb (生ラム) grilled Jingisukan-style on a domed hot plate, especially the shoulder loin (肩ロース); popular sides include a crunchy daikon-and-almond salad and Korean-style cold noodles (冷麺).

anecdotes.text: A blogger visited with family, including a Hokkaido-native mother curious about the restaurant, and found the raw lamb shoulder loin excellent along with the cold noodles, ramen salad, and daikon salad, describing the restaurant as clean and delicious.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The retail 'Daikokuya-supervised' Jingisukan sauce (same brand/recipe lineage as the restaurant's tare), made by Sorachi, lists ingredients including soy sauce (国内製造), apple, sugar, hydrolyzed protein, onion, rice fermentation seasoning, vinegar, spices, sake, salt, kelp extract, bonito extract, and states it contains wheat, soy, chicken, pork, and apple.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern chain restaurant with online table booking, but no confirmed English menu, multilingual staff, or dietary/allergy signage was found, so non-Japanese-speaking diners with allergies should bring a written allergy card.

places.name: 茶々工房 ふたつめ (Chacha Koubou Futatsume)
places.gf_detail: The signature and effectively only product is a mont blanc built on a wheat waffle cone with cake/cream components; no gluten-free version, alternative base, ingredient allergen labeling, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found. Treat as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The mont blanc contains mascarpone cream (dairy) and other non-vegan components (likely eggs/dairy in the cake/waffle base); no vegan version, substitution, or plant-based alternative was mentioned in any source found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (multiple sources agree on these hours); closed-day status conflicts across sources — see caveats.
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草 (Asakusa, Taito-ku) — approx. 3-minute walk from Asaku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Run by an individual craftsman (surname Aizawa per press coverage) who hand-built the extruding machine used to press the mont blanc to order, spending roughly six months developing it — the mechanical core is off-the-shelf but he built the wooden housing and hand-painted the sho · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Second branch ("ふたつめ" = "the second one") of 茶々工房, a Japanese-tea sweets shop originally founded in 2003 in Takadanobaba; this Asakusa location opened in 2022.
chef_bio.background: Run by an individual craftsman (surname Aizawa per press coverage) who hand-built the extruding machine used to press the mont blanc to order, spending roughly six months developing it — the mechanical core is off-the-shelf but he built the wooden housing and hand-painted the shop's logo on it himself.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sources matcha exclusively from Hattori Farm in Shizuoka, an organic, pesticide-free grower chosen because it shades its tea bushes with solar panels as an energy-conscious growing method; sources chestnuts from a grower in Kasama, Ibaraki, introduced through a relative of the owner and chosen partly because that farm also provides employment support for people with disabilities.
chef_bio.specialty: 生搾り抹茶モンブラン (fresh hand-pressed matcha mont blanc), extruded to order into a waffle cone with mascarpone cream, red beans (azuki), matcha sauce, amber sugar and lemon marmalade.

anecdotes.text: The machine used to press each mont blanc to order was hand-built by the shop's owner over about six months; the mechanism itself is off-the-shelf but he built the wooden casing and painted the shop's logo onto it himself.

All matcha used in the shop comes from a single organic, pesticide-free farm (Hattori Farm) in Shizuoka, chosen in part because the farm uses solar panels to shade its tea as an energy-efficient growing method.
The chestnuts come from a grower in Kasama, Ibaraki, introduced to the owner through a relative; that farm was selected partly because it also offers employment support to people with disabilities.
The fresh-pressed matcha mont blanc became a social-media sensation, drawing queues of both local and international visitors even on weekdays.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact, likely Japanese-only takeout counter, but it's a well-known social-media destination that regularly draws foreign tourists, so simple pointing/photo-menu ordering for its one signature item should be manageable.

places.name: クアトロラボ (Quattro Labo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found on the official site, Tabelog, or press coverage. The menu centers on curry-style dishes (e.g., the signature Lemon Rice) which commonly use wheat-containing roux and soy sauce in Japan; assume wheat exposure unless confirmed otherwise by staff at time of visit.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, labeling, or ingredient breakdown (e.g., dashi/katsuo/bonito use) was found on the official site, Tabelog, or press coverage. Menu appears to be a standard omnivore café/bar offering (curry, Lemon Rice, drinks); vegan diners should confirm ingredients with staff before ordering.
places.hours_raw: CAFE TIME 11:00-17:00 / BAR TIME 17:00-23:00 (Food L.O. 22:00, Drink L.O. 22:30); open year-round, following Shibuya PARCO hours
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (渋谷PARCO地下1階, Shibuya Station area)
places.notes: QUATTRO LABO is a music café and bar produced by CLUB QUATTRO, the Shibuya live-music venue operator. It originally opened in 2014 inside Kichijoji PARCO, then closed there in August 2019 and reopened in November 2019 in the basement of Shibuya PARCO, bringing along its collectio · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Music café & bar produced by CLUB QUATTRO
chef_bio.background: QUATTRO LABO is a music café and bar produced by CLUB QUATTRO, the Shibuya live-music venue operator. It originally opened in 2014 inside Kichijoji PARCO, then closed there in August 2019 and reopened in November 2019 in the basement of Shibuya PARCO, bringing along its collection of more than 5,000 analog records and CDs and a handmade large speaker system.
chef_bio.philosophy: By day it runs as a casual café; by night it becomes a dedicated music bar, serving food and drinks while classic analog records play through a custom high-end sound system spanning 1960s-70s rock, soul, jazz, roots music, and city pop.
chef_bio.specialty: Lemon Rice, a limited-quantity signature dish created in collaboration with curry devotee Yuki Komiyama of Hohdiiran

anecdotes.text: The venue originally opened in 2014 at Kichijoji PARCO before relocating to the basement of Shibuya PARCO in November 2019, bringing its collection of over 5,000 analog records and CDs.
The Lemon Rice, a limited-quantity signature dish, was produced by curry enthusiast Yuki Komiyama of Hohdiiran.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a major Shibuya shopping complex frequented by tourists, but no English menu or English-language service was confirmed in any source.

places.name: 花とごはんとクリームソーダ なぎさ食堂 (Hana to Gohan to Cream Soda Nagisa Shokudo ()
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program, GF menu labeling, or dedicated fryer was found in any source. The menu centers on rice plates with fish or meat (e.g., acqua pazza) and sauces that commonly rely on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi in Japanese cafe cooking; celiac diners should ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contamination before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. Dishes are fish/meat-centric (rice plates, acqua pazza) and likely use dashi/bonito-based stocks common in Japanese cafe cooking; the cream sodas may be vegan but ingredients (e.g., cream/syrups) were not confirmed. Vegan diners should ask staff directly.
places.hours_raw: Most sources (incl. official Tokyo Dome City gourmet guide): 11:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). One search snippet noted a change to 11:30-19:00 (L.O. 18:30) effective "from May 1" (year unclear). Hours may extend during Tokyo Dome events.
places.neighborhood: 文京区・東京ドームシティ（水道橋）
places.notes: Opened in December 2024 in the Meets Port area of Tokyo Dome City, directly in front of Tokyo Dome City Hall, a short walk from Suidobashi Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in December 2024 in the Meets Port area of Tokyo Dome City, directly in front of Tokyo Dome City Hall, a short walk from Suidobashi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: A flower-filled, photogenic and retro concept space designed as much for taking pictures as for eating — every corner of the shop, including a second-floor terrace, is treated as a photo spot, tying into Tokyo's cafe culture around fan-activity (oshikatsu) outings.
chef_bio.specialty: Photogenic cream sodas in a range of colors (including a pure-white version and a deep-indigo version, plus an adults-only version with alcohol), rice plates paired with fish or meat mains such as acqua pazza, and an afternoon tea set served with a drink.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's afternoon tea set, served amid a flower-filled dining room and priced with a drink included, is highlighted as a bookable elegant afternoon-tea experience at Tokyo Dome City.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; expect primarily Japanese-language service, though it sits within the tourist-heavy Tokyo Dome City complex.

places.name: This is Crêpe（ディスイズクレープ） (This is Crepe)
places.gf_detail: The crêpe batter itself is made from carefully sourced French wheat flour — described repeatedly across sources as a core selling point ("French premium wheat") — so gluten is baked into the base product, not just a cross-contact risk. No gluten-free batter or dedicated GF item was found. Not suitable for celiac guests.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu items were identified. The shop's signature item is a butter-forward crêpe built around Échiré dairy butter, and standard French crêpe batter typically contains egg and milk; no egg-free or dairy-free alternative is advertised on any source checked.
places.neighborhood: 新宿 / 新宿御苑前・新宿三丁目周辺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is Crêpe is a small crêpe-and-coffee specialty shop near Shinjuku Gyoen, built around a single hero product: a "Sugar Butter" crêpe that showcases the dough itself rather than piling on toppings. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: This is Crêpe is a small crêpe-and-coffee specialty shop near Shinjuku Gyoen, built around a single hero product: a "Sugar Butter" crêpe that showcases the dough itself rather than piling on toppings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's own framing (via its Instagram bio and store description) is built around "the highest-quality crêpes, the highest-quality coffee, communication full of smiles, and a comfortable atmosphere in the store" — an emphasis on letting a carefully made, chewy French-flour dough be delicious on its own, without needing heavy toppings.
chef_bio.specialty: Sugar Butter crêpe made with French wheat flour and Échiré butter, paired with specialty coffee (beans sourced from origins such as Ethiopia and Kenya)

anecdotes.text: The shop describes its signature style as "生地だけで美味しいもちもちSugar Butter" — a chewy Sugar Butter crêpe that's delicious on the strength of the dough alone.
In 2024 the shop added the "imako" mobile payment/ordering option, reported as part of the brand's push to make ordering more convenient for customers near Shinjuku Gyoen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not applicable as a cross-contact question in the usual sense — the standard crêpe batter is itself made with French wheat flour, so gluten is a base ingredient rather than an incidental contaminant, and no gluten-free batter option was found.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact Japanese specialty crêpe counter with no English menu confirmed — pointing at the photo menu and basic greetings should be enough to order.

places.name: ロッジ赤石 (Lodge Akaishi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options or accommodations found. Signature dishes are wheat-based: napolitan is wheat pasta, and the famous ebi sando is breaded, deep-fried shrimp on wheat bread. No mention of gluten-free substitutions or dedicated frying equipment. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options or accommodations found. This is a classic yoshoku (Western-style) kissaten built around eggs, dairy, meat, and shrimp (its two signature items — napolitan with meat/vegetables in an egg-and-butter-friendly kitchen, and a deep-fried shrimp sandwich — are both non-vegan). No mention of a vegetable-only menu or dashi/bonito-free preparation.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 観音裏 (Asakusa, Kannon-ura)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Lodge Akaishi opened in Asakusa's 観音裏 (Kannon-ura) backstreets in 1973. The name comes from Mt. Akaishi (赤石岳), a peak in the Southern Alps visible from the founders' hometown in Nagano — hence the log-cabin, mountain-lodge look of the wood-paneled interior. Because Asakusa was hi · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: kissaten (retro Japanese-style coffee shop / yoshoku diner)
chef_bio.origin: Founded in Showa 48 (1973) by a family originally from Nagano Prefecture.
chef_bio.background: Lodge Akaishi opened in Asakusa's 観音裏 (Kannon-ura) backstreets in 1973. The name comes from Mt. Akaishi (赤石岳), a peak in the Southern Alps visible from the founders' hometown in Nagano — hence the log-cabin, mountain-lodge look of the wood-paneled interior. Because Asakusa was historically a lively entertainment and hanamachi district, the shop originally stayed open into the early morning hours to serve the neighborhood's night-shift workers; today it still runs from morning until the middle of the night, so its clientele shifts through the day from local residents having a leisurely breakfast or lunch, to people getting off work late at night, to taxi drivers taking a break before dawn.
chef_bio.specialty: Napolitan spaghetti (ナポリタン) and its ebi sando / ebi katsu sandwich (fried shrimp sandwich), widely cited by food writers as among the best in Tokyo.

anecdotes.text: 店名は創業者である現マスターのお父さんと叔父さんが長野県の出身であったことから、赤石岳を由来としています。 (The shop's name comes from Mt. Akaishi, since the founders — the current master's father and uncle — were from Nagano.)
深夜まで営業しているのは、浅草がかつて花街として深夜まで賑わっていた名残りだと言われている。 (It stays open late as a holdover from Asakusa's days as a bustling hanamachi entertainment district.)
東京で最も美味しいエビフライサンドを食べるならココ！ (If you want the best fried-shrimp sandwich in Tokyo, this is the place.)

cultural_comfort.note: A classic Showa-era neighborhood kissaten with no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff, though it is well covered by Japanese food blogs and appears in the Tabelog 100 kissaten list.

places.name: 秀吉 神保町水道橋店 (Hideyoshi Jimbocho Suidobashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Yakitori tare (glaze) and the karaage/kushi-katsu batter are wheat-based soy-sauce/flour preparations typical of this style of izakaya; no gluten-free menu, substitutions, or dedicated-fryer claims were found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is chicken-centric (yakitori, karaage, kushi-katsu) with no vegetarian or vegan dishes mentioned in any source found; sauces/tare are not confirmed dashi- or bonito-free. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金: ランチ 11:30-14:00／ディナー 17:00-24:00。土・日・祝: 一部情報源では12:00-23:00、別の情報源では17:00-24:00と表記が分かれる。不定休。（Source listings disagree slightly on weekend hours — confirm directly before visiting.）
places.neighborhood: 神保町 (Jimbocho), Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: This is a branch of the OC Group's "Hideyoshi" yakitori/kushi-katsu izakaya chain, one of several Hideyoshi locations around Tokyo (this one straddling Jimbocho and Suidobashi). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Yakitori/kushi-katsu izakaya, part of a multi-location chain
chef_bio.background: This is a branch of the OC Group's "Hideyoshi" yakitori/kushi-katsu izakaya chain, one of several Hideyoshi locations around Tokyo (this one straddling Jimbocho and Suidobashi).
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated concept, repeated across listing sites, is speed, low price, and taste ("早い・安い・美味い") — high-volume, value-priced grilled and fried chicken rather than a chef-driven or artisanal approach.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori (skewers priced from around 80 yen) alongside a "baka-mori" (oversized-portion) series of karaage fried chicken and kushi-katsu.

anecdotes.text: Uses binchotan charcoal for the yakitori and an original sauce blended from eight seasonings and vegetables for some dishes.
Known among reviewers for very large karaage portions — one reviewer described sharing yakitori, a big karaage, and a large salad with drinks for about ¥4,000 for two people.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kushi-katsu and karaage are deep-fried items on the menu alongside grilled skewers; no separate/dedicated fryer for allergen-free items was mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's own original sauce and yakitori tare are described as blended seasoning sauces typical of this izakaya style, which in Japan are standardly soy-sauce (wheat-containing) based; no wheat-free tare or shoyu was mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A local Japanese-language chain izakaya with no evidence of an English menu, so pointing at photos on the menu or using a translation app is recommended.

places.name: 巖 (Iwao)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is mentioned anywhere in available sources. As a standard izakaya, dishes almost certainly rely on wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi, and some items appear to be tempura-style fried; there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or wheat-free soy sauce option. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should not assume safety here without asking staff directly (in Japanese) about individual dishes, and should treat this as a high-risk venue absent that confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling was found. The kitchen's signature items are fresh-fish sashimi and roast wagyu beef, and traditional izakaya dashi is typically bonito (katsuobushi)-based, so hidden animal-derived stock is likely throughout the menu even in vegetable-forward dishes. Not recommended for vegan diners without a detailed conversation with staff about specific dishes.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu & Sun: 17:30–24:00 (food/drink last order 23:30). Fri, Sat & days before public holidays: 17:30–1:00 (last order 24:30). Reported as open year-round with no fixed closed day.
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Iwao occupies a wooden house said to be around 70-80 years old that originally housed a tailor's shop, tucked down a back alley off Dogenzaka a few minutes from Shibuya Station. The building was renovated into a two-floor 'hideaway' izakaya, and as of 2025 the shop was marking ro · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Second restaurant opened (July 2000) by Asia Kitchen Co., a small independent Shibuya izakaya group founded by restaurateur Eiji Yoshizaki (also behind sister shops Naru, Sanchaki and Sanjuugoban)
chef_bio.background: Iwao occupies a wooden house said to be around 70-80 years old that originally housed a tailor's shop, tucked down a back alley off Dogenzaka a few minutes from Shibuya Station. The building was renovated into a two-floor 'hideaway' izakaya, and as of 2025 the shop was marking roughly 25 years in business.
chef_bio.philosophy: Japanese home-style dishes reworked with the kitchen's own original arrangements, built around fresh fish and quality-sourced ingredients (vegetables, fish and meat delivered directly from Kawada Farm), served in a quiet old-house setting meant to feel like a true neighborhood hideaway rather than a tourist-facing izakaya.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh sashimi moriawase (assorted raw fish platter) and roast Miyazaki 'Ozaki' wagyu beef

anecdotes.text: The building was originally a Western-style tailor's shop before being converted into the current izakaya.
Iwao is the second restaurant of Asia Kitchen Co., opened in July 2000, following the group's first shop Naru (1999).
A black-vinegar (kurozu) cocktail is a particular favorite among women who visit the bar.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a low-profile, locals'-favorite hideaway (the entrance is easy to miss) with a Japanese-language menu and no signs of English service, so non-Japanese-speaking diners should expect to lean on a translation app or a Japanese-speaking companion.

places.name: つけそば 神田 勝本 (Tsukesoba Kanda Katsumoto)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are wheat-based (a blend of two wheat-noodle types is the shop's signature), and the tare/broth is soy-sauce (shoyu) based, per the menu name 清湯（しょうゆ）つけそば. No gluten-free noodle substitution, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's only broth is fish- and chicken-based (niboshi, other dried fish, and multiple chicken varieties including Nagoya Cochin for flavor adjustment), with wheat noodles as the base product. No vegetable broth, plant-based, or vegan option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 10:00-18:00, Sun 10:00-17:00, or until the broth sells out for the day; Monday closures are irregular (不定休) — check the shop's social media (@kanda_katsumoto) before visiting. Sunday service was only added starting December 21, 2025 (previously closed Sundays).
places.neighborhood: Jimbocho / Kanda Sarugakucho (神保町/神田猿楽町)
places.notes: Kanda Katsumoto is the second shop opened by chef Matsumura Yasushi, who spent 36 years working in French cuisine, including a stint as executive chef at the former ANA Crown Plaza Hotel Kyoto, before shifting his career to ramen so he could serve carefully made food at an access · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Kanda Katsumoto is the second shop opened by chef Matsumura Yasushi, who spent 36 years working in French cuisine, including a stint as executive chef at the former ANA Crown Plaza Hotel Kyoto, before shifting his career to ramen so he could serve carefully made food at an accessible price. He first opened Chukasoba Katsumoto in Suidobashi in 2015, then Kanda Katsumoto in nearby Jimbocho, and later Ginza Hachigo, all under what is now known as the Katsumoto Group.
chef_bio.philosophy: Matsumura applies decades of French fine-dining technique — layered stocks, precise seasoning — to a bowl of noodles he keeps affordably priced, wanting more people to be able to enjoy carefully crafted food.
chef_bio.specialty: Kiyoshiru tsukesoba (清湯つけそば) — a clear niboshi-and-chicken dipping broth served alongside two kinds of noodles, thin and flat, blended together in one bowl.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's operating company went bankrupt in March 2022 amid pandemic-era losses, but all three Katsumoto Group shops, including Kanda Katsumoto, reopened within about two months under a new operator, Food Operation Japan Co.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth's tare is a shoyu (soy sauce) base, per the menu item 清湯（しょうゆ）つけそば, served with wheat noodles; no gluten-free noodle alternative is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is built from niboshi (dried sardines), other dried-fish stocks, and chicken (including Nagoya Cochin), with no vegetable-only broth option documented.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine at a small counter with no English menu reported, though the Katsumoto Group's official website has an English-language page.

places.name: つけ麺・らーめん 波海拉 (Namiura (Tsukemen & Ramen))
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-based ramen and tsukemen noodles; broth and soup base rely on soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature tsukemen and ramen broths are pork-bone (tonkotsu) blended with seafood/dried-fish (gyokai) stock — no vegan or vegetarian broth alternative is mentioned anywhere. No plant-based menu items or accommodations were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00～22:00 (daily; 定休日: 不定休)
places.neighborhood: 新宿 (Shinjuku)
places.notes: Opened July 30, 2025 on the 8th floor of the Shinjuku Youth Building (新宿ユースビル PAX), about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's south/southeast exit. The shop is run by 株式会社トータルウェル (Total Well Corporation), a restaurant-development company, under an official licensing agreement · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: operator: 株式会社トータルウェル (Total Well Corporation)
chef_bio.background: Opened July 30, 2025 on the 8th floor of the Shinjuku Youth Building (新宿ユースビル PAX), about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's south/southeast exit. The shop is run by 株式会社トータルウェル (Total Well Corporation), a restaurant-development company, under an official licensing agreement with 舎鈴 (Sharin) — the sister brand of the celebrated Tokyo Station tsukemen shop 六厘舎 (Rokurinsha) — allowing it to use Rokurinsha-lineage recipes and techniques.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 波海拉 (Namiura) draws on Katsushika Hokusai's ukiyo-e print 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa' from his Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji series, evoking a 'wave' of concentrated broth flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Tsukemen with a pork-bone and seafood (gyokai) blended dipping broth, served in a 'separate plate' style with chashu, menma, ajitama (seasoned egg), and nori plated apart from the noodles; also a dried-sardine-scented dark-soy-sauce ramen with curly noodles. Core menu: Tsukemen Iki, Tantan Tsukemen Homura, and Ramen Shinobi.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was inspired by Hokusai's 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa.'
Namiura operates under a licensing agreement with 舎鈴, sister restaurant of the famous Tokyo Station tsukemen shop 六厘舎 (Rokurinsha), using its recipes and methods.
Ingredients (chashu, menma, ajitama, nori) are served on a separate plate rather than atop the noodles, a signature presentation touch.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is built on a dark soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) base with a dried-sardine aroma; standard shoyu contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood (gyokai/dried-fish) blended stock used for both the tsukemen dipping sauce and the ramen soup — not vegan or vegetarian.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard Japanese-language neighborhood ramen/tsukemen shop near Shinjuku Station; no confirmed English menu, English signage, or English-speaking-staff reports found.

places.name: WE ARE THE FARM 渋谷 (We Are The Farm Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant has published its own blog post on enjoying gluten-free dining at this Shibuya location and offers a limited-quantity gluten-free (also vegan, low-sugar) tart from tart artist Akira Yamamoto. However, no source confirms the full menu is wheat-free, that soy sauce used is wheat-free, or that a dedicated gluten-free fryer/prep area exists — the vegetable-forward menu likely has some naturally GF dishes but cross-contamination and hidden-wheat risk (e.g., in sauces, tempura-style items) should be confirmed directly with staff, especially for celiac-level strictness.
places.vegan_detail: A vegan course exists but reportedly should be reserved in advance, since the regular menu features many animal-derived dishes. One signature hot-pot item mixes vegetable-scrap broth with Japanese dashi, which typically contains bonito unless a plant-based version is requested — diners wanting a strictly vegan meal should specifically order the vegan course and confirm dashi/bonito exclusions rather than order freely from the general menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun & holidays: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:00-23:30 (food L.O. 22:00, drinks L.O. 23:00); closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: 松濤・神泉 (Shoto / Shinsen), Shibuya
places.notes: WE ARE THE FARM Shibuya (formerly STAND BY FARM Shoto) is a "farm-to-table" vegetable bar run by a group that also operates its own organic farm in Sakura, Chiba. The restaurant cultivates roughly 150 heirloom ("koteishu"/fixed-seed) vegetable varieties without pesticides or chem · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Own farm in Sakura City, Chiba Prefecture
chef_bio.background: WE ARE THE FARM Shibuya (formerly STAND BY FARM Shoto) is a "farm-to-table" vegetable bar run by a group that also operates its own organic farm in Sakura, Chiba. The restaurant cultivates roughly 150 heirloom ("koteishu"/fixed-seed) vegetable varieties without pesticides or chemical fertilizers, and vegetables are said to be harvested each morning and brought straight to the Shibuya restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept centers on showcasing rare, disappearing heirloom vegetable varieties that farmers have preserved for generations but that have fallen out of commercial cultivation due to difficult growing conditions and short shelf life, paired with a low-waste ethos.
chef_bio.specialty: Field-style teppanyaki bagna cauda (畑の鉄板焼きバーニャカウダ) — a hot-plate bagna cauda piled with freshly harvested heirloom vegetables and a house-made dip

anecdotes.text: Their Antioxidant Hot Pot Course uses a "veggie broth" made from vegetable peels and scraps combined with Japanese dashi, filled with seasonal organic vegetables, as a way of using the whole vegetable rather than discarding trimmings.
anecdotes.source: Web search aggregation of official/restaurant descriptions

anecdotes.text: The restaurant offers a limited-quantity gluten-free, vegan, and low-sugar tart from tart artist Akira Yamamoto, and has published guidance for gluten-free dining at the Shibuya location.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Restaurant offers only a limited-quantity gluten-free/vegan/low-sugar tart as a specifically labeled GF item; no statement found that the broader menu, sauces, or fryer are gluten-free or cross-contamination controlled.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Regular menu contains many animal-based items, so the vegan course is recommended to be reserved in advance rather than ordered ad hoc from the standard menu.
vegan_cross_contact.source: Web search summary of official site / restaurant guides

vegan_cross_contact.text: A hot-pot dish combines a vegetable-scrap "veggie broth" with Japanese dashi, which commonly includes bonito (katsuobushi) unless a plant-based dashi is specifically requested.
vegan_cross_contact.source: Web search summary of official site description

positives.text: All vegetables are grown on the group's own organic farm in Sakura, Chiba, without pesticides or chemical fertilizers, and are marketed as harvested and delivered fresh each morning.
positives.source: Web search summary of official site / restaurant guides

cultural_comfort.note: The official site publishes an English-language version and offers 24-hour online booking, suggesting some accommodation for non-Japanese-speaking guests, though on-site English fluency of staff is unconfirmed.

places.name: ソウルフードインディア 水道橋店 (Soul Food India Suidōbashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or explicit allergy protocol is advertised. Indian-style curries are typically thickened with onion/spice bases rather than wheat roux, so a rice-based curry (skipping the naan, which clearly contains wheat and is baked in the same tandoor as other breads) may be workable, but this has not been confirmed by the restaurant and should be checked with staff, especially regarding shared prep surfaces and any wheat-containing thickeners or pre-made sauces.
places.vegan_detail: A meatless vegetable curry is listed on the regular menu, so a vegetarian meal is straightforward, but full vegan status is not confirmed: North Indian curries at shops like this are often finished with ghee (clarified butter) or a dab of cream/yogurt, and naan dough commonly includes dairy or yogurt, so vegan diners should confirm with staff whether the vegetable curry and any bread can be made without dairy.
places.hours_raw: 11:00～22:30（料理L.O. 22:00、ドリンクL.O. 22:00）、年中無休（年末年始を除く）
places.neighborhood: 本郷／水道橋 (Bunkyō-ku Hongō, 1 min walk from Suidōbashi Station)
places.notes: Chef Rachman trained at a high-end hotel in Mumbai before coming to Japan, and has been the head chef at this Suidōbashi shop for roughly a decade. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Mumbai, India
chef_bio.background: Chef Rachman trained at a high-end hotel in Mumbai before coming to Japan, and has been the head chef at this Suidōbashi shop for roughly a decade.
chef_bio.philosophy: Home-style North Indian cooking made accessible and affordable near the station, with charcoal-fired tandoor naan as the signature touch.
chef_bio.specialty: North Indian-style curry with rich, deeply spiced gravies, served with naan baked in a traditional Indian clay tandoor over charcoal.

anecdotes.text: Main chef Rachman honed his skills at a luxury hotel in Mumbai and has run the kitchen at this shop for about 10 years.
All naan is baked to order in an Indian clay tandoor fired with charcoal, giving it a distinctive smoky aroma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No specific gluten cross-contamination statement was found; naan and other wheat items are prepared in the same kitchen and tandoor as curries.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No specific vegan cross-contact statement found; curries and naan may be finished with ghee, butter, or yogurt-based dough unless requested otherwise.

positives.text: A vegetable curry (ベジタブルカレー, mild) is offered as a standing option on both the lunch and dinner menus, served with naan or rice.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, station-front curry shop with an Indian chef and a simple photo/English-friendly menu; basic Japanese or English food words are enough to order comfortably.

places.name: スタイルズケイクス＆カンパニー (Style's Cakes & Co.)
places.gf_detail: The regular tart and pie lineup uses standard wheat-flour pastry crusts. Separately, the shop sells a wheat-free 米粉の焼きドーナツ (baked rice-flour donut, ~¥250) mentioned in blog/menu coverage — but no source describes a dedicated gluten-free process, separate equipment, or cross-contamination controls, and it is baked in the same small kitchen as the wheat-based tarts. Celiac diners should ask directly about cross-contact before relying on this item.
places.vegan_detail: This is a butter-and-cream French-style tart/pie shop (fruit tarts, mont blanc, banana cream tart, fig clafoutis) — dairy and eggs are core to nearly every item, and no vegan-adapted recipes or vegan claims were found.
places.hours_raw: Reported as approximately 12:00-19:00 Monday-Friday and 12:00-17:00 Saturday, closed Sundays and public holidays; frequently sells out and closes early. Same-day telephone hold requests (取り置き) are taken roughly 9:00-10:30. Sources disagree somewhat on exact weekday closures (a few mention additional irregular closures), so hours should be treated as approximate.
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Ogawamachi, near Jimbōchō Station (Chiyoda-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The couple ran a café in the Kanda neighborhood for about 15 years and also did food-related publishing work before closing the café. They then opened Style's Cakes & Co. as a scaled-down, tart-focused shop suited to their own pace of life. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: 岩崎修 (Iwasaki Osamu) and his wife 百合子 (Yuriko)
chef_bio.origin: Kanda, Tokyo (both are Kanda locals)
chef_bio.background: The couple ran a café in the Kanda neighborhood for about 15 years and also did food-related publishing work before closing the café. They then opened Style's Cakes & Co. as a scaled-down, tart-focused shop suited to their own pace of life.
chef_bio.philosophy: To make handmade tarts "身の丈にあった形で" (in a form that fits their own scale/means) rather than running a larger café operation.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit tarts with a thick, crunchy, well-browned crust, especially the seasonal fresh fruit tart and the French chestnut mont blanc tart; also known for a banana cream tart and a fig clafoutis pie.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the tart crust as thick, crunchy and fragrant with very fresh fruit, and note that popular flavors like the mont blanc tart routinely sell out via the morning telephone hold line.
A blogger described the shop as delivering a sense of happiness/emotion through its tarts, listing the fresh fruit tart, mont blanc, and other seasonal offerings.

positives.text: Alongside its wheat-flour tarts, the shop offers a baked rice-flour donut (米粉の焼きドーナツ) as a wheat-free option on some days.

cultural_comfort.note: Same-day items are announced overnight on the shop's Japanese-language Facebook page and popular tarts sell out via a Japanese-only morning phone hold line, so visiting without Japanese help or a big appetite for uncertainty is risky.

places.name: 獅天鶏飯 (Shiten Keihan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. The signature Hainanese chicken rice is typically served with soy-sauce-based dips/sauces (dark and light soy sauce both contain wheat), and the kitchen also does Chinese-style frying and stir-frying, making cross-contact likely. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten-free needs without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around chicken (chicken-fat-cooked rice, chicken broth) and pork (bak kut teh) dishes typical of Singaporean-Chinese cuisine; no vegan menu, plant-based substitutions, or mention of avoiding fish sauce/oyster sauce/dashi was found in any source. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners based on available information.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷
places.cuisine: シンガポール料理・海南鶏飯
places.notes: Owner-chef Kazusei Sato first encountered Singaporean food as a university student, backpacking mainly through Southeast Asia as well as Europe and Africa. After graduating he took a sales job but left after about a year, then worked across a range of restaurant genres — Italian, · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (originally opened in Uchisaiwaicho/Shimbashi in July 2021)
chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Kazusei Sato first encountered Singaporean food as a university student, backpacking mainly through Southeast Asia as well as Europe and Africa. After graduating he took a sales job but left after about a year, then worked across a range of restaurant genres — Italian, a bakery, a teishoku diner, izakaya, and a yakitori shop — to build hands-on cooking experience. He opened his first Singaporean-Chinese restaurant in Shimbashi/Uchisaiwaicho in July 2021, then relocated it to its current Shibuya site (a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station) on April 7, 2022. A second, smaller location called 獅天鶏飯ハナレ opened nearby in spring 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sato aims to convey Singapore's multi-ethnic food culture through spicing (cumin, fish sauce/nampla) alongside Chinese-style fried and stir-fried dishes, and leans on Shibuya's location by buying vegetables from producer markets to fold Japanese seasonal produce into the small-plate menu.
chef_bio.specialty: 究極の海南鶏飯 (Hainanese chicken rice) — low-temperature-cooked chicken breast served with jasmine rice cooked in chicken fat/stock, with complimentary rice refills.

anecdotes.text: 店長で調理担当の佐藤一聖さんが海南鶏飯に対する強い思い入れを持ち、シンガポール料理店での経験をベースに立ち上げた。大学生の頃バックパッカーとして東南アジアを中心に世界を巡る中でシンガポール料理に出会った。
新橋発のシンガポール料理店として1年足らずでオープンから渋谷へ移転し、人気店となった。

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found in sources; expect a Japanese-language, locals-oriented izakaya-style experience near Shibuya Station.

places.name: かのや 新宿西口店 (Kanoya Shinjuku Nishiguchi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free option or accommodation is advertised anywhere for this shop. It is a standard tachigui soba/udon counter: the udon is 100% wheat, the soba is a blended-flour soba (not guaranteed juwari/100%-buckwheat), and the tsuyu (dipping broth) is a classic katsuobushi-and-shoyu based sauce, so wheat is present via the soy sauce as well as the noodles themselves. Tempura items (shrimp tempura, kakiage) are almost certainly fried in a shared fryer used for all breaded items. This is not a suitable choice for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling was found. As a traditional soba/udon tachigui shop, the standard tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito) based, and tempura toppings include shrimp and other non-vegan items. There is no indication of a kombu-only or plant-based dashi option, so vegan diners should assume hidden fish stock in the broth even for a plain zaru/mori order.
places.neighborhood: 新宿西口 (Shinjuku Nishiguchi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kanoya (かのや) is a small operator of stand-and-eat (tachigui) noodle counters clustered around Shinjuku — the Nishiguchi (west exit) and Tonan-guchi (south-east exit) shops — plus a Shimbashi location, all making their own fresh soba and thick Sanuki-style udon in house rather tha · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Kanoya (かのや) is a small operator of stand-and-eat (tachigui) noodle counters clustered around Shinjuku — the Nishiguchi (west exit) and Tonan-guchi (south-east exit) shops — plus a Shimbashi location, all making their own fresh soba and thick Sanuki-style udon in house rather than buying pre-made noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop sources its flours deliberately for each noodle: buckwheat for the soba and Hokkaido wheat for the multi-hydration Sanuki-style udon. Soba is served in a dark Kanto-style broth and udon in a lighter Kansai-style broth as the default pairing, but customers can ask staff to swap the broth style.
chef_bio.specialty: Tempura udon — a thick hand-made Sanuki-style udon topped with a large shrimp tempura — is the counter's most recommended order; kakiage soba, made with a sweet-onion kakiage, is another regular favorite.

anecdotes.text: Kanoya runs a handful of self-made-noodle tachigui soba shops around Shinjuku (Nishiguchi and Tonan-guchi) and Shimbashi, each turning out its own fresh soba and thick udon.
Soba is standard with a Kanto-style (dark) broth and udon with a Kansai-style (light) broth, but the broth style can be requested to change.
The shop's most recommended dish is the tempura udon with a generously sized shrimp tempura on top; the kakiage udon, with its sweet-onion kakiage, is also popular.

cultural_comfort.note: Order via a Japanese-only ticket machine (食券機) and hand the ticket to counter staff — no English menu was found, so a little Japanese (or menu photos) helps.

places.name: 炙り味噌らーめん 麺匠 真武咲弥 渋谷店 (Aburi Miso Ramen Mensho Shinbu Sakiya (Shibu)
places.gf_detail: Offers a dedicated gluten-free menu built on brown-rice (genmai) noodles sourced from a pesticide/chemical-fertilizer-free producer (Miyauchi-sha) in Unnan, Shimane -- e.g. "Aburi GF Genmai Noodle" ramen (from roughly ¥1,250-1,600 depending on flavor). The shop also developed a gluten-free karaage (fried chicken) and places wheat-free soy sauce at every table specifically as an allergy accommodation. However, this is a shared kitchen that also cooks standard wheat-noodle ramen and other wheat-containing items, and no source confirms a fryer used exclusively for gluten-free items, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out for celiac-level sensitivity -- ask staff about current allergy protocols.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated vegan ramen line is offered in four flavors -- miso, spicy miso (kara-miso), shoyu, and shio -- at a uniform price (around ¥1,250, or about ¥1,550 with the gluten-free brown-rice noodle upgrade). This sits alongside the shop's standard, non-vegan menu (which includes pork and other animal-derived ingredients), so it is not an all-vegan restaurant -- diners must specifically order from the vegan menu. No source directly confirmed the vegan broth's full ingredient list (e.g. whether it is purely kombu/vegetable-based with zero risk of shared-broth cross-contact), so very strict vegans should ask staff to confirm preparation.
places.hours_raw: Sources differ slightly; NAVITIME lists: Mon 11:00-翌2:30, Tue/Wed/Thu/Sun 11:00-翌4:30, Fri/Sat 11:00-翌6:30. Other aggregators give slightly different late-night cutoffs (e.g. Sun-Thu to 翌25:00/翌4:00-ish, Fri-Sat/pre-holiday to 翌7:00). Closed days reported as 不定休 (irregular).
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka 2-chome, Shibuya (about 5 minutes' walk from Shibu
places.notes: Owner/company president Okumura Munehiro developed a wheat allergy himself, which pushed him to create ramen that anyone could eat 'regardless of religion, constitution, or dietary belief' -- leading to the shop's gluten-free brown-rice noodle and vegan ramen lines alongside the  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Sapporo, Hokkaido (the shop's miso-ramen style and secret tare originate from Sapporo; this is the Shibuya branch of the Samurai Noodle company)
chef_bio.background: Owner/company president Okumura Munehiro developed a wheat allergy himself, which pushed him to create ramen that anyone could eat 'regardless of religion, constitution, or dietary belief' -- leading to the shop's gluten-free brown-rice noodle and vegan ramen lines alongside the standard menu. An earlier profile of the company also recounts Okumura rebuilding from an approximately 8-million-yen bankruptcy, working as a truck driver to save capital before eventually opening the ramen business that grew into today's shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Making a bowl of ramen that anyone can eat, whatever their religion, allergy, or food philosophy -- the stated reason for offering dedicated gluten-free brown-rice noodles and a full vegan ramen line alongside the standard wheat-noodle, non-vegan menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Aburi Miso Ramen (炙り味噌ラーメン) -- a secret miso tare blended from thirteen ingredients, seared (aburi) over high heat, then combined with a clear (chintan) soup for a charred, aromatic, umami-rich bowl in a Sapporo-style stir-fried-vegetable tradition.

anecdotes.text: 奥村社長は「生まれて初めてラーメンを食べられて、感動したと言ってくれる人もいる。多くの人に届けばうれしい」と話している (Okumura has said some customers tell him it was the first time in their life they were able to eat ramen at all, and were moved -- he hopes the food reaches more people like them).

Company/founder background piece describing Okumura's roughly 8-million-yen bankruptcy, his period working as a truck driver to rebuild capital, and the eventual founding of the Samurai Noodle aburi-miso ramen business.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source confirms separate cooking equipment/water for gluten-free noodles versus the shop's standard wheat noodles; the kitchen prepares both on the same menu.

When you buy the genmai-noodle ticket, staff confirm the allergy at the entrance and explain: because the same kitchen handles regular (wheat) ramen and soy sauce, trace contamination is unavoidable — are you OK with that? (同じ厨房で通常のラーメン（小麦麺）や醤油を扱っているので、微量のコンタミネーションは避けられませんが大丈夫ですかという確認があります)

Reviewer: 'not safe for celiac or gluten intolerant... everything is contaminated with the other ingredients if you are following a strict diet.' At least one celiac reported not feeling well after eating; other celiacs report no reaction — experiences are mixed.

FindMeGlutenFree listing summary indicates the kitchen handles wheat products with shared equipment/prep areas so cross-contact may occur, and that the restaurant cannot fully guarantee accommodation for celiac disease / severe wheat allergy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 各席に小麦不使用のしょうゆを置くなどアレルギーに配慮 (Wheat-free soy sauce is placed at every table as an allergy accommodation).

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owner developed the gluten-free noodle, gluten-free karaage, and vegan ramen lines specifically so customers with wheat allergies or dietary restrictions could eat there; some customers have told him it was the first ramen they were ever able to eat.

positives.text: Dedicated gluten-free brown-rice (genmai) noodle option made from noodles sourced from a pesticide/chemical-fertilizer-free producer in Shimane Prefecture; a blogger specializing in gluten-free living in Japan wrote it up as a standout GF ramen find in Shibuya.
Four flavors of dedicated vegan ramen (miso, spicy miso, shoyu, shio) at a uniform price, documented by a vegan-focused dining blog.

cultural_comfort.note: A food-ticket vending-machine ramen shop typical of Japan, but the operator publishes an English-language site and the shop is well known among Tokyo's gluten-free/vegan community, so basic English or pointing at photos should get you through ordering.

places.name: 神田神保町 天麩羅 はちまき (Kanda-Jimbocho Tempura Hachimaki)
places.gf_detail: All tempura here is prepared with a traditional wheat-flour batter, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is a classic soy-sauce-based sauce with bonito/kelp dashi - not wheat-free. No source (official site, Tabelog, or food blogs) mentions a gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free tare. This is not a safe choice for a celiac diner without extensive advance discussion with staff, and likely not accommodable at all given the shop's traditional, unchanged recipe.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dishes center on anago (conger eel) and ebi (shrimp) tempura, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is built on bonito (katsuo) and kelp dashi. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, plant-based tare, or kombu-only dashi option are mentioned in any source - this is a seafood-centered tempura restaurant with no documented vegan pathway.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町（すずらん通り沿い）/ Jimbocho, Chiyoda City
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in Showa 6 (1931) by Aoki Torakichi, who had trained at the kappo restaurant "Uoyasu" in Kanda. When Uoyasu closed following the Great Kanto Earthquake, Aoki went independent and opened his own tempura shop near Kanda Station. That original shop burned down in the Tokyo a · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded near Kanda Station, Chiyoda City, Tokyo; relocated to its current Jimbocho (Suzuran-dori) location in 1945 after the original shop was destroyed in WWII air raids
chef_bio.background: Founded in Showa 6 (1931) by Aoki Torakichi, who had trained at the kappo restaurant "Uoyasu" in Kanda. When Uoyasu closed following the Great Kanto Earthquake, Aoki went independent and opened his own tempura shop near Kanda Station. That original shop burned down in the Tokyo air raids of WWII, and from 1945 the restaurant has operated continuously at its present site on Suzuran-dori in Jimbocho.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name, "Hachimaki" (headband), comes from words the founder's mentor at Uoyasu gave him when he went independent: that he must work his whole life with a hachimaki tied on, forever training and studying his craft - a philosophy of lifelong dedication that the restaurant has kept as its guiding ethos for over 90 years.
chef_bio.specialty: 穴子海老天丼 (Anago-Ebi Tendon) - a rice bowl topped with two pieces of shrimp tempura, one whole conger eel (anago) tempura, and two pieces of seasonal vegetable tempura, served with miso soup and dressed in a long-simmered tare sauce built on bonito and kelp dashi.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was a favorite haunt of Showa-era literary and stage giants, including mystery writer Edogawa Ranpo, novelist Ibuse Masuji, playwright Hojo Hideji, and actor Sano Shuji, who all frequented the shop.
Edogawa Ranpo was especially fond of the anago-ebi tendon, the dish credited with hooking generations of regulars and still the restaurant's signature order today.

cultural_comfort.note: A 90+ year old, mostly Japanese-language neighborhood tempura counter near Jimbocho Station; there is no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so celiac and vegan diners should be prepared to explain dietary needs in Japanese or with a translation card.

places.name: タナゴコロ (Tanagokoro)
places.gf_detail: No allergen or gluten-free information is published by the shop. Plain rice and nori are naturally gluten-free, but common onigiri/onigirazu fillings and glazes in Japan (soy sauce, tsukudani, teriyaki-style sauces) typically contain wheat, and this shop's specific recipes, sauce ingredients, and cross-contamination practices were not documented in any source found. Diners must ask staff directly about individual filling ingredients before ordering; no dedicated gluten-free process or fryer was confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or options are advertised. Publicly visible fillings/products referenced in listings and social media include eel (unagi), spam, and tuna, indicating the shop's offerings are not vegan by default. No plant-based onigiri/onigirazu options were confirmed in any source, and typical Japanese seasonings may contain hidden dashi (bonito) even in vegetable-appearing fillings. Diners must ask staff directly.
places.hours_raw: 7:30〜16:00頃（売り切れ次第終了）、定休日：火曜・水曜
places.neighborhood: Asakusa (Hanakawado), Taito-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] No allergen or gluten-free information is published by the shop. Plain rice and nori are naturally gluten-free, but common onigiri/onigirazu fillings and glazes in Japan (soy sauce, tsukudani, teriyaki-style sauces) typically contain wheat, and this shop's specific recipes, sauce · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.specialty: Onigirazu (hand-formed, mold-free rice sandwiches) and onigiri — the shop is a small specialty stand dedicated entirely to these two rice-ball formats.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood takeout stand with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff; bring a translation app or point at photos.

places.name: 麺屋ぬかじ (Menya Nukaji)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are made from wheat flour (Hokkaido-produced) and the broth is a conventional pork-bone and bonito/mackerel/dried-sardine seafood stock finished with soy sauce (shoyu) — standard wheat-based tsukemen. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergy menu was found in any source; this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Both ramen and tsukemen broths are built on pork bone plus bonito/mackerel/dried-sardine fish stock (katsuobushi, sababushi, niboshi), and the free egg/rice service is not vegan either. No vegan broth, noodle, or dish substitution was found in any source — not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri: lunch 11:00–16:00, dinner 18:00–21:00 (some listings show 18:00–22:00); Sat/holidays: 11:00–17:00 (lunch only, no dinner service); closed Sundays. May close early ("売り切れ次第終了") if the broth sells out.
places.neighborhood: Udagawa-cho, Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Nukui Hiroshi spent roughly ten years training at ramen shops around Tokyo before going independent, opening his first shop in Setagaya's Kamimachi neighborhood in July 2010. He relocated to the current Udagawa-cho, Shibuya location in November 2014, where he now runs the s · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Owner Nukui Hiroshi spent roughly ten years training at ramen shops around Tokyo before going independent, opening his first shop in Setagaya's Kamimachi neighborhood in July 2010. He relocated to the current Udagawa-cho, Shibuya location in November 2014, where he now runs the small counter-only shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Aims for a thick, deeply savory dipping broth that is intentionally restrained on sweetness and acidity, so it works even for diners who dislike sweeter or sharper tsukemen styles.
chef_bio.specialty: Tsukemen: chewy medium-thick noodles made with Hokkaido wheat flour and Okinawan salt, served with a rich dipping broth that blends pork-bone stock with a cold-brewed bonito, mackerel, and dried-sardine seafood stock, rested overnight and reduced for concentrated depth.

anecdotes.text: Rice and a raw egg are offered free with meals, letting diners make tamago-kake-gohan (egg over rice) or stir the egg into their noodles or broth as they like.
The shop also serves craft beer, positioning itself as a relaxed 'ra-nomi' (ramen + drinking) spot to pair the rich tsukemen with a beer.
At lunchtime the shop has offered a limited Jiro-inspired ramen known as 'Nukaji-ro' as a special item.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsukemen noodles are made with wheat flour and the dipping broth is a conventional soy-sauce-seasoned pork-bone and seafood stock — no gluten-free noodle or broth alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dipping broth combines pork-bone stock with bonito, mackerel, and dried-sardine (niboshi) fish stock, so both the tsukemen and ramen broths are animal-based with no vegan version noted.

cultural_comfort.note: Japanese-language, cash-only counter shop with no confirmed English menu or signage — bring a translation app or be ready to point at photos.

places.name: カンパイ テラス (Kanpai Terrace)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, gluten-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found on any source. The menu is built around wheat-based dishes (carbonara and other cream pastas, pizza, croque monsieur, bread-based breakfast items), so a celiac or strictly gluten-free diner should not assume safety and must ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan labeling was found. Signature items are dairy- and meat-forward (cream-based carbonara with cheese, eggs benedict, meat platters like Angus beef tagliata), and hidden animal-derived ingredients (butter, cream, cheese, egg, possibly anchovy/dashi in sauces) should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise. Plant-based diners should ask about substitutions before ordering.
places.hours_raw: BREAKFAST 7:00-12:00 (L.O. 11:30) / LUNCH 11:30-14:00 / CAFE TIME 14:00-17:00 / BAR & DINNER 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00); overall open 7:00-23:00 daily, no closed days
places.neighborhood: Suidobashi, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: KANPAI TERRACE is the all-day dining restaurant on the ground floor of sequence SUIDOBASHI, a lifestyle hotel run by Mitsui Fudosan Hotel Management, one minute from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit. It opened alongside the hotel and underwent a full renovation of its dining roo · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: KANPAI TERRACE is the all-day dining restaurant on the ground floor of sequence SUIDOBASHI, a lifestyle hotel run by Mitsui Fudosan Hotel Management, one minute from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit. It opened alongside the hotel and underwent a full renovation of its dining room and riverside terrace, reopening on March 1, 2024, with a further refresh of the terrace zone (a daytime co-working terrace that turns into an evening beer garden) in mid-2025.
chef_bio.specialty: Truffle and pancetta carbonara finished tableside with a giant wheel of cheese

anecdotes.text: The terrace, which runs along the Kanda River, was redesigned with bench seating, tables, and a green-roofed area, and relaunched with a special reopening course menu in March 2024.
In 2025 the terrace was reworked again to function as a 'working terrace' during the day and a beer garden in the evening, with the lunch menu also renewed.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside an international lifestyle hotel a minute from Suidobashi Station, with an English-language Tabelog listing, suggesting reasonable comfort for non-Japanese-speaking guests, though this was not independently confirmed via direct menu access.

places.name: サラダボウル専門店 ウィズグリーン 新宿三丁目店 (With Green Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer, or allergy chart was found for this location. Bowls are built to order from raw/grilled vegetables, but pre-made dressings, sauces, and any breaded or soy-sauce-based toppings could contain wheat; celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should ask staff to check individual dressing and sauce ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Diners can build a vegetable-only bowl themselves by choosing toppings, and at least one dressing (a lemon-based dressing) is marked as vegetarian/vegan-compatible. However, most standard menu bowls (e.g. Spicy Chicken Cobb Salad, sesame chicken salad) are built around chicken, and whether other dressings/sauces contain dashi, bonito, or other animal-derived ingredients is not disclosed anywhere found — vegan diners should confirm sauce and dressing ingredients with staff before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00〜20:00（年中無休、年末年始を除く）
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目
places.notes: WithGreen is a made-to-order salad bowl chain that has grown from its 2016 Tokyo debut to roughly 40+ locations nationwide, including this Shinjuku Sanchome branch a one-minute walk from the station's A2 exit. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Salad bowl specialty chain, Shinjuku Sanchome branch
chef_bio.origin: WithGreen was founded in Tokyo in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi, on the idea that a salad bowl could be a full meal on its own rather than a side dish.
chef_bio.background: WithGreen is a made-to-order salad bowl chain that has grown from its 2016 Tokyo debut to roughly 40+ locations nationwide, including this Shinjuku Sanchome branch a one-minute walk from the station's A2 exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's guiding idea is that a salad should be substantial enough to be your main meal ("主食になるサラダボウル"), built around domestically grown, in-season produce rather than imported filler.
chef_bio.specialty: The Original Salad — a build-your-own bowl where diners pick from whichever vegetables are in peak season, sourced from contracted farms across Japan, plus a rotating seasonal salad introduced every two months.

anecdotes.text: Since its founding, aside from exceptional circumstances like typhoons or natural disasters, WithGreen has made a point of using only domestically grown Japanese vegetables, changing its seasonal limited salad every two months to showcase Japan's four seasons.
WithGreen has continued opening new locations across Japan, expanding well past its original Tokyo shops into cities including Nagoya and Osaka.

positives.text: The shop offers a vegetarian/vegan-compatible dressing option (e.g. a lemon-based dressing), and diners can build a fully vegetable bowl by choosing their own toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast-casual, order-at-counter salad shop where you point to and select ingredients, so basic Japanese or pointing at the visual menu is enough even without fluent Japanese.

places.name: そば処 信州屋 渋谷店 (Shinshuya (Sobadokoro Shinshuya) Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No source indicates this shop offers gluten-free accommodation. The soba is not advertised as 十割 (100% buckwheat); as a standard machine/hand-made soba counter it should be assumed to use a wheat-flour blend in the noodles. The tempura is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter, and traditional soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is soy-sauce based and typically contains wheat. No mention of a dedicated gluten-free fryer, GF noodles, or allergy menu was found in any reviewed source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, labeling, or accommodation was found. As a traditional soba shop, the tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) should be assumed to use standard katsuobushi (bonito) dashi unless a customer confirms otherwise with staff, and the menu includes egg, fried tofu (kitsune), and other animal-derived items alongside soba. No source confirms a vegan broth or dedicated vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 平日 7:30〜23:00、土日 9:00〜23:00（一部情報源では日・祝は9:00〜22:00）、無休 — Weekdays 7:30am-11pm, weekends from 9am (closing time reported as either 11pm or 10pm on Sunday/holidays depending on source); open every day.
places.neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (道玄坂, 渋谷) — one minute on foot from Shibu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sobadokoro Shinshuya (そば処 信州屋) opened on Dogenzaka in Shibuya in 1986 as a standing/fast soba-and-tempura counter aimed at busy office workers a minute's walk from Shibuya Station. It is operated by a local Tokyo restaurant company with roots going back over a century in neighbor · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nagano (Shinshū) soba tradition, opened in Shibuya's Dogenzaka in 1986
chef_bio.background: Sobadokoro Shinshuya (そば処 信州屋) opened on Dogenzaka in Shibuya in 1986 as a standing/fast soba-and-tempura counter aimed at busy office workers a minute's walk from Shibuya Station. It is operated by a local Tokyo restaurant company with roots going back over a century in neighborhood eateries, and Shinshuya carries on that tradition with a stone-mill-ground Shinshū (Nagano) buckwheat flour milled and made into noodles in house.
chef_bio.philosophy: 打ちたて、茹でたて、揚げたて — an emphasis on serving everything made fresh to order, quickly and at very low prices, for time-pressed commuters near Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly hand-made soba noodles topped with tempura that is fried to order at the counter as customers watch; the shop's motto is 打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて ("freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried"). A popular time-limited morning set (until around 11am) pairs a bowl of mori or kake soba with rice and a raw egg, which regulars mix with the tempura dipping sauce and tenkasu (tempura scraps).

anecdotes.text: The soba is served in hand-painted Arita-yaki porcelain bowls decorated with the traditional 唐子 (karako) pattern, while everything else at the shop is prepared in house.
The shop runs a budget morning set (soba + rice + raw egg) available until around 11am, popular with local office workers who mix the egg into the tempura dipping sauce or over rice with tenkasu.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional standing soba counter where an attendant takes your order verbally (no ticket machine, and menus/interactions are primarily in Japanese), so some Japanese ability or pointing/translation help is useful.

places.name: 六常庵 (Rokujoan)
places.gf_detail: Traditional soba shop; the signature noodle found in menu listings is a coarsely-milled nihachi (二八) soba, which by definition includes wheat flour as a binder (roughly 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) rather than a 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodle. The menu also features tempura (e.g., a shrimp tempura rice bowl), which is wheat-battered and typically fried in shared oil. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was reported in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-izakaya menu built around a bonito (katsuobushi) dashi tradition for the tsuyu dipping sauce, plus shrimp tempura and other seafood dishes. No vegan-specific dishes, dashi substitutions, or plant-based menu options were reported in any source.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidōbashi)
