places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The chef trained at Ryōgoku's renowned soba restaurant Hosokawa, itself a Michelin-listed shop, before opening Rokujoan near Suidōbashi Station in November 2021. Within about a year of opening, Rokujoan itself earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner-chef trained at Ryōgoku's Michelin-listed soba restaurant Hosokawa (ほそ川)
chef_bio.background: The chef trained at Ryōgoku's renowned soba restaurant Hosokawa, itself a Michelin-listed shop, before opening Rokujoan near Suidōbashi Station in November 2021. Within about a year of opening, Rokujoan itself earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing.
chef_bio.specialty: Stone-mill ground, hand-cut soba, including a coarsely-milled nihachi (buckwheat/wheat blend) noodle, alongside a shrimp tempura rice bowl (ebi tendon).

anecdotes.text: One food blogger highlighted a summer dish of cold soba with Tokushima-grown sudachi citrus, myoga, and scallion, describing the citrus's refined acidity working well against the sharp bite of the aromatic vegetables.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu listings show a coarsely-milled nihachi (二八) soba — a wheat/buckwheat blend, not 100%-buckwheat — plus a shrimp tempura rice bowl with a wheat-flour batter, indicating standard shared-kitchen preparation rather than gluten-free handling.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, traditional local soba shop with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app is recommended.

places.name: らーめん 鴨to葱 イイトルミネ新宿店 (Ramen Kamo to Negi - EATo Lumine Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options are indicated by any source. This is a standard ramen shop: wheat noodles are used, and the seasoning tare is very likely soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which typically contains wheat. No mention anywhere of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated fryer, or allergen-safe handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly: the signature broth is made purely from duck, green onion, and water (no vegetable/kombu-only broth offered), so it contains meat-derived stock by design. No plant-based or vegetarian noodle-soup option is mentioned by any source.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), 年中無休 (open daily, no closed days)
places.neighborhood: 新宿駅構内 (JR Shinjuku Station, EATo Lumine B1F) / 新宿区新宿
places.notes: Kamo to Negi built its reputation at its original Okachimachi counter shop, known for lines of roughly 40 customers before opening on weekends, and for rotating the variety/origin of its green onions monthly (diners often choose 2 of 3 types on offer). It has since expanded to se · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The original shop opened in Okachimachi (Ueno), Tokyo, under the JR railway overpass in the Ameyoko area; this Shinjuku branch (inside JR Shinjuku Station's EATo Lumine complex) opened in April 2024 as part of the brand's expansion.
chef_bio.background: Kamo to Negi built its reputation at its original Okachimachi counter shop, known for lines of roughly 40 customers before opening on weekends, and for rotating the variety/origin of its green onions monthly (diners often choose 2 of 3 types on offer). It has since expanded to several Tokyo locations (Shinjuku, Shibuya, Asakusa, Sugamo) and Umeda in Osaka, with the Shinjuku Station branch offering a quick counter-service format for commuters.
chef_bio.philosophy: Founder Amado Masayasu has said: "We make our soup using only duck, green onions, and water. We slowly simmer carefully selected crossbred duck for nearly two days on low heat. The duck's natural umami is extracted, and the green onion adds to the depth of flavor. Since we don't use chemical seasonings (no added MSG), achieving that balance is difficult. "
chef_bio.specialty: 鴨ラーメン (duck ramen) — a clear (清湯) broth made from only three ingredients: crossbred duck, green onion (negi), and water, slow-simmered for nearly two days.

anecdotes.text: On weekends, roughly 40 customers line up outside the original Okachimachi shop before it opens.
The shop's soup is made from just duck, negi, and water, simmered nearly two days, with no chemical seasoning added, per the founder.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast-turnover station counter shop (14 seats) with an official English-language brand page, so basic English/picture menu support is likely, but expect a Japanese-first, ticket-and-counter ramen-shop experience with little English conversation.

places.name: ヴィーガン スシ トウキョウ (Vegan Sushi Tokyo)
places.gf_detail: No source confirms dedicated gluten-free equipment, a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce, or a dedicated fryer at this restaurant. Being 100% vegan removes animal-based risks, but the menu includes items like tempura-style "fried shrimp" and sushi typically finished with soy sauce, which in Japan is usually wheat-based unless specified otherwise — celiac diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan menu — all sushi, sides, and desserts are plant-based, using vegetable and soy/plant-protein substitutes (e.g., mock ikura, vegan egg gunkan, slow-cooked eggplant nigiri) with no fish, meat, dairy, or fish-based dashi/bonito anywhere on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Reported as 18:00–22:00 daily (dinner, reservation-based) at the current Shōtō address since its Dec 1, 2025 opening; some sources also list lunch hours (approx. 11:00–15:30). Treat with caution — see caveats.
places.neighborhood: 神泉 (Shinsen) / 松濤 (Shōtō), Shibuya
places.notes: Opened as Japan's first specialized vegan sushi restaurant. Maeda, who became vegan several years earlier out of concern for animal welfare and the environment, and co-founder Shu Kudo built the restaurant around the idea that visiting vegans should not have to choose between sig · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Kitchen supervised by vegetable-sushi pioneer Akira Tanimizu (per secondary press coverage)
chef_bio.origin: Founded by a small team including Kazue Maeda and Shu Kudo, first opened June 2024 near Shibuya; moved to a permanent home in Shōtō (near Shinsen Station) December 1, 2025
chef_bio.background: Opened as Japan's first specialized vegan sushi restaurant. Maeda, who became vegan several years earlier out of concern for animal welfare and the environment, and co-founder Shu Kudo built the restaurant around the idea that visiting vegans should not have to choose between sightseeing and eating well in Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than imitate seafood, the kitchen leans into vegetables' own umami and sweetness, using ingredients like miso and sesame for depth instead of fish-based dashi or bonito.
chef_bio.specialty: Slow-cooked eggplant nigiri and plant-based gunkan (mock ikura, vegan egg) built entirely from vegetables and plant proteins

anecdotes.text: Co-founder Kazue Maeda became vegan out of concern for animal welfare and the environment; she and fellow founder Shu Kudo say they started the restaurant so that 'vegans can enjoy Japan 100%' instead of having to compromise between sightseeing plans and meals.

During development the team debated whether plant-based sushi should imitate seafood or build its own sensory language, ultimately choosing to amplify vegetables' natural umami and sweetness with miso and sesame rather than mimic fish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen and menu are vegan — no fish, meat, egg, or dairy is used anywhere on the menu, so there is no risk of cross-contact with animal products for vegan diners.

positives.text: Described by reviewers as Japan's first specialized vegan sushi restaurant, built specifically so vegan visitors don't have to compromise on traditional sushi experiences.

cultural_comfort.note: Bookings run through an English-language TableCheck reservation page and the restaurant is regularly covered by English-language Tokyo food/travel blogs, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking visitors to book and dine.

Whether individual sauces, broths, or accompaniments contain hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., dashi, butter, honey) could not be confirmed. Confirm current vegan status with the restaurant before relying on it.

places.name: ディゾン 水道橋（DIXANS） (DIXANS Suidobashi (Dizon))
places.gf_detail: The café's signature item is thick-cut wheat toast, and its cakes come from a conventional pâtisserie (patisserie de bon cœur). No gluten-free bread, dessert, or dedicated allergen-handling protocol was mentioned in any source reviewed (Tabelog, Chiyoda tourism page, cafe blogs). Not appropriate for celiac guests without direct confirmation from staff, and likely not accommodating given the wheat-centric concept.

roles: dessert flagship of the Musashi-Koyama patisserie "patisserie de bon cœur"
chef_bio.origin: Parent pâtisserie based in Musashi-Koyama; this café location sits in Nishi-Kanda, a short walk from Suidōbashi Station.
chef_bio.background: DIXANS (written "10 DIXANS", read ディゾン) is the flagship café of the Musashi-Koyama pâtisserie "patisserie de bon cœur. " It was opened to mark the pâtisserie's 10th anniversary — the name is a play on the French "dix ans," meaning "ten years. "
chef_bio.philosophy: The toast's unusual thickness is a deliberate design choice: when a loaf is halved to share between two guests, both resulting slices come from the center of the loaf, so no one is stuck with the drier end piece.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut toast (roughly 4cm) with a crisp, caramelized crust and a soft, pillowy crumb, served alongside cakes supplied by patisserie de bon cœur such as the Gâteau Pistache Fraise and Mont Blanc.

anecdotes.text: The signature toast was not on the original menu — it was added after the café's team encountered a brown-sugar toast in Kichijōji and later worked with the pastry chefs of patisserie de bon cœur to develop their own version, refining the exact height so that halving a loaf gives every guest an identical, crust-free center cut.

cultural_comfort.note: It's walk-in/first-come seating with a Japanese-language menu of toast, coffee, and cakes — simple to order with basic Japanese or by pointing at the display case; no confirmed English menu or signage.

The restaurant's own specialty list includes 生麩 (namafu), which is wheat gluten itself, plus yuba and obanzai dishes typically prepared with soy sauce and dashi that conventionally contain wheat. No mention of gluten-free menu items, wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, detailed confirmation from staff on-site.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-specific accommodations were found. The restaurant is a meat/fish-serving izakaya at its core (Tabelog categorizes it under Japanese cuisine and meat dishes); its obanzai and yuba dishes are plant-leaning but almost certainly prepared with katsuo/bonito dashi and standard soy sauce per typical izakaya practice, and this was not confirmed or denied by any source. Vegan diners would need to ask staff dish-by-dish.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto-style izakaya concept, Shinjuku, Tokyo (DD Holdings group restaurant)
chef_bio.background: 京町恋しぐれ 新宿 本館 is a large Kyoto-themed izakaya operated by the DD Holdings (Diamond Dining) restaurant group. The interior recreates a Kyoto machiya townscape inside a Shinjuku building, with a simulated river, stone paths, and several private-room styles, aiming to give guests "the feeling of a school trip to Kyoto" two minutes from JR Shinjuku Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant describes its aim as letting guests experience Kyoto's inherited food culture and flavors "with all five senses" without leaving Shinjuku.
chef_bio.specialty: Obanzai (Kyoto home-style vegetable dishes) alongside namafu (wheat gluten cake) and yuba (tofu skin), served with a curated selection of Japanese sake, in Kyoto-townscape-themed private dining rooms.

anecdotes.text: 「京の町屋をモチーフにした店内には斬新な「和」の空間が特徴で、川のせせらぎや石畳など、古都の風情を感じながら...食文化とこだわりの地酒を心ゆくまで味わえます」(The interior, modeled on a Kyoto machiya, features an innovative 'wa' space with the sound of a flowing river and stone paths, letting guests savor the food culture and carefully chosen local sake amid the atmosphere of the old capital.)

「JR新宿駅から徒歩2分で、古き良き京都の町並みを再現した店内で、新宿にいながら、修学旅行で京都を訪れたような気分で楽しめる」(Two minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station, the interior recreates an old Kyoto townscape, letting guests feel as if on a school trip to Kyoto while still in Shinjuku.)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's specialty dish list includes 生麩 (namafu / wheat gluten cake) as a featured Kyoto-style item, indicating wheat gluten is a core ingredient used in the kitchen rather than something avoided.

cultural_comfort.note: Large tourist-accessible chain izakaya with online reservation systems (Hotpepper, Tabelog English page) and a Tabelog English listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or a translation app is helpful.

places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredient labeling, or fryer information was found in any source. As standard Korean home cooking, the kitchen almost certainly uses soy sauce and gochujang (both traditionally wheat-based) and serves breaded/fried items like yangnyeom chicken, all prepared in a shared food-court kitchen with no mention of gluten-free protocols. Cross-contamination risk should be assumed; not suitable to rely on for celiac-level safety without asking staff directly (in Japanese) and getting specific reassurance.
places.vegan_detail: Bibimbap dishes center on housemade namul (seasoned vegetables) and mixed-grain rice, which reads as vegetable-forward, but the broader menu is meat-centric (yangnyeom fried chicken, cheese dakgalbi) and no source confirms a vegan-specific menu or ingredient breakdown. Standard Korean pantry items like kimchi, gochujang, and broths frequently contain fish sauce, anchovy stock, or other animal-derived ingredients, and none of the available sources rule this out dish-by-dish, so a fully vegan order cannot be confirmed without asking staff on site.

roles: Korean home-cooking food-court restaurant, part of the VEGEGO chain
chef_bio.origin: VEGEGO is a Korean home-cooking chain brand operated by Tokyo restaurant company Kichiri Holdings; this branch sits in the food court of RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK, about a two-minute walk from Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.background: The Miyashita Park location is one of roughly 20 VEGEGO branches nationwide. The Korean name for the concept, オヌレシクタン ('today's menu'), reflects the brand's set-meal format built around colorful, vegetable-heavy Korean home cooking rather than a single owner-chef's story.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's tagline is built around the idea that health and beauty come from enjoying delicious food every day, aiming to help diners eat more vegetables through housemade namul and mul-kimchi rather than through restrictive eating.
chef_bio.specialty: VEGEGO special stone-bowl bibimbap topped with six kinds of housemade namul (seasoned vegetable sides).

anecdotes.text: A visitor blogging about the Shibuya branch described the stone-bowl bibimbap and Korean spread as feeling like a quick trip to Seoul.
A Tokyo dining column recommended the branch as a place to enjoy Korean home cooking as an affordable set meal near Shibuya Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source discusses gluten-free cross-contamination practices; the kitchen is a small food-court stall serving fried chicken and standard Korean seasonings alongside everything else, so unconfirmed contact with wheat-based items should be assumed.

positives.text: The signature bibimbap is built on a base of housemade namul (seasoned vegetables) and mixed grains, giving a genuinely vegetable-heavy option on the menu compared with a typical Korean BBQ restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual food-court counter inside RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK with picture-menu ordering, manageable for visitors with minimal Japanese, though no English-language allergy support is confirmed.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is known for offering rare-grilled yakitori, which can only be made with very fresh chicken.
A popular menu item is the liver soy-sauce marinade, which has a creamy, melting texture and rich flavor.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional local yakitori-ya near Jimbocho Station with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff; comfortable for diners with some Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: 新宿 葡庵 (Shinjuku Buan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or preparation protocol is advertised on the official site or any review source found. As a French/Italian bistro, wheat is plausible in items such as the signature quiche pastry, bread, and pasta, and Japanese seasonings used in the kitchen (e.g., soy sauce) may contain wheat as well. No dedicated fryer or gluten-free prep area is documented. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead (03-3351-0141) to discuss specific ingredients before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is vegetable-forward (fresh, pesticide-free Miura produce is a headline feature) but not vegan-oriented overall — the kitchen equally highlights daily fish from Kochi, and as a French/Italian bistro using Japanese seasonings, dishes may contain hidden dashi (bonito-based) stock or animal fats in sauces. No vegan menu, labeling, or dedicated vegan dishes are documented in any source found; vegan diners should call ahead to confirm which specific dishes could be adapted.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) every day. Dinner: Mon–Fri 18:00–23:30 (L.O. 22:30); Sat 17:30–23:30; Sun/Holidays 17:30–22:30 (L.O. 21:30). No regular closed days.

(株式会社エムズダイニング), a small Tokyo hospitality group that operates a handful of Japanese-wine-centered bistros around the city (including sister shops in Kagurazaka and Nishi-Ogikubo), of which this Shinjuku Gyoenmae location is one. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Shinjuku Buan is run by M's Dining Co. (株式会社エムズダイニング), a small Tokyo hospitality group that operates a handful of Japanese-wine-centered bistros around the city (including sister shops in Kagurazaka and Nishi-Ogikubo), of which this Shinjuku Gyoenmae location is one.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach is to cook casual French and Italian dishes using Japanese ingredients and seasonings specifically to pair with Japanese wine, aiming to feel like an approachable, walk-in 'wine dining' spot rather than a formal restaurant — the walk-in wine cellar (around 300 varieties, 1,000+ bottles sourced from producers across Japan) is central to that identity.
chef_bio.specialty: Casual French- and Italian-leaning bistro cooking built on morning-picked, pesticide-free vegetables from a contracted farm on the Miura Peninsula (Kanagawa) and fish landed daily at Sukumo Port in Kochi Prefecture; a lunchtime quiche is called out by name in diner reviews.

positives.text: Vegetables are pesticide-free and delivered fresh each morning from a contracted farm on the Miura Peninsula, and fish is sourced fresh daily from Sukumo Port in Kochi — a whole-ingredient, made-to-order kitchen rather than a processed-food operation, though this is not a documented allergy protocol.

cultural_comfort.note: All findable sources (official site, Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi) are Japanese-only with no confirmed English menu, so English-speaking diners should be ready to communicate dietary needs in Japanese or via translation.

places.name: 徳太樓（徳太楼） (Tokutaro)
places.gf_detail: The signature and dominant product, kintsuba, is defined by a wheat-flour (komugiko) batter that is hand-applied with a spatula to the surface of the azuki filling and then baked — wheat is intrinsic to the recipe, not an optional ingredient. No source mentions any gluten-free item, dedicated gluten-free process, or cross-contamination protocol. This is not a safe or accommodating choice for celiac or gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature kintsuba's filling is described by several sources as simply simmered azuki beans, sugar, and water, which reads as plant-based, but no source confirms the full batter/coating ingredients, and other items in the shop's range (dorayaki, monaka, manju, nerikiri, sekihan) were not detailed enough to confirm the absence of egg, honey, or other animal-derived ingredients. No official vegan claim or menu marking was found.

places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 徳太樓 (Tokutaro) was founded in 1903 (Meiji 36) in Asakusa. Per multiple secondary sources, the founder came from a lower-ranking samurai family of the Akimoto clan from Tatebayashi domain, and after the Meiji Restoration trained in confectionery-making at a shop called Utaro, whic · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 徳太樓 (Tokutaro) was founded in 1903 (Meiji 36) in Asakusa. Per multiple secondary sources, the founder came from a lower-ranking samurai family of the Akimoto clan from Tatebayashi domain, and after the Meiji Restoration trained in confectionery-making at a shop called Utaro, which had ties to the well-known Eitaro Sohonten confectioner in Nihonbashi. The shop has continued in the same neighborhood, in what locals call "oku-Asakusa" (the quieter area behind Senso-ji), ever since.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately old-fashioned, hand-technique approach — most notably the spatula-applied (rather than dipped) batter coating — that the shop has kept as a point of difference from other Tokyo kintsuba makers since the Meiji era.
chef_bio.specialty: Kintsuba — a rectangular sweet of simmered Tokachi (Hokkaido) azuki beans, cooked down and skimmed to a lightly sweetened filling, then hand-coated with a wheat-flour batter applied with a spatula (rather than dipped, as most kintsuba makers do) and baked so all six sides brown evenly on a copper griddle. The technique gives Tokutaro's kintsuba an unusually thick, chewy skin compared to other shops' versions. The shop reportedly makes over 1,000 pieces a day, and recommends eating them within about three days.

anecdotes.text: A Tokyo Shimbun feature describes Tokutaro as a hidden, old-established kintsuba shop found in "oku-Asakusa," the quieter district behind Senso-ji, away from the main tourist crowds.
A gift-guide piece describes Tokutaro's kintsuba as a treasured hand-me-down gift within Asakusa's traditional hanamachi (geisha district) community.

positives.text: Multiple independent write-ups describe the kintsuba filling as made from just Tokachi (Hokkaido) azuki beans, sugar, and water — cooked down and carefully skimmed of impurities — suggesting a short, visible ingredient list for the signature item.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-established neighborhood wagashi shop off the main tourist route in "oku-Asakusa," with no evidence of English signage, menus, or English-speaking staff in any source — expect a Japanese-only, locals'-shop experience.

places.name: 肉うどん 肉めし 甚三 渋谷本店 (Nikuudon Nikumeshi Jinza Shibuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: All noodles are housemade Sanuki-style udon milled from Kagawa wheat flour — there is no gluten-free noodle alternative. The soy sauce and dashi are also sourced from Kagawa/Setouchi producers using traditional wheat-based shoyu. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate fryer, or celiac-safe protocol is indicated; not recommended for diners with celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric restaurant (niku udon / niku meshi featuring Chiba-sourced pork) built on an iriko (dried-anchovy) dashi broth. No vegetarian or vegan menu items are indicated, and the shared dashi base is fish-derived throughout the udon line.
places.hours_raw: 平日 11:00-20:00（玉切れ次第終了）／土日祝 11:00-15:00（玉切れ次第終了）｜Weekdays 11:00-20:00 (ends early if udon sells out), weekends/holidays 11:00-15:00 (ends early if udon sells out)
places.neighborhood: Shibuya, Dogenzaka-ue (near Shinsen Station)
places.cuisine: Sanuki Udon with meat, Japanese noodles
places.notes: Jinza is a small Tokyo restaurant group built around bringing genuine Sanuki (Kagawa-style) udon to Tokyo. It now has several locations — Daimon, Shimbashi, Kanda, Takadanobaba and Shibuya — with the original Daimon shop repeatedly named to Tabelog's Top 100 restaurant list. The  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kagawa-style (Sanuki) udon craft brought to Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Jinza is a small Tokyo restaurant group built around bringing genuine Sanuki (Kagawa-style) udon to Tokyo. The Shibuya (Dogenzaka-ue) shop opened on April 3, 2025, a short walk from Shinsen Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Source key ingredients — wheat flour, soy sauce, dashi kelp and sugar — directly from Kagawa and the Setouchi region to recreate an authentic Sanuki udon experience in Tokyo, paired with Chiba-sourced pork for the signature meat dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: Niku udon (meat udon) and niku meshi (meat rice) — housemade Sanuki udon noodles in a dried-anchovy dashi broth, topped with pork

anecdotes.text: The shop sources its udon wheat flour, soy sauce, and dashi ingredients directly from Kagawa and the Setouchi region, using thick kelp from Naruto and specialty sugar from the long-established Kagawa maker Baikodo; the pork used in the meat udon comes from Chiba Prefecture.
After opening on Dogenzaka in Shibuya on April 3, 2025, the local food blog Shibukichi described the shop's dashi-rich broth and chewy, freshly-made udon as sheer bliss.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce used in the broth is sourced from Kagawa/Setouchi producers using traditional wheat-based shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi broth is made with iriko (dried anchovies), and the signature menu centers on Chiba-sourced pork (niku udon, niku meshi), so shared broths and kitchen equipment are not vegan-free.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket machine, payment is cash-only, and there is no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: 七転八起 (Shichiten Hakki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature bowl uses thick straight wheat noodles from Mikawaya Seimen, and the broth/tare is a miso-based triple stock (chicken, pork, seafood) typical of shops that use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe preparation is mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The base broth is explicitly a chicken, pork, and seafood triple stock, and the shop's core concept is a "meat-piled" (肉盛り) spicy ramen with pork and stock-derived umami throughout — no plant-based broth or noodle option is offered.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町 / 水道橋駅前 (Kanda-Misakicho / in front of Suidobashi Stat
places.notes: 七転八起 (Shichiten Hakki) opened on July 8, 2025, at a site that had previously housed a shop called "すする担々麺." It is run by the company 株式会社MINORU, which also operates the ramen shop 麺やすする near Gakugei University station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 七転八起 (Shichiten Hakki) opened on July 8, 2025, at a site that had previously housed a shop called "すする担々麺. " It is run by the company 株式会社MINORU, which also operates the ramen shop 麺やすする near Gakugei University station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name is a Japanese idiom meaning "fall down seven times, get up eight" — a nod to resilience — and its own tagline frames the bowl as a 辛旨×肉盛りの勝負ラーメン, a spicy, savory, meat-piled "fighting ramen" meant to be a test of appetite as much as a meal.
chef_bio.specialty: 七転八起麺 — a bowl built on a triple stock of chicken, pork, and seafood, poured over thick, chewy straight noodles from Mikawaya Seimen, topped with sliced pork, stir-fried bean sprouts, and naruto fish cake. Spice level is adjustable from 1 to 15.

anecdotes.text: A local ramen blogger visiting near opening described it as a shop that seems poised to become a spot foreign visitors gravitate to, given its bold flavor concept and central Suidobashi location.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard counter-and-table ramen shop where you order at the counter or a ticket-style menu with numbered spice levels — pointing and basic greetings are enough, no English required.

places.name: ストロベリーフィールズ（フルーツショップカンノ） (Strawberry Fields (at Fruits Shop Kanno))
places.gf_detail: No source mentions gluten-free options, alternative bread, or allergen accommodations. The signature fruit sandwich is made with standard wheat bread (食パン). No indication of gluten-free bread, dedicated prep area, or allergen menu. Should be treated as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan mentions found. The signature fruit sandwich uses dairy whipped cream with no stated dairy-free alternative. Fresh fruit items (parfaits, juice) may be plant-based by nature but this is not confirmed as an intentional vegan offering, and no vegan-labeled menu items were found.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (most consistently reported); one source states 10:00-15:00/15:30 with fruit sandwiches often selling out by afternoon regardless. Closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays.

In 1995 the shop was renovated and the owning family added an attached café, Strawberry Fields, so customers could sit down and eat the shop's fruit rather than just buy it to take home. T · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: fruit shop and attached café, family-run across three generations
chef_bio.background: Fruits Shop Kanno is a fruit shop that opened shortly after World War II in Nishi-Shinjuku. In 1995 the shop was renovated and the owning family added an attached café, Strawberry Fields, so customers could sit down and eat the shop's fruit rather than just buy it to take home. The shop is now run by the third generation of the family.
chef_bio.philosophy: The third-generation owner selects seasonal fruit each morning at Tokyo's Ota wholesale market, and the café keeps its cream light and only lightly sweetened so the fruit itself stays the star of the dish rather than being buried in sugar and cream.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit sandwich (furutsu sando) — thick-cut fresh fruit (strawberry, orange and pineapple, or grapefruit, banana and kiwi) layered with lightly sweetened whipped cream between soft white bread.

anecdotes.text: The fruit sandwich is deliberately built with less cream and less sugar than typical versions so the fruit's own sweetness comes through; it is generously portioned (comes with a drink, plus banana and a small salad on the side) and is popular enough that it often sells out by mid-to-late afternoon.

The café was added onto the pre-existing fruit shop in a 1995 renovation, and the shop is described as having opened not long after the war; it is currently run by the third-generation owner who personally selects the fruit at Ota Market.
Bread is described as soft/fluffy (ふわふわ), with fruit sandwiches offered in two set combinations (strawberry-orange-pineapple, or grapefruit-banana-kiwi).

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, old-school neighborhood fruit-shop café with no English menu or website found — expect to point at the display case or use simple Japanese/gestures to order.

places.name: 浅草花月堂 (Asakusa Kagetsudo)
places.gf_detail: The signature jumbo melon pan is a wheat-flour yeasted bread with a sugar-cookie crust; no gluten-free bread, flour substitution, or dedicated GF preparation was mentioned in any source. Not appropriate for celiac diners — this is a wheat bakery through and through.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional melon pan dough is typically enriched with egg and dairy (butter/milk), and no source mentions a vegan or egg/dairy-free version of the jumbo melon pan or other menu items. No vegan accommodation could be confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 9:00〜16:30（売り切れ次第閉店）、年中無休
places.neighborhood: 浅草（浅草寺本堂左側・西参道商店街）
places.cuisine: 甘味処・メロンパン・カフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 浅草花月堂本店は戦後間もない昭和20年（1945年）創業の老舗菓子店で、浅草寺本堂左側、西参道商店街の入口という参拝客の目に留まる場所に店を構えている。看板商品のジャンボめろんぱんは通常の3倍の時間をかけて発酵させた生地を使い、外はサクサク、中はふんわりとした食感に仕上げている。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 和菓子店・メロンパン専門店
chef_bio.origin: 1945年（昭和20年）創業
chef_bio.background: 浅草花月堂本店は戦後間もない昭和20年（1945年）創業の老舗菓子店で、浅草寺本堂左側、西参道商店街の入口という参拝客の目に留まる場所に店を構えている。 看板商品のジャンボめろんぱんは通常の3倍の時間をかけて発酵させた生地を使い、外はサクサク、中はふんわりとした食感に仕上げている。
chef_bio.specialty: ジャンボめろんぱん（Jumbo Melon Pan）— 手のひらサイズの大きな菓子パンで、外側はカリッと香ばしく焼き上げられ、中はふんわり軽い食感。 1個250円前後で、本店では1日約3,000個、週末には2,000個以上が売れる人気商品。

anecdotes.text: 看板商品のジャンボめろんぱんは通常のメロンパンより3倍長く生地を発酵させることにこだわっており、その製法が外カリ中ふわの食感を生んでいる。

cultural_comfort.note: A famous walk-up bakery counter right beside Sensoji — you point at the jumbo melon pan in the case and pay cash, no Japanese conversation required, though don't expect much English beyond basic ordering.

places.name: 渋谷カオマンガイ (Shibuya Khao Man Gai)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found in any source. The signature dipping sauce is described as a fermented blend of Thai miso, soy sauce, and black vinegar soy sauce — soy sauce commonly contains wheat in Japan/Thailand unless specified otherwise, and no gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned. The chicken itself is poached rather than fried, but rice, broth, and soup seasoning ingredients are not detailed publicly. Ask staff directly about the sauce and broth ingredients before ordering if you have celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on poached chicken served over chicken-broth-cooked rice, so there is no naturally vegan version of the signature dish. Sauce and soup ingredients (which may include fish sauce, chicken fat/broth, or other animal products typical of Khao Man Gai) are not detailed in any available source, and no vegan option or substitution has been confirmed. Ask staff directly before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (L.O. 22:00), daily, per multiple independent listings
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（渋谷駅周辺） — Shibuya, near Shibuya Station new south exit
places.notes: Part of DineVita Group, a Tokyo restaurant company that also runs ramen and yakitori shops around the city. This is a dedicated Khao Man Gai (Thai poached-chicken rice) counter a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's new south exit; a sister delivery-only outlet was added on U · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: part of the DineVita Group restaurant company
chef_bio.origin: Thailand
chef_bio.background: Part of DineVita Group, a Tokyo restaurant company that also runs ramen and yakitori shops around the city. This is a dedicated Khao Man Gai (Thai poached-chicken rice) counter a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's new south exit; a sister delivery-only outlet was added on Uber Eats/Demae-kan/menu platforms starting in 2021.
chef_bio.philosophy: Operator DineVita Group frames its broader mission as moving diners from mere satisfaction to genuine 'delight' in the dining-out experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Khao Man Gai — jasmine rice cooked in chicken broth, topped with poached chicken and served with a fermented dipping sauce

anecdotes.text: The house sauce is described as a fermented blend of Thai miso, soy sauce, and black vinegar soy sauce, made with specially selected domestic herb-fed chicken.

Some food media market the shop's cooking as carrying on the style of Bangkok's Michelin-recognized 'Pink' Khao Man Gai (Go-Ang Pratunam Chicken Rice); independent blog research suggests any formal franchise tie to that Bangkok original was severed years ago, so this lineage should be read as marketing framing rather than a confirmed corporate link.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The special dipping sauce is a fermented blend of Thai miso, soy sauce, and black vinegar soy sauce; soy sauce used in Japan typically contains wheat unless a store specifies otherwise, which was not found for this shop.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; a fast-casual counter shop, so basic Japanese or a translation app is recommended for allergy questions.

places.name: 卸）神保町食肉センター 本店 (Jimbocho Shokuniku Center (Honten))
places.gf_detail: This is a standard yakiniku/hormone restaurant with no gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation confirmed anywhere in available sources. Some cuts (e.g. liver, heart, momo) are offered "shio" (salt) rather than in tare sauce, which would avoid the soy-sauce-based marinade, but tare (used on other cuts) is typically wheat-containing soy sauce, and meats are grilled on shared grills alongside sauced items, so cross-contact is likely. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about tare ingredients and request shio-only items grilled away from sauced cuts; nothing here should be assumed safe without asking.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric yakiniku/hormone all-you-can-eat restaurant (beef, pork, liver, heart) with no vegan dishes identified in any source. Rice and soup sides are not confirmed vegan and, given the restaurant's meat-broth-forward menu, likely contain animal-derived stock/dashi. Not recommended for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri & day before public holidays: 11:30-15:00, 17:00-23:30. Sat/Sun/Public holidays: 11:30-23:30. On the 1st of each month, lunch only (dinner closed for inventory).
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神保町（Jinbocho）
places.notes: Jimbocho Shokuniku Center is a yakiniku/hormone (offal) specialist built on a 'processing to plate' model: meat and organ meats are delivered fresh each morning from the company's own supply chain and served the same day, which the restaurant uses to justify unusually low prices  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of an integrated meat business rooted in Takasaki, Gunma — the company runs its own meat processing/wholesale operation (Takasaki Shokuniku Center) and sells directly through its own restaurants, cutting out middlemen.
chef_bio.background: Jimbocho Shokuniku Center is a yakiniku/hormone (offal) specialist built on a 'processing to plate' model: meat and organ meats are delivered fresh each morning from the company's own supply chain and served the same day, which the restaurant uses to justify unusually low prices for grilled offal in central Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep organ meat fresh enough to be odor-free by controlling the whole chain from processing to grill, and price it so grilled offal is an everyday lunch rather than a special occasion.
chef_bio.specialty: Jōshū Tonton (上州とことん) — the company's own proprietary pork breed (a crossbreed raised in the Annaka/Haruna area of Gunma) — served alongside morning-delivered beef liver (rebā) and heart (hatsu); the lunchtime all-you-can-eat course lets diners choose from beef/pork cuts plus liver and heart.

anecdotes.text: A June 2026 feature described the lunch all-you-can-eat (45 minutes, about ¥1,250, including rice/soup/onion salad) and reported that regulars in the know go straight for the liver and heart sets, praising the morning-delivered offal as tender and free of the usual gaminess.
A visitor blog described the ¥1,000 all-you-can-eat yakiniku lunch, noting the fresh, odor-free liver and heart and the popularity of the onion salad and soup refills.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Meat is cooked on shared tabletop yakiniku grills alongside items brushed with soy-sauce-based tare, creating likely cross-contact for gluten-sensitive/celiac diners; no dedicated gluten-free grill or preparation area was found in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's tare-marinated cuts follow standard yakiniku practice using soy sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-based unless specified otherwise; no wheat-free soy sauce or tamari alternative was mentioned in any source.

positives.text: Some cuts (liver, heart, some pork/beef) are offered plain with salt (shio) rather than tare, which structurally avoids the soy-sauce marinade if requested.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu was confirmed in available sources; the lunch all-you-can-eat is ordered via simple lettered sets (A/B/C), which makes pointing-and-ordering manageable even with minimal Japanese.

places.name: 松太郎 新宿小滝橋通り店 (Matsutaro Shinjuku Kotakibashi-dori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options identified. The ramen noodles are standard wheat noodles, and the signature shoyu ramen's tare is a blend of four soy sauces (typically wheat-containing). No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation process was mentioned in any source found.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu items or plant-based broth options were identified. Both standard ramen bowls (shoyu and shio) rely on animal-derived stock/dashi — the shio version explicitly includes a hidden seafood (海鮮) dashi — so neither is vegan-safe as served.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-23:00（L.O.22:30）※開業時は11:00-0:00（LO23:30）として案内されていたが、朝ラーメン開始後に変更された模様

This Nishi-Shinjuku (Kotakibashi-dori) shop, which opened July 30, 2025, is the brand's very first location, converted from a for · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan
chef_bio.background: Matsutaro (松太郎) is a ramen-specialty spin-off brand launched by Matsuya Foods Holdings — the major company behind the nationwide Matsuya gyudon chain. This Nishi-Shinjuku (Kotakibashi-dori) shop, which opened July 30, 2025, is the brand's very first location, converted from a former Matsuya beef-bowl outlet on the same street.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's stated concept is to be affordable and "never boring no matter how many times you eat it" — aiming to be everyone's everyday dining table ("みんなの食卓でありたい").
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen (680 yen), made with a tare blended from four different soy sauces for a light but full-bodied flavor; also offers a shio (salt) ramen seasoned with kombu extract and a hidden seafood dashi.

anecdotes.text: Coverage of the opening framed it as Matsuya Foods placing its first-ever ramen shop into one of Shinjuku's most competitive ramen districts, betting on a simple 680-yen bowl to stand out.
The shop later added a morning ramen service, a detail local ramen bloggers noted as a change from its original hours.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature dish is shoyu ramen made with a tare blended from four different soy sauces — standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shio (salt) ramen broth is seasoned with salt and kombu extract but also includes a hidden seafood dashi as a secret ingredient, and the shoyu ramen uses a soy-based tare — no vegan or plant-only broth option was identified.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard Japanese chain ramen counter with no indication of English menus or foreign-visitor-oriented service — expect Japanese-only ordering via counter/ticket-style service typical of Matsuya-group shops.

places.name: 雷門き介 (Kaminarimon Kisuke)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated-fryer information could be found. As a yakitori and oden restaurant, tare (grilling sauce) and oden broth very likely contain wheat-based soy sauce; shio (salt-only) skewers may reduce wheat exposure for individual skewers but this is not confirmed by the restaurant. Celiac diners should not assume safety and would need to ask staff directly (in Japanese) about ingredients.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-centric restaurant: the oden broth is made from chicken bones and kelp (not vegan), and the signature yakitori is entirely chicken-based. No vegetarian or vegan menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source checked.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (LO 14:00), Dinner 17:00-23:00 (LO 22:30). Sat/Sun/Holidays: 11:30-23:00 (LO 22:30). Closed: none (無休).
places.neighborhood: 浅草雷門 (Kaminarimon, Asakusa)
places.notes: Housed in a renovated 50-year-old traditional Japanese building (kominka) near Kaminarimon, blending old wood-and-tile character with a cleaned-up modern dining room; the second floor looks out over the old Edo-style street below. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Housed in a renovated 50-year-old traditional Japanese building (kominka) near Kaminarimon, blending old wood-and-tile character with a cleaned-up modern dining room; the second floor looks out over the old Edo-style street below.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around slow-made, comforting flavors: the oden broth is drawn out gently over time from kelp and chicken bones so the daikon and other ingredients soak up a mild, homely taste, while the yakitori relies on the quality of Iwate's free-range birds rather than heavy seasoning.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori made from branded free-range chicken from Iwate Prefecture, paired with Kyoto-style oden simmered in a broth of Hokkaido Hidaka kelp and chicken bones.

anecdotes.text: The oden here is simmered in a special dashi carefully drawn out over low heat from Hokkaido Hidaka kelp and chicken bones, giving the daikon and other ingredients a gentle, comforting flavor.

The restaurant occupies a renovated 50-year-old house near Kaminarimon, combining traditional Japanese atmosphere with a modern, clean dining space, with the second floor offering a view over the old street below.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No explicit statement was found about the wheat content of the tare/soy sauce used, but standard Japanese yakitori tare and oden broth typically contain wheat-based soy sauce, so this should be assumed present unless staff confirm otherwise.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found; expect a Japanese-language izakaya experience, though its location steps from Kaminarimon means some tourist familiarity is likely.

places.name: パンツェロッテリア (Panzerotteria)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's entire concept is panzerotti — fried, wheat-flour dough stuffed with fillings — so the signature dish is inherently wheat-based. No gluten-free dough, substitution, or dedicated gluten-free menu was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is built around Italian panzerotti fillings such as cured ham, cheese (mozzarella), meats, and seafood; no vegan menu, vegan panzerotti variety, or dedicated vegan options were found in any available source.
places.hours_raw: 18:00〜23:00 (LO 22:00)。定休日：月曜・火曜（月曜が祝日の場合は営業）
places.neighborhood: 松濤 (Shoto), Shibuya-ku — near Shinsen Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Nishi Masahiro spent about 25 years based in Italy, working in manufacturing-related businesses (interior design, apparel, bags) in Milan and extending his activities to New York and Ireland. After returning to Japan he opened Panzerotteria in the residential Shoto neighbor · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Owner Nishi Masahiro spent about 25 years based in Italy, working in manufacturing-related businesses (interior design, apparel, bags) in Milan and extending his activities to New York and Ireland. After returning to Japan he opened Panzerotteria in the residential Shoto neighborhood of Shibuya in April 2012, introducing panzerotti — Italian fried, stuffed pizza dough — a dish essentially unknown in Japan at the time.
chef_bio.philosophy: Presents panzerotti, which in Italy is typically eaten as fast food on the street, as a proper sit-down dish at the table; the restaurant fries its dough in domestically produced cottonseed oil, chosen for its quality and vitamin E content.
chef_bio.specialty: Panzerotti, Italian fried and stuffed pizza dough, served in roughly 40 varieties

anecdotes.text: Nishi Masahiro's 25 years living and working in Italy (Milan, with activity also in New York and Ireland) led him to open Panzerotteria in Shoto in April 2012, the first restaurant of its kind focused on panzerotti in Japan.

In 2014 the shop expanded with 'Panzerotteria Factory,' a second location with restaurant attached in Osaka, and began mail-order and wholesale sales of its fried pizza, later also running a Makuake crowdfunding campaign for frozen panzerotti.

positives.text: The restaurant fries its panzerotti in domestically produced cottonseed oil, chosen by the owner for its quality and high vitamin E content, as opposed to more common frying oils.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations run through Japanese-language platforms (Hot Pepper, Ikyu, OZmall, Hitosara, EPARK) and no English menu was confirmed, so English-speaking visitors should expect a mostly Japanese-language experience.

places.name: 眞踏珈琲店 (Mafumi Coffee Shop (Shinto Kohiten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or cross-contamination information were found anywhere. The signature curry rice and the "Rucola in the Sea" croque monsieur are both wheat-based (roux and bread respectively), and there is no evidence of soy-sauce wheat content, fryer dedication, or any gluten-free alternative being offered.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, dish, or ingredient substitution was found in any source. The core menu (curry rice with dairy/cheese, croque monsieur sandwiches, cheesecake) is dairy- and egg-based, and there is no indication of hidden dashi/bonito disclosure or a plant-based option.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 12:00–23:00 (L.O. 30 min before close); Sun & holidays 12:00–21:00 (L.O. 30 min before close); no fixed regular closed day; non-smoking; no reservations.
places.neighborhood: 神保町 / 神田小川町 (Jimbocho / Kanda-Ogawamachi), Chiyoda-ku — abou
