He buil · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Owner Oyama Mafumi was a devoted regular at the legendary Tsuta Coffee (蔦珈琲店) in Aoyama during his student years, visiting almost daily for about four years, then trained under its master for four more years before opening his own shop, 眞踏珈琲店, in Kanda-Ogawamachi in 2016. He built the shop's long counter specifically to recreate the counter conversations with the master that had drawn him to Tsuta Coffee.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'Heisei-era kissaten' meant to carry forward the DNA of old Showa-era coffee shops: unhurried counter conversation, self-roasted coffee, and a room lined with thousands of secondhand books for customers to read.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin coffee (Brazil Santos No. 2) hand-dripped through a cloth nel filter, plus a 15-hour cold-drip iced coffee rested overnight before serving; the curry rice, noted for its fruit acidity and cheese richness, has also been recognized in the Kanda Curry Grand Prix.

anecdotes.text: "Even with the same beans, you can create different flavors by making changes to the grinding and extraction methods," the owner has said of his approach to coffee.
On praise for his curry: "Even if customers compliment me on the curry, since this area is full of famous curry restaurants, I can only apologize" — a nod to Kanda's reputation as a curry district.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic, book-lined neighborhood kissaten with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so expect a Japanese-only ordering experience.

places.name: 浅草 なつの（呑み喰い処 浅草なつの） (Asakusa Natsuno)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free options were found in any source. As a traditional izakaya, the stew and other dishes almost certainly use soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) and are prepared in a shared kitchen; celiac diners should not assume safety and would need to ask staff directly (Japanese-only environment).
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or options were found. The signature dish is a beef tendon stew, and other offerings are fish-based; there is no indication of dashi/bonito-free preparation or plant-based alternatives.
places.neighborhood: Asakusa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Asakusa Natsuno is a small izakaya on Asakusa's Hoppy Street (ホッピー通り), open since around 2014-2015. Before running the shop, the owner worked at a ramen restaurant, and he applies ramen-style soup-making technique to the restaurant's signature beef tendon stew. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Asakusa Natsuno is a small izakaya on Asakusa's Hoppy Street (ホッピー通り), open since around 2014-2015. Before running the shop, the owner worked at a ramen restaurant, and he applies ramen-style soup-making technique to the restaurant's signature beef tendon stew.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner spends a full day preparing the beef tendon stew, carefully trimming excess fat and using only Japanese beef tendon, balancing several different cuts in the pot to give the finished dish varied texture alongside its sweet-savory broth.
chef_bio.specialty: 牛すじ煮込み (gyusuji nikomi) - slow-simmered beef tendon and tofu stew

anecdotes.text: The owner's tendon stew contains only beef tendon and tofu; he removes excess fat during preparation and the process takes a full day, an approach he brought over from his prior experience making ramen soup.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, local-feeling counter izakaya on the touristy but old-school Hoppy Street; expect a Japanese-only menu and limited English, though the area sees many foreign visitors.

places.name: 七婆串串香 (Qipo Chuanchuanxiang)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation info was found in any source (official site, Tabelog, Hot Pepper, or press coverage). The format is a self-serve buffet-style skewer shelf with 100+ types of skewered ingredients shared among all diners, plus a mala broth that in Sichuan-style cooking commonly includes soy-based seasonings and doubanjiang (fermented bean paste, sometimes containing wheat) — meaning cross-contact risk in the shared soup and skewer selection cannot be ruled out. Not verifiable as celiac-safe; diners with celiac disease should call ahead and ask specifically about broth ingredients and cross-contact before relying on this restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based broth, or dedicated vegan options were found in any source. The restaurant is built around a mostly meat-forward self-serve skewer bar (thick-cut meats plus vegetables) with a choice of 白湯 (herbal white broth) or 麻辣 (mala spicy) soup; neither soup's stock composition is disclosed anywhere found, so whether either broth is animal-based (common for both styles in China) is unconfirmed. Vegans should ask staff directly about the broth base before ordering.
places.neighborhood: Miyamasuzaka / Shibuya East (Jingumae, Shibuya)
places.notes: This Shibuya branch is reported to be the first Japan location of 七婆串串香 (Qipo Chuanchuanxiang), a Chinese chuan-chuan-xiang (skewer hot pot) chain that originated in Chengdu and has grown to over a thousand locations across mainland China (sources cite figures ranging from roughl · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Chengdu, China
chef_bio.background: This Shibuya branch is reported to be the first Japan location of 七婆串串香 (Qipo Chuanchuanxiang), a Chinese chuan-chuan-xiang (skewer hot pot) chain that originated in Chengdu and has grown to over a thousand locations across mainland China (sources cite figures ranging from roughly 1,100 to 1,800 stores). It opened in the GEMS Jingumae building near Miyamasuzaka, a short walk from Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-serve chuan chuan xiang (skewered hot pot): diners choose a 白湯 (mellow herbal white broth) or 麻辣 (numbing-spicy mala, in four spice levels) soup, then help themselves to over 100 kinds of skewered meats and vegetables from a self-service shelf to cook tableside.

cultural_comfort.note: It's a self-serve skewer-and-broth format (pick a soup, then grab skewers yourself from a shelf) with signage and staff interaction primarily in Japanese, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the picture menu) goes a long way.

places.name: 豚ノヴァ零-zero- 神田水道橋店 (Butanova Zero Kanda-Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options identified. The shop's entire menu centers on a custom wheat-blend noodle specifically engineered for the "shirunashi" (soupless) format — wheat is the signature ingredient, not something worked around. No mention anywhere of gluten-free noodles, alternative flour, or allergy accommodation; the soy-based tare (カエシ) used for seasoning almost certainly contains wheat as is standard for Jiro-style shops. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. This is a pork-centric Jiro-inspired shop (豚 = pork is literally in the name) built around a pork-based tare/broth-style seasoning ("汁なし" mazesoba with pork toppings). No plant-based menu items, dashi substitutions, or vegan accommodations were found in any source. Likely uses animal-based dashi/pork fat throughout. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:30〜21:00（通し営業／ラストオーダー情報は未確認）
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町・西神田（水道橋駅と神保町駅の間）
places.notes: 「豚ノヴァ 零-zero- 神田水道橋店」は、国分寺で人気の二郎系ラーメン店「豚NOVA（豚ノヴァ）」の2号店として、JR水道橋駅・神保町駅の間、水道橋駅東口徒歩5分の場所に2025年3月8日にグランドオープンした。国分寺本店とは異なり、通常のラーメンは提供せず「汁なし（マゼソバ寄りの二郎インスパイア）」専門の一杯に特化している。店主のGo氏は埼玉の人気二郎系店「富士丸 東浦和店」や「D麺」で修行した経歴を持つ。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 国分寺本店（豚NOVA）の2号店として2025年3月8日にオープン
chef_bio.background: 「豚ノヴァ 零-zero- 神田水道橋店」は、国分寺で人気の二郎系ラーメン店「豚NOVA（豚ノヴァ）」の2号店として、JR水道橋駅・神保町駅の間、水道橋駅東口徒歩5分の場所に2025年3月8日にグランドオープンした。 国分寺本店とは異なり、通常のラーメンは提供せず「汁なし（マゼソバ寄りの二郎インスパイア）」専門の一杯に特化している。 店主のGo氏は埼玉の人気二郎系店「富士丸 東浦和店」や「D麺」で修行した経歴を持つ。
chef_bio.philosophy: 経営上の数字よりも味への一切の妥協のなさを優先しており、一杯あたりの原価率は60%を超えるという。 国分寺本店の味をベースにしながら、カエシ・豚・トッピングを細かく調整し、神田水道橋店ならではの独自の味わいに仕上げている。
chef_bio.specialty: 汁なし（二郎インスパイア系の油そば・まぜそば）。 丼全体を混ぜると小麦の甘みを含んだ香りが立ち上る、汁なし専用に配合・形状を変えた自家製麺が看板。

anecdotes.text: 汁なし専用の麺は、汁なしのために小麦の配合や形状を変えたもので、丼全体をかき混ぜた時に小麦の甘みを含んだ香りがフワッと立ち上り、ムチっとした食感に密度の高い小麦感が味わえます。二郎系の名店で修行をしたGo店主が作り上げた、こだわりの麺です。
豚ノヴァは、経営的な数値を度外視して味に対して一切の妥協なく提供をしており、一杯の原価率は60%を超えており、これは最高の原材料で最高の味を提供するためです。
店主は埼玉の人気二郎系店「富士丸 東浦和店」と「D麺」で修行した。
2025年3月8日、国分寺に続く2号店として神田水道橋にグランドオープンした（水道橋駅徒歩5分）。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Jiro-style tare/soy-based seasoning is used; no wheat-free alternative or gluten information is published for this shop.

cultural_comfort.note: Ticket-machine (食券制) ordering in Japanese; Jiro-inspired shops often use topping "calls" (ニンニク・アブラ・カラメ等) that can be unfamiliar to first-time foreign visitors, though the shirunashi-only menu simplifies this somewhat.

places.name: 寿司・しゃぶしゃぶ ゆず庵 新宿西口大ガード店 (Sushi & Shabu-shabu Yuzuan Shinjuku Nishiguc)
places.gf_detail: Sushi rice vinegar and dipping soy sauce (typically wheat-based in Japan), plus likely tempura/breaded items on the all-you-can-eat menu, mean most dishes are not gluten-free by default. The parent company (Monogatari Corporation) publishes a chainwide allergen chart flagging wheat and six other major allergens per menu item, and states that items not on the chart can be investigated on request (about a two-week turnaround), but no dedicated gluten-free fryer, GF soy sauce, or GF-specific course was found. Cross-contact risk in the shared kitchen should be assumed high; celiac diners should review the allergen chart and speak with staff directly before eating.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-centric sushi-and-shabu-shabu all-you-can-eat restaurant. Sushi vinegar, dipping sauces, and shabu-shabu broths in this style of restaurant are typically dashi-based (often bonito/fish stock), and no vegan menu, substitutions, or plant-based broth option was found in any available source. Not recommended for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-24:00 (last entry 23:00); weekday lunch buffet available until 15:00
places.neighborhood: 新宿西口 (Nishi-Shinjuku, west of Shinjuku Station)
places.notes: Yuzuan is a nationwide sushi-and-shabu-shabu buffet brand run by Monogatari Corporation. This Nishi-Shinjuku 'Ogake' branch opened on June 25, 2025, roughly the chain's 106th location, about a two-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, in a stretch of Nishi-Shinjuku where · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Yuzuan is a nationwide sushi-and-shabu-shabu buffet brand run by Monogatari Corporation. This Nishi-Shinjuku 'Ogake' branch opened on June 25, 2025, roughly the chain's 106th location, about a two-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, in a stretch of Nishi-Shinjuku where the company also opened a sister yakiniku location the same day.
chef_bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat course pairing conveyor-style nigiri and rolls (30+ varieties) with shabu-shabu hot pot, meats and seasonal vegetables, ordered from the table via touchscreen tablets so food comes straight to your seat.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described treating themselves to a solo year-end dinner at Yuzuan on a roughly ¥5,000 budget and being so satisfied with the sushi-and-shabu-shabu spread that they lost track of time.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Monogatari Corporation allergy information page for the Yuzuan brand lists the seven mandatory allergens (wheat, buckwheat, egg, dairy, peanut, shrimp, crab) per menu item and says items not yet listed can be investigated on request, typically within about two weeks.

positives.text: Publishes a chainwide, regularly updated allergen chart covering the seven major allergens for its menu items, which diners can consult before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is done via tableside touchscreen tablets with photo menus, which makes navigating the all-you-can-eat course manageable with minimal Japanese, though an English-language menu was not confirmed.

places.name: MATCHA TIMES 浅草店 (MATCHA TIMES Asakusa)
places.gf_detail: Matcha powder and flavors like kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), kinako (roasted soybean flour) and houjicha are naturally wheat-free, and toppings such as warabimochi and anko are typically gluten-free, but the shop also serves wheat-based taiyaki pastries in the same small space, and no dedicated gluten-free protocol, fryer, or cross-contamination policy is documented in available sources. Celiac guests should confirm directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Matcha itself is plant-based and the drink system lets customers choose their milk, but press materials do not specify whether dairy-free milk alternatives (soy/oat) are offered, and the signature "demekin taiyaki" filled with matcha custard is not confirmed vegan (likely contains egg/dairy). Ask staff about milk options and pastry ingredients before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-17:30, no regular closed days (無休)
places.neighborhood: 台東区浅草
places.notes: MATCHA TIMES is a Japanese matcha-drink specialty brand that opened its Asakusa location on December 16, 2025, tucked on the 2nd floor of a residential backstreet a five-minute walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate and two minutes from Tawaramachi Station. Press coverage describes · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (brand also has other Tokyo locations such as Shibuya and Shinjuku)
chef_bio.background: MATCHA TIMES is a Japanese matcha-drink specialty brand that opened its Asakusa location on December 16, 2025, tucked on the 2nd floor of a residential backstreet a five-minute walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate and two minutes from Tawaramachi Station. Press coverage describes it as a deliberately low-key 'hideaway' offering an escape from the tourist bustle around the temple.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand states it wants to honor the spirit of traditional tea ceremony and the beauty of matcha while reinterpreting it, not as something formal, but in a form that fits naturally into modern daily life.
chef_bio.specialty: Build-your-own matcha latte — customers choose the matcha grade, intensity, sweetness, milk, flavor (options include kuromitsu, kinako, houjicha, melon, mango, strawberry, chocolate, yuzu) and toppings (warabimochi, anko, cream); also known for its handmade 'demekin taiyaki' filled with matcha custard.

anecdotes.text: MATCHA TIMES sources its matcha directly from five long-established manufacturers, each with over 100 years of history, to ensure high, consistent quality across its customizable drink menu.
The Asakusa shop was deliberately sited away from the main tourist strip near Kaminarimon, on a quiet residential street up a flight of stairs, to create a calmer 'hideaway' matcha experience.

positives.text: Core matcha lattes are built from matcha powder, milk, and naturally gluten-free flavor add-ins like kuromitsu, kinako, and houjicha, with no dashi or soy-sauce-based savory dishes on the menu to introduce hidden wheat or fish stock.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is menu-and-counter style with a build-your-own matcha system (grade, strength, sweetness, milk, flavor, toppings); an English-speaking visitor can point at menu boards but detailed customization is explained mostly in Japanese.

places.name: 庵狐 円山町店 (Anko Maruyamacho-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. As a standard izakaya it very likely uses regular (wheat-containing) shoyu and dashi, and fried items likely share a fryer; celiac diners should assume no gluten-free accommodation exists unless confirmed by phone/in person.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or specific vegan accommodation was found in any source. Dishes center on chicken and seafood; even the "organic vegetable" side dishes are likely to be seasoned with bonito/katsuo-based dashi and standard shoyu, which are not vegan. Vegan diners would need to ask staff directly and should not assume any dish is vegan by default.
places.neighborhood: Maruyama-cho, Shibuya (near Shinsen Station)
places.notes: 庵狐（あんこ）は三宿・恵比寿・代官山・円山町（神泉）に店舗を構える小規模な居酒屋グループで、円山町店は渋谷区円山町の加藤ビル1階、神泉駅から徒歩2〜3分の場所にある。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 庵狐（あんこ）は三宿・恵比寿・代官山・円山町（神泉）に店舗を構える小規模な居酒屋グループで、円山町店は渋谷区円山町の加藤ビル1階、神泉駅から徒歩2〜3分の場所にある。
chef_bio.specialty: 地鶏（じどり）と鮮魚を炭火で焼く料理、日本酒・焼酎に合わせた一品料理

anecdotes.text: 検索結果によれば、庵狐グループは全国の港（特に築地）から直接仕入れる魚介類を看板に、日本酒のラインナップと合わせて提供しているとされる（店舗横断的な特徴として言及されており、円山町店固有の逸話としての確認はできていない）。

cultural_comfort.note: This is a local neighborhood izakaya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff in any source found, so expect a Japanese-only experience.

places.name: 豚丼の丼達 水道橋店 (Dontatsu Suidobashi (pork rice bowl shop))
places.gf_detail: This is a soy-sauce-based pork bowl shop — the four house sauces (teriyaki garlic, teriyaki ginger, spicy soy sauce, salt-ginger) are built on standard Japanese shoyu, which is wheat-based. No gluten-free menu, sauce substitution, or dedicated preparation process was mentioned in any source found. Not a safe choice for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around pork bowls and a chicken-soboro bowl; no vegetarian or vegan rice bowl, side dish, or substitution was found in the menu listing or any review. Not viable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋（千代田区三崎町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, single-location pork rice bowl (butadon) counter shop a couple of minutes' walk from JR Suidobashi Station, in a neighborhood dense with university campuses (Nihon University's Suidobashi campus is nearby). The shop is known locally as a hearty, budget lunch spot for stu · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small, single-location pork rice bowl (butadon) counter shop a couple of minutes' walk from JR Suidobashi Station, in a neighborhood dense with university campuses (Nihon University's Suidobashi campus is nearby). The shop is known locally as a hearty, budget lunch spot for students and workers, and has built something of a reputation in Tokyo's "big-portion" (dekamori) dining scene for its oversized bowls and an eating-challenge menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Butadon (pork rice bowl) served over a large base of rice, with a choice of four house sauces — teriyaki garlic, teriyaki ginger, spicy soy sauce, or salt-ginger.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers a standing eating challenge: finish an oversized 1.5kg pork bowl together with three bowls of miso soup within 30 minutes to win a free bowl coupon, and food blogs describe it as a well-known stop on Tokyo's 'big-eater' circuit.
A visitor review describes the room as crowded with students, with counter-only seating and a no-frills, local-diner feel.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, counter-seating student-diner with a Japanese-only menu and no English service found in any source, so some Japanese or confident pointing/gesturing will be needed to order.

places.name: 新宿そば蔵（信州そば処 新宿そば蔵） (Shinjuku Soba Kura)
places.gf_detail: No source confirms the noodles are 十割 (100% buckwheat, jūwari); as a traditional Shinshu soba shop with no gluten-free claims found, the soba is presumed to be a standard wheat-flour blend (nihachi-style), and the menu includes tempura and soy sauce-based dipping sauce (tsuyu), both typically wheat-containing. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate boiling water, or dedicated fryer was mentioned anywhere. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-sake shop; the dipping sauce (tsuyu) at shops like this is standardly made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and the broader menu includes clearly animal-based items such as basashi (horse sashimi), so it is not vegan-friendly. No vegan options, substitutions, or dashi alternatives were mentioned in any source reviewed.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku-sanchome, Shinjuku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shinjuku Soba Kura (信州そば処 新宿そば蔵) is the Shinjuku Sanchome branch of Sobakura, a small two-shop Shinshu (Nagano)-style soba group that also runs a location in Komazawa. The Shinjuku Sanchome shop opened in January 2004, in a cozy, old-house-style interior with a nostalgic standing · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: run by two women who operate the counter and kitchen
chef_bio.origin: Nagano Prefecture (Shinshu) buckwheat and local sake, delivered fresh daily
chef_bio.background: Shinjuku Soba Kura (信州そば処 新宿そば蔵) is the Shinjuku Sanchome branch of Sobakura, a small two-shop Shinshu (Nagano)-style soba group that also runs a location in Komazawa. The Shinjuku Sanchome shop opened in January 2004, in a cozy, old-house-style interior with a nostalgic standing-soba-bar feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: The group's stated aim, per its official site, is '本場信州のそばを味わう喜び' — the joy of tasting authentic Shinshu soba — built around buckwheat noodles and sake sourced fresh from Nagano every day.
chef_bio.specialty: 錦そば (Nishiki Soba), plus shiso (青じそ) flavored variations and Shinshu-style mori soba

anecdotes.text: The shop is run day-to-day by two women in a small counter-and-table space (about 12 seats) with a casual, nostalgic standing-soba-shop atmosphere; regulars praise the firm, fresh-tasting Nagano buckwheat noodles and the crisp vegetable and shrimp tempura, alongside snacks like nozawana pickles and horse sashimi (basashi) to go with the local sake.
Weekday moriSoba runs around ¥550 and a half-rice teishoku set around ¥935, reflecting the shop's casual, affordable pricing.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual, locally-run soba stand with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; some Japanese ability (or pointing at the tabelog/menu photos) will help when ordering.

places.name: 神山（旧店名：清山） (Kamiyama)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Edo-style soba-ya, not a gluten-free-focused kitchen. Some descriptions reference stone-ground buckwheat prepared fresh daily, but there is no confirmation the noodles are consistently 十割 (100% buckwheat, wheat-free) rather than the common 二八 blend (buckwheat plus wheat flour as binder), and the dipping sauce/dashi (tsuyu) follows the standard soy-sauce-and-bonito-dashi style typical of old-line soba shops, which normally contains wheat. Tempura is also on the menu, fried in shared oil with a wheat-flour batter. No gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any available source, so celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not assume safety here without calling ahead and asking directly.
places.vegan_detail: This is a classic soba-ya built around bonito (katsuo) dashi, with tempura and sashimi also on the menu, so it is not vegan-oriented. Even a plain zaru soba would typically be served with a bonito-based dipping sauce. No plant-based dashi or vegan menu option was found in any source reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 12:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); Sat 12:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); closed Sun and public holidays. Dinner is course-only and reservation-required by the previous day, minimum 2 guests.
places.neighborhood: 神泉・神山町 (Shinsen / Kamiyama-cho, Shibuya-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1988 (Showa 63) on a quiet backstreet of Shibuya's Kamiyama-cho, a few minutes' walk from Shinsen Station, deliberately set apart from the bustle of central Shibuya. The shop was previously known as "清山" (Seizan) before taking the name 神山 (Kamiyama). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Opened in 1988 (Showa 63) on a quiet backstreet of Shibuya's Kamiyama-cho, a few minutes' walk from Shinsen Station, deliberately set apart from the bustle of central Shibuya. The shop was previously known as "清山" (Seizan) before taking the name 神山 (Kamiyama).
chef_bio.philosophy: A craftsmanship-first approach carried over from training at Takeyabu, one of Tokyo's most celebrated soba houses: domestic buckwheat is stone-ground fresh each morning and the dough is hand-worked to order, with the buckwheat variety changing by season.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-worked soba noodles, plus sobagaki (soba-flour dumplings) and a seasonal tempura assortment

anecdotes.text: The shop's soba craftsman trained at Takeyabu, a nationally known soba house, before opening this hideaway shop on a Shibuya backstreet.
Behind a glass wall, diners can watch the craftsman turn stone-ground buckwheat flour into noodles in one continuous, practiced motion.
Buckwheat is stone-ground on site each morning and mixed with spring water said to be drawn from the Haruna mountain area, used for both the noodles and the dashi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area, protocol, or wheat-free noodle guarantee has been documented; this is a shared traditional kitchen that also fries wheat-battered tempura.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce and dashi follow standard Edo-style soba preparation (bonito dashi with soy sauce); no wheat-free tamari or gluten-free sauce alternative was mentioned in any source found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is made with dried bonito (katsuobushi), and the menu includes tempura and sashimi, so the kitchen is not set up for vegan cooking; no plant-based dashi option was found in any source.

positives.text: Uses domestically grown buckwheat, stone-ground on the premises each morning for freshly milled noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood soba-ya with no confirmed English menu or online booking; dinner reservations are made by phone in Japanese.

places.name: 農家の台所 新宿三丁目店 (Nouka no Daidokoro (Shinjuku Sanchome))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification was found. The vegetable salad bar itself is naturally produce-based, but no source confirms whether shared dressings, soy sauce, dashi, or fried items are wheat-free, nor whether a dedicated fryer is used. WE TABLE tags the restaurant as accommodating gluten-free requests, but specifics were not documented — celiac diners should confirm details with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Among the lunch set menu, only the curry is confirmed vegan; the miso soup that otherwise accompanies lunch sets contains fish dashi but can be omitted on request. The all-you-can-eat salad bar's toppings are all free of animal-derived ingredients, making it a reliable vegan option on its own. Other mains (grilled meats, fish) are not vegan.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku Sanchome
places.notes: 農家の台所 ("Farmer's Kitchen") sources its vegetables directly from roughly 40 contracted farmers around Japan under the concept 「人×素材」("People × Ingredients"). The Shinjuku Sanchome branch has been operating for around 17 years and reports having served over a million customers, bui · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (small restaurant group with a second location in Tachikawa)
chef_bio.background: 農家の台所 ("Farmer's Kitchen") sources its vegetables directly from roughly 40 contracted farmers around Japan under the concept 「人×素材」("People × Ingredients"). The Shinjuku Sanchome branch has been operating for around 17 years and reports having served over a million customers, built around its all-you-can-eat vegetable salad bar.
chef_bio.philosophy: Direct-from-farmer sourcing and simple, hand-prepared cooking that lets the natural sweetness and character of seasonal vegetables speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: The all-you-can-eat salad bar (20+ types of vegetables daily) sourced straight from contract farms, served alongside a bagna cauda-style dip and grilled/simmered mains such as salt-koji chicken thigh and grilled salmon harasu.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers repeatedly single out the salad bar's rare vegetable varieties as noticeably sweeter and fresher-tasting than typical produce.
Described in a 2025 write-up as a bright, popular spot with a strong lunch salad-bar following.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The salad-bar dressing is described as a miso-type dressing (with salt, olive oil, vinegar); miso is frequently produced with barley (mugi) or wheat koji, i.e. a hidden gluten source on the shared bar that no GF claim addresses.
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://rocketnews24.com/2024/07/22/2314665/ (via web search summary; also tabelog matome)

gf_cross_contamination.text: A critical review notes there is no ingredient labeling on the salad-bar dishes and expresses doubt about the dressing/seasoning components (and possible excess sugar).

No celiac-specific reviews, cross-contamination protocols, or GF certification could be found on FindMeGlutenFree or HappyCow for this restaurant; only a generic 'rice flour batter so celiacs can enjoy' marketing-style statement appears, which ignores shared-fryer and wheat-soy-sauce risk.
The shop's own site and the WE TABLE aggregator page (which supplies the GF tag) returned HTTP 403 egress-policy denials and could not be read, so the claimed GF accommodation remains unverified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The miso soup included in lunch sets is made with fish dashi, but it can be requested to be left off the set when ordering.

positives.text: Only the curry among the lunch set options is vegan-compatible.
All toppings on the salad bar use no animal-derived ingredients, so vegan diners can eat freely from the salad bar alone.
Tagged on WE TABLE as accommodating allergy, vegan, halal, and gluten-free needs.

cultural_comfort.note: Listed on English-facing tourist/booking platforms (Tabelog EN, byFood) and buffet-style ordering is easy to navigate visually, but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: 手打蕎麦 おざわ (Teuchi Soba Ozawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional hand-cut soba-ya, not a gluten-free-focused establishment. Of its three noodle cuts, the medium ('yaya futomen') noodle is reportedly made juwari-style (十割, 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat binder), while the thin noodle uses roughly 90% buckwheat/10% wheat and the ultra-thick noodle about 95% buckwheat/5% wheat -- so two of the three cuts do contain some wheat flour. No source confirms dedicated gluten-free boiling water, separate prep equipment, or a wheat-free dipping sauce; standard soba tsuyu is soy-sauce (shoyu) based and typically contains wheat. Diners with celiac disease should not assume any cut is safe without asking staff directly, given the shared kitchen and likely shared boiling water across noodle types.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, labeling, or accommodation was found for this shop. As a traditional soba-ya, its dipping sauce (tsuyu) almost certainly rests on a katsuobushi (bonito) dashi with a soy-sauce-based kaeshi, which is standard practice for hand-cut soba shops of this type and is not vegan. No source confirms a kombu-only or vegetable-broth alternative.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa, Taito, Tokyo
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is a traditional hand-cut soba-ya, not a gluten-free-focused establishment. Of its three noodle cuts, the medium ('yaya futomen') noodle is reportedly made juwari-style (十割, 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat binder), while the thin noodle uses roughly 90% buckwheat/10% whea · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.philosophy: The shop varies the ratio of buckwheat to wheat binder to match each cut's texture: roughly 90% buckwheat for the thin noodle (smoother throat-feel), a 100%-buckwheat (juwari) blend for the medium noodle, and about 95% buckwheat for the ultra-thick noodle, using buckwheat flour sourced from various regions of Japan.
chef_bio.specialty: Three hand-cut soba noodle styles served side by side: a thin 'tsurutsuru hosogiri' (smooth, easy-swallowing thin cut), a nutty 'mochimochi arabiki' (coarse-ground cut), and an ultra-thick 'mogumogu futoi' (太打ち) noodle roughly 1cm wide, marketed as among the thickest hand-cut soba in Tokyo -- cut so wide that a single serving can contain as few as about 8 strands.

anecdotes.text: The 太打ち (ultra-thick) noodle is cut so wide that a single serving of soba contains only around 8 strands -- an oddity that has made it a minor food-blog phenomenon in Asakusa, often described as 'Japan's thickest soba'.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional neighborhood soba-ya with no confirmed English menu or online reservation system -- some basic Japanese (or pointing at the Tabelog photos) will help when ordering.

places.name: 餃子 小籠包 福包酒場 渋谷店 (Gyoza Xiaolongbao Fupao Sakaba Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free substitutions, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination information was found in any source. The shop's signature items (gyoza, xiaolongbao) use standard wheat-flour dumpling wrappers, and as a general Chinese-izakaya menu it almost certainly relies on wheat-containing soy sauce and other seasonings; no evidence of any gluten-free accommodation was found. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, dish, or accommodation was found in any source. The menu is centered on pork-filled gyoza and xiaolongbao and other meat-forward Chinese-izakaya dishes; no plant-based options were identified.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya — near Shinsen Station
places.notes: Fupao Sakaba (福包酒場) is a casual offshoot of the same operators' upscale restaurant, 中国家庭料理 香園 (Chinese home cooking restaurant Kōen); the brand was created to bring the same dim-sum techniques and recipes to a lower price point. It has since grown into a multi-location Chinese-iz · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: 特級点心師 (senior dim sum chef, unnamed) prepares the dumplings
chef_bio.background: Fupao Sakaba (福包酒場) is a casual offshoot of the same operators' upscale restaurant, 中国家庭料理 香園 (Chinese home cooking restaurant Kōen); the brand was created to bring the same dim-sum techniques and recipes to a lower price point. It has since grown into a multi-location Chinese-izakaya chain with branches across Tokyo (Roppongi, Shibuya, Ikebukuro), Kanagawa, Osaka, Sendai and other cities, and is actively recruiting franchisees. This Shibuya branch sits in the middle of Shibuya Chuo-gai on Dogenzaka, about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station's south exit / Inokashira Line west exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant describes itself as committed to its ingredients, cooking methods, dining space and hospitality ("食材や調理法、空間から接客まで、お客様をおもてなしすることにこだわっています"), aiming to entertain guests visually as well as through flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: 5-color soup dumplings (5色小籠包) — five hand-made xiaolongbao, each wrapped in a differently colored, differently flavored skin, served in a bamboo steamer that releases a cloud of steam when opened; listed as the shop's most popular item, especially with women.

anecdotes.text: Monday–Friday 4–6pm the restaurant runs a 60-minute all-you-can-drink deal on sour cocktails for 1 yen, letting guests choose two flavors from the menu.
The five-color xiaolongbao are prized as much for the burst of steam and color when the steamer basket is opened as for their flavor, making them a popular photo moment.

cultural_comfort.note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; ordering by pointing at photos or basic Japanese should be sufficient at this casual izakaya.

places.name: 名代 白木そば (Meidai Shiraki Soba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle, sauce, or accommodation is advertised anywhere. This is a conventional soba counter using standard (wheat-buckwheat blend, not 十割/juwari) noodles, and its signature dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce-based, which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing — not appropriate for celiac-level gluten-free needs. No allergy information was found on Tabelog or any secondary source.
places.vegan_detail: The menu revolves around beef and duck tsukesoba with soy/dashi-based dipping broths; no vegan-labeled dishes, plant-based broth, or dashi substitution were found in any source. Even the vegetable-leaning options (nori soba, tororo/yam soba) are served with the same soy-based dipping sauce, which likely contains bonito-derived dashi.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri) 11:00-17:00; closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
places.neighborhood: Suidobashi, Bunkyo-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Originally a standard stand-up (tachigui) soba counter on Hakusan-dori between Suidobashi and Korakuen near Tokyo Dome, the shop reinvented itself around early 2020 into a dipping-soba (tsukesoba) specialist built around a single signature bowl of beef tsukesoba. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Originally a standard stand-up (tachigui) soba counter on Hakusan-dori between Suidobashi and Korakuen near Tokyo Dome, the shop reinvented itself around early 2020 into a dipping-soba (tsukesoba) specialist built around a single signature bowl of beef tsukesoba.
chef_bio.specialty: Gyuniku (beef) tsukesoba — thick, firm soba noodles served alongside a rich, chili-oil-spiked dipping broth topped with simmered beef; other versions include nori, grated yam (tororo), and duck.

anecdotes.text: The shop shifted from a generic stand-up soba counter to a dedicated tsukesoba specialist focused on hearty beef soba made with deliberately hard, thick noodles.
Featured as a weekday lunch pick for the Hongo/Suidobashi office-worker crowd, ordered via a ticket vending machine at the counter.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine at the counter (PayPay QR accepted as an alternative), with no indication of an English menu — comfortable for diners with basic Japanese or point-and-order confidence.

places.name: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 新宿京王モール店（嵯峨谷） (Juwari Soba Sagatani — Shinjuku Keio Mall)
places.gf_detail: The soba noodles are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat flour used as a binder, which is unusual for soba (most soba blends in wheat flour) and a genuine plus for wheat-avoidant diners. However, the tsuyu dipping sauce is a standard Japanese preparation and Japanese soy sauce is normally brewed with wheat as well as soybeans, so the sauce very likely contains wheat; tempura toppings use ordinary wheat-flour batter and are fried in what is likely a shared fryer. No dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or allergen protocol for this location was found, so this cannot be confirmed safe for celiac disease — order plain soba and ask staff about the sauce and frying oil if strict avoidance is required.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-focused: the standard tsuyu/broth for both hot and cold soba is made with kombu and bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, so the default dipping sauce and hot soup contain fish. No plant-based dashi or dedicated vegan menu was found for this location. The soba noodles themselves are just buckwheat and water (no egg), but they are normally served with the fish-based sauce/broth, and tempura batter/toppings are not confirmed vegan either.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku Station West Exit / Keio Mall (西新宿)
places.notes: Sagatani was launched in Tokyo in 2011 by operator For You Co., Ltd. (株式会社フォーユー) to bring authentic 100%-buckwheat 'juwari' soba — normally the domain of pricier specialty soba-ya — into the cheap, fast tachigui (stand-and-eat) soba format. The chain grew to roughly 8-9 locations · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Branch of the Sagatani (嵯峨谷) 100%-buckwheat soba chain
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain founded 2011)
chef_bio.background: Sagatani was launched in Tokyo in 2011 by operator For You Co. , Ltd. (株式会社フォーユー) to bring authentic 100%-buckwheat 'juwari' soba — normally the domain of pricier specialty soba-ya — into the cheap, fast tachigui (stand-and-eat) soba format. The chain grew to roughly 8-9 locations across central Tokyo. This Shinjuku Keio Mall branch sits underground in the Keio Mall shopping arcade just off Shinjuku Station's west exit and opened in December 2021.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's motto is '挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて' (freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried): buckwheat is stone-ground on site and the flour's water content is adjusted daily to match the weather and season, aiming to deliver specialty-shop soba quality at stand-and-eat prices and speed.
chef_bio.specialty: 十割そば (juwari soba) — noodles made from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat used as a binder, milled fresh in-house each day on an in-store electric stone mill.

anecdotes.text: Another reviewer calls it their 'go-to spot when the weather turns cold' (寒くなった時の定番), describing it as a reliable, comforting choice near the station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Restaurants in most cases boil udon and soba in the same large pot (same water), so cross-contamination via the boiling water requires caution; and restaurant menus have no obligation to display allergens.

Many restaurants share frying oil for tempura, tonkatsu and other fried foods; Sagatani's tempura is described by a user as low-quality (soba is the flagship, tempura is cost-cut) — indicating no dedicated GF frying setup.

GF blog promoting Sagatani frames it as gluten-free for people doing GF 'for daily performance and health,' and notes tempura/tempura flakes contain gluten and regular soy sauce is brewed with wheat — i.e., strict-GF diners should avoid those — which is lifestyle-GF framing, not a celiac-safety guarantee.

Search-surfaced FindMeGlutenFree text ('ask for gluten free menu... they change the flour to rice for frying... gluten-free soy sauce available') does not clearly attribute these accommodations to the Shinjuku Keio Mall branch and appears conflated across Sagatani/Shibuya locations; unverifiable and single-source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), the base of soba tsuyu, is typically brewed from wheat and soybeans together — this is general Japanese food-labeling guidance, not a statement specific to this shop's own recipe, but it applies to conventional tsuyu of the kind this chain serves.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The chain's broth is described as made with kombu and a blend of dried bonito (katsuobushi) stocks for both the cold and hot tsuyu, meaning the soup base is fish-derived and not vegan.

positives.text: Noodles are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat used as a binder, milled fresh in-store daily on an electric stone mill — a genuine plus versus ordinary soba, which typically blends in wheat flour.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine (食券制) — cash or transit IC card — typical of fast soba counters, so a little Japanese or willingness to point at machine buttons/photos helps.

places.name: 麺屋武蔵 武骨外伝 (Menya Musashi Bukotsu Gaiden)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat noodles are used with no gluten-free noodle alternative found. The broths are built on chicken/pork-bone stock with dried sardine (niboshi) and bonito (katsuobushi), and the tare/dipping pastes (soy-based black, miso-based red) are traditional wheat-containing seasonings. No dedicated gluten-free process, allergen menu, or cross-contamination protocol was found for this location — not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broths are made from chicken/pork-bone stock combined with dried sardine (niboshi) and bonito flakes (katsuobushi) — not vegetarian or vegan. No plant-based tsukemen option was found advertised for this specific Shibuya branch (the company does sell a separate plant-based ramen line at other locations, but it is not confirmed here).
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:30, no closed days (無休)
places.notes: Menya Musashi (麺屋武蔵) is a Tokyo ramen company founded in 1996 in Aoyama by Yamada Yuu, part of the influential wave of "1996-generation" Tokyo ramen shops credited with reshaping the genre. Bukotsu Gaiden ("the rugged sequel") is one of the company's Shibuya spin-off/sister brand · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Shibuya branch of the Menya Musashi ramen group, specializing in tsukemen
chef_bio.background: Menya Musashi (麺屋武蔵) is a Tokyo ramen company founded in 1996 in Aoyama by Yamada Yuu, part of the influential wave of "1996-generation" Tokyo ramen shops credited with reshaping the genre. Bukotsu Gaiden ("the rugged sequel") is one of the company's Shibuya spin-off/sister brands, opened in the mid-2000s a short walk from Shibuya Station, and it has become one of the busiest tsukemen (dipping noodle) specialists in the neighborhood, known for very generous, freely-refillable noodle portions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Menya Musashi's founding concept was to run a ramen shop with the warmth and hospitality of a good izakaya, on the belief that the whole dining experience — not just the taste of the broth — is what makes a bowl of ramen genuinely satisfying.
chef_bio.specialty: Noukou Gaiden Kuro Tsukemen (rich black dipping noodles) — thick, chewy noodles served cold alongside a choice of three dipping broths: black (finished with a special soy-based tare), red (finished with a spicy miso paste), and plain white (no added paste), each available in a standard or richer katsuobushi-enhanced version.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers three colors of dipping sauce — white, black, and red — each built on a shared chicken-and-pork-bone base with dried sardine and bonito, letting diners compare three distinct flavor profiles in one visit.
A regular customer described it as the reward at the end of a long workday, praising the volume of noodles (up to 1kg free) and the richness of the black dipping broth topped with a soft-boiled egg.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The black-style dipping broth is finished with a special soy-sauce-based tare (paste), a wheat-containing seasoning typical of shoyu-style ramen.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only seating with a Japanese-language ticket machine and no reservations; the three broth colors (white/black/red) are easy to identify visually even without fluent Japanese, but ordering noodle amount/toppings may require pointing or basic phrases.

places.name: らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店 (Raamen Hayashida Shinjuku Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop: the noodles are wheat-based and the tare/seasoning for both the shoyu and shio versions relies on soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute, allergy-friendly menu, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination information was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a duck-and-chicken-bone clear consommé ("鶏清湯"), which is central to the shop's identity and offered in every ramen bowl. No vegetarian or vegan broth alternative, plant-based noodle, or meat-free menu item was found in any source, and additional non-vegan ingredients (e.g., chashu pork/chicken) are standard toppings. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 daily (無休 / no fixed closed day); may close early ("早仕舞い") if the soup runs out before closing time.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku 3-chome)
places.notes: Hayashida built its name on a clear, richly flavored chicken "seiyu" (clear consommé-style) broth. Whole chicken and duck are simmered from early morning for hours, with the cooks carefully skimming off scum (aku) so the finished soup stays clean and translucent rather than cloud · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Flagship ("honten") shop of the Hayashida ramen brand, run by the INGS restaurant group, located a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station; the brand has since expanded to other Tokyo locations such as Ikebukuro.
chef_bio.background: Hayashida built its name on a clear, richly flavored chicken "seiyu" (clear consommé-style) broth. Whole chicken and duck are simmered from early morning for hours, with the cooks carefully skimming off scum (aku) so the finished soup stays clean and translucent rather than cloudy.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen centers the bowl on pure chicken umami, using duck to add a rounder, sweeter backbone to the broth, then balances that roundness with a crisp, well-defined tare (seasoning sauce) so the soup doesn't taste flat.
chef_bio.specialty: Tokusei shoyu ramen (特製醤油らぁ麺) — a soy-sauce ramen built on the shop's signature clear chicken-and-duck broth, served alongside a salt (shio) version and tsukemen.

anecdotes.text: Duck and whole chicken are used generously from early morning and simmered for a long time, with the cooks skimming the scum thoroughly to produce a highly refined, clear soup.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (aggregated blog/site summaries for らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店)

anecdotes.text: Bowls are pre-warmed before the soup and noodles are added, a small technique meant to keep the ramen from cooling too quickly once served.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (aggregated blog/site summaries for らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店)

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku honten is known as a perpetually busy, queue-forming shop near Shinjuku Sanchome Station.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu (soy sauce) ramen's tare is a standard Japanese soy-sauce-based seasoning; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari or GF alternative was mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine typical of Tokyo ramen shops, so simple Japanese/pointing gets you through, but there's no need for fluent Japanese.

places.name: ヴィーガン居酒屋 真さか (Vegan Izakaya Masaka)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or wheat-free certification was found. As a full-menu izakaya it very likely uses standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce in marinades and dipping sauces, and the signature soy-meat karaage is deep-fried, so cross-contact with wheat-based batter/flour in the fryer is probable. The restaurant is listed on WE TABLE, a Tokyo directory of allergy-conscious dining spots, suggesting some willingness to discuss allergens, but no specific celiac-safety statement (dedicated fryer, wheat-free tamari, etc.) was confirmed. Contact the restaurant directly before relying on this for celiac-level gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based menu (20+ dishes always available) — soy-meat karaage, vegan gyoza, and other classic izakaya small plates, all made without meat, fish, dairy, or eggs. As a 100%-vegan kitchen there is no hidden dashi/katsuobushi (bonito) risk from the standard menu.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-22:00 daily (Lunch 12:00-15:00 / Dinner 15:00-22:00; Last order food 21:00, drinks 21:30)
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya) — Udagawa-cho, inside Shibuya PARCO
places.notes: Run by Bird Feather Nobu Co. (株式会社バードフェザーノブ), the shop set out to recreate a classic, everyday Japanese izakaya — complete with a Showa-retro noren curtain entrance — using only plant-based ingredients throughout the kitchen. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Vegan izakaya restaurant, part of a small two-location group (Tokyo + Kyoto)
chef_bio.origin: Opened in November 2019 when Shibuya PARCO reopened after renovation; a second location opened in Kyoto (Horikawa-Takatsuji) in December 2024.
chef_bio.background: Run by Bird Feather Nobu Co. (株式会社バードフェザーノブ), the shop set out to recreate a classic, everyday Japanese izakaya — complete with a Showa-retro noren curtain entrance — using only plant-based ingredients throughout the kitchen.
chef_bio.philosophy: The team wants vegan food to feel like normal, affordable izakaya fare rather than a special or expensive category, so that vegan and non-vegan customers can sit down and eat the same dishes together. In interviews the shop describes wanting to subvert the assumption that vegan cooking is difficult or costly to access, and to patiently walk first-time or unfamiliar customers through what 'vegan' actually means on the menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Soy-meat karaage (plant-based 'fried chicken') — crispy outside and moist inside — served with a choice of about seven sauces, alongside vegan gyoza and lemon sours.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant keeps a retro Showa-era izakaya look with a noren curtain entrance, and draws daily interest from both regular Japanese customers and international visitors curious about vegan food.

Staff are described as taking care to walk unfamiliar or skeptical customers through the menu, addressing the common feeling that vegan food is 'unfamiliar' or 'looks difficult,' rather than assuming everyone already understands veganism.
The shop's stated goal is to 'subvert expectations in a good way,' building an izakaya where vegans and non-vegans can enjoy the same affordable, everyday dishes together.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish on the menu is made using only plant-derived ingredients — the kitchen does not use meat, fish, dairy, or eggs at all, which removes the risk of animal-product cross-contact that vegan diners would face in a mixed kitchen.

positives.text: The restaurant is listed on WE TABLE, a Tokyo guide specifically curating restaurants that accommodate food allergies and special diets.
Also listed on Vegewel, a dedicated vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free restaurant directory for Japan.

cultural_comfort.note: It sits inside the very tourist-heavy Shibuya PARCO complex and multiple write-ups mention international visitors dining here, but no confirmed English-language menu was found, so basic Japanese/pointing may still be needed.

places.name: マルゴエス (MARUGO S)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or allergen chart was found in the official site, opening press release, or blog reviews checked. As a "spice curry" concept the sauces may or may not use a wheat-flour roux (unconfirmed either way), and the achar pickles, spiced egg preparation, and any soy sauce used are not documented for wheat content. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about roux composition and cross-contact before ordering — this cannot be confirmed as safe from available sources.
places.vegan_detail: The three rotating signature curries are all meat-based — lamb & spinach keema, chicken-wing and hazelnut butter chicken, and Ibaraki-grown Sanemon-pork vindaloo — served with jasmine rice, three types of spice achar pickles, a spiced egg, and herb salad. No vegetable-only or plant-based curry option, and no vegan menu, was found in the official site, press materials, or blog coverage reviewed. Hidden animal ingredients (butter, dairy in the butter-chicken curry, egg) are explicitly present, so this is not vegan-friendly based on current information.
places.hours_raw: 11:00〜23:00（FOOD L.O. 22:00／DRINK L.O. 22:30）。年中無休。12月31日のみ翌2:00まで延長営業、1月1日は通常通り営業。
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 / 東京ドームシティ (Suidobashi / Tokyo Dome City), Bunkyo-ku
places.cuisine: スパイスカレー・ワインバル
places.notes: MARUGO S opened December 9, 2025 as the MARUGO group's first food-hall concept store, inside the newly built FOOD STADIUM TOKYO on the 2nd floor of Tokyo Dome City's 'yellow building,' a few minutes from JR Suidobashi Station. The name's 'S' is said to stand for spice, stadium, s · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (opened by the Tokyo-based MARUGO restaurant group)
chef_bio.background: MARUGO S opened December 9, 2025 as the MARUGO group's first food-hall concept store, inside the newly built FOOD STADIUM TOKYO on the 2nd floor of Tokyo Dome City's 'yellow building,' a few minutes from JR Suidobashi Station. The name's 'S' is said to stand for spice, stadium, start, standing, and special. Rather than a normal shop, it runs a dedicated 24-seat island counter within the shared food hall, where staff handle the order-to-serve process directly.
chef_bio.philosophy: Builds on the MARUGO group's French-Italian bistro roots while layering in Asian and Cajun spices and herbs for bar-style food meant to pair with wine, beer, and highballs; the group is known for taking care with slow-prepared venison and lamb.
chef_bio.specialty: A monthly-rotating spice curry, served single or as a three-way combination plate (1,800 yen) — lamb & spinach keema, chicken-wing and hazelnut butter chicken, and Ibaraki Sanemon-pork vindaloo — with jasmine rice, three types of spice achar pickles, a spiced egg, and herb salad.

anecdotes.text: A blog reviewer described MARUGO S as a hidden-gem seating option inside the often fully-booked FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall, noting the three-way curry combo's flavor is approachable and not aggressively spiced, making it easy to eat.

The shop is also known for a delicate 'snow' dessert — yogurt-based shaved ice with fresh berries, pistachio sauce, and espuma — that reportedly must be eaten within about five minutes before it melts.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering and menu appear Japanese-only in available sources; however it sits inside Tokyo Dome City's food hall, a major tourist-trafficked complex, so staff are likely used to some non-Japanese-speaking visitors even though this isn't confirmed.

places.name: 豆腐料理 吉座 京王百貨店新宿店 (Tofu Ryori Kichiza (Keio Department Store Sh)
places.gf_detail: No source confirms gluten-free accommodation. As a traditional Japanese kaiseki-style restaurant, dishes very likely rely on standard shoyu (soy sauce, which typically contains wheat) and dashi that may include bonito/other seasonings; fried yuba items imply a shared fryer of unconfirmed dedicated status. Tofu itself is naturally gluten-free, but sauces, dips, and cooking liquids are the main risk points — celiac diners should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering, ideally in Japanese or with a translated allergy card.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant despite the tofu/yuba specialty framing: reported course menus include tuna & yuba sashimi and beef grilled on a magnolia leaf. Vegans could likely request tofu/yuba-only a la carte items, but dashi used in cooking liquids commonly contains bonito (katsuobushi) in traditional Japanese kaiseki cooking, and this has not been confirmed either way for this restaurant — ask staff directly about dashi and sauce ingredients.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. reported as 21:00 in some sources, 20:30 in the Keio Department Store floor guide), daily, aligned with department-store operating hours
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku / Shinjuku Station (Keio Department Store Shi
places.notes: Tofu Ryori Kichiza is a tofu/yuba specialty restaurant located on the 8th-floor dining floor of Keio Department Store's Shinjuku store, part of a small group of "Kichiza" tofu restaurants (a sister location operates in Kioicho) run under Create Restaurants Holdings. The kitchen b · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Nikko, Tochigi (spring water and soybean sourcing)
chef_bio.background: Tofu Ryori Kichiza is a tofu/yuba specialty restaurant located on the 8th-floor dining floor of Keio Department Store's Shinjuku store, part of a small group of "Kichiza" tofu restaurants (a sister location operates in Kioicho) run under Create Restaurants Holdings. The kitchen builds its cooking around Nikko tofu, made with domestic soybeans and spring water associated with Nikko, a region historically known for clean mountain water used in tofu-making.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes seasonal ingredients prepared with care around its signature Nikko-water tofu and yuba, in a traditional Japanese dining setting with private-room options.
chef_bio.specialty: Nikko tofu (日光豆冨) and kumiage yuba (fresh-scooped tofu skin), served as part of multi-course kaiseki-style set menus

anecdotes.text: Course menus feature tofu prepared multiple ways alongside dishes such as dobin-mushi (steamed savory custard/soup), tuna and yuba sashimi, fried yuba rolls, and beef grilled on a magnolia leaf, with staff noted by reviewers as attentive and dressed in traditional attire.
The clientele is reported to be predominantly women, reflecting its popularity as a refined lunch/dinner destination inside the department store.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source explicitly states whether wheat-free (tamari-style) soy sauce is available; traditional kaiseki restaurants of this type typically use standard wheat-containing shoyu unless otherwise noted.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: inference from cuisine type, not directly confirmed by a source

positives.text: Core ingredient (Nikko tofu/yuba) is naturally gluten-free and plant-based, giving a strong base to work from if staff confirm sauce and dashi ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Japanese department-store restaurant where menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese, though the Tabelog listing has an English page and picture-menu courses should be navigable for non-Japanese speakers with some help.

places.name: 定食サトウ (Teishoku Satō)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation is documented anywhere. This is a traditional Japanese home-style diner; dishes almost certainly use regular soy sauce (contains wheat) and bonito/dashi-based miso soup and sauces, with no indication of a gluten-free alternative. Not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff — and even then, cross-contamination in a small home-style kitchen is likely unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: Set meals are centered on a fish or meat main dish and miso soup, and traditional dashi in Japanese home cooking is typically bonito (fish)-based. No vegan menu, vegetable-only teishoku option, or dashi substitution has been documented at this shop. Not a reliable choice for vegan diners without direct confirmation, and even then hidden animal-based dashi in side dishes is a real risk.
places.neighborhood: 神泉（渋谷区円山町）／渋谷駅徒歩9分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small basement set-meal shop near Shinsen Station that changes character through the day: at lunchtime it runs as a slightly more considered teishoku-ya, and by evening the mood shifts into a calm, low-key drinking spot for the neighborhood. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A small basement set-meal shop near Shinsen Station that changes character through the day: at lunchtime it runs as a slightly more considered teishoku-ya, and by evening the mood shifts into a calm, low-key drinking spot for the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than a fixed menu, the kitchen rotates through home-style Japanese dishes each day, with particular attention to the small vegetable side dishes that accompany the main plate.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-changing teishoku built around a simple grilled or simmered main — such as tsuke-yaki salmon — served with about seven vegetable-forward side dishes, rice, and miso soup; an omakase course expands this to seven small dishes plus a main, rice, and soup.

anecdotes.text: A local food blogger titled their review of the shop "the meticulous cooking makes you want to sit up straighter out of respect," describing the care put into the small side dishes.
A dining writeup described it as a hidden local favorite near Shinsen worth discovering for its teishoku sets.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu, signage, and reviews are entirely in Japanese with no evidence of English support, so basic Japanese or pointing at the daily specials board will help.

places.name: 日本料理 縁（ゆくり） (Nihon Ryori Yukuri (Yukuri Japanese Cuisine))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any source. As a traditional kaiseki restaurant, dishes likely include soy-sauce-based seasonings, dashi, and tempura (all commonly containing wheat) alongside the restaurant's signature hand-cut soba (wheat-containing, cross-contact risk for anyone avoiding gluten). Celiac diners should call ahead to discuss ingredients and cross-contamination before booking.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or modification policy was found. The restaurant's signature offerings center on seafood delivered fresh from Boso and a seasonal kaiseki course, which in traditional Japanese cooking typically relies on bonito-based dashi. No mention of plant-based dashi alternatives or vegan course substitutions was found in official or third-party sources; vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:30–22:00 (course L.O. 20:30, food L.O. 21:00, drinks L.O. 21:30). Closed for dinner on Sundays, with additional irregular dinner closures in some months.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町 (Suidōbashi area)
places.notes: Yukuri carries forward the full-course kaiseki dining tradition of the property's ryokan predecessor while updating it for a contemporary boutique-hotel setting near Suidobashi Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: head chef sources ingredients personally from producing regions
chef_bio.origin: Grew out of the traditional Japanese inn (ryokan) that occupied the site before it became the boutique Hotel Niwa Tokyo; the restaurant now stands as a separate tile-roofed building beside the hotel entrance, reached by a stone path, overlooking an inner courtyard garden.
chef_bio.background: Yukuri carries forward the full-course kaiseki dining tradition of the property's ryokan predecessor while updating it for a contemporary boutique-hotel setting near Suidobashi Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Yukuri is an old Japanese word for "the bonds between people"; it was chosen with the hope that guests would meet, form connections, and unwind over an unhurried meal. The head chef travels personally to source regions to select seasonal ingredients, then prepares them with a blend of traditional technique and modern sensibility so that each monthly course reads like a seasonal narrative.
chef_bio.specialty: Monthly-changing kaiseki course built around seafood delivered directly from the Boso peninsula and Edo-vegetables sourced from nearby farms, finished with the chef's own hand-cut soba noodles.

anecdotes.text: Each monthly kaiseki course is described as woven with a theme and story like an illustrated tale (e-monogatari), meant to be experienced dish by dish with all five senses.
A seasonal opening dish (hajime no gozai) was highlighted by the hotel as evoking the scenery of winter through its texture and natural sweetness.

cultural_comfort.note: Housed inside a boutique hotel accustomed to international guests (Tabelog and reservation platforms list an English-language page), but no confirmation of fully English-fluent floor service was found, so expect basic English support rather than guaranteed fluency.

places.name: なぎ食堂 (Nagi Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The menu is rice- and vegetable-forward (brown rice sets, miso soup, vegetable sides), but standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce and miso typically contain wheat, and no source confirms wheat-free substitutes, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocols for gluten. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten sensitivity should call ahead and confirm specific dishes/condiments directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan menu: multiple independent sources confirm the restaurant uses no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish (肉・魚・卵など動物性食品を使わない). The signature "Nagi A-Set" includes brown rice, miso soup, soy-meat karaage, and rotating vegetable sides; lunch offers ~14 rotating vegetable side dishes with a choice of 3. Desserts are also stated to be dairy- and egg-free. No source specifically confirms dashi is kombu/vegetable-only rather than bonito-based, but the consistent "no fish" claim across sources implies bonito-free preparation.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-16:00 (L.O.15:00) / 18:00-23:00 (L.O.22:30); Sunday closes at 16:00 (lunch service only, no dinner). 定休日: 不定休 (irregular closures).
