places.notes: Oda Akinobu, a Kyoto-born (1967) former music-magazine editor and international touring/artist-booking coordinator, opened Nagi Shokudo in December 2007 in Shibuya's quiet Uguisudani-cho backstreet. He later authored several books about the shop, including "渋谷のすみっこでベジ食堂" ("A Veg  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (b. 1967)
chef_bio.background: Oda Akinobu, a Kyoto-born (1967) former music-magazine editor and international touring/artist-booking coordinator, opened Nagi Shokudo in December 2007 in Shibuya's quiet Uguisudani-cho backstreet. He later authored several books about the shop, including "渋谷のすみっこでベジ食堂" ("A Veg Diner in a Corner of Shibuya") and vegetable-recipe collections.
chef_bio.philosophy: Oda noticed that many of the touring overseas musicians he booked and worked with were vegetarian, and that Japan had very few vegetarian/vegan restaurants with bold, rice- and drink-friendly flavors. He set out to create a place serving hearty, flavorful all-vegan food where music-scene friends could also gather casually, like a friend's living room.
chef_bio.specialty: なぎAセット (Nagi A-Set): soft-cooked brown rice, miso soup, soy-meat karaage (fried soy-meat 'chicken'), and a rotating daily vegetable side — the restaurant's signature set meal (about ¥1,100). Lunch offers a choice of 3 from roughly 14 rotating vegetable side dishes.

anecdotes.text: Oda opened the restaurant after realizing, through his work booking international touring artists (many of whom were vegetarian), that Japan lacked vegetarian restaurants serving richly flavored food that pairs well with rice and alcohol — and wanted a spot where his music-industry friends could also gather.

The dining room has a lived-in, 'friend's house' atmosphere with indie rock playing, table seating plus a small raised tatami-style nook, and draws a mixed crowd of women, older men, Japanese residents, and foreign tourists/expats alike.

gf_cross_contamination.text: なぎ食堂 (vegan shokudo) in Shibuya Uguisudani-cho closed on December 23, 2023 after operating 16 years since 2007; owner pivoted to catering and event food services.
Tabelog now lists the Shibuya location with the 【移転】(relocated/closed) tag rather than as an operating restaurant.
Celiac review: the restaurant had limited gluten-free options — no soup or warm food was gluten free on the entire menu; the green salad was the solitary safe option.
Search corroboration that the Uguisudani-cho storefront (Shibuya-ku Uguisudanicho 15-10) permanently closed 2023-12-23 and did not relocate to a replacement Shibuya storefront.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares no meat, fish, or animal products at all — every dish on the menu is vegan by default, so there is no risk of animal-product cross-contact from shared equipment with non-vegan dishes.

positives.text: The restaurant uses no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish, making it explicitly safe for customers with egg and dairy allergies as well as vegans/vegetarians.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, casual neighborhood spot popular with both Japanese regulars and foreign residents/tourists (it is one of the few fully vegan options near Shibuya Station), though no source confirms an English menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: つけ麺 ズッパ 水道橋店 (Tsukemen Zuppa (Suidobashi))
places.gf_detail: No source mentions a gluten-free noodle, tamari (wheat-free soy sauce), or any dedicated gluten-free accommodation. The tsukemen and ramen use standard wheat noodles, and the soy-sauce and miso ramen bases most likely contain wheat-based shoyu. The shop's flexibility is aimed at vegan/vegetarian and allium-free requests, not gluten. This is not a place to rely on for celiac-safe dining without directly confirming details with staff in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: Every dish on the standard menu already excludes meat and fish, using soy "meat" and soy milk in place of animal protein and dashi. The default tsukemen set does come with a boiled egg in mayonnaise-style sauce, but telling staff you want it vegan gets the egg left off; the soy-milk tantanmen and soy-meat ramen variants can likewise be requested vegan and without the pungent alliums (garlic, onion, etc.). No source mentions bonito/katsuo dashi anywhere on the menu.
places.neighborhood: 神田三崎町 (Suidobashi), Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: Tsukemen zuppa opened in September 2021, a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station, as a compact counter-only noodle shop built entirely around meat- and fish-free tsukemen, ramen, and soy-milk tantanmen. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Tsukemen zuppa opened in September 2021, a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station, as a compact counter-only noodle shop built entirely around meat- and fish-free tsukemen, ramen, and soy-milk tantanmen.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leaves meat and fish off the entire menu and builds its soups and toppings from vegetables and soy protein instead, positioning itself as Suidobashi's only vegan-focused tsukemen and ramen shop; dishes can also be prepared without the "five pungent" alliums (garlic, onion, etc. ) on request.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable tsukemen (dipping noodles) — a soup and topping set made from vegetables and soy-based ingredients, served with a boiled-egg-and-mayonnaise side dish and fried lotus root.

anecdotes.text: Word of the all-vegetable menu has spread mainly by mouth among vegan visitors from overseas, who make up much of the counter's crowd.
The standard tsukemen set arrives with a boiled egg in mayonnaise-style sauce and a fried lotus root alongside a half salad; telling staff you want the vegan version gets the egg left off.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base menu already excludes meat and fish across every dish, using soy "meat" and soy milk instead of animal protein and dashi, so there is no shared meat/fish cooking line to cross with a vegan order.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Vegan modifications — such as leaving the boiled egg off the tsukemen set, or preparing the soy-milk tantanmen without the pungent alliums — are made to order once you tell staff after buying your ticket at the vending machine.

positives.text: The kitchen uses no meat or fish anywhere on the menu; soup and toppings are vegetable- and soy-protein-based throughout, marketed as guilt-free even if you finish the dipping broth.
Described as Suidobashi's only vegan-focused tsukemen and ramen shop, popular with vegan and vegetarian diners including foreign tourists.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-only ticket machine at the entrance, but the shop is well known among vegan foreign visitors and staff are used to fielding simple vegan-modification requests by ear.

places.name: お野菜小皿料理のワインバル KiboKo（キボコ／旧称 slowfood&wine KiboKo） (Oyasai Kozara Ryori no Wine Bar KiboKo (also)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant is indexed by Vegewel's gluten-free filter and multiple guides describe it as having gluten-free menu items, but it is not a dedicated celiac-safe kitchen. It's a small space essentially run by one person, so cross-contact between dishes is possible, and no source confirms wheat-free soy sauce or a dedicated fryer. Diners with celiac disease (rather than a general gluten preference) should call ahead (03-6380-1709) to confirm which specific dishes are truly gluten-free and how they're prepared.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is fully vegan by design — every dish, including sauces and any dashi-style bases, is made without meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or honey. This isn't a case of an omnivorous menu with a few vegan substitutions; the whole restaurant concept is animal-free, so there's no hidden bonito/katsuo dashi or fish sauce to watch for.
places.hours_raw: 水〜日 12:00〜17:00（ランチは前日までの要予約）、18:00〜22:00（完全予約制）。定休日：月・火曜日
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.cuisine: ビーガン・野菜小皿料理・自然派ワインバル
places.notes: Opened April 8, 2016 a one-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station. The restaurant is essentially run by its owner alone, who cooks and hosts the room herself, which is why both lunch and dinner are reservation-based rather than walk-in. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Numanami (owner, given name as referenced in press coverage)
chef_bio.background: Opened April 8, 2016 a one-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station. The restaurant is essentially run by its owner alone, who cooks and hosts the room herself, which is why both lunch and dinner are reservation-based rather than walk-in.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Kiboko is Swahili for hippopotamus: an animal with a calm, grass-eating image that is in fact powerful, and was revered in ancient Egypt as a protective goddess of women and children. The owner chose the name because she wanted to build a place that supports and heals hard-working women through honest, plant-based food and natural wine.
chef_bio.specialty: A faux 'Spanish omelette' made from pumpkin, mountain yam, and tofu in place of egg, and cilantro (pakuchi) gyoza dumplings, described as the most popular item on the menu.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name comes from the Swahili word for hippopotamus, chosen for the animal's gentle-but-powerful image and its ancient Egyptian association with protecting women and children — the owner wanted Kiboko to be a place that nourishes and heals busy working women.
The owner has hosted in-house workshops on traditional crafts such as kintsugi (gold-seam pottery repair) and miso-making alongside the restaurant's regular service.
The most recommended dish is a Spanish-omelette-style plate made without egg (pumpkin, mountain yam, tofu), and the No. 1 most-ordered item is the coriander/cilantro gyoza dumplings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built as 100% vegan from the ground up — no meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or bonito/katsuo dashi is used anywhere in the kitchen — so there is essentially no cross-contact risk from animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Handmade dishes use organic, additive-free vegetables and seasonings, and the concept was built around fully plant-based cooking rather than adapting an omnivorous menu.
Listed on Vegewel's vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free restaurant directory, which filters specifically for gluten-free-friendly menu items.

cultural_comfort.note: Roughly 40% of the clientele are visitors from abroad (many vegan themselves) and an English menu is available, so English speakers should feel comfortable — just be sure to reserve ahead since the small, owner-run kitchen doesn't take casual walk-ins.

places.name: 東京純豆腐 新宿店 (Tokyo Sundubu Shinjuku (Tokyo Jundoufu Shinj)
places.gf_detail: This is not a gluten-free-oriented restaurant. Chain-wide allergen disclosures for the sundubu broth list wheat, soy, and sesame among the ingredients (the soup is built on a gochujang-seasoned, beef-bone-and-seafood base), and abura-age (fried tofu) is used in the standard broth. No dedicated gluten-free menu or prep area is documented for this location. Some branches keep a printed allergy/ingredient chart near the register that staff can walk through with guests on request, but this has not been confirmed specifically for the Shinjuku branch. Celiac diners should ask staff to check the allergy chart for wheat before ordering and should not assume any dish is wheat-free by default.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The base broth used for every standard sundubu on the menu contains clams, fried tofu (abura-age), egg, and shellfish extract, and the wider chain allergen list for the soup also includes chicken, beef, and pork stock components. No vegan or vegetarian sundubu variant is documented for this location; one review noted that diners with a sundubu allergy sometimes order bibimbap instead, but that dish is not confirmed to be vegan either (it typically includes egg and/or meat). Vegan diners should not expect a safe default option here without extensive modification, if any is possible at all.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sun, holidays and pre-holidays: 11:00-22:00 (food last order 21:30)
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（新宿区新宿3丁目）
places.cuisine: 韓国料理・スンドゥブ
places.notes: Tokyo Sundubu (東京純豆腐) is a Korean soft-tofu-stew (sundubu jjigae) specialty chain run by Beans One Company. According to the company's own history page, the founders fell for sundubu while in New York and wanted Japanese diners to feel that same excitement, which led to the brand · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Korean sundubu (soft tofu stew) specialty restaurant, part of a nationwide chain
chef_bio.origin: Korean cuisine, chain concept inspired by sundubu encountered in New York
chef_bio.background: Tokyo Sundubu (東京純豆腐) is a Korean soft-tofu-stew (sundubu jjigae) specialty chain run by Beans One Company. According to the company's own history page, the founders fell for sundubu while in New York and wanted Japanese diners to feel that same excitement, which led to the brand's launch in 2006 — the first four shops, opened that year, included Aoyama, Shibuya, Azabudai and this Shinjuku location. The company describes its goal as making sundubu into a household 'national dish' in Japan and, as of 2026, marks 20 years as (by its own account) the largest sundubu-specialty chain in the country.
chef_bio.philosophy: In-house, daily-made tofu from domestic soybeans, paired with a long-simmered broth (beef-bone base with seafood and vegetables, gochujang-seasoned) that the company markets as its point of pride ('だしにこだわったスープ').
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order sundubu jjigae (soft tofu stew): diners choose a soup base (salt, miso, or white), a spice level (1-6), and rice portion. Tofu is made fresh in-house daily from domestic soybeans and served at the soft, just-set stage before it fully firms.

anecdotes.text: The founders discovered sundubu while in New York and wanted to bring that same excitement back to Japan, which is what led to Tokyo Sundubu's 2006 launch, with this Shinjuku shop among the brand's first locations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The standard sundubu broth used chain-wide contains clams, fried tofu (abura-age), egg, and shellfish extract, and is prepared in a shared kitchen alongside meat and seafood dishes, so cross-contact with wheat/gluten-containing ingredients is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chain-wide allergen listings for the soup include wheat (小麦), soy, and sesame among the ingredients used in the gochujang-based broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All standard sundubu broths include clams, egg, and shellfish extract as base ingredients, so the shared broth is not suitable for vegan diners and cross-contact with animal products is inherent to the standard menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: At least one Tokyo Sundubu branch (Ikebukuro) keeps a printed allergy/ingredient chart near the entrance/register that staff can check with guests on request; this practice is not separately confirmed for the Shinjuku branch.

positives.text: Diners can customize spice level (1-6), soup type, and rice portion at order time, which gives some control over what goes into the bowl even though the base broth itself is fixed.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is simple (pick soup type, spice level 1-6, and rice amount) and the chain has an English-language Tabelog listing, but in-store menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese, so basic Japanese or pointing/apps will help.

places.name: 魚がし日本一 立喰寿司 渋谷道玄坂店 (Uogashi Nihon-ichi Tachigui Sushi Shibuya Do)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated prep area is indicated anywhere. As a traditional Edomae sushi counter, nigiri is typically brushed with a soy-based tare/nikiri sauce that ordinarily contains wheat, and the kitchen also handles fried items, so cross-contact risk is likely. Nothing here is confirmed safe for celiac diners — it would need to be asked about directly at the counter, which may be difficult given the fast, Japanese-language-driven service style.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-centric sushi bar — nearly every item on the menu is fish or shellfish based, and rice/other preparations likely rely on dashi containing bonito (katsuobushi), which is standard in Edomae sushi kitchens. No vegetable-only rolls or vegan-specific items were confirmed in available sources. Not a realistic destination for vegan diners without asking staff directly, and options would likely be very limited even then.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-23:00; Sat 11:00-22:00; Sun 11:00-21:30, per most listings (one source instead states a flat daily 11:30-2:00) — open year-round with no listed regular closed days. Confirm by phone before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya, Dogenzaka
places.notes: Uogashi Nihon-ichi popularized the idea of hand-pressed Edomae sushi served fast, at a standing counter, for working commuters — a deliberate throwback to the roadside sushi stalls (sushi yatai) of Edo-period Tokyo roughly 200 years earlier. The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is one of · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of the "Uogashi Nihon-ichi" chain run by Nippan Co. — Japan's pioneering standing-sushi (tachigui-zushi) concept, whose flagship store opened near Shimbashi Station on June 19, 1989.
chef_bio.background: Uogashi Nihon-ichi popularized the idea of hand-pressed Edomae sushi served fast, at a standing counter, for working commuters — a deliberate throwback to the roadside sushi stalls (sushi yatai) of Edo-period Tokyo roughly 200 years earlier. The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is one of many locations across the chain, sourcing fish daily from markets stretching from Hokkaido in the north to Kyushu in the south.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain's guiding principle since its founding is "nana-tate" (七たて) — seven kinds of freshness: freshly caught, freshly filleted, freshly simmered, freshly grilled, freshly fried, freshly cooked rice, and freshly hand-pressed. The idea is to serve quality, reasonably priced ingredients simply — cold food served cold, hot food served hot.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-pressed nigiri sushi made to order at the standing counter, priced individually by piece so diners can eat as much or as little as they like.

anecdotes.text: The chain's June 19, 1989 opening date at its original Shimbashi store is commemorated on Japanese trivia calendars as related to the standing-sushi format's debut.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language menu and point-and-order style make it workable for non-Japanese speakers, but the fast-paced standing-counter format and staff interactions are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 阿酒羅 (Ashura (Suidōbashi Kaisen Ashura))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free substitutions, or dedicated fryer were mentioned in any source found. The signature dish is a wheat-wrapper shumai, and as a standard izakaya it almost certainly uses regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce and dashi throughout the menu. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: Simmered seasonal vegetables (nimono) are a prominent part of the menu, but the restaurant is explicitly seafood-centric (海鮮), the signature shumai is topped with seafood, and dashi used across the menu is very likely bonito-based. No vegan menu, plant-based substitutions, or vegan-safe dashi were confirmed in any source - ask staff directly before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 月～日・祝日・祝前日 17:00-23:30 (料理L.O. 22:30、ドリンクL.O. 23:00); one secondary source lists closing as late as 24:00 on some listings - primary/most consistent figure used is 23:30.
places.neighborhood: Suidōbashi / Kanda Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku
places.cuisine: Creative izakaya centered on seasonal simmer
places.notes: The owner previously worked in sales at a manufacturing company before moving into the restaurant industry, training for about two years at a restaurant in Nakameguro before opening Ashura near Suidōbashi in Kanda Misakichō in October 2023. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The owner previously worked in sales at a manufacturing company before moving into the restaurant industry, training for about two years at a restaurant in Nakameguro before opening Ashura near Suidōbashi in Kanda Misakichō in October 2023.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name comes from Ashura, the multi-armed Buddhist guardian deity, chosen to symbolize a wish to offer guests everything they might want - vegetables, seafood, meat, and sake. The logo's Ashura figure holds vegetables, fish, meat, and a sake cup in its outer arms while its front two hands are pressed together in a gesture of prayer and gratitude toward customers. The kitchen builds much of the menu around seasonal simmered vegetables (nimono) alongside seafood from Toyosu, aiming for food that lets guests eat and drink while staying health-conscious.
chef_bio.specialty: Ashura's Kaihō Shūmai (阿酒羅の海宝焼売) - handmade shumai dumplings filled with simmered seasonal vegetables and topped with fresh seafood delivered directly from Toyosu Market.

anecdotes.text: The name 阿酒羅 (Ashura) is drawn from the three-faced, six-armed Buddhist deity Asura, chosen to represent a wish to serve every food and drink a guest might want - vegetables, fish, meat, and sake - with the figure's front two hands held together in a gesture of gratitude toward customers.

Before opening Ashura, the owner worked in sales at a manufacturing company, then trained for roughly two years at a restaurant in Nakameguro before opening his own place near Suidōbashi in October 2023.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood izakaya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so expect a Japanese-only ordering and reservation experience.

places.name: ビストロ向日葵 (Bistro Himawari)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated-fryer, or wheat-avoidance information was found on any source. The signature dishes are a cream-based sea urchin pasta (wheat pasta) and a blancmange, and the kitchen appears to be a standard French/Italian-style bistro line using flour-based sauces and bread service — there is no indication of gluten-free adaptation. Not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were found on any source. This bistro's identity is built around sea urchin (uni) and cream-based dishes, both of which are core to its signature offerings, so it is not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog-sourced: 月〜金 18:00〜23:30 (LO22:00) / 土 17:00〜23:30 (LO22:00) / 日 17:00〜22:30 (LO21:00) / 祝 18:00〜22:30 (LO21:00). Other listings (Hot Pepper/EPARK aggregation) show a simpler 18:00〜23:00 daily, no fixed closed day.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-sanchome)
places.notes: No gluten-free menu, dedicated-fryer, or wheat-avoidance information was found on any source. The signature dishes are a cream-based sea urchin pasta (wheat pasta) and a blancmange, and the kitchen appears to be a standard French/Italian-style bistro line using flour-based sauces · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.philosophy: The chef, who is also a certified wine sommelier, wants guests to enjoy wine without formality or pretension (肩肘張らず気軽にワインを楽しんでいただきたい).
chef_bio.specialty: Overflowing sea urchin blancmange (こぼれるウニのブランマンジェ) and a rich uni cream pasta — the bistro's signature uni preparations

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature sea urchin dish is the result of roughly 15 years of refinement, with the kitchen still tweaking the recipe to make it better.
The bistro is tucked just off Yasukuni-dori near Shinjuku-sanchome Station Exit C3, just before a Starbucks, and is known locally as a casual spot for wine paired with generous portions of uni.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-run neighborhood bistro with Japanese-language menus and service; reservations are commonly made through Japanese booking platforms, so a translation app is useful for asking about ingredients.

places.name: らーめん 鴨to葱 渋谷宮益坂店 (Ramen Kamo to Negi Shibuya Miyamasuzaka)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are a custom wheat/whole-grain-wheat blend (not gluten-free) and bowls are finished with a soy-based tare, which in Japan is very often wheat-containing soy sauce unless specified otherwise. No gluten-free noodle substitute, GF menu option, or allergen accommodation was mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broth is made exclusively from duck bones, scallion, and water — there is no vegetable/kombu-based alternative broth. The menu centers on duck ramen, duck confit, duck chashu, and an egg-and-duck oyako-don. No vegan or vegetarian dish, and no plant-based broth option, was found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-翌4:00 (18-hour operation, effective from June 23, 2025); originally 10:00-22:30 at the May 11, 2025 opening. No listed closed days.
places.neighborhood: 宮益坂 (Miyamasuzaka, Shibuya)
places.notes: Kamo to Negi built its reputation on a duck-only ramen concept: broth simmered each morning from nothing but duck bones, scallion, and water, with no added chemical seasoning (無化調). The Shibuya branch sits partway up Miyamasuzaka hill, a short walk from Shibuya Station, and quick · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Part of the "Kamo to Negi" (鴨to葱, literally "Duck and Scallion") ramen chain, whose original shop opened under the elevated tracks between Okachimachi and Ueno in June 2017. The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka branch is a newer location that opened on May 11, 2025.
chef_bio.background: Kamo to Negi built its reputation on a duck-only ramen concept: broth simmered each morning from nothing but duck bones, scallion, and water, with no added chemical seasoning (無化調). The Shibuya branch sits partway up Miyamasuzaka hill, a short walk from Shibuya Station, and quickly drew queues after opening.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen limits the broth to three ingredients — duck, scallion, and water — sourcing duck from producers in Kyoto, Aomori, and Iwate, and finishing bowls with a soy-based tare. Diners choose two of three scallion preparations (including grilled thick white negi and Kyoto's Kujo negi) to customize each bowl. Noodles are a custom blend of whole-grain and white wheat flour made for the chain by an outside noodle maker.
chef_bio.specialty: Confit Duck Ramen (コンフィ鴨らーめん) and the shop's well-known "drinkable oyako-don" (飲める親子丼) — a small rice bowl topped with duck and a barely-set, custard-soft egg meant to be eaten (almost drunk) with a spoon.

anecdotes.text: The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka opening in May 2025 drew a line out the door, with local coverage singling out the drinkable oyako-don as a must-order alongside the duck ramen.
A blogger who visited soon after opening described the duck broth as simple but deep, calling it a worthy addition to the neighborhood's ramen scene.
A longtime fan's essay on the original Ueno-Okachimachi shop traces the chain's story back to its 2017 opening under the train tracks.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen's finishing tare is soy-based; sources describing the shop as a "duck broth soy sauce ramen" specialist imply standard (wheat-containing) shoyu rather than a wheat-free tamari.

cultural_comfort.note: Order at an entrance ticket vending machine as at most Tokyo ramen shops; basic Japanese (or pointing at photos/kanji for menu items like 鴨らーめん) helps, though its Shibuya location means some staff English or a picture menu is plausible.

places.name: タイガーデン 渋谷店 (Thai Garden Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: No source found any dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or explicit statement about wheat in soy sauce or oyster sauce. Rice noodles used for Pad Thai are naturally GF, but overall sauce composition is unconfirmed. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Only a general claim that the restaurant "accommodates vegetarian requests" was found (spice level and vegetarian adjustments); no source confirmed a dedicated vegan menu, or explicit avoidance of fish sauce (nam pla), shrimp paste (kapi), or oyster sauce — all of which are staples of Thai cooking and commonly hidden in sauces and curry pastes. Vegans should confirm specific dish ingredients with staff before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・センター街 (Shibuya, Center-gai)
places.notes: Thai Garden Shibuya (タイガーデン渋谷店) is a Bangkok-style Thai restaurant a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, staffed by chefs trained in Thailand. The restaurant has been certified as a "Thai Select" establishment by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce, a government ma · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Thailand (Bangkok-trained kitchen team)
chef_bio.background: Thai Garden Shibuya (タイガーデン渋谷店) is a Bangkok-style Thai restaurant a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, staffed by chefs trained in Thailand. The restaurant has been certified as a "Thai Select" establishment by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce, a government mark given to restaurants abroad that serve authentically prepared Thai food.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for the layered, herb-forward balance of sour, spicy, sweet and salty found in home-region Thai cooking, using shrimp paste, fish sauce and fresh herbs rather than a simplified 'Japanized' version of the cuisine, and will adjust spice level to taste.
chef_bio.specialty: Pad Thai and Tom Yum Kung are the dishes most frequently singled out by diners; the Tom Yum Kung is noted for a rounded, not-too-sharp spiciness with a fragrant herbal aroma and complex sour balance.

anecdotes.text: A lunch visit report highlights the restaurant's Pad Thai lunch set (with salad, soup and dessert) as excellent value and a reason to choose this location among Shibuya's many Thai restaurants.
The Tom Yum Kung is described as having 'a rounded, mild spiciness, a refreshing herbal aroma, and a complex flavor combining sourness' rather than being aggressively hot.
The restaurant is listed as a Thai-government 'Thai Select' certified restaurant, a recognition for serving authentic Thai cuisine overseas.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant accommodates requests to adjust spice level and vegetarian dietary requests, per multiple listing-site descriptions of the restaurant's service.

positives.text: Kitchen staff trained in Thailand and the restaurant holds Thai Select government certification for authentic Thai cuisine, suggesting attentiveness to traditional recipe composition.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in the heart of Shibuya Center-gai, an area with heavy foreign foot traffic, and listed with an English-language Tabelog page, but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

places.name: お好み焼き・もんじゃ焼き お好みもんじゃ 水道橋店 (Okonomiyaki & Monjayaki Okonomi Monja Suidōb)
places.gf_detail: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter here is standard wheat flour with no gluten-free alternative mentioned anywhere. The venue runs a shared-teppan all-you-can-eat format, which raises cross-contact risk further. No sources mention gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free soy sauce. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options are indicated in any source. Okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter and toppings typically include egg, pork/seafood, and bonito (katsuo)-based dashi and sauces, none of which are flagged as substitutable here. Vegan diners should assume no accommodation without directly contacting the restaurant.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町 (near Suidōbashi Station)
places.notes: This Suidōbashi branch is part of the Okonomi Monja chain operated by Daisho Group, a major Japanese restaurant company. It serves okonomiyaki and monjayaki (Japanese griddle-cooked savory pancakes) centered on a 2-hour all-you-can-eat, all-you-can-drink course offering 80+ dishe · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: This Suidōbashi branch is part of the Okonomi Monja chain operated by Daisho Group, a major Japanese restaurant company. It serves okonomiyaki and monjayaki (Japanese griddle-cooked savory pancakes) centered on a 2-hour all-you-can-eat, all-you-can-drink course offering 80+ dishes (okonomiyaki, monjayaki, yakisoba, yakiudon and more) plus 40+ drinks, served at horigotatsu (sunken-floor) tables suited to groups and parties.
chef_bio.specialty: 2-hour all-you-can-eat okonomiyaki and monjayaki course (80+ dishes)

cultural_comfort.note: Standard Japanese chain izakaya experience with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; ordering (especially the all-you-can-eat course) is likely Japanese-only.

places.name: 自家焙煎珈琲 凡 (Jikabaisen Kōhī Bon (Self-Roasted Coffee Bon)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee-focused kissaten with no stated gluten-free protocol, allergy menu, or dedicated prep area found in any source. Plain black coffee is inherently wheat-free, but the only confirmed food item — the signature shortcake — uses a housemade wheat-flour sponge. No other confirmed gluten-free food options were found. Celiac diners should ask staff directly and expect food choices to be very limited to none.
places.vegan_detail: Only unadorned black coffee is confirmed vegan-friendly. The shop's signature shortcake is built on fresh dairy cream (35% fat) and a housemade sponge (typically egg/dairy-based), and no vegan food items, plant-milk alternatives, or broth/dashi ingredient details were found in available sources.
places.hours_raw: Most sources: 12:30-23:00, L.O. 21:30, open daily/no fixed closed day. One blog gives a differing schedule (Mon-Sat 13:00-22:00 L.O.21:30; Sun/holidays 13:00-21:00) — hours may have changed or vary by source; confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 新宿西口駅 / 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku, near Shinjuku Station east side / S
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎珈琲 / 喫茶店
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A hushed, Shōwa-era kissaten reached by a stairway down into a basement near Shinjuku's east side, open since 1985. The dim, quiet room has individually partitioned counter seats so solo customers can sit undisturbed with their coffee, and shelves lined with roughly 1,500 antique · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A hushed, Shōwa-era kissaten reached by a stairway down into a basement near Shinjuku's east side, open since 1985. The dim, quiet room has individually partitioned counter seats so solo customers can sit undisturbed with their coffee, and shelves lined with roughly 1,500 antique cups and saucers from makers like Wedgwood, Meissen, Ginori and Royal Copenhagen — each order is served in a different cup, with a card explaining that day's maker.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coffee beans are roasted in-house every day and brewed to order using a slow, low-temperature extraction method the shop says it has kept largely unchanged for decades, then served hot, often enough for two or three cups per pot.
chef_bio.specialty: The 'Otona no Shortcake' (Adult's Shortcake) — capped at 16 slices a day and made only when good strawberries are available, with a housemade sponge and fresh dairy cream (35% fat, no preservatives or stabilizers) piled with whole strawberries.

anecdotes.text: Because the shortcake is capped at 16 pieces a day and only appears when the strawberries are good, regulars call it the 'phantom shortcake' (幻のショートケーキ).
The coffee is slow, cold/low-temperature extracted and served from a pot — enough for two or three cups per order — a method the shop has kept close to unchanged for decades.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, quiet Shōwa-era kissaten with no confirmed English menu — expect a Japanese-only ordering experience typical of an old-school Tokyo coffee shop, with individually partitioned counter seating that discourages loud conversation.

places.name: ラケル 宮益坂店 (RAKERU Miyamasuzaka-ten (Rakeru Miyamasuzaka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source checked (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, official site, blogs). The signature dishes center on demi-glace/ketchup rice sauces and the shop's own wheat-based sweet "RAKERU bread," both typical wheat-containing yoshoku preparations. Diners with celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity should not assume this restaurant is safe without calling ahead, and no such confirmation could be found.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based substitution, or vegan-specific mention was found. The restaurant's entire identity is built around egg dishes (omurice/omelette rice) and a butter/dairy-based bread, making it fundamentally incompatible with a vegan diet as currently documented.
places.hours_raw: Most consistently cited: 11:00-21:30 daily, no closed days (年中無休). One directory listed 11:00-22:00 with a shortened-hours note of 11:00-21:00; treat as approximate and reconfirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 宮益坂 (Miyamasuzaka), Shibuya
places.notes: RAKERU began as a small coffee/egg-dish shop where the founders made bread and egg dishes fresh each day; the omurice and a proprietary sweet egg bread ('RAKERU bread') became the flagship items and the chain grew into roughly 38 stores nationwide. The name RAKERU is a Japanese p · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Opened in 1975, the Miyamasuzaka store is the oldest currently operating location of the RAKERU chain, whose company was founded in 1963.
chef_bio.background: RAKERU began as a small coffee/egg-dish shop where the founders made bread and egg dishes fresh each day; the omurice and a proprietary sweet egg bread ('RAKERU bread') became the flagship items and the chain grew into roughly 38 stores nationwide. The name RAKERU is a Japanese phonetic rendering of the biblical name Rachel, chosen to be familiar to Japanese ears while preserving the original pronunciation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sincere, made-to-order cooking centered on eggs — the founders' original idea of 'heartwarming, delicious bread and egg dishes' still guides the menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Fluffy egg omurice (omelette rice), served over ketchup rice, dry curry, or pilaf, alongside the shop's signature sweet 'RAKERU bread'

anecdotes.text: The Miyamasuzaka store, opened in 1975, is the oldest surviving RAKERU shop and is described as the brand's birthplace for its fluffy omurice.
In 2016 the Shibuya Miyamasuzaka store exclusively revived a limited 40th-anniversary omurice menu, marking its status as the flagship original store.
The restaurant is located in a basement space about a 4-5 minute walk from JR Shibuya Station, with a red-gingham, Alice-in-Wonderland-inspired dining room.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running, Japanese-oriented omurice specialty shop near Shibuya Station; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app is useful, though the concept (omelette over rice) is easy to point at and order.

places.name: ロクメイコーヒー 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店 (Rokumei Coffee Shibuya Scramble Square)
places.gf_detail: Plain coffee and espresso drinks are naturally gluten-free, but no gluten-free labeling, dedicated prep equipment, or allergen menu was found in any source for the baked sweets (pound cakes etc.), which are wheat-flour based. Cross-contamination risk in shared prep areas is unconfirmed either way — celiac diners should stick to black coffee/espresso and ask staff before eating any food item.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee and straight espresso drinks are vegan by nature, but no plant-milk (soy/oat) option for lattes and no vegan-certified sweets were confirmed in any source; the shop's signature pound cakes are Japanese-style baked goods that typically include dairy and egg, so they cannot be assumed vegan without asking staff.
places.hours_raw: 平日 8:00〜21:00／土日祝 9:00〜21:00（土日祝は10:00までテイクアウト・物販のみ、イートインは10:00から）
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・スクランブルスクエア2階 (Shibuya Scramble Square, 2F)
places.cuisine: Specialty Coffee, Limited Shibuya Blend, Org
places.notes: Rokumei Coffee traces back to a Nara coffee shop called ROCOCO, founded in 1974. Koji Ida took over the family business and began in-house specialty roasting in 2010, earned CQI Q Grader certification in 2013, and won the Japan Coffee Roasting Championship (JCRC) in 2018. The Shi · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: 奈良県 (Nara)
chef_bio.background: Rokumei Coffee traces back to a Nara coffee shop called ROCOCO, founded in 1974. Koji Ida took over the family business and began in-house specialty roasting in 2010, earned CQI Q Grader certification in 2013, and won the Japan Coffee Roasting Championship (JCRC) in 2018. The Shibuya Scramble Square shop, which opened March 5, 2026, is the brand's second Kanto-region location (after Tokyo Station's GRANSTA Marunouchi shop), sitting on the mall's 2nd floor directly connected to Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company roasts 100% specialty-grade coffee, favoring a light roast shipped out soon after roasting to preserve the beans' natural fruit-forward aroma; each shop location gets its own seasonal, location-exclusive blend.
chef_bio.specialty: Shibuya Scramble Square limited blend (roaster-updated seasonally) and the shop's signature black bean & roasted-houjicha pound cake

anecdotes.text: Ida took over his family's Nara kissaten ROCOCO, started self-roasting in 2010, became a CQI-certified Q Grader in 2013, and won the 2018 Japan Coffee Roasting Championship before the brand expanded from Nara to Tokyo.
The Shibuya Scramble Square shop opened March 5, 2026 as Rokumei's second Kanto-area location, with a location-exclusive 'Shibuya Scramble Square Blend' that the roaster updates seasonally.

The black bean and roasted houjicha-tea pound cake is described as a Japanese-style cake made with a houjicha cream infused with a hint of miso, balanced against the mild sweetness of black beans, baked with minimal additives.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern counter-service specialty-coffee shop inside a major tourist-heavy mall directly connected to Shibuya Station, with mall-wide English signage and international credit card acceptance, though no English menu or staff-language info was independently confirmed.

places.name: ニューヨーカーズカフェ 水道橋東口店 (New Yorker's Cafe Suidobashi Higashiguchi-te)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free bread substitute, or allergen chart could be found on the official Ginza Renoir / NEW YORKER'S Cafe site or in review-site listings. The food menu centers on wheat-based items — bagels, focaccia sandwiches, and toast — so cross-contamination and hidden-wheat risk (e.g., sauces, dressings) are unconfirmed and should be assumed present. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or vegan labeling was found. Plain coffee and tea are inherently vegan, but the food menu (bagels with cream cheese, ham/camembert or roast chicken focaccia, breakfast sets) is built around dairy, meat, and egg, with no confirmed plant-based substitute. Hidden animal-derived ingredients in sauces, dressings, or broths (e.g., dashi/bonito) were not addressed in any source, so vegan diners should ask staff directly rather than assume any dish is safe.
places.neighborhood: 本郷 (Hongo), Bunkyo-ku — 1-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Sta
places.notes: NEW YORKER'S Cafe is a self-service espresso cafe brand created by Ginza Renoir, the long-running Japanese kissaten (traditional coffee shop) company. When Ginza Renoir hit a business downturn during Japan's deflationary period, management converted one of its underperforming ful · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Cafe chain operated by Ginza Renoir Co., Ltd.
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (Ginza Renoir Co., Ltd. chain brand)
chef_bio.background: NEW YORKER'S Cafe is a self-service espresso cafe brand created by Ginza Renoir, the long-running Japanese kissaten (traditional coffee shop) company. When Ginza Renoir hit a business downturn during Japan's deflationary period, management converted one of its underperforming full-service Takadanobaba locations into a self-service, New York-style espresso bar as an experiment. The revamped shop drew a completely new customer base of women and students who had never visited the original waiter-service Renoir cafes, and sales roughly tripled, leading the company to roll the concept out to additional locations including this Suidobashi east-exit branch.
chef_bio.philosophy: Branded around the concept of "My Style Cafe" and a space designed to feel like "a New York-inspired oasis in the city," the cafe aims to let each guest use the space in their own way — for coffee alone, a quick meal, work, or study — in a stylish, espresso-bar-style setting distinct from its parent chain's traditional waiter-service kissaten.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-poured espresso and coffee drinks alongside bread-based light meals such as salmon and cream cheese bagels, prosciutto and camembert focaccia, and roast chicken and cheddar focaccia, plus multiple breakfast sets (soup, bagel, hot dog, focaccia or buttered toast, each with a mini salad).

anecdotes.text: The NEW YORKER'S Cafe concept began as a make-or-break experiment: Ginza Renoir converted a money-losing, waiter-service Takadanobaba shop into a self-service espresso cafe, and it ended up tripling sales by attracting younger and female customers the original brand had not reached.

cultural_comfort.note: A self-service chain cafe with visual menu boards and standard order-at-counter flow, so basic greetings and pointing at the menu are enough even without Japanese fluency.

places.name: おすしあわせ 鮨ほり川 (Osushiawase Sushi Horikawa)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol. As a classic Edomae sushi counter, soy sauce (often wheat-based) and sushi rice seasoning are used throughout, and cross-contact in a small open kitchen/counter is likely. The restaurant states it will only try to avoid specific allergens or disliked ingredients if discussed in advance, and cannot guarantee full accommodation if many items must be excluded — celiac guests should call ahead (03-4400-0193) and confirm exactly which soy sauce and seasonings are used before booking.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi omakase built entirely around fish and seafood sourced from Toyosu Market and direct fishermen shipments; there is no vegan menu or plant-based course. Dashi and simmered items are presumed to use bonito/fish stock as is standard in this style. Vegan diners are not accommodated beyond a case-by-case advance conversation about which items to omit, which the kitchen says may not be possible if many ingredients need to be excluded.
places.hours_raw: Closed Mondays. Tuesday dinner only (17:00-22:00). Wed-Sun: lunch 12:00-14:00 (last entry 13:00) and dinner 17:00-22:00. Sunday dinner service is subject to reservation demand.
places.neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa Kannon-ura)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Kyohei Horikawa began his sushi apprenticeship at age 20, training for seven years, then worked as a sushi craftsman at respected shops in Ginza, Akasaka, Roppongi, and Shinjuku before going independent and opening Osushiawase Sushi Horikawa in Asakusa. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Chiba Prefecture, born 1978
chef_bio.background: Chef Kyohei Horikawa began his sushi apprenticeship at age 20, training for seven years, then worked as a sushi craftsman at respected shops in Ginza, Akasaka, Roppongi, and Shinjuku before going independent and opening Osushiawase Sushi Horikawa in Asakusa.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef adjusts seasoning ingredient-by-ingredient — switching between red vinegar (akazu) and rice vinegar depending on the fish — to give the omakase course its own rhythm, alongside seasonal specialties.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase Edomae nigiri built around fish sourced directly from Toyosu Market, fishermen nationwide, and Hakata Market, served over a rice blend of Sasanishiki and Koshihikari.

anecdotes.text: Horikawa trained for seven years before working his way through well-regarded sushi counters in Ginza, Akasaka, Roppongi, and Shinjuku prior to opening his own 6-seat shop in Asakusa.

The restaurant emphasizes fish sourced directly from Toyosu Market, direct shipments from fishermen around Japan, and the Hakata Market, with rice made from a Sasanishiki/Koshihikari blend and both red and rice vinegar used depending on the fish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a small 6-seat open sushi counter with a single shared kitchen, there is no separation between regular preparation and any allergen-avoidance requests.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant will only attempt to avoid specific allergens or disliked ingredients if discussed with staff in advance, and states that full accommodation may not be possible if many ingredients need to be excluded.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run omakase sushi counter with Japanese-only listings and reservation-based service; English support is not confirmed, so some Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 焼鳥 酉乃洲 (Yakitori Torinosu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free tare was confirmed in any source checked (official site and most review platforms returned access errors during verification, so this is based on aggregator search summaries only). As a tare-and-shio yakitori restaurant, the sauce-glazed (tare) skewers almost certainly use a wheat-based soy sauce reduction; shio (salt-only) skewers are the more likely gluten-safer option but this was not explicitly confirmed for this restaurant, and cross-contact risk on a shared grill/tare-dipping station is unverified. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-focused yakitori restaurant; no vegan menu or plant-based tasting course was found in any source. A few grilled-vegetable skewers may appear on the menu, but they would likely be cooked on the same charcoal grill as meat and sauced with the same tare, so they cannot be assumed vegan-safe without asking staff directly. No dashi/katsuobushi disclosure was found either way.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (Jinnan), about a 5-8 minute walk from Shibuya Stati
places.notes: Torinosu opened on April 8, 2022 on the 11th floor of Park Way Terrace Shibuya, a short walk from Shibuya Scramble, with night-skyline views over the neighborhood. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Torinosu opened on April 8, 2022 on the 11th floor of Park Way Terrace Shibuya, a short walk from Shibuya Scramble, with night-skyline views over the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant centers its menu on tracking down distinctive, lesser-known Japanese chicken breeds from small producers and preparing them simply over charcoal to let the meat's character come through, paired with seasonal vegetables and served on Kutani porcelain.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers made from rare, carefully sourced regional chicken breeds, including Miyazaki jidori (free-range heritage chicken) and Amami kokei-dori (a black-feathered breed from Amami), alongside other select local birds.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant developed its own original gin, "Torinosu Shibuya Remix," in collaboration with a sake brewery in Kagoshima, served alongside a natural wine list to pair with the grilled chicken courses.

cultural_comfort.note: Listed on English-language Tabelog and reservation platforms (TableCheck), suggesting some accommodation for non-Japanese-speaking diners, but no confirmed English menu.

places.name: 焼鳥 神保町とり瑛 (Yakitori Jimbocho Tori-ei)
places.gf_detail: Skewers can be ordered shio (salt) instead of tare (sauce), avoiding the soy-based glaze on that item, but Japanese tare is almost always soy-sauce-based and therefore likely contains wheat; no statement about the tare's exact ingredients was found. No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is mentioned in any source, and skewers of all kinds share the same charcoal grill, so cross-contact is likely. Ask staff directly about tare ingredients and shared cooking surfaces if you need strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: This is a chicken-focused yakitori restaurant; no vegan menu or vegan accommodation was found in any source. The kitchen does source fresh seasonal vegetables from contracted Hokkaido farms and vegetable-based dishes appear on the menu, but tare sauce and overall cooking are meat-centered and skewers are grilled alongside meat, so it is not a suitable choice for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 11:30-14:00 (food L.O. 13:30, drink L.O. 13:30) and 17:00-22:30 (food/drink L.O. 21:30); Sat & holidays: 13:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30); closed Sundays. Lunch menu is weekdays only.
places.neighborhood: 千代田区神保町（Jinbocho/Kanda）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2020 inside a nostalgic, Showa-era wooden building tucked down a quiet back alley just off Jinbocho's famous secondhand-bookstore streets, this unmarked (no outdoor signage) yakitori shop is found mostly by word of mouth and online listings rather than a street-facing s · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Yakitori craftsman/grill chef (name not published in available sources)
chef_bio.background: Opened in 2020 inside a nostalgic, Showa-era wooden building tucked down a quiet back alley just off Jinbocho's famous secondhand-bookstore streets, this unmarked (no outdoor signage) yakitori shop is found mostly by word of mouth and online listings rather than a street-facing storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen insists on chicken slaughtered that same morning rather than a fixed named brand, skewering and grilling everything in-house over binchotan charcoal, and pairs the yakitori with seasonal vegetables shipped from contracted farms in Hokkaido and a rotating list of roughly 30-40 Japanese sakes.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori featuring rare cuts such as chochin (unlaid-egg chicken heart) and sena-gimo (liver) alongside classic parts, served simply with salt or tare sauce; also known for a lunchtime oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) made with burdock root instead of onion, including an off-menu 'ura oyakodon' version with offal that regulars ask for by name.

anecdotes.text: A Yahoo News food columnist described being captivated by the craftsman's careful heat control while eating the restaurant's oyakodon inside the charmingly retro, Showa-era old house down a Jinbocho back alley.
A dining blogger who visited in 2025 wrote about discovering the restaurant's unlisted 'ura oyakodon' (hidden-menu parent-child rice bowl with offal) that regulars order off-menu.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, unsigned neighborhood yakitori shop down a back alley with a mobile-phone reservation number — no English menu or website was found, so speaking some Japanese or using a translation app is recommended.

places.name: 百日紅（さるすべり） (Menya Sarusuberi (Hyakujikkou))
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles with a soy-sauce (shoyu)-based tare; broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) and tonkotsu (pork bone) stock. No gluten-free noodle substitute, no allergen menu, and no cross-contamination information is published anywhere found (Tabelog, official social media, or food blogs). Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners as currently documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu items are documented. Broths rely on niboshi (dried sardine) and/or tonkotsu (pork bone), both animal-based, and the tare uses standard soy sauce; there is no plant-based broth option or mention of accommodating vegan requests found on Tabelog, official social media, or blog coverage.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 7:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30, with a 500-yen weekday-only 7:00-11:00 cash-only "asa-ramen" morning service); Sat 11:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sun 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30)
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome), Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Manager Yasuda previously worked in Italian restaurants and traditional Japanese inn (ryokan) kitchens before opening this tsukemen counter in 2010, tucked in the basement of the Keio Frente building directly across from Isetan Shinjuku, connected to Shinjuku Sanchome Station's C · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Manager Yasuda previously worked in Italian restaurants and traditional Japanese inn (ryokan) kitchens before opening this tsukemen counter in 2010, tucked in the basement of the Keio Frente building directly across from Isetan Shinjuku, connected to Shinjuku Sanchome Station's C2 exit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yasuda has said the goal is to give time-pressed commuters and station passersby something genuinely delicious rather than just convenient, tuning the broth so it clings properly to thick noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Niboshi-tonkotsu tsukemen — thick, well-chilled dipping noodles served with a concentrated broth that blends dried-sardine (niboshi) and pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock; a lighter shio (salt) tsukemen is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The shop has run a 500-yen weekday-only 'morning ramen' (asa-ramen) from 7:00-11:00 since well before 2025, offered in shoyu or shio versions and paid in cash.
Large (tokumori) noodle portions are given free of charge, a service that has made the shop especially popular with big-appetite regulars.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic no-frills Tokyo ramen/tsukemen counter inside a station shopping mall — expect Japanese-only signage and ordering with no particular English-language or allergy-accommodation support for foreign diners.

places.name: 茶和々 浅草店 (Sawawa Asakusa (Chawawa))
places.gf_detail: No allergen menu or gluten-free certification was found for this shop. Its signature warabi mochi is a traditional Japanese confection typically made from bracken/starch bases rather than wheat, and the kinako (soybean flour) topping is also wheat-free in general, but this has not been confirmed for this specific location's recipe, and the shop also sells Western-style baked sweets in the same space with no stated separation of equipment. There is no confirmed information on cross-contamination handling, so ask staff directly if you need certainty for celiac safety.
places.vegan_detail: The matcha soft-serve ice cream contains dairy and is not vegan. Some warabi mochi items appear to be plant-based (starch, matcha, kinako, sugar syrup) but exact recipes and any hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., honey, gelatin, dairy) were not confirmed from available sources, and no vegan labeling was found on the menu. Ask staff to confirm ingredients for a specific item before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily (no stated closed day found)
places.neighborhood: 浅草・浅草寺参道 (beside Sensoji's Hozomon Gate, next to the Nison-b
places.notes: 茶和々 (Sawawa) is a matcha confectionery brand operated by Terakoya Corporation (株式会社寺子屋), with shops in Kyoto's Nishiki Market, Kanazawa, Narita, and Otaru in addition to this Asakusa location, which sits immediately beside Sensoji's Hozomon Gate area next to the Nison-butsu Buddh · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto (brand origin — Nishiki Market flagship)
chef_bio.background: 茶和々 (Sawawa) is a matcha confectionery brand operated by Terakoya Corporation (株式会社寺子屋), with shops in Kyoto's Nishiki Market, Kanazawa, Narita, and Otaru in addition to this Asakusa location, which sits immediately beside Sensoji's Hozomon Gate area next to the Nison-butsu Buddha statue.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand centers its sweets on drawing out the aroma and depth of Uji matcha
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha warabi mochi (抹茶わらび餅), hand-cut fresh in-house and dusted generously with high-grade matcha, plus matcha soft-serve and other matcha confections

anecdotes.text: Craftsmen hand-cut the warabi mochi fresh and coat it generously in high-grade matcha; it has been the shop's signature best-seller for years.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple walk-up/takeout sweets counter where items are visually displayed and easy to point at, so minimal Japanese is needed to order.

places.name: 挽肉と米 渋谷 (Hikiniku to Come Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu is one dish: a charcoal-grilled ground-beef hamburger-steak set. Traditional Japanese hamburg-steak patties are typically bound with breadcrumbs (wheat), and no gluten-free version, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated GF preparation process is advertised on the official site or in press coverage found. Wheat-based soy sauce is also standard in Japanese hamburg-steak sauces and miso soup bases. With no menu flexibility and no GF statement from the restaurant, this is not a place to trust for celiac-level safety without direct, in-person confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: The sole dish is a beef ground-meat hamburger-steak set with a complimentary raw egg, and the miso soup served alongside is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi in standard Japanese style. No plant-based or vegetarian alternative, and no vegan-related statement from the restaurant, was found in any source checked. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 昼の部 11:00-15:00 / 夜の部 17:00-21:00（定休日：第1・3水曜日、一部情報源では毎週水曜定休）
places.neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂
places.notes: Hikiniku to Come was founded in 2019 by restaurateur Yamamoto Shohei (previously behind the hamburger chain "Ore no Hamburg") together with Konishi Toshiyuki of the design firm POOL inc. and Kiyomiya Toshiyuki, a food-service veteran who had worked at Culture Convenience Club (TS · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kichijoji, Tokyo (the brand's first shop); this location is the Shibuya branch, opened March 22, 2021
chef_bio.background: Hikiniku to Come was founded in 2019 by restaurateur Yamamoto Shohei (previously behind the hamburger chain "Ore no Hamburg") together with Konishi Toshiyuki of the design firm POOL inc. and Kiyomiya Toshiyuki, a food-service veteran who had worked at Culture Convenience Club (TSUTAYA) and Chikara no Moto Holdings (Ippudo ramen). The first shop opened in Kichijoji in June 2020, and the concept quickly grew into a multi-location chain; the Shibuya branch on Dogenzaka opened in March 2021 inside a renovated older building, giving it a hidden, back-alley feel despite the crowds it draws.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant is built around a "3-fresh" (3たて) idea — meat ground fresh, patties grilled fresh over charcoal, and rice cooked fresh to order — reflecting founder Yamamoto Shohei's belief that ground meat deserves to be the star of the dish rather than just a component of a burger or curry.
chef_bio.specialty: 挽肉と米定食 — a single set meal of freshly ground, charcoal-grilled beef hamburger-steak patties (up to three 90g patties), rice cooked fresh in kamado pots, and miso soup, with free rice refills and a complimentary raw egg. The Shibuya location also has its own house sauce blended from three types of miso.

anecdotes.text: Yamamoto Shohei's path to Hikiniku to Come ran through his earlier venture "Ore no Hamburg," and he has spoken about why he became fixated specifically on ground meat (挽肉) as a craft rather than treating it as a secondary ingredient.

Since opening in 2021, the Shibuya branch has been known for long same-day queues, moving from a paper numbered-ticket (整理券) system to a mix of same-day sign-up and limited paid online reservations for a small 6-seat counter area.

cultural_comfort.note: The menu is a single set meal so ordering itself is simple even without Japanese, but the entry process (same-day sign-up/queue system, occasional online reservation windows) is explained mainly in Japanese and can be confusing for first-time foreign visitors.

places.name: 後楽園飯店 (Korakuen Hanten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or celiac accommodation was found in any source checked (official site, Tabelog, hotel press materials). As a traditional Chinese restaurant, dishes very likely use wheat-based soy sauce and wheat-starch thickening (an-kake style sauces), and fryers are shared for multiple items including breaded/battered dishes. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about ingredients and possible substitutions before ordering; do not assume any dish is safe by default.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. Average dinner budget is around ¥7,000 (lunch around ¥2,000-¥3,000, with a weekday lunch set near ¥1,080), built around dishes like Peking duck and shark-fin soup noodles, so the menu is meat/seafood-centric. Vegetable dishes are likely available (as in most Chinese restaurants) but stocks and sauces probably contain chicken, pork, or oyster-based ingredients unless requested otherwise; vegan diners should ask staff directly about ingredients.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:00), daily, no regular closed day
places.neighborhood: 水道橋・後楽園 - 東京ドーム隣接（Korakuen Hall Building 2F, next to Tokyo D
places.notes: Korakuen Hanten opened on April 16, 1962, on the 2nd floor of the Korakuen Hall building next to Tokyo Dome, and has operated ever since as a directly-run restaurant of the Tokyo Dome Hotel group. Long popular with sports figures and fans visiting Korakuen Hall and Tokyo Dome, th · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Chinese restaurant, directly operated by Tokyo Dome Hotel
chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (Korakuen/Suidōbashi)
chef_bio.background: Korakuen Hanten opened on April 16, 1962, on the 2nd floor of the Korakuen Hall building next to Tokyo Dome, and has operated ever since as a directly-run restaurant of the Tokyo Dome Hotel group. Long popular with sports figures and fans visiting Korakuen Hall and Tokyo Dome, the restaurant underwent a full renovation and reopened on November 14, 2025 with a 'neo-Chinoiserie' interior while keeping its traditional menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the traditional taste the restaurant has served since 1962 while incorporating contemporary menu elements and ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Peking duck, and its signature shark-fin soup noodles (フカヒレ入り汁そば); the renovated menu also added a fo tiao qiang (佛跳牆, 'Buddha Jumps Over the Wall') course.

anecdotes.text: 後楽園飯店は1962年4月16日に開業した伝統と格式のある中国料理店。東京ドームホテル直営で、各界のスポーツ選手や関係者をはじめ多くの顧客に親しまれている。
1962年創業の東京ドームホテル直営中国料理店「後楽園飯店」が11月14日(金)リニューアルオープン。'ネオシノワズリ'空間で味わう、中国皇帝が愛した幻のスープ「佛跳牆」コースも新登場。

cultural_comfort.note: A hotel-affiliated restaurant with an English-language Tabelog page and English online reservations, so English-speaking visitors can book and dine comfortably.

places.name: 粗挽き蕎麦 トキ (Arabiki Soba Toki)
places.gf_detail: Every soba served here is jūwari — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat blended into the noodle dough itself, which is unusual and notable (most Tokyo soba shops use some wheat as binder). However, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) at Japanese soba shops is almost always made with regular wheat-containing soy sauce, and no source confirms a wheat-free tsuyu, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or protocol for avoiding cross-contact with wheat-based menu items (the shop also serves izakaya-style dishes). Celiac diners should not assume safety and should ask staff directly about the tsuyu and any shared cooking surfaces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan dashi, or plant-based accommodation is documented anywhere. The shop is described as a soba-and-sake izakaya, implying a broader menu with meat/fish dishes alongside the noodles, and traditional soba tsuyu in Japan is normally made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. Vegans should assume standard katsuo-based broth unless staff confirm otherwise.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 11:00/11:30–14:30, 17:30–22:00 (sources vary slightly on exact lunch open time) / Sat: lunch only, ~11:00/11:30–14:30 / Closed Sundays and the first Saturday of each month
places.neighborhood: 南新宿（Minami Shinjuku）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] According to a noodle-machine maker's client profile and food blogs, the owner previously ran an udon specialty shop near Shinjuku's west exit for many years, having trained in udon-making back in his hometown in Shikoku before that first opening. Years later he decided to open a · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shikoku (owner's hometown, where he trained in udon-making before his first restaurant)
chef_bio.background: According to a noodle-machine maker's client profile and food blogs, the owner previously ran an udon specialty shop near Shinjuku's west exit for many years, having trained in udon-making back in his hometown in Shikoku before that first opening. Years later he decided to open a soba-and-local-sake shop instead, and consulted noodle-machine maker Abe Giken after being impressed by a demonstration of their equipment, which he now uses to help produce the shop's distinctive coarse-ground noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner has said the shop's mission is to help more people rediscover the flavor of soba through coarsely-ground, 100%-buckwheat noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Jūwari (100% buckwheat) soba made from the rare Mogami-Wase variety of buckwheat grown in Yamagata, stone-ground extra-coarse so the noodles keep visible flecks of buckwheat hull; it is hand-made in view of guests during lunch service and traditionally eaten with a pinch of salt on the first bite before dipping.

anecdotes.text: The shop's stone-ground buckwheat flour is sourced specifically for this restaurant (and one restaurant in Osaka), giving the noodles their rustic, thick, dark appearance with visible buckwheat hull flecks — a deliberately rougher style than smooth Edo-mae soba.

A 2025 diner described the noodles as chewy, dark, thick 'country-style' soba — the opposite in character to smooth Edo-style soba — best paired with the shop's rotating selection of about 20 pure rice sakes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Restaurant serves chicken tempura made from Oyama chicken and shrimp tempura as side orders alongside the soba — confirming wheat-battered deep-fried items prepared on premises (shared-fryer / wheat-flour cross-contact risk).
gf_cross_contamination.source: https://retty.news/35062/ (via web search of 粗挽き蕎麦 トキ menu)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's sauce/broth is described as containing a generous amount of dashi with kombu, sweet and fragrant with strong umami — a traditional dashi-based tsuyu with no indication of wheat-free soy sauce; standard soba tsuyu uses wheat-containing shoyu.

General Tokyo GF guidance flagged for this exact shop context: soy sauce contains wheat and appears in broth, dipping sauces, and seasoning; diners must confirm broth ingredients and ask about gluten-free soy sauce — i.e., no confirmed wheat-free tsuyu here.
No results for this shop on FindMeGlutenFree/HappyCow and no celiac or 'got glutened' reviews located anywhere — no independent corroboration of any GF handling.
Venue is a soba-and-sake izakaya serving ~20 rotating junmai sake with appetizers and course menus including chicken tempura and grated-yam sets — broad non-GF, non-vegan menu with shared prep.

positives.text: All soba served at the restaurant is made from 100% coarsely-ground buckwheat flour, with no wheat flour blended into the noodle dough itself.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional counter-style soba-and-sake shop aimed at Japanese regulars — no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or pointing at photos helps.

places.name: さくま (Sakuma)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation was identified in any source. As a traditional izakaya, the signature beef tendon stew and other dishes are very likely seasoned with soy sauce (contains wheat) and prepared in a small shared kitchen with no indication of allergen separation. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct in-person confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options, dedicated preparation, or menu information were identified. The restaurant's entire identity is built around a beef tendon stew (gyusuji nikomi) that has been simmered in an accumulated meat-based broth for decades, and the broader menu appears to be conventional home-style izakaya fare likely including dashi and other animal-derived ingredients. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.
places.hours_raw: 月〜土 17:00-21:30、日曜定休（祝日は営業）

The founding family had previously run a sake retail business before turning the shop into a dedicated beef-tendon-stew specialist. It has been passe · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Proprietress (女将) and family — currently run by a mother-daughter team with staff
chef_bio.background: Opened in 1947 (Shōwa 22), just after the end of World War II, in the Asakusa Kannon-ura district directly behind Sensō-ji Temple. The founding family had previously run a sake retail business before turning the shop into a dedicated beef-tendon-stew specialist. It has been passed down through the family and is now counted among the oldest surviving izakaya in the Kannon-ura backstreets.
chef_bio.philosophy: The recipe has been kept deliberately unchanged since the shop opened. Beef tendon — sourced from Hiyama, a long-established wholesaler in Nihonbashi — is simmered for hours in a broth built up over decades, preserving the same flavor generation to generation.
chef_bio.specialty: Gyusuji nikomi (牛すじ煮込み) — Asakusa-style beef tendon stew, the dish the shop was founded on and has served continuously since 1947.

anecdotes.text: The current proprietress has said that her father passed away ten years ago, and if he woke up and tasted today's beef tendon stew, he might still say 'this is a bit too sweet' — a sign of how carefully the original recipe is still followed.
The beef tendon is simmered for roughly four hours a day using tendon from Hiyama, a century-old Nihonbashi meat wholesaler, continuing a broth-and-technique tradition built up over decades.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, regulars-heavy local izakaya with no confirmed English menu, though it has appeared on Japanese TV programs and is listed in English on Tabelog.

places.name: 奈加野 (Nakano)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation found in any source. As a classic seafood izakaya, soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) and bonito dashi (which often incorporates soy sauce or wheat-based seasonings) are used throughout — in sashimi dipping sauce, simmered fish, nameroo, and the tsumire soup. No mention of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free soy sauce alternative. Celiac travelers should not assume safety without direct, in-person confirmation in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-labeled dishes found. This is a seafood-forward izakaya (sashimi, grilled/simmered fish, nameroo, sardine tsumire soup); dashi is very likely bonito-based (katsuobushi), which is not vegan, and vegetable side dishes would need individual confirmation for hidden fish stock.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷（センター街・公園通り沿い）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 渋谷センター街に約40年続く老舗の魚介居酒屋。田中ビル2階に店を構え、創業者自身が今も切り盛りしていると伝えられる。サラリーマン街として知られる宇田川町エリアの中でも数少ない、腰を据えて飲める落ち着いた店として地元で語り継がれている。 · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: 渋谷センター街に約40年続く老舗の魚介居酒屋。 田中ビル2階に店を構え、創業者自身が今も切り盛りしていると伝えられる。 サラリーマン街として知られる宇田川町エリアの中でも数少ない、腰を据えて飲める落ち着いた店として地元で語り継がれている。
chef_bio.specialty: 鰹のたたき（土佐造り）となめろう、いわしのつみれ汁

anecdotes.text: 東京農業大学名誉教授で食文化研究家の小泉武夫氏が通う店として知られ、店主は氏のために築地の信頼できる仲卸から仕入れた鰹を切らさず用意しているという。
渋谷宇田川町は『ロクな店がない』とも言われる中で、この店は創業50年に迫る老舗として一目置かれていると紹介されている。
いわしのつみれ汁は臭みがなく、口の中でうまみが広がる一品として評判。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce mentioned in any source found; this is a conventional seafood izakaya kitchen with no allergen-specific accommodations documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard izakaya use of soy sauce is implied by dishes like sashimi, nameroo, and simmered fish, but no source confirms whether a wheat-free (tamari-style) soy sauce is available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is fish/seafood-centric (sashimi, grilled and simmered fish, sardine tsumire soup); no vegan-specific dishes or cross-contact information found.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood izakaya popular with local office workers; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so guests comfortable navigating Japanese-only ordering (or using a translation app) will fare best.

places.name: レック コーヒー 水道橋店 (REC COFFEE Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation area, or allergen labeling was found for this cafe. Baked goods (financiers, sables, pound cakes) are conventional wheat-based bakery items with no stated gluten-free alternative. Coffee itself is inherently gluten-free, but cross-contact in a shared kitchen for pastries is unconfirmed -- celiac guests should stick to drinks only and ask before eating any food item.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or plant-milk program could be confirmed for this location.
