chef_bio.origin: Fukuoka, Japan (company founded 2008)
chef_bio.background: REC COFFEE was founded in Fukuoka in 2008 by Yoshikazu Iwase and his university friend and business partner Osamu Kitazoe, starting as a mobile coffee truck before opening its first storefront near Yakuin Station in 2010. Iwase discovered specialty coffee while working part-time at Manu Coffee in Fukuoka under roaster Sonobu Nishioka, which shaped his approach to sourcing and roasting. The company grew from Fukuoka into multiple Tokyo locations (including this Suidobashi shop, on the ground floor of the Nine Hours Suidobashi capsule hotel building by the Kandagawa river) and into Taiwan.
chef_bio.philosophy: Iwase has said his goal is to serve coffee good enough to change how everyday consumers think about coffee, treating quality specialty coffee as something for daily life rather than a niche connoisseur product.
chef_bio.specialty: Single-origin, self-roasted specialty pour-over and espresso coffee, paired with house-made pastries (honey financiers, almond sables, pound cakes) baked by an in-house pastry team.

anecdotes.text: Iwase won Japan's national barista championship (JBC) in both 2014 and 2015, then took silver at the 2016 World Barista Championship in Dublin, Ireland -- accolades that grew out of the roasting and brewing standards set at the original Fukuoka shop.

Iwase has described wanting to bring specialty coffee into Fukuoka's cafe culture in a way that changes ordinary consumers' image of what coffee can taste like, rather than keeping it a specialist pursuit.

cultural_comfort.note: REC COFFEE maintains an English-language website and section for this shop, and as a specialty-coffee destination near a major JR station it is used to walk-in foreign visitors, though menu boards and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

Any vegetable-forward sides could still contain hidden dashi (bonito stock) or animal fats typical of Japanese-Italian fusion kitchens. Not a reliable choice for vegan diners without asking extensively in advance, and suitable options may not exist.

chef_bio.background: Bistro Rita Shinjuku opened on February 27, 2025, run by LINK STYLE, a Tokyo hospitality group known locally for its popular neighborhood izakaya brands. It is the group's first bistro/bar-style concept and its largest location, set in a basement space near Shinjuku Station's west exit with a gray, marble, and stained-glass interior and several private rooms.
chef_bio.philosophy: Designed as a stylish 'hideaway' bistro-bar for after-work drinks or group gatherings, aimed at guests in their late 20s and 30s, pairing hearty Italian-leaning meat and wine dishes with an all-you-can-drink course format.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant reached monthly sales of about 13.5 million yen just two months after opening, with its tableside iron-plate wagyu hamburger steak and a foie gras egg-yolk dish cited as signature draws.
Bistro Rita is LINK STYLE's first bistro-format restaurant, following the same company's dominant-strategy izakaya brands 'Sakaba Tsumugido' and 'Meshiya Hiroki Club' in the Shinjuku area.

No vegetarian or vegan curry option, plant-based dish, or dashi/stock substitution was found in any source; assume the curry base and stock are meat-based and not suitable for vegetarians or vegans without direct confirmation from staff.

chef_bio.chef_name: 松岡憲策 (Kensaku Matsuoka, founder); now run by third-generation owner (Katsuyama family)
chef_bio.origin: Founded 1951 in Shibuya as "Ekuriya," later renamed "Indo Ryōri Murugi"
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1951 by Kensaku Matsuoka, a mining engineer whose prospecting trips took him repeatedly to then-Burma (Myanmar), where he became a regular at a local curry stall and grew friendly with its owners. After returning to postwar Shibuya, he opened a small shop called "Ekuriya," adapting what he had tasted overseas into a curry suited to Japanese palates; the shop was later renamed "Indo Ryōri Murugi. " It is now run by the third generation of the family.
chef_bio.philosophy: A postwar recipe deliberately reworked from an overseas original to suit Japanese taste, kept essentially unchanged for over 70 years
chef_bio.specialty: Tamago-iri Murugi Curry — the signature chicken curry, slow-simmered with layered spices and topped with a boiled egg, served over a distinctive mountain-shaped mound of rice

anecdotes.text: The shop's origin traces to founder Kensaku Matsuoka's work as a mining engineer, whose ore-prospecting trips to then-Burma (Myanmar) brought him to a local curry stall he visited so often he became friends with the owners; after the war he opened 'Ekuriya' in Shibuya based on that experience, later renamed 'Indo Ryōri Murugi.'
Novelist Ikenami Shōtarō, who worked for the Tokyo metropolitan government near Shibuya after the war, was a regular at Murugi and wrote about the shop in essays recalling his memories of curry.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, decades-old neighborhood curry shop with Japanese-only signage/menu expectations and cash-based, walk-in local service — not set up for English-speaking or special-diet guidance.

chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes ingredient quality: 100% domestic chicken for freshness (fresh enough that they can serve low-temperature-cooked raw liver, 生レバー), and tsukune (chicken meatballs) that are hand-formed fresh every day rather than pre-made.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef tongue (牛タン), specifically the prized "tanmoto" (タン元) cut — the fattiest, most marbled section of the tongue, sourced through the company's own dedicated supply route — alongside yakitori skewers grilled from all-domestic Japanese chicken.

anecdotes.text: Opened July 10, 2024 as the chain's 4th location, marking the launch with a nine-day campaign offering draft beer, highballs, and sours for ¥99 per glass.

No gluten-free batter, rice-flour option, dedicated fryer, or allergen menu was found in any source consulted. The restaurant's identity is built around shrimp, conger eel (anago), and seasonal fish tempura; the shared tentsuyu dipping sauce is bonito-dashi based. Vegetable tempura items likely exist on the menu but would be fried in oil shared with seafood and served with the same fish-based sauce, so this is not a safe or comfortable choice for strict vegans without prior arrangement (and even then, success is not confirmed).

Historic honten pattern: open daily, closed only Dec 31–Jan 1 plus occasional maintenance closures; this pattern is carried over for the temporary annex but not independently reconfirmed store-by-store.

chef_bio.origin: Founded 1924 (Taisho 13) in Tsunohazu, Shinjuku by Kyuzo Shimura
chef_bio.background: Tsunahachi traces back to 1922, when founder Kyuzo Shimura opened a stand-and-eat tempura shop called "Amihachi" (網八). Customers habitually misread the character 網 (ami) as tsuna, and the mispronunciation stuck so firmly that in 1924 the shop was formally reopened under the name it kept the same location in Shinjuku's old Tsunohazu district as today's Sohonten. The original building was destroyed in the 1945 air raids and post-war land readjustment; the shop rebuilt and reopened on the same ground in 1948. Over nine decades the company grew into one of Japan's best-known tempura chains, with roughly 23 branches nationwide (Sapporo, Toyama, Nagoya, Kyoto, Osaka and more) as of early 2026. In 2026 the flagship Sohonten building closed for a full rebuild planned to run until summer 2028; during construction the original staff and craftsmen relocated a minute's walk away to a temporary location, 'Sohonten Bekkan' (総本店別館), inside the TRES SHINJUKU building, which opened June 23, 2026.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house motto, carried since the founder's era, is to 'always serve the finest tempura at an accessible price' and to 'add sincerity to every dish' (常に最高の天ぷらをお手頃な価格で / おいしさに真心をそえて).
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style tempura courses centered on shrimp (ebi), conger eel (anago), seasonal fish and vegetables, and kakiage, fried to order at the counter

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name is a happy accident: it originally opened as 'Amihachi' (網八), but customers kept misreading the first character as 'tsuna' instead of 'ami' — the mistake spread so widely that the owners renamed the shop 'Tsunahachi' when they formally reopened it in 1924.
anecdotes.source: https://r.gnavi.co.jp/g021901/ (via aggregated search of company history / tunahachi.co.jp/history)

anecdotes.text: The Sohonten building was leveled in the 1945 wartime destruction and postwar land readjustment of Shinjuku; the shop rebuilt on the same plot and reopened in 1948, and has operated there continuously until its 2026 reconstruction closure.

With the historic Sohonten building slated for full reconstruction (reopening planned summer 2028), the restaurant relocated its craftsmen and staff a minute's walk away to a temporary 'Sohonten Bekkan' inside the TRES SHINJUKU building, which opened June 23, 2026.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol, alternative batter, or dedicated fryer is advertised anywhere; tempura is fried in shared oil used for the full menu, so cross-contact with wheat batter is expected on every item.
gf_cross_contamination.source: Inference from menu/format — no source describes GF accommodation

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tentsuyu dipping sauce central to the meal is made with standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce and bonito dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was found in any source.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: General knowledge of Edomae tentsuyu preparation; not specifically confirmed for this location

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura, if ordered, would still be fried in oil shared with shrimp, fish, and eel, and the tentsuyu sauce is dashi-based (bonito), so a fully plant-based meal free of animal cross-contact is not achievable here.
vegan_cross_contact.source: Inference from menu format — no vegan-specific statement found in sources

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, century-old tempura house well accustomed to tourists (English Tabelog listing exists), but day-to-day counter service is conducted primarily in Japanese.

Ask staff which specific items are wheat-free before buying.

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1887 (Meiji 20) as a neighborhood tofu and yuba maker on Denpoin-dori, a side street near Nakamise and Sensoji in Asakusa. The shop is now run by the fifth generation of the same family, still making tofu and fresh yuba in a factory attached to the storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep to the old recipe — domestic whole soybeans and natural nigari only, no shortcuts — even as the storefront itself has modernized.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh yuba (nama-yuba) and hand-pressed tofu made only from 100% domestic soybeans and natural nigari (bittern), plus soymilk-and-okara doughnuts and house-made almond tofu.

anecdotes.text: The current building has a modern wood-grain facade and tiled roof, a deliberate departure from the look of an old-fashioned machiya tofu shop, marked by a green noren reading '栃木家'; it sits on Denpoin-dori close to Nakamise, in the heart of Asakusa.
The shop manufactures its tofu and fresh yuba on-site in an attached factory and has been passed down through five generations of the same Asakusa family since its 1887 founding.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No cross-contamination policy, dedicated equipment, or gluten-free certification is published by the shop in any source found.

positives.text: Core products — tofu, fresh yuba, and edamame tofu — are made from 100% domestic soybeans and natural nigari with no listed animal-derived dashi or additives.

Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation — and even then, cross-contact in a shared udon kitchen is likely unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based dashi option, or vegan labeling was found for this location. As a standard udon shop, broths and sauces likely rely on conventional dashi (often bonito/katsuo-based) unless otherwise specified, and the "oyatsu" snack-table items are not confirmed to be plant-based. chef_bio.background: "めん、色いろ。 いつでも、おやつ。 " is a casual udon concept operated by DD Holdings (the restaurant group behind numerous Tokyo dining brands), located on the 7th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly at Shinjuku Station's east side.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept plays on "itsudemo oyatsu" (snacks anytime) — pairing a health-conscious, vegetable-forward, visually colorful take on udon with the playful, help-yourself snack-table format, aiming for a dining experience closer to a customizable pasta bar than a traditional udon-ya.
chef_bio.specialty: Colorful udon made with five varieties of noodles that have vegetables and grains kneaded directly into the dough (giving them their signature bright colors), served hot or cold with a choice of sauces and toppings. Diners also select three items from a rotating central "oyatsu" (snack/dessert) table with each udon order. The menu was fully relaunched in April 2026 around a new line of creamy, milky-white udon with over 25 selectable toppings.

anecdotes.text: Udon using 5 types of colorful noodles with grains and vegetables kneaded in; ordering udon lets you pick 3 items from the in-store 'oyatsu' snack/dessert table, plus a drink bar, for around ¥1,500.
From April 20, 2026 the Shinjuku location relaunched its full menu around ultra-creamy, milky-white udon with over 25 types of selectable toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A Tokyo spot near Asakusa/Shibuya/Suidōbashi/Shinjuku; a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: らせら（青森郷土料理 らせら） (Rasera (Aomori Kyodo Ryori Rasera))

No mention of a dedicated fryer or any celiac-aware protocol. Do not assume safety here without calling ahead and confirming in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat- and seafood-centric Aomori/Tsugaru izakaya — squid dishes, Shamo (Aomori jidori) chicken, Towada-style bara-yaki beef, horumon, and dashi-based nabe are the draw. Dashi (most likely bonito/katsuo-based) underlies broths, simmered dishes, and hot pots such as senbei-jiru. No vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi substitutions were found in any source — not a workable choice for a vegan diner.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict slightly: one aggregator quotes the official site as 平日17:00〜28:00（late night, past midnight）／日曜・祝日18:00〜23:00, while another lists dinner service simply as 18:00〜24:00. Table above uses the conservative 17:00-24:00 (Mon-Sat) / 18:00-23:00 (Sun) reading; true weekday closing may run later into the night. Confirm by phone (03-3770-7522) before a late visit.
places.neighborhood: 道玄坂／神泉、渋谷（渋谷マークシティ近く、渋谷駅から徒歩約4分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Rasera is a Tsugaru/Aomori regional-cuisine izakaya in a Dogenzaka basement near Shibuya Mark City, opened to bring a taste of home to Aomori natives (and curious Tokyoites) via vegetables, fish, and sake shipped directly from Tsugaru. It is run by a brother-and-sister pair origi · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Run by Aomori-born siblings from Tsuruta, Kitatsugaru; the elder sister, Hitoe, tends the evening izakaya service
chef_bio.origin: Tsugaru region, Aomori Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Rasera is a Tsugaru/Aomori regional-cuisine izakaya in a Dogenzaka basement near Shibuya Mark City, opened to bring a taste of home to Aomori natives (and curious Tokyoites) via vegetables, fish, and sake shipped directly from Tsugaru. It is run by a brother-and-sister pair originally from Tsuruta-cho in Kitatsugaru, Aomori; the sister, Hitoe, is the face behind the counter at night.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name plays on Tsugaru dialect and the ritual shout of Aomori's Nebuta festival ('Rasera, Rasera! '), meant to carry a bit of Aomori's spirit and hospitality into a Shibuya basement.
chef_bio.specialty: Ika no Goroyaki — grilled squid liver from Ajigasawa, Aomori — is cited as the shop's signature dish, alongside Tsugaru staples like kayaki-miso (scallop-shell simmered miso), shoyu-zuke nagaimo, Towada-style bara-yaki, and senbei-jiru hot pot.

anecdotes.text: Described in a bar-hopping feature on Tokyo's Aomori-themed izakayas as a night spot where the elder of two Kitatsugaru-born siblings, Hitoe, pours Aomori sake and cooks home-style dishes from the region she grew up in.
The restaurant's name draws on Tsugaru dialect and Nebuta-festival chants so that the name itself signals its Aomori identity to visitors before they even sit down.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, Japanese-language-oriented regional izakaya tucked in a Dogenzaka basement — no English menu or signage was confirmed, so some Japanese ability or a translation app will help.

places.name: らぁめん 満来 (Raamen Manrai)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen and tsukemen noodles only; the broth is shōyū (soy sauce) based, which contains wheat, and the char siu-heavy bowls are cooked in a shared, single-menu kitchen. No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen accommodation is mentioned anywhere in available sources.
places.vegan_detail: Pork-centric shop built around a shōyū/animal-based broth and a signature bowl piled with 7+ thick slices of char siu; no vegetarian or vegan menu item, plant-based broth, or dashi/katsuobushi-free option is documented anywhere.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–23:00, open every day of the week (only closed around New Year's holidays)
places.neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku) / Shinjuku Station West Exit
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Standard wheat ramen and tsukemen noodles only; the broth is shōyū (soy sauce) based, which contains wheat, and the char siu-heavy bowls are cooked in a shared, single-menu kitchen. No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen accommodation is mentioned any · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1961 (Shōwa 36) in Nerima Ward's Tagashira, the shop later moved to Nakano, then to Shinjuku's Shokuan-dōri, before settling at its current West Shinjuku location behind Bic Camera, near Shinjuku Station's west exit.
chef_bio.specialty: Chāshū Zaru — a cold dipping-noodle (tsukemen) bowl piled with seven or more slices of pork char siu, each over a centimeter thick, served alongside a tangy, savory soy-based dipping broth and thick, chewy curly noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop's founder, referred to as Mr. Takano, is described as the figure whose ramen and career later became the root of the well-known 'Horiuchi' shōyū-ramen family tree in Shinjuku, after moves through Nerima and Nakano before landing at the current West Shinjuku spot.

Reviewers repeatedly describe the chāshū zaru as burying the bowl in meat — thick-cut pork slices covering the noodles almost entirely — a signature so consistent that customers say the taste 'hasn't changed' across decades of visits.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a cash-only ticket machine with a short, simple menu (ramen or tsukemen), so basic Japanese or pointing at photos is enough — no English menu or English-speaking staff is confirmed.

places.name: ジカセイ メンショウ (Jikasei MENSHO (Shibuya PARCO))
places.gf_detail: The signature noodles are wheat-based dough with quinoa kneaded in to reduce carbohydrate/gluten content — sources describe this as "lower in gluten," explicitly not gluten-free. No dedicated gluten-free noodle, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe protocol was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac disease or a strict gluten-free diet; cross-contamination in the shared kitchen (which also handles wheat-containing soy-sauce ramen) is not controlled for.
places.vegan_detail: Four dedicated vegan ramen items are offered alongside the main (non-vegan) menu: vegan dandan noodles (with soup), vegan soupless (mazesoba-style) dandan noodles, vegan miso ramen, and spicy vegan miso ramen. These are prepared as fully plant-based bowls separate from the restaurant's chicken-paitan and A5-wagyu ramen, though made in the same shared kitchen. No source found explicitly detailing whether shared broth pots or hidden bonito/katsuo dashi are used elsewhere on the menu that could risk cross-contact for strict vegans concerned about shared equipment; ask staff if that matters to you.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 daily (some secondary sources give slightly different weekday/weekend last-order times — 21:40 weekdays / 20:40 weekends — so confirm current hours before visiting, as this is a shopping-mall tenant).
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (Udagawa-cho, inside Shibuya PARCO B1F), ~5 min walk
places.notes: Jikasei MENSHO is the Shibuya branch of ramen chef Tomoharu Shono's MENSHO group, whose San Francisco outpost was awarded a Michelin star. The Shibuya PARCO shop pairs the group's wagyu-topped chicken-paitan ramen with a separate line of four dedicated vegan bowls, and uses noodl · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Branch of the MENSHO ramen group (flagship San Francisco location holds a Michelin star); this is the group's Shibuya PARCO location, opened as its 10th Japan branch under a "WAGYU × RAMEN" concept.
chef_bio.background: Jikasei MENSHO is the Shibuya branch of ramen chef Tomoharu Shono's MENSHO group, whose San Francisco outpost was awarded a Michelin star. The Shibuya PARCO shop pairs the group's wagyu-topped chicken-paitan ramen with a separate line of four dedicated vegan bowls, and uses noodles kneaded with quinoa.
chef_bio.philosophy: "We honor the ingredients and connect with the people behind them. A bowl rooted in its place. From ramen, culture emerges, and happiness spreads. " — chef Tomoharu Shono
chef_bio.specialty: For vegan diners: vegan dandan noodles (with or without soup) and vegan miso ramen (regular or spicy). The house's overall signature is chicken-paitan ramen topped with A5 Japanese wagyu.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes its vegan menu as part of a broader effort to make ramen enjoyable for guests with religious or allergy-related dietary restrictions, alongside stated interest in raising environmental awareness through ramen.
English-language reviewers highlight friendly, accommodating staff and a clearly marked vegan menu, describing it as a convenient stop near Shibuya Scramble for a warm, flavorful bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles use a wheat-based dough with quinoa mixed in only to reduce gluten content, not eliminate it; the shop shares a kitchen that also makes wheat-containing soy-sauce ramen.
gf_cross_contamination.source: web search synthesis of Japanese press coverage (menya-shono.com / general reporting)

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan dandan and vegan miso ramen are prepared in the same kitchen as the restaurant's chicken-paitan and A5-wagyu ramen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Cross-contamination in the cooking environment cannot be guaranteed; guests with severe allergies are advised to confirm directly with the shop before ordering.

positives.text: English-language reviewers praise friendly, accommodating staff and a clearly marked vegan menu, calling it a convenient, warming stop near Shibuya Crossing.
Ordering screens include English options, easing the process for non-Japanese-speaking vegan diners.
positives.source: web search synthesis (Tabelog EN / Ramen Gateway listings)

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering runs through a touch-panel/self-checkout system with English menu options, and the shop is described as extremely popular with foreign tourists, making it an easy, low-stress stop for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 松翁 (Matsuou)
places.gf_detail: The zaru soba is made namagona-uchi style with 100% buckwheat flour (no wheat binder in that noodle), which is unusual and noteworthy. However, this is a traditional, non-specialized soba shop: the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce based (soy sauce in Japan is typically wheat-containing unless stated otherwise), the shop also serves wheat-containing "kawari-soba" and tempura (wheat-flour batter) on the same menu, and there is no indication the noodles are boiled in separate water from any wheat-containing items or that a dedicated fryer is used. No allergen or celiac-specific information was found from the restaurant itself. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the tsuyu recipe and boiling/frying setup before ordering, and should not assume safety from the 100% buckwheat noodle alone.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-tempura shop, not vegan-oriented. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is dashi-based and almost certainly includes bonito (katsuobushi), and the tempura menu centers on live shrimp and conger eel. No plant-based dashi or vegan menu option was found in any source reviewed. A plain zaru soba with soy-based tsuyu might be closest to vegan-adjacent, but the bonito dashi would need to be confirmed or substituted by staff, which is unconfirmed.
places.neighborhood: Kanda Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku (near Jimbocho Station, close
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 松翁 opened in Kanda Sarugakucho in 1981 as part of a new wave of independent, self-taught soba shops that broke from the established techniques of the old-line Edo soba lineages. Tucked a short walk from Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, it became a favorite of local salarymen and · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1981 by owner Onodera, an independent-minded soba craftsman who set out to make soba unbound by the old guild techniques of the era.
chef_bio.background: 松翁 opened in Kanda Sarugakucho in 1981 as part of a new wave of independent, self-taught soba shops that broke from the established techniques of the old-line Edo soba lineages. Tucked a short walk from Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, it became a favorite of local salarymen and soba enthusiasts, and count novelist Shotaro Ikenami among its regular patrons in his lifetime.
chef_bio.philosophy: A soba-zen approach that treats the meal as an unhurried sequence: soba-zakura small dishes and sake first, tempura fried and served one piece at a time, and the noodles eaten last so nothing arrives lukewarm.
chef_bio.specialty: Namagona-uchi zaru soba, hand-made with 100% buckwheat flour, and the shop's signature two-color "awase-mori" plate pairing a plain soba with a seasonal kawari-soba; tempura (shrimp and conger eel kept live in a tank by the entrance) is fried to order and brought to the table piece by piece.

anecdotes.text: Novelist Shotaro Ikenami, one of Japan's best-loved food-and-fiction writers, was a regular customer of the shop.
The shop keeps live shrimp and conger eel in a tank by the entrance and fries tempura to order, bringing each piece to the table as it's ready.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No restaurant-specific statement on separating gluten-free (100% buckwheat) noodle boiling water from wheat-containing noodles or tempura batter was found; this is a traditional soba shop without a specialized allergen protocol.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No confirmation of a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce used in soba tsuyu typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares shrimp and conger-eel tempura and bonito-based dashi alongside the soba, so cross-contact with animal products is likely for any vegan order.

positives.text: The zaru soba is made with 100% buckwheat flour (namagona-uchi), meaning the noodle itself, unlike standard nihachi soba, contains no wheat binder.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, cash-only neighborhood soba shop with no English menu or online presence found; a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will go a long way.

places.name: アインソフ ジャーニー 新宿店 (AIN SOPH. Journey Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: The menu clearly marks gluten-free items in English, and the signature pancakes have been reformulated using rice flour. However, this is a shared kitchen, not a dedicated allergen-free facility: reviewers report that French fries are cooked in the same fryer as wheat-containing items, and at least one dish labeled gluten-free was found to use a miso that is not gluten-free. The restaurant does not guarantee zero cross-contamination, so celiac diners should confirm each dish with staff before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is 100% plant-based (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs), and includes an 'oriental vegan' selection that additionally omits garlic, onion, and other strong-flavored roots for diners following that tradition. No animal products are used anywhere in the kitchen.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目
places.cuisine: ビーガン・野菜料理・インターナショナル
places.notes: AIN SOPH. is a Tokyo vegan restaurant group founded in 2009; its name comes from the Hebrew phrase for 'great source.' The Shinjuku Journey location, opened in Shinjuku-Sanchome, is the birthplace of the group's now-famous 'Heavenly Vegan Pancakes,' and the brand has since grown  · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: AIN SOPH. is a Tokyo vegan restaurant group founded in 2009; its name comes from the Hebrew phrase for 'great source. ' The Shinjuku Journey location, opened in Shinjuku-Sanchome, is the birthplace of the group's now-famous 'Heavenly Vegan Pancakes,' and the brand has since grown to additional locations in Ginza, Ikebukuro, and Kyoto.
chef_bio.philosophy: The 'Journey' name reflects a concept of 'creating the self one wants to be': a space designed so vegan and non-vegan diners can share the same table and menu without compromise.
chef_bio.specialty: Heavenly Vegan Pancakes (天上のヴィーガンパンケーキ) — egg- and dairy-free pancakes refined over successive chefs — alongside spice-forward plates like green curry and dry curry served on dishware sourced from Morocco.

anecdotes.text: The founder has described being inspired to start AIN SOPH. after an experience during a tea ceremony, where the flavor of the tea evoked a sense of nature and the power of life, prompting the idea of a restaurant as 'a place to return to one's origin.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: AIN SOPH's kitchen is a shared space where wheat and non-wheat items are prepared together; it is not a dedicated allergen-free facility and the restaurant cannot guarantee that cross-contamination will not occur.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free and other allergen items are clearly marked in English on the menu, but a diner who asked staff directly learned that some items labeled gluten-free actually shared a fryer with wheat items or used a non-gluten-free miso — confirming each dish individually with staff is recommended.

positives.text: Diners have praised the Shinjuku location for its convenient location and clearly available gluten-free options on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant runs an official English-language website and marks gluten-free/allergen items in English on the menu, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking diners to navigate.

places.name: 東京ドミニカ (Tokyo Dominica)
places.gf_detail: One independent blog post calls this shop's curry the "original gluten-free curry" (元祖グルテンフリーカレー), and like most Sapporo-style soup curry it is not thickened with the wheat-flour roux used in standard Japanese curry-rice sauce. However, this claim is not corroborated on the restaurant's own official materials, the soup is seasoned with a "Japanese-style dashi" that likely includes ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce, and several toppings (e.g., fried chicken) are typically flour-battered and cooked in a shared fryer with no confirmed dedicated gluten-free fryer or prep process. Treat any gluten-free claim as unverified — ask staff in detail about the specific soup base, seasoning, and toppings before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, marking, or plant-based stock option was found in any source. The soup base is described by the restaurant as using a "Japanese-style dashi," which in standard Japanese cooking is typically bonito (fish) and/or kelp based — there is no indication this shop offers a fish-free alternative, so the curry is very unlikely to be vegan-safe as served. Vegans should assume the soup base contains fish-derived dashi unless staff confirm otherwise.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-sanchome)
places.notes: Tokyo Dominica opened its doors in Shinjuku on March 11, 2011 — the exact day of the Great East Japan Earthquake. With trains across Tokyo stopped and the city in chaos, almost no customers could reach the brand-new shop, but the staff kept going because they wanted people to tas · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Founder was inspired by a bowl of soup curry he ate in Sapporo, Hokkaido, and later opened his own shop in Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Tokyo Dominica opened its doors in Shinjuku on March 11, 2011 — the exact day of the Great East Japan Earthquake. With trains across Tokyo stopped and the city in chaos, almost no customers could reach the brand-new shop, but the staff kept going because they wanted people to taste their soup curry. The shop grew out of the founder's own experience: he had once eaten soup curry in Sapporo and was moved enough by it to bring the dish to Tokyo. The company later opened a sister shop, Yellow Spice, in Ginza in 2012, and another in Osaka in 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its soup from dozens of spices simmered with a Japanese-style dashi stock, aiming for a curry that is easy to enjoy on a first bite but rewards repeat visits with its layered, evolving spice character — bringing Sapporo-style soup curry into everyday Japanese food culture rather than treating it as a novelty.
chef_bio.specialty: Build-your-own Sapporo-style soup curry: diners choose from five soup bases (yellow, black — with pork bone for richness, red — tomato and garlic, white — soy milk, and deep yellow — coconut milk), roughly twenty spice levels, and a wide range of toppings.

anecdotes.text: Tokyo Dominica opened in Shinjuku on the day of the 2011 Tohoku earthquake, when public transport across the city ground to a halt and the new shop had almost no customers on its first day — the staff pushed through the rocky start because they wanted people to taste their soup curry.
The shop's founder had eaten soup curry in Sapporo years earlier and was moved enough by the experience to open his own soup curry restaurant in Tokyo.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free fryer or prep line is confirmed; fried toppings are likely cooked in shared equipment.
gf_cross_contamination.source: inferred, not directly stated by the restaurant

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant describes its soup as built on dozens of spices plus a "wa-fu dashi" (Japanese-style stock); Japanese-style dashi is conventionally made with bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and/or kelp, and the restaurant does not state that a fish-free or plant-based stock option exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, casual soup-curry counter shop with mostly Japanese-language signage, but the point-and-choose format (soup color, spice level 1-20, toppings) makes ordering manageable even with only basic Japanese.

places.name: 薬膳火鍋 真巴石 渋谷店（シンバセイ） (Yakuzen Hinabe Shinbasei Shibuya Ten (Yakuze)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen guide was found. This is a DIY hot pot restaurant: guests cook shared raw ingredients (vegetables, tofu, mushrooms, meat, noodles) in a communal pot and mix their own dipping sauce from a bar of 10+ condiments that includes soy sauce and oyster sauce — both typically wheat-containing in Japan — plus sesame paste and doubanjiang, all served with shared utensils. One of the four broth bases is a long-simmered chicken broth; the ingredient makeup of the other three (mala, mushroom, tomato) is not confirmed. Because sauces and pots are shared and self-served, cross-contact with wheat-based condiments is likely. Anyone needing a gluten-free meal should ask staff in detail before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu was found. The hot pot format lets guests choose vegetables, mushrooms, and tofu for their pot, but the shared self-serve sauce bar includes oyster sauce (not vegan), and one of the four confirmed broth bases is a chicken broth simmered over 10 hours; the mushroom and tomato broth bases' ingredients (whether they use meat/bonito-based stock) are not confirmed from available sources. Vegans should ask staff specifically about broth composition and avoid the chicken broth and oyster-sauce-based condiments.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷センター街・宇田川町（渋谷駅から徒歩3〜5分）
places.notes: The restaurant specializes in yakuzen hot pot prepared by cooks trained in authentic Sichuan technique, built on the traditional Chinese idea of 医食同源 (food and medicine share the same root). · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Sichuan-trained hot pot cooks (chain concept, no individually named chef confirmed)
chef_bio.origin: Yakuzen (medicinal) hot pot restaurant chain with locations in Shibuya and Ueno, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The restaurant specializes in yakuzen hot pot prepared by cooks trained in authentic Sichuan technique, built on the traditional Chinese idea of 医食同源 (food and medicine share the same root).
chef_bio.philosophy: The broths are simmered with over 30 herbal and medicinal ingredients on the premise that the right soup can support detox, recovery from fatigue, and skin health.
chef_bio.specialty: Yakuzen hot pot with a choice of four broth bases — Sichuan-style spicy mala, a chicken white broth simmered over 10 hours, a mushroom broth, and a tomato broth — plus a build-your-own dipping sauce bar of 10-plus condiments (sesame paste, doubanjiang, soy sauce, oyster sauce, chopped chives and more).

anecdotes.text: 店の中央に胡麻油や芝麻醤、豆板醤、醤油などの調味料が10種類以上置かれ、自分でオリジナルのタレを作れる（1人200円で2皿分、お代わり自由）。
鶏白湯スープは10時間以上煮込まれ、コラーゲンが豊富で旨味が濃い。
30種類以上の漢方食材が配合されたスープが、デトックスや疲労回復、美肌効果につながるとされる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 店の中央に胡麻油、芝麻醤、豆板醤、醤油など10種類以上の調味料が置かれ、客が自分でタレを作る仕組み（共有の調味料・器具を使用）。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 自家製タレ作りの調味料バーに醤油（しょうゆ）が含まれる。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 4種類のスープベースの一つは10時間以上煮込んだ鶏白湯スープ。タレ作りの調味料にオイスターソースを含む。

positives.text: 火鍋形式のため、客が自分で新鮮な野菜・きのこ・豆腐などの具材を選んで鍋に入れて調理できる。

cultural_comfort.note: Listings and menus found are in Japanese with online reservation via Japanese portals (Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Tabelog); no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: インド富士子 (India Fujiko)
places.gf_detail: No page mentions gluten-free accommodation, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation. This is spice-forward South Indian-style curry (not the roux-based Japanese curry that reliably contains wheat), served with basmati rice, which is naturally lower-risk than a wheat-roux curry house, but ingredients like ghee, marinades, or fried items (e.g. "Chicken 65") could involve flour or shared equipment. Nothing confirms dedicated GF prep or cross-contamination controls, so celiac diners should ask staff directly about each curry and any fried items before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The rotating daily curry lineup is meat/fish-forward (pork vindaloo, chicken, fish curry) but typically includes a dal (lentil) curry, which is plant-based unless prepared with ghee or other animal fat — not confirmed either way. No mention anywhere of a dedicated vegan menu, and no information on whether shared pots/utensils are used across meat and vegetarian curries. Vegan diners should ask which of the day's curries are meat/fish-free and whether ghee is used.
places.hours_raw: Lunch (curry, as インド富士子): Tue-Fri 12:00-14:30. Dinner (spice bar, as ムンド不二): Tue-Sat 18:00-23:00. Closed Sundays, Mondays, and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Kanda-Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku (Suidōbashi Station area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Before opening his own shop, the owner worked at the original インド富士 (India Fuji) curry restaurant in Higashi-Koganei. In February 2016 he opened インド富士子 (India Fujiko) as that restaurant's sister shop in Koenji, running an 8-seat counter that became a cult favorite among Tokyo cur · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner-chef (name in Japanese order: 小城正樹, romanized Koshiro Masaki / Oshiro Masaki — exact preferred romanization unconfirmed)
chef_bio.origin: Sister shop lineage from インド富士, Higashi-Koganei
chef_bio.background: Before opening his own shop, the owner worked at the original インド富士 (India Fuji) curry restaurant in Higashi-Koganei. In February 2016 he opened インド富士子 (India Fujiko) as that restaurant's sister shop in Koenji, running an 8-seat counter that became a cult favorite among Tokyo curry enthusiasts. In October 2021 the shop relocated to Suidōbashi (Kanda-Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku), where it now runs a dual identity: a South Indian-style curry lunch counter by day under the name インド富士子, and an evening spice/izakaya bar called ムンド不二 (Mundo Fuji) by night.
chef_bio.philosophy: A personal, idiosyncratic take on South Indian cooking informed by firsthand experience of South Indian food culture, prized by curry devotees for depth and individuality rather than a standardized 'Indian restaurant' menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Lunch curry plate — choose 2 of the day's rotating curries (e. g. pork vindaloo, spinach chicken curry, fish curry, dal curry) served over basmati rice, ¥1,300

anecdotes.text: Described by a travel-blog reviewer as 'a hidden gem serving a distinctive South Indian plate backed by firsthand experience in India, for curry maniacs' (水道橋発のインドカレー店「インド富士子」～現地での体験に裏打ちされた個性的な南インドプレートをカレーマニアに提供する隠れた名店).
The owner was interviewed alongside musician Saya of Tenniscoats and Koiken of Atarashii Hito about the shop's identity and its move to Suidōbashi.

In an October 2023 social post marking two years at the Suidōbashi location, the owner wrote candidly that the shop had barely survived with supporters' help and was 'honestly about at its limit,' asking people to visit while it was still open.

cultural_comfort.note: Small, cash-only, regulars-heavy indie shop with a Japanese-only social-media presence and no confirmed English menu, so basic Japanese or pointing at photos will help.

places.name: 油そば総本店 新宿東口アルタ裏店 (Aburasoba Sohonten Shinjuku Higashiguchi Alt)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is a wheat-noodle abura-soba tossed in a soy-sauce-based tare; no gluten-free noodle alternative or allergen menu was found in any source. Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is typically wheat-based, and this shop's tare is described only as "a blend of several soy sauces," with no gluten-free claim anywhere. This is a small, ticket-machine-driven ramen counter with no indication of allergen protocols, so cross-contamination risk should be assumed high. Not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu option was found in any source. The default bowl includes pork chashu, and the proprietary soy-sauce tare's full ingredient list (which may include animal-derived stock or extracts) is not disclosed. There is no dashi-based soup here (it's a tossed, not brothed, noodle), but the tare itself is undisclosed and unconfirmed as plant-based. Do not assume vegan-safe without asking staff directly.
places.hours_raw: 24時間営業・年中無休 (Open 24 hours, 365 days a year)
places.neighborhood: 新宿東口 (Shinjuku East Exit / behind Studio Alta)
places.notes: Part of the 東京油組総本店 (Tokyo Abura Gumi Sohonten) abura-soba chain, which the operator's own promotional material traces to a long-established (multi-decade) noodle manufacturer that branched into running its own oil-noodle shops across Tokyo. This Shinjuku East Exit branch sits di · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: Part of the 東京油組総本店 (Tokyo Abura Gumi Sohonten) abura-soba chain, which the operator's own promotional material traces to a long-established (multi-decade) noodle manufacturer that branched into running its own oil-noodle shops across Tokyo. This Shinjuku East Exit branch sits directly behind Studio Alta, tucked into a building with counter seating on the ground floor and further seating in the basement, and runs 24 hours a day so it doubles as a late-night/after-the-last-train option.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chain markets abura-soba as a lighter, lower-calorie alternative to broth ramen, built around the quality of the tare (sauce) and noodle rather than a long-simmered soup, with all bowl sizes (regular/large/extra-large) priced the same so portion size is the diner's choice.
chef_bio.specialty: 特製油そば (tokusei abura-soba) — thick, springy noodles tossed (not served in broth) with a proprietary blended soy-sauce tare, topped with menma, sliced pork chashu, negi and nori; a spicy miso version is also offered. Diners finish the bowl themselves with a few circles of vinegar and rayu chili oil, mixing before eating.

anecdotes.text: House description: the tare blends several different soy sauces to build depth of flavor under the oil-noodle format, with a separate spicy-miso tare balancing heat and miso richness.
Blog account of visiting the chain describes it as a specialty shop built around a rich soy-sauce tare finished tableside with vinegar and chili oil.

cultural_comfort.note: Order and pay at a Japanese-language ticket vending machine near the entrance; no English menu was confirmed, though the shop is well used to late-night and international foot traffic in the Alta-back area.

places.name: つるとんたん UDON NOODLE Brasserie 渋谷店 (TsuruTonTan UDON NOODLE Brasserie Shibuya)
places.gf_detail: The udon here is traditional wheat-flour noodle, made fresh in-house at each location — there is no confirmed gluten-free noodle substitute for this restaurant in any source checked. The broth (kake-jiru) is soy-sauce based and soy sauce traditionally contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free fryer or kitchen process was found. This is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house dashi (broth) used across the standard menu is fish-based (kombu and bonito flakes), so the great majority of dishes, including many vegetable-topped bowls, are not vegan even when they look plant-based. No confirmed dedicated vegan menu or fish-free broth option was found for this Tokyo (Shibuya) location — claims of vegan udon appear tied to other TsuruTonTan branches (e.g., in the US) and could not be verified for Shibuya specifically. Vegan diners should not assume any dish is animal-free without asking staff directly, and even then cross-contact with the shared fish-based broth is likely.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 (L.O. food 22:00), daily, no listed closed days
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷)
places.notes: TsuruTonTan traces back to 1978, when Kato Seiichi — who grew up in the udon country of Sanuki, Kagawa — opened a hand-pulled udon shop called Honke Sanuki in Osaka, at a time when chewy, Sanuki-style udon wasn't common there. That shop's technique became the basis for TsuruTonTa · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Sōemonchō, Osaka (1989); founder grew up in Sanuki, Kagawa
chef_bio.background: TsuruTonTan traces back to 1978, when Kato Seiichi — who grew up in the udon country of Sanuki, Kagawa — opened a hand-pulled udon shop called Honke Sanuki in Osaka, at a time when chewy, Sanuki-style udon wasn't common there. That shop's technique became the basis for TsuruTonTan, which opened in Osaka's Sōemonchō district in 1989, blending Sanuki noodle-making with Osaka's food culture. The brand is now run by Kato Pleasure Group and has grown into a chain across Osaka, Tokyo, and other cities, plus locations in the United States. The Shibuya Scramble Square branch opened in 2019 as the chain's seventh Tokyo location.
chef_bio.philosophy: Each restaurant makes its own noodles daily rather than using a central factory, aiming to serve udon at its peak just after boiling. The broth recipe — Hokkaido Rishiri kombu paired with Kagoshima Makurazaki bonito flakes — has reportedly stayed unchanged for more than three decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Oversized bowls of udon made fresh at each location — noodles kneaded, cut, and boiled to order — served in dozens of styles from classic bukkake and curry udon to cream-based seafood bowls.

anecdotes.text: The Shibuya location sits on the 13th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, offering views over the famous scramble crossing while diners eat; it opened in 2019 as the chain's 7th Tokyo shop.

TsuruTonTan's dashi is built from thick kombu from Hokkaido's cold Rishiri waters and quality bonito flakes from Kagoshima's Makurazaki, a combination the company says has defined its flavor since founding.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The noodle broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) base and the noodles are standard wheat udon, made fresh at each location — wheat is present throughout the core menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dashi is built from Hokkaido Rishiri kombu and Kagoshima Makurazaki bonito (fish) flakes, and is used as the base for most dishes on the standard menu, including many vegetable-topped bowls, so most dishes are not vegan by default.

positives.text: Table-side ordering tablets support English, Korean, and Chinese, which can make it easier to ask staff about ingredients even without shared Japanese.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is done via tablet with English, Korean, and Chinese language support, and the location inside Shibuya Scramble Square regularly serves international visitors.

places.name: 中華そば 勝本 (Chuka Soba Katsumoto)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles are used, made specially for the shop by a long-established Tokyo noodle maker, and the broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which is typically wheat-containing. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated allergen protocol was found in any source, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strictly gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is built on dried sardines (niboshi) simmered with chicken, and the noodles are standard wheat noodles — there is no plant-based broth or noodle option, so this is not vegan or vegetarian.
places.hours_raw: Most-cited hours: Mon-Sat 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:45), Sun 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:45); no fixed closed day except New Year holidays. An older/alternate source instead reports Mon-Sat 11:00-20:00 or 21:00 (closing when soup runs out) with Sunday and irregular Monday closures. Treat with some caution and reconfirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Kanda Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidobashi Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner, Matsumura Yasushi, spent 36 years working in French cuisine, rising to executive head chef at the former Kyoto ANA Hotel. Wanting to serve carefully made food at a price more people could enjoy, he moved from haute French kitchens into ramen, opening this small counter · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: Owner / Chef: Matsumura Yasushi (松村康史)
chef_bio.background: The owner, Matsumura Yasushi, spent 36 years working in French cuisine, rising to executive head chef at the former Kyoto ANA Hotel. Wanting to serve carefully made food at a price more people could enjoy, he moved from haute French kitchens into ramen, opening this small counter shop in Suidobashi in 2018 as the starting point of his ramen career.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing the discipline and refinement of French technique into a humble bowl of ramen, so that thoughtfully crafted food can reach more people at an accessible price.
chef_bio.specialty: Niboshi shoyu chuka soba — a soy-sauce ramen built on a broth of dried sardines (niboshi) from Kujukushima in Nagasaki Prefecture, simmered together with chicken, paired with noodles made specially for the shop by a long-established Asakusa noodle maker.

anecdotes.text: Matsumura's ramen career began at this Suidobashi counter in 2018; its success led him to open Tsukesoba Kanda Katsumoto and the Michelin-recognized Ginza Hachigo, both of which now draw daily lines.

The shop's original operating company went through bankruptcy proceedings in March 2022 and reopened under new management (Food Operation Japan Co., Ltd.) that May, continuing at the same Suidobashi counter.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine (券売機) and payment is cash only, so a little familiarity with basic Japanese ramen terms (or pointing at photos on the machine) helps, though the shop is popular enough with visitors that queuing and ordering are fairly self-explanatory.

places.name: 新宿割烹 中嶋 (Shinjuku Kappo Nakajima)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated preparation process could be confirmed from any source. As a traditional kappo/kaiseki restaurant, dishes almost certainly include soy sauce (standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based) and fried ("揚げ物") courses, both common wheat sources, and no source mentioned tamari or a gluten-free adaptation. Guests with celiac disease should not assume safety here without calling ahead and confirming directly with the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-centered kappo/kaiseki restaurant; the chef personally sources fish each morning and courses include sashimi, grilled fish, and traditional dashi (Japanese kaiseki dashi is typically bonito/katsuo-based). No vegan menu, plant-based substitution, or dashi alternative was found in any source, so it should not be assumed vegan-friendly.
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.notes: Founded as "Kappou Nakajima" in Ginza in 1931 by the grandfather of current chef-owner Nakajima Teiji, who had been the first head chef at Kitaoji Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo. Nakajima's father later opened the Shinjuku branch. Nakajima Teiji took over as owner in 1980 after trai · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (b. 1956)
chef_bio.background: Founded as "Kappou Nakajima" in Ginza in 1931 by the grandfather of current chef-owner Nakajima Teiji, who had been the first head chef at Kitaoji Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo. Nakajima's father later opened the Shinjuku branch. Nakajima Teiji took over as owner in 1980 after training in Kyoto, and also teaches at culinary schools.
chef_bio.philosophy: Continues a culinary lineage traced to Kitaoji Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo, combining Kyoto-trained kaiseki fundamentals with an emphasis on drawing out the natural flavor of each season's ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kappo/kaiseki courses built around fish and other ingredients the chef selects each morning; a sardine (iwashi) preparation in the lunch set is called out by name in diner reviews.

anecdotes.text: Chef Nakajima personally visits the market each morning to select the day's seasonal ingredients, continuing a family tradition begun by his grandfather at Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo.
A diner review of the restaurant is titled "鰯三昧嬉しやランチ" (a joyful feast-of-sardines lunch), referencing the sardine-centered lunch set as a house favorite.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, reservation-based kappo restaurant with a bilingual (JP/EN) website but reservations and service are primarily conducted in Japanese, so calling ahead or booking through a Japanese-capable platform is recommended.

places.name: アイコウシャ (Aikousha (I-Kousya))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free bun, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation was mentioned in any source. The burger is served deconstructed with a standard wheat bun used as a "lid" the diner adds themselves, so a diner could in theory skip the bun, but no gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free sauce/dressing information was confirmed anywhere. Treat as not gluten-free-aware; sauces (mayonnaise-based dressings) and any flour-dusted components are unconfirmed for wheat content.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or plant-based patty/menu option was found in any source; this is a beef-focused burger shop with mayonnaise-based dressings and cheese options, and no indication of accommodating vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri/Sat 11:00–17:30, Sun/holidays 11:00–15:30, closed Wednesdays; closes early once sold out (売り切れ次第終了)
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Kanda / Suidōbashi East, Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop takes its name from 愛光舎 (Aikousha), a Meiji-era dairy/milk shop that the current owner's great-grandfather ran in the same Kanda Misakicho neighborhood; the family's milk business passed through three generations on nearby ground before the site became a hamburger counte · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.chef_name: Matsuhashi (owner/founder, family name from Tabelog/blog reviews; given name not confirmed)
roles: founder (of current hamburger concept; shop name inherited from great-grandfather's dairy business)
chef_bio.origin: Kanda Misakicho / Nishi-Kanda, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The shop takes its name from 愛光舎 (Aikousha), a Meiji-era dairy/milk shop that the current owner's great-grandfather ran in the same Kanda Misakicho neighborhood; the family's milk business passed through three generations on nearby ground before the site became a hamburger counter around 2003. In July 2025 the shop relocated a few minutes away to Nishi-Kanda after more than a decade in its original spot, reopening in a refreshed space under the same name.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Matsuhashi says he never copied another shop's recipes; he sketches out 'what would taste good' in his head before developing a dish, refining American-style touches like mayonnaise application and bean preparation with guidance from relatives in Alabama.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-formed 100% beef patty burgers served deconstructed — patty and toppings on the plate with the bun set on top as a lid for the diner to assemble — paired with house-made potato chips that take two full days to prepare.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name preserves the memory of 愛光舎, the owner's great-grandfather's Meiji-era milk shop that once operated in the same Kanda Misakicho area.
Matsuhashi builds recipes from his own imagination rather than borrowing from other burger shops, consulting American relatives in Alabama on techniques.
The burger is served unassembled with the bun as a lid so customers build it themselves, and the side potato chips are made in-house over a two-day process.
After more than ten years in Kanda Misakicho, the shop relocated to nearby Nishi-Kanda and reopened on July 11, 2025.

cultural_comfort.note: Small, old-school cash-only counter shop with no indication of English menus or staff — manageable with basic pointing/ordering but not set up for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 魚真 新宿店 (Uoshin Shinjuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce option was found in any source. The menu includes tempura and fried fish (wheat-based batter) and dishes are typically served with standard Japanese soy sauce (usually wheat-based). No allergy protocol was confirmed, so this cannot be recommended as safe for celiac diners without direct, detailed confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-and-seafood specialty izakaya built entirely around sashimi, grilled/simmered fish, fried seafood, and seafood sushi (e.g., its signature uni/negitoro/crab/salmon-roe hand roll). No vegan dishes, plant-based dashi, or vegan menu were found in any source, making it unsuitable for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Shinjuku Station Southeast Exit (新宿駅東南口)
places.notes: 魚真 (Uoshin) Shinjuku is a seafood-focused izakaya and sushi counter run by Uoshin Co., Ltd. (株式会社シン), which operates a small handful of Uoshin locations built around a fresh-fish-wholesaler concept — fish is sourced daily and served the same day. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo, Shinjuku
chef_bio.background: 魚真 (Uoshin) Shinjuku is a seafood-focused izakaya and sushi counter run by Uoshin Co. , Ltd. (株式会社シン), which operates a small handful of Uoshin locations built around a fresh-fish-wholesaler concept — fish is sourced daily and served the same day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fish delivered fresh each day is prepared multiple ways — sashimi, grilled, simmered, deep-fried (tempura/furai), and sake-steamed — with counter seating so diners can watch the chefs work.
chef_bio.specialty: "Nokke-zushi" (のっけ寿司) — a hand roll piled high with sea urchin (uni), negitoro, crab, and salmon roe; also known for its daily-changing sashimi platter of seasonal fish.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature 'nokke-zushi' piles uni, negi-toro, crab, and salmon roe on top of a hand roll, a specialty shared across Uoshin's locations.
Fish bought fresh daily is served as sashimi or cooked as grilled fish, simmered fish, fried/tempura, or sake-steamed, with counter seats where guests can watch the chef prepare each dish.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations are taken by phone only (no confirmed English menu or online English booking), so some Japanese ability or translation help will make the visit easier.

places.name: ウッドベリー ベーカリー（WOODBERRY BAKERY） (Woodberry Bakery)
places.gf_detail: The bakery's breads and baked goods are made with vegan and gluten-free-conscious recipes and organic ingredients, and it appears in gluten-free directory listings for Shibuya. However, a gluten-free directory source explicitly notes that gluten-free items are prepared in a shared kitchen/bakery environment and are "not recommended for highly sensitive celiacs" — so treat it as gluten-conscious rather than a dedicated gluten-free facility; no information was found on a separate gluten-free prep area or dedicated equipment.
places.vegan_detail: Described by the brand itself and multiple third-party sources as a predominantly vegan bakery/café: vegan-recipe breads and baked goods, a lunch menu built around organic vegetables, and non-dairy milk options for drinks. A vegan directory listing notes clearly labeled vegan pastries throughout the case. No confirmed information was found on hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., dashi/katsuo) in the savory lunch items, so full 100%-vegan status across the entire menu is not certain — ask staff to confirm any non-bakery items.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (Food last order 17:00)
places.neighborhood: Shibuya-ku, on Meiji-dori roughly midway between Shibuya and
places.notes: WOODBERRY COFFEE built a following through its Shibuya-area specialty coffee shops before extending the brand into baking, opening this dedicated bake shop as a production base for vegan and gluten-free-recipe breads and pastries. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: First stand-alone bakery from WOODBERRY COFFEE, a Tokyo specialty coffee roaster (opened its first café in Yoga); the bakery opened along Meiji-dori between Shibuya and Ebisu in spring 2024 as the brand's first bake shop.
chef_bio.background: WOODBERRY COFFEE built a following through its Shibuya-area specialty coffee shops before extending the brand into baking, opening this dedicated bake shop as a production base for vegan and gluten-free-recipe breads and pastries.
chef_bio.philosophy: Described by the shop itself as a bakery that is "kind to both your body and the planet" — using carefully selected organic ingredients and vegan/gluten-free recipes for a wellness- and sustainability-minded neighborhood bakery.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic, vegan-recipe breads and baked goods (including gluten-free items and, more recently, naturally leavened sourdough), plus lunch plates built around organic vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The bakery's own opening announcement frames it as WOODBERRY COFFEE's first bake shop, with a "park in the town" interior of wood and tile and large street-facing windows, built around the concept of body- and earth-friendly baking.
A Japanese food blogger described the vegan breads and pastries as not leaving the stomach feeling heavy, and noted the shop draws visible lines from the street at busy times.
A magazine feature framed the shop's appeal as an 'organic lunch and sweets that are kind to the earth and the body' in the Shibuya area.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gluten-free options are prepared in a shared bakery environment and are not recommended for highly sensitive celiacs.

positives.text: Reviewers describe lots of clearly labeled vegan pastries throughout the case, a full coffee selection with non-dairy milk options, and staff willing to pack items into customers' own containers on request.
A Japanese food blogger noted the vegan bread and pastries had good texture and didn't feel heavy in the stomach after eating.

cultural_comfort.note: A trendy, design-forward Shibuya café with bilingual (Japanese/English) social-media presence and clearly labeled vegan items, but no confirmed English-language menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: 魚料理 のじま (Sakana Ryōri Nojima)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or allergy accommodations were found in any source. The signature tennen buri-don (wild yellowtail bowl) is marinated in a soy-sauce-and-wasabi tare, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; sashimi and grilled-fish sets may be safer in principle but likely still involve soy-sauce-based dips or glazes. No information on dashi composition, cross-contamination practices, or a dedicated fryer was available. Celiac diners should not assume safety here without calling ahead.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-focused restaurant with no vegetarian or vegan menu items identified in any source; all signature and side dishes (yellowtail bowl, sashimi sets, grilled and simmered fish) are fish-based. No information found on whether dashi/broths used in miso soup or side dishes are fish-free. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri approx. 11:00-15:00 (or 11:00-14:00 LO) and 18:00-22:00 (or 18:00-22:00 LO); Saturday hours reported inconsistently across sources as either 18:00-22:00 or 11:00-14:00/until sold out; closed Sundays and public holidays. Sources disagree on exact minute-level times — confirm by phone (03-3400-6365) before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Shibuya (Namikibashi, near Shibuya Station new south exit)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nojima began as a fishmonger ("魚仁") founded in the Meiji era (around 1910); the current owner-chef, born in 1937, started helping his father at the fish shop after middle school and has worked in the trade for roughly 68 years. The shop shifted into serving cooked fish meals to c · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Nojima began as a fishmonger ("魚仁") founded in the Meiji era (around 1910); the current owner-chef, born in 1937, started helping his father at the fish shop after middle school and has worked in the trade for roughly 68 years. The shop shifted into serving cooked fish meals to customers starting in 1989. The owner still rises at 5am most days to buy fish himself at the Toyosu market, relying on decades of experience to judge quality, and personally fillets every fish and tallies the bill by mental arithmetic at the register.
chef_bio.philosophy: Only natural, wild-caught fish is used — no frozen or farmed fish — reflecting the eye for quality the owner developed over decades as a fishmonger before the shop began serving meals.
chef_bio.specialty: Tennen buri-don (天然ぶり丼) — a bowl of wild yellowtail sashimi dressed in a soy-and-wasabi marinade, served with miso soup, potato salad, kelp tsukudani and pickles; also grilled and simmered fish sets (aji tataki, kinki, isaki, buri kama).

anecdotes.text: At 80 the owner gave up his driver's license and now gets around by bicycle, with a set routine of a morning coffee in Ebisu and an afternoon break at a nearby Doutor.
He still personally negotiates for and buys the day's fish at the market at 5am, and does all the filleting and register accounting himself, including tallying bills by mental math.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's signature dish, the wild yellowtail (buri) rice bowl, is dressed in a soy-sauce-and-wasabi marinade (tare), meaning standard wheat-containing shoyu is used in at least this core menu item.

positives.text: The owner personally selects only fresh, wild-caught (never frozen or farmed) fish each morning at the Toyosu market, and fillets it himself, reflecting decades of fishmonger experience.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-school, counter-style fish shokudo run by its elderly owner-chef with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese (or pointing at the menu board) will help.

places.name: ミッドタウン バーベキュー トウキョウ (Midtown BBQ Tokyo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found in any available source. As an American BBQ smokehouse, the menu centers on wheat-bun burgers and pulled-pork sandwiches, and rubs, glazes, or barbecue sauces commonly contain wheat or soy sauce; none of this is documented one way or the other for this restaurant. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should assume meaningful risk and confirm directly with staff (in Japanese if possible) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around smoked and grilled meats (A5 wagyu brisket, spare ribs, pulled pork, steak, burgers, chicken, meat-topped nachos and tacos). No vegan dishes, substitutions, or a vegetarian menu section were identified in any source reviewed. Vegan diners should look elsewhere or confirm directly with the restaurant before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 飯田橋 (Iidabashi), Chiyoda-ku
places.notes: Midtown BBQ began as a small steak-and-smokehouse concept in Nagoya and built a loyal following among BBQ fans in Japan before expanding to Yokohama and this Tokyo (Iidabashi) location. Rather than following one single regional American barbecue tradition, the kitchen blends seve · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Owner reportedly grew up in Canada with family roots in the American South
chef_bio.background: Midtown BBQ began as a small steak-and-smokehouse concept in Nagoya and built a loyal following among BBQ fans in Japan before expanding to Yokohama and this Tokyo (Iidabashi) location. Rather than following one single regional American barbecue tradition, the kitchen blends several influences, making its own rubs and smoking meats in-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: Real, slow-smoked American barbecue done with a mix of regional influences rather than a single house style, paired with char-grilled steaks, burgers, and craft beer in a casual, come-as-you-are setting.
chef_bio.specialty: A5 Wagyu Brisket, slow-smoked over many hours — described as the dish that put the restaurant on the map

anecdotes.text: Midtown BBQ started as a small steak and smokehouse in Nagoya and built a loyal following of BBQ lovers in Japan before growing into locations in Yokohama and Tokyo.
The A5 Wagyu Brisket is considered the dish that made Midtown BBQ famous, made without adherence to one specific regional barbecue recipe.

cultural_comfort.note: American-owned smokehouse with an English Tabelog page and English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck, TableAgent), so English-speaking diners should feel comfortable.

places.name: 上海小籠包厨房 阿杏 本店 (Shanghai Xiaolongbao Kitchen Aan (Honten))
places.gf_detail: Xiaolongbao and other dim sum here are hand-made in-house from wheat-flour dough (the dumpling-making station is visible near the entrance), so the signature dish is inherently wheat-based. No gluten-free menu, alternative wheat-free wrapper, or dedicated-fryer/allergy-protocol information was found on any listing or review consulted; dipping condiments (vinegar/soy sauce) typical of this cuisine commonly contain wheat. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten sensitivity should not consider this restaurant safe without direct, in-person confirmation, which could not be verified here.
places.vegan_detail: This is a pork-focused Shanghai xiaolongbao specialist: the signature dumplings and grilled xiaolongbao are filled with 100% domestic pork and served in pork-based broth/aspic. No vegan menu, plant-based dumpling, or meat-free broth option was found in any source consulted; the cuisine is built around meat fillings and animal-based stock.
places.neighborhood: 新宿区 新宿三丁目（新宿駅より徒歩2〜3分）
places.cuisine: 中華・飲茶・小籠包・点心
places.notes: Founded in 2010 by an owner originally from Shanghai, opened in Shinjuku to bring authentic Shanghai-style xiaolongbao and dim sum to Tokyo. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shanghai, China
chef_bio.background: Founded in 2010 by an owner originally from Shanghai, opened in Shinjuku to bring authentic Shanghai-style xiaolongbao and dim sum to Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Dumpling skins are hand-rolled and filled to order in an open 'xiaolongbao room' visible from the entrance, so every order is freshly wrapped and cooked rather than pre-made; filling is 100% domestic (Japanese) pork.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made xiaolongbao (soup dumplings), including a grilled/pan-fried version (焼き小籠包) with a crisp seared bottom and juicy broth inside a chewy, made-to-order skin.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant description highlights that skins are made from scratch and cooked to order so they arrive freshly baked and hot, with a chewy skin and crispy bottom, and that at the kitchen entrance there is a dedicated 'xiaolongbao room' where staff continuously stretch dough and wrap filling for guests to watch.
Listed as a Shinjuku dim sum/yum-cha specialist near Shinjuku Station's central east exit, known specifically for its grilled xiaolongbao (焼き小籠包).

cultural_comfort.note: Japanese-only web presence and menus were found (no confirmed English-language menu or English-speaking-service info), though the restaurant does appear on Japanese reservation platforms that some visitors use with translation apps.

places.name: 嚔（アチュー） (Achuu (Kushami))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, rice-flour substitution, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination information could be found for this restaurant despite targeted searching. As a standard Japanese izakaya/standing bar it almost certainly uses conventional wheat-based soy sauce and likely wheat-based batters/tempura in at least some dishes. Diners with celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity should not assume any gluten-free accommodation and must ask staff directly (in Japanese) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based substitution, or dashi/bonito information could be confirmed. Reported dishes (e.g., yellowtail with sweet ginger) indicate this is a fish/meat-forward izakaya menu; as with most Japanese izakayas, dashi is likely bonito-based unless a dish is explicitly marked otherwise, which was not found here. Vegan diners should not assume options are available and should ask staff directly.
places.hours_raw: 18:00〜26:00（アバウト／approximate); most sources report 無休 (open daily), though hours are described as casual/approximate and one older source cited Sunday closures.
places.neighborhood: Shinsen, Dogenzaka, Shibuya-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened around May 2023 near Shinsen Station as the second location of the brothers' popular Shibuya standing-bar izakaya "Otonage" (大人気). Elder brother Kenta trained at a culinary school and a Japanese-cuisine restaurant before apprenticing within the Osaka-based SASAYA group (ya · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Shinsen, Shibuya, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: Opened around May 2023 near Shinsen Station as the second location of the brothers' popular Shibuya standing-bar izakaya "Otonage" (大人気). Elder brother Kenta trained at a culinary school and a Japanese-cuisine restaurant before apprenticing within the Osaka-based SASAYA group (yakitori, kamameshi, oden izakayas across roughly 47 locations); younger brother Kota came from consulting and real-estate work before joining Tokyo's "Product of Time" restaurant-development group.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name "嚔" (kushami, "sneeze") is styled "Achuu" — the sneeze sound people jokingly say happens when someone is gossiping about you. The bar deliberately does not publish its own address, aiming to spread purely by word of mouth and build a devoted, in-the-know following through old-fashioned personal connection rather than social-media marketing.
chef_bio.specialty: Playful, craft-driven izakaya small plates (reported items include yellowtail with sweet ginger and a chrysanthemum-topped takoyaki) alongside an extensive spirits, highball and craft-gin list.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name plays on the Japanese word for "sneeze": the idea is that when people talk about the bar behind its back, that gossip should spread the way a sneeze spreads — so the concept intentionally withholds the address and lets rumor do the marketing.

Achuu is the Sunada brothers' second Shibuya venture after their hit standing bar Otonage; Kenta apprenticed through a 47-restaurant Osaka izakaya group before returning to Tokyo, while Kota's background is in consulting and real estate before moving into restaurant production.
A visitor review describes the cooking as "quality that's on another level" and notes that although the menu item names are full of playful humor, the food itself is seriously accomplished.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, address-undisclosed standing bar built around Japanese wordplay and word-of-mouth culture, with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking service, so some Japanese ability or a translation app will help.

places.name: 鯛担麺専門店 恋し鯛 (Koishitai (Tai-Tantanmen Specialty Shop))
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's identity is built around its "specially developed" wavy wheat noodles paired with the tai broth, and shoyu/soy-based seasoning is standard for this style of ramen (wheat-containing soy sauce). No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated GF menu, or allergy-specific handling was found in any source. This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop; it should be treated as unsafe for celiac diners absent direct confirmation from staff.
places.vegan_detail: All signature dishes are built on a fish (tai/sea bream) broth from Ehime Prefecture, and the tantanmen style typically includes meat-based elements (ground pork, sesame paste). No vegetarian or vegan menu items, substitutions, or plant-based broth options were found in any listing, review, or the official site's menu descriptions.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon/Wed/Thu/Fri): 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Saturday: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30), continuous. Sunday: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30), lunch only. Holidays: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). As of July, Tuesday also switched to lunch-only service (11:00-15:00).
places.notes: The shop's chef/owner, Saka Shota, spent roughly 14 years learning and practicing French cuisine before turning to ramen, and built Koishitai around applying French culinary technique to a ramen bowl. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The shop's chef/owner, Saka Shota, spent roughly 14 years learning and practicing French cuisine before turning to ramen, and built Koishitai around applying French culinary technique to a ramen bowl.
