chef_bio.philosophy: Fusing French cuisine technique with the ramen format, and treating each bowl as a small piece of visual craftsmanship — the shop deliberately breaks from conventional ramen-shop presentation with a stylish, modern interior and plating.
chef_bio.specialty: Tai-tantanmen (鯛担麺): a rich, creamy broth made by slow-simmering abundant red sea bream (tai) from Uwajima, Ehime Prefecture, using a French long-simmer technique, paired with specially developed wavy noodles designed to hold the broth; the same tai broth is also used in the shop's popular sea-bream rice dish.

anecdotes.text: A French-trained chef who spent about 14 years in French cuisine fell in love with ramen and fused the two disciplines to create Koishitai's tai-broth ramen.

Koishitai is the Suidobashi/Kudanshita branch of a tai-tantanmen concept whose sister restaurant, 'Dakishimetai,' originated in Osaka's Higobashi neighborhood, opened by 縁petit株式会社 as part of a French-ramen chain expanding nationally since 2020.

cultural_comfort.note: Japanese-only signage and menu (an English machine-translated Tabelog listing exists, but no restaurant-provided English materials were found), though ramen-shop ordering conventions make it approachable without fluent Japanese.

places.name: すし岩瀬 (Sushi Iwase)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free protocol or menu was found for this restaurant. It serves a single fixed omakase course of nigiri alternated with tsumami side dishes — traditional Edomae nigiri is finished with a soy-based nikiri/tsume sauce (soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the tsumami courses likely include dashi-based preparations. Because the menu is chef's-choice with no à la carte substitution described anywhere, there is no confirmed way to avoid wheat-based soy sauce or ensure no cross-contact; not recommended as a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, advance confirmation from the restaurant by phone.
places.vegan_detail: This is an entirely seafood-based Edomae omakase counter (nigiri and fish/seafood-based tsumami served in a single fixed course) with no vegetarian or vegan menu, alternative, or accommodation found in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Dinner (omakase only): Tue-Sat 15:00-21:30; Lunch: Wed 12:00-14:30 (some sources also list Sat lunch); Closed Mondays and the 1st & 3rd Sunday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (near Tochomae Station), Shinjuku
places.cuisine: 本格江戸前鮨 / 握り寿司
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Iwase came to sushi relatively late, having started his culinary career at 21 after an earlier stint as a salaryman. He trained within the well-regarded "Sushi Sho" (すし匠) lineage of Edomae sushi restaurants, working at Sushi Sho Masa in Nishi-Azabu, Sushi Subaru in Aoyama, and Ta · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: Kanagawa Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Iwase came to sushi relatively late, having started his culinary career at 21 after an earlier stint as a salaryman. He trained within the well-regarded "Sushi Sho" (すし匠) lineage of Edomae sushi restaurants, working at Sushi Sho Masa in Nishi-Azabu, Sushi Subaru in Aoyama, and Takumi Tatsuhiro in Shinjuku before striking out on his own in September 2012. The restaurant later relocated to its current Nishi-Shinjuku location near Tochomae Station in 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: Food writers describe the restaurant's approach as "呑ます鮨" (sushi that makes you want to drink) — richly seasoned tsumami dishes interspersed with delicate nigiri, built around the rhythm of pairing food with sake rather than nigiri alone.
chef_bio.specialty: An alternating omakase of nigiri and otsumami (small savory side dishes) in the "Sushi Sho style," running to roughly 33-35 courses in total. Fish is sourced directly from the Yanagibashi Rengo Ichiba market in Hakata, Fukuoka, with Iwase reportedly traveling there himself to select seasonal seafood before it is shipped to the restaurant.

anecdotes.text: A restaurant reviewer describes Iwase's food as carrying on the Sushi Sho lineage in his own style, characterizing the meal as "呑ます鮨" because the richness of the otsumami keeps guests reaching for their sake cup between bites of nigiri.

Iwase trained through several branches of the Sushi Sho family of restaurants (Nishi-Azabu, Aoyama, Shinjuku) before opening his own counter in 2012, later moving to the current Nishi-Shinjuku address in 2017.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, ten-seat, chef-owned sushi counter with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff; reservations are typically made by phone or Japanese booking platforms, so going with a Japanese-speaking companion or a hotel concierge's help is recommended.

places.name: らーめん 穀雨 (Ramen Kokū)
places.gf_detail: Traditional wheat ramen noodles and wheat-wrapper wontons; the shoyu-based broths also contain wheat via soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated allergy preparation, or any related accommodation was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broths are built on animal (pork/chicken) and seafood (gyokai) dashi in the shina-soba tradition; the signature dishes are pork chashu and pork/shrimp wontons. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, plant-based broth, or substitution options were found in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 12:00–15:00 (L.O.), 18:00–20:00 (L.O.); Sat 12:00–15:00 (L.O., open only 2nd & 4th Saturdays); Closed Sun, public holidays, and 1st/3rd/5th Saturdays
places.neighborhood: 桜丘町 (Sakuragaoka-cho), Shibuya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ramen Kokū is a tiny, tucked-away 9-seat counter shop in the quiet Sakuragaoka backstreets near Shibuya Station, run by an alum of Meguro's well-regarded Kazuya lineage of clear-broth ramen makers. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The chef trained at the respected "Shina Soba Kazuya" (支那そばかづ屋) in Meguro before opening this shop independently in Shibuya in November 2012.
chef_bio.background: Ramen Kokū is a tiny, tucked-away 9-seat counter shop in the quiet Sakuragaoka backstreets near Shibuya Station, run by an alum of Meguro's well-regarded Kazuya lineage of clear-broth ramen makers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Follows the refined "shina soba" style of its parent lineage: light-looking but deeply flavorful shoyu and shio broths built from animal and seafood dashi, without heavy seasoning. The shop's name, Kokū (穀雨), is one of the 24 traditional solar terms and refers to the spring rains that nourish young grain.
chef_bio.specialty: Wonton ramen (ワンタンメン) — particularly the shio wonton men, often ordered with extra wontons — alongside a shio/shoyu ramen and a tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from 穀雨 (Kokū), one of the 24 traditional Japanese solar terms meaning the spring rains that nourish all grains.
Described by a food blogger as "a Kazuya alum's deeply flavorful clear-broth ramen" (「かづ屋」出身が作る滋味深い清湯ラーメン).

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only 9-seat counter ramen shop with no English menu or signage found in any source; some Japanese ability will help when ordering.

places.name: フラックス カフェ (FLUX CAFE)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen avoids wheat flour in most dishes — noodles (ramen/udon-style) and dorayaki are made with rice flour, and fried chicken is coated in potato starch rather than wheat flour — but the soy sauce used in seasoning still contains some wheat/gluten, so the menu is described as "close to gluten-free" rather than certified gluten-free. Staff can suggest suitable dishes if asked, but there is no confirmation of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or fully segregated prep for celiac-level safety.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or clear vegan labeling was found. Signature dishes (obanzai sets, oyakodon, chicken bowls) are built around egg, chicken and likely dashi/bonito, so the menu leans omnivorous rather than plant-based. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about ingredient substitutions and dashi base before ordering.
places.neighborhood: Daikanyama / Sarugaku-cho, Shibuya-ku
places.notes: FLUX CAFE occupies the ground floor of the Daikanyama Kinoshita Building, the same small building that houses the swim facility AQUALAB and the training gym FLUX CONDITIONINGS on the upper floors. The whole complex is run by IMPRINT, the company headed by Olympic gold-medal swimm · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

roles: cafe attached to a fitness/swim training complex
chef_bio.origin: Daikanyama, Shibuya-ku, Tokyo — part of an athletic training complex founded by swimmer Kosuke Kitajima's company IMPRINT
chef_bio.background: FLUX CAFE occupies the ground floor of the Daikanyama Kinoshita Building, the same small building that houses the swim facility AQUALAB and the training gym FLUX CONDITIONINGS on the upper floors. The whole complex is run by IMPRINT, the company headed by Olympic gold-medal swimmer Kosuke Kitajima, who serves as general manager of the training side of the operation. The café was built to feed the athletes and gym members training upstairs, then opened its doors to the neighborhood as a health-minded lunch and coffee spot.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen organizes its menu around two ideas: home-style Japanese obanzai cooking kept as close to gluten-free as practical, and 'athlete's meals' that are low-fat, low-gluten and high-protein for people training hard next door.
chef_bio.specialty: Obanzai lunch set — five rotating home-style Japanese side dishes with miso soup and rice, built around the café's low-fat, low-gluten, high-protein 'athlete's meal' concept

anecdotes.text: The café concept pairs 'gluten-free-leaning obanzai home cooking' with 'athlete's meals' — Japanese-based healthy set meals that are low-fat, low-gluten and high-protein, including the five-dish obanzai lunch set with miso soup and rice.
Noodles used for ramen/udon-style dishes and the dorayaki are made with rice flour instead of wheat flour, and the fried chicken is coated in potato starch rather than wheat flour.

FLUX CAFE sits in the same Daikanyama Kinoshita Building as the AQUALAB swim facility and FLUX CONDITIONINGS training gym, both part of the IMPRINT complex overseen by Olympic swimmer Kosuke Kitajima.

gf_cross_contamination.text: お醤油にグルテンが入っているため『低グルテン』と表現 — the cafe describes itself as 'low gluten' because the soy sauce contains gluten; ramen/udon noodles are rice flour and fried chicken is potato starch, but seasoning is not gluten-free.
Concept is 低脂質・低グルテン・高タンパク (low-fat, LOW-gluten, high-protein) 'athlete meals' — the venue markets low-gluten, never gluten-free/celiac-safe.

'The dashi (broth) is made with bonito and kombu, with no granular dashi stock used' — confirms hidden katsuo/bonito (fish) in the base used across obanzai and soups, making dishes non-vegan by default.
Described as offering menus based on the concept 'グルテンフリーに近いおばんざい' (obanzai CLOSE TO gluten-free) — a deliberate hedge, not a gluten-free claim.
Third-party guide notes FLUX CAFE is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and serves regular gluten-containing items alongside low-gluten menu, implying cross-contamination risk for celiacs.
gf_cross_contamination.source: WebSearch summary referencing tokyo-furnished.com / tsunagujapan.com gluten-free guides

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy sauce used in the kitchen contains a small amount of wheat/gluten; aside from that, wheat flour itself is largely avoided elsewhere in the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Diners who are mindful of gluten can ask staff, who will suggest suitable menu items accordingly.

positives.text: Noodles used for ramen/udon-style dishes and the dorayaki are made with rice flour, and the fried chicken is coated in potato starch instead of wheat flour, keeping most of the menu low in gluten.

cultural_comfort.note: The café has an English-language Tabelog listing and Uber Eats presence and sits in cosmopolitan Daikanyama, but menus and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 南インド料理 シリバラジ 水道橋店 (Sri Balaji Suidobashi)
places.gf_detail: South Indian dishes such as dosa, idli, and rice-based "Meals" sets are naturally rice/lentil-based and often gluten-free by tradition, but no source found addresses whether asafoetida (hing, a very common hidden-wheat ingredient in Indian cooking), spice blends, or fryers are handled to avoid cross-contact with wheat. No dedicated gluten-free fryer or process was confirmed. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff about specific dishes before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant offers South Indian vegetarian dishes (e.g., sambar, vada, vegetable-based "Meals") alongside meat and fish curries, and the whole food menu is prepared to halal standards. However, South Indian cooking commonly uses ghee (dairy) and no source confirmed ghee-free/vegan-specific preparation or dashi-free sauces, so this is vegetarian-friendly with likely-but-unconfirmed vegan options — vegan diners should confirm specific dishes with staff.
places.hours_raw: Lunch: Mon-Sun (incl. day-before-holidays and holidays) 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30). Dinner: Mon-Sun (incl. day-before-holidays and holidays) 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). No fixed closed day.
places.neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidobashi), Chiyoda-ku, near Jimbocho
places.notes: The head chef is from Tamil Nadu in South India and previously worked at well-known hotels there before bringing his cooking to Tokyo. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.origin: The restaurant's flagship location is in Nakameguro; the Suidobashi branch opened on August 15, 2015 as the group's second location.
chef_bio.background: The head chef is from Tamil Nadu in South India and previously worked at well-known hotels there before bringing his cooking to Tokyo.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen imports its spices and herbs directly from India and cooks South Indian dishes without altering their original flavors. Although the owners are not Muslim themselves, they prepare the entire food menu to halal standards as part of their commitment to quality and hospitality for all guests.
chef_bio.specialty: South Indian thali-style 'Meals' sets, dosa, and biryani

anecdotes.text: スパイスやハーブは全てインドから直輸入したものを使用しており、シェフはタミルナドゥ州の有名なホテルで働いていた南インド出身の方です。
イスラム教徒ではありませんがフードメニューは全てハラールにこだわっています。
水道橋店は2015年8月15日にオープンした2号店で、本店は中目黒にあります。

positives.text: 南インドのベジタリアンメニュー代表のサンバルをはじめ、豆のペーストを揚げたワダなどヘルシーでユニークな料理がたくさんあります。また、米と魚のカレーや新鮮な野菜を使用したベジタリアンのメニューもお任せくださいとのことです。

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant maintains a full English-language official website with an English menu page, making it easier for non-Japanese-speaking diners to browse the menu and order with confidence.

places.name: 中華菜房 達磨 (Chuka Saibo Daruma)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options or labeling found. The menu is built around wheat noodles (ramen, tanmen, dandan noodles, cold ramen) and wheat-wrapper gyoza, and Chinese-style cooking here relies on soy sauce (wheat-containing) for seasoning. No evidence of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or GF-adapted dishes; not recommended for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options identified. The menu centers on meat dishes (fried chicken cutlet sets, stir-fried liver with chives) and noodle soups that are very likely made with meat/bone-based broths and oyster sauce, standard for this style of town-Chinese cooking. No vegan labeling or plant-based substitution found in any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30) daily, per most sources; open year-round with no fixed closed day reported by the majority of listings (one conflicting index lists Wednesday closed, unconfirmed).
places.neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established neighborhood Chinese restaurant (町中華, machi-chuka) near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, in business for more than 40 years. The shop occupies the ground floor of the Pegasus-kan building with entrances from both the Shinjuku Higashiguchi shopping street and the Maru · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: A long-established neighborhood Chinese restaurant (町中華, machi-chuka) near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, in business for more than 40 years. The shop occupies the ground floor of the Pegasus-kan building with entrances from both the Shinjuku Higashiguchi shopping street and the Marui department store side, and has long been a go-to spot for local office workers and shoppers for filling, good-value meals.
chef_bio.specialty: Seafood ankake noodles (海鮮うま煤そば) — thick-sauced noodles topped with seafood — along with classic town-Chinese staples such as cold ramen (冷やし中華), tanmen, dandan noodles, and generous set meals like fried chicken cutlet or stir-fried liver with chives served with rice.

anecdotes.text: A Rocket News 24 writer visiting in January 2025 called the seafood ankake noodles so good they didn't want the bowl to end, praising the shop as a 40-plus-year-old neighborhood institution just three minutes from Shinjuku Station's east exit.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese-style soy sauce (containing wheat) appears to be used throughout the menu, as is typical for machi-chuka cooking; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was found in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a classic local machi-chuka diner with no English website found and no confirmed English menu; expect Japanese-only signage and ordering, though pointing at a photo menu should work fine.

places.name: もつ鍋と焼酎のお店 とうか (Touka (Motsunabe to Shochu no Mise Touka))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was identified in any source. The motsunabe broths are traditionally soy-sauce or miso based (both normally contain wheat), and menu items such as fried squid karaage and gyoza dumplings use wheat-based coatings/wrappers, so there is meaningful likely wheat exposure and cross-contact risk for celiac diners; nothing in available sources indicates accommodation for gluten-free needs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat- and offal-focused hot pot and izakaya menu (wagyu motsu/beef offal, shabu-shabu meats, raw ham, smoked duck, wagyu hamburger steak); no vegetarian or vegan dishes were found in any source, and the signature hot pot broths are meat-based.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays: lunch 11:45-14:00, dinner 17:30-24:00 (last order 23:00). Saturday/Sunday: dinner only, 17:30-24:00 (last order 23:00), no lunch service. No regular closed days.
places.neighborhood: 渋谷・道玄坂（渋谷駅南口から徒歩2分）
places.notes: The restaurant's chef previously worked as a cook aboard the luxury cruise liner Queen Elizabeth 2 and at the Hilton Tokyo before opening this motsunabe and shochu izakaya near Shibuya Station. · 📍 Approx. map location — confirm the exact spot (auto-placed from address).

chef_bio.background: The restaurant's chef previously worked as a cook aboard the luxury cruise liner Queen Elizabeth 2 and at the Hilton Tokyo before opening this motsunabe and shochu izakaya near Shibuya Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around carefully selected wagyu beef offal and a range of hot-pot broth styles, paired with a large selection of shochu.
chef_bio.specialty: Wagyu beef motsunabe (offal hot pot)

anecdotes.text: The wagyu beef offal is prized by regulars for its crisp texture and the sweet, fatty flavor it releases into the broth; the hot pot broth is offered in five flavors, including soy sauce, miso, and pork bone.
The lunchtime wagyu hamburger steak is made from A5-grade wagyu neck meat mixed with salt, pepper, panko breadcrumbs, and onion, and diners choose from ponzu, demi-glace, or yakiniku sauce.
The menu also features raw ham, smoked duck, thick fried omelet, fried squid karaage, and gyoza dumplings alongside the hot pot courses.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried squid karaage and fried gyoza dumplings, both typically using wheat-based coatings/wrappers; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or preparation area was mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The motsunabe hot pot broth is offered in five flavors including soy sauce, miso, and pork bone; standard Japanese soy sauce and miso contain wheat, and no wheat-free tamari alternative was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on wagyu beef offal, shabu-shabu meats, raw ham, and smoked duck; no vegetarian or vegan dishes were identified in any source, and hot-pot broths are meat-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A standard Japanese-language izakaya near Shibuya Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though it is listed on Tabelog's English site.
